findings.claim: In FusedSharedExpertMLP._fused_grouped_swiglu_no_comm, `tokens_per_expert` (torch.full of size 1) and `scales` (torch.ones of size total_tokens) are freshly allocated on the device every single forward call. The `scales` buffer scales linearly with the number of tokens and is recomputed each iteration even though it is a constant (all ones). This adds per-step device allocations and a kernel launch that could be avoided by caching/reusing a buffer sized to the current token count, or by passing None if the fused kernel treats absent scales as unity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark the shared-expert forward under use_grouped_gemm_for_shared_expert=True with large batch*seq token counts; profile allocator activity and kernel-launch count for the scales/tokens_per_expert setup vs a cached-buffer variant.

findings.claim: In the overlapped shared-expert path, FusedSharedExpertMLP.linear_fc1_forward_and_act now computes FC1+activation+FC2 fused together, and linear_fc2_forward becomes a no-op assert. The original SharedExpertMLP design split FC1 and FC2 into separate stages so each could be individually overlapped/interleaved with the token dispatcher's comm stages. Collapsing both GEMMs into the FC1 stage may reduce the amount of shared-expert compute that overlaps with dispatcher communication, potentially lengthening the critical path when moe_shared_expert_overlap is enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end MoE layer step time with moe_shared_expert_overlap=True for FusedSharedExpertMLP vs SharedExpertMLP, measuring overlap of shared-expert GEMMs with alltoall dispatch via a profiler timeline.

findings.claim: _fused_grouped_swiglu_no_comm enters a te.pytorch.fp8_autocast context on every forward invocation. This is standard TE usage, but entering an fp8 autocast context has non-trivial Python/state overhead per call; when this path is hit for every layer every step, the cumulative Python overhead may be measurable for small token counts where GEMM time is short.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Python-side overhead of fp8_autocast entry/exit relative to GEMM execution time at small token counts for the fused shared expert path.

findings.claim: The new sequence-parallel-TP path adds extra collective communication in the DSAttention forward. When self.config.sequence_parallel and tp_size > 1, key and value tensors are now each gathered across the TP group via gather_from_sequence_parallel_region(key/value, group=tp_group) before the CP gather. These are additional all-gather collectives on the critical forward path (previously the SP TP gather of K/V was not performed here). For long sequences this materializes full-length K/V (tp_size x local rows) on every rank, increasing both communication volume and peak activation memory proportional to tp_size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSAttention forward with sequence_parallel=True, tp_size in {2,4,8}, measure step latency and peak memory vs. parent.

findings.claim: For the non-packed sequence-local TP path, get_cp_positions_from_layout is called with sq=sequence_parallel_tp_full_rows and the result is sliced to the local rank's rows. This computes full-length position metadata (tp_size x local rows) on every rank only to keep a 1/tp_size slice, adding redundant compute/allocation that scales with tp_size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark extract_query_positions / get_cp_positions_from_layout path with large seqlen and tp_size>1.

findings.claim: Setting average_gradients_across_tp_domain=True on all DSAIndexer parameters causes finalize_model_grads to issue an additional per-parameter all-reduce (AVG) over the TP domain during gradient synchronization. This is functionally required for duplicated params, but it adds TP-group collective traffic each step proportional to the number of indexer parameters.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure finalize_model_grads time with and without indexer params flagged, tp_size>1.

findings.claim: The indexer forward_before_topk is now wrapped in torch.enable_grad() when use_indexer_loss is true, retaining the autograd graph for the indexer computation. Previously the code path did not force grad enablement here; retaining activations for the indexer forward increases memory during training when the indexer loss is active.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Training step peak memory with indexer_loss_coeff>0 vs parent.

findings.claim: The DeepEP dispatcher's SM count was previously controlled by `moe_deepep_num_sms` (default 20). This commit replaces it with `moe_flex_dispatcher_num_sms`, falling back to 20 only when None. If a user had previously set a custom `moe_deepep_num_sms` (e.g. tuned above/below 20 for their hardware), that value is now silently ignored unless they migrate to `moe_flex_dispatcher_num_sms`. Mismatched SM allocation for DeepEP dispatch/combine kernels can reduce overlap efficiency and GPU utilization for existing tuned configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE training with DeepEP backend and a previously-tuned moe_deepep_num_sms; compare throughput before/after the rename.

findings.claim: HybridEP dispatch/combine SM count was previously `moe_hybridep_num_sms` (default None, letting HybridEP pick its own default). It is now sourced from `moe_flex_dispatcher_num_sms`. For users who tuned `moe_hybridep_num_sms`, that tuning is now silently dropped; also the combine API SM count is now tied to the same knob as dispatch (previously both were hybridep_num_sms but a shared knob may not be optimal per-op). Mis-tuned SM budget can expose communication and lower throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: HybridEP MoE run with a custom moe_hybridep_num_sms set; compare dispatch/combine kernel overlap and step time before/after.

findings.claim: In `_NCCLEPManager.get_permuted_hidden_states_by_experts` (non-static-shape path), `int(self.tokens_per_expert.sum().item())` forces a device-to-host synchronization on the hot path of every MoE layer forward. This stalls the CPU until the dispatch kernel completes, breaking comm/compute overlap and adding per-layer latency. DeepEP/HybridEP avoid such syncs where possible. static_shape=True avoids this, but it requires sm100+ hardware, so most current deployments hit the sync path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NCCL EP MoE forward on pre-Blackwell GPU (static_shape=False); profile CPU stalls / step time from the .item() sync per layer.

findings.claim: `get_restored_hidden_states_by_experts` performs a `torch.cat` with a freshly allocated zero buffer of `pad_rows x H` to re-expand the narrowed expert output back to full recv_capacity on every combine (non-static-shape path). This allocates and copies the full recv-capacity buffer each MoE layer, adding memory traffic and a transient allocation proportional to recv_capacity_per_rank, which is sized generously (max_tokens * topk * capacity_factor).
findings.suggested_benchmark: NCCL EP combine microbenchmark measuring allocator pressure/time for the pad+cat vs. a preallocated buffer.

findings.claim: In `_NCCLEPManager.dispatch`, a fresh `EpBuffer` is allocated per dispatch via `new_nccl_ep_buffer` and dropped after combine. Allocating/freeing the handle_mem routing table and receive buffers (sized to recv_capacity_per_rank x hidden) on every MoE layer forward, rather than reusing a pooled buffer, adds allocator churn and potential fragmentation across many layers/microbatches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-layer EpBuffer alloc/free overhead across a multi-layer MoE model with NCCL EP.

findings.claim: When use_symm_mem is enabled, unshard_parameters() calls self._unsharded_model_weight.rendezvous(gather_axis) on every unshard, and reduce_gradients() calls partial_grad.rendezvous(reduce_axis) on every reduction. symm_mem.rendezvous() is itself a blocking collective handshake across the process group that exchanges memory handles. Unshard runs every forward (and re-forward during backward) and reduce runs every backward, so this injects an extra process-group-wide synchronizing collective on every micro-step, adding latency and a serialization point on the critical path that the non-symm-mem path does not have. Rendezvous is normally a one-time-per-allocation operation, not per-iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-GPU FSDP step-time comparison with use_symm_mem=True vs False over many steps, measuring per-step latency and NCCL timeline for redundant rendezvous handshakes.

findings.claim: unshard_parameters() calls reallocate_storage() (resize storage up from 0) inside the symmetric-memory MemPool context, and reshard_parameters() calls release_storage() (resize to 0) every step. Repeatedly freeing and re-allocating the unsharded buffer's storage from the symmetric-memory pool each iteration likely forces the symmetric-memory registration to be torn down and re-established each step (which is exactly why rendezvous() must be re-called every unshard). This defeats the persistent-registration benefit of symmetric memory and adds per-step allocation + registration overhead versus keeping the symmetric buffer resident.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocator/registration activity per step with symm-mem enabled; compare against a variant that keeps the symmetric buffer resident across steps.

findings.claim: The symm-mem path replaces ReduceOp.AVG with ReduceOp.SUM plus a separate self.main_grad.local_buffer.div_(grad_divisor) (or reduced_grad.local_buffer.div_) applied to the full gradient buffer after every reduce-scatter. This adds an extra full-buffer elementwise division kernel launch per parameter group per backward that the AVG path fuses into the collective, increasing GPU work and kernel-launch overhead proportional to gradient size and micro-batch count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark reduce_gradients() with symm-mem SUM+div_ vs AVG on a large gradient buffer, measuring added div_ kernel time per step.

findings.claim: symm_mem.set_backend('NCCL') and symm_mem.get_mem_pool() are invoked per FsdpParameterGroup construction. This is one-time setup and comments note set_backend is cached in C++, but with many parameter groups (many modules) these repeated Python/C++ round-trips add measurable but bounded startup cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time fully_shard() over a model with hundreds of shardable modules with use_symm_mem=True vs False.

findings.claim: This revert reintroduces an unconditional torch.cuda.empty_cache() on every checkpoint save (inside save_checkpoint_and_time). empty_cache() releases all cached-but-unused blocks back to the CUDA driver, forcing the caching allocator to re-request memory from the driver (cudaMalloc) on subsequent iterations. Those cudaMalloc/cudaFree round-trips are expensive and synchronize the device, so the training iterations immediately following each checkpoint will run measurably slower (allocator warm-up cost). With frequent checkpointing this repeatedly thrashes the allocator.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration step time for the N iterations immediately after a checkpoint save with and without the empty_cache() call (e.g. checkpoint every 100 steps, compare step-time distribution of steps 101-110 vs steady state).

findings.claim: Calling model_chunk.free_overlap_buffers() on every checkpoint frees the overlap param-gather buffers. These buffers must then be reallocated (and, for the caching allocator, re-fetched from the driver after the accompanying empty_cache()) when training resumes after the checkpoint. This adds allocation and buffer-setup latency to the first post-checkpoint iterations, partially serializing what would otherwise be overlapped param-gather.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With distributed optimizer + overlap_param_gather enabled, compare the first several post-checkpoint iteration times with/without the free_overlap_buffers() call.

findings.claim: The reclaim workaround runs unconditionally on every save regardless of whether async checkpointing is actually enabled (the comment justifies it only for the async checkpoint worker's D2H headroom). For synchronous checkpointing or configs with ample GPU headroom, this pays the empty_cache()/free_overlap_buffers() cost with no benefit; gating it behind the async-save flag would avoid the slowdown in those cases.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare checkpoint-adjacent step times for synchronous-save configs where the reclaim provides no headroom benefit.

findings.claim: When the user does NOT pass --mamba-training-ssm-states-dtype (the common/default case) and an installed mamba_ssm supports the state_dtype kwarg (MAMBA_HAS_STATE_DTYPE=True), the code now explicitly forwards state_dtype=config.params_dtype to mamba_split_conv1d_scan_combined / mamba_chunk_scan_combined. Previously state_dtype was not passed and the kernel defaulted the materialized inter-chunk SSM states to C.dtype (the activation dtype). If params_dtype differs from the activation dtype (e.g. fp32 master params while activations are bf16), the inter-chunk states are now materialized in fp32 instead of bf16. These states have shape (nchunks, nheads, headdim, dstate) and are materialized both in the forward and again for the backward, so doubling their element size measurably increases activation memory and adds extra dtype conversions/bandwidth on the SSM path. This is a behavior change that can silently increase memory usage relative to the parent commit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Train a Mamba/hybrid model with a mamba_ssm build that supports state_dtype, comparing peak GPU memory and step time on the parent commit vs this commit with the flag left unset, under a config where params_dtype != activation dtype.

findings.claim: MAMBA_HAS_STATE_DTYPE performs two inspect.signature() calls at module import time. This is a one-time cost paid at import and is negligible, but if mamba_split_conv1d_scan_combined/mamba_chunk_scan_combined were MagicMock (HAVE_MAMBA_SSM False) the short-circuit avoids it; correctly guarded. No runtime hot-path impact.

The new `tensor_need_offloading_checker` now calls `_te_do_not_offload(tensor)` for every candidate tensor. That helper does `getattr(tensor, '_TE_do_not_offload', ...)`, and if absent, checks `hasattr(tensor, 'get_data_tensors')` and (for TE wrapper tensors) calls `tensor.get_data_tensors()` inside a try/except and iterates the returned list. `tensor_need_offloading_checker` is invoked once per saved tensor per forward. For TE quantized/wrapper tensors this adds an extra Python method call + list iteration on the hot activation-save path, adding measurable CPU overhead (which for small step times can bottleneck GPU utilization). The try/except and attribute probing are pure-Python overhead paid on every activation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark tensor_need_offloading_checker throughput with a TE wrapper tensor (get_data_tensors present) vs a plain cuda tensor, and measure per-step CPU time on an MoE model with fine_grained_activation_offloading enabled.

findings.claim: `tensor_push` and `tensor_need_offloading_checker` both now call `_can_manage_tensor_for_offload`, which does an isinstance-against-a-tuple check plus a `tensor.device.type == 'cuda'` access and a Parameter isinstance check, for every saved tensor. Previously `tensor_push` only did a single isinstance check + assert. This roughly doubles the per-tensor Python guard cost on the activation-save hot path. Minor, but paid on every activation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step CPU time / tokens-per-sec with and without the added guards on a model that saves many activations per layer.

findings.claim: The new `fused_group_mlp` offload group is added to the set that disables the CPU pinned-memory pool (`use_cpu_pool = False`). Without pooling, each offloaded fused-grouped-MLP activation allocates a fresh pinned CPU tensor per iteration instead of reusing one, which increases pinned-memory allocation churn and can slow down repeated iterations. This mirrors expert_fc1/moe_act (whose shapes are dynamic), so it may be intentional, but the fused group MLP input shape (permuted_local_hidden_states) is also token-count dependent and thus non-poolable — the cost is inherent to offloading dynamic-shape activations.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure iteration-to-iteration pinned host allocation time / total throughput with fused_group_mlp offload enabled vs a poolable module.

findings.claim: Enabling `fused_group_mlp` offload wraps the entire fused grouped MLP input (permuted_local_hidden_states) through the offload interface and forces its release, meaning the full permuted hidden-states activation (token_count x hidden_size) is copied D2H in forward and H2D in backward. If host-device bandwidth cannot fully overlap with compute, this adds latency to every MoE layer's backward. This is the intended tradeoff of offloading, but worth benchmarking the overlap efficiency vs recompute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end MoE (e.g. DeepSeek-style) training step time with fused_group_mlp offload on vs off, measuring backward-pass reload stall time.

findings.claim: The reusable TensorReusePool (which recycled input/output buffers across cudagraphs) was removed and replaced with alloc_tensor_from_graph_mempool, which allocates a fresh tensor via meta.zeros_like() inside the graph mempool on every _resolve_input_buffer / static_grad_output request. Whereas the old pool reused matching (shape/dtype/device) buffers via .get()/.insert(), the new path never returns tensors for reuse — each capture site now allocates its own buffer. For partial CUDA graphs with many layers this can increase the cudagraph mempool footprint since buffers are no longer shared across graphs where shapes match, contradicting the stated goal unless the mempool driver-level reuse fully compensates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture partial local CUDA graphs on a multi-layer transformer and compare peak reserved/allocated GPU memory before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: In _CudagraphReplayNode.forward, when runner.is_last_layer, the outputs are now cloned unconditionally: `outputs = tuple(torch.clone(t) for t in runner.fwd_graph_output_surface)` on every forward replay. This adds one extra full-tensor copy per last-layer replay per microbatch that did not exist before (previously fwd_graph_output_surface was returned directly). For large hidden-state activation tensors this is a measurable per-step memory-bandwidth cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile forward replay time and copy bandwidth for the last-layer graph replay with large sequence/hidden sizes.

findings.claim: start_grad_sync in param_and_grad_buffer.py now iterates over every param of every bucket in the group on each call and does current_stream.wait_event(event) for each distinct _cudagraph_wgrad_ready_event. This adds a per-bucket-group loop over all params plus stream wait_event calls on the grad-reduce path. The wait_event forces the reduction stream to block until the bwd cudagraph replay completes, which can serialize gradient reduction behind graph replay and reduce overlap of grad_reduce with compute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure grad-reduce/compute overlap and end-to-end backward time with overlap_grad_reduce enabled and local cudagraphs, comparing before/after.

findings.claim: gc.collect() and torch.cuda.empty_cache() were removed from create_cudagraphs() before capture. This is likely a speedup for capture time but removing empty_cache() before capturing many graphs may increase peak reserved memory / fragmentation during the capture phase because cached blocks are not returned to the allocator before the mempool captures begin.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak reserved GPU memory during create_cudagraphs() before/after removal of gc.collect()/empty_cache().

findings.claim: wgrads are now accumulated into param.main_grad via param.main_grad.add_(wgrad) inside the captured backward graph (per param, per graph). This replaces the previous emulated grad_added_to_main_grad path. If main_grad.add_ is executed eagerly during capture recording (rather than fused into the graph replay) it is fine, but if params_to_backprop is large the per-param Python loop over add_ adds launch overhead per backward capture; verify it is captured into the graph and not re-run per replay.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile backward graph capture and replay to confirm the per-param main_grad.add_ is captured once and not launched per-param on every replay.

findings.claim: In `_unshard_parameter_groups`, after issuing the all-gather (and, in forward, the per-microbatch `sync_model_weight_from_main_weight` cast) on `allgather_stream`, the code does an unconditional `current_stream.wait_stream(allgather_stream)`. This blocks the compute stream on the *entire* body just enqueued on the allgather stream, including the potentially expensive `sync_model_weight_from_main_weight` cast/redistribute. Because sync and unshard are both placed on the side stream and joined immediately, the compute stream cannot proceed until both finish, so the prefetch/overlap window for the current unit is effectively zero — only the *next* sibling's all-gather overlaps this unit's compute. The weight-sync copy (run every microbatch, per the TODO) is serialized in front of compute rather than overlapped, adding its latency to the critical path each microbatch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile forward of a multi-layer FSDP model with mixed precision (main_params_dtype != model dtype) so sync_model_weight_from_main_weight performs a real cast; compare per-iteration time and the overlap of the sync copy vs GEMMs against a variant that only joins on the all-gather.

findings.claim: The unsharded buffer's storage is reallocated and filled by the all-gather on `allgather_stream`, but it is consumed by compute on the current (compute) stream, and later freed (via `release_unsharded_storage`/`reallocate_storage`->resize_) again on `allgather_stream`. No `record_stream(compute_stream)` is issued for these cross-stream-used buffers. The caching allocator tracks free-readiness by the stream a block was allocated on; a block allocated/freed on the allgather stream but read on the compute stream can be handed back to a new allgather-stream allocation before the compute stream has finished reading it. The event-based `drain_delayed_releases` waits for the consumer event only before the *resize-to-0*, but the subsequent CUDA-caching-allocator reuse of that freed block by the next all-gather is only ordered on the allgather stream, so a fresh all-gather can overwrite storage still being read by an in-flight GEMM, forcing either correctness fallbacks or extra synchronization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress test many microbatches with small child layers and compare numerical results / run under compute-sanitizer to detect races; measure whether adding record_stream changes allocator reuse behavior and peak memory.

findings.claim: `enqueue_release` calls `record_event()` with no argument, which allocates a brand-new `torch.cuda.Event` on every enqueue — i.e., once per FSDP unit per forward and per backward, every microbatch. Event creation/destruction incurs CPU-side driver overhead and churn; reusing a small pool of pre-created events (or the module's own persistent event) would avoid the per-call allocation on the training hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark a deep FSDP model (many units) over many iterations, counting CUDA event allocations and comparing CPU-side hook overhead with a pooled-event implementation.

findings.claim: At root `pre_forward`, `allgather_stream.wait_stream(current_stream)` forces the all-gather stream to wait on all prior compute-stream work before the first all-gather can start. This is a hard join every forward that can delay the root's first all-gather behind unrelated trailing compute (e.g. previous iteration's optimizer/other-stream work if it landed on the compute stream), reducing the head-of-forward prefetch benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the gap between forward entry and first NCCL all-gather kernel start across iterations with and without the root wait_stream, under a workload with residual compute-stream work at forward entry.

findings.claim: For a fixed prefix_caching_mamba_gb budget, the number of durable Mamba cache slots is now reduced by scratch_slots = MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST (3) * max_requests. When max_requests is large (e.g. auto-derived from a big KV buffer), the extraction scratch reservation consumes a large fraction of the budget, leaving far fewer durable slots than before. Fewer durable slots means a lower prefix-cache hit rate, so more Mamba recurrent state must be recomputed across requests, reducing prefill-skip savings and increasing latency/compute for the same configured budget. The tests confirm the magnitude: budgets had to be raised 10-40x (0.05->2.0, 0.2->4.0, 0.001->0.01) just to retain any durable slots.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a hybrid Mamba model with a large KV buffer (large auto-derived max_requests) and a fixed prefix_caching_mamba_gb, measure prefix-cache hit rate and end-to-end prefill throughput before vs. after this commit for a workload with shared prefixes.

findings.claim: Previously a too-small Mamba cache budget logged a warning and disabled Mamba caching (context continued running with prefix caching effectively degraded but functional). This commit converts that path into a hard ValueError. This is not itself a runtime slowdown, but it changes a graceful degradation into a startup failure; deployments that previously ran (slowly, without Mamba caching) will now fail to initialize unless prefix_caching_mamba_gb is increased or max_requests reduced. Worth noting because operators may need to raise the budget substantially given the new scratch reservation.

The number of concurrent generation tasks (and the submission-gate semaphore size) is changed from `num_workers = parallel_generation_tasks // groups_per_worker` to the full `parallel_generation_tasks`. In non-rollout-granularity modes each `generate_task` still calls `group_rollout` which itself fans out `rollouts_per_group` concurrent `agenerate` calls. Previously the outstanding in-flight rollouts were bounded to ~`num_workers * groups_per_worker * rollouts_per_group`; now that becomes `parallel_generation_tasks * groups_per_worker * rollouts_per_group`, i.e. up to `groups_per_worker`x more concurrent inference requests than before. This increases peak concurrent load on the inference engine (more KV-cache pressure, more preemption/eviction, higher latency) unless `parallel_generation_tasks` is retuned. The gate size increased in lock-step so it does not throttle this back.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run GRPO streaming rollouts with grpo_group_size>1 and num_groups>1 and measure peak concurrent agenerate calls / KV-cache eviction count and end-to-end rollout throughput before vs after.

findings.claim: In batch-consumption mode the new `while not await generate_and_enqueue(batch_id, 0): pass` loop unconditionally regenerates a group whenever it is filtered out (all rewards equal under filter_groups_with_same_reward). Each retry performs a full set of `rollouts_per_group` inference calls, so a run where many groups are filtered can waste substantial GPU inference work with no upper bound on retries. Previously a filtered group simply decremented submitted_groups and released the gate (no forced immediate re-generation on the same task).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable filter_groups_with_same_reward with a reward function that frequently yields identical rewards; measure number of agenerate calls and wall-clock vs baseline.

findings.claim: httpx connection-pool `concurrency_limit` now derives from `get_rl_parallel_generation_tasks(args)` which for submission_granularity 'B' equals `rl_generation_lag + 1` (default lag small), instead of the previous default of 512. If the computed value is smaller than the actual number of concurrent agenerate tasks spawned by get_grouped_rollouts, the httpx client will queue connections beyond `max_connections`, serializing inference requests and reducing GPU utilization / throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare max_connections computed value against actual concurrent request count; measure request queueing latency at the httpx layer under high parallel_generation_tasks.

findings.claim: This commit removes the `free_overlap_buffers()` + `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` block that ran right before saving a checkpoint. That block was explicitly a workaround to release overlap param-gather buffers and return cached GPU allocator blocks to the driver so the async checkpoint worker process had enough free GPU memory for its device-to-host tensor transfers. Removing it means, at checkpoint time, the CUDA caching allocator keeps holding the freed overlap-buffer blocks (and other cached blocks) as reserved memory. When an async checkpoint save is enabled, the separate D2H copy worker can now fail to allocate or, at best, drives peak GPU memory higher during checkpointing — a memory-usage regression and a potential OOM-induced hang/failure under memory pressure. The comment being removed states this rationale directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a training job with distributed optimizer + overlap_param_gather and async checkpoint saving enabled at high GPU memory utilization; measure torch.cuda.max_memory_reserved()/allocated during save_checkpoint_and_time and check the async D2H worker does not OOM, comparing parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: Dropping the pre-checkpoint `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` removes a point where cached-but-unused allocator blocks were returned to the driver. If the workaround was genuinely load-bearing, removing it could increase GPU memory fragmentation/peak reserved memory during and after checkpointing; if it was not load-bearing, its removal actually AVOIDS a costly full-cache flush (empty_cache forces a device sync and subsequent re-allocation cost), so removal could be a small performance win instead. The net effect depends on whether the async worker relied on the freed headroom.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare checkpoint-save wall time and post-checkpoint step latency (re-allocation warmup cost) parent vs this commit, since empty_cache() itself forces a device synchronize and later re-allocations.

findings.claim: Before this change, FusedMLASelfAttention inherited MLASelfAttention.backward_dw(), which calls self.linear_q_down_proj.backward_dw() and self.linear_kv_down_proj.backward_dw(). The fused variant does not build those attributes (it only has self.linear_qkv_down_proj), so the inherited backward_dw() would raise AttributeError. This override restores correct delayed weight-gradient (dwgrad) computation by calling backward_dw() on linear_qkv_down_proj / linear_q_up_proj / linear_kv_up_proj / output proj. Without this fix, the delayed-wgrad feature is broken for the fused MLA path — either failing outright or (if it silently no-oped) leaving weight gradients uncomputed. The added override is the correct/required behavior; the pre-change state was the performance/correctness defect. No new performance degradation is introduced by the added code.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a fused-MLA training step with delay_wgrad_compute (delayed weight gradient) enabled and verify backward_dw executes all four sub-projection dwgrad calls without AttributeError, and that gradients match the non-fused path.

findings.claim: set_for_recompute_input_layernorm on the fused path now calls set_save_original_input only on linear_qkv_down_proj (the single fused down projection), which is the correct single input for the fused layout. This matches the intent of the base-class method (which sets it on both linear_q_down_proj and linear_kv_down_proj) and prevents extra memory usage from storing a quantized input tensor during fp8/fp4 recompute of the input layernorm. No slowdown introduced; this is a memory-correctness fix for the fused variant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable fp8 with input-layernorm recompute on fused MLA and measure peak activation memory vs. the non-fused path to confirm the extra quantized-input tensor is not retained.

findings.claim: This change removes the `inspect.currentframe()` / `inspect.getframeinfo(frame)` calls from `_get_validation_call_info`, which is invoked on every `validate_result()` call (once per validated result per training step). `inspect.getframeinfo()` internally reads the source file from disk via `linecache` (a `stat`/`open` syscall the first time, and frame/traceback object introspection is generally expensive relative to a simple attribute read). Eliminating it removes per-validation-call overhead on the training hot path, so this is a net performance improvement, not a regression. The only behavioral trade-off is that stats logging now identifies calls by their `message` string instead of file/line.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark calling validate_result() in a tight loop (e.g. 1e5 iterations) with rerun validation enabled, before vs after, measuring wall-clock time per call. Also profile a real train_step with REPORT_DETERMINISM_STATS mode to confirm reduced Python overhead.

findings.claim: The new THD path builds the a2a permutation on the fly via `_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm`, which is not cached (only `_build_head_perm_for_split_sections` has `@lru_cache`). It runs several GPU tensor ops (`torch.arange`, `torch.bucketize`, `torch.diff`, `torch.where`, indexing, `torch.empty_like`+scatter) on every forward call. `cu_seqlens` also enters `_resolve_cu_seqlens` and the code calls `cu[-1].item()`/`.tolist()` in tests but in production `_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm` reconstructs indices per step. This adds per-step GPU kernel launches and index_select overhead each forward that were previously not present (the old loop did the a2a directly per sequence). Net effect depends on cp_size/num_seqs but adds index-permutation cost proportional to t_global.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark GDN forward with THD packed sequences (many short sequences) at cp_size=2/4, comparing old per-sequence loop vs new fused path, measuring per-step latency including the permutation-index construction.

findings.claim: The fused THD path introduces two full-tensor `index_select` operations on qkvzba (dim 0 permute of size t_global x hidden) in cp2hp plus another `index_select` on norm_out_hp in hp2cp. Each `index_select` allocates a new full-size tensor (gather copy), adding transient memory equal to the activation size and extra memcpy bandwidth versus the old per-sequence a2a which concatenated smaller pieces. For large hidden (Qwen-Next-sized: qk_dim*2+v_dim*2 ~ thousands) this is a measurable extra activation copy per forward and per backward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak memory and step time for GDN forward+backward with THD packing at cp_size>1 before/after; watch for the extra index_select copies.

findings.claim: The head-dim `index_select` with `_build_head_perm_for_split_sections` is added unconditionally for cp_size>1 in the non-THD path too, whereas previously the split_sections argument let a single a2a be done per section without a preliminary gather. The pre-permute adds one full-activation index_select on the last dim before the a2a; whether this is faster than the previous multi-section a2a depends on the number of sections (6) vs one extra gather. On the non-THD path the previous code did 6 separate a2a calls, so fusing to 1 a2a + 1 gather is likely a win, but the gather is a new allocation worth validating.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare non-THD cp2hp path: 6 sectioned a2a (old) vs 1 index_select + 1 a2a (new) at cp_size=2/4 for realistic hidden sizes.

findings.claim: This commit forcibly disables the Transformer Engine cross-entropy loss fusion path (`cross_entropy_fusion_impl='te'` now triggers an assertion when combined with `cross_entropy_loss_fusion`) and rewrites the release/GB200/deepseekv3 performance configs to use `native`. The `te` impl was previously listed in the MoE README under 'general performance flags that can help to achieve higher performance' and was selected in the tuned release benchmark YAMLs (gpt3_15b_8t_release_gb200, deepseekv3_proxy_flex_*_release), which implies it was chosen for a loss-computation throughput benefit. The `native` path (`fused_vocab_parallel_cross_entropy`) is a different kernel; on the affected large-vocab GB200 configs the loss/output-layer step may become measurably slower or use more memory than the TE fused kernel it replaces. Note this is an intentional stability-motivated tradeoff per the commit message, so any slowdown is deliberately accepted rather than an accidental regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a large-vocab GPT/Mixtral config (e.g. gpt3_15b_8t_release_gb200), measure end-to-end step time and peak memory for the cross-entropy/loss step with cross_entropy_fusion_impl='te' vs 'native' at matched batch/seq, comparing throughput of te_parallel_cross_entropy against fused_vocab_parallel_cross_entropy.

findings.claim: After passing group=self.bridge_pg to dist.send/recv and P2POp, the dst/src arguments are still GLOBAL ranks (dest_rank, src_rank derived from grid.rank_offset and comm_map). When a process group is supplied, torch.distributed interprets dst/src as GROUP-LOCAL ranks (indices into the group's rank list), not global ranks. Since the bridge_pg only contains the leader ranks (bridge_ranks = sorted(set(src_tp_leaders)|set(dest_tp_leaders))), a global rank like 4 will be reinterpreted as group-local index 4, targeting a completely different rank or raising an out-of-range error. In the common case this causes mismatched send/recv pairing and a deadlock/hang across the pipeline bridge (send waits on a peer that never posts a matching recv). This applies to send_forward (line ~381), recv_forward (line ~424), send_backward (line ~514), recv_backward (line ~553), and all P2POp batch_isend_irecv sites (activation/grad exchange and _communicate_shapes at lines ~668, ~791, ~917-961). Ranks must be converted to group-local via dist.get_group_rank(bridge_pg, global_rank) before use.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run test_bridge_communicator.py send_forward/recv_forward and 1F1B multimodule schedule tests on a multi-GPU (>=8) job with fan-in and fan-out grid configs; measure whether P2P completes or hangs, and validate received tensor correctness.

findings.claim: _get_or_create_bridge_pg calls dist.new_group(ranks, backend='nccl') during BridgeCommunicator.__init__. dist.new_group is a collective that must be called by ALL ranks in the default (WORLD) group, and it eagerly creates a NCCL communicator. Creating a new dedicated bridge PG per grid-pair adds NCCL communicator setup cost (extra GPU memory for communicator buffers and initialization latency) at construction time. If many bridge communicators are constructed (e.g. multi-module pipelines with several grid pairs), this multiplies NCCL communicator count and memory. The caching by sorted-rank key mitigates duplicates but still adds one communicator per distinct leader-rank set beyond the previously-used global group.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory and init time when constructing N bridge communicators with distinct leader sets, before vs after the dedicated PG change; count NCCL communicators created.

findings.claim: dist.new_group must be entered by every rank in WORLD even if a rank is not part of 'ranks'. __init__ is called on all ranks that construct a BridgeCommunicator, but if construction is guarded so that only ranks within the two grids build the communicator (while other WORLD ranks skip it), the new_group collective will hang because non-participating WORLD ranks never call new_group with matching arguments. This should be verified against how BridgeCommunicator is instantiated across the job.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a job where WORLD contains ranks outside both grids, construct a BridgeCommunicator and confirm no hang in new_group.

findings.claim: The new `_drain_offload_pending` inserts `main_stream.wait_event(old_evt)` joins when the per-name pending offload queue exceeds `_max_inflight_offloads`. With `max_inflight_offloads=0` (the minimum valid value the config assertion permits for full_iteration CUDA graphs), the main stream is forced to wait on the D2H offload event after every single offload commit. This effectively serializes the compute (main) stream against the D2H copy stream, defeating the overlap that fine-grained offloading is designed to provide and can significantly reduce GPU utilization / throughput. Choosing too small a cap (0 or 1) trades away compute/copy overlap for lower peak memory. This is an inherent tuning risk of the knob's semantics rather than a bug, but 0 is the documented default suggestion for the full_iteration path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run fine-grained activation offloading with full_iteration CUDA graphs at max_inflight_offloads in {0, 1, 2, 4, None} and measure per-iteration step time and peak memory to characterize the overlap-vs-memory tradeoff.

findings.claim: `_offload_pending_by_name` retains references to `OffloadTensorGroup._offload_event` objects until either the cap is exceeded (draining older events) or `reset()` clears the FIFO at iteration boundary. Since each group's event is reused across iterations (the group object persists), holding references in the deque is cheap, but if `max_inflight_offloads` is large relative to the number of groups of a given name in one iteration, the joins never fire during the iteration and are simply cleared at reset — meaning the main stream never waits, and the intended backpressure/memory cap does not actually take effect for that name. Users setting a large cap may get no memory protection while believing the knob is active.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable the knob with a cap larger than the per-name group count per iteration and verify via peak-memory measurement whether any main-stream waits actually occur.

findings.claim: In mixed-prefill mode under the new default EXPONENTIAL distribution, the per-token-count loop now iterates over a geometric P-grid `p_values = {1, 2, 4, ..., max_requests}` and calls `add_if_valid` for each P value, instead of the single fixed `prefill_req_count = min(cuda_graph_mixed_prefill_request_count, max_requests)` used before. This multiplies the number of mixed CUDA graph batch dimensions by up to `log2(max_requests)+1` (≈8–10 for typical max_requests in the hundreds) per prefill token count. Each additional captured graph costs graph-capture time at engine startup AND permanently reserves GPU memory in the CUDA graph mempool (static input/activation buffers per graph). This can substantially increase startup latency and steady-state GPU memory footprint versus the parent behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build the engine with mixed-prefill CUDA graphs enabled (num_cuda_graphs=-1, default EXPONENTIAL) at a representative max_requests/max_tokens; measure len(cuda_graph_batch_dimensions_list), create_cuda_graphs() wall time, and peak reserved GPU bytes vs the parent commit.

findings.claim: `_cuda_graph_mempool_bytes()` calls `torch.cuda.memory_snapshot()` and iterates every allocator segment, building Python dicts, once per graph-capture iteration (plus twice more). memory_snapshot() is O(number of segments) and materializes a full snapshot each call; with many graphs (further amplified by the P-grid growth above) this adds non-trivial CPU overhead to the capture loop. This is startup-only (not steady-state inference), so impact is bounded, but it is new per-iteration work compared to the parent, which only sampled memory_stats() twice total.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare create_cuda_graphs() wall time with and without the per-iteration `_cuda_graph_mempool_bytes()` call for a config that captures many graphs.

findings.claim: CUDA_GRAPH_ROUNDER was reduced from 8 to 2, quadrupling the granularity of token-count rounding. In the LINEAR explicit-N path and the exponential rounding step this can produce more distinct token counts (finer-grained sizes) that survive dedup, increasing the number of captured graphs and thus capture time and mempool memory. The default EXPONENTIAL log-spacing offsets this for the default path, but LINEAR configs and any explicit-N usage will see denser size sets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: For a LINEAR sizing_distribution config with explicit num_cuda_graphs, count distinct captured token counts and total graphs with ROUNDER=2 vs ROUNDER=8.

findings.claim: Enabling `fallback_to_persistent_buffer` on the `transpose_weight_alloc` FixedPoolAllocator means that MXFP8 transpose-weight buckets for 'outlier' (asymmetric, non-double-buffered) FSDP units will now be allocated as *persistent* buffers in the GlobalMemoryBuffer rather than being freed/reused. This trades peak-memory spikes / allocation failures for a permanent increase in steady-state GPU memory footprint: the transpose-weight buffers for outlier units are never freed after use (as the test explicitly asserts they 'must remain there after a full training step'). For models with several asymmetric FSDP units and large transpose-weight tensors this raises resident memory. This is the intended behavior of the feature (it only activates when `fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail=True`), so it is a deliberate memory-for-stability tradeoff rather than a regression, but it does increase memory usage in the enabled path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable fsdp_double_buffer + fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail on an MXFP8 model with asymmetric FSDP units and measure peak/steady-state GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) across a training step versus the prior behavior.

findings.claim: In `ParamAndGradBuffer.reset()`, when `megatron_fsdp_cuda_graph_mode=True` the code no longer dereferences `param.grad`/`param.decoupled_grad`/`self.dist_main_grad[name]._local_tensor`. When the gradient shard is NOT a view of the sharded gradient buffer (e.g. when a cast to the parameter dtype was applied because `main_weight_buffer is not None` and `use_decoupled_grad=False`, or when main-params/main-grads dtypes differ), the persistent casted copy of every gradient shard is kept alive across iterations instead of being freed. This increases steady-state memory by roughly one extra full sharded-gradient copy per rank. The code comments acknowledge this tradeoff, and the mode is only enabled with CUDA graphs, but users who enable full-iteration CUDA graphs without the recommended decoupled-grad / matching-dtype config will pay a measurable memory-footprint increase.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable --cuda-graph-impl full_iteration with Megatron-FSDP using mismatched main_params_dtype != main_grads_dtype and measure peak GPU memory vs. the eager (non-cuda-graph) path.

findings.claim: In `finalize_model_grads`, the host-side guard `if num_tokens > 0:` was replaced by always calling `model_chunk.scale_gradients(scaling)` after computing `scaling = 1.0 / torch.clamp(num_tokens, min=1)`. This removes an implicit device->host synchronization (a latency win, and required for CUDA graph capture), but it also means the gradient-scaling kernel now runs unconditionally on every model chunk even in the (rare) case where num_tokens==0 or scaling==1.0, adding a small elementwise pass over all gradients each step. Net effect is almost certainly positive (avoiding the sync), but it is a behavior change worth noting.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step time with and without the clamp-based unconditional scale_gradients on a large model to confirm the removed device->host sync outweighs the extra scaling kernel.

findings.claim: HybridEPDispatch.forward now defaults num_sms_preprocessing_api=108 (mirrored by the config default moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing=108). This is a hardcoded SM count for the preprocessing/metadata-scan kernel. On GPUs with fewer than 108 SMs, or where the previous DeepEP-internal default differed, this forces the preprocessing kernel to request a fixed 108 SMs; on smaller GPUs it may over-subscribe SMs (contending with overlapped compute), and on larger GPUs 108 may under-utilize available SMs. Because the value is passed through unconditionally rather than left to the backend's adaptive default, throughput of the dispatch path may change relative to the previous behavior where this parameter was not specified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure MoE token-dispatch (HybridEP) latency/throughput with moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing at the new default 108 vs. the prior (unspecified) backend default, on the target GPU SKU, under an overlapped combined-1f1b schedule.

findings.claim: When high_priority_a2a_comm_stream is enabled, the communication stream is created at the highest CUDA priority. A high-priority comm stream can preempt/deprioritize the default-priority compute stream, so if the a2a comm kernels are long-running they may delay GEMM/attention kernels and reduce overall GPU utilization. This is opt-in (default False), so it is speculative and only relevant when the flag is turned on.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end training step time and SM occupancy with high_priority_a2a_comm_stream=False vs True under the combined-1f1b schedule to confirm the high-priority comm stream does not starve compute kernels.

findings.claim: num_sms_preprocessing_api is only consumed inside init_hybrid_ep_buffer, which runs solely when _hybrid_ep_buffer is None. Since the buffer is cached globally and never reset unless reset_hybrid_ep_buffer() is called, changing moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing after the first dispatch (e.g., across differently-configured layers/managers sharing the module-global buffer) will silently have no effect, so the intended SM tuning may not take hold and expected performance gains would not materialize.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate two HybridEP managers with different moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing values and verify the second value actually reaches the kernel (i.e., that buffer re-init occurs), measuring preprocessing kernel SM usage.

findings.claim: The commit splits the previously-fused fp8 context block: `mtp_post_process.forward` is now moved out from directly after `moe_combine.forward` and placed after the backward `b_layer.attn.backward(b_grad)` (in the `not ep_overlap_early_attn_memory_release` branch). This introduces a second `with f_layer.get_fp8_context()` block, so the fp8 context manager is now entered/exited twice per layer instead of once. `get_fp8_context` can have non-trivial setup (fp8 recipe / amax bookkeeping); entering it a second time per forward layer adds a small per-layer overhead. This is minor and only affects fp8-enabled MTP runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MTP + fp8 1F1B overlap training and compare per-iteration time / fp8 context enter/exit count before vs after the reorder.

findings.claim: By reordering the forward `mtp_post_process` to run AFTER `b_layer.attn.backward`, the mtp_post_process forward (which runs on the comp_stream and includes the MTP output_layer/loss, a compute-heavy op) is now serialized after the attention backward on the same compute stream rather than being issued right after moe_combine. If the intent of the 1F1B overlap is to hide the mtp_post_process compute behind the backward-pass communication, this reorder could either help or hurt depending on stream assignment; issuing a large compute op after attn.backward on the comp_stream may delay the launch of the next layer's attn.forward and reduce compute/comm overlap. This is speculative without profiling the actual stream timeline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the CUDA stream timeline (nsys) for an MTP MoE model with 1F1B EP overlap, comparing GPU idle gaps on the comp stream before vs after this reorder.

findings.claim: The default of `cuda_graph_use_single_mempool` is flipped from False to True, and the meaning is repurposed so that full-iteration training, validation, AND optimizer CUDA graph captures all share one process-wide graph memory pool (`get_shared_graph_pool`). CUDA graphs that share a single mempool have their internal allocations overlapped/aliased by the allocator, so two such graphs cannot be safely 'live' at the same time — replaying one graph can clobber buffers owned by another. In the full-iteration path the optimizer graph replays in the same step immediately after the forward/backward graph, and both now draw from the same pool; if the fwd/bwd graph's persistent buffers (e.g. grads consumed by the optimizer) share storage with optimizer-pool allocations this can produce wrong results or force extra synchronization/serialization between the graphs, negating the latency benefit of graph replay. This behavior change ships silently to every user via the default flip.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run full_iteration cuda_graph training with optimizer_cuda_graph enabled, comparing use_single_mempool True vs False: measure step latency, memory_reserved, and numerical correctness (loss/grad match) over N steps.

findings.claim: All full-iteration (training + validation) and optimizer graph *captures* now share one non-default capture stream via `get_shared_capture_stream()` instead of a fresh `torch.cuda.Stream()` per wrapper. Sharing one capture stream forces all captures onto the same stream serially. This is a one-time capture cost (only at warmup steps), so runtime replay throughput is unaffected, but if any two captures were previously expected to overlap, they are now serialized. Impact is minor since capture happens once.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure total graph-capture (warmup) wall time with shared vs per-capture stream.

findings.claim: Because a single process-wide pool (`_shared_graph_pool`) is now reused across the separate training graph, validation graph, and optimizer graph, and each captured graph holds live allocations in that pool for the lifetime of the graph, the pool must be large enough to hold the union of all three graphs' persistent buffers simultaneously (since all three graphs coexist across an eval-interleaved run). If the intent (per docstring 'reduce memory_reserved') is only realized when graphs are sequential/one-at-a-time, holding multiple coexisting graphs in one pool could instead pin peak memory_reserved at the sum rather than the max. Worth validating that shared-pool actually reduces, not increases, reserved memory when train+eval+optimizer graphs all persist.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare torch.cuda.memory_reserved() peak across a run that includes training, periodic evaluation, and optimizer CUDA graphs, single_mempool True vs False.

findings.claim: The new cleanup block in train() adds an unconditional torch.distributed.barrier() plus a per-buffer nccl_allocator.deregister_mem_pool() loop on the should_exit path. deregister_mem_pool() (nccl_allocator.py) calls group._get_backend(...) and backend.deregister_mem_pool(pool) which is a collective/NCCL operation. This only runs once at shutdown, so steady-state training throughput is unaffected; the cost is confined to teardown. It is worth noting as a minor addition to exit latency, not a runtime hotpath regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-of-run teardown wall-time with nccl_ub enabled vs disabled to confirm the barrier + deregister loop adds only a fixed one-time cost.

findings.claim: The added torch.distributed.barrier() and deregister loop only execute when should_exit is True. deregister_mem_pool is effectively a collective; if should_exit is not reached identically on all ranks (e.g. divergent exit conditions), the barrier/deregister could hang at shutdown. This does not affect training throughput but is a potential teardown hang if exit is not globally consistent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Simulate rank-divergent exit conditions with nccl_ub enabled to verify the shutdown barrier/deregister does not deadlock.

findings.claim: _normalize_state_dict_for_grouped_params scans the entire flattened state-dict with str.endswith for each (module, param_name, gemm_idx) combination. For every TEGroupedLinear module it runs O(num_gemms) full linear passes over all keys in the checkpoint state dict (once for the grouped suffix, and once per indexed suffix). With many MoE layers × many experts × a large state dict this is O(num_modules * num_gemms * len(state_dict)) string matching. This only runs at checkpoint reload (reload_model_params), not per step, so it is a one-time startup cost, but can be noticeably slow for large MoE checkpoints.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time reload_model_params / optimizer state reload for a large MoE model (many layers, high expert count) with and without this normalization pass.

findings.claim: When use_transformer_engine_op_fuser is enabled, Fp8Padding/Fp8Unpadding are constructed with align_size=256 instead of the TE default. Larger alignment increases the number of padding tokens per expert, growing the padded token count fed into the GEMMs and the associated activation memory/compute each forward pass. For workloads with small per-expert token counts, rounding each expert up to a multiple of 256 can substantially inflate FLOPs and activation memory relative to the previous (smaller) default alignment.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step MoE forward time and peak activation memory with fused op-fuser path (align_size=256) vs non-fused path for a config with few tokens per expert.

findings.claim: _reset_metadata_and_sync_gather_all_model_params now expands grouped quantized params on every optimizer step via _expand_quantized_param_shard_for_cast, which for each grouped param calls split_into_quantized_tensors() and builds new Python lists / per-member slices before quantize_param_shard. This adds per-step Python-level work and potential tensor splits proportional to num_gemms for every grouped quantized parameter. For non-grouped fp8 params the early return keeps overhead minimal, but for grouped MoE params this is executed each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile optimizer.step() quantize_param_shard path for an fp8 MoE model using grouped quantized weights, measuring added CPU overhead per step from the expansion loop and split calls.

findings.claim: reset_cuda_graph() unconditionally calls gc.collect() at the end. A full Python garbage-collection pass stops all Python execution and traverses the entire object graph, which for large training processes (millions of live objects: parameters, buffers, autograd nodes) can take tens to hundreds of milliseconds. If reset_cuda_graph is invoked as part of the paged-stash 'rerun once' path (per the docs, a step reruns without capacity padding whenever stash overflow / token over-budget hits any rank), this gc.collect() will run on every rerun and introduce a large, hard-to-attribute host-side stall that also blocks the CPU from launching GPU work, hurting throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure step time with paged-stash reruns triggered vs. not, and isolate the cost of gc.collect() by timing reset_cuda_graph() around a large model.

findings.claim: When moe_paged_stash is enabled, every TEGroupedMLP forward now (1) computes tokens_per_expert.sum() — a GPU reduction producing a device scalar — and (2) wraps the fused GEMM in a paged-stash context that copies the permuted activations into paged CUDA buffers via a custom Triton stash kernel (and pops them back in backward). This adds two extra full-hidden-size activation copies (stash + pop) plus several small metadata/freelist kernel launches per MoE layer per microbatch. The activation copy traffic scales with (tokens x hidden_size) and is pure memory-bandwidth overhead on top of the GEMM, reducing MoE-layer throughput relative to the non-stash path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark MoE-layer forward+backward throughput and memory with moe_paged_stash on vs off at fixed capacity factor; measure the stash/pop Triton kernel time as a fraction of the grouped GEMM time.

findings.claim: The paged-stash design (per docs) reruns the entire forward-backward step once, without capacity padding and without paged stashing, whenever stash overflow OR token over-budget is detected on ANY rank. A full step rerun roughly doubles compute for that iteration. If capacity/stash-buffer factors are under-sized relative to real routing skew, overflow can trigger frequently, causing repeated ~2x-cost iterations and a large aggregate throughput regression. The rerun is a global all-rank event (any rank overflow forces all ranks to rerun), so a single skewed rank penalizes the whole job.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MoE training with an intentionally low moe_expert_rank_capacity_factor / stash buffer factor and measure the rerun rate and its impact on average step time vs a well-sized configuration.

findings.claim: The overflow / over-budget check described in the runner requires reading the shared overflow flag (a device tensor) back to the host after each pass to decide whether to rerun. Such a device-to-host read forces a CUDA stream synchronization once per forward-backward, which serializes the CPU with the GPU and can reduce pipeline overlap. This is inherent to the rerun decision and is incurred every step whenever a capacity factor is set (even without --moe-paged-stash).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile for cudaStreamSynchronize / d2h copies introduced per step when a capacity factor is set, and compare CPU-GPU overlap vs baseline.

findings.claim: PagedStashBuffer allocates several persistent auxiliary device tensors per buffer (free_list_cuda, _reset_free_list_cuda, plus host mirrors when spill enabled) in addition to the large paged activation buffer sized by moe_paged_stash_buffer_size_factor_cuda (default 1.10x the balanced case). This reserves activation memory up-front for the worst-case skew headroom; with several MoE layers/experts this fixed reservation grows the persistent memory footprint and can reduce room for other activations even when routing is balanced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak reserved GPU memory with moe_paged_stash enabled vs disabled across the buffer-size factor sweep.

findings.claim: In combined_forward_backward_step, the FSDP reshard-hook wiring loop (`for i in range(f_schedule_plan.num_layers()): layer_plan.set_fsdp_reshard_hooks(...)`) runs on every forward step of every microbatch. It (a) calls find_megatron_fsdp(f_model), which walks the entire wrapper chain, once per microbatch, and (b) creates two new closures/lambdas per layer per microbatch and re-invokes set_fsdp_reshard_hooks, which does an isinstance assertion and re-imports MultiTokenPredictionLayer/TransformerLayer inside the function body every call. For deep models (many layers) and many microbatches, this adds O(num_layers * num_microbatches) Python-level overhead and allocations per iteration on the critical scheduling path. The hooks are static per layer plan and could be wired once, not per microbatch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration CPU/schedule overhead of combined_1f1b_schedule_for_no_pipelining with FSDP optim_grads_params and a large num_layers x num_microbatches vs. wiring hooks once.

findings.claim: set_fsdp_reshard_hooks performs `from megatron.core.transformer.multi_token_prediction import MultiTokenPredictionLayer` and `from ...transformer_layer import TransformerLayer` on every call. Since this method is invoked per-layer per-microbatch (from combined_forward_backward_step), these imports (even though cached in sys.modules) add repeated dict lookups and function-call overhead on the training critical path. Hoist the imports to module scope.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Python overhead of repeated local imports vs module-level import in the per-layer hook wiring path.

findings.claim: TransformerLayerNode.backward_dw now iterates over all parameters of bwd_dw_callables (module.parameters()) to collect post_wgrad_grad_acc_hooks. Although guarded by `self.post_wgrad_grad_acc_hooks is None` so it only builds the list once per node instance, new node instances are created per microbatch (schedule plans are rebuilt each combined_forward_backward_step), so the full parameter walk over attention + shared-expert modules recurs every microbatch backward. For layers with many params this is extra per-microbatch Python iteration on the backward critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark backward_dw cost per microbatch with delay_wgrad_compute enabled and count of parameters iterated per node.

findings.claim: The grad_acc_hook lambda now evaluates `getattr(p, 'skip_backward_post_hook', False)` for every parameter's post-accumulate-grad hook on every backward. Previously deferred params were filtered out at registration time; now the check happens per gradient accumulation. This adds a small per-parameter getattr on the backward path, though the cost is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark grad accumulation hook dispatch overhead with vs without the per-param getattr guard.

findings.claim: The removal of `agv_kwargs = {"max_num_blocks": 16} if self.shared_experts is not None else {}` (and the `**agv_kwargs` passthrough) causes multimem_all_gatherv_3tensor to fall back to its default MAX_NUM_BLOCKS=128. When a shared expert is present, the AGV collective now launches up to 128 CTAs instead of being capped at 16. Per the original comment, this cap existed specifically so the AGV would not starve the shared-expert GEMMs running concurrently on a side stream. Removing it lets the AGV kernel consume most/all SMs, contending with and delaying the shared-expert GEMMs, degrading overlap and increasing MoE layer latency in the shared-expert configuration. This is a behavioral change not just a metadata rename, and it is unclear from the diff whether it is intentional.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVLS MoE inference decode step latency with a model that has shared_experts enabled (e.g. Nemotron MoE), comparing AGV CTA cap 16 vs default 128 while shared-expert GEMMs run on a side stream; measure per-layer MoE latency and end-to-end decode tokens/s.

findings.claim: The autotune config set for the causal conv1d varlen kernel was reduced from 4 configs (BLOCK_T in {128,256}, BLOCK_C in {64,128}) to 3 configs, replacing several with a single vLLM-style BLOCK_T=8/BLOCK_C=256 config and removing the BLOCK_C=64 and larger-BLOCK_T options. If actual production conv_dim values are small (over-parallelized by the vLLM regime) or if launch overhead dominates, the removed BLOCK_C=64 / larger-tile configs could have been optimal; pruning them from the autotune search means those workloads can no longer select their best tiling and may run slower. The impact depends on conv_dim and token counts seen at runtime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Autotune-select over both the old (4-config) and new (3-config) sets for a sweep of conv_dim (small to large) and total_tokens; compare selected kernel wall-clock time per config set.

findings.claim: The new `cuda_graph_all_prefills=True` path sets `cuda_graph_max_tokens = self.max_tokens` for prefill/mixed CUDA graphs instead of `max_requests * (num_speculative_tokens + 1)`. Since `max_tokens` (default 16384) is typically far larger than `max_requests` (derived from KV block count, e.g. ~80 in the unit test, 128 elsewhere), this dramatically widens the prefill/mixed graph token-count range. The test cases confirm this: non-decode token counts jump to e.g. [1024, 512, ...] versus [80, ...] for decode-only. Each additional/larger captured graph pool consumes GPU memory for its own static input/output/activation buffers, so enabling this flag substantially increases CUDA graph memory footprint and capture time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory and startup/capture time with cuda_graph_all_prefills False vs True at max_tokens=16384, max_requests=128.

findings.claim: Enabling `--inference-cuda-graph-all-prefills` (which extends mixed/prefill CUDA graph capture to the full `max_tokens` budget) can OOM during graph capture. The commit's own added test comment states 'capturing mixed graphs across the full range will OOM' and the test was forced to use `use_cuda_graphs_for_non_decode_steps=False` to avoid it. Capturing mixed-prefill graphs at token counts up to max_tokens (16384) allocates large per-graph activation memory, risking out-of-memory on realistic configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run dynamic inference with --inference-cuda-graph-all-prefills on a large model and observe peak memory during CUDA graph capture.

findings.claim: The added `cuda_graph_step_size = max(cuda_graph_step_size, tp_size)` floor prevents a zero step size when max_tokens < num_cuda_graphs, which is a correctness fix. However, when the step is floored up to tp_size while num_cuda_graphs is large, `range(step, max_tokens, step)` produces fewer graphs than requested near the small-token regime; this is benign for performance but means num_cuda_graphs may be under-honored (fewer graphs captured), slightly reducing coverage/graph-hit rate for those tiny configs.

This change is a micro-optimization that avoids an unnecessary autograd-function call and .contiguous() when TP size == 1. Previously, when reduce_scatter_embeddings was False and TP==1, the code went through reduce_from_tensor_model_parallel_region -> _ReduceFromModelParallelRegion.apply -> _reduce, which internally already short-circuits (group.size()==1 returns input_ unchanged). So the all-reduce was already skipped; the only overhead removed is the autograd.Function.apply dispatch overhead. Net effect: a tiny reduction in per-forward Python/autograd overhead when TP==1 (no functional change, no communication was being issued before). Positive/neutral for performance, no degradation introduced.

The commit adds `_training_ep_dispatcher` (true whenever EP>1 and transformer_impl == 'transformer_engine') to `force_disable_non_decode_cuda_graphs`. This forcibly sets `use_cuda_graphs_for_non_decode_steps=False` for the legacy A2A / training TE dispatcher path. As a result, non-decode (prefill / mixed) steps run in eager mode instead of under a captured CUDA graph, losing CUDA-graph launch-overhead reduction on those steps. For workloads with substantial prefill/mixed traffic under EP>1 + TE, this degrades throughput/latency versus the prior behavior where non-decode graphs could be enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill/mixed-batch inference latency with EP>1 and transformer_engine impl, comparing eager vs CUDA-graphed non-decode steps.

findings.claim: match_ep_token_counts is now enabled for the training A2A dispatcher (self._nccl_ep_dispatcher or self._training_ep_dispatcher), whereas before it was only enabled for the NCCL dispatcher. This re-enables the per-step EP all-reduce-max token-count sync (either a NCCL AllReduce on the compute stream with H2D/D2H, or a ZMQ CPU round-trip) for the A2A dispatcher path. Each decode step now incurs an extra cross-EP-rank synchronization it previously skipped, adding per-step latency and a synchronization barrier across all EP ranks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step decode latency under EP>1 with the legacy A2A/TE dispatcher, comparing with and without the EP token-count all-reduce sync.

findings.claim: Removing the request-count sync and `smallest_non_decode_cuda_graph_size` elevation from adjust_batch_dims_for_expert_parallelism simplifies EP sync to only token_count + is_non_decode. Now that force_disable_non_decode_cuda_graphs also covers the A2A path, is_any_ep_rank_in_non_decode always returns None (eager) for those dispatchers; but for the NVLS path (non-decode graphs allowed, match_ep_token_counts=False) the removed token-count elevation could cause EP ranks to select different-sized decode graphs. This is primarily a correctness concern but any resulting fallback to eager execution on some ranks would reduce GPU utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: EP>1 NVLS dispatcher mixed decode/prefill workload measuring rate of eager-mode fallback and GPU utilization.

findings.claim: With `disable_ep_consensus=True`, when there is no local work (`local_pending == 0`) the control loop calls `controller.dummy_forward()` on every iteration and then only `await asyncio.sleep(0)`. This turns an idle engine into an unbounded busy-loop that continuously launches a full GPU forward (dummy_forward) plus a blocking `step_end_event.synchronize()` per iteration. The prior consensus path yielded/blocked on an all-reduce (throttling idle spin). The new idle path will peg one CPU core at 100% and keep the GPU busy running dummy forwards even when no requests are pending, consuming GPU cycles/power and hurting any co-located work. The unit test even asserts `idle_calls > 0` within 0.2s, confirming the intended busy spin.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Start engine with disable_ep_consensus=True and no requests; measure dummy_forward() calls/sec, CPU utilization of the loop thread, and GPU SM utilization/power over a 10s idle window vs. the consensus path.

findings.claim: The consensus all-reduce is now only re-run every 20 iterations (`self._ep_consensus_loop_counter % 20 == 0`) once global work is non-zero, caching `_last_ep_consensus`. As the comment states, this can delay pausing by up to 20 steps (~200-400 ms). This is an intentional latency/throughput tradeoff, but it adds up to 20 steps of latency to PAUSE responsiveness whenever there is active work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure time from PAUSE signal delivery to EngineState.PAUSED under continuous decode load, comparing per-iteration consensus vs. the 20-step cached consensus.

findings.claim: When `global_work_from_last_consensus == 0` (idle), consensus all-reduce is run every single iteration (the `or` short-circuits before the modulo check). Combined with a tight idle loop this means an EP all-reduce on every idle iteration, adding NCCL collective overhead per idle step rather than throttling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Idle EP>1 engine; count _ep_establish_consensus all-reduces per second and measure collective overhead during idle.

findings.claim: The TP broadcast path replaced the separate length-publish socket with a single `send_multipart` that always sends a header frame ([Headers.TP_BROADCAST]) even when there are zero messages. Previously the code skipped the multipart send entirely when `messages_to_dequeue == 0`. Now every drain iteration sends and every subscriber receives at least a 1-frame message, adding a small unconditional per-iteration ZMQ round-trip on TP>1 setups. This is a minor overhead but is now unconditional.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP>1 setup with an idle request stream; measure ZMQ messages/sec and per-step drain latency on subscriber ranks before/after.

findings.claim: The new shared-embedding bucketing places each shared (tied) embedding alone in shard 0 of its own bucket and pads shards 1..dp_size-1 with same-numel empty slots. As the code's own NOTE admits, this costs (dp_size-1)*pad_to_divisor(numel, shard_divisor) elements per shared embedding. For a large vocab×hidden embedding (e.g. 128k×8192) at dp_size=8 this is ~7×(vocab*hidden) extra elements — many GB per shared embedding, allocated in BOTH the param buffer and the grad buffer. This is a substantial memory-footprint regression versus the prior ping-pong sharding scheme which did not force per-embedding isolated padded buckets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure param+grad buffer total_buffer_numel (logged as 'total_padding' / overhead%) for a model with tied embeddings at dp_size=8 before vs after; compare peak GPU memory.

findings.claim: Because shared embeddings and any unique-large seed params consume a full padded shard while other shards are empty padding, the reduce-scatter / all-gather collectives over these buckets move (dp_size-1)x more bytes than the real data. This inflates the DDP grad reduce-scatter and param all-gather traffic for embedding-heavy models, reducing effective communication bandwidth and GPU utilization during the overlapped param/grad sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-iteration all-gather/reduce-scatter time (NCCL) for the embedding buckets before vs after at dp_size>=8.

findings.claim: _next_unassigned() rescans the pool from overall_cursor and _next_with_size() rescans size groups; in the worst greedy-packing case (many small params packed into a large seed slot) the inner while-loop repeatedly calls _next_unassigned() which advances overall_cursor but the assigned-id set membership tests make layout computation roughly O(P log?) to O(P^2) in pathological orderings. This is one-time setup cost at optimizer construction, not per-step, so impact on steady-state throughput is negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time compute_full_param_layout for a model with tens of thousands of params.

findings.claim: A new INFO log_single_rank is emitted inside _ParamAndGradBucketGroup construction stating whether reduce-scatter or all-reduce is used. This runs once per bucket group at setup; log_single_rank guards to rank 0 so overhead is trivial and not a steady-state concern.

The change passes model_chunks unconditionally to LayerWiseDistributedOptimizer (previously only when config.overlap_param_gather was set). This enables the buffer-based param-sync path (use_buffer_param_sync) whenever a full_param_layout exists, which switches from the variable-size all_gather (allgather_params) to the distributed-optimizer buffer all-gather. This is intended as an optimization but forces the padded shard-aligned layout (with its padding overhead) to always be used when layouts are available; verify it does not regress memory relative to the legacy ping-pong path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak memory and param-sync latency with vs without layout-driven path.

findings.claim: sample_kernel allocates a fresh `torch.empty(n, ...)` output tensor plus intermediate tensors (`scaled`, `probs`, `top_k_safe`, `top_p_safe`) on every decode step. `scaled` and `probs` are each `[n, vocab_size]` float tensors — a full extra materialization of the logits at fp32. For large vocabularies this is a non-trivial per-step allocation/dealloc churn on the decode critical path and defeats the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase (pre-allocated static `_sampled_tokens_cuda` buffers for CUDA-graph stability). Under CUDA-graph replay these dynamic allocations can also force re-capture or break graphability.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput (tokens/s) with flashinfer sampling backend vs torch backend at max batch, and per-step CUDA memory allocation profiling.

findings.claim: The FlashInfer path computes `torch.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)` over the full [n, vocab_size] logits before calling `top_k_top_p_sampling_from_probs`. FlashInfer provides fused `top_k_top_p_sampling_from_logits` variants that avoid materializing a full-vocab softmax probability tensor; explicitly dividing by temperature and softmaxing in eager PyTorch adds two full-vocab passes (divide + softmax) per step that the fused kernel could subsume, increasing memory bandwidth and latency on the decode critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step sampling latency for flashinfer backend with and without the explicit softmax, at varying vocab sizes and batch sizes.
