findings.claim: build_active_slices now runs `torch.cumsum(...)` + `sub_(1)` into `active_request_last_token_idxs` every step. If `request_query_lengths` and the destination staging buffer are CPU-pinned tensors (the surrounding staging buffers are pinned CPU), this is a per-step CPU-side elementwise op over max_requests; small, but adds unconditional per-step host work even when the flashinfer path is not selected (torch backend). Consider guarding these new ops behind `sampling_backend == 'flashinfer'`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step host-side overhead (CPU time in build_active_slices) with torch backend before vs after this change.

findings.claim: transfer_bookkeeping_to_gpu now unconditionally copies temperature/top_k/top_p staging slices (3 extra CPU-to-CPU slice assignments plus the enlarged H2D buffer of ~15KB vs ~7.5KB) every step regardless of sampling backend. When backend is 'torch' these sampling params are never read on GPU, so the extra staging copies and doubled request-staging memcpy size are pure per-step overhead for the default configuration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step H2D transfer size and time (torch backend) before vs after, at max_requests.

findings.claim: sample_speculative builds `token_to_request_index` each step via `torch.arange(...).repeat_interleave(...)` + `torch.cat(...)` on the GPU. These small allocations and kernel launches occur on the speculative-decode critical path every step; they could be precomputed/cached into a persistent buffer for CUDA-graph stability and to avoid per-step launch overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Speculative decode step latency with flashinfer backend, profiling kernel-launch count per step.

findings.claim: StaticInferenceEngine sets the process-wide InferenceMode._is_active=True in its constructor and never clears it (there is no suspend/resume/unset path in this file). Because InferenceMode is a class-level global read by all models/modules, once a static inference engine is constructed the flag stays True for the rest of the process. In colocated RL / interleaved training+inference (explicitly a supported scenario, see the 'colocated RL' assertion in experts.py), any subsequent training forward will now believe it is in inference mode. This flips MoELayer.forward to select self._inference_token_dispatcher and recompute shared_expert_overlap, sends the inference expert path (apply_module(self.experts)(..., routing_map=...)) during training, makes InferenceTopKRouter.forward take the inference _forward path during training, and makes MultiTokenPredictionLayer run an extra inference_all_gather during training — all of which degrade or break training throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate a StaticInferenceEngine, then run a training step on an inference_optimized MoE model and measure MoE dispatch/overlap throughput vs. a run without the engine constructed.

findings.category: semantics change: eval() no longer takes fast/inference path
findings.claim: TransformerBlock._should_call_local_cudagraph previously gated on `not self.training` (i.e. eval mode) but now gates on InferenceMode.is_active(). During validation / generation loops that put the model in eval() but do not go through an inference engine (so the flag is never set), the local cudagraph replay path is now skipped, forcing the slower eager path and losing CUDA-graph capture/replay speedups. The same change to HybridModel._should_call_local_cudagraph, MambaLayer, and HybridStack has the same effect.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run eval()/validation forward on a model with cudagraph_manager configured (without going through the inference engine) and compare per-step latency before vs after the commit.

findings.claim: Dispatcher selection (previously done once per train()/eval() transition) now runs on every MoELayer.forward: an attribute-exists check, InferenceMode.is_active() call, and dispatcher reassignment. The overhead is tiny per layer, but it executes on every layer every step; more importantly it reassigns self.token_dispatcher and self.shared_expert_overlap each forward, which is redundant work compared to the previous one-shot swap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MoELayer.forward host-side overhead per step across many layers with/without the per-forward dispatcher selection.

findings.claim: The top-level `from megatron.core.transformer.moe.moe_layer import MoELayer` in utils.py was moved inside _init_moe_expert_cache to break a new circular import (moe_layer.py now imports InferenceMode from inference.utils). This is functionally fine but the deferred import executes on each _init_moe_expert_cache invocation; Python caches the module so the cost after first call is negligible.

The default symmetric-memory buffer size (`_default_size_mb`) is doubled from 256 MB to 512 MB. This value is used for any `get_buffer` call that does not pass an explicit `size_mb` — in particular the `"tp"` key allocated in inference_layers.py (InferenceLayerNormColumnParallelLinear / InferenceColumnParallelLinear / InferenceRowParallelLinear all call `get_buffer("tp", ...)` with no size_mb). As a result the TP-path NVLS symmetric buffer now reserves 512 MB of device-visible symmetric memory per rank instead of 256 MB, doubling that fixed allocation regardless of whether the extra capacity is ever needed. On memory-constrained inference deployments this reduces the memory available for KV cache / activations and can push large models into OOM. Note the MoE NVLS buffers are now precisely self-sized, so this default only affects the TP path, which is exactly the caller that was left relying on the default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak CUDA memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated + symmetric-memory reservation) for TP-only inference with inference_optimized transformer impl before/after the commit; confirm ~256 MB/rank increase attributable to the tp symmetric buffer.

findings.claim: The `failed` list comprehension now calls `SymmetricMemoryManager.get_buffer(name)` again for each of the 5 buffer keys to read `init_failure_reason`. These are dictionary lookups on already-created buffers (no reallocation), and this runs once at model init, so the cost is negligible. Flagging only because it introduces redundant lookups where the already-bound `buf`/handle could have carried the reason; no runtime hot-path impact.

The default value of moe_hybridep_num_sms was changed from a fixed 16 to None (transformer_config.py). Previously HybridEP would run with 16 SMs (documented as generally achieving good NVL bandwidth). With None, the code no longer passes num_sms_dispatch_api/num_sms_combine_api into HybridEPBuffer (init_hybrid_ep_buffer only adds the kwarg when the value is not None), so the DeepEP library-internal default is used instead. If the DeepEP default differs from 16 (e.g. more SMs consumed for comm, leaving fewer for compute, or fewer SMs yielding lower A2A bandwidth), this silently changes runtime SM allocation and can degrade throughput/GPU utilization for existing HybridEP users who relied on the 16-SM tuning.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a HybridEP MoE layer forward/backward micro-benchmark with the old default (num_sms=16) vs the new default (None -> DeepEP internal default) and compare dispatch/combine A2A bandwidth and end-to-end MoE layer throughput.

findings.claim: In HybridEPDispatch.forward, when fused or num_blocks_permute/num_blocks_unpermute is set, the code calls inspect.signature(HybridEPBuffer.dispatch_with_permute) on every forward invocation to check for the 'fuse_permute_dispatch' parameter. inspect.signature is relatively expensive (introspection + Signature object construction) and is executed per MoE-layer dispatch call on the hot path rather than cached once. For many layers/microbatches this adds measurable Python CPU overhead that can expose the launch-bound MoE path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated calls to HybridEPDispatch.forward with fused=True and measure the per-call Python overhead of inspect.signature vs a cached capability check.

findings.claim: When fused=True is requested but the installed DeepEP version lacks 'fuse_permute_dispatch', the code silently falls back to unfused dispatch (warns once via warnings.warn). Users enabling moe_permute_fusion_into_hybridep expecting the fused (faster) path will instead run the slower unfused path with no error; combined with the per-call inspect.signature cost this is a silent perf regression relative to the user's intent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare MoE dispatch/combine latency with fused permute enabled on a DeepEP build that supports it vs one that does not (fallback path).

findings.claim: The optimizer/grad-buffer restore (model[0].restore_grad_buffers() + optimizer.restore_from_cpu()) is moved from the end of get_environment_rollouts (right after rollouts are collected) into get_grpo_data_iterator, which per the commit title runs after logprobs computation. The intent is to keep the optimizer on CPU during the logprobs forward pass to reduce GPU memory. However, restoring only after logprobs computation places the CPU->GPU optimizer-state and grad-buffer transfers strictly serially before the first training step, with no work left to overlap them. If restore_from_cpu()/restore_grad_buffers() are synchronous (blocking H2D copies), this adds their full latency to the critical path each RL iteration rather than hiding it behind the (potentially long) logprobs/inference forward pass. Verify that these transfers are async and that no useful compute follows to overlap; otherwise this trades a memory saving for added per-iteration wall-clock latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-RL-iteration time for the 'rl/restore-optimizer-after-inference' nvtx range (grad-buffers + optimizer-state) and compare total iteration wall-clock with rl_offload_optimizer_during_inference on vs off, to quantify how much of the restore is on the critical path vs overlapped.

findings.claim: The restore block is now unconditionally inside the get_grpo_data_iterator branch that builds fresh rollouts. If get_grpo_data_iterator can be entered without a preceding offload (e.g. buffered_rollouts path or a correction step), restore_from_cpu()/restore_grad_buffers() may be called when the optimizer was never offloaded, or conversely the restore may be skipped on a code path that did offload, leaving the optimizer on CPU and forcing a fallback restore during the optimizer step. Confirm the offload in get_environment_rollouts and this restore are strictly paired across all call paths so no extra or missing transfer occurs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trace offload/restore pairing across buffered vs fresh-rollout and correction code paths; assert restore is called exactly once per offload.

findings.claim: In the FA4 decode path (the `else` branch used for the non-varlen/decode case), the call to flash_attn4_varlen_func hard-codes num_splits=1. The pre-existing FA2/FA3 path used num_splits=0 (i.e. `0 if not self.batch_invariant_mode else 1`), which lets the kernel auto-tune the split-K / flash-decoding parallelism. Forcing num_splits=1 disables split-K parallelization of the KV dimension, which for decode (seqlen_q small, large KV cache) leaves most SMs idle and can substantially reduce GPU utilization / increase per-token latency for long-context decode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Dynamic-batching decode latency with a long KV cache (e.g. 8-32k context, batch 1-8) comparing num_splits=1 vs auto (num_splits=0), measuring per-token decode latency and SM occupancy.

findings.claim: The FA4 decode path adds two extra tensor reshape operations per attention call: q.reshape(-1, H, D) before the kernel and output_total.reshape(num_requests, tokens_per_request, ...) after. If q or the output is not contiguous in the required layout, .reshape() silently triggers a full device-to-device copy of the query/output activations on every layer, every decode step. Combined with the subsequent reshape to (B*S,1,H,D) this adds redundant memory traffic in the hot decode loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile decode step memory traffic / kernel count per attention layer with FA4 enabled vs FA3 to detect extra copy kernels from reshape.

findings.claim: The FA4 decode branch recomputes softmax_scale (checking getattr(self,'softmax_scale',...) and q.shape[-1]**-0.5) inside the branch even though softmax_scale was already computed earlier in the same function for the prefill/varlen path. This is minor per-call redundant Python-side work but is in the per-token decode hot path.

When CUDA graphs are enabled (`cuda_graph_impl == 'local'`), a new persistent GPU buffer `_all_logits_cuda` of shape `(1, max_logits, vocab_size)` is allocated for the whole controller lifetime. With `materialize_only_last_token_logits`, `max_logits = max_requests * (num_speculative_tokens + 1)`; otherwise it is `max_tokens`. In the non-materialize path this is `max_tokens * vocab_size` floats/half — e.g. 16384 tokens * a 128K vocab * 2 bytes ≈ 4 GB of persistent GPU memory reserved up front, plus the per-step `.copy_()` into it. This is a large new steady-state GPU memory footprint (and an extra full-size logits copy each step) that did not exist before, where logits were used directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory and per-step latency with cuda_graph_impl='local' at large max_tokens/vocab, comparing before/after this commit, with and without materialize_only_last_token_logits.

findings.claim: `_dynamic_step_forward_logits` now unconditionally copies the model logits into the pre-allocated `_all_logits_cuda` buffer on every forward step when CUDA graphs are enabled (`self._all_logits_cuda[:, :logits_seq_len, :].copy_(logits[...])`). Previously the logits tensor was returned directly and used in place. This adds an extra device-to-device copy of a `[1, seq_len, vocab_size]` tensor every decode step, which for large vocab sizes is a non-trivial bandwidth cost on the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step decode latency (nsys) before/after this commit with cuda_graph_impl='local', measuring the added copy kernel time relative to sampling.

findings.claim: `build_active_slices` is newly invoked on every forward step (from `initialize_attention_state`/update near line 1727) and copies every request-metadata tensor's active slice into a new `active_request_metadata` buffer via per-label `.copy_(..., non_blocking=True)`. This replaces the previous per-step pinned-CPU D2H copies with per-step GPU-to-GPU copies for all 7 metadata fields. It is an added set of small copies per step; while individually cheap, on the decode critical path with many small tensors this launches extra kernels each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile decode step launch overhead / kernel count before vs after for a batch with many active requests.

findings.claim: `active_request_metadata` is a newly allocated full-size mirror (`torch.empty_like`) of every request_metadata tensor, doubling the persistent memory for request metadata. These tensors are small (max_requests-sized), so the memory impact is minor, but it is new steady-state allocation.

Metadata `on_device` flag was removed; previously fields like temperature/top_k/top_p/return_log_probs were kept on CPU (pinned) for torch sampling, and only termination_id lived on GPU. Now all metadata is materialized on GPU and later `.tolist()`'d in `_dynamic_step_sample_bookkeeping` from `active_request_metadata[...][:active_request_count].tolist()`. Reading GPU tensors via `.tolist()` triggers an implicit device-to-host sync each step, which the previous pinned-CPU-buffer design was specifically structured to avoid/pipeline. This could add a synchronization stall per decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile whether `.tolist()` on active_request_metadata causes a blocking cudaMemcpy/sync each decode step vs the prior pinned-buffer path.

findings.claim: In `_get_decode_A_neg_exp` (mamba_mixer.py), the inference path recomputes `-torch.exp(self.A_log.float())` and does an in-place `copy_` into `self._A_neg_exp_cache` whenever `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` is True. This means the three small elementwise kernels the cache was meant to eliminate are re-run on *every* CUDA-graph capture. More importantly, the copy is captured into the graph, so on every graph replay the recompute is baked in and executes each decode step anyway. The stated optimization (avoid per-token recompute) is largely defeated for the graphed decode path, which is exactly the hot inference path this commit targets. Only the non-captured, non-stale path actually skips the recompute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Mamba decode with CUDA graphs enabled: count elementwise kernel launches (float cast/exp/neg + copy) per replayed decode step before and after this commit.

findings.claim: The cache is only refilled when `self._A_neg_exp_cache_stale` is True or during graph capture. `_A_neg_exp_cache_stale` is set True only in `train()`. If A_log weights are updated in-place during inference (e.g. RL weight refresh that writes into the parameter tensor without calling `.train()`), the cache will not be invalidated and decode will use stale A values, and no recompute happens — but this is a correctness risk rather than a slowdown. It also means the very first inference call (module constructed and never `.train(False)`-toggled after a weight load) may read an uninitialized `torch.empty_like` buffer if stale flag were ever reset incorrectly.

`_batch_indices_decode_buffer` changed from int32 to int64 (mamba_metadata.py), doubling the size of this per-context buffer from 4B to 8B per request. The claimed benefit is avoiding a per-layer upcast kernel inside selective_state_update (the `.to(torch.int64)` in mamba_mixer.py was removed). The removal of the per-layer upcast is a genuine win (one cast per layer per step eliminated); the extra memory is negligible (max_requests * 4 bytes). Net effect is likely positive, no real concern.

`torch.topk(scores, k=topk, dim=1, sorted=torch.is_grad_enabled())` now returns unsorted top-k during inference (grad disabled). Turning off sorting can speed up top-k slightly, but if any downstream consumer of these routing probs/indices assumes descending order (e.g. tie-breaking, first-expert selection, or index-order-dependent unpermute), inference routing results could differ from training. This is the fallback (non-group-limited) branch. If downstream is order-agnostic (scatter into routing_map), there is no correctness issue and the speedup is real but small.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark torch.topk with sorted=True vs sorted=False on router score shapes; verify inference routing_map/probs identical up to permutation.

findings.claim: `selective_state_update` now calls `torch.cuda.get_device_capability(x.device)` on every invocation. This function is on the per-token SSM/Mamba decode critical path (called once per decode step per layer). The result (whether the GPU is Blackwell) is constant for the lifetime of the process, so recomputing it per call adds a small but repeated host-side CPU cost (a device-property query + Python branching) to the launch path. It should be computed once and cached (e.g., module-level lazy cache keyed by device) rather than on each call. Impact is small because get_device_capability is internally cached by PyTorch, but the extra Python work still runs every decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark selective_state_update launch latency (CPU wall time, kernel excluded) before/after, e.g. 100k calls with small dstate to isolate host overhead.

findings.claim: The response now includes full `prompt_token_ids`, `generation_token_ids`, and `generation_log_probs` lists for every choice, plus an O(events) scan `sum(1 for e in result['events'] if e.get('type') == 'EVICT')`. For long prompts/generations this materially increases the size of the JSON payload that must be serialized and transmitted per request, and adds a linear scan over the events list. This raises per-request server CPU/serialization cost and network payload size relative to the previous minimal response (which only returned index/text/logprobs).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure server response latency and payload size per completion request with long generations (e.g. 2k tokens) before vs after, and with large event lists.

findings.claim: Switching `torch.zeros` to `torch.empty` removes the memset and is a small performance improvement, but padding rows (where the kernel early-returns on permutation_map < 0) are now left uninitialized (garbage) instead of zero. This is only a performance win if downstream consumers truly never read padding rows; if any downstream op reads or reduces over these rows it could change numerics. No runtime performance regression here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: N/A (correctness check): verify all consumers of padded_squared_relu output mask out permutation_map<0 rows.

findings.claim: The new ROCm branch in storeCacheElems adds an extra materialization pass: it first converts every element to the model dtype (rounded_t) in one loop, then converts rounded_t to the FP8 cache dtype in a second loop, instead of the single-pass float->fp8 conversion used on CUDA. This doubles the per-element conversion work and adds a stack-resident intermediate array (rounded[kElemsPerLane]) in the FP8-cache write path on ROCm. It is intentional (matches ROCm's model-dtype materialization for numerical parity), but it is measurably more instructions/register pressure per KV-cache store on the hot FP8 serving path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark fused_minimax_m3_qknorm_rope_kv_insert with fp8 kv_cache on ROCm, comparing the two-pass vs single-pass conversion throughput for the KV insert.

findings.claim: _ensure_aiter_sparse_pa_kv_cache runs a per-forward guard that compares self.kv_cache.data_ptr() against a cached pointer and, on mismatch, re-runs unbind(1) plus contiguity checks. As long as the KV cache tensor is stable across steps this is a cheap early-return, but the contiguity validation (is_contiguous on the unbound key/value views) executes whenever the cache pointer first changes; ensure this is not re-triggered every forward (e.g. if kv_cache is re-viewed/reallocated), which would add per-layer host overhead in the decode loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-layer host time of the AITER sparse PA path across many decode steps to confirm the pointer-guard early-returns and unbind is not re-run each step.

findings.claim: The pad-aware kernel launch caps the row grid dimension at min(num_tokens, 256), then processes rows via a strided persistent loop. For large token counts (M*num_topk can be tens of thousands with expert parallelism) and small/moderate hidden_size (hidden_size <= BLOCK_SIZE=1024 -> the column grid dim is 1), the kernel launches only ~256 CTAs total for a purely memory-bound elementwise activation. On GPUs with more SMs / when many rows are valid, this can leave the device under-occupied compared to the fused CUDA op `silu_and_mul_with_clamp`, which parallelizes across all rows. The persistent design serializes up to ceil(num_tokens/256) rows per CTA.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _swiglu_limit_pad_aware vs torch.ops._C.silu_and_mul_with_clamp on shapes like [num_tokens=65536, 2*hidden=2*4096] and [65536, 2*1024], all topk_ids valid, measuring kernel time; sweep num_tokens and hidden_size.

findings.claim: When expert_map is present but most/all topk_ids are locally routed (low EP sparsity), the pad-aware Triton kernel performs the same gate/up loads and silu*up compute as the fused CUDA op but with an extra per-row topk_ids load plus an expert_map gather, and (per the grid analysis) potentially fewer CTAs. The benefit (skipping non-local/padded slots) only materializes when a significant fraction of slots are non-local; otherwise it is pure overhead versus the previous fused CUDA op path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end MoE activation latency with expert_map where 0%, 50%, 90% of slots are non-local, against the fused silu_and_mul_with_clamp CUDA op.

findings.claim: BLOCK_SIZE is hardcoded to 1024 with num_warps=4 regardless of hidden_size. When hidden_size < 1024, each CTA masks off (1024-hidden_size) lanes, wasting register/compute; when hidden_size is much larger, a single 1024-wide column tile per CTA may not maximize memory throughput. No autotuning is applied, unlike other kernels in this file.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep BLOCK_SIZE in {256,512,1024,2048} and num_warps in {2,4,8} for representative hidden sizes and measure kernel bandwidth.

findings.claim: Non-creator processes (all TP workers except the one that created the file) busy-wait in `_wait_for_file_size` with `time.sleep(0.005)` polling `os.fstat` until the mmap file reaches the expected size. This is a startup-only cost, but the 5 ms polling granularity plus the 30 s timeout adds serialized latency to worker initialization proportional to how long the creator takes to `ftruncate`. It is a coarse busy-poll rather than an event-based wait; on many workers it wastes CPU and can add measurable init delay.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure multi-TP-worker init time with the EC CPU connector enabled vs disabled; instrument time spent in _wait_for_file_size.

findings.claim: The creator calls `madvise(MADV_POPULATE_WRITE, 0, total_size_bytes)` over the entire region, forcing all pages of the ec_cpu_bytes region to be prefaulted (physically committed) at startup rather than lazily. For large `ec_cpu_bytes` this eagerly consumes host RAM up front and adds a one-time page-fault/zeroing cost to init even if only a fraction of blocks are ever used.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare RSS and init time with large ec_cpu_bytes (e.g. tens of GB) with and without MADV_POPULATE_WRITE.

findings.claim: `get_finished` is invoked every engine step by the worker finalize path (`_get_ec_connector_output`), and it unconditionally calls `self.connector_worker.flush_saves()`. If flush_saves synchronizes on a CUDA stream/event to guarantee GPU->CPU DMA completion, this can serialize the worker's per-step critical path (blocking the next forward on outstanding save DMAs). Even when there is nothing to flush this adds an unconditional call each step. Worker copy implementation was not visible in the diff, so this is speculative.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode-step latency with ec_producer enabled and many multimodal saves in flight; measure time spent in flush_saves per step.

findings.claim: Every EmbeddingCache access (get/alloc/pin/unpin/mark_ready) acquires a `threading.Lock`. These are called multiple times per scheduler step per multimodal feature. In the normal single-threaded scheduler this lock is uncontended and cheap, but it adds fixed per-call overhead on the scheduling hot path proportional to the number of mm_features scheduled per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile scheduler step time with high multimodal request concurrency and many mm_features per step.

findings.claim: Quant allreduce+RMS fusion now defaults (auto) to the mnnvl backend, for which _select_flashinfer_allreduce_use_oneshot() returns None (delegating strategy choice to FlashInfer's AUTO). Previously quant patterns always ran on trtllm, where vLLM applied its own per-world-size one-shot size thresholds (_FI_ALLREDUCE_ONE_SHOT_MAX_SIZES_MB). For token/tensor sizes where vLLM would have forced two-shot but FlashInfer AUTO picks one-shot (or vice-versa), the achieved allreduce latency for quant fusion could change and, on some topologies/sizes, regress relative to the previously tuned trtllm behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fused allreduce+RMS+NVFP4/FP8 quant latency across token counts (1, 16, 64, 256, 1024) on a single-node NVSwitch box, comparing VLLM_FLASHINFER_ALLREDUCE_BACKEND=trtllm vs mnnvl (auto).

findings.claim: In get_fi_ar_quant_workspace, when auto selects mnnvl and mnnvl workspace creation fails on a single-node topology without NVSwitch multicast, a second _create_workspace call is issued for trtllm. This means one failed mnnvl workspace-creation attempt (which does a collective handshake/allocation before failing) is paid at startup before falling back. This is a one-time initialization cost, not a steady-state runtime regression, but it adds startup latency on non-NVSwitch single-node setups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup time with auto backend on a single-node non-NVSwitch (PCIe/NVLink-bridge) topology, before vs after this change.

findings.claim: On s390x, cpu_core.numa_node holds a synthetic book/socket ID (from `lscpu -e=...,BOOK`), not a real OS memory node. In CPUWorker.__init__, when this synthetic ID is not in allowed_memory_nodes (which it will not be for book/socket IDs > 0), the code falls back to allowed_memory_nodes[0] for memory binding (init_cpu_memory_env). Under multi-worker TP/PP/DP runs, every worker on every book/socket therefore binds its allocations to the SAME first memory node. This destroys NUMA locality: workers running on other books access memory remotely (higher latency, lower bandwidth) and all KV cache / activation memory is concentrated on node 0, creating memory-bandwidth contention and imbalance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-socket/multi-book s390x run with TP>=2: compare per-worker memory-access latency / decode throughput when memory is bound to node0 for all workers vs. correctly co-located per-node binding.

findings.claim: determine_available_memory() (unchanged by this commit) still calls get_memory_node_info(cpu_core.numa_node) using the s390x synthetic book/socket ID. For book IDs that do not correspond to a /sys/devices/system/node/nodeN/meminfo path, get_memory_node_info falls back to system-wide psutil totals rather than per-node figures. Combined with the __init__ fallback to allowed_memory_nodes[0], the memory accounting used to size the KV cache can be inconsistent with where memory is actually bound, potentially oversizing KV cache relative to the effective node and causing later allocation pressure/thrashing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On multi-book s390x, verify KV cache sizing matches the memory node actually bound; watch for OOM/paging when node0 is oversubscribed.

findings.claim: In OMPProcessManager._get_autobind_cpu_ids, allowed_numa_nodes is overridden on s390x to the set of book IDs derived from the CPU list, and rank N is bound to book N's CPUs. Because these domains are not memory nodes, thread binding per book is not co-located with memory (bound to node0 per the worker fallback), so autobind provides CPU affinity without matching memory affinity — reducing the benefit of thread pinning on NUMA-sensitive workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare throughput/latency on s390x with auto CPU binding across books vs. single-book pinning to isolate cross-domain memory-access cost.

findings.claim: The rewrite replaces two large `torch.cat(...).contiguous()` calls (a handful of kernels) with a Python loop that, per 64MB chunk, issues a `.clone()` plus two slice-assignment copies for BOTH the weight and scale tensors. For large MoE weights this multiplies the number of small copy/clone kernel launches (and per-iteration Python overhead) versus the old single-shot concat. This is a one-time weight-load-time cost (reorder_w1w3_to_w3w1 is only called from prepare_*_moe_layer paths, not the forward hot path), so end-to-end throughput/latency is unaffected; it only marginally lengthens model load time. This is the intended tradeoff for the stated peak-memory reduction (transient buffer capped at ~64MB instead of a full duplicate of the weight tensor), so the change is a net win on memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time reorder_w1w3_to_w3w1 (and torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) on a large MoE w13 tensor (e.g. e=256, 2n=1024, k packed) before/after this commit to confirm load-time delta is small and peak transient memory dropped from ~2x weight size to ~64MB.

findings.claim: The memory profiler now runs CUDA-graph memory estimation on ROCm/HIP by changing `current_platform.is_cuda()` to `current_platform.is_cuda_alike()`. The previous code path deliberately returned `cudagraph_memory_estimate = 0` on ROCm because 'graph pool handles and get_memory_info behave differently and can produce incorrect/negative estimates.' If the ROCm estimate is still unreliable in some configs (the commit's own justification relies on #44825 having made it reliable), an over-estimate reserves extra memory that is subtracted from `available_kv_cache_memory`, shrinking the KV cache. A smaller KV cache reduces max concurrent sequences / block table capacity, directly degrading throughput and effective batch size on ROCm. An under/negative estimate would inflate KV cache and risk runtime OOM later. This is a behavioral change to the memory budgeting path on all ROCm deployments, not just the test.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a ROCm GPU, compare determine_available_memory() output and resulting num_gpu_blocks / KV cache GiB before and after this change for a model with cudagraph_mode != NONE; measure serving throughput (req/s) at fixed gpu_memory_utilization.

findings.claim: `wait_for_rocm_memory_to_settle()` is inserted after each LLM teardown in the eagle correctness test loop, adding a busy/poll wait for VRAM to settle. This increases wall-clock time of the test (and any parametrization sweep multiplies it). It is test-only, but if the polling helper lacks a timeout or the memory never fully settles it could stall the CI job. Impact is confined to test runtime, not production performance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure test_spec_decode eagle correctness suite wall-clock time on ROCm before/after; confirm wait_for_rocm_memory_to_settle has a bounded timeout.

findings.claim: When any layer uses FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel, _flashinfer_autotune_skip_ops returns {"fp4_gemm"} which disables autotuning for the entire 'fp4_gemm' op. Per the code comment, ALL mm_fp4 backends (cutlass, trtllm, cudnn, b12x) share the 'fp4_gemm' op name. In a mixed model that has both CuTeDSL and CUTLASS/TRTLLM NVFP4 GEMM layers, this will also skip autotuning for the CUTLASS/TRTLLM/cuDNN GEMMs that DO benefit from tuning, forcing them onto the heuristic fallback tactic at inference time. flashinfer_autotune's own docstring warns heuristics 'may be significantly slower', so runtime GEMM throughput/latency for those non-CuTeDSL FP4 layers degrades.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-request decode latency / GEMM throughput for an NVFP4 model mixing CuTeDSL and CUTLASS FP4 linear layers, with vs without the fp4_gemm skip (VLLM_FLASHINFER_AUTOTUNE_SKIP_OPS='' vs default).

findings.claim: By default (env var unset) fp4_gemm autotuning is now skipped whenever the CuTeDSL NVFP4 kernel is selected, so those GEMMs run with the heuristic fallback tactic at inference time. The commit assumes the CuTeDSL fallback already equals the heuristic-optimal choice; if that assumption is incorrect on some shapes/GPUs, steady-state GEMM latency could regress relative to the previously-tuned behavior. This is a behavior change to the default path, not opt-in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark NVFP4 CuTeDSL linear-layer GEMM latency across batch/token sizes: autotuned (old behavior) vs heuristic fallback (new default), to confirm no steady-state regression.

findings.claim: _flashinfer_autotune_skip_ops iterates over runner.get_model().modules() and, for each module, does getattr chains for 'quant_method' and 'scheme'. This is a one-time O(num_modules) scan during warmup (not on the hot path), so cost is negligible; it returns early on the first CuTeDSL match. Minor and only during warmup.

In Qwen3NextDecoderLayer.forward, the pre-change code allocated a scratch tensor `self_attention_output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` for every layer on every forward pass and passed it as an out-parameter. The new code removes that allocation and instead has the attention sub-modules return a freshly-computed tensor (the out_proj output). This removes one `torch.empty_like` allocation per decoder layer per step. This is a small positive change (slightly fewer allocations), but note that the returned tensor from out_proj is a new allocation anyway, so the net memory footprint is roughly neutral rather than a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3-Next decode/prefill throughput and peak GPU memory before/after this commit at various batch sizes.

findings.claim: The old GDN paths wrote `output[:num_tokens], _ = self.out_proj(core_attn_out)`, implying the caller-provided `output` buffer could be larger than `num_tokens` (padded to a captured/graph batch size). The new code returns `out, _ = self.out_proj(core_attn_out)` directly, so the returned tensor's leading dim equals `core_attn_out`'s token count rather than the padded buffer size. If the surrounding CUDA-graph / padded-batch machinery relied on a fixed-size output buffer (e.g. `torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` sized to the padded batch), returning an unpadded tensor could change downstream tensor shapes and potentially force recompilation or extra copies. Confidence is limited because `num_tokens` and `hidden_states.size(0)` may already be equal in these code paths.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify no torch.compile/CUDA-graph recompilation is triggered across padded batch sizes for Qwen3-Next linear-attention layers after this change.

findings.claim: decode_indices() performs the D2H copy with `host.copy_(gpu, non_blocking=True)` followed by `torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize()`. This is called from the media-loading thread pool (global_thread_pool, VLLM_MEDIA_LOADING_THREAD_COUNT workers) which shares the process CUDA context with model execution. torch.cuda.current_stream() on that thread is the default stream; issuing a copy on and then fully synchronizing the default stream forces a host-side block that can serialize against / stall concurrent GPU model-forward work, and blocks the media thread for the full copy. With up to 8 concurrent media threads each doing this, GPU utilization for inference can be intermittently degraded. A dedicated non-default CUDA stream + event-based wait (not full-stream sync) would avoid contending with the compute stream.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Serve a VL model with deepstream backend under concurrent video requests and measure decode-stream synchronization impact on model forward latency / GPU SM occupancy vs. using a dedicated stream.

findings.claim: The DeepStream backend decodes the entire container stream to extract only the sampled frame indices ('The whole stream is decoded; the pool keeps exactly the frames whose decode-order index is in frame_indices ... and sends EOS once the last one is matched'). For sparse uniform sampling (e.g. 8-32 frames out of a long video) this decodes every frame up to the last requested index, unlike the PyAV backend which seeks to keyframes and decodes only GOP prefixes. For long videos with few sampled frames this is O(total_frames) decode work rather than O(sampled_frames), materially increasing per-request decode latency and NVDEC engine occupancy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode 8 uniformly-sampled frames from a 10-minute 1080p video with deepstream vs pyav/torchcodec and compare wall-clock decode time and NVDEC busy time.

findings.claim: The decode pool defaults to VLLM_MEDIA_LOADING_THREAD_COUNT (8) workers, but decode() is invoked synchronously from within the media-loading thread pool that also has 8 workers. Under load all 8 media threads may call the shared singleton DecodePool concurrently, contending for the fixed number of NVDEC decode workers; if pool worker count < media thread count, media threads block waiting on the pool with a 120s timeout, adding queueing latency. The 'first request's value wins' singleton semantics also means an early small pool_size caps throughput for the process lifetime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fire N>pool_size concurrent video decode requests and measure per-request queuing/latency distribution as pool_size varies.

findings.claim: torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize() is a full-stream host block executed on every decode_indices call. Even without cross-stream contention this serializes each media-loading thread on GPU completion of the D2H copy, preventing overlap of the copy with subsequent CPU-side post-processing; using a per-batch cuda event wait would reduce host stall.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark decode_indices D2H copy path with full-stream sync vs event-scoped wait for a batch of frames.

findings.claim: The batch-ordering sort key is now a Python lambda `lambda r: ((num := num_tokens_per_req[r]) != decode_query_len, num)` that runs per-request per-step on the hot execute path, instead of the previous `key=num_tokens_per_req.get` (a C-implemented dict method). The lambda builds a 2-tuple and does a dict lookup + comparison in interpreted Python for every request, so `sorted` invokes a Python callable O(n) times per forward step. For large batches (max_num_seqs of hundreds) this adds measurable per-step CPU overhead versus the C-level `dict.get` key. The correctness fix is necessary, but the key could be precomputed (e.g. build the list of (flag, num, req) tuples once) to avoid repeated interpreted-callable dispatch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark sort_batch_req_ids vs the old sorted(..., key=dict.get) for num_reqs in {64,256,1024}, measuring wall time per call; also profile prepare-inputs CPU time per step at high concurrency.

findings.claim: Changing `skip_topk=_skip_topk` to `skip_topk=_skip_topk and not is_mtp_layer` forces MTP layers to always run the (more expensive) topk index computation rather than skipping it. This is intentional for correctness of spec decode, but for MTP layers it means the topk/indexer path is always executed at draft step 0 rather than being skipped, which increases per-draft-step compute for those layers. No throughput regression for non-MTP layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare MTP draft-step latency for DeepSeekV2 with spec decode enabled before/after this change.

findings.claim: `_call_with_monitor` wraps `JITImpl.__call__`, so it runs on every TileLang kernel invocation during inference, including the common cache-hit case. On every call it invokes `_tilelang_cache_miss_key(self, args, kwargs)` which does non-trivial work per call: `_tilelang_call_kwargs` copies the kwargs dict (`dict(kwargs)`, `.pop`, `.update`) and then calls `func.parse_args(*args, **call_kwargs)` to recompute the cache key. `parse_args` is the same argument-parsing/key-derivation TileLang performs internally in the real `__call__`, so this effectively doubles the per-call key computation for every kernel dispatch on the hot path — a measurable per-token/per-layer overhead when the monitor is active, even though nothing is actually being compiled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With the monitor active (mode=warn), measure per-call overhead of a TileLang JITImpl kernel that always hits cache (steady-state inference), comparing wrapped vs. unwrapped __call__ over e.g. 100k invocations; watch for the extra parse_args + dict-copy cost.

findings.claim: In `_tilelang_cache_miss_key`, when `jit_impl.mode == 'auto'`, `_infer_jit_mode(*args, **call_kwargs)` is invoked and `func.set_mode(mode)` is called on every kernel dispatch, adding further per-call work beyond `parse_args`. The whole block is wrapped in a broad `try/except Exception` on the hot path; if any per-call exception is repeatedly raised and caught (e.g. an arg shape TileLang cannot bind), it silently degrades to returning None but still pays the raise/catch cost each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark a JITImpl with mode='auto' under the active monitor to measure added per-call cost of _infer_jit_mode + set_mode vs. baseline.

findings.claim: `_init_with_monitor` wraps `JITKernel.__init__` and runs per kernel construction; even on the fast path it does `_tilelang_arg` list-length/kwargs lookups and reads `_tilelang_jitimpl_compile_depth`. This is minor relative to the __call__ overhead but adds constant per-construction cost when the monitor is active.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark JITKernel construction with and without the wrapper installed.

findings.claim: This change is a performance optimization: previously `logitsprocs_need_output_token_ids` was forced True whenever `reasoning_config is not None` (i.e., any reasoning/thinking-budget-capable model), which unconditionally populated the per-request output-token-id rows in `_make_sampling_metadata` (line ~887-891, `cast(list[list[int]], self.req_output_token_ids)`) on every sampling step regardless of whether any request in the batch actually uses a thinking token budget. After the change, output token ids are only materialized dynamically when a budgeted request is present in the batch (via `thinking_budget_state_holder.has_tracked_requests()`). Thus this commit *reduces* per-step CPU overhead for reasoning-capable deployments with no active thinking-budget requests; it does not introduce a slowdown. The only risk is correctness in async scheduling if the holder does not request output_token_ids when needed, but that is a correctness concern, not a performance regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Steady-state decode throughput on a reasoning-capable model (e.g. with reasoning_config set) with a batch of requests that do NOT set thinking_token_budget; compare per-step sampling_metadata build time and tokens/s before vs after.

findings.claim: The new modular apply path (`TrtLlmBf16ExpertsModular.apply`) calls `flashinfer.fused_moe.trtllm_bf16_routed_moe(...)` without passing `tune_max_num_tokens`, whereas the monolithic BF16 path and every other TRTLLM expert (fp8/nvfp4) pass `tune_max_num_tokens=fi_moe_largest_bucket(self.moe_config)`. Relying on FlashInfer's default autotuning bucket can cause the kernel to select a suboptimal tactic for the actual (larger, DP-scaled) token counts, or to re-autotune across varying token counts, degrading MoE throughput. The class already stores `self.moe_config`, so the value is available.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark BF16 TRTLLM modular MoE forward throughput/latency across a sweep of token counts (e.g. 1..8192) with vs. without an explicit tune_max_num_tokens to check for autotuning-induced regressions/stalls.

findings.claim: In `TrtLlmBf16ExpertsModular.apply`, the FlashInfer BF16 routed wrapper does not expose an `output=` argument, so the result is written with `output.copy_(result[...])`. This adds a full-tensor (M x K) device-to-device copy of the MoE output on every forward, which the monolithic path avoids by returning the kernel tensor directly. Minor but per-invocation overhead proportional to the activation size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-layer MoE apply latency for the modular BF16 path and quantify the fraction spent in the output.copy_ vs the kernel itself at representative (M, K) sizes.

findings.claim: The modular apply always runs `trtllm_moe_pack_topk_ids_weights`, which launches a Triton kernel to pack topk ids/weights into int32 on every forward. This is required by the routed-MoE kernel format, but it is an extra kernel launch + intermediate allocation per invocation not present in the monolithic path (which passes raw router_logits). At small token counts the launch/allocation overhead may be non-negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the modular BF16 MoE apply at small batch sizes to measure the overhead of the topk pack kernel launch and its output allocation relative to total MoE time.

findings.claim: This is a bugfix that improves performance for num_ubatches > 2. Previously, `_capture_ubatches` stored the captured CUDA graph under a key equal to the token count of only the first two ubatches (`ubatch_metadata[0].num_tokens + ubatch_metadata[1].num_tokens`), while the lookup path in `__call__` computes `num_tokens = sum(ubatch_slice.num_tokens for ubatch_slice in ubatch_slices)` over ALL ubatches. When num_ubatches > 2 these keys never match, so `self.cudagraphs[num_tokens]` lookups always miss, meaning the captured graph is never replayed and each step re-captures / falls back — a severe throughput regression. The fix aligns the capture key with the lookup key via `sum(m.num_tokens for m in ubatch_metadata)`. The change itself introduces no new cost (the sum is over a small list, once per capture); rather it removes a latent slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DBO with num_ubatches>2 and CUDAGraphMode.FULL; measure per-step latency and confirm captured graph is replayed (cache hit) rather than re-captured each step.

findings.claim: Switching these output-copy/prepare-inputs synchronization events from spin-waiting (default `torch.cuda.Event()`) to `blocking=True` trades CPU-busy-polling for a kernel-mediated (sleep/wakeup) wait. This is the intended goal (avoid contending on the CUDA driver lock), but blocking events add OS scheduler wakeup latency (typically tens of microseconds) to each `synchronize()` call. `prepare_inputs_event.synchronize()` and `async_copy_ready_event.synchronize()` are on the per-step critical path (called every decode step in async scheduling), so at very high decode throughput / tiny batch sizes where the copy is already complete when synchronize() is reached, the added wakeup latency can slightly increase per-step latency versus the busy-poll path that would return immediately. Net effect is expected positive under TP contention but potentially a minor regression in the uncontended single-rank case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step decode latency (single-request, batch=1) with async scheduling enabled, comparing blocking vs non-blocking copy_event/async_copy_ready_event; also measure aggregate throughput under TP=8 with heavy contention to confirm the intended improvement.

findings.claim: The pre-change code used `torch.Event()` (device-agnostic) at these two AsyncGPU*Output sites, whereas the new code uses `torch.cuda.Event(blocking=True)`. This is functionally fine on CUDA, but if these paths were previously exercised on non-CUDA backends the hard `torch.cuda.Event` could break them; not a performance concern per se but worth noting since the spec_decode/async_utils sites already used `torch.cuda.Event`.

In the branch where input does NOT fit in the drafter, when data_parallel_size > 1 the change adds a `self.drafter.dummy_run(num_tokens=1)` call every step where the drafter runs a GPU model forward (EAGLE/DraftModel/etc). This is an extra draft-model forward pass (with its TP/EP/DP collectives) that did not exist before. Although the reason is correct (preventing a hang from DP ranks disagreeing on input_fits_in_drafter), it introduces a small but non-trivial recurring GPU forward + collective per affected step. This is a deliberate correctness fix, but it is added GPU work on the critical path whenever input does not fit in the drafter.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP>1 spec-decode (MTP/EAGLE) throughput/latency measured on steps where input_fits_in_drafter is False, comparing pre/post-change.

findings.claim: In the draft_after_bookkeeping path, the second dummy_run(num_tokens=1) is added for the DP>1 case where input doesn't fit. Same class of fix: a small extra draft-model dummy forward per affected step. Necessary to avoid the collective-mismatch hang but adds recurring cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP>1 spec-decode throughput with frequent input-doesn't-fit steps.

findings.claim: The tiled loop introduces a per-converged-request Python loop with `.item()`, `.nonzero()`, and per-element indexing (`local_idx.item()`, `slot.item()`, `valid_canvas_len_np[start_req + li]`). Previously this ran once over all decode requests; now it runs once per tile. `.item()`/`.nonzero()` force GPU->CPU synchronizations. With tiling, the number of these device-host syncs multiplies by the number of tiles (num_decode/group), serializing the sampler and adding latency at high concurrency — the exact scenario the change targets. Each tile boundary forces a sync before the next _compiled_sample_step can be dispatched.

Splitting the single `_compiled_sample_step` call into a Python loop over tiles multiplies kernel-launch overhead and the number of compiled-graph invocations by num_decode/group. Because the function is torch.compile(dynamic=True), each tile with a differing group size can trigger recompilation/guard checks, and the previous single-pass amortization of the fp32 pipeline (temp schedule, sampling, probs, self-conditioning matmul) is lost. For batches that fit comfortably in memory this is pure added overhead.

`current_platform.mem_get_info()` is called on every sample step whenever num_decode>0. mem_get_info is a CUDA driver call (cudaMemGetInfo) that can synchronize/serialize with the device and adds fixed per-step latency, even in the common case where the whole batch fits in one tile and no tiling is needed.

The last tile has size (num_decode % group) which differs from full tiles, producing at least two distinct dynamic shapes for the compiled `_compiled_sample_step` per call whenever num_decode is not a multiple of group. Under dynamic=True this is usually handled, but variable `group` across steps (it depends on live free memory) increases the set of shapes seen, raising the chance of Inductor recompilation and guard overhead versus the single fixed-shape call before.

The metadata fingerprint now computes two extra per-request numpy reductions/allocations per build() call: clamped_context_lens = np.minimum(seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy() - seg_lengths, topk_tokens) plus two additional .tobytes() serializations (clamped_context_lens and seg_lengths) that are added to metadata_key. This adds O(num_reqs) CPU work and small transient bytes allocations on every attention-metadata build, executed once per forward step. The cost is small relative to the get_mla_metadata_v1 kernel launch it gates, so this is a minor CPU-side overhead rather than a hot-path regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark ROCMAiterMLASparseMetadataBuilder.build() CPU time for large num_reqs (e.g. 256 requests) before/after this commit to quantify the added per-step numpy/tobytes overhead.

findings.claim: For chunked-prefill continuations the persistent path (and thus get_mla_metadata_v1) was previously skipped entirely (use_persistent=False). This commit removes that gating and always uses the persistent path, keying metadata on per-request context lengths. Because context lengths grow with each prefill chunk, the metadata_key will differ every step during chunked prefill, forcing a get_mla_metadata_v1 launch on every continuation step where it was previously never launched. This is intended (the persistent kernel gives better CU load balancing) and the main kernel is expected to be faster, but it does introduce an additional small metadata kernel launch per chunked-prefill step that did not exist before; the net effect is a tradeoff that should be validated on chunked-prefill workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end chunked-prefill decode throughput/latency on ROCm (DSv3.2 sparse MLA) comparing persistent vs. non-persistent path, measuring the extra get_mla_metadata_v1 launch cost against the persistent main-kernel speedup.

findings.claim: In the new vectorized FP32Vec8::tanh(), the loop runs over both 4-lane halves (i=0..1). For each iteration it constructs a temporary FP32Vec8 (temp_vec) with BOTH halves set to the same value two_x, then calls temp_vec.exp() and uses only .reg.val[0]. exp() itself iterates over both halves, so it evaluates the (relatively expensive) polynomial exp on 8 lanes while only 4 are used, and this happens twice. Net effect: ~2x the exp work actually needed (16 lane-exps computed vs the 8 required). It should either pack the two halves of x into one temp_vec and call exp() once, or set only tmp.val[0] and read val[0]. Same wasteful pattern in er().
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark FP32Vec8::tanh() throughput on POWER9/10 vs an implementation that calls exp() once per real half; also benchmark GELU-tanh activation on a PowerPC CPU build.

findings.claim: FP32Vec8::er() has the same inefficiency as tanh(): inside the i=0..1 loop it builds exp_input with both halves equal to neg_x_squared and calls exp_input.exp() (which computes exp for both 4-lane halves) but consumes only .reg.val[0]. Half of every exp() evaluation is discarded and this occurs for each of the two outer iterations, roughly doubling the exp cost of the erf approximation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark FP32Vec8::er() and the erf-based GELU (approximate='none') on a PowerPC CPU build; compare against a variant computing exp only once per used half.

findings.claim: tanh() and er() allocate a full FP32Vec8 temporary (temp_vec/exp_input) inside the per-half loop just to reuse exp(). This constructs/copies an f32x4x2_t each iteration. Since exp() operates on a whole FP32Vec8, the two halves of x could be exp'd in a single FP32Vec8 outside the loop, avoiding the temporary construction and the discarded half.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare assembly/instruction count and throughput of current tanh()/er() vs a refactor that computes exp() once on the full vector.

findings.claim: The final weight-adjustment block in both moeTopK and topkGating now runs unconditionally (it multiplies each of the k output weights by `scale`), whereas previously it was entirely skipped when `renormalize == false`. In the common no-renormalize, routed_scaling_factor == 1.0 case this adds a k-element single-threaded write loop per token row that multiplies by 1.0 (a no-op numerically) but still touches global memory. This is negligible (one thread, k elements per row, hidden behind the surrounding kernel work) and is far outweighed by the win described below.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark topk_sigmoid kernel latency for large num_tokens with renormalize=False, routed_scaling_factor=1.0 vs the parent commit.

findings.claim: This change is a net performance improvement, not a regression: for the sigmoid scoring path it folds `routed_scaling_factor` into the topk_sigmoid CUDA kernel and removes the separate `topk_weights *= routed_scaling_factor` PyTorch elementwise op, eliminating one extra CUDA kernel launch + full-tensor read/write per MoE layer per forward step. Noted only for completeness; no action needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode-throughput comparison on DeepSeek-V3/MiniMax-M3 with sigmoid routing, parent vs this commit, measuring per-layer MoE routing time.

findings.claim: The new persistent `unified_scores_buffer` is allocated as `[max_num_batched_tokens, num_index_heads, MAX_K_TILES]` fp32 with MAX_K_TILES hardcoded to 8192 (=1M tokens of context). For the config default num_index_heads=4 and a typical max_num_batched_tokens=8192 this is 8192*4*8192*4B = 1 GiB of permanently reserved GPU memory (256 MiB even at 2048 tokens), regardless of the actual max sequence length. The pre-change code allocated transient score tensors sized to the actual `round_up(cdiv(max_seq_len,128),16)` tile dim, so for common context lengths (e.g. 32K tokens -> 256 tiles) the new buffer is ~30x larger and never freed. This reserved memory reduces the KV-cache budget and can trigger OOM on smaller GPUs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident GPU memory (torch.cuda.memory_reserved) after model init with sparse_attention_config enabled, comparing MAX_K_TILES=8192 vs a value scaled to the served max_model_len; also measure KV-cache blocks allocated.

findings.claim: `unified_scores.fill_(_SCORE_SENTINEL)` memsets the entire `[num_tokens, H, MAX_K_TILES]` view every forward pass, even though only the causally-valid tail (cdiv(seq_pos+1,128) tiles per row, typically far fewer than 8192) is ever read by the top-k. For a full prefill batch (num_tokens=max_num_batched_tokens) this is a ~1 GiB memset per forward on the critical path, whereas the old per-side score tensors were sized to the actual block count. This adds fixed DRAM-bandwidth-bound overhead proportional to MAX_K_TILES=8192 rather than the real context length.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile time spent in the fill_ / memset before the indexer top-k for a prefill batch at moderate context length (e.g. 8K) vs the old transient-score-buffer path.

findings.claim: The single unified `sparse_topk_select` scans the unified score buffer whose tile dimension is the constant MAX_K_TILES=8192. Unless the kernel is internally bounded by `num_valid_pages` for its inner loop (not just for the -1 clamp/force_end), it will read/iterate over the full 8192 tiles per token even for short sequences, wasting memory bandwidth versus the previous top-k which iterated only up to `valid_blocks`. Worth confirming the MSA kernel bounds its per-row work by num_valid_pages rather than the buffer's tile dim.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark sparse_topk_select over a buffer with tile dim 8192 vs a tile dim matched to the actual max context for a batch of short (<4K) sequences.

findings.claim: The old code passed `output=output` into `flashinfer_b12x_fused_moe`, letting the kernel write results directly into the caller-provided output buffer. The new code calls `wrapper.run(...)`, which returns a freshly-allocated tensor, and then performs `output.copy_(wrapper_output)`. This adds (a) an extra (M, K) device allocation per forward pass and (b) an extra full-size device-to-device copy on every MoE invocation. For every decode/prefill step this is redundant traffic proportional to output size that was previously avoided. If the wrapper supports an out= / output argument it should be used to keep apply() copy-free as the original did.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark FlashInferB12xExperts.apply() end-to-end latency for a range of M (e.g. 16..4096) tokens comparing the previous direct-output kernel path vs. the new wrapper.run + output.copy_ path; measure per-call latency and peak memory.

findings.claim: B12xMoEWrapper is now constructed lazily inside the first apply() call via _ensure_wrapper(), with use_cuda_graph=True. The first forward pass therefore pays the wrapper construction / kernel-setup cost inline, and if that first apply() ever runs inside an outer CUDA graph capture or a latency-sensitive first step it can cause a one-time stall (or interfere with graph capture). Constructing the wrapper eagerly in __init__ or in process_weights_after_loading (as the shape params are already stored) would move this cost out of the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-token latency (TTFT) for a Nemotron-style non-gated MoE model on SM120 hardware, comparing lazy wrapper creation on first apply() vs. eager creation at load time.

findings.claim: In flashinfer_autotune(), the condition for disabling the persistent autotune cache was broadened from 'only DeepEP a2a backends' to 'any world_size > 1'. Since almost all production LLM serving runs distributed (TP/PP/DP => world_size > 1), the persistent FlashInfer autotune cache is now effectively disabled for the common case. The persistent path wrote autotune results to disk (keyed by config hash under VLLM_CACHE_ROOT) and reloaded them on subsequent startups; with use_persistent_cache=False the results are never written/reloaded, so every server startup re-runs the full FlashInfer autotuning benchmark loop from scratch, adding measurable startup latency on every launch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure server startup/warmup time on a multi-GPU (world_size>1) FlashInfer-enabled config across two consecutive launches; before the change the second launch reuses the persistent cache (fast), after the change both launches pay the full autotune cost.

findings.claim: With use_persistent_cache=False, the non-persistent path is taken which runs runner._dummy_run() inside fi_utils.autotune() on EVERY rank, i.e. the expensive per-implementation autotuning benchmark loop executes on all GPUs. Previously (persistent path), only the leader (rank 0) executed autotune in tune_mode and the remaining ranks did a plain _dummy_run, with results broadcast from rank 0. The new default forces full autotuning work on every rank for all distributed deployments, wasting GPU cycles during warmup relative to the leader-only-tunes-and-broadcasts scheme.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare warmup GPU time per rank on a world_size>1 non-DeepEP FlashInfer config before vs after: before, non-leader ranks skip autotune; after, all ranks run the full autotune benchmark loop.

findings.claim: The numba function `_write_fused_patches` (`@njit(parallel=True, cache=True)`) is never warmed up during model/processor initialization, unlike `NgramProposer` which explicitly triggers JIT compilation in its `__init__`. The first image/video preprocessing request will therefore pay the full numba JIT compilation cost inline on the request path, producing a large one-time latency spike (potentially hundreds of ms to seconds). `cache=True` only helps on subsequent process starts, not the first-ever compile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure latency of the first Kimi image preprocessing call vs. subsequent calls (cold vs. warm) to quantify the JIT compilation spike; compare against explicit warmup in processor init.

findings.claim: `numba_workqueue_threading_layer()` forces numba's `workqueue` threading layer for the fused preprocessing. `workqueue` is generally the slowest of numba's threading backends (vs. `omp`/`tbb`), so the parallel patch-writing loop will scale worse across CPU cores than the default layer. Because the threading layer choice is sticky for the process lifetime, this also degrades any other `@njit(parallel=True)` code (e.g. the ngram proposer) that runs afterward in the same process.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark _write_fused_patches throughput under workqueue vs omp vs tbb threading layers across 4/8/16 cores; also measure ngram batch_propose throughput after the fused processor forces workqueue.

findings.claim: In `_write_fused_patches` the `prange` loop iterates the linear index in input frame (T,H,W,C) order but scatters writes into `out` in patch layout (out[patch_idx, c, y%p, x%p]). Consecutive iterations of the loop write to widely separated output locations (different patch rows/patches), giving poor spatial write locality and potential cache-line contention/false sharing between threads, reducing effective memory bandwidth utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare current scatter-write kernel vs. a variant that parallelizes over patch index (contiguous per-patch writes) at typical image sizes; measure CPU time and cache-miss rate.

findings.claim: The fused kernel iterates over the entire padded volume (t_size * padded_h * padded_w * 3), writing 0 for every padded position (y>=new_h or x>=new_w). Since `out` is allocated with `np.empty`, the padding must be zeroed, but doing so inside the per-element parallel loop over the full padded region wastes iterations on padding rather than zeroing the buffer once (e.g. via np.zeros / a targeted memset) and only writing the real pixels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: For images whose (new_h,new_w) are much smaller than padded dims, compare full-padded-volume iteration vs. zero-init + iterate-only-real-pixels.

findings.claim: recursive_replace now instantiates Fusers(self.model, self.model_config), which iterates over every module in the model (`{type(m): get_fuser(m) for m in model.modules()}`) and, for each distinct module class, runs a torch.fx trace (`trace(module)`) plus pattern matching against GLU/QKV/RMSNorm fusers. This is model-construction (one-time) cost only, not per-forward, but it adds startup/model-load latency proportional to the number of distinct module classes (fx tracing can be non-trivial). It does not affect steady-state throughput/latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load time for a Transformers-backend model before/after this commit (e.g. a mid-size MoE model with many module classes).

findings.claim: vllm_attention_forward (moved from base.py, logic unchanged) still executes `self_attn.impl.scale = float(scaling)` and three transpose+reshape ops on every attention forward. This is not a regression (identical to the pre-commit code), just noting the per-call Python-level attribute write and tensor reshapes remain in the attention hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile attention forward overhead per decode step.

findings.claim: HummingFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel is inserted as the FIRST entry in _POSSIBLE_WFP8A16_KERNELS[CUDA] (ahead of MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel). Because Humming's is_supported() only checks CUDA + SM75+ and can_implement() unconditionally returns (True, None), it will be selected for ALL W-FP8-A16 layers on any CUDA SM75+ device, unconditionally displacing the previously default Marlin kernel. If the Humming GEMM is slower than Marlin on any of these shapes/hardware, this is a silent throughput/latency regression for every FP8-weight-only linear layer, with no shape- or hardware-specific gating to fall back.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FP8 W8A16 linear (e.g. Qwen2-1.5B-FP8W8) decode/prefill throughput comparing Humming vs Marlin on SM75/SM90/SM100 across common token counts.

findings.claim: Humming FP8/Int8/Mxfp8/NvFp4/MxFp4 kernels are appended to nearly every CUDA kernel priority list with can_implement() hardcoded to (True, None) and is_supported() gated only on CUDA + SM75+. Since these are appended after existing kernels they usually won't win selection, but any list where an existing kernel returns unsupported for a given shape will now fall through to Humming instead of raising, which may pick a slower path than an unlisted specialized kernel on newer hardware (SM90/SM100). Lack of shape/capability-aware can_implement means no fallback to a faster backend for shapes Humming handles poorly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare Humming vs Cutlass/FlashInfer FP8 and NVFP4 GEMM latency across a matrix of (M,N,K) and compute capabilities to confirm Humming is not selected for shapes where it is materially slower.

findings.claim: get_humming_moe_gemm_type() previously returned None when VLLM_HUMMING_MOE_GEMM_TYPE was unset (allowing is_supported_config to accept any gemm type), but now defaults to 'indexed'. In is_supported_config the check 'if preferred_gemm_type is not None' will now always be True and force gemm_type == 'indexed', excluding the grouped_contiguous experts variant by default. If grouped_contiguous is faster for a given deployment (e.g. large-batch prefill), Humming MoE will now silently use the indexed path even when the grouped path would give higher throughput, with no way to auto-select the faster one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Humming MoE (e.g. Qwen3-30B-A3B-FP8) with gemm_type=indexed vs grouped_contiguous at various batch sizes to verify 'indexed' default does not regress high-throughput cases.

findings.claim: HummingExpertsBase.__init__ changed self.num_experts from self.layer.num_experts to self.layer.local_num_experts. estimate_local_valid_shape_m uses num_experts to scale global_valid_shape_m for kernel tuning-config selection (moe_block_size). If local_num_experts differs from the previously-used num_experts under EP/TP, the estimated shape_m used to pick the MoE block size can change, potentially selecting a suboptimal tuning config (wrong block_size) and degrading MoE GEMM efficiency. This is a behavioral change affecting tuning, not a clear win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under expert-parallel deployment, compare MoE throughput and the selected moe_block_size before/after the local_num_experts change.

findings.claim: Enabling `use_ep` (and hence All2All manager initialization) whenever `use_sequence_parallel_moe` is True changes MoE dispatch to the all2all/naive-dispatch path even when data_parallel_size==1. Combined with `use_all2all_kernels` and `do_naive_dispatch_combine` now returning True for the SP case, MoE forward now performs reduce_scatter + all_gather collective communication per MoE layer where previously (TP-only, no DP) it used a plain all-reduce. This is intended to reduce duplicate expert compute, but on configurations where the token count is small (decode-heavy / low batch) the extra collectives and padding can add latency rather than throughput. Worth benchmarking the low-batch decode regime, since the reported 1.9-5.0% E2E gain is an average and the SP path can regress small-batch latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode latency (small batch, e.g. bs=1..8) with TP=8, EP enabled, DP=1, SP on vs off; measure per-token latency and MoE-layer collective time.

findings.claim: The refactor replaces the conditional pad (`if sp_remainder:`) with an unconditional `torch.nn.functional.pad(hidden_states, (0,0,0,sp_pad))`. When the token count is already divisible by the TP world size, `sp_pad` is 0, so previously no pad op was issued; now `pad` is always called. `F.pad` with pad size 0 still allocates/returns a (possibly new) tensor and launches a copy op, adding a small per-layer overhead in the divisible-token case. This runs once per MoE layer per forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark torch.nn.functional.pad with pad=0 vs skipping it on a large hidden_states tensor; count extra kernel launches per forward across all decoder layers.

findings.claim: New branch `num_tokens_across_dp = torch.tensor([num_tokens], dtype=torch.int32)` allocates a small CPU tensor on every forward pass when SP-MoE is active without DP. This is a tiny per-step allocation but occurs on the hot path (once per model forward) and feeds DPMetadata.make. Minor, but avoidable by reusing a cached/pinned buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CPU time in set_forward_context per step with SP-MoE enabled, DP=1.

findings.claim: build() now unconditionally calls hpc.assign_attention_decode_task(...) on every decode step whenever num_decodes > 0, even in the common non-speculative single-token decode case (decode_query_len == 1). This is a new per-forward-step kernel/host launch (dynamic-schedule task assignment over seq_lens_decode) that did not exist before this commit, adding fixed latency to every decode iteration and CPU launch overhead in the critical decode loop. For workloads that never use MTP/spec-decode this is pure added cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-token decode latency / tokens-per-second on a standard (non-speculative) decode workload with HPC_ATTN before vs after this commit, isolating time spent in HpcAttnMetadataBuilder.build().

findings.claim: assign_attention_decode_task is passed seq_lens_decode = seq_lens[:num_decodes], which is a GPU tensor. If the hpc op inspects sequence lengths on the host to build the dynamic-schedule task_map, it could trigger an implicit device->host synchronization each decode step, stalling the pipeline. Cannot be confirmed without the external hpc op implementation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile for CUDA syncs (e.g. nsys / torch profiler) inside build() during decode to check for D2H stalls introduced by assign_attention_decode_task.

findings.claim: get_cudagraph_support downgrades from UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE to UNIFORM_BATCH when speculative_config with num_speculative_tokens>0 is set. UNIFORM_BATCH is generally a weaker/broader CG mode than single-token decode; if it results in fewer captured graphs or falls back to eager for some batch shapes under spec-decode, effective GPU utilization could drop. This is only triggered when spec-decode is enabled, and is likely necessary for MTP correctness, so it is a speculative concern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With num_speculative_tokens>0 and HPC_ATTN, compare decode throughput and cudagraph capture/replay hit-rate before vs after.

findings.claim: `_get_video_max_pixels` calls `BaseVideoProcessor.get_video_processor_dict(...)` directly (bypassing vLLM's cached processor helpers). Unlike `cached_get_video_processor`/`cached_processor_from_config`, this raw transformers call is not wrapped in an `lru_cache`, so every invocation re-parses the video preprocessor config (and may perform an HF Hub `hf_hub_download`/`file_exists` round-trip for a remote model each time). If `get_mm_max_tokens_per_item` is invoked more than once during startup, this reintroduces per-call config I/O that the old `get_video_processor()` path avoided via caching. The commit's goal is to avoid full processor init, which is a net win, but the un-cached dict fetch is a minor regression vs a cached helper.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time server startup / engine init for a GLM4V video-capable model repo on HF Hub (remote, no local cache) and count hf_hub_download/list_repo_files calls; compare parent vs commit.

findings.claim: `get_max_image_tokens`->`_get_video_max_pixels` and the new image path call `ImageProcessingMixin.get_image_processor_dict(...)` / `BaseVideoProcessor.get_video_processor_dict(...)` when the local image_processor_config lacks a `size`. These transformers calls hit the filesystem/Hub and are not memoized, so if they fall into the fallback branch on every profiling/dummy-input pass they add repeated config reads. For a purely local model the cost is small; for remote models it can add extra Hub metadata requests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument get_image_processor_dict / get_video_processor_dict call counts during a full engine startup for GLM4V and compare against parent commit.

findings.claim: The new MLADualRMSPerTokenQuantPattern replaces a q RMSNorm+FP8-quant op (rocm_aiter_rmsnorm_fused_dynamic_quant) plus a separate kv rms_norm with a single fused_mla_dual_rms_norm_per_token_quant kernel. This is intended to reduce kernel launches and improve MLA FP8 attention perf (~1-2% claimed). No degradation is expected in the fused path; the main risk is behavioral, not performance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA FP8 attention microbench on ROCm/MI3xx comparing rocm_aiter_rmsnorm_fused_dynamic_quant + rms_norm vs fused_mla_dual_rms_norm_per_token_quant, measuring per-token latency and kernel count for DeepSeek-V3 geometry (q_dim=1536, kv_c=512, k_pe=64).

findings.claim: MLADualRMSPerTokenQuantPattern is registered for both epsilon values (1e-5, 1e-6) alongside the existing MLADualRMSNormPattern, doubling the number of MLA fusion patterns the post-grad PatternMatcher must attempt. This is a one-time torch.compile cost, not a runtime cost, and is small relative to overall pass count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile pass time for MLADualRMSNormFusionPass before/after adding the per-token pattern on a DeepSeek-V3 graph.

findings.claim: _fused_mla_dual_rms_norm_per_token_quant_impl pre-allocates q_out, q_scale, and kv_normed with torch.empty before calling the aiter kernel. These are necessary output buffers for the fused kernel and match the semantics of the ops they replace (which also allocate their outputs), so no net extra allocation vs the unfused path. kv_normed uses kv.shape which correctly preserves the strided split view's logical shape.
