The default V2 model runner selection was inverted from an opt-in allowlist to an opt-out policy: previously only a small explicit set of architectures (Qwen3, DeepseekV2, Qwen2Moe, GraniteMoe, Llama, Mistral) got V2 by default; now `_default_use_v2_model_runner` returns True for essentially every dense (non-MoE, non-hybrid, non-attention-free) generate model via `or not model_config.is_moe`. This silently switches the model runner for the vast majority of models. The V2 model runner is a distinct execution engine whose per-model performance characteristics (throughput/latency/GPU utilization) have not been validated for the full universe of dense architectures now defaulting to it; any regression in V2 for a given architecture now becomes the default behavior. The change also removes the explicit Llama/Mistral/Qwen3 entries, meaning correctness/perf tuning that was specific to the allowlist path no longer applies distinctly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare throughput and per-token latency (TTFT, ITL) of V1 vs V2 model runner on a representative set of dense models (e.g. Llama, Gemma2, Mistral, Qwen2 dense) at O2 across small and large batch sizes; verify no regression under the new default.

findings.claim: The buildkite test file removed the `ENFORCE_EAGER=1` requirement for async scheduling tests, with the note that it was needed 'until we sort out CG correctness issues.' Enabling V2 by default for all dense models while CUDA graph correctness with async scheduling was previously gated behind eager mode risks either falling back to eager (losing CUDA-graph throughput benefits) or exposing CUDA-graph/async-scheduling interactions that were the original reason for enforcing eager. If any dense model now defaulting to V2 must run eager to be correct, it loses full CUDA graph acceleration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run async scheduling tests with V2 model runner and cudagraphs enabled (non-eager) on dense models; measure decode throughput vs eager and confirm no hang/correctness fallback that forces eager.

findings.claim: With V2 now the default for dense models, when async scheduling is enabled `max_concurrent_batches` becomes `pp_size + 1` (one extra in-flight batch to overlap async scheduling) instead of `pp_size`. This extra concurrent batch is now the default path for many more deployments and increases peak activation/intermediate memory and KV scheduling working set by one batch, which can raise GPU memory pressure for previously-V1 dense models.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory and max achievable batch/KV blocks with V1 (max_concurrent_batches=pp) vs V2 (pp+1) under async scheduling for a dense model near memory limits.

findings.claim: In vllm/v1/worker/gpu/cudagraph_utils.py `_init_candidates`, when Dynamic SD is enabled the decode-graph capture loop now iterates over every distinct decode query length in `num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size` (up to num_speculative_tokens+1 distinct values) instead of a single `decode_query_len`. This multiplies the number of FULL decode CUDA graphs captured (roughly by the number of distinct scheduled K values) across all capture_sizes and LoRA cases. Each captured FULL graph consumes additional GPU memory (static input/activation buffers in the graph pool) and significantly increases startup capture time. Although deduped by `desc not in descs_by_mode[...]`, the dedup uses a linear `in` scan over a growing list, and the resulting graph count/memory can grow substantially for wide DSD schedules.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure startup CUDA-graph capture time and peak GPU memory with Dynamic SD enabled (FULL_AND_PIECEWISE) for a schedule spanning many distinct K values (e.g. K in 1..7) versus a single fixed K, at large max_num_seqs.

findings.claim: The dedup check `if desc not in descs_by_mode[decode_mode]` is an O(n) linear scan over the list of already-appended descriptors, executed inside the (capture_sizes x decode_query_lens x lora_cases) nested loop. As the number of decode query lengths and capture sizes grows, this makes candidate initialization O(N^2) in the number of decode descriptors. This is one-time startup cost, not a runtime regression, but can be noticeable for large DSD schedules; a set-based membership check would avoid it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time `CudaGraphManager._init_candidates` (CPU only) with a large capture_sizes list and a wide DSD schedule to quantify quadratic growth.

findings.claim: Because DSD now adds multiple decode descriptors per token count into `_candidates`, the per-call `dispatch()` loop over `self._candidates[key]` iterates over more BatchExecutionDescriptor entries before finding a compatible one. This slightly increases per-forward dispatch overhead (a hot path executed every step), though the lists remain short relative to model forward time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark `dispatch()` throughput with DSD enabled vs disabled to measure added per-step CPU overhead.

findings.claim: The workspace buffer was renamed from `trtllm_gen_workspace_buffer`/`_get_trtllm_gen_workspace_buffer` to a single shared `trtllm_workspace_buffer`/`_get_trtllm_workspace_buffer`, but XQA (SM90) and trtllm-gen (SM100) decode paths now both allocate/share the SAME global buffer sized `VLLM_FLASHINFER_WORKSPACE_BUFFER_SIZE`. If XQA and native prefill run within the same forward and both request this workspace concurrently, sharing one global buffer can create serialization/contention or (worse) overwrite in-flight scratch, and if they were previously distinct allocations, memory footprint semantics changed. Confirm the single shared buffer is safe when both prefill (FI native) and XQA decode run in the same batch on SM90.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run mixed prefill+decode batch on SM90 with FP8 KV cache and measure per-step latency/allocated workspace vs SM100 trtllm-gen.

findings.claim: The metadata builder now computes Q dtype for BOTH prefill and decode phases unconditionally at init via two `get_q_data_type()` calls, and `__init__` now also calls `FlashInferBackend.supports_sink()` (which itself calls `supports_trtllm_attention` twice plus `force_use_trtllm_attention`) plus `can_use_trtllm_attention`. While `supports_trtllm_attention` and `has_nvidia_artifactory` are `functools.cache`-d, this is init-time only and low cost; the concern is minor but the added `supports_sink()` path replaces a cheap `can_use_trtllm` boolean with multiple function calls. Impact is one-time per builder init, so negligible at runtime.

`supports_trtllm_attention` is decorated with `@functools.cache` and now takes an `is_prefill` parameter. This doubles the cache entries (two keys: True/False) rather than one, which is harmless, but callers that previously called it with no argument now sometimes pass `is_prefill=True` and sometimes `is_prefill=False`, meaning the SM90 branch calling `is_device_capability(90)` and `has_nvidia_artifactory()` is evaluated for each distinct key. Since both are cheap/cached this is negligible, but worth noting that the cache no longer collapses to a single computed value.

`supports_trtllm_attention(is_prefill=...)` now short-circuits on `has_nvidia_artifactory()` before the device-capability check. `has_nvidia_artifactory()` performs a network request (with 5s timeout) when cubins are not pre-downloaded. The new per-phase call pattern (prefill and decode) plus additional call sites (supports_kv_cache_dtype, supports_sink) mean this may be invoked earlier/more often during config validation; while `functools.cache` prevents repeated network calls, the first invocation now potentially blocks in more code paths (e.g., nvfp4 kv_cache_dtype validation) than before.

This is a bugfix that reduces the sliding_window value in the pooled SlidingWindowSpec by dividing it by block_pool_size (cdiv(sliding_window, block_pool_size)). Before this change, the KV cache manager treated the window as `block_pool_size`x too large, causing it to reserve ~block_pool_size times as many pooled blocks per request as needed (max_admission_blocks_per_request and max_memory_usage_bytes are derived from sliding_window). The fix therefore *reduces* memory over-reservation rather than degrading performance. No runtime slowdown is introduced; the kernel window is unaffected (it comes from the attention impl, not this spec). Note: it fixes a pre-existing memory-waste issue.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure encoder KV cache memory reservation / max concurrent requests for Voxtral-Mini-4B-Realtime before and after; verify pooled block count per request drops to expected value (e.g. 13 blocks) without accuracy regression.

findings.claim: The change removes the `tune_max_num_tokens=max(self.max_capture_size, 1)` argument to `flashinfer_cutlass_fused_moe`, causing the FlashInfer CUTLASS MoE kernel to fall back to its library default tuning bound. This parameter bounds the number of tokens the autotuner considers when selecting CUTLASS tile/tactic configurations. If the removed value was previously *lower* than the actual max token count seen at runtime (which was the described bug — an incorrect/too-small token bound), removing it is a correctness/perf fix, not a regression. But if the FlashInfer default is smaller or larger than the real workload, kernel tactic selection may become suboptimal, resulting in modestly lower MoE GEMM throughput for large-batch/prefill token counts. Net effect is workload-dependent and expected to be small; the commit is titled as a bugfix correcting a wrong bound. Recommend confirming the FlashInfer default tune bound matches expected serving token sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FlashInfer CUTLASS MoE layer throughput/latency across a sweep of token counts (e.g. 1, 128, 1024, 8192, > max_capture_size) before vs after this commit to verify autotuner picks equal-or-better tactics.

findings.claim: This change adds `and not current_platform.is_device_capability_family(120)` to the predicate that enables the cooperative top-K kernel path. On SM120 (Blackwell consumer) hardware, this disables the cooperative top-K kernel and forces a fallback to the alternative top-K selector (the `use_persistent_topk` branch or the generic `top_k_per_row` path). If the cooperative top-K kernel is faster on the relevant decode shapes (num_rows <= 32, TMA-aligned logits), then SM120 devices will now take a slower selection path for the sparse-attention indexer's top-K, increasing decode latency. This is a functional correctness fix (the cooperative kernel presumably misbehaves/crashes on SM120), so the performance cost is likely an accepted trade-off, but it is a measurable regression on SM120 relative to a correctly-supported cooperative kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the sparse_attn_indexer decode path top-K selection on an SM120 GPU with topk_tokens in {512,1024,2048}, num_rows<=32, comparing latency of the cooperative kernel path vs the fallback path used after this change.

findings.claim: vllm/platforms/cpu.py now unconditionally appends "+gelu_and_mul" to compilation_config.custom_ops for ARM. This force-enables the eager custom-op path for GeluAndMul, which prevents torch.compile/Inductor from fusing GeluAndMul into surrounding elementwise/GEMM epilogue graphs. Since GeluAndMul.forward_cpu now calls forward_cuda (a single opaque torch.ops._C kernel), the activation becomes an un-fusable op boundary that materializes an intermediate output tensor each call. For GEGLU-based models with many layers this can measurably reduce throughput vs. the previously-compiled forward_native path when Inductor is active.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tokens/s on an ARM CPU for a GeGLU model (e.g. Gemma) with compilation enabled, before vs. after this commit, toggling +gelu_and_mul.

findings.claim: fast_tanhf_f32x4 evaluates vcagtq_f32 + any_u32 (vmaxvq reduction) on every 4-lane group and conditionally calls special_case (an extra vdivq). The horizontal reduction vmaxvq is a cross-lane op that adds latency to every tanh call in gelu_tanh/gelu_new/gelu_fast, even when no lane is in the special range. This is inherent to the AOR design and is usually still faster than the previous torch-vectorized tanh, but it adds a per-vector branch that can stall on inputs that mix large and small magnitudes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark fast_tanhf_f32x4 vs. previous OPT_TORCH_IMPL(tanh) on a range of activation input distributions on ARM.

findings.claim: fast_tanhf_f32x4 and special_case call ptr_barrier(&kTanhfConstants), an inline-asm optimization barrier that hides the constant pointer from the compiler. This forces the polynomial coefficients (special_bound, two, c0..c4, ln2, exponent_bias) to be reloaded from memory on every invocation and blocks CSE/hoisting of these loads out of the activation_kernel inner loop, adding memory traffic per vector. In a tight per-element activation loop this prevents the compiler from keeping the constants resident in registers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark gelu_tanh over a large tensor on ARM with and without the ptr_barrier to measure per-element cost.

findings.claim: GELUTanh.forward_cpu and GeluAndMul.forward_cpu allocate torch.empty_like/torch.empty per call and go through a Python-level op dispatch. When self.op is None (non-ARM CPU or missing symbol) GELUTanh.forward_cpu falls back to F.gelu with approximate='tanh' each call; and GeluAndMul.forward_cpu references self.native(x) which does not exist (the method is forward_native), so the None-op fallback path would raise AttributeError rather than run the native activation. This is a correctness/robustness bug on the fallback path more than a perf issue, but it means the intended native fallback never executes.

On single-node setups the non-quant allreduce workspace now defaults to the 'mnnvl' backend, while quant fusion patterns (FP8/FP4) always use the 'trtllm' backend via get_fi_ar_quant_workspace. Previously both were 'trtllm' on single node, so get_fi_ar_quant_workspace/get_fi_ar_workspace aliased the same workspace object (see the reuse-if-backend-matches checks). With mnnvl selected for non-quant, the backends no longer match, so a model that uses both quant and non-quant allreduce+RMSNorm fusion patterns will allocate two separate flashinfer workspaces instead of one shared one, roughly doubling the allreduce-fusion workspace memory (buffers sized by max_token_num * hidden_dim). This regression only disappears on topologies without NVSwitch multicast, where the new allow_trtllm_fallback path reverts non-quant back to trtllm and restores sharing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable FlashInfer allreduce fusion on a single-node TP>1 run with an FP8-quantized model that triggers both quant and non-quant AR+RMSNorm fusion patterns; compare GPU/host workspace memory footprint before vs after this commit (expect ~2x on the fusion workspace when mnnvl succeeds).

findings.claim: The default single-node fused-allreduce backend flips from 'trtllm' to 'mnnvl'. The two backends have different kernel implementations (e.g. layout_code/SWIZZLED_128x4 handling is only applied for trtllm) and different one-shot/two-shot behavior, so per-step allreduce+RMSNorm latency and GPU utilization on single-node TP runs may change. The commit message only justifies the switch on correctness grounds (a cudagraph hang fix), not performance, so whether mnnvl matches or beats trtllm latency on single-node NVSwitch systems is unverified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce + RMSNorm fusion latency on a single-node NVSwitch box (H100/GB200) with VLLM_FLASHINFER_ALLREDUCE_BACKEND=trtllm vs mnnvl across representative token counts (batch=1 spec-decode shapes and prefill shapes).

findings.claim: When mnnvl is auto-selected on a single node without NVSwitch multicast, get_fi_ar_workspace now attempts to create the mnnvl workspace first (which fails inside _create_workspace after a flashinfer call and exception handling), then creates the trtllm workspace. This adds a wasted workspace-creation attempt (a flashinfer C++ call plus exception unwinding) during one-time initialization. Minor, one-time cost, but it is extra work on the previously trtllm-direct path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine init time contribution of get_fi_ar_workspace on a single-node non-NVSwitch topology before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: On the default ROCm path (HAS_AITER_MHC and hidden_size % 256 == 0), the decoder now sets use_fused_mhc=False, forcing DeepseekV4DecoderLayer.forward() into _forward_unfused_post_pre instead of _forward_fused_post_pre. The unfused path splits each MHC post+pre boundary into two separate kernel launches (self.hc_post + self.hc_pre) per attn/ffn sub-block instead of the single fused mhc_fused_post_pre call, roughly doubling the number of MHC kernel dispatches per layer and adding an extra materialized residual roundtrip. Even though the individual pre/post kernels are now the (faster) aiter kernels, giving up fusion at every layer boundary adds per-launch overhead and extra global-memory traffic that the tilelang fused kernel avoided. Since the aiter mHC ops file (mhc/__init__) exports mhc_fused_post_pre_aiter, a fused aiter path appears to exist, so disabling fusion is likely leaving performance on the table.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepSeek-V4 decode/prefill throughput on MI300 with aiter enabled and hidden_size%256==0, comparing the fused tilelang path vs the new aiter unfused pre/post path (kernel launch count and tokens/s).

findings.claim: The aiter path selection is decided per-call at runtime in MHCPreOp.forward_hip/MHCPostOp.forward_hip via `HAS_AITER_MHC and hidden_size % 256 == 0`, while the layer-level use_fused_mhc uses the same condition on self.hidden_size. These are computed redundantly on every forward; the residual.shape[-1] read and modulo per op call is negligible but the branch structure means when aiter is present but hidden_size%256!=0 it silently falls through the fused tilelang path in _forward_fused_post_pre while the standalone pre/post ops would still take tilelang — consistent, but the two condition sites can drift. No measurable perf cost, flagged only for the extra per-forward branch bookkeeping.

The previous code disabled the aiter mHC pre/post path specifically because it 'has accuracy problem at large number of tokens' (referenced aiter bugfix commit). This commit re-enables aiter as the default without any visible version guard on the aiter build. If the deployed aiter lacks the bugfix, correctness (not just performance) degrades at large token counts; from a pure performance standpoint any accuracy fallback/retry mechanism triggered downstream would cost throughput. Cannot substantiate an actual slowdown from the diff alone.

In the decode branch of _forward_impl, `qo_indptr_decode = torch.arange(num_decode_reqs + 1, dtype=torch.int32, device=qkv.device)` allocates and computes a fresh device tensor on every decode step, for every attention layer. In the decode hot loop this adds a per-layer kernel launch / allocation of a tiny [0..num_decode_reqs] prefix-sum that is identical across steps for a fixed batch shape and could be precomputed once (e.g. cached in metadata or a persistent buffer). This is pure per-step overhead multiplied by num_layers and directly on the latency-critical decode path. It also complicates CUDA-graph capture since a new tensor is materialized each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only latency (tokens/s) sweeping num_layers with a fixed decode batch; compare against a variant that precomputes qo_indptr_decode once per step or in the metadata builder.

findings.claim: `import hpc` is executed inside _forward_impl, which runs once per attention layer per forward step. After the first import it is a sys.modules dict lookup, but placing the import at module scope avoids repeated interpreter import machinery on the per-layer hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark of _forward_impl call overhead with import at module scope vs inside the function.

findings.claim: When HPC rope_norm is enabled, get_rope is built with dtype=torch.float32, so the RoPE cos/sin cache is float32 (2x the memory of bf16/fp16). Additionally HpcRopeNorm.__init__ registers a second float32 copy of cos_sin_cache (`self.register_buffer('cos_sin_cache', cos_sin_cache.float(), persistent=False)`). Since the rotary_emb cache is already float32 in this path, `.float()` copies it again, giving two float32 copies of the cos/sin cache resident in GPU memory per attention module.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory of the model with HPC backend enabled vs disabled, isolating the rotary_emb + HpcRopeNorm cos_sin_cache buffers across all layers.

findings.claim: forward_cuda allocates a fresh `output` tensor via torch.empty on every call (per layer, per step) rather than reusing a persistent buffer. For decode this is a small allocation but recurs num_layers times per step; when not covered by CUDA graph capture this adds allocator pressure on the decode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode throughput with per-call torch.empty vs a reused output buffer.

findings.claim: In the weightless (weight is None) path of rms_norm, the code now allocates a fresh `torch.ones(x.shape[-1], ...)` tensor on every call. This tensor allocation and initialization occurs on the hot path of the XPU kernel and is repeated for every forward invocation, adding a per-call device memory allocation and fill overhead that the previous native path avoided. If weightless RMSNorm is invoked frequently (once per layer per token step) this introduces measurable per-call allocation overhead and GPU/host synchronization pressure.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rms_norm with weight=None on XPU vs. the previous native path, measuring per-call latency and allocation count over many iterations.

findings.claim: In fused_add_rms_norm the weightless path now passes a ones-vector weight into the _C kernel, meaning the kernel performs an element-wise multiply by 1.0 that was previously skipped by the native implementation. This adds unnecessary multiply work per element on top of the per-call `torch.ones` allocation. For large hidden dimensions and high call frequency this is extra wasted GPU compute and memory bandwidth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare fused_add_rms_norm throughput on XPU with weight=None before and after this change across representative hidden sizes.

findings.claim: cutedsl_warmup() is enabled by default (enable_cutedsl_warmup: bool = True) and is invoked unconditionally during kernel_warmup() on every worker. For FA4 MLA prefill setups it triggers a series of CuTeDSL JIT compilations (each compile unit is a real kernel compile, typically seconds). The number of compile units is combinatorial: num_shape_probes x 2 (causal options) x 2 (return_lse options), reaching up to ~24 units on SM100-family diff-head-dim configs. This adds measurable one-time startup latency to server boot. It is deduped by request_key so it is bounded, and it is by design a trade-off (moving JIT cost out of the serving hot path), but the default-on behavior means every FA4 MLA deployment pays this startup cost even if it never hits those exact shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure server cold-start time (engine init to ready) on an SM100/SM90 FA4 MLA model with enable_cutedsl_warmup True vs False; report added wall-clock seconds and count of compiled units.

findings.claim: _compile_cutedsl_warmup_units() calls torch.accelerator.synchronize() once after compiling all units. Because CuTeDSL compilation is largely a host-side/JIT operation, the sync mainly forces a device barrier at the end; this is fine functionally. However cutedsl_warmup() runs on all ranks identically with no rank-0-only leader branch (unlike flashinfer_autotune), so if compile-unit sets ever diverge across ranks (e.g. heterogeneous GPUs mapping to different arch_family / shape-probe sets in _shape_probes_for_context), ranks could compile a different number of units and spend materially different time before any subsequent collective, risking straggler-induced stalls. On homogeneous clusters this is a non-issue.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a multi-rank homogeneous FA4 MLA deployment, log per-rank compiled unit count and warmup duration to confirm balance; verify no downstream collective timeout.

findings.claim: _sync_dbo_comm_if_needed() calls torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize(), a full host-blocking device sync, and it is invoked after every DeepEP HT dispatch (line ~173) and after every combine (line ~389) whenever DBO is enabled on ROCm. This is called per MoE layer, per microbatch, on every forward pass. A CPU-blocking .synchronize() drains the entire stream and stalls the host until all queued GPU work completes, destroying CPU/GPU overlap (no kernel launch pipelining) and forcing serialization of dispatch/combine with subsequent work. The whole purpose of DBO is to overlap one ubatch's comm with the other ubatch's compute via stream events; a full stream sync defeats that overlap entirely, so throughput of the ROCm DeepEP-HT DBO path will regress substantially versus the intended overlapped execution.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode/prefill throughput (tokens/s) and GPU utilization for a DP+EP MoE model (e.g. DeepSeek) on ROCm with --enable-dbo and --all2all-backend deepep_high_throughput, comparing before/after this commit; measure per-layer wall time of prepare/finalize.

findings.claim: For ROCm DeepEP HT DBO, comm_sms is forced to 0, which disables the SM/CU-partitioning between communication and compute kernels (set_comm_sms and DeepGEMM set_compute_sms become no-ops because they are only wired up when comm_sms > 0). Without carving out dedicated CUs for the communication kernels, the comm and compute kernels contend for all CUs when co-scheduled, which can reduce effective overlap efficiency. This is a deliberate correctness/accuracy tradeoff, but it removes an optimization that existed to improve concurrent comm+compute throughput on the DBO path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput comparison of DBO overlap efficiency with comm_sms=0 vs a nonzero reserved value on ROCm DeepEP-HT, if accuracy allows.

findings.claim: Forcibly disabling async scheduling (self.scheduler_config.async_scheduling = False) for ROCm DeepEP-HT DBO removes the scheduler/execution overlap that async scheduling provides. This is a correctness-motivated decision (async scheduling can corrupt DP+EP accuracy here), but it means CPU scheduling and GPU execution can no longer be pipelined for this configuration, adding per-step latency relative to a hypothetical async-enabled run.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end request latency and throughput with async scheduling on vs off for the ROCm DeepEP-HT DBO configuration.

findings.claim: _all_gather_batched_symm_mem allocates fresh symmetric-memory output tensors inside a `nccl_symm_mem_context` on every call. That context re-snapshots the whole NCCL mem pool (_pool.snapshot()) and re-scans it for window registration on each invocation. This is exactly the per-call overhead (~0.5 ms, as noted in _get_symm_scratch's docstring) that the persistent-scratch design was introduced to eliminate. The single-tensor path uses persistent pre-registered scratch, but the batched (list) all-gather path does not, so batched sequence-parallel/EP all-gathers keep paying the snapshot+registration cost every call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Repeatedly call all_gatherv with a list of tensors under VLLM_USE_NCCL_SYMM_MEM=1 vs the ring fallback; measure per-call CPU overhead / end-to-end SP throughput.

findings.claim: _reduce_scatter_symm_mem calls is_symmetric_memory_tensor(input_tensor) on every reduce-scatter. is_symmetric_memory_tensor iterates over all segments and all blocks of _cached_pool_snapshot (nested O(segments x blocks) loop) to match the storage data_ptr. Because _get_symm_scratch permanently caches a distinct symm buffer per (role, shape, dtype), the pool (and thus this scan) grows with the number of distinct shapes seen, adding CPU overhead to the launch of every reduce-scatter.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark reduce_scatter launch overhead after many distinct-shape collectives have populated the symm pool.

findings.claim: _get_symm_scratch caches a persistent, pre-registered symmetric-memory buffer per (role, shape, dtype) key and never evicts. For workloads with variable token counts (dynamic batch sizes in sequence parallelism), each new dim-0 size produces a new key, so the framework retains a live full-size symmetric buffer for every distinct shape ever seen (rs_out, rs_in, ag_out). This grows GPU memory monotonically, and each new shape also triggers a fresh ncclCommWindowRegister via nccl_symm_mem_context. The 'safe for serial eager SP' comment does not bound this growth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run SP/TP inference with many distinct sequence lengths under VLLM_USE_NCCL_SYMM_MEM=1 and track allocator reserved memory over time vs the non-symm baseline.

findings.claim: The all_gather override calls self._all_gather_symm_mem(input_.contiguous()). For non-contiguous dim-0 inputs this forces an extra device-to-device copy that the base-class ring all_gather (dist.all_gather_into_tensor) does not necessarily require in the same way. Minor, and only for non-contiguous inputs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare all_gather of a non-contiguous dim-0 tensor via symm-mem path vs ring path.

findings.claim: _all_gather_symm_mem and _reduce_scatter_symm_mem return the persistent scratch buffer directly. Callers that previously received a freshly-allocated output now get a shared buffer overwritten by the next same-role/same-shape collective. If a consumer must retain the result across a subsequent same-shape all-gather, this forces defensive .clone() copies at call sites (or silent correctness issues), potentially adding copies that offset the symm-mem gains. Worth validating that all sequence-parallel call sites consume the result before the next collective.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trace whether SP gather-before-GEMM results are consumed before the next same-shape all_gather; measure any added clones.

findings.claim: warm_v1_block_table_kernels() constructs a full BlockTable with slot_mapping buffer sized max_num_batched_tokens (int64) on both CPU and GPU, for each of two block sizes (3, 16), on every non-pooling v1 model at startup. This runs unconditionally for ALL v1 models (not just sparse-MLA/DSv4 models), adding a small but universal startup allocation + Triton JIT cost. The buffer allocations are max_num_batched_tokens*8 bytes each, which is modest, but the compute_slot_mapping Triton kernel JIT compile is paid at startup for every model. This is warmup (one-time), so it does not affect steady-state throughput/latency; the impact is only slower engine startup time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine cold-start / warmup wall-time before vs after this commit for a small non-sparse model (e.g. a Qwen dense model), isolating kernel_warmup().

findings.claim: sparse_mla_triton_warmup, when the DSv4 path is taken, iterates over a large cross-product of warmup cases: _warm_combine_topk_swa_indices_kernel runs 3 input variants x up to 2 M values x up to 2 N values per combine case, across 5 DSv4 combine cases, plus _warm_prefill_chunk_metadata_kernel over 2 compress ratios x 2 seq-len multipliers x 2 uncompressed variants x 4 query slices, plus sparse-swa prefill metadata over 4x3 combinations. Each invocation launches real Triton kernels and allocates output tensors. This is one-time warmup, but the combinatorial expansion measurably lengthens startup for DSv4 sparse-MLA models. It is guarded to only DSv4/sparse backends, limiting scope.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time kernel_warmup() for a DSv4 sparse-MLA model before/after this commit; report added warmup seconds.

findings.claim: The revert reintroduces synchronous, blocking host-memory pinning on the worker initialization critical path. In the parent commit, pinning of the CPU offload buffer (cudaHostRegister over potentially many GB) was done on a background 'CPUTensorPinThread' so worker init could proceed concurrently. This diff removes that thread and instead (a) allocates CPU tensors with pin_memory=pin_memory (True on CUDA), forcing pinned allocation synchronously at torch.zeros() time, and (b) calls pin_mmap_region(mmap_region) inline in CPUOffloadingWorker.__init__. cudaHostRegister / pinned allocation over the full KV offload region can take seconds to tens of seconds for large caches, and this now serially blocks worker startup. The removed comment explicitly stated 'Pinning here would block worker initialization.' Net effect: measurably longer worker/engine startup time, no longer overlapped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPUOffloadingWorker.__init__ wall-clock time with a large num_cpu_blocks (e.g. tens of GB of CPU offload) on CUDA, comparing parent (background-thread pinning) vs this commit (synchronous pinning). Report engine cold-start time to first token.

findings.claim: Because pinning is now on the synchronous init path, the cudaHostRegister cost of multiple offload workers/ranks can no longer overlap with each other or with the MADV_POPULATE_WRITE page-population and gpu_tensor setup, whereas the background-thread version allowed pinning to proceed while the rest of initialization continued. This reduces initialization parallelism in multi-rank/multi-worker deployments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tensor-parallel startup time (multiple ranks) for KV offload enabled, parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: attn_logits is now a view into a single shared workspace buffer reused by every MLA layer/call. This is functionally correct only because vLLM runs attention sequentially on one CUDA stream; if any future path (e.g. dual-batch overlap / multi-stream) issued two MLA decodes concurrently that both draw from the same ubatch workspace slot, the second call would overwrite the first call's split-KV logits before its stage2 reduction consumed them, producing a silent data race. No perf regression today, but the shared-buffer reuse removes the isolation the per-call torch.empty provided.

The workspace is reserved at absolute worst case: B=max_num_seqs, q_num_heads*dcp_world_size, max_splits=min(next_pow2(max_model_len/512), 2*sm_count), and lse_dim=kv_lora_rank+1 in fp32. For long max_model_len and high SM count this fp32 buffer can be tens of MB that stays resident for the whole process even when actual decode batches use far fewer splits/seqs, increasing steady-state reserved GPU memory versus the old transient torch.empty that was freed each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak/steady reserved GPU memory (torch.cuda.memory_reserved) with large max_model_len and max_num_seqs before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: The reservation computes q_num_heads = self.num_heads * self.dcp_world_size while forward_mqa uses q.shape[1] at runtime. If these ever diverge (e.g. head padding or a DCP config where the gathered head count differs), a runtime get_simultaneous would request more bytes than reserved and, since the workspace is locked after warmup, raise instead of growing — turning a mild sizing mismatch into a hard failure on the decode hot path.

The tcgen05 MMA/commit primitives (mma_f16, mma_ts_f16, commit) now each wrap their single NVVM instruction in `with cute.arch.elect_one():`. In kernel_h.py the call sites previously did one coarse `elect_one()` around an entire unrolled MMA loop and now call these primitives directly, so the elect-one lane-selection predicate is materialized per MMA instruction instead of once per loop. For unrolled `range_constexpr` loops issuing many tcgen05_mma ops this adds a redundant per-instruction elect/branch predicate. Impact is small (the tcgen05 issue is single-thread anyway) but it is strictly more predication work than the previous single election.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the GDN prefill h-kernel (Sm100ChunkHKernel) on SM100 comparing per-op vs per-loop elect_one to measure MMA-issue warp overhead.

findings.claim: kernel_o.py still wraps its tcgen05 MMA/commit loops in `with cute.arch.elect_one():` (lines 241, 258, 318, 332), but mma_f16/mma_ts_f16/commit in _tcgen05.py now also each open their own `elect_one()` internally. This produces nested elect_one regions (elect-one inside an already elected single-lane region), duplicating the lane-selection predicate for every tcgen05 op in the O kernel and adding redundant predication overhead versus the pre-change single-thread issue path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile-and-run the Sm100ChunkOKernel and inspect generated SASS/PTX for duplicated elect.sync predicates; benchmark O-kernel MMA-issue latency before/after.

findings.claim: num_warps for the kkt/uw kernel increased from 2+4+4=10 (320 threads/block) to 4+4+4=12 (384 threads/block), and launch now pins min_blocks_per_mp=1. Larger blocks consume more registers/smem per SM which can reduce achievable occupancy if resources are tight; combined with the fixed grid of num_sms//Hv*Hv this hard-limits to one block per SM. If the added warps are not fully utilized this raises per-block resource pressure without proportional throughput gain.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile kkt_inv_uw kernel occupancy and end-to-end GDN prefill throughput with 10 vs 12 warps on target SM100 GPU.

findings.claim: This is a bugfix that RESTORES performance rather than degrading it. Before the change, `_is_wNa8o8_int` matched W8A8 int-quantized models whenever EITHER input OR output activation quant was static int8 (`is_static_int8_in or is_static_int8_out`). Because `_is_wNa8o8_int` is checked earlier in `_get_scheme_from_parts` than the `_is_static_tensor_w8a8` / `_is_dynamic_token_w8a8` checks (line 724 vs 776/783), a standard W8A8 int model (input-only static int8, no output activation quant) was incorrectly routed to `CompressedTensorsWNA8O8Int` (a fake-quant path) instead of `CompressedTensorsW8A8Int8` (the true int8 GEMM path). Fake-quant executes the matmul in the original dtype with dequantized weights/activations, which is substantially slower and uses more memory than the native int8 GEMM. Changing `or` to `and` ensures WNA8O8Int only matches when both input and output activation quant are present, letting real W8A8 int8 models fall through to the fast int8 kernel. The commit therefore fixes a latent throughput/latency regression for W8A8 int-quantized models on all layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a W8A8 channel/tensor static-int8 compressed-tensors model (e.g. int8 quantized Llama) and measure decode throughput/prefill latency before vs after; verify the selected scheme is CompressedTensorsW8A8Int8 (int8 GEMM) rather than CompressedTensorsWNA8O8Int (fake-quant).

findings.claim: This commit re-enables the FlashInfer persistent autotune cache by default (use_persistent_cache=True), reverting the guard added in PR #43119. The removed TODO comment explicitly warned that the persistent file cache suffers collisions (e.g. use_8x4_sf_layout=True/False) that cause 'invalid tactics to be chosen'. Choosing an invalid/suboptimal tactic means FlashInfer ops at runtime may use a slower kernel implementation than the true best one, degrading attention/GEMM throughput. Re-enabling relies entirely on the flashinfer 0.6.13 bump actually fixing the upstream cache-collision bug; if the fix is incomplete, runtime kernel performance regresses relative to per-rank autotuning.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FlashInfer attention/GEMM microbenchmarks on Hopper/Blackwell comparing per-rank autotune (old path) vs persistent-cache leader-broadcast path (new path) to confirm selected tactics match the true fastest kernel; measure decode/prefill throughput end-to-end.

findings.claim: For DeepEP all2all backends the code keeps use_persistent_cache=False so every rank runs _dummy_run autotune. The comment notes DeepEP dispatch/combine can timeout when only rank 0 autotunes and other ranks race ahead. This is a hang/timeout mitigation, but for non-DeepEP configs the new path only autotunes on rank 0 and broadcasts; if a non-DeepEP collective is also latency-sensitive during warmup, the same lead/lag skew that motivated the DeepEP carve-out could in principle cause slow barriers. Worth confirming the broadcast/barrier sequencing does not stall other ranks for the full leader-only tuning duration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-rank warmup wall-clock and barrier wait time on a multi-GPU non-DeepEP deployment to ensure non-leader ranks don't idle-block excessively while rank 0 tunes.

findings.claim: Gemma3's encoder cudagraph methods (prepare_encoder_cudagraph_capture_inputs, prepare_encoder_cudagraph_replay_buffers, encoder_cudagraph_forward) omit the `path` parameter that the EncoderCudaGraphManager invokes them with. The runner calls `prepare_encoder_cudagraph_capture_inputs(token_budget, max_batch_size, max_frames_per_batch, device, dtype, path)`, `encoder_cudagraph_forward({...}, path=path)`, and `prepare_encoder_cudagraph_replay_buffers(mm_kwargs, max_batch_size, max_frames_per_batch, path)`. If the deployed manager passes `path` positionally/keyword, these signatures will raise TypeError, causing encoder cudagraph capture/replay to fail. Depending on fallback behavior this either crashes or silently falls back to eager, defeating the intended cudagraph speedup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/models/multimodal/generation/test_vit_cudagraph.py::gemma3 and confirm cudagraph capture/replay succeeds rather than falling back to eager.

findings.claim: select_encoder_cudagraph_items builds a Python-level cumulative-patch prefix sum by iterating over all num_patches every call and then constructs the selected pixel_values via a Python list comprehension of tensor slices fed into torch.cat. This is executed per finalized batch on every forward (not just at capture). For many images this adds per-request Python/host overhead and an extra full copy of pixel_values via torch.cat, increasing latency in the encoder path relative to a vectorized index_select/segment approach.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark select_encoder_cudagraph_items with e.g. 32 images each with several patches, measuring host time and extra memory of the torch.cat copy vs the total encoder step time.

findings.claim: encoder_cudagraph_forward runs self.vision_tower on the padded pixel_values buffer sized to num_images = token_budget // mm_tokens_per_image (up to max_batch_size). With a token budget of 512 and mm_tokens_per_image=256 this is small, but for larger budgets the captured graph processes a full padded batch of images each replay even when the actual batch is smaller, wasting GPU compute on padded/dummy images (inherent to budget-based cudagraph but worth confirming waste stays low).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare encoder throughput/GPU utilization for single-image requests under the 512-token budget cudagraph vs eager to measure padding waste.

findings.claim: In DeepseekV2DecoderLayer.forward, when use_sequence_parallel_moe is on and the incoming hidden_states are NOT yet sequence-parallel (input_is_sequence_parallel is False, e.g. the first MoE layer after a dense/non-SP layer), the code first all-gathers hidden_states to the full length (tensor_model_parallel_all_gather on line ~1268), runs attention/o_proj without reducing (reduce_results=False), and then immediately reduce-scatters the result. This means for the transition layer an extra all-gather is added on the critical path compared to the previous chunk-based path, partially offsetting the reduce-scatter savings. For all-MoE layer stacks with no dense layers this fires only once, but for configs with dense-to-MoE boundaries or aux_hidden_state layers it can recur.

Each MoE layer with use_sequence_parallel_moe pads hidden_states with torch.nn.functional.pad when the token count is not divisible by tensor-parallel world size, allocating a fresh padded tensor per layer per forward. For decode batches whose token count is rarely divisible by world size this adds a per-layer allocation + copy of the full hidden_states before the reduce-scatter. Minor but repeated across all MoE layers.

The new per-layer guard in DeepseekV2Model.forward all-gathers cat([hidden_states, residual]) (a 2x-hidden-size tensor) whenever a preceding layer left the state sequence-parallel but the current layer does not use SP MoE. Concatenating and all-gathering the doubled-width tensor, then splitting, adds a collective + two copies at every SP->non-SP transition; for models interleaving dense and MoE layers this recurs each boundary rather than once.

For aux_hidden_state layers (speculative/eagle drafting) an additional tensor_model_parallel_all_gather of hidden_states+residual is performed per collected aux layer when the state is sequence-parallel. This adds one all-gather per aux layer per forward that did not exist before; impacts eagle3 aux-hidden collection throughput specifically.

Adding Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION to the auto priority list `_get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss()` makes the emulation backend eligible for *automatic* selection. The emulation experts (OCP_MXQuantizationEmulationTritonExperts) dequantize both w1 and w2 weights on the fly on *every forward pass* (see apply(): `self._dequantize_weights(w1, ...)` / `w2`), whereas native quantized MoE keeps weights packed. Because EMULATION's `_supports_quant_scheme` unconditionally returns True and it inherits TritonExperts' `_supports_current_device` (CUDA-alike/XPU), it will be chosen automatically whenever the earlier-priority native backends report unsupported — on many CUDA/ROCm devices this previously raised NotImplementedError instead of silently falling back to the slow path. This can cause a large, silent throughput regression (per-forward weight dequantization + BF16 GEMM instead of native FP4) with much higher memory use (dequantized BF16 weights are ~4x the packed FP4 size).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a GPT-OSS / MXFP4 MoE model on a device without native MXFP4 support and compare tokens/sec and peak GPU memory with vs. without EMULATION in the auto priority list; also verify auto-selection does not pick EMULATION on devices where a native backend is supported.

findings.claim: EMULATION is appended at the end of the priority list, which is the correct position (lowest priority). However, note that if any native backend fails its is_supported_config check for a given deployment, the auto path will now land on the emulation fallback rather than raising, so operators lose the explicit signal that they are running an emulated/slow path unless they read the warning_once log. This trades a hard error for a silent slow default.

The HPC MoE entrypoints (`hpc_fuse_moe` / `hpc_fuse_moe_blockwise`) are NOT registered as torch.library custom ops — the `@torch.library.custom_op` / `register_fake` decorators are all commented out, and the wrappers directly call `from hpc import fuse_moe`. Every other accelerated MoE/attention path in this codebase (e.g. flashinfer.py's `direct_register_custom_op` / `torch.library.custom_op`) registers a custom op precisely so that torch.compile / Inductor and CUDA-graph capture can treat the kernel as an opaque, graph-safe node. An unregistered opaque Python call inside the compiled MoE forward will cause a graph break (or fail piecewise-cudagraph capture / require full fall-back), losing the compilation + CUDA-graph replay benefits for the whole MoE layer. This degrades throughput and increases per-step CPU overhead on the hot decode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode throughput and per-step CPU launch overhead for an FP8 MoE model (e.g. Qwen3-235B-A22B-FP8) with the HPC backend under torch.compile + CUDA graphs enabled vs. a custom-op-registered variant; check for graph breaks via TORCH_LOGS=graph_breaks.

findings.claim: `workspace_shapes` returns `workspace1 = (M, K)` (hidden dim K) while the first (gate_up) GEMM intermediate is normally sized to the intermediate dim N (typically 2*N for gate+up). The docstring states workspace13 'must be large enough to hold the result of either expert gemm'. Because the HPC kernel is fully fused and writes directly into `output`, the workspace may actually be unused, so this is likely harmless — but if any generic modular-kernel path allocates/zeros this workspace based on the returned shape, an incorrectly small (M,K) allocation vs a real (M,2N) requirement could be either wasted memory or a latent correctness/perf issue. Worth confirming the fused kernel truly ignores workspace13.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument the modular kernel to log workspace13/workspace2 allocation sizes for the HPC backend and verify no oversized/undersized allocation relative to actual kernel usage.

findings.claim: `supports_chunking()` returns True and `supports_expert_map()` returns False. If the fused HPC kernel internally handles all experts but chunking splits tokens, the per-chunk re-invocation of a fully-fused grouped GEMM (which pays fixed per-launch overhead for expert routing/alignment) could reduce efficiency at small chunk sizes vs. a single fused launch. Confirm the chunk size chosen for HPC does not fragment the grouped GEMM into many small launches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep TP chunk sizes with the HPC MoE backend and measure GPU utilization / kernel launch count to ensure chunking does not underutilize the grouped GEMM.

findings.claim: DeepseekV32Attention.o_proj is now created with reduce_results=False, delegating the attention TP all-reduce to fused_allreduce_rms_norm. If the flashinfer fused-allreduce fast path is unavailable (flashinfer_trtllm_fused_allreduce_norm is None, or _can_use_flashinfer returns False for the current token count/dtype), the code falls back to an explicit tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce + separate RMSNorm. This fallback splits what the compile-time allreduce_rms_fusion pass would otherwise fuse into two kernels plus a discrete collective per attention layer, which is slower than the pre-change RowParallelLinear(reduce_results=True) path that at least did a single reduction. The manual eager fused_allreduce_rms_norm only wins on the SM100/flashinfer-available path; on any config where the fast path is not selected it is a net regression relative to the compiler-fused baseline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-throughput and per-layer attention latency on TP>1 with and without the flashinfer fused-allreduce fast path available (e.g. non-SM100 GPU) vs the reduce_results=True baseline.

findings.claim: The whole attention (cache writes, fused_norm_rope, fused_q, sparse indexer, forward_mqa, and the V up-proj bmm) is now inside a single @eager_break_during_capture _fused_attention. In PIECEWISE (prefill) capture this entire region runs eagerly, whereas the pre-change code kept the V up-projection and o_proj as captured metadata-independent GEMMs outside the eager break. Moving the final torch.bmm(x, self.W_UV) inside the eager break removes it from CUDA-graph capture in the prefill path, adding per-call Python/launch overhead for that GEMM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill latency (PIECEWISE cudagraph) comparing captured vs eager V up-projection GEMM.

findings.claim: _DUMMY_CACHE in kernels.py keys on (shape, dtype, device) and never evicts. In practice the shapes are fixed so growth is bounded, but the cached dummy tensors are held for the process lifetime; if shapes vary (e.g. differing batch dims passed as tensor shapes) this leaks GPU memory monotonically.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Monitor GPU memory over many forwards with varying batch sizes.

findings.claim: In moe_runner.py, skip_final_all_reduce now suppresses both the shared-expert all-reduce and the late combined all-reduce so the caller fuses the reduction into the next RMSNorm. This is only correct/beneficial when the caller actually performs the fused reduction; if skip_final_all_reduce is set on a path where the downstream fused_allreduce_rms_norm falls back or is not wired, the partial per-rank sum is used unreduced (a correctness hazard) or the reduction re-appears as an extra un-fused collective, negating the intended fusion savings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end TP>1 MoE-layer output correctness and per-layer collective count with skip_final_all_reduce enabled.

findings.claim: The newly added `img_output_tokens` and `patch_output_tokens` are `@property` methods that recompute `_compute_spatial_tokens` (with math.sqrt / integer division) on every access. They are accessed multiple times per call in `get_encoder_cudagraph_item_specs`, `postprocess_encoder_output`, and `prepare_encoder_cudagraph_capture_inputs`. Previously the values were computed once into a local variable per method. Because these depend only on immutable config fields, they could be cached (e.g. `functools.cached_property`). This is a tiny CPU cost on the host side and does not affect GPU work, so the impact is negligible, but it is a minor regression versus the previous local-variable caching pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated access to img_output_tokens/patch_output_tokens vs cached_property; measure encoder scheduling CPU overhead per batch.

findings.claim: Switching Step3-VL to dual-path CUDA graphs captures two independent graph sets (global + local), each across a power-of-2 budget ladder, instead of a single combined graph set. This increases the number of captured CUDA graphs and thus GPU memory reserved by the graph pool and total capture (warm-up) time at startup. This is an expected trade-off of the dual-path design (matching deepseek_ocr) and generally improves per-request efficiency by avoiding wasted budget from padding both paths together, but the capture-time memory/latency cost is worth noting.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure startup encoder graph capture time and peak reserved GPU memory before vs after enabling dual-path for Step3-VL.

findings.claim: This change replaces the weight-shuffle path in `convert_weight_to_mxfp4_moe_kernel_format` for the AITER_MXFP4_BF16 backend. The prior code used `e8m0_shuffle` on the scales; the new code uses aiter's `shuffle_scale` with `num_experts`/interleave flags. This is a one-time (per model load) weight-preparation routine, so it does not run in the hot inference path and does not directly affect steady-state throughput/latency. The whole PR is framed as a runtime performance *optimization* (the intent is that these new shuffle layouts feed a faster aiter MoE kernel for DeepSeekV4). The changed code itself is not a per-token/per-step cost, so runtime performance impact is neutral-to-positive as long as the resulting kernel-format weights are consumed correctly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure DeepSeekV4 MoE decode/prefill throughput on ROCm with the AITER_MXFP4_BF16 backend before/after this commit to confirm the intended kernel speedup and that the new scale/weight layout is consumed by the faster aiter path.

findings.claim: The new code sets `os.environ["AITER_BF16_FP8_MOE_BOUND"] = "0"` as a global process-wide side-effect inside the weight-conversion function. Setting this env var may change the aiter kernel dispatch/bounds behavior for ALL aiter MoE layers in the process (not just DeepSeekV4), potentially forcing a slower/unbounded code path or disabling a bound-based fast path. Because it is only read by aiter at kernel-launch time and applied globally, it could unintentionally affect the runtime performance of other MoE layers that share the process. The comment ("Necessary for AITER side from crashing") indicates it is a correctness workaround, so if the '0' value disables an optimization it would be a real slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare aiter MoE kernel latency with AITER_BF16_FP8_MOE_BOUND unset vs '0' on ROCm to determine whether forcing this env var degrades kernel performance for models beyond DeepSeekV4.

findings.claim: When VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK is set to "warn", `with_gpu_sync_check` calls `torch.cuda.set_sync_debug_mode("warn")` around every `execute_model`/`sample_tokens` invocation (i.e. every decode/prefill step). PyTorch's sync-debug mode installs per-op checks in the CUDA dispatch path, which measurably slows every kernel launch for the duration of the call. This is a per-step hot-path cost that persists for the whole run in "warn" mode (unlike "error" mode where it aborts). It is opt-in and off by default, so blast radius is limited to users who explicitly enable the debug env var, but such users should be warned it is not a production-safe setting.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run decode throughput (tokens/s) for a small model with VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK=warn vs unset and compare steady-state per-step latency.

findings.claim: Wrapping `execute_model` and `sample_tokens` with `with_gpu_sync_check` adds a Python function wrapper plus two `torch.cuda.get/set_sync_debug_mode` calls per step when the gate is enabled. When VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK is unset the decorator returns the original function unchanged (zero overhead), so this only affects the opt-in debug path; overhead is small relative to model execution but non-zero.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark per-step CPU dispatch overhead with the gate enabled vs a bare function call.

findings.claim: `trigger_inductor_lazy_init` is invoked unconditionally during warmup whenever compilation mode != NONE and backend == inductor, adding a one-time inductor lazy-init (SFDP pattern matcher etc.) with CUDA syncs. This is a one-time startup cost intended to move syncs out of the runtime path, so it is acceptable, but it does add startup latency even when VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK is unset (the feature it supports is otherwise inactive), so the eager init could be gated behind the env var to avoid slowing normal startup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup/warmup time with vs without the trigger_inductor_lazy_init call for a compiled model.

findings.claim: The `_fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_kernel` is launched with a 2D grid `(q.shape[0], q.shape[1])` = (num_tokens, num_heads) and `num_warps=1`, i.e. one warp (32 threads) per (token, head). Each program only processes 128 elements of head_dim, and with a single warp the 128-element loads/stores and the reductions (`tl.max`, `tl.abs`) are serialized within a single warp with no ILP across warps. For the decode path (few tokens, many heads) this can under-occupy the SM. If num_heads is small in decode this may reduce GPU utilization relative to a wider-block design. This is a suggestion pending measurement; the commit reports net E2E gain, so it is likely net positive but the single-warp choice is a possible bottleneck.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fused_indexer_q_rope_quant across (num_tokens, num_heads) shapes typical of decode vs prefill, comparing num_warps=1 vs 2/4 and measuring kernel latency + SM occupancy.

findings.claim: In the new fused path, RoPE for K is computed via a full `rotary_emb(positions, q_dummy, k_pe.unsqueeze(1))` call where `q_dummy = torch.empty_like(k_pe.unsqueeze(1))` is allocated and rotated only to be discarded. This allocates a throwaway tensor every forward and does the rope math on it. In the non-fused branch the same rotary_emb was applied to the real q, so here it is wasted compute+alloc. For MQA K (single head) it is small, but it is a per-layer, per-step allocation and unnecessary rope work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the Indexer.forward fused branch to measure time/allocations spent in the q_dummy rotary_emb call at decode batch sizes.

findings.claim: The fused branch adds `k_pe = k_pe.reshape(-1,1,rope_dim)` then `k = torch.cat([k_pe.squeeze(-2), k_nope], dim=-1)`, an extra concat allocating a new K tensor each step. The pre-change unfused path also did a cat, so this is not clearly worse, but the reshape/squeeze/cat chain introduces contiguity copies that could be avoided if k were written in-place. Low confidence it is a regression versus baseline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare memory allocations and latency of the k_pe reshape+cat sequence vs an in-place rope write.

findings.claim: EplbState.prepare_forward is now invoked on every model forward pass (from GPUModelRunner.execute_model and every speculator path). Even when EPLB recording is not currently active (recording is gated separately by should_record_tensor), it unconditionally calls compute_hash_cached(model_config) (a dict lookup) and issues one or more device tensor .fill_() operations (num_unpadded_tokens_tensors[i].fill_(...)). Each .fill_() launches a small host-to-device scalar copy/kernel per forward pass per ubatch, adding CPU launch overhead and a GPU op to the critical path of every step, whereas before there was no per-forward EPLB work at all. For small-batch decode steps where each token is cheap, this fixed per-step overhead is not negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput/latency benchmark with EPLB enabled but recording disabled, comparing steps/sec before and after this commit at small batch sizes.

findings.claim: compute_hash_cached uses a module-level dict _config_hash_cache keyed by id(config) with no eviction. Because id() values are reused after an object is garbage-collected, a short-lived config object could collide with a later object at the same address and return a stale hash (correctness risk); and for long-lived configs the cache entries are never removed, so the dict grows across the lifetime of the process. In practice configs are long-lived singletons so growth is bounded, but the id()-keyed design is fragile if any transient SupportsHash object is ever passed.

The Triton router kernel now performs an extra tl.load(num_unpadded_tokens_ptr) and a comparison per block when HAS_NUM_UNPADDED is set. This is a tiny per-block scalar load (broadcast) and unlikely to be measurable, but it does add a global memory read to the hot MoE routing kernel that runs on every MoE layer forward.

add_model stores model_states keyed by model_config.compute_hash() (uncached, expensive JSON+SHA-256) while prepare_forward looks up via compute_hash_cached(model_config). The cache is populated by the lookup path; the first prepare_forward for each config still pays the full compute_hash() cost, but subsequent calls are cheap as intended. The main residual concern is that compute_hash() (JSON serialization + SHA-256) was previously never on the per-forward path at all; the caching only helps because add_model and prepare_forward may be given the same object identity. If a different-but-equal config object were ever passed, the cache would miss and re-run the expensive hash on the forward path.

The `from aiter.ops.flydsl.moe_common import GateMode` import was moved out of the `if quant_config.use_mxfp4_w4a16:` branch (where it only ran for mxfp4-w4a16 models, inside a try/except) to the unconditional top of the non-tkw1 `else` block. It now executes on EVERY call to `rocm_aiter_fused_experts`, i.e. once per MoE layer per forward step, for all quant methods (fp8, unquantized, etc.), not just mxfp4-w4a16. Although a re-import of an already-loaded module is a cached `sys.modules` dict lookup plus attribute binding, doing it on a per-layer decode hot path adds repeated small Python overhead that was previously incurred only for one model family (and cached to a local variable per call anyway). Hoisting this import to module scope would remove the per-call cost entirely.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rocm_aiter_fused_experts dispatch overhead (CPU-side Python time) with the import at call site vs hoisted to module scope, across many small decode iterations on a ROCm MoE model.

findings.claim: In FusedTopKBiasRouter._compute_routing, when shared-expert fusion is active the router allocates two fresh tensors (torch.arange for shared_ids and torch.full for shared_w) and then performs two torch.cat operations on every forward pass. The shared_ids and shared_w tensors are constant across all forwards (they depend only on global_num_experts, num_fused_shared_experts, shared_expert_weight, batch size m, and dtype/device), yet they are rebuilt every call, and the torch.cat forces a full copy of topk_ids/topk_weights each time. Caching the constant slot tensors (keyed by m) and/or preallocating the concatenated buffer would remove this recurring allocation/copy from the routing hot path. The cost is small relative to the grouped GEMM but is incurred once per MoE layer per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark FusedTopKBiasRouter._compute_routing with num_fused_shared_experts>0 vs =0 across decode (m small) and prefill (m large) batch sizes, measuring per-call latency and allocator activity; compare against a cached-slot-tensor variant.

findings.claim: fused_moe_mxfp8_native now bins by num_align_experts = w13.shape[0] (routed + fused shared experts) instead of global_num_experts, and the router appends T*num_fused_shared_experts extra top-k slots. This increases both topk_ids.numel() and num_experts inputs to moe_align_block_size, so max_num_tokens_padded = topk_ids.numel() + num_experts*(block_size-1) grows and the sorted_ids/expert_ids buffers plus the grouped-GEMM M dimension increase. This is the intended cost of fusing the shared expert into the grouped GEMM (replacing separate dense-MLP launches) and is expected to be a net win, but it does enlarge the aligned token buffers and the number of GEMM tiles processed versus the pre-change routed-only alignment.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end MoE layer latency/throughput on MiniMax-M3 mxfp8 (gfx950) with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_FUSION_SHARED_EXPERTS on vs off, at decode and prefill batch sizes, to confirm the fusion is a net speedup over the separate shared-MLP path.

findings.claim: PYNVVIDEOCODEC_MAX_RETAINED_DECODERS = 1 means only a single PyNvVideoCodec decoder slot exists per process. Every GPU video decode (both metadata reads via _read_source_metadata and frame decodes via _decode_to_pinned_host) borrows this one slot through _borrow_decoder_slot, whose Condition.wait() blocks all other threads until the slot is returned. Under concurrent serving load — where the renderer runs MM preprocessing across renderer_num_workers threads — all video decode requests are fully serialized on this single decoder, eliminating the concurrent-decode throughput that the opencv/pyav backends explicitly provide. This is a severe throughput regression for multi-request video workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrently decode N videos (e.g. 8–32 simultaneous requests) via the pynvvideocodec backend and measure aggregate decode throughput vs. opencv/pyav; expect near-serial scaling (throughput flat as concurrency increases).

findings.claim: decode_frames_pynvvideocodec borrows the decoder slot twice: once in _read_source_metadata (reads metadata, releases slot) and again in _decode_to_pinned_host. Because the slot is released between the two phases, a concurrent request can borrow the single slot in between and reconfigure the decoder to a different source. When the original thread re-borrows for the decode phase it finds source_path != file_path and must call reconfigure_decoder (or fall back to a full SimpleDecoder rebuild — CUVID parser + surface-pool allocation, described in-code as the dominant per-request cost). This causes redundant, expensive decoder reconfiguration/rebuilds under concurrency that would be avoided if the slot were held across metadata+decode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Interleave decodes of two distinct videos across 2+ threads and count reconfigure_decoder/_construct calls; compare against holding the slot for the full metadata+decode lifetime.

findings.claim: In _decode_to_pinned_host, stream.synchronize() is called while both the single decoder slot and (in the caller) the GPU memory lease are held. The synchronous device-to-host copy blocks the whole critical section on the sole decoder slot, so the D2H transfer time of one request adds directly to the wait latency of every other queued video request rather than overlapping.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request tail latency for pynvvideocodec decode under concurrency and attribute time spent in stream.synchronize() while the slot is held.

findings.claim: MultiModalGPUMemoryPool.acquire() blocks on Condition.wait() with no timeout, and pynvvideocodec decode runs on the renderer's shared _mm_executor (== _executor), which also serves tokenization and all other multimodal preprocessing. When mm_ipc_gpu_memory_gb gating is enabled and several video requests block waiting for GPU memory (compounded by the single-decoder serialization), they can occupy all renderer_num_workers threads, starving unrelated text/tokenization and non-video MM preprocessing and increasing latency across the whole frontend.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable mm_ipc_gpu_memory_gb with a small budget, saturate with large video decodes, and measure latency of concurrent text-only/tokenize requests through the same renderer executor.

findings.claim: decode_frames_pynvvideocodec writes the full raw video bytes to a temporary file on disk (tempfile.mkstemp + write) on every request because PyNvVideoCodec's SimpleDecoder is opened by file path. This adds a synchronous disk write and unlink per video request, adding latency and filesystem I/O proportional to video size that the in-memory opencv/pyav paths avoid.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-request decode latency of large videos writing to tmpfs vs. spinning/networked disk to quantify the temp-file write/unlink overhead.

findings.claim: The relaxed compatibility filter in get_configs_compute_bound now keeps configs where BLOCK_SIZE_N < block_n (e.g. N=64 or N=32 with block_n=128). This is functionally correct (the kernel indexes scales via offs_bn // group_n), but the smaller N tiles increase the number of thread blocks along N and the number of separate scale loads per element. If the benchmark selects such a tile for a mid/large batch shape not exercised in the tuning sweep, it could pick a tile that is worse than the previous N=128 default. The risk is bounded because it only affects benchmark-generated candidate lists, not the shipped configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run benchmark_moe.py block-fp8 sweep across M in {1,8,64,512,4096} and confirm the newly-allowed N<block_n tiles are only ever selected for the low-M rows.

findings.claim: The new default-config path forces BLOCK_SIZE_N=64 for M<=8 (CUDA, cap 90). Combined with BLOCK_SIZE_M=16 this keeps enable_swap_ab() true (needs BLOCK_SIZE_M<64 and BLOCK_SIZE_N>=64), so the swap-AB fast path stays active for decode. However the tuned E=256,N=256 JSON now uses BLOCK_SIZE_N=32 for M=1/2, which is < 64 and would disable swap_ab for those shapes on H200 -- the swap-AB kernel is what keeps small-M efficient on Hopper, so if 32-wide tiles were only measured without accounting for the loss of swap_ab, decode latency for that specific shape could regress vs a 64-wide swap-AB tile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode (M=1,2) for the E=256/N=256 H200 shape comparing the shipped BLOCK_SIZE_N=32 (swap_ab off) vs a BLOCK_SIZE_N=64 (swap_ab on) config to confirm the 32-wide tile is actually faster.

findings.claim: get_default_config now hard-codes block_n=64 and num_stages=4/3 for M<=8 on all CUDA capability-90 devices whenever block_shape[0] % 64 == 0. This is only validated on H100/H200. For other Hopper-class or future SM90 devices without a matching tuned JSON, the fall-through default now differs from the previous (block_n=block_shape[0], num_stages=3) and could be slightly worse for shapes where a 128-wide N tile was better; this is a generalization risk rather than a proven regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare get_default_config's new M<=8 branch (N=64) vs old (N=block_shape[0]) on an SM90 device without a tuned config file, across a range of E/N block-fp8 MoE shapes.

findings.claim: The scalar fallback path (used for non-contiguous hidden, e.g. the transpose layout, or unaligned/odd hidden sizes) launches with grid=dim3(num_tokens) and block=min(hidden_size,1024), i.e. one block per token doing strided per-element gathers. For small hidden dims (e.g. 128) this yields only ~128 threads/block and one memory op per element via VLLM_LDG with stride_hidden!=1, giving poor memory coalescing and low occupancy. This mirrors the pre-change design for the non-templated case so it is not a regression, but for the newly-supported non-contiguous inputs (transpose) it can be substantially slower than the vectorized path and than a plain ATen sum reduction. Consider a grid-stride 2D mapping for the scalar path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: moe_sum with transpose layout (m in {1,33,222}, k in {128,1024}, topk=8, bf16/fp32) comparing kernel time vs torch.sum(dim=1)

findings.claim: moe_sum_vec_dynamic_kernel's accumulation loop over topk is not unrolled (topk is runtime), unlike the templated moe_sum_vec_kernel. For topk values not in the specialized set {1,2,4,6,8,9} (e.g. 3,5,7,>9) the loop cannot be unrolled and the per-vector accumulation loses some ILP. Impact is small since most production topk values are covered by templates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: moe_sum with topk=3,5,7 vs topk=4,6,8 on contiguous bf16 input to measure dynamic vs templated throughput difference

findings.claim: All paths now accumulate in fp32 (previously the templated fp16/bf16 path accumulated directly in scalar_t). This improves accuracy but adds per-element static_cast to/from float; for the common bf16 case this adds conversion work in-register. The cost is negligible relative to memory bandwidth (kernel is memory-bound), so no meaningful regression expected, but the accumulator array acc[VEC] uses more registers which could marginally affect occupancy at high VEC/TOPK.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare bf16 moe_sum kernel throughput before/after commit at large num_tokens to confirm no bandwidth regression

findings.claim: For a request taking its first decode step (num_new_tokens == 1), the change pads num_new_tokens to 1 + num_spec_tokens and inserts placeholder (-1) spec draft tokens. This deliberately schedules and computes num_spec_tokens extra query positions per such request that carry no useful draft (placeholder -1), so the worker does forward compute / attention over positions that produce no accepted tokens. This is intentional to keep the CUDA graph uniform (avoid recapture / eager fallback), but it is a real trade-off: on steps where the padded request would otherwise have run as a cheap 1-token decode, throughput/compute is increased by roughly num_spec_tokens extra positions for that request. Net effect is positive only when it avoids a costly mixed-shape / graph-break; in workloads that seldom hit the graph-break path it is pure wasted GPU work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Spec-decode serving benchmark with a mix of prefix-cache-hit requests entering their first decode step alongside existing speculative decodes; measure tokens/s and per-step latency with and without the padding to confirm the padding does not regress steady-state decode throughput.

findings.claim: When padding pushes num_new_tokens over token_budget or max_model_len, the code executes `break`, abandoning the rest of the waiting-queue scheduling loop for this step. Because the unpadded request would only have needed 1 token (which likely fit the budget), the padding decision can cause the scheduler to stop admitting otherwise-schedulable requests this step, reducing batch occupancy and delaying admission by a step. A `continue`/skip-of-this-request would be less disruptive than aborting the whole loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler microbenchmark with a near-full token_budget and several waiting decode requests; measure number of requests admitted per step and TTFT/queueing latency with the break vs. skipping only the padded request.

findings.claim: Placeholder draft tokens are set to -1 (`[-1] * self.num_spec_tokens`). These are guaranteed-reject drafts, so the padded request cannot accept any speculative tokens on this step and effectively runs a plain 1-token decode wrapped in a spec-shaped batch. This is expected behavior, but it means the padding provides zero speculative benefit for that request's step while consuming spec-shaped compute; if such first-decode-step requests are frequent, effective spec-decode acceptance/throughput is diluted.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure spec-decode acceptance rate and effective tokens/s in a prefix-cache-heavy workload where many requests enter their first decode step, comparing against a variant that does not pad.

findings.claim: The fused kernel loads the packed weight byte, extracts both nibbles, decodes E2M1 (`_e2m1_inline`), loads and applies the FP8 block scale, and does `tl.trans(tl.interleave(...))` every time a weight tile is visited inside the K-loop. In a MoE GEMM, each weight tile is re-read/re-decoded once per BLOCK_SIZE_M row-block that maps to the same expert (i.e. once per pid_m). For an expert receiving T>BLOCK_SIZE_M tokens the same weight bytes are dequantized ceil(T/BLOCK_SIZE_M) times. This is intrinsic to fused weight-only dequant GEMMs and is generally a net win vs. materializing the whole dequantized weight, but for decode/low-token-count workloads (T small, N,K large) the on-the-fly decode + interleave + transpose overhead per tile can be higher than the previous approach of a single dequant pass, especially since the triton config is an untuned bf16 config (see below).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end MoE layer latency/throughput of the fused NVFP4-emulation kernel vs the pre-change dequantize_to_dtype + TritonExperts path across (a) prefill (large M) and (b) decode (M=1) for a real NVFP4 MoE model (e.g. Qwen3-30B-A3B-NVFP4).

findings.claim: The fused kernel reuses `try_get_optimal_moe_config(..., self.quant_config.config_name(hidden_states.dtype), ...)`, i.e. the tuned tiling config for a plain BF16 MoE GEMM. This fused kernel does substantially more work per tile (nibble unpack, E2M1 decode, FP8 scale load/mul, interleave, transpose) and has different register/shared-memory pressure, so the BF16-optimal BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, num_warps, num_stages are unlikely to be optimal here. The code itself flags this with a TODO that benchmark_moe.py cannot tune these hyperparameters. Suboptimal tiling can measurably reduce GPU utilization and throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, num_warps, num_stages for the fused NVFP4 emulation kernel at representative (M,N,K,E,topk) and compare against the default BF16 config to quantify the achievable throughput gap.

findings.claim: Weight and scale offsets are computed with `.to(tl.int64)` (offs_bn, offs_token, off_experts) and the B-tile is loaded N-major then materialized via `tl.trans(tl.interleave(low_scaled, high_scaled))`. The interleave doubles the K width in registers (BLOCK_SIZE_N x BLOCK_SIZE_K fp32) before transpose+cast, and int64 index arithmetic increases register usage, which can lower occupancy versus a plain BF16 dot. This is speculative without profiling but is a plausible utilization regressor for large BLOCK_SIZE_N/K.
