findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile achieved occupancy and register spills (ncu) for the fused kernel at typical tile sizes; compare to the standard fused_moe triton kernel.

findings.claim: The new WVSPLIT_TILE dispatch macro calls on_gfx1151() (and on_gfx1x()) at kernel-launch time to select the tile configuration. on_gfx1151() memoizes its query via a function-local static bool, so after the first call it is a cheap branch. There is negligible added host-side cost per launch; not a runtime GPU-perf concern. Included only as a note that the runtime arch checks execute on every wvSplitK call rather than being resolved at compile time, but the cost is a single already-computed static comparison.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark host dispatch overhead of wvSplitK across many small launches on gfx1151 vs. previous build.

findings.claim: This is a device-specific autotuning change: on gfx1151 the new WVSPLIT_TILE picks smaller YTILE/UNRL configs (mostly YTILE=1, UNRL in {1,2,4}) than the previous WVSPLIT_TILE_CFG default (which used YTILE up to 4). If the heuristic thresholds (e.g. K_in%1024==512, sYT<=26, fit_lds) are miscalibrated for some shapes not covered by the AMD tuning, those shapes could regress vs. the prior generic config. This is speculative; on non-gfx1151 hardware behavior is unchanged (falls through to the original WVSPLIT_TILE_CFG paths). No degradation is provable from the code alone.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep wvSplitK over a grid of (M,N,K) shapes on a gfx1151 device comparing throughput before/after this commit, especially near the branch boundaries K%1024==512, K in {1024,2048}, and sYT around 26/40.

findings.claim: In CodecQ3::recv, the dequantization of int-to-fp16 values goes through packed_from_int16_pair<T> for BOTH half and bfloat16. For the half path this calls __int2half_rn twice per 32-bit lane (i.e. 8 integer->half hardware conversions per atom, plus __halves2half2). The sibling codecs (CodecQ4/CodecQ8) deliberately avoid this for the half case by using a magic-constant bit trick (int32 | kHalf2_1024 then a single packed_add<half> with kHalf2_1032/1152), which turns the whole 8-value unpack into a couple of packed vector ops. Using the slower per-element conversion path in the INT3 decode inner loop (executed kRankAtoms times per block over the entire message) reduces decode throughput relative to what the other codecs achieve, partially eroding the communication-volume savings INT3 is meant to provide.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark qr_all_reduce with quant_mode=INT3 vs INT4 on TP2, fp16 dtype, over message sizes 1MB-256MB; compare achieved GB/s and per-call latency. Compare a variant of CodecQ3::recv that uses the kHalf2_1024 magic-constant unpack for the half path.

findings.claim: CodecQ3::send/recv split each 3-bit value into a 2-bit and a 1-bit payload stored in two separate regions (uint16 q2 region + uint8 q1 region), and the pack/unpack loops run 8 scalar shift/mask iterations per lane rather than the vectorized packed ops used by CodecQ4. This extra scalar bit-shuffling (noted in the commit message itself as pack/unpack overhead) is the reason INT3 is restricted to TP2; on the supported TP2 path it still adds ALU work in the hot per-atom loop that can make INT3 no faster (or slower) than INT4 at small/medium message sizes where the kernel is compute/latency bound rather than bandwidth bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On TP2 fp16, sweep message sizes across the _QR_MIN_SIZE threshold (1MB) and compare INT3 vs INT4 vs FP latency to confirm INT3 actually wins at the configured threshold and does not regress smaller sizes.

findings.claim: CodecQ3 uses a q1 payload region written as individual uint8 stores (*q1w_ptr = q1w, a plain 1-byte store, not a nontemporal/vectorized store like the q2 payload). Byte-granular writes to the shared IPC buffer produce sub-word, poorly-coalesced transactions across the 256 threads (each thread writes a distinct byte at kRankTileQ1Offset+thread), which is less efficient than the 32-bit/128-bit stores the other codecs use for their payloads and can lower effective write bandwidth on the send side.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the send phase of the INT3 kernel (rocprof) for the q1 byte-store region vs the q2 halfword-store region; compare store transaction counts and stalls against CodecQ4's 32-bit payload store.

findings.claim: Adding SWAP_AB as a new tl.constexpr parameter to fused_moe_kernel adds another specialization axis to Triton's kernel cache. Since SWAP_AB can be both True and False depending on config/platform, this can double the number of compiled kernel variants for fp8 MoE, adding a one-time JIT compilation cost at warmup. This is not a runtime hot-path cost (constexpr branches compile away).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start/warmup time for fp8 MoE layers on SM90 before/after this change (kernel autotune + compile time).

findings.claim: load_uint4_as_int8_rvv computes packed_group = group/2, so groups 0 and 1 (and groups 2 and 3) read the exact same 8 packed bytes via the same strided vlse8 load, differing only by the >>4 nibble shift. The kernel therefore issues 4 strided vlse8 loads per K-iteration when only 2 distinct loads are needed. Strided (gather-style) loads are among the most expensive RVV memory operations, and this doubles the load traffic in the innermost GEMM loop (executed M*K*4 times). A single load per packed_group with two nibble extractions would halve the strided-load count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark W4A8 INT4 GEMM on a RISC-V RVV target (e.g. BLOCK_N=32, K=4096, M=1..8); compare current per-group strided loads vs. loading each packed_group once and extracting both nibbles.

findings.claim: gemm_accum_uint4_rvv reloads and processes the weight zero-points (vle8_v_i8 of qzeros_b + group*8, then vsub) on every K-iteration, even though qzeros_b is invariant across the entire K loop for a given group. The zero-point vector could be loaded once per (m,group) or once per group and hoisted out of the K-loop; instead this vle8 + vsub_vv runs M*K*4 times in the innermost loop, adding load and vector-ALU pressure to the hottest path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GEMM throughput with qzeros_b loaded once outside the K loop vs. reloaded every K iteration.

findings.claim: _dequant_gemm_accum_rvv loops m in the outer position and re-unpacks the entire B block (strided loads, nibble shifts, masks, zero-point subtraction, int8->int16 sign-extension) inside the K loop for every m. The unpacked weights are not reused across M rows, unlike the AVX512 small-M kernel which loads/unpacks vb only when row==0 and reuses it for all M rows. This repeats all weight-unpacking work M times instead of once, roughly multiplying the weight-side cost by M for the common small-batch decode case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare RVV GEMM with M-blocked weight reuse (unpack B once, accumulate multiple activation rows) vs. current per-m re-unpacking for M in {2,4,8}.

findings.claim: The kernel fixes vl=8 (256-bit int32 tiles) and steps K one element at a time with a scalar-broadcast vwmacc. On VLEN>=128 hardware this underutilizes the vector register file and issues one widening MACC per K element, versus VNNI-style 4-wide int8 dot products used on AVX512. Without K-unrolling or wider LMUL accumulation, effective GPU-equivalent ALU throughput per K step is low.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile issued vector ops/element and compare against a K-unrolled / wider-LMUL variant of the RVV INT4 GEMM.

findings.claim: This commit enables FlashInfer fused allreduce at world_size=16 (adding 16 to FI_SUPPORTED_WORLD_SIZES and to FI_ALLREDUCE_FUSION_MAX_SIZE_MB with a 64MB threshold), but does NOT add a world_size=16 entry to _FI_ALLREDUCE_ONE_SHOT_MAX_SIZES_MB in vllm/compilation/passes/fusion/allreduce_rms_fusion.py. In call_trtllm_fused_allreduce_norm, max_one_shot_size = _FI_ALLREDUCE_ONE_SHOT_MAX_SIZES_MB.get(cap, {}).get(world_size, None) will therefore return None for ws=16, and the next line `use_oneshot = (max_one_shot_size is None or current_tensor_size <= max_one_shot_size*MiB)` forces use_oneshot=True for ALL tensor sizes up to the 64MB fusion cap. The dict's own comment states 'OneShot max size is at most 64MB / world size (FlashInfer restriction)', i.e. only ~4MB at ws=16. As a result, large multi-node (mnnvl) allreduce messages between 4MB and 64MB will be pushed through the one-shot Lamport path instead of two-shot, which does not scale to 16 ranks / large sizes and will degrade allreduce latency and GPU utilization (and risks exceeding the FlashInfer one-shot size restriction).
findings.suggested_benchmark: On GB300 (SM 10.3) at TP/world_size=16 with mnnvl backend, benchmark benchmark_fused_collective.py sweeping tensor sizes from ~1MB to 64MB and compare fused-allreduce-rms latency with vs without a proper ws=16 one-shot cutoff (e.g. 4MB); measure whether large sizes regress when forced onto the one-shot path.

findings.claim: Setting fi_allreduce_fusion_max_size_mb to 64MB for world_size=16 on both cap 100 and 103 (vs the previous pattern where ws=8 uses only 1-2MB) means far larger tensors will now be routed through the FlashInfer fused allreduce+RMSNorm path at 16-way parallelism. At high world size the fused kernel may be slower than the standard NCCL allreduce path for large messages; if the 64MB threshold is not empirically validated for ws=16, latency of large-message layers could regress relative to the unfused path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end decode/prefill latency at world_size=16 on GB300 with the fused allreduce-rms threshold set to 64MB vs a smaller value, across representative hidden sizes and batch sizes.

findings.claim: `_zero_block_ids` was previously a no-op on CPU (relying on -INF logits masking) and is now a per-step loop that, for every newly-allocated block id, calls `kv[block_id].zero_()` individually. This runs once per scheduler step (called from gpu_model_runner's `_zero_block_ids(scheduler_output.new_block_ids_to_zero)`), and issues a separate Python-level zero_() op for each (dedup'd cache tensor x block_id) pair. With B blocks to zero across L distinct full-attention layer caches this is O(B*L) small tensor-zeroing ops each holding the GIL and dispatching through the ATen op path, versus the GPU path which batches all blocks/segments into a single Triton kernel launch. On CPU this adds measurable per-step latency during prefill/decode whenever new blocks are allocated, degrading throughput. Consider batching the zeroing (e.g. index the block dimension with a single tensor of block_ids and zero in one op per cache) rather than a Python loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU decode/prefill throughput for a full-attention model with prefix caching + chunked prefill enabled, measuring per-step latency when new_block_ids_to_zero is large (e.g. many blocks allocated in one step).

findings.claim: The `_batch_memcpy_impl` fallback (used when triton-cpu is absent) performs a Python-level loop over `zip(src_ptrs.tolist(), dst_ptrs.tolist(), sizes.tolist())` calling `ctypes.memmove` per element. Each `.tolist()` materializes the whole tensor into Python objects and each iteration incurs Python/ctypes call overhead plus GIL. When this replaces the Triton batch-copy kernel used in mamba/GDN state handoff during chunked prefill and prefix-cache restore, it becomes a serialized per-batch CPU bottleneck scaling with the number of copies. ctypes.memmove also always uses the overlap-safe path (slower than memcpy). This is on the mamba state-copy path invoked each step that continues/restores linear-attention state.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU linear-attention (qwen3.5 GDN) chunked-prefill throughput with many concurrent requests, comparing the ctypes fallback vs a batched copy, measuring per-step state-copy latency.

findings.claim: Removing the block that force-disabled prefix caching and chunked prefill for linear-attention on AMX CPU now enables these features, which is the intended functionality. This is not itself a slowdown, but it activates the newly-added per-step KV-block zeroing (previously a no-op) and the batch-memcpy state handoff on the hot path, so the net effect on CPU throughput depends on those two code paths being efficient.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end CPU throughput for qwen3.5 with and without prefix caching/chunked prefill enabled.

findings.claim: In per_token_group_quant_8bit_kernel, cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() is emitted at the very end of the kernel, after ComputeGroupScale, the scale store, __syncthreads(), and QuantizeGroup's global stores. PDL's benefit comes from firing the 'launch dependents' signal as early as possible so the dependent kernel's prologue (grid setup, its own griddepcontrol.wait region) can overlap with the tail of this kernel. Emitting it only at the last instruction means the dependent kernel is essentially serialized after this one, so the added PDL machinery (cudaLaunchKernelEx attribute setup + cudaGridDependencySynchronize at kernel entry) yields little to no overlap and may be net-neutral or slightly negative vs. the previous plain <<<>>> launch. Consider triggering right after the last global read of `input`/before the heavy quantize+store phase if the dependent kernel does not consume this kernel's outputs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark end-to-end latency of two back-to-back quant kernels on Hopper (sm_90) comparing this trigger-at-end placement vs. plain launch and vs. earlier trigger placement, over representative (num_groups, group_size) shapes.

findings.claim: The non-ROCm path switches from the lightweight triple-chevron launch to cudaLaunchKernelEx with a per-call cudaLaunchConfig_t / cudaLaunchAttribute setup. This adds a small fixed CPU-side launch overhead per invocation. For workloads that call per_token_group_quant_8bit at high frequency on tiny tensors (small num_groups), the extra host-side launch cost could be non-trivial relative to a very short kernel, and the PDL overlap only pays off on Hopper+ (sm_90). On pre-sm_90 GPUs the PDL device intrinsics compile out but the heavier host launch path remains.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure host-side launch latency (kernel launch overhead) of cudaLaunchKernelEx vs. <<<>>> for this kernel on a small tensor, and end-to-end throughput on a pre-sm_90 GPU to confirm no regression where PDL cannot help.

findings.claim: Pinning of the CPU offload buffers (cudaHostRegister on cpu_tensors or the mmap base) is moved to a background thread that is started but never awaited before transfers can begin. The two SingleDirectionOffloadingHandler instances are constructed and become usable immediately, and neither transfer_async nor submit_store/submit_load checks whether pinning has completed (no is_pinned gate). Any offload (GPU->CPU) or onload (CPU->GPU) transfer submitted during the pinning window therefore runs against *unregistered* host memory, i.e. unpinned/pageable DMA. Pageable H2D/D2H copies are typically 2-3x slower than pinned copies and cannot overlap asynchronously with compute (the driver stages through an internal pinned bounce buffer and effectively serializes). Since pinning multi-GB KV regions with cudaHostRegister can take seconds, the earliest offload transfers after startup will silently be slow, degrading offload/onload bandwidth exactly when the KV cache begins filling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU->CPU and CPU->GPU transfer bandwidth (transfer_size/transfer_time from get_finished) for the first N transfers issued immediately after worker init, comparing against the previous synchronous-pinning build; expect a marked bandwidth drop until the background pin thread completes.

findings.claim: cudaHostRegister is a device-synchronizing runtime call; page-locking a large (multi-GB) region blocks and can synchronize the CUDA context. Previously this ran synchronously during worker init, before any inference/transfer traffic. Moving it into a background thread (CPUTensorPinThread) that runs concurrently with model execution means the registration can now stall the compute stream / GPU mid-inference, producing latency spikes during the early serving window. Doing an expensive synchronizing CUDA call from a side thread while the main thread issues kernels can serialize both.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run inference immediately after startup and record per-step latency; compare a run where cudaHostRegister executes in the background thread vs. one where pinning is completed before serving. Look for latency spikes correlated with the 'Completed CPU memory pinning' log timestamp.

findings.claim: self._manually_pinned_tensors is appended to by the background pin thread and read/iterated by SingleDirectionOffloadingHandler.shutdown() after join(); while join() provides a happens-before barrier for normal shutdown, the same list reference is shared and mutated from the pin thread without synchronization. If shutdown ever races the pin thread (e.g. early failure before join is reached, or the join path not exercised), unregister could operate on a partially-populated list, leaving some large regions registered/pinned and consuming pinned host memory that is never released.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress-test rapid init/shutdown cycles and monitor host pinned-memory usage (e.g. via cudaMemGetInfo / process RSS locked pages) for growth indicating leaked cudaHostRegister registrations.

findings.claim: cu_seqlens_q is built via `torch.arange(0, num_actual_toks + 1, device=q_rope.device)` inside `forward_mqa`, which runs once per attention layer per forward step. This allocates a new GPU tensor and launches an arange kernel on every layer of every decode step (dozens of extra allocations/launches per step for a many-layer MLA model). All sibling backends (flashattn_mla.py, flashinfer_mla_sparse.py) instead reuse a `query_start_loc` tensor that is materialized once per step in the metadata builder. Since each decode token is its own sequence here, this arange is identical across layers and steps of a given batch shape and should be precomputed/cached in the FlashAttnMLASparseMetadataBuilder (which already carries query_start_loc) rather than rebuilt per layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput / per-step latency on a sparse-MLA DeepSeek model on Hopper with FLASH_ATTN_MLA_SPARSE vs. a variant that precomputes cu_seqlens_q once in the builder; measure per-layer CPU launch overhead and end-to-end tokens/s at high layer count.

findings.claim: The backend declares AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH, but forward_mqa constructs cu_seqlens_q with torch.arange inside the captured region on every layer instead of slicing a persistent buffer from the metadata. This bakes a redundant arange op into the CUDA graph for each of the model's layers, increasing graph size / capture cost relative to reusing a shared precomputed tensor as the other MLA backends do.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CUDA graph capture time and captured graph memory for a full-graph run of a sparse-MLA model with this backend vs. a buffer-reuse variant.

findings.claim: The INT4 read kernel `_attn_packed` maintains two full-width float32 accumulators (`acc_s0`, `acc_s1`, each `[BLOCK_M, PACKED_HEAD_PADDED]`) and two Q streams (`Q_s0`, `Q_s1`), doubling the register/shared-memory footprint and the number of `tl.dot` MMA calls per tile versus the single-accumulator core kernel. It also performs two `tl.dot(P_v, V_s*)` plus an extra `Pv_zp_sum` reduction and subtraction per tile. This roughly doubles the compute work of the attention read path relative to INT8/FP8 per-token-head, and higher register pressure will reduce occupancy — a measurable decode-latency/throughput cost for the INT4 mode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-latency and tokens/s microbenchmark of unified_attention_int4 vs int8_per_token_head at fixed seq_len and head_size (e.g. 128), comparing kernel time and achieved occupancy via Nsight Compute.

findings.claim: For INT4, the data occupies head_size//2 bytes but a full float32 scale_pad (4 bytes) is still appended per (block, slot, head). With head_size=128 the packed data is 64 bytes and the scale adds 4 bytes uint8 -> ~6% storage overhead per head; more importantly the scale cache is a strided float32 view interleaved with packed nibble data, so scale loads in the read kernel are gather-style (stride = padded_head bytes) rather than contiguous, adding uncoalesced memory traffic in the per-tile scale load compared to a separate contiguous scale tensor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure global memory transactions / L2 hit rate for the scale loads in the INT4 read kernel vs a contiguous scale layout.

findings.claim: `_ensure_scale_caches` calls `_k_scale_cache.fill_(1.0)` and `_v_scale_cache.fill_(1.0)` on strided (non-contiguous) float32 views over the whole KV allocation on first use. Because the views are strided (stride = padded head bytes), the fill is an uncoalesced scatter over the entire cache; for large KV caches this is a non-trivial one-time init cost and is done identically for the INT4 path. Not per-step, so low severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the first forward()/prefill of a model with int4_per_token_head vs a native kv-cache to isolate the strided fill cost.

findings.claim: The `prev_key_per_group`/`new_block_hashes` initialization is now gated behind `if self.enable_kv_event:`. If any code path in the subsequent event-emission loop references these when `enable_kv_event` is False, it would raise (a hang/failure rather than a slowdown). This is a correctness concern more than a performance one; assuming the loop body is also fully gated, there is no perf impact.

The core change (delta storage via `mask_num`/`_saved_offset`) is a net performance improvement: process_tokens now iterates only the new suffix [start_chunk, max_chunks) instead of all chunks from 0 each tick, and stride/mask filtering is applied before hash materialization (`chunk_hashes[chunk_id]`), avoiding per-chunk hash-hex work and PoolKey object allocation for chunks that are skipped. Previously every chunk from index 0 was processed and keyed on every call, then sliced. No degradation identified.

The new `_saved_offset` dict adds a per-request entry. It is cleaned up in `delete_finished_stored_request` (pop) and guarded by liveness in `_record_saved`, so it should not leak beyond `stored_requests` lifetime. Negligible memory overhead.

Precomputing `self._key_prefix = PoolKey.build_prefix(metadata)` once in __init__ and using `build_key_string` avoids re-building the full prefix string per chunk via `PoolKey(...).to_string()`. This reduces per-chunk string formatting work on the store/recv hot path — a small improvement, not a regression.

The change removes `or current_platform.is_xpu()` from the `_cudagraph_support` predicate. On XPU, `get_flash_attn_version()` returns 2 (see vllm/v1/attention/backends/fa_utils.py), so the `== 3` branch is now False and `_cudagraph_support` drops from `AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS` to `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH`. Per docs/design/cuda_graphs.md and vllm/config/compilation.py, `UNIFORM_BATCH` forces the runtime cudagraph mode to be downgraded (e.g. FULL -> FULL_AND_PIECEWISE), so mixed prefill-decode iterations on XPU will no longer be replayed as full CUDA graphs and instead fall back to piecewise/eager attention execution. This adds per-op kernel-launch/dispatch overhead for mixed batches on XPU and reduces GPU utilization relative to the previous behavior — a measurable throughput/latency regression for those iterations. (Note: this appears to be an intentional correctness alignment with CUDA FA2, whose max_query_len=1 packed-GQA graphs do not work for mixed prefill-decode, so the prior ALWAYS setting on XPU was likely unsafe.)
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an XPU device running FA2, benchmark decode-heavy vs mixed prefill-decode throughput/latency (e.g. serving with continuous batching) before and after this commit to quantify the loss of full-cudagraph coverage for mixed batches.

findings.claim: FlashInferCutedslMxfp8LinearKernel is inserted at the front of _POSSIBLE_MXFP8_KERNELS for CUDA, making it the auto-selected MXFP8 kernel ahead of the previously-preferred FlashInferCutlassMxfp8LinearKernel on all SM100/SM103 devices. can_implement() unconditionally returns (True, None) and is_supported() only gates on Blackwell + cute_dsl module presence, so on any Blackwell box the cute-dsl kernel will always win. If the cute-dsl mm_mxfp8 path is slower than the CUTLASS path for the shapes actually used (e.g. small batch / decode), this silently regresses MXFP8 GEMM throughput/latency compared to the prior default. The change should be benchmarked against the CUTLASS kernel across decode and prefill shapes to confirm it is actually the faster default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MXFP8 linear layer microbenchmark on SM100: sweep M (1..8192) x typical K,N; compare FlashInferCutedslMxfp8LinearKernel vs FlashInferCutlassMxfp8LinearKernel latency/throughput.

findings.claim: In process_weights_after_loading the weight is materialized as weight.contiguous().t(): .contiguous() forces a full copy of the [N,K] weight, then .t() produces a non-contiguous [K,N] view. apply_weights then passes this non-contiguous column-major tensor directly to mm_mxfp8 without a contiguity guard (unlike the CUTLASS variant which does `if not weight.is_contiguous(): weight = weight.contiguous()`). If the cute-dsl kernel internally requires contiguous operands it will trigger a per-call copy of the weight on every forward, and if it does not, the stored weight is a transposed view whose memory layout may not match what the kernel expects for operand B, potentially forcing an internal repack each call. This is a one-time weight-load cost concern plus a possible per-forward copy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile a single forward of the MXFP8 layer and inspect for hidden weight contiguous()/repack copies via torch profiler memory/copy events.

findings.claim: In `mode="error"` the monitor raises RuntimeError on ANY post-warmup CuTeDSL/Triton compilation. CuTeDSL kernels here are compiled lazily behind functools.cache with dynamic `cute.sym_int()` shapes (e.g. PrepMetaKernel.compile), and Triton autotune/jit can compile new shape/config specializations at runtime. If warmup does not cover every runtime shape/config, the first request that hits a new specialization will raise inside the compile path and abort that inference request instead of merely warning. This is a hard failure/latency-cliff vector rather than a throughput regression, but it is opt-in (default mode is `warn`).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run with --jit-monitor-mode=error and issue requests with sequence lengths/shapes not seen during warmup on a CuTeDSL/Triton path; verify no RuntimeError is raised on legitimate new shapes.

findings.claim: `_setup_cutedsl_jit_hook` monkeypatches `cutlass.cute.compile` globally with a Python wrapper that runs a getattr chain + logging on every call. This adds a small constant overhead to each cute.compile invocation. Because all in-tree call sites wrap cute.compile behind functools.cache, the wrapper only runs on genuine (cold) compilation events, so per-request/steady-state inference cost is effectively zero; overhead is limited to compilation events which are already expensive.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark cute.compile wrapper overhead vs. direct call, and confirm steady-state decode throughput is unchanged with the monitor active.

findings.claim: launch_cooperative_cluster() issues cudaFuncSetAttribute (MaxDynamicSharedMemorySize, and NonPortableClusterSizeAllowed for CS>8) on every kernel launch. This kernel explicitly targets 'low-latency' decode (num_rows<=32), where per-call host-side driver overhead matters most. These attribute-setting calls only need to be done once per (kernel, smem) combination; doing them per-invocation adds fixed host latency to the very path that is supposed to be latency-critical. The sibling persistent_topk launcher caches device properties in static variables specifically to avoid repeated device queries, but the new launcher does not memoize these func-attribute calls.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark end-to-end launch latency of cooperative_topk for num_rows in {1,4,8,32}, comparing a per-launch cudaFuncSetAttribute path vs a one-time (memoized) attribute setup, measuring host CPU time per call.

findings.claim: launch_cooperative_topk_impl calls get_device_prop()->major on every invocation to decide the CS=16 vs CS=8 path. If get_device_prop() performs a cudaGetDeviceProperties query rather than returning a cached pointer, this adds non-trivial host latency per decode step. persistent_topk deliberately caches multiProcessorCount/sharedMemPerBlockOptin in static ints to avoid exactly this.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile host-side cost of get_device_prop() and confirm whether it is cached; measure its contribution to per-step decode latency at small batch.

findings.claim: The decode top-k path fetches a RADIX_TOPK_WORKSPACE_SIZE (1 MiB) uint8 workspace via workspace_manager.get_simultaneous on every decode call. If the cooperative_topk path (unlike persistent_topk) does not require the full 1 MiB workspace or a per-call memset, retaining the same workspace acquisition adds unnecessary work in the low-latency path. Cannot fully confirm without the truncated Python routing diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step decode latency of the cooperative_topk path with and without the 1 MiB workspace fetch/memset for batch sizes <=32.

findings.claim: In InputBatch.make_dummy (input_batch.py ~line 137-138) `input_buffers.is_padding[:num_tokens].fill_(True)` is executed unconditionally, i.e. NOT gated by envs.VLLM_MOE_SKIP_PADDING (which is off by default). This launches an extra GPU fill kernel on every dummy-batch construction (used during CUDA graph capture and profiling runs) even for the vast majority of deployments where the feature is disabled and no MoE kernel consumes is_padding. The is_padding tensor is also always populated into the InputBatch and passed through set_forward_context regardless of the flag, adding a wasted memset that provides no benefit when the feature is off.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step overhead / CUDA graph capture time with VLLM_MOE_SKIP_PADDING unset, comparing parent vs this commit for a dummy batch loop.

findings.claim: modular_kernel.py _prepare adds `topk_ids = torch.where(is_padding[:n].unsqueeze(1), -1, topk_ids)` which, when VLLM_MOE_SKIP_PADDING is enabled and is_padding is present, allocates a new topk_ids tensor and launches an extra elementwise kernel on EVERY MoE layer of EVERY forward pass. For deep MoE models (dozens of layers) this is a non-trivial number of extra small kernel launches and allocations per step. The prior topk_ids buffer is discarded (extra allocation churn). This runs in the a2a prepare path per layer, on the critical inference path when the flag is on.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable VLLM_MOE_SKIP_PADDING and measure MoE prepare step latency per layer vs the fused prepare_megamoe path; compare against padding-token savings to confirm net win.

findings.claim: InputBuffers now permanently allocates `is_padding = torch.zeros(max_num_tokens, dtype=torch.bool)` on the device regardless of whether VLLM_MOE_SKIP_PADDING is set. This is small (1 byte * max_num_tokens) but is unconditional persistent GPU memory that is unused when the feature is off (the default).

In DeepseekV4MegaMoEExperts.forward, when EPLB is active and is_padding is set, topk_ids is masked with torch.where(is_padding.unsqueeze(1), -1, topk_ids) before eplb_map_to_physical_and_record, AND is_padding is then also passed into prepare_megamoe_inputs which re-applies the same -1/0.0 masking inside the Triton kernel. The out-of-kernel torch.where allocates a new topk_ids tensor and launches an extra kernel that overlaps in intent with the in-kernel masking; only needed to keep EPLB load accounting correct, but doubles the masking work on the padded-token path when EPLB is enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With EPLB + VLLM_MOE_SKIP_PADDING enabled, profile the extra torch.where kernel vs relying solely on kernel-side masking.

findings.claim: On ROCm, python_unmap_and_release (called once per allocation during sleep()) now performs an extra cuMemAddressFree immediately followed by a re-reservation of the same virtual address (reserve_rocm_address) as a placeholder. This adds two additional driver calls (address free + address reserve, which for large ranges may internally retry with default alignment) per allocation on every sleep() cycle. For models with many cumem allocations this increases sleep-mode transition latency. This is correctness-motivated (ROCm#6021 requires freeing the VA to actually reclaim physical VRAM), so the extra work is intentional, but it does make each sleep() slower than the prior ROCm path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure sleep()/wake_up() cycle latency on ROCm (mi300) for a model with many pooled allocations, comparing before/after this commit.

findings.claim: release_pools() invokes an explicit gc.collect() between dropping MemPool references and dropping allocator wrapper references. gc.collect() is a full-heap collection and can take non-trivial time on a large interpreter heap. This runs at worker shutdown and via the atexit hook, so it lengthens teardown time (relevant for fast restart / test iteration, and the AMD basic_correctness timeout was cut from 180 to 40 minutes). It is not on the inference hot path, so impact is limited to shutdown latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time Worker.shutdown() and interpreter teardown with and without release_pools()'s gc.collect() when a cumem MemPool has been created.

findings.claim: release_pools() is now invoked both from Worker.shutdown() and from the atexit-registered _shutdown_singleton hook. On a normal shutdown path both fire; the second call short-circuits (allocator_and_pools is empty) so the redundant gc.collect() is avoided, but the guard depends on the first call clearing the dict. This is low risk but means the atexit hook is generally redundant work registered for the lifetime of the process.

`send_to_engine` previously performed the ZMQ send inline with `.await`. It now does `self.handle.spawn(async move {...}).await?`, spawning a fresh task on the ZMQ runtime and awaiting its JoinHandle for every engine send. This is on the hot request-submission path (Add request, Utility, and each auto-Abort). Each call now incurs a task allocation, a cross-thread hand-off to the ZMQ runtime worker, and a wake-back to the caller — extra latency and scheduler churn per request that did not exist before. Under high request rates this adds measurable per-request latency and CPU overhead versus sending inline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark add_request/send_to_engine throughput and p50/p99 latency at high concurrency (e.g. 10k req/s) comparing inline send vs spawn+join-on-ZMQ-runtime.

findings.claim: The new request runtime defaults to `min(available_parallelism, 32)` worker threads, in addition to the existing HTTP runtime worker threads and the new 4-thread ZMQ runtime per engine-core client. On many-core hosts this can create a large number of OS threads (HTTP + up to 32 request + 4 ZMQ), increasing thread-stack memory and context-switch overhead, and potentially over-subscribing CPU relative to the previous single-runtime design.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident memory and context-switch rate (vmstat/perf) on a high-core-count machine before/after, under idle and load, counting spawned OS threads.

findings.claim: Offloaded handlers (including GenerateStream and streaming chat/completions) run on the request runtime, but the returned streaming response body is subsequently polled on the HTTP runtime. Every poll of the token stream that touches state produced on the request runtime crosses runtime boundaries; for long-lived streaming responses this can add per-chunk wakeups scheduled across two runtimes, increasing latency jitter versus a single-runtime path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare inter-token latency distribution for streaming completions with the offload layer enabled vs disabled.

findings.claim: For MoE layers whose intermediate_size_per_partition is not a multiple of the Marlin thread tile (lcm(64, group_size)), this change zero-pads the intermediate dimension up to `padded_N`. At runtime, `_fused_marlin_moe` derives N from the (now padded) weight shapes via `marlin_moe_intermediate_size(w1, w2)`, so both `moe_wna16_marlin_gemm` calls, the `intermediate_cache1/2/3` allocations, and the activation kernel all operate over `padded_N` instead of the true `N` on every forward pass. Padding can be up to ~63 extra columns per gate/up shard (e.g. 96->128 is +33%, 160->192 is +20%), which is pure wasted GEMM FLOPs and extra intermediate-activation memory that persists for the lifetime of the model. Note this is a deliberate trade-off: previously these layers fell back to the (slower) MoE WNA16 kernels, so end-to-end this is likely a net speedup for those layers; but relative to a naturally tile-aligned layer it is a measurable per-forward overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fused_marlin_moe throughput/latency for an MoE layer with a tile-misaligned intermediate size (e.g. 96, 160) before (WNA16 fallback) and after (padded Marlin), and compare against an aligned intermediate to quantify the padding overhead.

findings.claim: The new padding helpers (`_pad_w13_shard_cols`, `_pad_rows`, `_pad_w13_bias`, `_moe_pad_shard_rows`, `_moe_pad_last`) each perform an F.pad plus reshape/contiguous copy of the full weight/scale/zero-point tensors. This adds extra allocations and copies during weight preparation (model load) for padded layers. This is one-time cost at load, not on the hot path, so impact is limited, but it does increase peak host/device memory transiently during load for large expert tensors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load time and peak memory for a large padded MoE checkpoint with and without the padding path.

findings.claim: Padding increases the persistent size of the quantized weight, scale, and zero-point tensors stored on the GPU by the ratio padded_N/N (up to ~1.33x for small intermediate sizes). For large expert counts this permanently raises GPU weight memory usage compared to the WNA16 fallback which used the unpadded shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare resident GPU memory of a padded MoE layer's weights against the unpadded WNA16 representation.

findings.claim: The new maybe_roundup_sizes override additionally rounds intermediate_size_per_partition up to a multiple of block_size (128), whereas the base only rounded hidden_size. This increases the allocated w13/w2 weight (and scale) tensor sizes for models whose intermediate size is not a multiple of 128, raising persistent GPU memory usage for the MoE experts. This padding is functionally required for the per-block fp8 kernel's block alignment, so it is largely unavoidable, but it does grow resident weight memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-layer MoE weight memory footprint for a model with intermediate_size not divisible by 128 (e.g. 1408) before/after this change.

findings.claim: _zero_padding performs several strided slice-assignments (=0) over the full w13/w2 weight (and bias) tensors during process_weights_after_loading. This adds a one-time per-layer memset cost at model load; it runs once per layer and is not on any inference hot path, so the runtime impact is negligible, but it does slightly increase model initialization time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time process_weights_after_loading for the block-quant online MoE method across all layers to quantify added load latency.

findings.claim: FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the TOP of the CUDA _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS auto-selection list, making it the default NVFP4 GEMM backend on all SM10x devices (its is_supported() only checks is_device_capability_family(100) + has_flashinfer(), and can_implement() always returns True). This displaces FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel as the auto-selected kernel for every NVFP4 layer. If the cutedsl kernel is not consistently faster than the previously-tuned CUTLASS backend across the shapes/batch-sizes seen in production, this silently regresses throughput/latency for all auto-selected NVFP4 models. A new backend being placed first in a list documented as 'priority/performance order' is a runtime-performance-sensitive default change and should be validated against the CUTLASS backend across the shape distribution before being made the default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark NVFP4 linear (decode + prefill, batch sizes 1..256, several hidden sizes) with FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel vs FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel on SM100 hardware; compare TTFT/ITL and GEMM kernel time.

findings.claim: Unlike FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel, the new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel does not override input_quant_key() (inherits the base None) and does not accept a pre-quantized QuantizedActivation in apply_weights. As a result it always re-runs scaled_fp4_quant on x inside apply_weights and cannot benefit from activation-quant fusion / reuse of an already-quantized activation that the CUTLASS path supports via as_quantized_activation. When this kernel becomes the auto-selected default, layers that previously fused/shared input quantization will instead pay a per-layer quantization of the activation, adding extra memory traffic and a small kernel launch per linear.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer activation quant overhead: profile a decoder layer where consecutive NVFP4 linears could share a quantized activation, cutlass (with input_quant_key) vs cutedsl (without).

findings.claim: An additional FlashInferAllGatherFP4Pattern (cute-dsl) is unconditionally registered in AsyncTPPass. Each registered pattern adds work to the inductor pattern matcher at compile time (extra pattern to trace/match over every graph). This only affects torch.compile/AsyncTP compilation time, not steady-state runtime, and is minor, but it does grow the fusion pass cost with each new backend pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure AsyncTPPass / inductor pattern-matching compile time before vs after adding the cute-dsl FP4 all-gather pattern.

findings.claim: Each dispatch in do_expand=False (decode) mode now performs `dp_meta.num_tokens_across_dp_cpu.max()` and `int(...)` plus a bit_length()/shift computation on the host every forward pass. These are CPU-side operations on a small CPU tensor and add negligible overhead relative to the collective dispatch, but they run per-MoE-layer per-step. This is not a regression versus the goal (it prevents processing ~R*8192 rows for decode), just a small unconditional host-side cost. No GPU sync is introduced since num_tokens_across_dp_cpu is already a CPU tensor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark decode-path MoE dispatch loop overhead (per-layer host time) with DP>1 vs. the prior default-buffer path; verify the added .max()/bit_length cost stays below ~1us and does not appear on the critical path.

findings.claim: The power-of-2 rounding bounds the number of distinct num_max_tokens_per_rank values to ~log2(max_num_batched_tokens), which caps DeepEP dispatch-kernel JIT recompiles. However, the first time each new power-of-2 bucket is encountered a JIT compile still occurs; on a cold cache a workload that ramps concurrency will trigger a burst of compiles (one per power-of-2 size seen) that transiently stalls the dispatch. This is far better than recompiling per raw batch size, but the tail compilations can still cause latency spikes until all buckets are warmed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Warm-start vs cold-start decode latency sweep across increasing batch sizes with DeepEP V2; measure per-step latency spikes attributable to DeepEP dispatch-kernel JIT compilation at each new power-of-2 boundary.

findings.claim: The async EPLB transfer loop now performs a blocking CPU-group all_reduce (over eplb_cpu_group / gloo) on every layer iteration to sync the `rebalanced` flag before transferring each layer. Previously the loop just checked the local GIL-synchronized `model_state.rebalanced` flag with zero communication. This adds one CPU/gloo collective per MoE layer per rearrangement (e.g. ~60 collectives for a 60-layer model), each of which blocks the async worker until all ranks arrive. On top of the per-layer allocation of a fresh CPU int32 tensor, this measurably slows the background transfer cadence and can stall the worker if ranks are not perfectly in lockstep, reducing overlap of EPLB transfers with the forward pass.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time a full async-EPLB rearrangement (all layers) for a many-layer MoE model (e.g. 60 layers) before/after this change, measuring wall-clock of transfer_run_periodically and the resulting impact on decode throughput during rebalancing.

findings.claim: drain_async() busy-waits in a Python loop with time.sleep(0.001) polling `_all_ranks_result_ready`, which itself issues an all_reduce on the EP device group each iteration. During scale up/down this blocks the executor thread with a 1ms polling granularity plus a collective per poll, adding latency to elastic scaling transitions proportional to the number of pending layers. This is on the scaling control path (not steady-state) so impact is bounded, but the fixed 1ms sleep can add non-trivial stall when many layers must be drained.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure scale-up / scale-down latency with async EPLB enabled and an in-flight rearrangement, comparing time spent in perform_scale_*_eplb_reshuffle with and without drain_async.

findings.claim: With defer_remote_setup=True (elastic EP), the NIXL remote-agent metadata exchange (two collective all_gather_object calls) is moved out of construction and into the first set_transfer_context() call. This makes the first EPLB layer transfer after a scale event pay the full collective agent-setup cost inline, adding a one-time latency spike to the first rearrangement on newly joined ranks. Amortized cost is unchanged, but the first post-scale rearrangement is slower.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure latency of the first set_transfer_context/add_recv cycle after an elastic scale-up with NIXL backend vs. the eager-init path.

findings.claim: The pre-change code performed a per-video text-level prompt expansion: for each video it decoded the expanded token sequence, then did `prompt.replace("<|vision_start|><|video_pad|><|vision_end|>", video_placeholder, 1)`. Each `str.replace` on the growing prompt is O(len(prompt)), and because each replaced placeholder is expanded into thousands of tokens, the prompt grows very large; doing N of these replacements yields O(N * total_expanded_length) work — quadratic in the number/size of videos. The new `_replace_video_token_placeholders` does a single linear pass over the token-id list, replacing all placeholder triplets in O(len(prompt_ids) + total_replacement_length). This is an improvement, not a regression. Net effect of the commit is a performance win for multi-video prompts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark _call_hf_processor / processor() wall-clock time with num_videos in {1,2,4,8,16} and large frame counts to confirm the old text-replace path scaled super-linearly and the new token-replace path scales linearly.

findings.claim: `_replace_video_token_placeholders` is a pure-Python element-by-element loop over the full (post-expansion) token id list, with `result.append` per non-placeholder token. For very long prompts (many thousands of tokens after expansion) this pure-Python per-element loop has non-trivial constant-factor overhead compared to a vectorized/numpy approach. It is still linear and far better than the old quadratic path, but could be a minor CPU hotspot during preprocessing for large videos.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CPU time of _replace_video_token_placeholders on a prompt with a single very large expanded video sequence vs. a numpy-based split/concatenate implementation.

findings.claim: `self.info.get_hf_config()` is called twice in _call_hf_processor (once inside the videos branch to fetch token ids, and again in the post-processing block that builds `video_target`). If get_hf_config is not cheaply cached this is a minor redundant call; hoisting to a single local would avoid it.

BlobBlockHashes.__getitem__ constructs a fresh BlockHash by slicing the underlying memoryview (self._blob[off:off+self._hash_len]) on every index access. In the server (LookupKeyServer), block_hashes is passed to store_worker.lookup(), which iterates the hashes multiple times: once per KV cache group (coord.block_hashes_for_spec), and inside lookup() every accessed element calls h.hex() and bytes(h). Each of those triggers a new memoryview slice + bytes materialization. For a request with large token_len and small hash_block_size the number of fine-grained hashes is large, so the 'zero-copy lazy view' actually re-materializes each hash on every access rather than once; the previous code materialized once into a list. This can be a net slowdown in the hot lookup path, offsetting the intended savings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark LookupKeyServer path: repeated lookup() over BlobBlockHashes with 4096 hashes vs. previous list[BlockHash] materialization, measuring per-lookup latency.

findings.claim: BlobBlockHashes.__getitem__ for a slice returns a plain list ([self[i] for i in ...]). _CompactChunkHashList.block_hashes_for_spec and other consumers that slice the sequence would materialize a full list, negating the zero-copy intent for any slice consumer.

LookupKeyClient._lookup now calls send_multipart(all_frames, copy=False) where the payload frame is a freshly-created b''.join(block_hashes) temporary. copy=False makes zmq send asynchronously without copying; the joined-bytes object must stay alive until zmq is done. Since it's an immediately-discarded temporary in an otherwise synchronous REQ/REP (recv() follows), it likely stays referenced by the frame list until the call returns, so this is probably fine — but copy=False on a short IPC payload provides negligible benefit and adds fragility if the send path is ever made async.

findings.category: positive: reduced key size / wire payload
findings.claim: The compact chunk-hash keying (single hash digest per chunk instead of concatenating scale_factor hashes) reduces Mooncake key length and the lookup wire payload substantially when block_size >> hash_block_size (e.g. 64x for 256/4). The wire format also drops msgpack encode/decode in favor of raw concatenation. These are net performance improvements, not regressions.

The change moves inline `role: system` messages from a merged leading system message to their original in-array position. For clients that send an inline system message as a de-facto leading prompt with no user turn before it (see updated test_inline_system_string_only / test_inline_system_list_content, which now yield order [user, system] instead of [system, user]), the resulting OpenAI message order places a `system` message after a `user` message. Depending on the chat template, this reordering changes the tokenized prompt prefix relative to how such requests were previously canonicalized, which can reduce cross-request prefix-cache (KV-cache) hit rates for those clients — the opposite of the commit's stated goal. The net effect is client/template dependent: it likely helps the Claude Code case (keeping per-request-varying inline content out of the front prefix) but may hurt clients that relied on inline-system hoisting to produce a stable leading system prompt.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure prefix-cache hit rate / TTFT across repeated requests for (a) Claude Code style requests with a billing-header inline system block and (b) requests that place a single inline system message before the first user turn, comparing parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: After packing, each layer's KV cache is now a strided view into a shared backing where each block occupies only `page_bytes` out of a larger `block_stride` (all layers interleaved per block). Previously each layer was a fully contiguous [num_blocks, page] tensor. The new per-layer view (kv_raw_tensor.view(-1, block_stride)[:, offset:offset+page_bytes]) is non-contiguous in the block dimension: consecutive blocks for one layer are `block_stride` bytes apart rather than `page_bytes`. Attention read/write kernels that touch a layer's KV cache now stride across the full packed block, spreading each layer's accesses over a much larger address range and reducing cache/TLB locality per layer. This can measurably reduce effective memory bandwidth for attention (prefill/decode) since a single layer's blocks are no longer densely laid out. Impact scales with number of packed layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek V4 decode/prefill attention throughput with packed vs. unpacked KV cache layout, measuring HBM bandwidth utilization and tokens/s.

findings.claim: In _register_packed_kv_cache the KV cache is registered as 1 NIXL region with num_descs = num_blocks descriptors, each of block_stride bytes covering ALL interleaved layers of a block. For KV transfer of a single logical block this is fine, but if a consumer only needs a subset of layers, the packed layout forces transferring the entire per-block stride (all layers) as one contiguous chunk. Per-block descriptors are coarse; for partial-layer transfers this increases bytes moved over the network. Verify downstream transfer patterns don't need sub-block (per-layer) granularity, otherwise this increases NIXL transfer volume.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NIXL disaggregated prefill KV transfer bytes/latency for DSv4 packed layout vs. per-layer descriptors under partial-layer transfer scenarios.

findings.claim: register_kv_caches now, on every call with >1 cache and no mamba, builds two Python sets by iterating all caches and calling untyped_storage().data_ptr() and data_ptr() per cache to detect the packed layout. This is a one-time startup cost (registration), not per-step, so overhead is negligible, but it does allocate two sets over all layers each registration call.

gemm_micro_rvv_mx32_ku4 emits the NSize=32 output tile by calling gemm_micro_rvv_fma_mx8_ku4 four separate times, and each of those calls runs its own full loop over K re-reading all M rows of the A matrix (a0..a(M-1)) and re-broadcasting each A scalar. This means every A element is loaded and broadcast 4x per micro-tile. By contrast the reference VEC kernel (TileGemm82) loads each A scalar once per K step and reuses it across the full 32-wide N tile (two 16-lane FMAs). The RVV design therefore performs ~4x the A loads and runs 4 independent K-loops with 4x the loop overhead, reducing FMA/load ratio and hurting throughput on the WNA16 GEMM inner loop for RISC-V CPUs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark cpu_gemm_wna16 with isa_hint='rvv' vs a fused single-K-loop variant that broadcasts each A scalar once and issues 4 FMAs into 4 accumulators, over representative K (e.g. 4096) and M in {1,8}; compare inner-loop cycles / GEMM latency.

findings.claim: When __riscv_zvfh is not available, load_row8_b_as_f32<c10::Half> falls back to a scalar for-loop that converts 8 half values one at a time into a stack array and then reloads the array with vle32. This scalar conversion runs once per K step per 8-column tile in the GEMM inner loop, so it becomes a per-K scalar bottleneck (8 scalar conversions + a store + a load every iteration) that will dominate the FMA cost and severely reduce throughput on hardware lacking zvfh. Same concern applies to the bf16 non-zvfbfmin path, though that one stays vectorized.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile without __riscv_zvfh and benchmark half-precision cpu_gemm_wna16 inner loop vs the zvfh path to quantify the per-K scalar conversion overhead.

findings.claim: The kernel fixes the vector length to RVV_MGEMM_N8 = 8 fp32 lanes (fixed_fp32x8_t = LMUL_256, which is m2 at VLEN=128 and m1 at VLEN=256). It never widens the tile to exploit machines with VLEN>256 or higher LMUL, so on wider RVV implementations the FMA units are underutilized relative to what a VL-agnostic (vsetvl-based) formulation could achieve. This is a missed-throughput opportunity rather than a regression against existing code.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an RVV core with VLEN=512, compare the fixed 8-lane kernel against a vsetvl-based variant that processes 16 fp32 lanes per instruction for WNA16 GEMM.

findings.claim: Reverting PDL (Programmatic Dependent Launch) in the DeepGEMM per-token-group FP8 quant kernel removes the griddepcontrol.wait / griddepcontrol.launch_dependents primitives and the cudaLaunchKernelEx path with programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed=1. This disables overlap between the tail of this kernel and the prologue of the next dependent kernel on the same stream on SM90+ (Hopper/Blackwell). The result is that inter-kernel launch latency and epilogue-to-prologue gaps are no longer hidden, reducing GPU utilization and lowering throughput for the chain of quant->GEMM kernels. The launch also reverts from cudaLaunchKernelEx to a plain <<<>>> launch, dropping the programmatic serialization attribute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark per_token_group_quant_8bit_packed back-to-back with the consuming GEMM on H100/B200; measure end-to-end latency and SM occupancy gaps with/without PDL for various mn/groups sizes.

findings.claim: The fused inverse RoPE + FP8 quant Triton kernel loses its GDC (grid dependency control) launch: USE_GDC / gdc_launch_dependents() / gdc_wait() are removed and launch_pdl is forced to False on all non-ROCm/XPU platforms (previously launch_pdl=True on PDL-supporting SM90+). This removes the overlap of this kernel's launch with the preceding attention output kernel, reintroducing kernel-launch/scheduling latency in the DeepseekV4 attention decode/prefill path on Hopper/Blackwell.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepseekV4 attention+fused_inv_rope_fp8_quant path on H100/B200 with launch_pdl True vs False; measure per-step latency and kernel gap via Nsight Systems.

findings.claim: Removing _apply_pdl(_dg, True) from _lazy_init disables PDL globally inside the DeepGEMM library (via set_pdl) on SM90+ architectures. This turns off dependent-launch overlap for all DeepGEMM GEMM kernels (fp8_gemm_nt, grouped GEMMs, mqa logits, etc.), potentially reducing throughput across many quantized matmul paths, not just the two kernels above.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end DeepGEMM fp8_gemm_nt / m_grouped_fp8_gemm throughput on Hopper with DeepGEMM PDL enabled vs disabled at typical MoE token counts.

findings.claim: This commit reverts optimization PR #45309 by re-adding @eager_break_during_capture to DeepseekV4Attention.attention_impl. During breakable-cudagraph capture, this decorator calls BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.add_eager, which ends the current graph segment, runs the whole attention_impl eagerly, and begins a fresh segment. This adds an extra eager break (and thus an extra graph-segment boundary) per attention layer per captured batch descriptor. At replay time each break executes eagerly on the launch stream (no graph replay) and each additional segment boundary adds a separate CUDAGraph.replay() launch, inflating CPU launch overhead and reducing GPU utilization during decode/prefill. The reverted PR explicitly measured this exact break-reduction as a 26.8%~27.9% E2E TTFT improvement, so this revert reintroduces a regression of comparable magnitude.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure E2E TTFT and per-step latency for DeepSeek-V4 (C4A layers, compress_ratio==4) with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH enabled, comparing this commit against its parent; expect ~25% TTFT regression.

findings.claim: The revert removes the BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.is_active() special-case that used a 2-way overlap (wq_b+kv_insert || compressor) plus a separate run_indexer() during capture, and now unconditionally uses the 3-way execute_in_parallel. Because attention_impl is now itself an eager break, its internal multi-stream execute_in_parallel and the indexer's own nested @eager_break_during_capture op now run inside the eager segment where capture._capturing is False. The whole heavy attention body (q up-proj, kv-insert, indexer, compressor, MLA attention) now executes outside any captured graph on every replay, losing the graph-launch savings that keeping these metadata-independent GEMMs inside the captured graph provided.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the number of eager breaks vs graph segments (BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.__repr__) per layer before/after this commit, and correlate with measured decode step latency.

findings.claim: weakref.finalize(self, LLMEngine._cleanup_instance_caches, model) stores `model` as a callback argument, which the finalize object keeps as a *strong* reference for the entire lifetime of the LLMEngine (the finalizer is only released once `self` is collected and the callback runs). This creates an extra strong reference path to the full compiled model. If any teardown logic ever tries to release the model/executor (and its GPU memory) before the LLMEngine object itself is garbage-collected, that GPU memory cannot be freed because the finalizer still pins the model. In practice the model already lives as long as the engine, so this does not usually extend lifetime, but it removes the ability to drop the model early and can delay GPU memory release until the LLMEngine (and its finalizer) is actually collected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate LLMEngine in non-multiprocess mode (VLLM_ENABLE_V1_MULTIPROCESSING=0), delete the engine, and measure time-to-free of GPU memory (nvidia-smi / torch.cuda.memory_allocated) versus without the finalizer holding the model.

findings.claim: torch._dynamo.reset() added to VllmRunner.cleanup() clears all dynamo/inductor compilation caches on every runner teardown. In test suites that repeatedly create and destroy VllmRunner with the same model, this forces full recompilation each time (no reuse of dynamo cache within the process), increasing per-test setup latency. This is test-only code so it does not affect production runtime, but it can noticeably slow test-suite wall time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a test that constructs multiple VllmRunner instances of the same compiled model in one process and compare total compilation time with and without the torch._dynamo.reset() call.

findings.claim: prepare_encoder_cudagraph_capture_inputs computes per_mm_item_output = ceil(token_budget / max_batch_size), then builds a synthetic grid of that size for EVERY item (max_batch_size items). total_patches = sum(h*w for all items) approximates token_budget * (kh*kw), so the dummy pixel_values buffer is sized for the full token budget PLUS ceil-rounding overhead in every batch slot. When token_budget is not divisible by max_batch_size, each item is rounded up (via wo/ho ceil), so the captured buffer over-allocates GPU memory (pixel_values, pos_embeds, rope_freqs_cis, gather_idx) beyond the true budget, and this memory is held persistently for the lifetime of the captured graph.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure captured CUDA-graph replay-buffer GPU memory footprint for Kimi-VL ViT across a range of (token_budget, max_batch_size) values and compare vs. the true worst-case token budget.

findings.claim: In encoder_cudagraph_forward the metadata max_seqlen tensor is intentionally kept on CPU (prepare_encoder_metadata comment) and the attention wrappers call max_seqlen.item() on it. Inside a captured/replayed CUDA graph replay this is fine per-capture, but the eager path (encoder_eager_forward -> MoonVitEncoder.forward with cu_seqlens=None) still computes max_seqlen = (cu_seqlens[1:]-cu_seqlens[:-1]).max() as a GPU scalar and then .item() forces a device->host sync each layer. This per-layer GPU->CPU sync in the eager fallback (unchanged behavior, but now the default whenever CUDA graph is not used) serializes the ViT encoder and reduces GPU utilization for models/batches that fall outside captured shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Kimi-VL ViT eager (non-cudagraph) encode with nsys and count device->host syncs per encoder layer; compare to a precomputed-max_seqlen variant.

findings.claim: _build_merge_gather_idx builds the gather index in numpy with a Python loop over grid_hws_list and per-item np broadcasting/reshape/concatenate on every replay-buffer preparation (prepare_encoder_cudagraph_replay_buffers is called per request). For many small images per batch this CPU-side index construction plus torch.from_numpy(...).to(device) transfer happens on the critical path each step and can add measurable host overhead relative to the previous single-pass patch_merger loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark prepare_encoder_metadata (gather_idx build + H2D copy) wall time for batches with many images vs. legacy patch_merger, on CPU.

findings.claim: select_encoder_cudagraph_items concatenates non-contiguous slices of pixel_values via torch.cat([pixel_values[start:end] for i in indices]). When indices select a subset of items this materializes a new tensor copy each call; for the common case where all items are selected in order this is a full copy of the entire pixel_values tensor that could be avoided with a direct slice/view.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GPU memory traffic/time of select_encoder_cudagraph_items when all items selected vs. a contiguous-slice fast path.

findings.claim: The per-channel FP8 weight_quant_key was changed from kFp8StaticTokenSym (GroupShape.PER_TOKEN, row=1,col=-1) to kFp8StaticChannelSym (GroupShape.PER_CHANNEL, row=-1,col=1). This is passed to init_fp8_linear_kernel() as weight_quant_key and drives which GEMM backend/path is selected. The stated intent (correct) is to make AITER's pre-shuffled FP8 GEMM selectable. This is a positive perf change (selecting a faster kernel). However, the risk is directional: if a kernel that previously matched kFp8StaticTokenSym for the weight no longer matches PER_CHANNEL, kernel selection could fall back to a slower generic scaled_mm path. The change should be validated against every ScaledMMLinearKernel.can_implement/get_min_capability check that inspects config.weight_quant_key on ROCm to ensure the intended faster AITER path is actually selected and no path silently falls back.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On ROCm/AITER with a Quark per-channel FP8 checkpoint (e.g. Llama-3 quantized), benchmark decode+prefill throughput before/after; confirm the AITER pre-shuffled FP8 GEMM is chosen (log kernel selection) rather than the generic scaled_mm fallback.

findings.claim: In process_weights_after_loading the per_channel branch still reshapes weight_scale to (-1,1) only when self.activation_quant_key.scale.group_shape == GroupShape.PER_TOKEN. The weight_quant_key group shape changed to PER_CHANNEL but this reshape logic is gated on the activation key, not the weight key. If the AITER pre-shuffled GEMM expects a specific weight_scale layout (e.g. (N,1) vs (N,)) matched to PER_CHANNEL, an inconsistent scale shape could force an internal copy/reshape at runtime or block the fast path, adding per-call overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify weight_scale tensor shape passed into the selected AITER GEMM matches the pre-shuffled kernel's expected layout; measure any per-forward reshape/copy overhead.

findings.claim: On XPU, the new weightless (weight is None) path for fused_add_rms_norm dispatches to the native PyTorch implementation and then performs two additional device-to-device copies (x.copy_(output) and x_residual.copy_(residual)) to satisfy the in-place contract. Compared to the previous path (which called the _C kernel directly with a ones-weight and wrote in place), this adds two full-tensor copies plus the extra intermediate tensors materialized by the native impl per call on the weightless XPU path. This only affects XPU with has_weight=False, but it is a measurable per-layer overhead for those models.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark RMSNorm with residual on XPU for a has_weight=False layer (e.g. hidden_size=4096, num_tokens=4096) comparing the old ones-weight _C path vs the new native+copy fallback.

findings.claim: In the CPU rms_norm_impl / fused_add_rms_norm_impl inner vectorized loops, has_weight is a runtime bool (not a template/constexpr), so the branch is re-evaluated on every VEC_ELEM_NUM iteration rather than being hoisted out of the loop. The branch is highly predictable so impact is small, but templating on has_weight (as done for the CUDA kernels) would eliminate it entirely and allow better vectorization for the weightless path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark CPU rms_norm for large hidden_size comparing runtime-bool branch vs a templated HasWeight specialization.

findings.claim: This commit removes the `pipeline_parallel_size == 1` gate in `enable_allreduce_rms_fusion`, re-enabling the FlashInfer fused all-reduce+RMSNorm pass for PP>1. The removed docstring explicitly documented that the gate existed because the fused op performs a GPU-side peer-signal spin-wait that assumes byte-identical kernel launches across TP peers, and that concurrent independent warmup of multiple TP subgroups (which occurs when PP>1 creates multiple TP subgroups) can cause ranks to pick divergent FlashInfer launch configs and DEADLOCK. The paired fix in flashinfer_all_reduce.py only forwards `group` to `create_allreduce_fusion_workspace` so the workspace-setup collective is scoped to the correct TP subgroup. That addresses cross-subgroup collective interference, but does NOT obviously address the second documented deadlock mechanism: autotune-driven launch-config divergence. FlashInfer autotune is enabled at O2/O3 (the levels that also enable this fusion), and in the non-persistent-cache path each rank runs its own `_dummy_run` autotune independently with no cross-subgroup synchronization of chosen tactics — so different TP subgroups (one per PP stage) can still select divergent launch configs. If they do, the spin-wait can deadlock, hanging the entire multi-GPU job. The removal of the gate should be justified against this specific autotune-divergence path, which the group fix does not clearly close.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Launch a PP=2,TP=2 (or larger) deployment on Hopper/Blackwell with flashinfer installed at -O2/-O3 (default) and enable_flashinfer_autotune=True, and verify the model completes warmup and serves requests without hanging in the fused all-reduce spin-wait. Repeat several times to catch nondeterministic autotune divergence between the two TP subgroups.

findings.claim: In the cudagraph-capture path (`BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.is_active()`), the indexer no longer overlaps with `wq_b_kv_insert`. The previous code ran a 3-way overlap (wq_b+kv_insert on default, indexer on aux[0], compressor on aux[1] all concurrent). The new capture path runs only a 2-way overlap (wq_b_kv_insert || compressor on aux[1]) and then executes `run_indexer()` sequentially on the default stream afterward. The indexer is a heavy op (fp8/fp4 mqa_logits + top_k_per_row), so on the captured (and therefore replayed at inference) graph it now serializes after the wq_b/kv-insert work instead of overlapping it, and aux stream[0] sits idle. This is the cost side of the trade-off; it is worthwhile only if the reduction in graph-segment count (fewer eager breaks) more than compensates. On shapes where the indexer is large relative to wq_b_kv_insert the lost overlap could partially or fully erode the claimed TTFT win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 C4A layer TTFT / per-layer attention latency at varying prefill lengths, comparing captured-graph replay time before vs after, isolating indexer overlap contribution vs graph-split reduction.

findings.claim: The overlap structure is chosen based on `BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.is_active()`, which is only true during capture. Because the captured segments are what get replayed, the sequential-indexer (2-way overlap) structure is baked into every replay when breakable cudagraph is enabled, while the non-capture path (eager / FULL cudagraph) keeps the 3-way overlap. Two divergent execution structures now coexist; a future change to one is easy to leave un-tuned in the other, and the breakable-graph path permanently loses the third overlap stream even outside profiling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-request TTFT with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH on vs off for DSV4 C4A to confirm the breakable path is not slower on the indexer-heavy segment.

findings.claim: In FusedStagedWriter.apply, the staged contents from every group are concatenated into a single new Python list via `contents.extend(t._staged_write_contents)`, and `group_ids`/`indices`/`starts`/`cu_lens` are rebuilt from scratch each call. Previously, the per-group `apply_write()` fed each group's already-built lists directly to async_tensor_h2d without re-copying. This adds an extra O(total_blocks) Python-level copy of the block-id contents on every apply_staged_writes() call (per engine step, per multi-group model). For large batches with many blocks this measurable CPU-side overhead partly offsets the kernel-launch savings, and it happens on the hot per-step path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark apply_staged_writes() on a multi-group config with large batch (e.g. 256 reqs, many blocks per req) comparing per-group loop vs fused writer CPU time.

findings.claim: FusedStagedWriter allocates 4 UvaBufferPool pools (group_ids, indices, starts, cu_lens), each sized `num_kv_cache_groups * max_num_reqs` int32 and each holding `max_concurrency` (default 2) pinned CPU buffers. This is extra persistent pinned-host memory allocated per BlockTables instance for the multi-group path; it is modest but grows with num_kv_cache_groups * max_num_reqs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident pinned-host memory before/after constructing BlockTables with num_kv_cache_groups>1 and large max_num_reqs.

findings.claim: The fused kernel is launched with grid `(len(group_ids),)` = one program per staged write across all groups, same total program count as before. The MULTI_GROUP path adds a per-program indirect pointer load (`_load_ptr(output_ptr + group_id)`) and a stride load. This is a small per-program overhead but is dominated by the win from launching one kernel instead of num_kv_cache_groups kernels, so net positive. No degradation expected beyond the negligible extra loads.

TARGET_GRID was lowered from 4096 to 512 (an 8x reduction) which directly reduces the number of split-K chunks (num_kv_chunks) chosen for the decode score kernel. num_kv_chunks = 1 << (target.bit_length()-1) where target = min(MAX_NUM_KV_CHUNKS, TARGET_GRID // ctas_per_chunk). With fewer chunks, each score CTA iterates over more KV blocks serially and grid parallelism (grid_score = (num_reqs, num_kv_chunks)) drops. For small-batch decode with long sequences (the common latency-bound decode case), this can leave SMs idle and increase decode latency because the split-K reduction is the mechanism that fills the GPU. This retune benefits the wide 4-head x 4-position spec-decode tile but risks under-utilizing the GPU for plain single-token decode with long context.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark minimax_m3_index_decode latency for decode_query_len=1 across batch sizes {1,2,4,8} and long seq_len (e.g. 32k, 128k) comparing TARGET_GRID=4096 vs 512; measure kernel time and SM occupancy.

findings.claim: The split-K chunk count target changed from being scaled by batch*num_idx_heads (TARGET_GRID // (batch*num_idx_heads)) to being scaled only by seq_lens.shape[0] (num_reqs). Combined with grid_score dropping the per-token dimension (now (num_reqs, num_kv_chunks) instead of (batch, num_kv_chunks)), each score CTA now processes all decode_query_len query positions and all heads for a request in one program via the HQ tile. For decode_query_len=1 this reduces the launched grid size vs before; if the kernel becomes memory-latency-bound on few requests, fewer CTAs can reduce achievable memory bandwidth utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode score kernel throughput for num_reqs in {1,2,4} at decode_query_len=1 before/after; verify no regression at low request counts.

findings.claim: For num_idx_heads>1 and max_decode_query_len>1 (TP=1 spec-decode), the score kernel now materializes a q tile of shape [head_dim, num_idx_heads*BLOCK_SIZE_Q] and computes kq of shape [BLOCK_SIZE_K(128), HQ] per K-block, versus the previous [D,H] q and [N,H] kq for a single token. This larger tile increases register/shared-memory pressure; the commit compensates by forcing num_warps=4/num_stages=2 for that config. If BLOCK_SIZE_Q (next_power_of_2(max_decode_query_len)) rounds up (e.g. max_decode_query_len=3 -> 4), the kernel wastes compute on masked-out query lanes, and the tl.max reduction and dot run over padded columns.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark the decode score kernel at TP=1 with num_idx_heads=4 and decode_query_len in {1,3,4} (max_decode_query_len=4) measuring effect of the padded BLOCK_SIZE_Q and forced num_warps/num_stages on latency.

findings.category: per-request max recompute over query lanes
findings.claim: num_blocks is now derived from kv_len_max = tl.max over query lanes rather than per-token, so all query lanes in a request iterate up to the same (largest) block count. For spec-decode where query positions within a request differ by up to decode_query_len, earlier tokens redundantly iterate a few extra K-blocks (masked out via is_visible_block). This is a small constant overhead per request (bounded by decode_query_len blocks) and generally negligible, but it is strictly more block iterations than the previous per-token loop bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark score kernel with decode_query_len=4 vs 1 at equal request count to quantify the extra block iterations from the shared kv_len_max loop bound.

findings.claim: The new prefill cadence (`prefill_schedule_interval` / `_should_throttle_prefills`) intentionally defers admitting new prefill requests (and in-flight chunked-prefill chunks) on non-cadence-aligned steps in DP deployments. With `prefill_schedule_interval=N`, a newly arrived prefill can wait up to N-1 engine steps before being admitted, directly increasing time-to-first-token. The mid-flight chunked prefill deferral in schedule() (skipping `request.is_prefill_chunk` in the running loop and `break` in the waiting loop) also lengthens total prefill wall-clock for long prompts because their chunks are spread across more steps. This is by-design balancing, but it is a latency regression whenever the throttle actually engages and running work exists.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under DP (MoE/EP) with prefill_schedule_interval=4, measure per-request TTFT and end-to-end prefill latency for a stream of staggered requests vs interval=1; also measure single-rank throughput when other ranks are idle.
