findings.claim: On a throttled step where the running queue is non-empty with only in-flight prefill chunks (all deferred) and there are no decodes to run, `defer_prefills` skips every running prefill chunk and breaks out of admitting new prefills. If decodes are also unavailable this step can produce a batch with zero or very few scheduled tokens, wasting a forward pass / lowering GPU utilization while the prefill work sits idle waiting for the next cadence-aligned step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP run with only chunked-prefill requests (no active decodes) and interval>1: count near-empty scheduler steps and measure aggregate prefill throughput vs interval=1.

findings.claim: `defer_prefills` computes `any(not r.is_prefill_chunk for r in self.running)` on every schedule() call. This is an O(len(running)) scan added to the hot scheduling path on every step (even when throttling is off it short-circuits, but when throttle_prefills is True and not capacity-bound it always scans). For large running batches this adds a small but non-trivial per-step cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark schedule() wall time with large running batch (e.g. 512+ running requests) with throttle_prefills=True vs baseline.

findings.claim: The MoE launch-tile regime is chosen from the raw token count T (`_mxfp8_moe_tiles(T)`), but the grouped GEMM actually processes M = T * top_k routed rows partitioned across E experts. The per-expert row count seen by each program is ~T*top_k/E, which for MoE with many experts is far smaller than T. Gating the prefill tile (BLOCK_M=128, BLOCK_N=256) purely on T>=1024 can select the large tile in cases where per-expert occupancy is low, wasting matrix-core cycles on padded rows; conversely the analogous linear kernel gates on the true GEMM row dim M. This mismatch may pick suboptimal tiles for intermediate token counts. Speculative without gfx950 benchmarks across MoE shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep fused_moe_mxfp8_native on gfx950 across T in {256,512,768,1024,2048,4096} and top_k/E typical of MiniMax-M3; compare achieved TFLOP/s of the prefill vs decode tile at each T to verify the T>=1024 threshold matches the true crossover.

findings.claim: The prefill tile enlarges BLOCK_N from 128 to 256 while keeping BLOCK_M=128 and num_stages=2. The fp32 accumulator alone grows to 128x256 (128KB) and software pipelining with num_stages=2 doubles the K-loop operand buffering (LDS/VGPR). On CDNA4 this can reduce wavefront occupancy and, if it spills, degrade throughput. Verify that occupancy did not drop versus the previous 64x128 tile with default staging.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile _mxfp8_grouped_gemm_kernel with the prefill tile and inspect rocprof/ISA for VGPR/LDS usage and occupancy; compare wall-clock at prefill shapes against the old 64x128 configuration.

findings.claim: In the prefill regime block_m becomes 128 (was 64) and is also passed to moe_align_block_size, so each expert's token list is padded up to a multiple of 128 instead of 64. With many experts and uneven routing this roughly doubles the number of padded (masked-out but still computed) rows per expert, adding wasted GEMM work. Amortized well only when per-expert token counts greatly exceed 128.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure sum of post-padding sorted_ids length vs num_valid_tokens at block_m=64 vs 128 for representative MiniMax-M3 routing, and correlate with grouped-GEMM latency.

findings.claim: When VLLM_TRITON_FORCE_FIRST_CONFIG is enabled, Autotuner.run is replaced so it selects the first *valid* config instead of the fastest one. The first config in a Triton autotuner's config list is generally not the optimal one for the current problem shape, so every autotuned kernel (GEMMs, MoE, attention, etc.) will run with a potentially far-slower configuration. This is the intended tradeoff of the flag (determinism over speed), but any run with the flag on will see reduced throughput / GPU utilization across autotuned kernels. Default path (flag off) is unaffected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end decode/prefill throughput of a Triton-heavy config (e.g. fused MoE or Triton flash-attn kernels) with VLLM_TRITON_FORCE_FIRST_CONFIG=1 vs =0.

findings.claim: The per-call cache key only reads autotuning key values from kwargs: `key_vals = tuple(kwargs[name] for name in self.keys if name in kwargs)`. Triton kernels are frequently launched with the key arguments passed positionally (via *args), in which case they are absent from kwargs and key_vals collapses to an empty tuple. As a result cache_key = (id(self), ()) is identical across all problem shapes for that autotuner, so the config chosen for the *first* shape seen is reused for every subsequent shape. When the flag is enabled to measure/compare kernel performance across shapes, all shapes silently reuse one config, which both defeats the determinism intent and can pin a badly-mismatched (slow) config for shapes it was never validated against.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With the patch installed, invoke an autotuned kernel with the key argument passed positionally and observe that key_vals is empty and the same cached index is reused across different shapes.

findings.claim: cache_key uses id(self) to identify the autotuner instance. Python may reuse memory addresses after an autotuner object is garbage-collected, so a stale entry in the module-global _picked_cache could be matched to a different, newly-allocated autotuner, causing an out-of-range or wrong cached index to be used. Only relevant when the flag is enabled, but could produce an incorrect (non-first-valid) config selection.

The new free_blocks() unconditionally prepends all hashless (uncached) blocks to the head of the free queue and appends all hashed (cached) blocks to the tail, replacing the previous LRU semantics where free_blocks() (without prepend) appended freed blocks in eviction-priority order. Previously in SingleTypeKVCacheManager.free() blocks were freed in reverse order (tail-first) so the LRU/most-recently-tail-of-chain ordering among *cached* blocks was preserved when they were appended. Now cached blocks are always sent to the tail via append_n regardless of the intended per-request eviction priority ordering, and the caller no longer distinguishes prepend vs append. Because the free queue is the eviction order, disrupting the LRU ordering of the cached blocks will change which cached prefix blocks survive under memory pressure. This can reduce prefix-cache hit rate under load, causing more recomputation and lower throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefix-cache hit-rate benchmark under memory pressure: many requests with shared prefixes exceeding pool capacity, measure cache hit rate / TTFT before vs after.

findings.claim: SlidingWindowManager.free() override was removed so SWA now uses the base free() which frees blocks in reverse allocation order and relies solely on free_blocks() to segregate hashless vs hashed blocks. The removed override iterated req_blocks in reverse and split cached/uncached, then prepended uncached and appended cached. The new base free() reverses the whole list and passes it to free_blocks, which re-buckets. For SWA where many interior blocks are null/hashless, the ordering of cached blocks within the tail may differ from the previous per-request-reverse ordering, potentially affecting which SWA prefix blocks are evicted first and thus SWA prefix-cache hit rate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: SWA model prefix-cache hit rate under memory pressure with shared prefixes.

findings.claim: free_blocks() now builds two Python lists (blocks_with_hash, blocks_without_hash) per free call and performs both a prepend_n and an append_n on every call, whereas before it built one freed_blocks list and did a single queue splice. This adds a small constant per-free overhead (extra list, extra linked-list splice) on the hot request-completion / block-recycling path. Negligible per call but occurs on every free during high request turnover.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark free_blocks throughput with large ordered_blocks lists at high request completion rate.

findings.claim: The XPU sequence-parallelism thresholds (min_hidden_size=4096, min_per_gpu_size_mb=8.0) are significantly more permissive than the CUDA thresholds (H100/Blackwell require hidden_size>=8192, and Blackwell uses 32MB/GPU). SP replaces AllReduce with ReduceScatter + local RMSNorm + AllGather; below a hardware-dependent size the extra collective and slicing overhead can outweigh the benefit. If XPU interconnect/collective latency is not actually favorable at hidden_size=4096, enabling SP for these smaller models could reduce rather than improve throughput. The values appear hand-picked rather than empirically derived, so they warrant benchmarking before assuming a net win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On XPU with TP>=2, benchmark decode/prefill throughput for a model with hidden_size=4096 (e.g., ~7B-class) with SP enabled vs disabled across a range of token counts near the computed min_token_num threshold, to confirm SP does not regress at the lowered threshold.

findings.claim: In `select_encoder_cudagraph_items`, the loop `for num_patches in patches_per_image: cum_patches.append(cum_patches[-1] + int(num_patches))` iterates over a torch tensor and calls `int(num_patches)` on each element. If `images_spatial_crop`/`patches_per_image` live on the GPU (they flow through the model), each `int()` forces a blocking GPU->CPU synchronization, serializing the host with the device once per image. This method is invoked once per packed sub-batch AND once per DP shard during every encoder step, so the syncs happen on the hot path. Building `cum_patches` from a CPU copy (e.g. `.tolist()` / `torch.cumsum(...).cpu()` in a single transfer) would avoid the per-element sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek-OCR image inference with many tiled images per batch and profile time spent in select_encoder_cudagraph_items / count cudaStreamSynchronize calls per encoder step.

findings.claim: `_get_num_input_output_tokens` derives token counts from `image_spatial_crop.prod(dim=-1)` (a tensor). The returned `num_input_tokens`/`num_output_tokens`/`local_output_token` may then be tensors rather than Python ints. These values populate `EncoderItemSpec` fields that the manager sums, sorts and compares against budgets (`sorted(range(n), key=lambda i: per_item_out_tokens[i])`, `current_batch_tokens + item_tokens <= max_budget`). Comparing/summing GPU scalar tensors triggers implicit device syncs per item during greedy packing. Casting to `int()` when building the spec would keep the packing logic host-side.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark get_encoder_cudagraph_item_specs + greedy packing for a large batch of images; check for GPU syncs and per-item overhead.

findings.claim: `image_side`, `global_image_output_token`, `patch_side`, and `single_patch_output_token` are implemented as `@property` methods that recompute `math.ceil(...)` on every access. They are constants for the model lifetime but are read repeatedly in the encoder hot path (item spec generation, capture/replay buffer sizing, batched forward reshapes). Caching them as instance attributes at init would avoid the redundant per-call recomputation, though the cost is small.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-call overhead of the properties during a high-throughput OCR run; compare against cached constants.

findings.claim: Removing AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer eliminates micro-batching of tokenizer encode/decode requests in the async serving path. Previously, up to `max_batch_size=32` concurrent encode requests arriving within a `batch_wait_timeout_s=0.002` (2ms) window were coalesced into a single batched `tokenizer(prompts, **kwargs)` call, and decode requests into a single `tokenizer.batch_decode(token_ids_list)` call. HF fast (Rust) tokenizers process a batch of N inputs substantially more efficiently than N separate single-input calls (amortized Python/FFI dispatch overhead, internal parallelism). The replacement (`make_async(self._encode)`/`make_async(self._decode)`) dispatches each request as an independent single-item `tokenizer.encode`/`tokenizer.decode` call. Under high-QPS online serving with many concurrent short prompts, this loses the batched-tokenization throughput advantage and increases per-request tokenization CPU cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Online serving benchmark (e.g. benchmark_serving) with high concurrency (e.g. 100-500 concurrent requests) and short prompts; measure tokenization-bound throughput and TTFT vs parent commit. Also microbenchmark tokenizer.encode called 32x sequentially vs one batched call of 32 prompts.

findings.claim: The new `_async_tokenizer_encode`/`_async_tokenizer_decode` run on the shared `self._executor` (ThreadPoolExecutor sized by `renderer_num_workers`, default 1). With the default single worker, all now-unbatched per-request tokenizer calls are serialized through one thread, and additionally contend with multimodal/other work submitted to the same shared executor. Previously the AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer coalesced these into far fewer executor submissions per unit time, so the number of blocking tokenizer tasks queued through the single worker grows roughly linearly with concurrent request count, increasing queueing latency under load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare TTFT and tokenization queue depth under bursty concurrent load with renderer_num_workers=1 (default) before vs after the change.

findings.claim: calcu_kv_tile_pos() was refactored to compute the left/right sliding-window sizes internally by calling calcu_sliding_window_size(window_size, causal) on every invocation, instead of receiving the two precomputed ints as arguments. calcu_kv_tile_pos is invoked inside the innermost attention tile loops (e.g. around lines 1646 and 1760), so the branchy window-size derivation (previously hoisted once outside the loop) is now repeated per q/kv tile iteration. Since calcu_sliding_window_size is FORCE_INLINE and only does a couple of comparisons/subtractions the cost is small and likely optimized away by the compiler when causal is loop-invariant, but in the dynamic_causal path causal varies per work-item group and the derivation must be redone, adding a few extra integer ops per tile. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cpu_attention_with_kv_cache prefill throughput on a batch with sliding-window enabled, comparing per-tile overhead before/after the calcu_kv_tile_pos signature change.

findings.claim: In AttentionScheduler::schedule the per-request q_start_pos is now always computed as seq_len - q_token_num (dropping the casual?...:0 branch) and a per-request req_causal lookup (input.dynamic_causal[req_id]) is added inside both the total-kv-len loop and the task-split loop. This adds a bool array load per request in the scheduling pass. num_reqs is small relative to the attention compute, so overhead is negligible; noted only for completeness.

gelu_tanh_and_mul replaced the scalar std::tanh loop over a 16-element temp buffer with a single vectorized Sleef_tanhf16_u10 call. This is a performance improvement for the gelu_tanh activation path (removes the store/scalar-loop/reload and computes 16 tanh lanes at once). The accompanying comment notes fast_exp cannot be used for correctness, so Sleef u10 (1-ulp) is the chosen accurate-and-vectorized replacement. No regression; if anything a speedup. Flagged as informational — verify Sleef is actually linked/available for all CPU builds to avoid a link-time or fallback path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark GeluTanhAndMul MoE activation kernel throughput before/after (scalar std::tanh loop vs Sleef_tanhf16_u10) on a representative expert/token shape.

findings.claim: The new ParallelLoader path defaults VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE=0, which per the env-var docstring disables producer/consumer pipelining ('Default 0 preserves the non-pipelined memory footprint'). The entire point of switching to ParallelLoader is to overlap preparing the next shard's device buffer with copying the current shard into model params; with queue_size=0 that overlap is off, so out-of-the-box weight-loading latency is unchanged (no speedup) despite the migration. Users must opt in via the env var to get any throughput benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time fastsafetensors_weights_iterator loading a multi-shard model (e.g. a 7B safetensors checkpoint) with VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE=0 vs 1 vs 2, measuring end-to-end weight load time and peak GPU memory.

findings.claim: The docstring states peak load-time VRAM is roughly model_weights + (1 + queue_size) * shard_size. Setting VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE > 0 keeps queue_size additional shard-sized device buffers resident during loading. For models with large shards (e.g. 5GB safetensors files), even queue_size=1 adds a full shard of extra peak GPU memory, which can push borderline-fitting models into OOM during load. This is a documented tradeoff but worth flagging as a memory-vs-speed knob that can regress the loadable-model envelope if raised.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a model whose shards are large relative to free VRAM with queue_size=0 vs queue_size=2 and record peak allocated CUDA memory during loading.

findings.claim: The original code sliced hf_weights_files into sub-lists of pg.size() and built an explicit per-rank rank_file_map so that each of N ranks read a distinct 1/N of the files (distributed disk I/O), closing each batch's device buffer before the next. The new code hands the full unsliced hf_weights_files list to ParallelLoader with the same pg. If ParallelLoader does not internally reproduce the same per-rank file partitioning, TP>1 loading could serialize or duplicate disk reads across ranks, degrading multi-GPU load throughput. This cannot be confirmed without ParallelLoader internals, but the removal of the explicit rank_file_map is a behavioral change worth verifying.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a TP=4 setup, measure total weight-load wall time and per-rank disk read bytes with the old batched loader vs the new ParallelLoader to confirm files are still partitioned 1/N per rank.

findings.claim: get_prefill_chunk_plan() is invoked fresh inside _forward_prefill for every DeepseekV4 attention layer (~60 layers) on each prefill step, but its inputs (prefill_seq_lens_cpu, prefill_query_lens_cpu, compress_ratio, prefill_chunk_size, window_size) are shared, per-step-constant metadata identical across all layers. The plan is therefore recomputed ~60x per step. Each computation runs a Python while-loop over num_prefills requests doing per-element int(tensor[i].item()) scalar reads plus a couple of elementwise tensor ops (torch.clamp / torch.div). For large prefill batches this is O(num_prefills) Python-level scalar extraction repeated once per layer, adding measurable CPU-bound host overhead that could serialize ahead of GPU kernel launches. Computing the plan once during metadata build (or memoizing it on the DeepseekSparseSWAMetadata instance on first call) would eliminate the duplication.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv4 prefill step with a large chunked-prefill batch (e.g. many short prefill requests, num_prefills in the hundreds) measuring per-step host/CPU time and E2E prefill throughput, comparing computing the chunk plan once vs once-per-layer.

findings.claim: get_prefill_chunk_plan performs per-request Python .item() calls (compressed_lens_cpu[i].item(), gather_lens_cpu[i].item()) inside a while-loop. Although these are CPU tensors (so no GPU sync), each .item() is a Python-to-scalar conversion; the loop cost scales with num_prefills. Vectorizing the chunk-boundary computation (or precomputing prefix maxima) would reduce the per-step Python overhead, which matters when this runs once per layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark get_prefill_chunk_plan() with num_prefills = 256 vs a vectorized implementation, measuring wall-clock per call.

findings.claim: The per-step dispatch path in model_runner.py builds `req_ids = list(scheduler_output.num_scheduled_tokens.keys())` and then calls `get_num_active_loras_for_dispatch`, which invokes `lora_state.get_activate_loras(req_ids)`. This iterates over every request and does a dict lookup + set insertion on the hot execution path for every forward step whenever LoRA is enabled, adding O(num_reqs) Python-level work per step that did not exist before. For decode steps with large batches this adds measurable per-iteration CPU overhead that can lower throughput / raise inter-token latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-throughput benchmark with LoRA enabled and a large num_reqs (e.g. 256) to measure per-step CPU overhead of get_num_active_loras_for_dispatch vs. the previous no-op path.

findings.claim: When `cudagraph_specialize_lora` is True, `get_lora_capture_cases` returns `[0] + powers-of-2 up to max_loras plus max_loras+1`. The capture loop in `_init_candidates` now iterates over `product(capture_sizes, lora_capture_cases)`, so the number of captured CUDA graphs is multiplied by the number of LoRA cases (roughly log2(max_loras)+2). Each captured graph consumes GPU memory for its static buffers and increases warmup/capture time proportionally. This is inherent to the feature but is a real memory/startup-time cost that scales with max_loras.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure startup capture time and peak GPU memory with LoRA enabled and specialize_lora True vs False, across max_loras in {1,4,8,16}.

findings.claim: In `dispatch()`, `_resolve_effective_loras` is called on every dispatch. When `num_active_loras` exceeds the largest captured case it falls back to `self._max_lora_case` via `dict.get`, and the linear scan of `self._candidates[key]` still occurs. This is functionally equivalent to the old bisect but the extra dict lookup + method call add tiny per-step overhead; negligible unless dispatch is extremely hot.

The dummy warmup path in `_dummy_run` now wraps `execute_model` in `maybe_dummy_run_with_lora(..., remove_lora=True)`, which on each dummy run adds `max_loras` dummy LoRA adapters and then removes all adapters afterward. If `_dummy_run` is called many times (profiling, per-size warmup), repeated add/remove of dummy adapters adds one-time startup cost proportional to max_loras per invocation, but does not affect steady-state runtime.

`_update_after_schedule` now re-allocates `self._spec_token_placeholders = [-1] * scheduler_output.num_spec_tokens_to_schedule` on every scheduling step. Previously this list was allocated once in `AsyncScheduler.__init__` (see the comment 'reusable read-only placeholder list') and reused for the lifetime of the scheduler. `_update_after_schedule` runs once per engine step in the async scheduling hot path, so this introduces a per-step Python list allocation of length K. It also defeats the original object-identity sharing: every request's `request.spec_token_ids` was assigned the same shared list object; now they still share the freshly-created list within one step, but a new list is built each step, adding GC pressure and per-step overhead in the scheduler loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure scheduler step time / decode TPOT under async scheduling with spec decode enabled at high batch size, comparing per-step allocation cost before/after.

findings.claim: `_maybe_override_dynamic_sd_cudagraph_mode` forces `cudagraph_mode = PIECEWISE` whenever dynamic SD is enabled and full cudagraphs were requested. Piecewise cuda graphs generally have higher per-step launch overhead than full cudagraphs, so enabling dynamic SD silently degrades GPU utilization / latency for users who had configured FULL cudagraph mode. This is an intended limitation (documented) rather than a bug, but it is a measurable perf regression for the affected configuration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode latency/throughput of a model with FULL cudagraph vs forced PIECEWISE for the same effective K.

findings.claim: On every `schedule()` call, when dynamic SD is enabled, `len(num_scheduled_tokens)` is computed and used to index `self.dynamic_sd_lookup`. This is cheap (O(1) dict len + list index), but note `len(num_scheduled_tokens)` counts scheduled requests including prefill-chunk requests, which may not equal the intended decode batch size the schedule table was built against; if it exceeds `max_num_seqs` the index could be out of range for the dense lookup table (sized max_num_seqs+1). This is more of a correctness edge than pure perf, but an out-of-range index would raise and stall the scheduler.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress test scheduler with batch sizes approaching and mixing prefill+decode near max_num_seqs with dynamic SD schedule configured.

findings.claim: _get_cached_wo_a_bf16 now keeps a permanent bf16 copy of wo_a on the module (wo_a._dsv4_wo_a_bf16). For fp8-quantized weights, the original fp8 weight + scale are still held while an additional full bf16 dequantized copy is now retained for the lifetime of the process. This trades per-step dequant compute for a persistent increase in GPU memory (roughly a full bf16 copy of the o-projection A-weight per attention layer). This is the intended optimization tradeoff, but on tight-memory deployments it increases steady-state footprint versus the previous transient-temporary behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure steady-state GPU memory of DSV4 on ROCm before/after this commit (nvidia-smi/rocm-smi peak allocated) and per-decode-step latency to confirm the compute win outweighs the memory cost.

findings.claim: The bf16 wo_a cache is keyed only on presence of the _dsv4_wo_a_bf16 attribute and never invalidated. If wo_a weights are ever updated in place after first use (e.g. weight reloading, LoRA/adapter hot-swap, or requantization), the stale cached bf16 tensor will be silently reused, which is a correctness/behavioral hazard rather than a pure perf issue. For static weights (the assumed case) this is fine and is a clear perf win.

The swap_ab path is selected purely on `a.size(0) % 4 != 0` (odd M) in cutlass_gemm_blockwise_sm90_fp8_dispatch. The swap_ab kernel uses a small MMA tile (Shape<_128,_16,_128>) with a Pingpong scheduler and a 1x1x1 cluster, which is tuned for small M (decode). If M is large but happens not to be divisible by 4 (e.g. an odd token count during prefill), this small-tile Pingpong config is selected instead of the cooperative 128x128 tile, which can be markedly slower than a padded cooperative GEMM for large-M cases. The heuristic ignores the magnitude of M.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark blockwise fp8 GEMM on SM90 for large odd M (e.g. M=1023, 2047 with N,K in the 4096-8192 range) comparing the new swap_ab pingpong kernel path against padding M up to a multiple of 4 and using the cooperative kernel.

findings.claim: For encoder-only attention, build() allocates a fresh KV cache tensor via torch.zeros((total_block_num, num_kv_heads, block_size, 2*head_dim)) on every metadata build (i.e. every forward pass). This allocation is not pooled/reused, and its size scales with the total number of tokens in the batch. Because it uses torch.zeros rather than torch.empty, it also incurs a full memset of the buffer each time. Since the C++ kernel was updated to clamp reads to kv_end_pos (masking beyond valid positions), the zero-fill of the padding tail is likely unnecessary and torch.empty would avoid the memset. For long encoder sequences this per-iteration allocate+zero adds measurable CPU overhead and memory churn versus the previous SDPA path that needed no KV-cache buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an encoder-only model (e.g. an embedding model) on CPU with the ENCODER_SEQ_LENS batches from test_cpu_attn.py and measure per-iteration latency and peak RSS; compare torch.zeros vs torch.empty for the encoder_cache allocation.

findings.claim: The encoder path in build() constructs auxiliary tensors (block_nums, start_block_ids via cumsum, block_offsets via arange, and the broadcasted encoder_block_table) and calls .item() twice (total_block_num, max_block_num) on every build. These are small per-request CPU operations, but they run on the metadata-build hot path each forward pass. The two .item() calls force host reads that were not present on the previous encoder SDPA path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CPUAttentionMetadataBuilder.build() for encoder-only workloads and measure the fraction of time spent in the block-table/slot-mapping setup versus the attention kernel.

findings.claim: Encoder attention now routes through reshape_and_cache + cpu_attention_with_kv_cache instead of the previous _run_sdpa_forward path. This introduces an extra reshape_and_cache pass (writing K/V into a paged cache) plus block-table indexed reads on every encoder forward, where the old path fed K/V directly to scaled_dot_product_attention. For encoder-only (bidirectional, full-attention) workloads this adds a KV-cache round-trip that the SDPA path did not require; whether this is net faster depends on the vectorized kernel, so it should be benchmarked rather than assumed a win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark encoder-only attention end-to-end on CPU (x86 AVX512 and ARM) comparing this commit's kernel path against the prior SDPA path for the mixed ENCODER_SEQ_LENS batches.

findings.claim: The new op module is auto-imported via vllm/kernels/helion/ops/__init__.py (pkgutil.iter_modules loop), and its @register_kernel decorator eagerly runs HelionKernelWrapper.__init__ -> _get_or_register_custom_op -> get_configured_op -> _load_platform_configs at import time. On a matching platform (B200/H100) this parses the large per-platform JSON (the b200.json alone is ~2000+ lines / dozens of CaseKey entries) and constructs a helion.Config object for every entry, all unconditionally whether or not the kernel is ever invoked. This adds a fixed one-time startup/import cost that grows with each additional kernel + config matrix. It does not affect steady-state throughput/latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `import vllm.kernels.helion` wall-clock time (and time inside ConfiguredHelionKernel._load_platform_configs) before vs after this commit on a B200/H100 host to quantify added startup cost.

findings.claim: If this kernel reuses the pick_config caching pattern from per_token_group_fp8_quant.py, the `_pick_cache.get(cache_key)` check treats a cached None identically to a cache miss (since pick_config may legitimately return None). In that case the closest-hidden_size / closest-num_tokens search (linear scans over config_keys) would be recomputed on every call for that shape rather than cached. This is per-shape overhead on the Python side of each kernel invocation, negligible relative to the GPU kernel but non-zero for tiny token counts. Cannot fully confirm without the new .py source (not present in the truncated diff).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark pick_config() repeatedly for a fixed shape and confirm whether the linear config scan is hit on every call or served from cache.

findings.claim: get_encoder_cudagraph_item_specs() and select_encoder_cudagraph_items() both call patches_per_image.tolist() (a GPU->CPU sync if the tensor is on-device) and rebuild the cumulative-offset list on every invocation. In _execute_local these methods are called several times per batch (get_item_specs is invoked repeatedly via _get_item_specs, _get_per_item_out_tokens, and _run_budget_graph, and select is called once per packed sub-batch). The repeated .tolist() can trigger multiple device syncs and O(N) Python loops per encoder forward, adding host-side latency proportional to the number of images. This is only a per-request host overhead, not on the GPU compute path, but it recurs for every multimodal batch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Encoder cudagraph path with many images per request; measure host time spent in get_encoder_cudagraph_item_specs / select_encoder_cudagraph_items vs. total encoder time.

findings.claim: select_encoder_cudagraph_items() builds selected_pixel_values via torch.cat of per-image slices. When indices cover contiguous ranges this allocates and copies a new pixel_values tensor even when a view/narrow would suffice, adding an extra allocation+copy of the (chunks, C, H, W) pixel data per sub-batch. For large images this is a non-trivial device memory copy on the encoder path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile encoder path memory allocations and copy time for batches with several large images.

findings.claim: The preprocessing thread pool (and the matching semaphore in make_async_with_semaphore) is capped at VLLM_MAX_AUDIO_PREPROCESS_WORKERS = max(1, min(os.cpu_count(), 2)), i.e. at most 2 threads regardless of how many CPU cores are available. Since the semaphore is sized to executor._max_workers, at most 2 audio decode/chunk operations can run concurrently; under high request concurrency on many-core hosts, additional requests block on the semaphore and CPU preprocessing (soundfile/ffmpeg decode + split_audio) is serialized to 2 lanes, limiting throughput. This is an intentional profiled default per the PR note, but the hard cap may leave CPU idle on machines where more decode parallelism would help; operators must manually raise the env var.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure ASR RTFx / transcription requests-per-second at increasing client concurrency (e.g. 1,4,16,64) on a >8-core host, sweeping VLLM_MAX_AUDIO_PREPROCESS_WORKERS in {2,4,8}, to see whether the default-2 cap becomes the bottleneck under load.

findings.claim: make_async_with_semaphore uses asyncio.get_event_loop() inside the async wrapper. This is a deprecated API and, more importantly, resolves the loop on every call rather than caching it; while functionally equivalent to the existing make_async, it adds minor per-request overhead in the hot preprocessing path. Not a regression relative to prior code but worth using asyncio.get_running_loop().
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark per-call overhead of make_async_with_semaphore vs a get_running_loop()-cached variant under high request rate.

findings.claim: The `@support_torch_compile` decorator is removed from the `DeepSeekV4MTP` module. This disables torch.compile graph capture (and any associated CUDA/XPU graph and fusion optimizations) for the entire MTP draft model. The MTP forward is executed once per speculative token per decode step, so it is on the hot path of speculative decoding; running it eagerly instead of through a compiled graph will add per-op Python/dispatch overhead and lose kernel fusion, degrading draft-model latency and overall speculative-decoding throughput. Part of the norm work is now moved into the two new fused ops (fused_mtp_input_rmsnorm / mtp_shared_head_rmsnorm), which partly compensates, but the rest of the block (projections, decoder layer, MoE) no longer benefits from graph-level compilation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure MTP draft-model forward latency and end-to-end speculative decoding tokens/sec on XPU with num_speculative_tokens>=1, comparing the compiled (parent) vs eager (this commit) path.

findings.claim: A new `self.mtp_block.hc_post(hidden_states, residual, post_mix, res_mix)` call is added after every mtp_block forward. This introduces an additional per-step post-processing op on the speculative-decode hot path. If this represents relocating work that previously happened inside mtp_block (a correctness sync with upstream) the net cost may be neutral, but if it is additive it adds a kernel launch/elementwise pass per spec step. Cannot fully confirm without the mtp_block.forward source, so flagged as low-confidence.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the MTP layer forward before/after to confirm hc_post is not double-computing post-mix work already done inside mtp_block.

findings.claim: The watermark defaults to 0.0 (disabled), so no behavior change occurs unless a user opts in via --watermark. When enabled, the watermark reserves a fixed number of KV cache blocks (int(watermark * num_blocks)) that waiting/preempted requests cannot allocate into. This intentionally reduces effective KV cache capacity: at watermark=0.10, ~10% of blocks become unusable for admitting new/preempted requests, lowering achievable concurrency and potentially reducing peak throughput for workloads that were NOT preemption-bound. The feature trades a fixed capacity reduction for fewer preemptions; on workloads that don't thrash, it is a pure throughput/utilization loss. This is a tuning trade-off rather than a bug, but users should be aware watermark>0 can lower steady-state throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a prefill-heavy or balanced workload (not decode-heavy) at high concurrency with watermark=0 vs 0.1 and compare output token throughput and max concurrent running requests; expect lower throughput/concurrency at watermark=0.1 when preemptions are already rare.

findings.claim: The watermark is applied to WAITING/PREEMPTED requests only when has_scheduled_reqs is True (i.e. self.running is non-empty). This means when at least one request is already running, admission of a preempted/waiting request now requires num_blocks_to_allocate + watermark_blocks free blocks. If watermark_blocks is large relative to free capacity, an otherwise-admissible waiting request may be repeatedly rejected across many scheduling steps, increasing TTFT/queueing latency for those requests even though blocks physically exist. This is the intended headroom mechanism, but a misconfigured (too high) watermark can starve admission and increase tail latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure TTFT p99 for newly-arriving requests under a saturated running set with watermark=0 vs a high value (e.g. 0.15); expect increased admission delay / TTFT at high watermark.

findings.claim: _evict_stale_engines() is called at the top of the handshake-initiation path (before the _handshake_lock acquisition) each time a new remote engine appears. It scans the entire _engine_last_active dict with list(self._engine_last_active.items()) on every invocation. In steady state with few remotes this is negligible, but the scan happens on the hot main thread and its cost grows O(N) with the number of remote engines. Since it only runs when a *new* engine is discovered (not per request), the overhead is generally small; still, it is unbounded in N and runs on the request-processing thread rather than a background thread (a design choice the author acknowledges).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Register a large number (e.g. 10k) of stale remote engines and measure the latency of the first new-engine handshake that triggers _evict_stale_engines().

findings.claim: _read_blocks_for_req now writes self._engine_last_active[engine_id] = time.perf_counter() on every KV read request. This adds a time.perf_counter() syscall-ish call plus a dict write per request on the transfer hot path. The cost is tiny per call but is now paid for every single remote read; under high request rates it is a small constant overhead added to each transfer setup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _read_blocks_for_req before/after to measure the added per-request cost of the timestamp update.

findings.claim: TTL defaults to 3600s (1 hour). Remote-engine NIXL resources (dlist handles, remote agents, base addrs, tp_mappings) are only reclaimed lazily when a NEW remote engine triggers _evict_stale_engines(). If no new remote handshake occurs, stale engines are never evicted even after their TTL expires, so CPU memory for old remotes can be held far beyond the intended TTL. This is a memory-retention tradeoff inherent in the no-background-thread design and is acknowledged by the author, but it means the fix does not bound memory in the absence of new handshakes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Register several remotes, let them go idle past TTL with no new handshake, and observe that CPU RSS does not drop until a new remote is added.

findings.claim: release_device_memory_under_pressure() is invoked inside the per-module loop in process_weights_after_loading (once for every module that has a quant_method). On UMA GPUs, whenever the pressure condition holds it calls torch.accelerator.synchronize() followed by torch.accelerator.empty_cache(). synchronize() blocks the host until all queued GPU work drains, and empty_cache() releases all cached allocator blocks back to the driver, forcing subsequent allocations to go back through cudaMalloc. Doing this repeatedly (potentially hundreds of times for a large model with many quantized layers) during weight loading serializes the pipeline and re-triggers expensive cudaMalloc/cudaFree cycles, measurably slowing model load. A single release after the whole loop, or a rate limit, would be far cheaper.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a quantized model on a UMA device (e.g. GH200/DGX Spark) under simulated memory pressure and measure process_weights_after_loading wall time vs. calling release once after the loop.

findings.claim: Even on non-UMA CUDA devices, release_device_memory_under_pressure is now called for every quantized module, executing current_platform.is_integrated_gpu(device.index) each time. If that check is not cached it performs a device-property query per module. On the common (non-UMA) path this adds per-module overhead to weight loading that did not exist before. Confirm is_integrated_gpu is cached; if not, hoist the check out of the loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark is_integrated_gpu() call cost and multiply by module count for a large model.

findings.claim: For every quantized module, the function queries torch.accelerator.memory_reserved() and memory_allocated() plus psutil.virtual_memory(). These are relatively cheap but invoked per-module across the whole model; aggregated over many layers this adds non-trivial host-side overhead to the loading path compared to a single post-loop check.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count quant modules for a large model and measure combined cost of memory_reserved/memory_allocated/psutil.virtual_memory calls.

findings.claim: For any dense Marlin layer whose logical (N, K) is tile-misaligned, marlin_padded_nk now rounds up the weight and every forward pass pays new per-call costs in apply_gptq_marlin_linear / apply_awq_marlin_linear (and the fp4/fp8/mxfp8 analogues): (1) marlin_pad_dim does a torch.nn.functional.pad on the activation reshaped_x from [M, K] to [M, padded_k], allocating and copying a new tensor each forward; (2) marlin_unpad_output does output[..., :size_n].contiguous(), an extra allocation+copy of the [M, N] result each forward; (3) the marlin_gemm itself is invoked with the padded (padded_n, padded_k), so it computes more FLOPs than the logical shape. These are recurring runtime costs the commit's own docstring acknowledges ('padded/sliced on every forward; performance may be degraded'). Net effect can still be a win versus the previous non-Marlin fallback, but it is a measurable per-token overhead for the padded shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark a decode/prefill loop on a quantized (fp8/awq/gptq) linear layer whose N or K is tile-misaligned (e.g. N not %64, K not %128) before vs after this commit: measure per-forward latency and peak activation memory; compare to the pre-change fallback path.

findings.claim: marlin_padded_nk minimizes padded_n*padded_k, but the padded weight columns/rows are still fully materialized and fed to marlin_gemm, so the kernel does extra multiply-adds over the zero-padded region on every forward. For shapes just above a tile boundary (e.g. K=130 -> padded to 256 when group forces lcm(128,group)), the K dimension can nearly double, roughly doubling the GEMM cost and the repacked weight memory footprint for that layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct a layer with K just over a tile boundary that forces a large round-up (e.g. K such that round_up(K, lcm(128, group_size)) ~= 2*K); measure GEMM throughput and weight memory vs the unpadded/fallback path.

findings.claim: should_use_atomic_add_reduce is now called with padded_n/padded_k instead of the logical output_size_per_partition/input_size_per_partition. Since the heuristic gates on n>=2048 and k<2048, padding N up across the 2048 boundary (or K up across it) can flip the atomic-add-vs-global-reduce decision for a layer, changing its reduction strategy and thus its latency relative to the unpadded shape. Impact is small and only affects borderline shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Pick a layer whose logical n is just under 2048 (or k just under 2048) but whose padded value crosses the threshold; compare marlin_gemm latency with the two reduction strategies.

findings.claim: The new gfx950 decode path replaces a single fused decode kernel launch with two kernel launches per decode step (`_sparse_attn_decode_partial_kernel` + `_sparse_attn_decode_reduce_kernel`). In the common latency-bound decode regime where the batch already fills the device, `_decode_num_splits` returns 1 split, so the split-K provides no extra parallelism but the extra reduce-kernel launch, its grid, and the intermediate HBM round-trip (writing/reading part_m/part_l/part_acc) are still paid on every decode iteration. This adds a fixed per-step launch + HBM latency compared to the single-kernel baseline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only latency microbenchmark on gfx950 at batch sizes that already fill the device (num_splits==1), comparing the old single-kernel path vs the new partial+reduce two-kernel path.

findings.claim: The partial path allocates three fresh float32 scratch tensors per decode call: part_m and part_l of shape [num_queries, num_splits, num_heads] and part_acc of shape [num_queries, num_splits, num_heads, comb_dim] (comb_dim = nope+rope = 512). These are torch.empty (not workspace-pooled like the mqa_logits path which uses current_workspace_manager), so each decode step incurs allocator activity and extra HBM writes/reads of the full partial accumulators, which grow linearly with num_splits. This increases memory traffic and peak memory vs. the baseline that wrote only the final `out`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-decode HBM bytes moved and allocator overhead for the partial+reduce path at num_splits in {1,4,8} vs baseline.

findings.claim: The partial and reduce kernels are launched with num_warps=4, while the baseline fallback decode kernel uses num_warps=8. With NOPE_BLOCK=512 (next_pow2 of 448) accumulators of shape [BLOCK_H=16, 512] f32 plus dot products, fewer warps can reduce ILP/latency hiding per workgroup; this is a tuning choice that may leave compute latency unhidden for larger segments in the single-split case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep num_warps in {4,8} for the partial kernel on gfx950 across representative segment lengths.

findings.claim: The reduce kernel loads part_m twice: once for the parallel max/sum reduction (Phase 1, pm_split load over SPLITS_PAD) and again inside the Phase 2 static_range loop (per-split m_s load). This doubles the part_m HBM reads in the reduce kernel; the Phase 2 loads could reuse the m_all already resident in registers, avoiding NUM_SPLITS extra scalar loads per (query,head-block).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile reduce-kernel HBM read bytes with and without reusing m_all in Phase 2 across num_splits.

findings.claim: The change rejects torch_nccl/pynccl EPLB communicators when async EPLB is enabled, and removes the DeepEP-low-latency branch from override_envs_for_eplb. This is a validation/env-setup change that runs once at config initialization; it does not add work to any steady-state training/inference hot path. No measurable throughput, latency, memory, or GPU-utilization regression is introduced by the diff itself.

The rewritten store_mask removes the O(1) fast path and per-lcm-segment template caching, and now calls manager_cls.reachable_block_mask over the full range [0, num_chunks) for every kv-cache group on every store. Previously, the common case (single group / all-full-attention / uniform block sizes) returned tuple([True]*n ...) with zero per-block computation, and the hybrid case computed a single lcm-sized template mask and replicated it n_segments times. Now every store recomputes a mask spanning the entire aligned_token_len for each group, so the cost grows linearly with the number of blocks and is repeated for every group and every store call in the KVCacheStoreSendingThread hot path. For long prefills this is measurably more work per stored request.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark store_mask() latency for a hybrid full+SWA config over increasing aligned_token_len (e.g. 4k/16k/64k tokens) comparing the parent template-replication path vs the new per-block reachable_block_mask path.

findings.claim: store_mask now performs, per store call and per group, a KVCacheSpecRegistry.get_manager_class(spec) lookup plus a full reachable_block_mask allocation of a list[bool] of length num_chunks. The manager-class lookup and the fresh list allocation on every send-thread iteration add per-request overhead that the previous fast path avoided entirely for full-attention-only deployments (the common case).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated store_mask() calls for a full-attention-only group set; compare parent fast path (constant-time) vs new per-call get_manager_class + list construction.

findings.claim: The new branch adds a per-request, per-schedule-iteration compound predicate (`self.connector is not None and self.has_mamba_layers and isinstance(self.kv_cache_manager.coordinator, HybridKVCacheCoordinator)`) evaluated on the waiting-request scheduling hot path for every new request with num_computed_tokens == 0. The `isinstance` check in particular is executed on every eligible request even for non-Mamba / non-connector deployments (until the short-circuit conditions cut it off). The first two conditions short-circuit for the common (non-PD, non-Mamba) case, so overhead is negligible there, but for Mamba+connector deployments the isinstance is done repeatedly. This could be precomputed once into a boolean flag in __init__ to avoid the repeated attribute lookup / isinstance. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark scheduler.schedule() throughput (requests/s admitted) with a Mamba hybrid model under a connector config, comparing repeated isinstance evaluation vs a cached boolean flag.

findings.claim: find_longest_cache_hit_per_group performs one find_longest_cache_hit call per spec group (same as the existing joint find_longest_cache_hit), so it does not add extra cache-lookup passes versus get_computed_blocks. However, it rebuilds fresh per-group Python lists (`hit_blocks = [[] for _ in range(num_groups)]`, `hit_lengths = [0] * num_groups`) and a BlockHashListWithBlockSize wrapper per non-matching group on every invocation. For hybrid models where the attention groups share a spec, the per-group evaluation forgoes the early-exit / hit_length narrowing optimization present in the original find_longest_cache_hit (which shrinks max_length across groups via `curr_hit_length`), so each group is searched up to the full max_cache_hit_length independently. For very long prompts this can search more blocks per group than the original narrowing path. Speculative and workload-dependent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare TTFT / scheduler CPU time for a ~9000-token repeated prompt through Mamba hybrid PD, measuring wall time inside find_longest_cache_hit_per_group vs the original find_longest_cache_hit narrowing path.

findings.claim: The new branch duplicates the prefix_cache_stats.record() call that get_computed_blocks already performs, but records num_hits=max(per_group_hits) (the FA hit length) rather than a per-request aggregate. This does not degrade performance meaningfully but the extra assert + record per request adds trivial CPU cost; noted only for completeness.

`num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` is stored as instance state on the HybridKVCacheCoordinator inside `find_longest_cache_hit`, but it is only recomputed there. The scheduler reads it via `getattr(..., 0)` for hybrid (mamba) requests only when `request.num_computed_tokens == 0` and only inside `get_computed_blocks`. However, the attribute is left set to the value from the *previous* request's lookup. If a request skips the recompute path (e.g. `find_longest_cache_hit` not invoked for it, or a code path where get_computed_blocks does not reach the hybrid coordinator), the scheduler will read a stale prefix length from another request and force `num_new_tokens = num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens`, truncating the prefill chunk. Truncating prefill chunks below the token budget reduces prefill throughput / GPU utilization because more scheduler steps are needed to consume the prompt.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill throughput on a hybrid (mamba) model with many concurrent requests sharing partial prefixes; measure tokens/s and number of scheduler iterations to complete prompts.

findings.claim: The new Marconi admission branch in `_mamba_block_aligned_split` forcibly reduces `num_new_tokens` down to `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` (block-aligned) whenever the detected shared prefix is >= block_size and the scheduled chunk is larger. This intentionally shrinks the prefill chunk for the request so the shared-prefix boundary lands on a block edge and gets cached. The tradeoff: instead of prefilling the whole prompt in one large chunk (up to token_budget), the request is split into (prefix chunk) + (remainder chunk) across multiple scheduler steps. For requests where the shared-prefix cache admission never actually helps (single-shot prompt, prefix not reused later), this adds extra scheduler iterations and smaller GPU batches, lowering prefill throughput and increasing TTFT.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Hybrid-model prefill benchmark comparing TTFT and prefill tokens/s with and without the Marconi admission branch, for workloads with and without repeated shared prefixes.

findings.claim: The scheduler now performs a `getattr(self.kv_cache_manager.coordinator, 'num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens', 0)` on the hot per-request scheduling path for every new hybrid-model request. `getattr` with a default and a chained attribute lookup is slightly more expensive than a direct attribute read; guarding on `self.has_mamba_layers` mitigates this for non-hybrid models, but for hybrid models it runs per request per schedule step. Impact is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark scheduler.schedule() overhead per step for a hybrid model with high request concurrency.

findings.claim: The commit converts the previously synchronous FileSystemTierManager.lookup() (an os.path.exists() call) and ObjectStoreSecondaryTierManager.lookup() (a NIXL _exists() query) into an async batched lookup: the first lookup() for any key returns None (result not yet computed) and only gets a definitive True/False on a *later* scheduler step, after flush() posts the batch to a background thread and drain_results() picks it up in the next step. In the scheduler's _lookup path (offloading/scheduler.py _maximal_prefix_lookup / _sliding_window_lookup / _lookup), a None result sets defer_lookup=True and the whole request lookup returns None, meaning the request's prefix/cache load decision is deferred by at least one full engine step (and the block cannot be scheduled to load until the async result arrives). For the FS tier this trades a cheap in-process os.path.exists() for at least a one-step latency penalty per cold key set, adding scheduling latency to first-token / prefix-load for every request whose blocks weren't previously probed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request scheduling-to-first-load-submission latency (steps deferred) for FS tier with a warm cache of known keys vs cold keys; compare sync os.path.exists lookup (parent) vs async manager (this commit).

findings.claim: _lookup_state and _req_keys in AsyncLookupManager are only pruned by cleanup(req_id), which the tier calls from on_request_finished(). Keys probed by a request accumulate in _lookup_state (one LookupState per unique OffloadKey) and _req_keys until that request finishes. For long-running requests that probe large prefixes, or if on_request_finished is missed, these dicts grow with the number of distinct keys probed, holding result-cache entries longer than a single synchronous lookup ever did. Previously lookup() held no persistent per-key state at all.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run many requests probing large distinct key sets and monitor resident memory of _lookup_state / _req_keys over time vs the synchronous baseline.

findings.claim: Every SecondaryTierManager instance now spawns and joins a dedicated daemon background thread (AsyncLookupManager._thread) at construction. With multiple tiers this adds one always-live Python thread per tier that blocks on queue.get(); minor overhead but a new resource that did not exist when lookup was a direct synchronous call, and could contend the GIL with the scheduler thread during batch processing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare scheduler-thread CPU time / step latency with and without the background lookup thread active while it is doing os.path.exists work under GIL contention.

findings.claim: tests/v1/kv_offload/tiering/test_fs_tier.py increased drain() max_rounds from 40 to 100 and idle threshold from 5 to 20 rounds, and lookup_and_wait polls with time.sleep(0.01) up to a 1s timeout. This lengthens test execution time (more polling rounds and busy-wait sleeps per lookup); not a runtime-product concern but increases CI wall-clock.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure wall-clock time of the tiering test module before and after the change.

findings.claim: This is a pure Triton fused-MoE autotune config file (no code change). It adds tuned tile sizes for E=512 (Qwen3-Next-80B tp=4) fp8_w8a8 block-shape [128,128] on H100. It only defines entries for M in {96,128,256,512,1024}. The config-selection logic (get_moe_configs -> configs[min(configs.keys(), key=lambda x: abs(x - M))]) snaps any observed batch size to the nearest tabulated M. Because the tabulated points are sparse and start at 96, small-batch/decode shapes (M<96, e.g. M=1..64) will now be served from the M=96 tile config (BLOCK_SIZE_M=16, num_stages=2) instead of a default heuristic tuned for small M. If the previously-used fallback (get_default_config) picked a better small-batch tile, small-batch latency could regress. This is speculative and depends on whether the previous path was the default heuristic; the commit claims +25% only for the measured batch range 96-512.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fused_moe latency/throughput for E=512, N=128, fp8_w8a8, block_shape=[128,128] on H100 across M in {1,8,16,32,64,96,128,256,512,1024,2048}, comparing this JSON present vs. absent (default-config fallback), to confirm no regression at small M and out-of-range large M (M>1024).

findings.claim: This commit reverts the warp-shuffle optimization of silu_and_mul_per_block_quant_kernel, reintroducing the older one-block-per-group design. The kernel goes from a pure warp-shuffle abs-max reduction (no __syncthreads) back to a shared-memory log2(group_size)-level tree reduction, adding one __syncthreads per reduction level plus an extra __syncthreads for scale broadcast. These barriers serialize execution and stall warps, reducing GPU throughput. Per the reverted optimization's own note, the reverted-to design is ~2x off peak.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark silu_and_mul_per_block_quant over representative num_tokens x hidden_size (e.g. 4096-8192 hidden) with group_size 64 and 128, measuring kernel latency/throughput before vs after this revert.

findings.claim: The revert replaces per-lane wide vector loads/stores (each lane owns EPT=group_size/32 contiguous elements loaded as one aligned vector load for gate and up, and one vector store on output) with single scalar element loads/stores per thread (token_input_gate[tid], token_output[tid]). This makes the kernel memory-latency-bound with narrower memory transactions and lower effective bandwidth utilization, degrading throughput on a memory-bound kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile achieved DRAM bandwidth / memory throughput of the kernel (e.g. via ncu) comparing scalar-load vs vector-load versions at hidden_size >= 4096.

findings.claim: The revert changes the launch config from packing kWarpsPerBlock(4) groups into one 128-thread block to one block per (token, group) with block size = group_size (64 or 128 threads). With group_size=64 this launches 2x as many blocks and half-populated blocks relative to the packed design, and drops the __launch_bounds__ hint, which can reduce scheduling efficiency and occupancy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel occupancy and total blocks launched for group_size=64 and 128 across the token dimension before/after revert.

findings.claim: In the fused path `_project_qkv_gate` recomputes `self.q_norm.weight.float() + 1.0` and `self.k_norm.weight.float() + 1.0` on every forward call. Each of these launches a float-cast plus an add kernel (producing a new (head_dim,) fp32 tensor) per attention layer per forward, i.e. once per decode step across all full-attention layers. The GemmaRMSNorm effective weight (`weight+1`) is constant after weight loading, so this per-step recomputation is pure overhead and adds extra tiny GPU kernel launches on the critical decode path. It should be precomputed once (e.g. cached in __init__ / on first use) and reused.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-latency microbenchmark of Qwen3Next full-attention layer forward with the fused path enabled, comparing against a variant that caches (weight+1).float() once; measure per-step host+GPU time and kernel-launch count.

findings.claim: The kernel is launched with grid=(n_tokens, num_q_heads+num_kv_heads), giving one program per (token, head) with each program doing only O(head_dim) work over a single head. For small batch/decode (n_tokens=1) this yields very few, tiny programs (e.g. 28 for the test config) and low GPU occupancy, so launch overhead may dominate and the fusion win over the unfused path could be marginal in the decode regime. Consider processing multiple heads/tokens per program block.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep n_tokens in {1,4,16,64} and measure fused kernel wall-time vs the unfused split+RMSNorm+RoPE reference to confirm the fused kernel is not slower at small token counts.

findings.claim: The kernel reloads the rotary halves of the input and weights (x_rot1/x_rot2/w_rot1/w_rot2) separately from the initial full-head RMSNorm load and recomputes the normalization on them, as acknowledged in the comment. This is extra global-memory traffic (~rotary_dim elements plus weight reloads per program) rather than reusing the already-loaded/normalized values. Cost is expected to be small (L1-resident) but it is measurable extra work per program.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the kernel's DRAM/L1 load counts (e.g. via nsight) comparing the current double-load approach vs an in-register slice, at head_dim=256/rotary_dim=64.

findings.claim: This revert removes the Gemma3-specific `_get_max_encoder_tokens` path so that `get_max_image_tokens` again returns the pooled `num_image_tokens` (e.g. 256) instead of the larger pre-pooling patch count (`patches_per_image * max_image_patches`). `get_max_image_tokens` feeds `get_mm_max_tokens_per_item`, which drives `compute_mm_encoder_budget` / encoder cache sizing and the memory-profiling dummy inputs in encoder_budget.py. Since the SigLIP vision encoder actually runs over the pre-pooling patch grid (a much larger token count), profiling/budgeting against the smaller pooled count under-estimates the peak encoder activation memory. This can lead to an over-optimistic encoder cache/compute budget and thus a risk of OOM or a lower effective GPU utilization ceiling for Gemma3 multimodal serving. No steady-state throughput regression on the decode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a Gemma3 multimodal model via the Transformers backend and compare the computed encoder_compute_budget/encoder_cache_size and peak GPU memory during profiling before vs. after this revert; stress with max-size images to check for OOM.

findings.claim: In the waiting-queue loop, for each async KV-connector load candidate the scheduler now calls `_inflight_prefill_reserved_blocks()`, which sums `_request_remaining_blocks(req)` over every request in `self._inflight_prefills`. `_request_remaining_blocks` invokes `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate`, which itself loops over all `single_type_managers` (and touches `req_to_blocks` / evictable-block scans). This makes the per-candidate cost O(|inflight_prefills| * num_kv_cache_groups) and it is recomputed from scratch for every async-load candidate considered in a single schedule() call, i.e. potentially O(num_async_candidates * |inflight_prefills| * groups) per step. Under heavy P/D disaggregation with many concurrent async loads plus many in-flight prefill chunks, this adds measurable scheduler CPU overhead on the hot scheduling path. It is only incurred when `load_kv_async` is true, so it does not affect non-connector workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark scheduler.schedule() latency with a KV connector configured, many concurrent async loads (e.g. 64-256 WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS requests) and a large set of in-flight prefill chunks; compare scheduler step time before/after this commit.

findings.claim: `_request_remaining_blocks` recomputes the full-sequence block requirement each call using `min(request.num_tokens, self.max_model_len)`; the reservation for a given in-flight prefill does not change within a schedule() call, yet `_inflight_prefill_reserved_blocks()` is re-evaluated for every async-load candidate in the same step rather than being computed once per step and cached. Hoisting/caching the reservation sum for the duration of a schedule() pass would remove the redundant recomputation across candidates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare scheduler step latency with the reservation sum computed once per step (cached) vs per-candidate, under a workload with several async-load candidates admitted in one step alongside a large inflight-prefill set.

findings.claim: The new per-group quant impls (`_rocm_aiter_fused_allreduce_rmsnorm_quant_per_group_impl` and the `_with_bf16_norm` variant) recompute the 1-stage vs 2-stage AITER eligibility on every op call at runtime (numel*element_size, hidden/pack-size checks, world_size/full_nvlink branches). These are host-side scalar computations plus `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` executed on the hot decode path per fused AR+RMS+quant call. While each is cheap individually, they are duplicated verbatim across two impls and run per invocation rather than being precomputed/cached; on a latency-bound decode step (DSv3.2 TP4) this adds measurable host overhead in front of the collective. Precompute the world_size/full_nvlink-derived size thresholds once at pattern registration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark decode-step CPU launch overhead of rocm_aiter_fused_allreduce_rmsnorm_quant_per_group vs the AR+RMS-only op at token_num<=80, TP4.

findings.claim: In `AiterAllreduceFusedRMSNormGroupQuantFP8Pattern._replacement`, the no-residual path allocates `residual = torch.zeros_like(input)` (a full hidden-sized bf16 tensor that must be memset to zero) on every invocation, then discards result[1]. This mirrors the pre-existing AR+RMS no-add pattern's `zeros_like`, so it is not a regression relative to that path, but for the newly-fused quant path it adds a per-call zero-fill kernel and allocation whose output is unused. If the AITER launcher does not actually require a zeroed residual on the no-add path, using `torch.empty_like` (or reusing a persistent buffer) would avoid the memset.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the per-decode-step cost of the zeros_like memset for the no-residual AR+RMS+quant fusion at DSv3.2 hidden size.

findings.claim: The changed lines add `weight.to(input.dtype)` inside the fusion replacements (and the new patterns). If the norm weight and input dtype already match at runtime (the common case), `Tensor.to` with the same dtype returns the same tensor and is a no-op; but if they ever differ, this inserts a per-call weight cast kernel of size hidden_dim on the hot path. Since norm weights are static per layer, this cast should be hoisted/cached once at load rather than executed each forward if a genuine dtype mismatch occurs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Check whether weight.to(input.dtype) materializes a copy for DSv3.2 norm weights; if so, benchmark the extra cast kernel per decode step.

findings.claim: The indexer fan-out replacement rewires `rocm_unquantized_gemm` to consume the emitted `bf16_norm` output instead of the original `rms`. This depends on the AITER `emit_bf16=True` variant producing a bf16 activation numerically/layout-identical to the standalone RMS output. If the emitted bf16 activation is not bit-for-bit the same tensor object the graph expected, the pattern still returns `bf16_norm` as the 5th output to substitute for `rms`; correctness aside, this keeps an extra full hidden-sized bf16 output alive (memory) that the single-consumer quant path does not need. Confirm the bf16 emit does not increase peak activation memory versus reconstructing the norm.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak activation memory of the with-indexer fused path vs the single-consumer quant path on DSv3.2 TP4 decode.

findings.claim: In GDNAttentionMetadataBuilder.build (cutedsl branch), prefill_query_start_loc_cpu is now derived as `non_spec_query_start_loc_cpu[num_decodes:] - num_decode_tokens` and then `total_tokens = int(prefill_query_start_loc_cpu[-1].item())` is called. Since prefill_query_start_loc_cpu is already a CPU tensor this .item() is fine, but the previous code path already used a CPU tensor too, so no new sync here. However the new slicing/subtraction creates an extra CPU tensor allocation and elementwise op per build call. This is minor but recurs every step for mixed batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GDNAttentionMetadataBuilder.build latency for mixed prefill+decode batches before/after.

findings.claim: In _forward_core, when split_non_spec is True, the peeled decode outputs are stitched in front of the prefill outputs via torch.cat([core_attn_out_decode, core_attn_out_non_spec], dim=1). torch.cat allocates a new tensor and copies both operands each layer/step, adding a copy proportional to total non-spec token count. The previous unified path wrote directly into a single output with no concatenation. For long-prefill + many-decode batches this per-layer allocation+copy adds overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer core_attn latency for mixed batches with the new split path vs unified chunk path, measuring the torch.cat cost.

findings.claim: The split now issues two separate recurrent/chunk kernel invocations (fused_sigmoid_gating_delta_rule_update for decodes + chunk_gated_delta_rule for the prefill tail) plus an extra rearrange_mixed_qkv on the decode slice, instead of one chunk kernel over the whole non-spec batch. When there are few decode tokens relative to prefill tokens, the added launch overhead and extra rearrange may not be amortized and could slow small mixed batches, though it should help decode-heavy batches (the intended win).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep decode:prefill token ratios in mixed batches; verify the split is a net win across the range and not a regression for prefill-dominated batches.

findings.claim: The prefill initial_state gather changed from `ssm_state[non_spec_state_indices_tensor].contiguous()` to `ssm_state[prefill_state_indices]` (no .contiguous()). Advanced indexing already returns a contiguous tensor, so dropping .contiguous() is fine, but the subsequent in-place `initial_state[~prefill_has_initial_state, ...] = 0` boolean-mask write still runs; no perf regression here, just noting the removal is safe.

RopeStaticQQuantKVCachePattern is registered for every attention layer that reports fused_rope_kvcache_supported() and for both is_neox values whenever _supports_static_q_fp8_quant_fusion() is true, in addition to the existing RopeReshapeKVCachePattern. This roughly doubles the number of registered rope-kv patterns, adding pattern tracing/registration cost and per-graph pattern-matching cost during torch.compile. On models/graphs that never emit a rope->static_scaled_fp8_quant->kv_cache_update sequence (e.g. non-FP8 attention paths), this pattern can never match yet is still traced and attempted, so it is pure compile-time overhead. This affects only one-time compilation latency, not steady-state runtime throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile / RopeKVCacheFusionPass initialization + apply() time for a ROCm GPT-OSS FP8 model with vs without the new pattern registered (count patterns registered and total pass apply wall time).

findings.claim: set_skip_topk(bool) iterates over all MTP layers, and set_skip_topk(True) is issued after step 0's forward. But when index_share_for_mtp_iteration is enabled the layer's skip_topk was already statically configured in DeepseekV2MLAAttention (via the _skip_topk / _index_skip_topk_offset logic). Toggling skip_topk=False on step 0 forces every sparse MTP layer to run the indexer/top-k selection that it was previously configured to skip, which is extra GPU work per step-0 forward. Confirm the offset-based skip pattern was intended to be overridden every iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MTP spec-decode throughput on DeepSeek-V3.2 (sparse indexer) with index_share_for_mtp_iteration enabled vs disabled, measuring tokens/s and indexer kernel time via nsys.

findings.claim: _get_qk_rope_head_dim() may trigger a HuggingFace Hub file fetch (get_hf_file_to_dict('config.json', model_path)) whenever qk_rope_head_dim==qk_nope_head_dim (or one is 0). get_hf_file_to_dict falls through to _try_download_from_hf_hub, which performs a network round-trip (hf_hub_download) for remote repos. This is called from get_head_size()/convert(), which can run for both target and draft configs during startup, adding remote-IO latency to model initialization. It is not cached, so if get_head_size is invoked multiple times it repeats the lookup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine cold-start time for a DeepSeek MLA model with a remote (non-local) repo, comparing with the file lookup path taken vs not taken.

findings.claim: Sharing topk_indices_buffer now iterates over self.model.model.named_modules() to reassign per-module buffer references. This is a one-time setup cost (runs during model load / share_language_model), so runtime overhead is negligible; only flagging because named_modules() over a large model is O(num_modules) but executed once.
load_eagle_model now scans draft_inner.named_modules() to reassign topk_indices_buffer per module instead of a single top-level assignment. One-time cost during model load; negligible at runtime.

In prefill mode (do_expand=True, recv_topk_idx is None), `_receiver` builds `recv_topk_idx` with a Python `for` loop over `recv_expert_num_tokens` (one entry per local expert, e.g. up to 32-256). Each non-empty expert issues a separate `recv_topk_idx[offset:offset+count].fill_(...)` GPU kernel launch. This produces up to num_local_experts tiny device kernel launches per MoE layer, per forward, adding significant launch/dispatch overhead versus a single vectorized scatter (e.g. torch.repeat_interleave of expert ids). For deep models with many MoE layers this is a measurable CPU-side and GPU-launch cost on the prefill path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill-mode MoE forward with DeepEP v2, high num_local_experts (e.g. 32-256), measure per-layer prepare() latency vs a vectorized repeat_interleave construction of recv_topk_idx.

findings.claim: `_receiver` consumes `handle.num_recv_tokens_per_expert_list` (a Python list of per-expert counts) via `sum(recv_expert_num_tokens)` and `ExpertTokensMetadata.make_from_list`, which builds a CPU tensor. Materializing this per-expert count list from the buffer forces a device->host synchronization each MoE layer. On the decode path this contradicts the cudagraph-capturable claim (the count-list read cannot be captured) and on prefill it serializes the pipeline; either way it stalls the GPU each layer and hurts throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile a decode step under nsys/torch profiler with DeepEP v2 and check for implicit cudaStreamSynchronize between dispatch and expert compute; compare against a path that keeps counts on-device.

findings.claim: `_finalize` performs `output.copy_(combined_x, non_blocking=True)` after `buffer.combine`. This is a full extra device-to-device copy of the combined activation tensor every layer. If the ElasticBuffer.combine API can write directly into the caller-provided output (as deepep_ll does via `out=output`), this copy is avoidable and wastes bandwidth proportional to num_tokens*hidden per MoE layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare finalize() bandwidth/latency with output.copy_() vs a combine variant writing directly into `output`.

findings.claim: Decode-mode `_receiver` executes `torch.where(valid_mask, recv_topk_idx + offset, recv_topk_idx)` which allocates a boolean mask and a new index tensor every layer instead of an in-place masked add. Minor per-layer allocation/kernel overhead on the latency-sensitive decode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark torch.where vs in-place masked_add_ for topk_idx remap at decode batch sizes.

findings.claim: The MoE kernel keeps the dense kernel's K-split design (gridDim.z = ceil(size_k/BLOCK_KN_SIZE)) where every K-tile block atomically accumulates its partial sum into the same output element via a 64-bit atomicCAS spin-loop (atomic_add_pk4_f16/bf16). For MoE with output_topk>0, this contention is *compounded*: not only do all K-tile blocks of one expert hit the same c[out_row][n], but multiple experts routed to the same original token map to the same out_row (out_row = token_id/output_topk) and also atomic-add there. The number of atomicCAS retries at a hot address scales with gridDim.z * top_k, which can materially serialize the epilogue on gfx1100 (which has no native packed f16/bf16 atomics and emulates them with global_atomic_cmpswap_b64 + retry).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fused MoE W4A16 forward on gfx1100 with large K (e.g. 4096-8192) and top_k>=4, measuring epilogue atomic time / total kernel time vs a scratch-accumulator variant.

findings.claim: Activations are re-loaded into LDS (or from global for bf16 M=1) independently by every block along gridDim.y (the N-tile dimension). For a given (token_block, offset_k) the same A slice is fetched once per N-tile, i.e. ceil(size_n/1024) times. This is inherited from the dense kernel and generally cheap due to L1 broadcast, but for MoE the extra sorted_token_ids[]/top_k address computation per N-tile adds redundant integer work and global loads of sorted_token_ids that scale with the number of N tiles.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel time for wide-N experts (size_n large) with and without hoisting the sorted_token_ids gather; profile L1 hit rate on the A loads.
