findings.claim: num_tokens_post_padded[0] is read from global memory by every thread of every block as an early-exit guard, and expert_ids[token_block] is likewise re-read per block. Since these are tiny and uniform, this is minor, but for padding blocks the kernel still launches a full THREADS_X=256 block that only reads one int and returns, wasting launch/occupancy for the padded tail (padded blocks = num_token_blocks up to alignment). With many small experts this padding overhead can be non-trivial.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure fraction of launched blocks that early-exit (expert_id==-1 or beyond num_tokens_post_padded) for realistic routing with imbalanced experts.

findings.claim: hipb_mm is invoked with solution_index=-1 on every forward pass, so hipBLASLt performs runtime heuristic/algorithm selection per call rather than using a cached tuned solution. Combined with HIP_ONLINE_TUNING=1 (auto-set globally in rocm.py at import when the flags are enabled), the first invocation for each new GEMM shape triggers an online tuning pass that benchmarks multiple candidate algorithms and writes results to hip_online_tuning_res.csv. In LLM inference the effective heuristic N dimension is the flattened token count (as the test comments confirm: 'the flattened token count becomes the effective N dimension'), which varies constantly across prefill/decode/batch-size changes. Each new shape therefore incurs a tuning burst (extra kernel launches, host-side heuristic search, and disk I/O), producing large per-shape latency spikes during serving until every shape has been seen.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On MI3xx with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1, VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR=1, VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR_HIPBMM=1, run a fp8 linear layer repeatedly while sweeping the token count (M) across many distinct values and measure per-call latency; compare warm vs. first-seen-shape latency and count csv writes to hip_online_tuning_res.csv.

findings.claim: _rocm_aiter_hipb_mm_fp8_impl runs `from aiter import hipb_mm` and calls _ensure_hipb_mm_extension_initialized() (which itself does `import aiter` and torch.accelerator.current_device_index() + a set membership check) on every GEMM invocation. The imports hit Python's module cache so are cheap, but they still execute inside the dispatched custom op on the hot path for every linear layer call; the device-index query and set lookup add small but avoidable per-call host overhead relative to the sibling kernels that do the import once.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark the Python overhead of a single hipb_mm_fp8 dispatch vs preshuffled_per_token_w8a8_gemm dispatch with fixed shapes, isolating host-side time.

findings.claim: AiterHipbMMPerTokenFp8ScaledMMLinearKernel is prepended to the front of _POSSIBLE_FP8_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.ROCM], making it the highest-priority FP8 kernel on ROCm ahead of the already-tuned AiterPreshuffledPerTokenFp8ScaledMMLinearKernel. When enabled, this displaces the preshuffled path (which only reports can_implement when it has a tuned config) with the online-tuning hipb_mm path even for shapes where the preshuffled kernel would be faster. This is gated by VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR_HIPBMM which defaults to False, limiting impact to opt-in users.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end fp8 linear throughput on MI3xx with the hipbmm kernel enabled vs. the preshuffled kernel for shapes both can implement.

findings.claim: The new full-cache kernel (fusedDeepseekV4FullCacheKernel) uses a fixed 1-CTA-per-warp-tile launch (grid = ceil(num_tokens_full*(num_heads_q+1)/warpsPerBlock)) and does NOT implement the reduced-grid variant that the sibling quant-insert path uses (guarded by NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF=1024, one CTA per token iterating over head slots). For decode workloads with small num_tokens_full (batch of decode tokens), this launches a very large number of tiny warp-sized work items with the same launch-overhead / low-occupancy tail behavior that the reduced-grid variant was specifically added to avoid. The legacy path chose the reduced-grid kernel for num_tokens_full < 1024; the new path always uses the wide grid. Expect measurably higher launch/scheduling overhead and worse GPU utilization in the small-token (decode) regime that this MLA cache-insert kernel is on the critical path for.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the full-cache bf16/fp8 insert op at num_tokens_full in {1, 8, 64, 256, 1024} against the quant-insert path; compare wall time and SM occupancy.

findings.claim: In the KV branch of fusedDeepseekV4FullCacheKernel, RoPE is only applied to rope lanes (is_rope_lane), but every lane recomputes and stores its 16-wide segment via the same code path. The nope lanes still perform the full bf16->fp32 decode and fp32->bf16/fp8 re-encode even though those elements are unchanged by RMSNorm(no)+RoPE(no). This is inherent to the fused design and cheap, but on the KV path the round-trip conversion is pure overhead for the 448 nope dims that could be a straight copy. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare KV-only insert throughput vs a memcpy+selective-rope reference.

findings.claim: The grid packs num_heads_q Q-slots plus 1 KV-slot per token into consecutive warps within a 256-thread (8-warp) block. Warps in the same block execute different branches (Q RMSNorm+RoPE vs KV quant+cache-insert), and the KV warp additionally does an early-out for DP-padded tokens and slot_id<0. This inter-warp divergence within a block plus the extra warpSum reduction only on Q warps yields uneven per-warp latency; the tail (KV warp) can stall the block. Effect is small but real for imbalanced num_heads_q.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-warp active cycles for the full-cache kernel with nsight-compute.

findings.claim: The 'lmcache' offloading backend is switched from LMCacheConnectorV1 (in-process CPU offloading via lmcache.local_cpu=True) to LMCacheMPConnector, which the comment says talks to a standalone LMCache server over tcp://localhost:5555. This moves the KV-cache offload path from same-process CPU memory copies to cross-process TCP/IPC transfers. For workloads that were previously relying on the fast in-process local-CPU path, every KV store/load now incurs socket serialization + a process hop, adding latency and reducing effective offload throughput/GPU utilization on prefix-cache reuse.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefix-cache/KV-offload reuse benchmark (e.g. repeated long-context prompts) comparing time-to-first-token and offload store/load latency with kv_offloading_backend=lmcache before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: kv_offloading_size is no longer propagated into the connector config (previously it set lmcache.max_local_cpu_size = kv_offloading_size/num_kv_ranks). Capacity is now assumed to be owned by an external LMCache server that this code path does not configure or start. If the server defaults to a small cache (or is absent), the effective KV offload cache shrinks versus the user's requested size, lowering cache hit rate and forcing more prefill recompute — a runtime throughput/latency regression that is invisible from the config alone.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure KV-cache hit rate and prefill FLOPs saved with a fixed kv_offloading_size across many-request reuse workloads, before vs after; verify capacity honored.

findings.claim: LMCacheMPConnector requires a separate standalone LMCache server process reachable at tcp://localhost:5555. If that server is not started, KV offload operations may block/retry on connection attempts, risking added latency or a hang during KV store/load rather than a fast local fallback as before. This is not visible in the diff but is implied by the connector's remote design.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run with kv_offloading_backend=lmcache and no LMCache server up; measure request latency / detect blocking on socket connect during KV transfer.

findings.claim: GemmaRMSNorm.forward_native now passes a float32 weight (self.weight.float() + 1.0) into the dispatched ir.ops.rms_norm / fused_add_rms_norm. The registered fast CUDA kernels (vllm_c torch.ops._C.rms_norm/fused_add_rms_norm) and AITER kernels only support 16-bit activations with matching-dtype weights; with a fp32 weight against fp16/bf16 activations these ops fall back to the pure-torch 'native' impl instead of the fused C++ kernel. In eager (non-compiled) mode this may make the Gemma norm slower than a dedicated CUDA path. Note this fp32-weight-per-call behavior existed before too, so this is not strictly a regression, but the change consolidates all Gemma norms onto the dispatched path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark GemmaRMSNorm.forward_cuda (eager, no torch.compile) for fp16/bf16 activations at typical hidden sizes (e.g. 2048-8192) with residual and without, comparing this commit vs parent.

findings.claim: weight = self.weight.float() + 1.0 allocates a new fp32 tensor of hidden_size on every forward call (both residual and non-residual branches). This is a small per-call allocation + add + upcast that is not cached; for small hidden sizes it is negligible, but it prevents the fused CUDA kernels (which expect a same-dtype weight) from being selected and adds kernel-launch overhead on the hot norm path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-call overhead of the weight.float()+1.0 recomputation and resulting dispatch selection in GemmaRMSNorm across many forward calls.

findings.claim: The base-class `cache_blocks` fast path was removed. Previously, when `alignment_tokens is None or <= block_size`, `block_mask` was set to `None` with no per-block work. Now `cache_blocks` unconditionally calls `self.reachable_block_mask(...)` on every caching invocation for every manager. For full-attention/MLA/chunked-local managers the base `reachable_block_mask` still returns `None`, but it is now called on the hot per-request caching path (called from the coordinator's `cache_blocks`, invoked every scheduler step for growing requests). This adds an extra Python method call + argument construction per group per step where before it was a cheap branch. Minor per-call, but on the hot scheduling path across many requests/groups it is measurable overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cache_blocks() throughput for full-attention groups over many scheduler steps, comparing extra reachable_block_mask call vs the old None fast-path branch.

findings.claim: In SlidingWindowManager.reachable_block_mask the mask is now materialized as a full Python list `mask = [False] * (end_block - start_block)` and populated with an explicit per-block Python `for i in range(...)` loop, plus a second per-block loop for the replay-boundary tail. For long prompts (many thousands of blocks) this builds and iterates a large Python list on the caching hot path each time cache_blocks is called for the SWA group. The previous implementation returned a list comprehension in one pass; the new code does two passes and always allocates the list even in cases the fast path used to short-circuit. This increases CPU cost and transient memory for long-context sliding-window models.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark reachable_block_mask on SlidingWindowSpec with num_full_blocks in the thousands, comparing single-pass comprehension vs two-pass list-fill.

findings.claim: remove_skipped_blocks now partitions freed blocks into cached vs uncached lists (branch on block_hash per block) and issues two separate `free_blocks` calls (one append, one prepend) instead of one. Each free_blocks materializes its list and iterates. For SWA requests that skip many blocks per step, this doubles the number of free_blocks invocations and adds a per-block hash check. Small but on the recycling hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark remove_skipped_blocks over many decode steps for a sliding-window request, comparing single free_blocks vs the split cached/uncached two-call version.

findings.claim: SlidingWindowManager.free() now iterates all request blocks in reverse, branching per block on block_hash to build two lists, then issues two free_blocks calls. The base free() did a single free_blocks(reversed(req_blocks)). For requests holding many blocks this adds O(n) Python branching and an extra pass/allocation at request teardown. Minor, but request completion is frequent under high throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark request free() for SWA requests with many blocks, comparing single-pass reverse free vs the cached/uncached partition.

findings.claim: NixlEplbCommunicator.add_recv now issues one NIXL RDMA READ (with its own get_xfer_descs / prep_xfer_dlist for both local and remote descriptor lists, make_prepped_xfer, and transfer) per received expert. The previous implementation batched all experts received from a given peer into a single _create_peer_xfer / transfer() (one READ per peer). With many experts moving per layer, this multiplies the number of small RDMA transfers and descriptor-preparation calls by (experts-per-peer), increasing per-transfer setup overhead and reducing effective RDMA throughput (many small transfers vs one large batched one). Descriptor handles are also allocated and released per expert instead of once per peer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Rebalance a large MoE (e.g. many experts moving across ranks) over NIXL and compare wall-clock transfer time per layer and issued-transfer count vs the batched-per-peer parent implementation.

findings.claim: Each add_recv builds fresh local/remote xfer descriptor lists and prepped dlist handles that are released after execute(). Because add_recv is called once per expert every rebalance, the transfer descriptor preparation (get_xfer_descs + prep_xfer_dlist for INIT and remote agents) is repeated for every expert on every rebalance rather than being cached/prepared once. This adds Python + NIXL setup overhead proportional to expert count on the EPLB critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile time spent in _create_peer_xfer / prep_xfer_dlist during a rebalance with a large expert count.

findings.claim: A persistent expert_buffer ([torch.empty_like(w) for w in expert_weights[0]]) is allocated and registered with NIXL, and all layers' expert weights are now registered with NIXL (get_reg_descs over every tensor of every MoE layer). This increases pinned/registered device memory footprint vs the parent which registered only two flat buffers sized to a single layer. The zero-copy design trades extra registered memory (and registration time at init) for eliminating pack/unpack copies.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory registered with NIXL and initialization time for a model with many MoE layers vs the parent single-layer buffer approach.

findings.claim: _wait_for_all_transfers still busy-polls check_xfer_state with time.sleep(0.0005) between rounds. With per-expert transfers there are now many more handles to poll each round (the pending set is larger), so the completion-polling loop performs more check_xfer_state calls per iteration; this is minor but scales with the increased number of transfers introduced by the per-expert issuance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPU time in _wait_for_all_transfers polling loop as a function of number of in-flight transfers.

findings.claim: For FULL cudagraph descriptors, the returned dict now stores an AttentionStatePair holding BOTH the warmup and the captured AttentionState, whereas the parent code discarded the warmup attention state and kept only the freshly-created capture state. This means the warmup attn_metadata/slot_mappings (previously eligible for GC before capture) are now retained alongside the capture state and propagated to the speculator's capture() call. This raises peak host/device memory held during the one-time CUDA-graph capture (warmup) phase in proportion to the number of FULL descriptors. It is not a runtime/steady-state regression (the dict is transient and freed after capture), and slot_mappings are largely shared views, so the effect is small and startup-only. This is a necessary correctness fix (FlashMLA-style lazy-init backends need distinct warmup/capture metadata), so the extra retention is likely intended.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU/host memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated + RSS) during cudagraph capture with an Eagle/MTP speculator and FULL cudagraph mode, comparing parent vs this commit across the full set of FULL capture sizes.

findings.claim: In XPUFp8BlockScaledMMKernel.apply_block_scaled_mm, the weight is transposed with B.t() on every forward pass (every layer, every step). The comment asserts this is a zero-copy [K,N] view, but B.t() yields a NON-contiguous tensor. If the underlying torch.ops._xpu_C.fp8_gemm kernel requires a contiguous weight (as GEMM kernels typically do — note the sibling XPUMxFp8LinearKernel and XPUFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel deliberately transpose the weight ONCE in process_weights_after_loading and pass layer.weight directly), the kernel will implicitly materialize a contiguous copy of the entire weight matrix on each call. That is a recurring per-forward, per-layer full-weight copy (O(N*K) elements) that inflates memory bandwidth/traffic and latency. The transpose should be done once at load time in process_weights_after_loading (like the MXFP8 XPU path) rather than per call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On XPU, run a decode/prefill loop through an fp8 block-scaled linear layer with this kernel vs. a variant that transposes the weight once in process_weights_after_loading; compare per-layer GEMM latency and device memory traffic.

findings.claim: apply_block_scaled_mm passes torch.Tensor() as the bias placeholder, which allocates a fresh empty tensor object on every forward invocation (every layer, every step). This is a small but avoidable Python/allocator overhead in the inference hot path; a cached/shared empty tensor or passing None (if the op accepts it) would avoid the repeated allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the Python-side dispatch overhead of apply_block_scaled_mm calling torch.Tensor() per call vs. a reused empty-tensor constant, across many iterations.

findings.claim: The FlashInfer sampler is disabled for the ENTIRE batch whenever any single request is greedy (temperature==0) or has an explicit seed (any_greedy / any_explicit_seed use np.any over the whole batch). In the V2 model runner, batches commonly mix greedy and random requests, so in practice the FlashInfer fast path (which avoids the full-vocab sort in apply_top_k_top_p) will rarely trigger and the batch will fall back to the sort-based apply_top_k_top_p + gumbel_sample path. This means the intended sampling-latency win from FlashInfer will not materialize for typical mixed batches; the rejection-sampling speedup only helps when the whole batch is non-greedy, unseeded, and uses top_k/top_p.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sampling latency for a batch mixing e.g. 1 greedy request with N random top-p requests, vs an all-random top-p batch; compare time in Sampler.sample().

findings.claim: flashinfer_sample(...).to(torch.int64) forces an extra device-side dtype conversion/copy of the sampled-token tensor every step (FlashInfer returns int32). This is a tiny [batch]-sized tensor so cost is negligible, but it is a per-step allocation+kernel that the previous gumbel path did not incur.

get_top_k_top_p is now always invoked in sample() before deciding the path. When the FlashInfer path is chosen it materializes top_k/top_p GPU gather tensors (self.top_k.gpu[expanded_idx_mapping]); when the native fallback is chosen apply_top_k_top_p re-derives the same masks. This is functionally equivalent to the parent for the native path (no regression), but the eager gather plus the additional any_greedy/any_explicit_seed numpy scans add a small constant per-step overhead relative to the parent's single lazy check.

get_mm_features_in_window relies on mm_features being sorted by mm_position.offset AND on offset+length being sorted (non-overlapping). The bisect for `lo` uses key `offset+length`, which is only monotonic if items are strictly non-overlapping. If any request ever has mm_features that are not sorted by offset, or where an item with a smaller offset extends past a later item (overlapping placeholders), bisect will return an incorrect [lo,hi) window and silently drop encoder inputs from scheduling. Dropped/mis-windowed encoder inputs mean the model runner will not gather those embeddings, causing either correctness failures or forced recomputation later — a much larger perf hit than the O(n) scan this replaces. The sortedness precondition is documented only as an assumption in the docstring and not asserted, so a mis-ordered feature list would degrade rather than error.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct requests with many mm items (n=64,256) and measure schedule() and gpu_model_runner encoder-gather time vs parent; additionally test with deliberately overlapping/unsorted placeholder ranges to confirm no items are dropped.

findings.claim: A new per-request dict self.request_cached_ids (request_id -> set[int]) is added and mutated in check_and_update_cache/allocate/free_encoder_input. This trades the previous O(n) recompute in get_cached_input_ids for O(1) lookups, which is the intended win, but it adds persistent memory proportional to number of live requests times number of cached mm inputs per request. Cleanup happens in free_encoder_input/free; if a code path caches an input for a request but never calls free (e.g. an abort path that bypasses free), the set entry would leak. EncoderDecoderCacheManager does not initialize request_cached_ids, which is fine only because it overrides get_cached_input_ids/free; any future call into the base free_encoder_input on an enc-dec manager would AttributeError.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a long multimodal serving workload with request churn and monitor EncoderCacheManager.request_cached_ids size over time to confirm it does not grow unbounded.

findings.claim: In free_all inputs, get_cached_input_ids now returns the live request_cached_ids set directly (not a fresh copy), and free_encoder_input mutates/deletes that same set during iteration. The commit wraps it in list(...) to avoid mutation-during-iteration, which is correct, but this allocates a new list every free(). Minor; negligible relative to the algorithmic win.

The top-k full-space search pivots were changed from 1/4 and 2/4 (a genuine ternary/3-way split that shrinks the search interval to ~1/2 per iteration using two probes) to 1/3 and 2/3. However the two-pivot k-search still uses two full data scans per iteration (above_0 and above_1). Since the k-search was NOT converted to a single-pivot binary search (unlike the p-search), it retains the more expensive two-probe cost while the pivot placement change slightly alters convergence geometry. The bounded 18-iteration cap protects worst case, but if the k-search fails to converge quickly it may consistently exhaust more iterations, each doing two comparisons/min-reductions over the buffer, keeping this path more expensive than the now-binary p-search.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _topk_topp_kernel with combined top-k+top-p over a large vocab (e.g. 128k) and varied k, counting the number of while-loop iterations until convergence before/after this commit.

findings.claim: The p-pivot search was converted from a ternary search (two probes at 1/3 and 2/3 that shrink the interval to ~1/3 per iteration) to a binary search with a single probe at 0.5 (shrinks to 1/2 per iteration). Although each iteration is now roughly half the memory-bandwidth cost (one fused scan instead of two scans of the probs buffer), the interval shrinks more slowly (log(1/2) vs log(1/3) per iter), so to reach the same tolerance the loop may take ~1.58x as many iterations. Net effect on total scans of the vocab/probability buffer is roughly 0.5 * 1.58 ≈ 0.79x (a modest improvement), but if the extra iterations push against the 18-iteration cap for wide distributions the p_pivot precision can degrade, and the number of full-vocab scans (the dominant cost) does not shrink as much as a naive '2 passes -> 1 pass' reading of the diff suggests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark the top-p-only path (no top-k) with VOCAB_SIZE=128k across batch sizes and p in {0.5,0.9,0.99}, measuring kernel wall time and the achieved num_iters at convergence, comparing parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: When the binary p-search hits the num_iters>=18 / range-collapse fallback, it now assigns min_larger_prob/num_min_larger/p_pivots_sum from the *_0 accumulators computed at the current probe (0.5 midpoint), not necessarily the tightest bracket. Combined with the change of duplicate_logit's max reference from max_logit to max_sample, any drift in the resolved pivot can change how many tokens land in the duplicate-logit tie-breaking pass, which iterates the vocab again. If the pivot is less accurate, more tokens may be flagged as duplicates, increasing the work in the subsequent duplicate-resolution scans. This is speculative without a correctness test but could increase per-row work on tie-heavy distributions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct logits with many tied probability values near the top-p boundary and measure the cost of the duplicate-resolution loop (duplicate_count/keep_mask) before and after; also verify output token sets match a reference torch top-p implementation.

findings.claim: The ROCm path of GroupReduceMax uses 16-thread logical groups within a 64-lane wavefront (warpSize=64), and the register-resident packed kernel uses THREADS_PER_GROUP=8. On wave64 hardware these group sizes leave 4-8 lanes per group and pack multiple groups per wavefront, which was tuned for wave32 (CUDA). If the group-per-wavefront packing and block sizing (GetGroupsPerBlock / kGroupsPerBlockX) are not re-tuned for wave64, per-group thread utilization and occupancy on ROCm may be suboptimal versus a wave64-native tiling. This is speculative without a ROCm microbenchmark.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark per_token_group_fp8_quant on MI300 across a range of (num_tokens, hidden_dim, group_size=64/128) comparing the native kernel vs the Triton fallback and vs a wave64-tuned tiling.

findings.claim: rms_quant_fusion.py now unconditionally registers the group-quant fusion patterns (previously gated on current_platform.is_cuda()). The nested loop registers 2 (group_shape) * 2 (col_major) * 2 (e8m0) * 2 (tma) = 16 pattern instances each for two pattern classes. On ROCm this adds pattern-matcher registration and matching work to every torch.compile invocation of the fusion pass; on platforms where the C++ op is not actually present this could also fail at match time. The per-pattern cost is compile-time only, not runtime throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile fusion-pass registration/matching wall time on ROCm before/after this change for a representative model.

findings.claim: The NCCL_MAX_CTAS=8 override, previously only applied to async EPLB with the DeepEP low-latency backend, is now also applied whenever the MoE backend is deep_gemm_mega_moe and EPLB is enabled (even with synchronous EPLB). NCCL_MAX_CTAS caps the number of thread-block (CTA) grids NCCL kernels may use, i.e. the SM occupancy of every NCCL collective in the process. Capping it to 8 reduces the bandwidth of all NCCL collectives (all-reduce, all-gather, EPLB weight exchange, DP/TP communication), which can measurably lower throughput for large collectives. This is an intentional correctness tradeoff to avoid the cooperative-launch deadlock, but it will slow NCCL communication for mega-MoE + EPLB deployments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure NCCL all-reduce / all-gather bandwidth (e.g. nccl-tests or an in-framework collective microbenchmark) with NCCL_MAX_CTAS unset vs =8 on the target GPU/interconnect, and end-to-end decode throughput for DeepSeek v4 mega-MoE with EPLB enabled.

findings.claim: The MoE forward path (_run_mega_moe) now conditionally launches an extra Triton kernel (eplb_map_to_physical_and_record) per layer per forward step when EPLB is active. This adds a launch + a per-topk-id gather and atomic-add over expert_load_view on every decode/prefill step. This is inherent to EPLB and gated on logical_to_physical_map being set, so the overhead is only incurred when EPLB is enabled, but it is a small recurring per-step cost on the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step MoE layer latency with EPLB disabled vs enabled for DeepSeek v4 mega-MoE, isolating the eplb_map_to_physical_and_record kernel via profiling.

findings.claim: _map_global_expert_id changed from an O(1) index computation to an O(n_local_physical_experts) Python loop over the local expert range, invoked once per (expert_id, shard_id) during weight loading. With redundant experts this is a modest increase in checkpoint-load time but not on any runtime hot path; runtime performance is unaffected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model weight-load wall time for DeepSeek v4 mega-MoE with and without redundant experts to confirm the loop cost is negligible.

findings.claim: Before this change, the `not keys` and `not missing_indices` early-return branches called `dec_stored_request(req_id)` but did NOT call `request_queue.task_done()`, so each such request left the `queue.Queue`'s unfinished-task counter incremented. Since `request_queue.join()` is used (worker.py:1483) to wait for the send thread to drain, those missing `task_done()` calls could make `join()` block forever (a hang) or at minimum leave the counter permanently skewed. This commit wraps the whole body in try/finally with `task_done()` in `finally`, which incidentally fixes that missing-`task_done` accounting bug. That is a correctness/latency improvement, not a regression — but note the pre-change branches were the buggy ones. No new performance regression is introduced by the refactor itself: the same operations run in the same order.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enqueue N store requests where all blocks already exist (missing_indices empty) and where keys is empty, then call request_queue.join() and assert it returns promptly instead of hanging.

findings.claim: The change now re-raises the exception from `batch_is_exist` (via bare `raise` inside the inner try) which propagates out of `_handle_request` up to `run()`'s broad `except Exception` handler, but only after the `finally` runs `dec_stored_request`/`task_done`. This is behaviorally the same cost as before; the added `finally` block adds negligible per-request overhead. No measurable throughput impact expected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _handle_request success path throughput before/after to confirm try/finally overhead is negligible.

findings.claim: make_sibling_device_group() calls torch.distributed.new_group once per rank-group and is invoked to build a second NCCL communicator (the PP 'side stream' group) with the same membership as device_group. Each extra NCCL communicator permanently allocates GPU communication buffers (typically tens of MB per communicator, more with larger buffer sizes) on every rank, increasing steady-state GPU memory usage. It is also a collective that adds startup latency. This is only justified if concurrent PP broadcast on a separate stream is actually used; the memory cost is incurred for all PP runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory delta and init time with PP>1 before/after commit; compare kv-cache blocks available.

findings.claim: The new decode-throttle in scheduler.schedule() forces `pp_size` steps between successive decode steps of the same request (next_decode_eligible_step = current_step + pp_size). If the number of concurrently running requests is smaller than pp_size (e.g. low concurrency / interactive latency-sensitive workloads), each request can only be decoded every pp_size steps, so some pipeline stages run with fewer or zero eligible requests. This can reduce per-request decode throughput and increase token latency at low batch occupancy relative to a scheme that packs microbatches more tightly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Latency/throughput sweep at low concurrency (num_reqs < pp_size) with PP>1 async scheduling.

findings.claim: The throttle check iterates over the running list and skips ineligible requests (req_index += 1; continue) every schedule() call. When many requests are throttled (next_decode_eligible_step in the future), the scheduler still walks the full running list each step doing per-request comparisons without scheduling them, adding CPU overhead to every schedule iteration proportional to num_running.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile scheduler.schedule() CPU time with large num_running and PP>1.

findings.claim: set_default_max_concurrency(vllm_config.max_concurrent_batches) raises the default round-robin depth of all UVA/staged buffer pools from 2 to pp_size+1 (for V2+PP+async). Every pooled buffer now allocates (pp_size+1) copies instead of 2, increasing pinned-host/UVA memory roughly proportional to (pp_size+1)/2. For large PP sizes this is a non-trivial host memory increase applied globally to all pool-backed buffers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure host/UVA memory of buffer pools at PP=4 vs PP=1.

findings.claim: In step_with_batch_queue the early-return condition was changed to `return None, model_executed` and now also returns without blocking when `self.scheduler.has_requests()` is true even if no model was executed. Removing the `not batch_queue[-1][0].done()` guard means the loop may return control (None) more aggressively; if callers busy-loop when the returned executed flag is False but there is pending work, this could increase engine-loop CPU spin. Behavior depends on the caller's handling of the returned flag.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine core loop CPU utilization at steady state with PP>1 async scheduling.

findings.claim: The new FP16Vec16(const FP32Vec16&) conversion on PowerPC/VSX spills all 16 lanes to a stack array via 4 vec_xst, then converts each element with a scalar c10::Half(temp_fp32[i]) loop, then reloads via 2 vec_xl. This round-trips through memory and does 16 scalar float->half conversions per 16-element block. Comparable backends do this in-register: ARM uses native vcvt_f32_f16/f16_f32 intrinsics, and the s390x (vxe) backend uses fully-vectorized bit-manipulation helpers (fp32_to_fp16_bits/fp16_to_fp32_bits). The scalar+store/reload path is substantially slower and defeats vectorization in any kernel that converts FP16<->FP32 in its inner loop (e.g. FP16 activation/attention paths), and the store-then-load also creates a store-forwarding stall.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark converting a large FP16 buffer to FP32 and back (e.g. an elementwise op or attention over FP16 tensors) on a POWER9/10 CPU; compare against a vectorized bit-manipulation implementation like the s390x path.

findings.claim: FP32Vec16(const FP16Vec16&) has the same store-to-stack + 16-element scalar float(temp_fp16[i]) + reload pattern as the FP16 store path, so FP16->FP32 loads in inner loops are also scalarized rather than using in-register unpack/convert as done on ARM and s390x.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Same as above; isolate the FP16->FP32 direction.

findings.claim: An empty file literally named '=4.5.1' was accidentally committed (likely from a mistyped pip command redirecting to a file). It has no runtime effect but pollutes the repo; harmless for performance.

The selective scan is parallelized only over grid=(batch_size, dim); the sequence dimension is walked strictly sequentially in a per-token Python-level `for local_pos in range(chunk_tokens)` loop with a data-dependent recurrence (state = dA*state + delta_u*B). No chunked/parallel-scan decomposition is used, so per-program latency grows linearly with sequence length and long-context prefill will be dominated by this serial scan, leaving the XPU largely idle for long seqlen. This is much slower than a Blelloch/chunked scan formulation for long sequences.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark selective_scan_fwd on XPU across seqlen in {128, 512, 2048, 8192} at fixed batch*dim, measuring wall time vs seqlen to expose linear serial-scan scaling.

findings.claim: In the inner per-token loop, B and C are reloaded every token via strided gather `tl.load(B_base + dstate_offs*B_dstate_stride + pos)` / `C_base + dstate_offs*C_dstate_stride + pos`. Because loads are indexed by dstate_offs*stride these are non-contiguous (strided) gathers along dstate performed once per sequence position, giving poor memory coalescing and low bandwidth utilization on every scan step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile memory throughput of the inner loop loads (B/C) on XPU vs a version that preloads or reshapes B/C for contiguous access.

findings.claim: Each program only processes a single (batch, dim) pair over a BLOCK_DSTATE-wide vector (dstate typically 16-128). With grid=(batch, dim) and no BLOCK over dim, the work per program is a small reduction over dstate per token, which underutilizes vector width and wastes launch parallelism compared to batching multiple dim channels per program.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare throughput of grid=(batch,dim) launch vs a launch that blocks multiple dim channels per program on XPU.

findings.claim: softplus uses tl.math.log(tl.math.exp(x)+1.0) rather than log1p(exp(x)); the existing CUDA-path helper switches to log1p for numerical stability/efficiency. Minor, but exp+log with explicit +1 is slightly more expensive and less accurate than log1p.

The new `dynamic_padded_cutlass` custom op adds an extra host-side alignment check (`qx.shape[0] % 4 != 0`) before dispatching. Where the direct path (`run_direct` -> `cutlass_scaled_mm`) is taken, this is equivalent to the old `_padded_cutlass` fast path (which also branched on `dim % 4 == 0` and called `cutlass_scaled_mm` directly). So in eager mode the change is essentially a no-op for the already-aligned case and only removes a redundant zero-alloc/copy for the padded case. Net effect is a small improvement, not a regression; flagged only because the added indirection/wrapper functions add negligible Python-side overhead per call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cutlass fp8 block-scaled MM on Hopper (SM90) with M not divisible by 4 vs divisible by 4, comparing padded_cutlass vs dynamic_padded_cutlass latency.

findings.claim: Under torch.compile the op now uses `torch.cond` to select between the padded and direct paths based on `qx.shape[0] % 4`. If the batch/token dimension is static (specialized) at compile time, `torch.cond` forces both branches to be traced/compiled and prevents the compiler from constant-folding the branch, potentially adding graph-break/subgraph overhead and extra compiled code versus the old unconditional `padded_cutlass` which handled padding internally. If M is dynamic this is the intended behavior and fine, but on a fully static shape it is unnecessary overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile the block-scaled linear layer with a fixed M and inspect the compiled graph for torch.cond subgraphs; measure compile time and steady-state latency vs the previous padded_cutlass op.

findings.claim: During memory profiling, the encoder CUDA graph manager now fully captures ALL token-budget graphs via encoder_cudagraph_manager.capture(graph_pool=encoder_profiling_pool). Unlike the decoder path, which only samples the first two descriptors (profile_descs = descs[:2]) to estimate per-graph memory, the encoder path captures every budget graph, then throws them all away in the finally block (encoder_cudagraph_manager.clear()). The real EncoderCudaGraphManager later re-captures the same full set of graphs during capture_model(). This doubles the encoder graph-capture work at startup (each budget runs model.encoder_cudagraph_forward twice for warmup + capture, then again for the real capture), materially increasing initialization time for multimodal models with many token budgets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine init / capture_model wall-clock time for a multimodal model (e.g. Qwen2-VL) with cudagraph_mm_encoder enabled and several encoder token budgets, comparing this commit vs parent.

findings.claim: _create_encoder_cudagraph_manager() is now invoked both inside profile_cudagraph_memory() (temporary manager) and later via _maybe_init_encoder_cudagraph_manager() (persistent manager). Manager construction re-runs get_model() unwrapping, get_encoder_cudagraph_config(), and budget generation / range inference twice. This is minor CPU-side work but is duplicated per startup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile time spent constructing EncoderCudaGraphManager during startup with encoder cudagraphs enabled.

findings.claim: A separate encoder_profiling_pool (current_platform.graph_pool_handle()) is allocated for encoder profiling in addition to the decoder profiling_pool. During profiling both pools coexist and hold captured graph memory until the finally block clears them; because the encoder manager captures all budgets (not just 2), the transient peak GPU memory during profiling is higher than a sampled estimate would require, which could matter on memory-tight configs where profiling itself risks OOM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track peak GPU memory during profile_cudagraph_memory() with encoder cudagraphs enabled and many budgets.

findings.claim: Broadening the trial-import guard in _has_module from `except ImportError` to `except Exception` means that any exception raised during importlib.import_module (including slow or side-effect-heavy failures) is now swallowed and logged rather than propagated. This is a robustness change with negligible perf impact, but a module that raises a non-ImportError on import (e.g. hardware probe timeout) will now be silently treated as unavailable, potentially masking a slow import path rather than surfacing it.

Adding `and cfg.parallel_config.pipeline_parallel_size == 1` to `enable_allreduce_rms_fusion` disables the allreduce+RMSNorm fusion pass for all configurations that combine TP>1 with PP>1 on Hopper/Blackwell with flashinfer. Those configs will now execute un-fused allreduce and RMSNorm kernels, incurring the extra kernel launches and memory round-trips the fusion was designed to eliminate — a measurable throughput/latency cost per decode/prefill step for TP+PP deployments. This is, however, the intended cost of the fix: the commit disables the pass precisely because the fused op's GPU-side peer-signal spin-wait deadlocks (a hang, i.e. zero throughput) under PP>1 concurrent TP-subgroup warmup. Trading a lost fusion for avoiding a hang is net-positive; the finding is only that TP+PP users lose this optimization and there is no alternative fused path provided for them.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tokens/sec and per-step latency for a Hopper/Blackwell run with TP=2,PP=2 (flashinfer installed) at optimization level O2/O3 before vs after this commit; verify the pre-commit run actually completes (no deadlock) to quantify the fusion's standalone gain.

findings.claim: In SparseAttnCompressNormRopeStoreC4Kernel, tb_size is reduced from head_size//2 = 256 threads/block to num_warps*32 = 64 threads/block (num_warps drops 8->2). The grid launches one block per token (grid=(slot_mapping.shape[0],1,1)). With only 64 threads (2 warps) per block, each block occupies an SM with far fewer resident warps unless many blocks co-reside; if the CUDA occupancy is limited by blocks/SM or shared-memory rather than warps, total in-flight warps can drop, reducing latency hiding for the memory loads from state_cache. The compensating benefit is 128-bit vectorized copies (cp_f32x4) that coalesce 4 f32 per lane, which typically outweighs this, but the block is now quite small. Worth benchmarking occupancy vs. the 256-thread baseline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the C4 fused compressor kernel (SparseAttnCompressNormRopeStoreC4Kernel) at typical batch/token counts on the target GPU; compare achieved occupancy and kernel latency before/after (256 vs 64 threads/block).

findings.claim: The rewrite promotes per-thread scalars (max0/max1, sum0/sum1, product0/product1) to 8-wide rmem tensors (local_max, local_sum, local_product, x, q -> 5 arrays of 8 f32 = 40 registers plus kv_vals/score_vals copy buffers). Each lane now holds 4x more live state across the window loop, which raises register pressure per thread. If this pushes past the register cap it can lower occupancy or cause spills. Given only 2 warps/block this is likely tolerable, but should be checked with the compiler's register report.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Inspect ptxas register-per-thread report for the compiled C4 kernel before/after and confirm no local-memory spills; compare achieved occupancy.

findings.claim: In SparseAttnCompressKernel, rows_per_warp goes 8->16 and num_warps 16->8, halving threadblock size (tb_size 512->256) and shortening the global_max/global_sum shuffle reductions from 4 to 3 steps. This is a deliberate retuning (each warp handles more rows) and should be neutral-to-faster, but halving warps per block again reduces resident warps per block; net effect depends on how many blocks co-reside per SM. No correctness issue is evident, flagged only for validation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark SparseAttnCompressKernel (C128 split path) latency/throughput before vs after the rows_per_warp/num_warps retune on target hardware.

findings.claim: This change removes Eagle's dedicated CUDA graph memory pool (EagleCudaGraphManagerBase, which called torch.cuda.graph_pool_handle()). Both Eagle managers now inherit the base CudaGraphManager, whose __init__ sets self.pool = current_platform.get_global_graph_pool(), i.e. the SAME pool shared with the main target model's CUDA graphs. The removed code carried an explicit warning that Eagle's internal allocations (e.g. gumbel_sample temporaries) can conflict with the main model's allocations when sharing the pool. On the memory side, sharing one pool is generally *cheaper* (a shared graph pool reuses/reserves less GPU memory than two separate pools), so this is not obviously a memory regression — it is actually intended to reduce reserved memory footprint. The performance/reliability *risk* is the inverse of the previous rationale: if the buffer-reuse/liveness assumptions in a shared pool are violated (e.g. an Eagle temporary occupies memory the main model's captured graph also owns), a replay could read/overwrite live buffers, producing wrong draft tokens (silently lowering speculative acceptance rate and thus throughput) rather than a clean slowdown. Whether the original conflict warning was ever real is unclear from the diff alone; treat as a low-confidence correctness/throughput risk to validate via a spec-decode acceptance-rate benchmark.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Eagle/MTP speculative decoding with FULL CUDA graphs enabled (num_speculative_tokens>1) and compare (a) peak GPU reserved memory and (b) speculative-token acceptance rate / end-to-end tokens-per-second before vs after this commit. Confirm shared-pool capture does not corrupt draft outputs and that reserved memory is not higher.

findings.claim: In the non-OpenBLAS fallback of blas_gemm (blas_gemm.h), the GEMM is dispatched via `at::native::cpublas::gemm_no_downcast_stub.DEFAULT`. Using the `.DEFAULT` member explicitly selects the reference/default implementation instead of the ISA-dispatched (e.g. NEON/SVE-optimized) kernel that DispatchStub would otherwise pick for the running CPU. This is on the hot path: chunk_gated_delta_rule_kernel_impl issues ~7 GEMMs per chunk per value-head per sequence, so every one of those falls back to a slow, non-dispatched matmul on any ARM build whose PyTorch wheel does not ship libopenblas (VLLM_HAS_OPENBLAS undefined). Prefill throughput of GDN/Mamba models on such CPUs will be materially lower than the brgemm/AMX path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/kernels/mamba/cpu/test_cpu_gdn_ops.py-style chunk_gated_delta_rule prefill on an ARM CPU with and without libopenblas in the torch wheel; compare per-token prefill latency of the C++ kernel vs. the reference-BLAS fallback.

findings.claim: The bf16 fallback path in blas_gemm.h calls OpenBLAS `sbgemm_` (bf16xbf16->fp32). Not all OpenBLAS builds provide a well-optimized sbgemm kernel for a given microarchitecture; where it is missing or naive, the bf16 GDN GEMMs will run far slower than the AMX brgemm path they replace. Since the whole point of the change is to keep bf16 GEMMs fast on non-AMX CPUs, throughput will depend heavily on the wheel's OpenBLAS quality and could regress silently.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark sbgemm_ for the chunk-size x head-dim shapes used by the kernel (e.g. 128x128xhead_dim) on the target ARM CPU and compare against fp32 sgemm and the AMX brgemm baseline.

findings.claim: In the brgemm_supported()==false branches the code passes the unpacked source buffers directly to blas_gemm and skips pack_vnni/pack_vnni2, which is correct. However the thread scratch buffers (k_transpose, v_pack, k_beta_g_pack, etc.) are still allocated per thread (buff_size_16bit_per_thread) even though they are unused on the fallback path, wasting scratch memory allocation/zeroing on non-AVX512 builds. Minor memory-bandwidth/allocation overhead per parallel_for invocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-invocation setup overhead / peak scratch memory of chunk_gated_delta_rule_kernel_impl on a non-AVX512 build vs a build that sizes the scratch buffer to the packing-free path.

findings.claim: This commit flips the EPLB default `use_async` from False to True. The async path (async_worker.py / eplb_state.py) is explicitly designed to REDUCE forward-pass latency by moving expert-weight transfers into a background CUDA stream/thread instead of blocking the model forward. So the change is intended as a performance improvement, not a regression, for the common case. However, there are indirect performance considerations worth flagging: (1) When `communicator` is left at its default None and async is on, auto-selection uses the `torch_gloo` staged CPU communicator (per the docstring: 'torch_gloo for async, torch_nccl for sync'). Gloo staging routes every expert weight through a device->host->network->host->device copy path (see TorchDistGlooStagedEplbCommunicator.execute), which is far slower per-transfer than the NCCL P2P path used by sync mode. Because async EPLB is now the default, deployments that previously used the faster NCCL sync path will silently switch to a slower per-transfer backend unless they explicitly set communicator/use_async. (2) Async EPLB spawns a persistent daemon background thread (start_async_worker) and allocates additional send/recv/staging buffers and a second CUDA stream, adding steady-state host-thread and GPU-memory overhead even between rebalance events. These are the substantive performance-relevant side effects of making async the default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an MoE model (e.g. DeepSeek-V2-Lite or Qwen3-30B-A3B) with EPLB enabled and step_interval small enough to trigger several rebalances, comparing steady-state decode throughput/latency and per-rebalance stall between use_async=false (NCCL sync) and use_async=true default (gloo-staged async). Also measure GPU memory delta from the extra send/recv/staging buffers and background stream.

findings.claim: Enabling async by default causes a persistent daemon thread per worker (transfer_run_periodically loop) plus an extra CUDA stream to exist for the process lifetime. The GIL-synchronized coordination (rebalanced flag, CpuGpuEvent record/wait) between the main forward thread and the async worker introduces cross-thread GIL contention during rebalance windows; on CPython this can intermittently slow the main forward loop while the background thread holds the GIL for its Python-side transfer bookkeeping. This is now on by default for all EPLB users.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile main-thread forward-step latency variance during an active async EPLB rebalance vs an idle window, and vs sync EPLB, to quantify GIL contention / jitter introduced by the always-running background transfer thread.

findings.claim: prompt_embeds decode is offloaded to `self._executor`, the same pool shared by tokenization, chat-template rendering and multimodal preprocessing. With the default `renderer_num_workers=1`, a large `torch.load`/`to_dense`/base64-decode for prompt_embeds now occupies the single worker thread and can serialize behind / block concurrent tokenization work for other requests. This is still a net improvement over the previous behavior (which blocked the asyncio event loop entirely), but under embeds-heavy load with the default 1-worker pool the offloaded decode can throttle unrelated tokenization throughput. Consider a dedicated executor or ensuring renderer_num_workers>1 for embeds workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrently issue N requests mixing large prompt_embeds payloads and text prompts against the async renderer with renderer_num_workers=1; measure tokenization latency of text requests with vs. without concurrent embeds decodes.

findings.claim: `safe_load_prompt_embeds` runs under `torch.sparse.check_sparse_tensor_invariants()`. `asyncio.gather` now fans out multiple embed decodes concurrently onto the executor threads; if this context manager toggles a process-global (rather than thread-local) flag, concurrent enter/exit could momentarily disable the check for a peer thread. This is primarily a correctness/validation concern rather than performance, and pre-existing for the MM media paths, but it is newly exercised concurrently by the batched gather here.

ZentorchWNA16LinearKernel is inserted ahead of CPUWNA16LinearKernel in _POSSIBLE_KERNELS[CPU] and its can_implement() accepts a layer whenever the base CPUWNA16 accepts it, the platform is Zen and the zentorch ops are registered — it does NOT check the fast-path eligibility constraints (GPTQ CT pack layout, pack_factor==8, in_dim==pk_dim==1, etc.) that are only re-checked later inside _zentorch_woq_eligible() at process_weights_after_loading() time. For AWQ-packed or otherwise ineligible W4A16 layers on a Zen CPU, the selector will pick ZentorchWNA16LinearKernel, then silently fall back to CPUWNA16LinearKernel.process_weights_after_loading via super(). Functionally correct, but the effective GEMM path is then the exact same generic oneDNN path as before — i.e. no speedup — while the selection logic mis-reports the kernel. More importantly, layers that COULD be served by the eligible fast path but fail one of the many strict predicates get the slow path with no diagnostic beyond an info_once. This is a correctness-of-selection issue rather than a hard slowdown, but the mismatch between can_implement acceptance and actual fast-path eligibility means the ordering does not guarantee the promised performance improvement.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Zen CPU, load a W4A16 GPTQ model and a W4A16 AWQ model; confirm the AWQ case falls back to CPUWNA16 and matches its pre-change latency (no regression), and that GPTQ hits zentorch_woq_linear.

findings.claim: ZentorchInt8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_weights passes x directly to torch.ops.zentorch.zentorch_dynamic_qlinear without the 3D->2D reshape that the fallback CPUInt8ScaledMMLinearKernel._apply_weights_onednn performs (x.reshape(-1, x_shape[-1])). If zentorch_dynamic_qlinear does not itself flatten leading batch dims efficiently, prefill with >2D activation tensors could take a slower/less-vectorized path or force an internal copy. Cannot be confirmed without the zentorch op's implementation, hence speculative.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark zentorch_dynamic_qlinear on 2D vs 3D (batch, seq, hidden) int8 activations to check for a per-call reshape/copy overhead.

findings.claim: has_zentorch_op is called from can_implement/is_supported for every quantized linear layer during model construction. It does hasattr lookups on torch.ops.zentorch per op per layer. This runs at load time (not in the hot inference path) so the cost is negligible for throughput/latency, but for models with thousands of linear layers it adds a small, repeated attribute-resolution cost at startup. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model-load time on a large W4A16/W8A8 model on Zen CPU before/after; expect negligible difference.

findings.claim: The original code called `int(indptr[-1].item())` to size the `flat` buffer. Because `indptr` is a CUDA tensor computed via `torch.cumsum`, `.item()` forces a device-to-host synchronization (a full pipeline stall): the CPU must wait for all previously queued GPU work to complete before it can read the value. In a hot attention path this serializes the CPU launch stream against the GPU each call. The commit removes this by sizing `flat` as `indices.shape[0] * max_width` (host-known values), eliminating the stall. This diff is a performance FIX, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark build_ragged_indices_from_dense in the sparse attention decode loop, comparing wall-clock latency and CPU-launch-to-GPU-exec overlap before/after removing the .item() sync.

findings.claim: The new `flat` buffer is sized to `indices.shape[0] * max_width` (the full dense capacity) rather than to the actual sum of lengths (`indptr[-1]`). When many rows have `lengths` far below `max_width`, this over-allocates the flat indices buffer. This slightly increases peak memory and the amount of memory written by the subsequent kernel compared to the tight allocation, but is generally an acceptable tradeoff for removing the sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak allocator memory for build_ragged_indices_from_dense with sparse (low-fill) length distributions vs the tight indptr[-1] allocation.

findings.claim: select_deepseek_v4_mxfp4_moe_backend now prefers AITER_MXFP4_BF16 over TRITON_UNFUSED for DeepSeek-V4 on ROCm. This changes the runtime MoE kernel that executes on every forward pass. The commit's stated rationale is 'better perf + accuracy after shuffle/TP-offset fixes', so this is likely a net win for throughput/latency, but it is a runtime-path change: if the AITER FlyDSL path is slower or falls back at runtime for certain shapes/routing modes, this could degrade decode/prefill latency. Worth benchmarking end-to-end tokens/s vs the previous Triton-unfused default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V4 MXFP4 MoE decode+prefill throughput/latency on ROCm comparing AITER_MXFP4_BF16 vs TRITON_UNFUSED backends across TP sizes.

findings.claim: convert_weight_to_mxfp4_moe_kernel_format replaces the previous in-place de-interleave (w13_weight.view(...).copy_(...)) plus rocm_aiter_ops.shuffle_weight_a16w4 with aiter's shuffle_weight((16,16)) + e8m0_shuffle, wrapping results in new torch.nn.Parameter objects. Wrapping in fresh Parameters allocates new tensors rather than reusing the existing storage (the old code did an in-place copy_ for w13), which transiently increases host/device memory during weight conversion. This runs once at load time, so runtime throughput is unaffected; the concern is peak memory during model loading for large MoE weights.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory during DeepSeek-V4 MXFP4 weight loading before/after this change.

findings.claim: The TP-offset fix in _load_w13/_load_w2 changes start_offset from shard_size*tp_rank (padded) to loaded_per_rank*tp_rank (unpadded), computing loaded_per_rank = loaded_weight.shape[shard_dim] // tp_size. If loaded_weight.shape[shard_dim] is not evenly divisible by tp_size, integer division truncates and narrow_size = min(loaded_per_rank, available) may under-load the last rank's shard, leaving part of the parameter uninitialized. That is a correctness issue rather than pure perf, but a mis-loaded weight could trigger runtime fallback kernels or NaN-guard recompute paths. Primarily flagged for validation.

This commit enables the aiter sampler by default (removing the DISABLE_AITER_SAMPLER fallback to forward_native). The now-active top-k-only branch of aiter_sample uses torch.multinomial(renorm_probs, num_samples=1), which triggers a CPU-GPU synchronization on every top-k-only sampling step. The codebase's own random_sample() docstring explicitly avoids torch.multinomial for exactly this reason ('torch.multinomial causes CPU-GPU synchronization'). For decode workloads that use top-k without top-p, this per-step sync can stall async scheduling and hurt throughput/latency relative to the previously-active forward_native path (which uses the div/exponential trick). Whether this is a net regression depends on whether the aiter top-k renorm kernel outweighs the sync cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode throughput/latency on ROCm with sampling params using only top_k (no top_p) before and after this commit, measuring per-step host stall from torch.multinomial vs the native random_sample path.

findings.claim: The new `offload_prompt_only` flag defaults to `True` (base.py: `self.extra_config.get("offload_prompt_only", True)`). This changes the default behavior of the CPU offload connector so that decode-phase (generated) KV blocks are no longer offloaded to CPU. For workloads that rely on caching decode-phase KV (e.g. multi-turn conversations that keep prior generated tokens, or long-generation reuse), this reduces the CPU-offload cache hit rate, causing more full recomputes/prefills on subsequent turns and degrading throughput/latency. The default flips existing behavior silently rather than being opt-in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-turn conversation workload measuring prefix-cache/offload hit rate and TTFT across turns with offload_prompt_only=True vs False (previous default of offloading all blocks).

findings.claim: The clamp `num_offloadable_tokens = min(num_offloadable_tokens, req.num_prompt_tokens)` is evaluated on every scheduling step for every request when offload_prompt_only is enabled. This is a cheap min() and negligible, but note that once prompt blocks are stored, next_stored_idx stops advancing, so no further store jobs are created for decode blocks — this is the intended savings (fewer store transfers), not a slowdown.

On CPU, apply_top_k_top_p now unconditionally routes to the Triton kernel (apply_top_k_top_p_triton) whenever HAS_TRITON, with no batch-size guard. The GPU path deliberately gates Triton behind `logits.shape[0] >= 8` because the Triton pivot-search kernel is slower than the plain PyTorch sort/topk for tiny batches. CPU decode is dominated by batch_size==1 requests (single-token autoregressive decode), which is exactly the small-batch regime where Triton has the worst relative overhead (kernel launch + JIT dispatch + few parallel programs). This will increase per-step sampling latency for the common single/low-batch CPU decode case compared to the previous PyTorch-native path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a CPU build with triton-cpu, benchmark apply_top_k_top_p latency for batch sizes 1,2,4,8 with a realistic vocab (e.g. 128k) top-k and top-p set, comparing the Triton path vs apply_top_k_top_p_pytorch(allow_cpu_sync=True).

findings.claim: forward_native previously called apply_top_k_top_p_pytorch(logits, k, p, allow_cpu_sync=True); it now calls apply_top_k_top_p(logits, k, p). forward_native is the assigned forward for POWERPC/RISCV CPU (chosen specifically to avoid Triton/torch.compile issues) and for other non-CUDA fallbacks. On CPU this now dispatches into the Triton kernel, defeating the intended native fallback and potentially reintroducing the argmax/torch.compile problems that PowerPC/RISCV fell back to native to avoid, as well as running Triton where it was intentionally not used.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/v1/sample sampler correctness + a decode latency benchmark on a PowerPC/RISCV-emulated or generic CPU without a fast triton-cpu build to confirm forward_native no longer avoids Triton.

findings.claim: Dropping allow_cpu_sync=True from the forward_native call means that for the top-k-only case (p is None) the code no longer takes the apply_top_k_only fast path (which avoids sorting the full vocab). On any fallback platform where apply_top_k_top_p ends up in the PyTorch branch with allow_cpu_sync=False, a top-k-only request now performs a full descending sort of the entire vocab instead of a cheaper topk+gather, increasing sampling cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark top-k-only sampling (p=None) via forward_native on a fallback path for large vocab, comparing full-sort vs apply_top_k_only.

findings.claim: In build(), the new `clamped_seq_lens` fingerprint reads `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs]`. `seq_lens_cpu` is a lazily-materialized property that does `self.seq_lens.to('cpu')` (a device->host copy + sync) the first time it is accessed. If nothing else in this decode path touches `seq_lens_cpu`, this micro-opt introduces a new per-step GPU->CPU synchronization (D2H copy of the seq_lens tensor) on the critical decode path, which can stall the pipeline and hurt decode latency — potentially negating (or exceeding) the savings from skipping the get_mla_metadata_v1 launch. Confirm whether seq_lens_cpu is already materialized elsewhere in this step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv3.2 MI355X TP4 decode: measure per-step build() latency and check for added D2H syncs (torch profiler / nvtx) with and without the fingerprint.

findings.claim: The metadata fingerprint calls `clamped_seq_lens.tobytes()` and stores it in `_prev_metadata_key`, then compares full byte-strings each step. For large batch sizes this is an O(num_reqs) per-step CPU allocation + hash/compare. It is cheap relative to a kernel launch when it succeeds in skipping work, but adds unconditional CPU work every step even on cache-miss steps. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark build() CPU time at max batch size.

findings.claim: The metadata-skip caches persistent MLA work metadata (`_mla_work_*` / `_mla_reduce_*` buffers) keyed only on (num_tokens, max_query_len, num_heads, min(seq_lens, topk_tokens)). Those same buffers are shared/returned by reference in every ROCMAiterMLASparseMetadata. If two consecutive decode steps hit the same key but the actual per-request layout differs in a way not captured by the clamped-seq-lens fingerprint (e.g. request ordering / block-table content affecting work-stealing splits), the kernel will consume stale work metadata, which can silently misbalance CU work (throughput loss) or, worse, index incorrectly. The correctness of the skip hinges entirely on the fingerprint being a complete key; if it is not, this is a latent hang/incorrectness risk rather than a perf win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DSv3.2 decode with varying batch shapes that collide on the fingerprint but differ in per-req seq layout; compare outputs and CU utilization vs. always-recompute baseline.

findings.claim: The optimization to only re-zero the shrink-tail of `paged_kv_indices`/`req_id_per_token_buffer` relies on `_prev_indices_extent`/`_prev_req_extent` being accurate. Because these buffers are also used for cudagraph capture/replay (the original code zero-filled explicitly 'for cudagraphs'), if a cudagraph is captured at one extent and replayed at a smaller one, stale non-zero entries beyond new_indices_extent could be read by the captured graph (which reads fixed address ranges). The change assumes 'entries past new_indices_extent are never read', which is true for the dynamic path via paged_kv_indptr but may not hold for a statically-captured cudagraph. If wrong, this produces incorrect attention rather than a slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture cudagraph at large batch, replay at small batch, compare attention output to eager path.

findings.claim: The change adds a new N=5 template instantiation of wvSplitK_hf_sml_ (via WVSPLIT_TILE_CFG(...,5) for both wave32 and wave64). Since N is a template parameter, this instantiates additional copies of the kernel with per-N register arrays (float sum[N][YTILE], scalar8 sum4[N][YTILE], bigType bigA[N][UNRL]). With N=5, register pressure grows ~25% versus N=4, which can reduce occupancy (fewer concurrent waves per CU) for that specialization. This only affects the N=5 speculative-decoding path, not existing N<=4 paths.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark wvSplitK skinny GEMM at N=5 vs falling back to torch.nn.functional.linear on gfx942/gfx950, measuring latency and achieved occupancy (rocprof) across representative m,k shapes.

findings.claim: The Python dispatch threshold in rocm_unquantized_gemm_impl is widened from `0 < n <= 4` to `0 < n <= 5`, so for n==5 (e.g. 5 speculative-decode tokens) the skinny wvSplitK kernel is now used instead of the previous fallback (hipblaslt/torch linear). wvSplitK is a memory-bound skinny-GEMM kernel tuned for very small n; if the N=5 tile config is not well-tuned it could be slower than the general GEMM path for some m/k shapes. Worth validating that N=5 is actually faster than the fallback for the target shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end speculative-decoding decode throughput (num_speculative_tokens=4, giving n=5) with and without the wvSplitK N=5 path enabled on ROCm.

findings.claim: The fp32_router_gemm kernel is essentially a bandwidth-bound skinny GEMV (M<=32, N=256, K=3072). When the caller passes fp32 activations (MiniMaxM2MoE.forward converts hidden_states via .to(torch.float32) before the gate), the activation path uses VPT=4 and k_iterations=6 (3072/512), reading 4 bytes/element = ~12KB per token-column, versus the bf16 activation path (VPT=8, k_iterations=3, ~6KB). Since the kernel supports bf16 activation directly, passing bf16 activation would roughly halve activation memory traffic and avoid the separate full-tensor .to(float32) conversion op before the gate call. The dominant cost is the 3MB fp32 weight read, so the net win is small at M=1 but grows toward M=32.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmark_router_gemm.py --model MiniMax-M2 comparing bf16-activation vs fp32-activation input at M=1,8,16,32, and end-to-end MoE gate latency with/without the .to(float32) conversion

findings.claim: The main K-reduction loop `for (int ki = 0; ki < k_iterations; ki++)` in fp32_router_gemm_kernel is not annotated with #pragma unroll, unlike the surrounding inner loops. k_iterations is a small compile-time constant (3 for bf16, 6 for fp32) so the compiler will most likely unroll it anyway, but an explicit unroll would guarantee the register-resident b_float/a_float accumulation avoids loop overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: microbenchmark the kernel with and without #pragma unroll on the K loop at M=1 and M=32

findings.claim: The kernel splits K across gridDim.z = K/BLOCK_KN_SIZE blocks and accumulates each block's partial sum into the T-typed output via a 64-bit atomicCAS retry loop (atomic_add_pk4_f16/_bf16). On gfx11 there is no native packed-fp16/bf16 atomic add, so each write is a read-modify-write CAS that must retry on contention. All z-blocks that own the same (m,n) output tile contend on the *same* 8-byte word. For typical LLM K (e.g. 4096 -> gridDim.z=16), 16 blocks race on each output word; under contention the CAS loop spins, serializing the epilogue writes and degrading throughput especially at larger M where many rows write concurrently. An fp32-scratch + single-pass reduction (the design the comment explicitly rejected to save memory) avoids atomics entirely. This is a latency/throughput risk that scales with K-splits and CU occupancy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench gemm_q4_kernel_rdna3 on gfx1100 sweeping K in {2048,4096,8192,16384} at fixed M,N; compare achieved TFLOP/s and epilogue time vs an fp32-accumulator/split-K-reduce variant to quantify atomicCAS retry overhead.

findings.claim: In atomic_add_pk4_f16/_bf16 the initial `old = *addr_u` is a plain (non-atomic) 64-bit load before entering the CAS loop. This is functionally handled by the retry, but it forces every accumulating block to issue a redundant global load before its first CAS; combined with the guaranteed CAS on each 4-column write it doubles memory traffic per output element relative to a plain store, contributing to the epilogue cost noted above.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile global memory traffic (rocprof, FETCH_SIZE/WRITE_SIZE) of the epilogue with vs without the pre-load, at M in {1,8,32}.

findings.claim: BLOCK_KN_SIZE=256 with gridDim.z=K/256 is a fixed heuristic. The author's own comment notes that for small M (decode, M=1) with K=N=4096 only ~8 of 96 CUs are saturated, breaking memory-latency hiding for the memory-bound fp16 decode path. The chosen constant (256) is a compromise that leaves the fp16 decode path under-occupied on large-CU parts; there is no adaptive block/grid sizing based on M or CU count, so decode latency on wide GPUs may be suboptimal.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep BLOCK_KN_SIZE and derived gridDim.z for M=1 decode on gfx1100 (RX7900XTX, 96 CU) at K=N in {4096,8192}; measure achieved occupancy and latency.

findings.claim: bf16 forwards to the WMMA kernel only at M>=16 while fp16 always stays scalar. These are hard-coded crossover thresholds not validated against all K/N shapes or newer ROCm compilers; if the real crossover point differs for a given model's layer shapes, the scalar path can be selected where WMMA would be faster (or vice versa), yielding measurable slowdown on prefill-heavy workloads at M just below the threshold.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep M around the 16 boundary (M in {8,12,16,24,32,48,64}) for representative Qwen/Llama layer shapes; compare scalar vs WMMA path latency to validate the crossover.

findings.claim: When PCT auto-detection fires (which the docstring says is the DEFAULT behavior for a numa_bind worker on the target Xeon 6776P/6774P/6962P SKUs, i.e. DGX B300), `_maybe_get_pct_cpu_binding` restricts each worker to only the priority cores where `cpu_id % stride in (0,1)`. With stride=16 and a node cpulist like `0-31,64-95` (64 threads) this yields only 8 CPUs — 1/8 of the NUMA node's threads. Previously (numa_bind=True with no explicit numa_bind_cpus) `_get_cpu_binding` returned None, so the worker got `--cpunodebind=<node>` and access to the ENTIRE node's CPUs. Confining a GPU worker to 1/8 of the cores can throttle CPU-side work (tokenization/detokenization, sampling/logits post-processing, input prep, NCCL/CUDA progress threads, Python GC) and reduce throughput / raise latency at high concurrency, since priority cores boost per-core frequency but drastically cut total CPU parallelism. This is now on-by-default on the exact hardware the PR targets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Xeon 6776P (or by stubbing the PCT gates), run an online serving benchmark (e.g. sharegpu, high concurrency) with numa_bind enabled comparing the new PCT physcpubind (8 cores) vs the old full-node cpunodebind (~64 threads); measure output token throughput and TTFT/ITL under load.

findings.claim: `_get_numactl_enginecore_args` now emits `--membind=<all shard nodes>` (e.g. `0,1`) for any EngineCore whose DP shard spans multiple NUMA nodes. This is deliberate (a memory superset so worker spawns pass strict --physcpubind validation), but it means EngineCore's own allocations (scheduler state, request/block queues, KV block manager metadata) are no longer pinned to a single node and can be satisfied from a remote node, adding cross-socket memory latency for EngineCore's hot data structures compared to a single-node bind. Previously the process was bound to a single numa node's memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare EngineCore-side scheduling latency / step time on a TP shard that spans two NUMA nodes with the new multi-node --membind vs a single-node membind, e.g. measure per-step scheduler overhead under high request rate.

findings.claim: `_maybe_get_pct_cpu_binding` reads `/proc/cpuinfo`, `acpi_cppc/highest_perf`, and each node's `cpulist` on every worker/EngineCore spawn. `_pct_sku_config` is @cache'd so the cpuinfo/highest_perf reads are amortized, but the per-node `cpulist` reads and set unions are re-done on each call. This runs only at subprocess-launch time (one-time), so the impact is negligible, but it is unmemoized redundant filesystem work per spawn.

In `forward`, the change removed the pre-computed `g1 = fused_kda_gate(...)` and replaced it with a plain `rearrange`, deferring the gate computation into the attention op. This is correct for the prefill path (fused into `chunk_kda_with_fused_gate`). However, in the decode branch of `_forward` (num_prefills == 0), `fused_kda_gate(...)` is now called *inside* `_forward`, on every decode step, computing softplus over the raw gate on the whole `g1` tensor (rearrange -> fused_kda_gate -> unsqueeze). Because `_forward` runs under the custom op `torch.ops.vllm.kda_attention`, this now runs a rearrange+kernel launch per decode iteration that previously ran once eagerly in `forward`. Net effect is neutral-to-slightly-worse for decode (extra rearrange round-trips), but not a regression in FLOPs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only latency for KimiGatedDeltaNet layer (num_prefills==0) comparing per-step wall time before/after.

findings.claim: The new `kda_gate_cumsum_fwd_kernel` uses `@triton.autotune` over 6 configs (BD in {32,64} x num_warps in {2,4,8}) keyed on [H, D, BT, IS_VARLEN]. The first invocation for each new key shape will trigger autotuning (up to 6 recompilation/benchmark passes), adding one-time latency spikes when a new sequence-length/varlen shape is seen. Since chunk kernels typically bucket to fixed BT/D this is minor, but repeated autotune triggers on shape churn could add warmup jitter.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-call latency for `chunk_kda_with_fused_gate` across varying cu_seqlens shapes to observe autotune warmup cost.

findings.claim: `fused_kda_gate_chunk_cumsum` allocates a fresh `y = torch.empty_like(raw_g, dtype=output_dtype or raw_g.dtype)` (fp32 by default). This is the same allocation the old `chunk_local_cumsum` path did (it also allocated an fp32 output), so it is not a regression; but the fused kernel computes cumsum via a `tl.dot` with a lower-triangular mask matrix (BT x BT) for every (D-block, chunk, B*H) program, which is more FLOPs than a scalar cumsum. For the vector cumsum path the original also used tl.dot, so this is expected. No net regression identified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark `fused_kda_gate_chunk_cumsum` vs `chunk_local_cumsum + gate + scale` for typical (T,H,D).

findings.claim: `senders_for_outputs` collects into a freshly allocated `Vec<Option<OutputSender>>` for every engine output batch on the hot dispatcher path. The previous `take_sender_for_output` processed outputs one at a time with no intermediate collection. For large batches this adds one heap allocation per batch plus cloning of each active request's `OutputSender` into the vector (the senders are `mpsc::UnboundedSender`, whose clone is a refcount bump, so cheap, but the Vec growth/allocation is new). This is offset by the reduced lock churn, so net effect is likely positive, but the extra per-batch allocation is a measurable cost that could be avoided by draining/zipping without materializing the Vec.
