findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark run_output_dispatcher_loop throughput with large RequestBatch sizes, comparing per-output lock (parent) vs batched-lock-with-Vec (this commit) to confirm the batched version is a net win and the Vec allocation is not dominant.

findings.claim: `get_moe_dispatch_policy` is decorated with `@if_aiter_supported`, which calls `is_aiter_found_and_supported()` on EVERY invocation (not cached). This method is now called on the MoE hot path — once per `AiterExperts.apply()` call, i.e. every forward pass of every MoE layer. `is_aiter_found_and_supported()` does `current_platform.is_rocm()` plus `on_mi3xx()` and re-imports `vllm.platforms.rocm` each time. Even though `on_mi3xx()` reads a cached bool, the repeated Python-level dispatch (decorator wrapper + import statement + method call chain) adds per-layer host overhead that scales with model depth and inference step count. The intended cache field `_MOE_DISPATCH_POLICY` only avoids the env-var read, not the decorator's support-check.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark: time 1e6 calls of rocm_aiter_ops.get_moe_dispatch_policy() vs directly reading a cached int; compare per-decode-step CPU overhead on a deep MoE model (e.g. DeepSeek-V3, 60+ layers) at low batch size where host overhead dominates.

findings.claim: The cache guard uses `if cls._MOE_DISPATCH_POLICY is None`. This works for the current int values (0/1/2 are all not-None), but the `@if_aiter_supported` decorator returns `None` when aiter is not supported. On a non-supported platform, `get_moe_dispatch_policy()` returns `None`, which is then passed as `moe_sorting_dispatch_policy` down to the aiter kernel call. If reached, this could cause a type error or fall back to a slow default path. In practice this path is only hit when aiter MoE is enabled, so risk is low.

The two `while True` polling loops in save_kv_layer (`write_ready_flags`) and start_load_kv (`load_ready_flag`) previously ran as tight busy-spins with a bare `continue` (100% CPU spin with no sleep). This commit ADDS `time.sleep(0.001)` to those loops, which mitigates the original spin. However, the pre-existing tight spin (the `continue`-only version) was CPU-burning on the worker thread; the new version adds a 1ms sleep on every iteration. While the sleep reduces CPU, it also introduces up to ~1ms latency granularity per poll before the ready flag is observed, on the hot KV-transfer path (per request at high concurrency). This is a minor latency addition but a large CPU-utilization improvement over the old spin.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure prefill worker CPU utilization and per-request KV-transfer completion latency at high concurrency (e.g. 256 concurrent requests) before and after, comparing the busy-spin vs sleep(0.001).

findings.claim: waiting_for_transfer_complete() was changed from a blocking `status.Wait()` (which sleeps in the RDMA library until completion) to a busy poll loop calling `status.Succeeded()`/`status.Failed()` with `time.sleep(0.001)`. The old code blocked efficiently on Wait(); the new code polls every 1ms until all statuses complete. This is called once per request when all layers are done (on the producer's write-worker thread). Poll granularity adds up to ~1ms latency and a CPU wakeup every 1ms for every in-flight transfer. Under high concurrency with many outstanding transfers this converts an efficient blocking wait into repeated polling, increasing CPU usage on the transfer thread and adding per-transfer latency jitter.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare producer write-worker thread CPU and end-to-end KV transfer completion latency under sustained high concurrency, blocking Wait() vs the new 1ms poll loop.

findings.claim: update_connector_output is now called every scheduler step and iterates over connector_output.finished_sending twice plus building an `expired_reqs` list comprehension over all _deferred_send_deadlines. For producers with many outstanding deferred sends at high concurrency, this adds O(N) work (two passes over finished_sending and one over deadlines) to every scheduler step on the critical scheduling path. The early-return when _deferred_send_deadlines is empty mitigates it in idle cases, but under load the dict is non-empty and the work runs every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile scheduler step time on the producer with hundreds of concurrent deferred sends outstanding, measuring added per-step overhead of update_connector_output.

findings.category: unbounded set growth / retry each step
findings.claim: _unmatched_write_completions accumulates write completions that arrived before their transfer_id was registered and is retried every get_finished() call. If a transfer_id is never registered (lost/mismatched), those entries are never removed and the set grows unboundedly, and each get_finished iterates the whole set to compute matched_xfer_ids every scheduler step. At high concurrency with lost mappings this is a slow memory leak plus growing per-step iteration cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Inject transfer completions with unregistered transfer_ids under load and monitor _unmatched_write_completions size and get_finished latency growth over time.

findings.claim: enable_notification=False disables MoRIIO's inbound transfer status notifications so completion is signaled purely via ZMQ. This is intended to fix the hang, but it means the connector relies entirely on polling status objects (Succeeded/Failed) instead of the library's notification mechanism, which can increase per-transfer polling work. Correctness/perf tradeoff should be validated: without notifications, completion detection latency is bounded by the ZMQ notify path and the 1ms poll loops.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare KV transfer completion latency and NIC utilization with enable_notification=True vs False at high concurrency to confirm no throughput regression from disabling notifications.

findings.claim: By removing @support_torch_compile from DeepseekV4Model (nvidia/model.py and amd/model.py) and DeepSeekV4MTP (nvidia/mtp.py, amd/mtp.py), the full transformer forward is no longer captured by torch.compile/Inductor. Only two hand-fused RMSNorm ops (fused_mtp_input_rmsnorm, mtp_shared_head_rmsnorm) are provided as replacements, covering just the enorm/hnorm/shared-head-norm at the top and end of the MTP path. All other elementwise ops, residual adds, and inter-op fusions across the whole decoder stack (both the main model and the MTP block) that Inductor previously fused now run as many separate eager kernels. This increases per-layer kernel-launch count and CPU dispatch overhead, degrading throughput/latency for DeepSeek V4, unless the breakable-cudagraph path fully amortizes it (which it only does for captured shapes).
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode throughput/latency for DeepSeek V4 with speculative MTP, comparing torch.compile PIECEWISE path vs the new breakable-cudagraph path, at both small (bs=1) and large batch sizes.

findings.claim: The auto-enable of VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH replaces the previous PIECEWISE torch.compile+cudagraph path. If breakable cudagraph fails to capture some shapes/branches (e.g. dynamic MTP spec-step behavior, MoE routing, or the newly-added drafter wrapping), the model may fall back to eager execution for those shapes, losing cudagraph replay benefits and significantly reducing GPU utilization vs the prior compiled path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure fraction of decode steps served from cudagraph replay vs eager under the breakable-cudagraph path for DeepSeek V4, and compare decode latency.

findings.claim: mtp_shared_head_rmsnorm replaces mtp_layer.shared_head(hidden_states) with a standalone Triton RMSNorm launch. Previously under torch.compile this norm could be fused with adjacent ops (or with the head GEMM epilogue path). As a separate always-launched kernel it adds one extra kernel dispatch per compute_logits call; the shared head norm is a small (T,H) op so launch overhead can dominate at small T.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark mtp_shared_head_rmsnorm launch+exec time vs fused RMSNorm at small token counts (T=1..8).

findings.claim: In _fused_mtp_input_rmsnorm_kernel the grid is (num_tokens, hc_mult+1) where task 0 does enorm and tasks 1..hc_mult do hnorm. Every enorm program (pid_task==0) redundantly reloads positions and does a branch; more importantly the enorm and hnorm share BLOCK_SIZE = next_power_of_2(hidden), so all hc_mult+1 programs per token launch identical-width blocks. This is fine functionally but a single fused RMSNorm over a (T, (hc_mult+1)*H) view could reduce launch grid size; current layout multiplies program count by (hc_mult+1). Likely minor since these are tiny norm kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare fused_mtp_input_rmsnorm kernel time vs separate enorm+hnorm RMSNorm launches across hc_mult values and token counts.

findings.claim: Each RotaryEmbeddingBase instance with use_aiter=True and a non-bf16 base cache now allocates and permanently holds an additional bf16 copy of cos_sin_cache (cos_sin_cache_bf16). This is a one-time, per-instance memory increase (roughly the size of the bf16 cache, ~half the fp32 cache or full size if base cache is fp32). For models with many rotary layers this adds up, though it is intentional and small relative to the perf win of avoiding repeated casts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure device memory footprint of a GPT-OSS model on ROCm with AITER enabled before/after this change (sum over all rotary layers).

findings.claim: The added conditional in _match_cos_sin_cache_dtype (use_aiter / is_compiling / dtype check plus getattr with default) executes only after the fast-path early return fails, so it adds negligible per-call overhead. The change overall is a net performance improvement because it removes a repeated cos_sin_cache.to(bf16) cast on every torch.compile trace of the AITER rotary path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile-time / graph-build latency of the AITER rotary embedding path on ROCm before vs after; verify no regression in steady-state forward latency.

findings.claim: fused_experts_impl now unconditionally allocates a new output buffer via torch.empty_like(hidden_states) instead of optionally writing in place (out_hidden_states = hidden_states if inplace else torch.empty_like(...)). Previously, on torch < 2.9 with no shared experts, the MoE forward could reuse the input activation tensor and avoid a full activation-sized allocation per MoE layer. After this change the extra allocation always happens. On torch >= 2.9 the inplace path was already gated off (disable_inplace() returns True and the config default disable_inplace=True), so there is no regression there; the extra cost only materializes on torch < 2.9. Impact is a per-layer activation-sized allocation (M x K) that must be malloc'd/freed by the caching allocator on every MoE forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a dense MoE model (e.g. Mixtral) forward/decode throughput on torch < 2.9 comparing this commit vs parent; watch per-layer allocator activity and end-to-end tokens/s.

findings.claim: FusedMoEKernelModularImpl.apply now always does output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states) instead of reusing hidden_states in the inplace branch. Same trade-off as fused_experts_impl: the modular MoE path loses the ability to write results into the input buffer, incurring an extra activation-sized allocation per invocation. This only affected torch < 2.9 (where disable_inplace() was False and inplace could be selected by the oracles), so the practical impact is confined to older torch builds; on torch >= 2.9 inplace was already asserted off.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Modular-kernel MoE microbenchmark (e.g. tests/kernels/moe) on torch < 2.9 measuring peak memory and latency vs parent.

findings.claim: _resolve_gdn_prefill_backend is now called once per ChunkGatedDeltaRule instance (per layer) at model init. On Blackwell with head_k_dim==128 and cuda_runtime>=13, when flashinfer is auto/requested, it calls _is_libs_cu13_install_intact(), which re-hashes every file the nvidia-cutlass-dsl-libs-cu13 wheel owns (SHA-256 of full file contents). This is @functools.cache'd so it only runs once per process, so amortized cost is one-time only; not a per-token runtime cost. Flagged as a minor init latency concern, not a runtime throughput/latency regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model-init time on Blackwell with gdn_prefill_backend=auto vs triton; confirm _is_libs_cu13_install_intact runs at most once.

findings.claim: In `_tilelang_hc_prenorm_gemm`, when DeepGEMM is unavailable (the ROCm path this PR enables) `n_splits` is forced to 1 for the pre-norm GEMM (see mhc_pre_tilelang / mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang). On CUDA the previous code computed `n_splits` via `compute_num_split` to spread the K reduction across many SMs (split-K). With `n_splits=1` and the `hc_prenorm_gemm_tilelang` kernel gridded over (num_tokens, n_tiles, n_splits), the K-dimension (hc_mult*hidden_size, e.g. tens of thousands) is reduced entirely inside a single block per token with no split-K parallelism. For medium token counts (128 <= num_tokens < 1024 outside the block_m path) this under-utilizes the GPU: only num_tokens*n_tiles blocks are launched and each performs a long serial k_iters loop, leaving many SMs idle and serializing the largest reduction of the pre block.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark mhc_pre_tilelang on ROCm across num_tokens in {64,128,256,512,1024,2048} with hc_mult=4, hidden_size=4096; compare wall time of the hc_prenorm_gemm path (n_splits=1) against the deep_gemm split-K path and against the block_m kernel to confirm SM occupancy on mid-range batch sizes.

findings.claim: `is_deep_gemm_supported()` is now called on every `mhc_pre_tilelang` and `mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang` invocation to branch between the DeepGEMM and TileLang prenorm GEMM. It is decorated with `functools.cache`, so cost is amortized, but the added `from vllm.utils.deep_gemm import is_deep_gemm_supported` import inside the hot function executes an import-system lookup each call. This is negligible relative to kernel time but adds a small per-layer Python overhead on the decode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Python-side overhead of mhc_pre_tilelang dispatch per decode step (num_tokens small) to measure the cost of the added import + branch vs the pre-change direct call.

findings.claim: In `mhc_fused_tilelang`, `T.get_warp_idx()` / `T.get_lane_idx()` were replaced with `tid // 32` / `tid % 32`. On architectures with a 64-wide wavefront (AMD CDNA/RDNA), warps are 64 lanes wide, so hardcoding 32 changes the intra-warp reduction grouping. `T.warp_reduce_sum` presumably reduces across the hardware wavefront (64 lanes on ROCm) while `s_warp` is indexed by `tid//32` and `num_warps = n_thr//32`. This mismatch between the hardware wavefront width and the assumed 32-lane grouping can cause double-counting/undercounting in the cross-warp reduction or over-sized shared-memory staging, and at minimum produces more shared-memory slots than necessary — a correctness/efficiency risk on ROCm where this kernel is now the primary path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a ROCm GPU (64-lane wavefront), validate mhc_fused_tilelang output against the torch reference and compare timing of the tid//32 grouping vs the original get_warp_idx/get_lane_idx builtins to ensure no extra shared-memory pressure or redundant reductions.

findings.claim: This PR adds four additional `@tilelang.jit`-decorated kernels (hc_prenorm_gemm_tilelang, hc_prenorm_gemm_block_m_tilelang) plus dispatch that can select 1024-thread / different tile_n configurations. Each distinct (config) instantiation triggers a TileLang JIT compilation on first use, adding one-time latency spikes during warmup on ROCm. The block_m vs non-block_m vs 1024-thread branches mean up to three separate kernel compilations are required to cover the num_tokens<128 / 128-1024 / >=1024 ranges seen during a serving run.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-call latency (JIT compile time) for hc_prenorm_gemm_tilelang and hc_prenorm_gemm_block_m_tilelang across the three num_tokens ranges to quantify warmup cost on ROCm.

findings.claim: The kernel now dispatches a grid sized on kNumHeadsQPadded (rounded up to 8/16/32/64/128) instead of num_heads_q. For DeepSeek V4, n_local_heads is padded up to 64 or 128, so the fused kernel now launches warps/CTAs for the full padded head count and each pad-Q slot performs a 32-byte zero-fill store to global memory. This adds warp slots and DRAM write traffic that did not exist when the kernel only processed the num_heads_q live slots. Whether this is a net win depends on it replacing the separate F.pad(q) op (which itself did a copy of live q plus a zero-fill of the pad region); if the padding gap is large (e.g. 64 heads padded to 128, or a small live-head count) the extra pad-Q warps could reduce achievable occupancy/efficiency versus a tighter kernel that only wrote pad zeros without spawning full warps per slot.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fused_deepseek_v4_qnorm_rope_kv_rope_quant_insert for (num_heads_q=64, padded=128) and (num_heads_q live vs padded=64) at representative num_tokens (e.g. 1, 32, 256, 1024, 4096) comparing wall-clock vs the pre-change kernel+F.pad path.

findings.claim: The op now allocates a fresh padded q_out tensor ([N, q_head_padded, 512]) inside the kernel wrapper via torch.empty on every call, and returns it, instead of operating in place on q. This moves the pad-region allocation from the previous F.pad into the op. It should be roughly allocation-neutral versus F.pad (which also allocated a padded output), but note the previous in-place path reused q storage for the live region while the new path always materializes a full padded buffer even when n_local_heads == padded_heads (no padding needed). In the no-padding case the change introduces an unnecessary extra full-size allocation + write of the live region compared to the old in-place code path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare allocator activity and latency when n_local_heads == padded_heads (no padding required): old in-place kernel vs new always-allocate q_out.

findings.claim: In the reduced-grid variant the per-token warp loop still iterates over pad-Q slots (warpId < slot_end where slot_end == kNumHeadsQPadded[+1]); load_slot early-returns for pad slots but processDeepseekV4Slot is still invoked for each pad slot and performs a 32B zero store. With padded head counts up to 128 and warpsPerBlock=8, each block loops ceil(padded/8) times per token, adding iterations that do only zero-fill work — increasing per-token latency for tokens whose live head count is far below the padded count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark the reduced-grid path (num_tokens_full < 1024) for num_heads_q=64 vs padded=128 and measure per-token latency vs pre-change kernel+F.pad.

findings.claim: The new dispatch wrappers in fused_compress_quant_cache.py (_compress_kv_sparse_attn_cutedsl / _norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn_cutedsl / _fused_kv_compress_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn_cutedsl) route every call through _get_sparse_attn_cutedsl_impls()[i](*args, **kwargs). While _get_sparse_attn_cutedsl_impls is @cache-guarded (import happens once), each invocation still incurs a Python-level indirection: an extra function call, a tuple index, and *args/**kwargs re-packing/unpacking on the per-token-batch hot path. This is minor per call but happens on every compressor forward. Prefer binding the concrete function object once (e.g., resolve at import/first-use and rebind the module global) rather than re-dispatching through *args/**kwargs each time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark the wrapper call overhead vs. a directly-bound function reference for a batch of compressor forward calls; measure host-side dispatch time.

findings.claim: SparseAttnCompressNormRopeStoreC4Kernel.compile (and the C128 SparseAttnCompressKernel) are decorated with @cache @staticmethod and call cute.compile(...). The first invocation for each unique argument signature triggers a full CuTe DSL / MLIR compilation, which is a substantial one-time latency spike on the request/warmup path. Unlike the existing indexer wrappers that pre-compile all coarsen variants up front, there is no evidence these sparse-attn kernels are warmed up ahead of the serving hot path, so the first real request that hits this path will stall on JIT compilation. Confirm a warmup call compiles these kernels during engine init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end latency of the first compressor forward that hits the CuTe DSL path (cold compile) vs. subsequent calls; verify a warmup pass exists.

findings.claim: The C4 kernel launches grid=(slot_mapping.shape[0],1,1) with block=(tb_size=head_size//2=256,1,1), i.e. one threadblock per token. Because the kernel early-exits (active=False) for all non-boundary tokens (only positions where (position+1)%compress_ratio==0 are active), the vast majority of launched threadblocks do essentially no work but still consume launch/scheduling slots. With compress_ratio=4 roughly 3/4 of blocks are idle. Launching over all slot_mapping tokens rather than only the boundary tokens wastes grid slots and reduces effective occupancy for the useful work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel wall time launching one block per token (with early-exit) vs. a compacted grid over only boundary tokens, across representative batch sizes and compress_ratio=4.

findings.claim: The commit ports the MiniMax QK-norm fusion from a compile-time PatternMatcher pass (MiniMaxQKNormPass) to a runtime 'manual fusion' in MiniMaxText01RMSNormTP.forward_qkv. The old pass deliberately gated the Lamport-based custom-IPC all-reduce+RMS kernel (`minimax_allreduce_rms_qk`) to *decode-size* ranges only: `is_applicable_for_range` fired only when `compile_range.end <= min(MAX_TOKEN_NUM=2048, max_num_batched_tokens)`, explicitly noting 'large prefill batches fall through to the original forward_qk'. The Lamport custom all-reduce is only a win for tiny (decode) token counts; for large prefill batches it is typically slower than the NCCL all-reduce used by the eager path. If the new runtime forward_qkv dispatches to the fused kernel unconditionally (no equivalent token-count guard), prefill throughput will regress relative to the NCCL path that was previously used for large batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MiniMax-M2 with tp_size>=4: measure prefill (large batch, e.g. 4096-8192 tokens) throughput / per-layer QK-norm latency comparing the new runtime fused path vs eager NCCL all-reduce+RMS. Also measure decode latency to confirm no regression there.

findings.claim: The Lamport all-reduce workspace (get_allreduce_workspace) is sized to `max_tokens`; the compute_comm_size_for_minimax cost grows linearly with max_tokens. The old pass allocated the workspace once with `max_token_num = min(2048, max_num_batched_tokens)`, bounding the IPC allocation. The convenience default in lamport_workspace.get_allreduce_workspace is `max_tokens=16384`. If the new runtime path allocates the workspace using max_num_batched_tokens (or the 16384 default) instead of the capped 2048, the per-rank triple-buffered IPC allocation (3 * comm_size) grows ~8x, increasing GPU memory reserved for the workspace with no corresponding decode-time benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-rank GPU memory reserved for the Lamport workspace under the new runtime path vs the old max_token_num=min(2048, max_num_batched_tokens) sizing, across max_num_batched_tokens in {2048, 8192, 16384}.

findings.claim: Moving the fusion out of the compiled Inductor graph into a runtime CustomOp call means the fused-vs-eager dispatch decision (and any workspace lookup / dict cache lookup in get_allreduce_workspace under a threading.Lock) now executes on every forward_qkv call in the hot path rather than being resolved once at compile time. Per-call Python overhead (cache dict lookup, lock acquisition, branch on token count) adds fixed latency to every attention layer invocation for MiniMax-M2, which is noticeable at small decode batch sizes where the layer is launch-bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile decode-step CPU/launch overhead per attention layer for MiniMax-M2 tp>1, comparing the compiled-pass approach vs the runtime forward_qkv approach; watch for per-call lock/dict-lookup cost in get_allreduce_workspace.

findings.claim: has_finished_requests() (called every engine step via has_requests()) now computes len(self.waiting) + len(self.skipped_waiting) + len(self.running) and len(self.requests) whenever a connector is configured and finished_req_ids is empty. These are all O(1) len() calls, so the added cost is negligible per iteration; the concern is only that a previously trivial predicate now does a few extra len() computations plus a subtraction in the scheduler busy loop. Not a meaningful slowdown.

The engine-core busy loop's sleep condition was widened from has_unfinished_requests() to has_requests(). This means when the only remaining work is finished requests pending delayed KV-connector frees, the loop now keeps polling with a 1ms time.sleep instead of potentially going idle. This intentionally keeps the scheduler alive (the purpose of the commit) so background transfer threads can free KV, but it also means the engine stays in an active 1ms-poll spin (waking ~1000x/sec, running schedule()/post_step each time) until connector cleanup completes rather than blocking on a queue. For workloads that frequently reach a state with only pending-free requests, this can raise idle CPU usage of the engine-core process. This is a deliberate correctness fix but has a minor sustained CPU cost.

In the vision transformer metadata computation the change adds two eager `cu_seqlens.cpu().numpy()` / `cu_window_seqlens.cpu().numpy()` conversions (cu_seqlens_np, cu_window_seqlens_np). A `.cpu()` on a CUDA tensor is a blocking device-to-host copy that forces a full CUDA stream synchronization, stalling the GPU pipeline. Previously `compute_attn_mask_seqlen` and the subsequent `cu_seqlens.to(device, non_blocking=True)` kept the offsets on-device / async; now every vision-encoder metadata build introduces synchronous D2H copies on the hot path for all attention backends (not just FlashInfer), removing overlap between metadata prep and other GPU work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Qwen2.5-VL image/video encoder metadata build latency (per forward) comparing FLASH_ATTN backend before/after; measure end-to-end vision encoder throughput with many small images to amplify per-call CPU sync overhead.

findings.claim: max_seqlen_full/window are now derived from numpy arrays via MMEncoderAttention.compute_max_seqlen and then wrapped in torch.tensor(...) on CPU. The prior path used compute_attn_mask_seqlen directly on the (possibly device) tensor. Depending on implementation this adds extra numpy reductions and host-side tensor allocation per forward; minor but repeated for every vision batch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark compute_metadata for a batch of vision inputs, comparing CPU time before/after.

findings.claim: maybe_recompute_cu_seqlens is now called for both cu_seqlens and cu_window_seqlens on every forward and reconstructs device tensors from the numpy offsets. For non-FlashInfer backends this replaces the cheap `cu_seqlens.to(device, non_blocking=True)` async transfer with a path routed through numpy + helper dispatch, potentially adding host-side work and an extra H2D copy per call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-forward metadata build overhead for FLASH_ATTN vs FlashInfer with the recompute path exercised.

findings.claim: The h-block loop was changed from `T.Pipelined(T.ceildiv(h, h_blk), num_stages=2)` to `T.Serial(...)`. The pipeline double-buffered the per-iteration global-memory loads of `b_shared` (hc x h_blk bf16) and `d_shared` (h_blk bf16), overlapping their DRAM transfer latency with the compute of the previous iteration. Serializing the loop forces each iteration to wait for the loads to complete before computing (load -> compute -> store dependency chain), exposing full global-memory latency on every h-block and reducing throughput of this kernel. Note the two sibling kernels in the same file (mhc_pre_big_fuse_tilelang and hc_head_fuse_tilelang) still use num_stages=2 pipelining for the identical access pattern, so this kernel is now the outlier.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark mhc_post_tilelang on realistic (num_tokens, hc, hidden) shapes (e.g. hidden=4096/7168, hc=4) measuring kernel latency before vs after; compare Serial vs Pipelined(num_stages=2).

findings.claim: The inner reduction loop over `i_hci` was changed from `T.serial(hc)` to `T.vectorized(hc)`, but the loop body accumulates into a single scalar `x_local[i_hco, i1_h]` (a loop-carried reduction with a true read-after-write dependency on the same accumulator). `T.vectorized` targets independent element-wise / contiguous-memory operations, not serial reductions into one location; the dependency prevents genuine SIMD vectorization, so the intended speedup is unlikely to materialize and the change is at best a no-op for performance (and risks incorrect lowering). hc is also small (typically 4), so vectorization payoff is marginal regardless.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel correctness and latency of the inner reduction with T.serial(hc) vs T.vectorized(hc) for hc=4; verify output matches the reference implementation.

findings.claim: This commit removes a `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` that was explicitly documented as needed: the comment stated 'We need this synchronize to run, but I don't know why.' The sync sat between allocating `weights_buffer = [torch.empty_like(w) ...]` and the CPU-side reads `old_global_expert_indices.cpu().numpy()` / `new_global_expert_indices.cpu().numpy()`, immediately before the async-stream weight-transfer path (move_to_buffer / move_from_buffer, which use non_blocking=True copies on a separate cuda_stream and a CpuGpuEvent for stream ordering). Removing a synchronize whose necessity was acknowledged-but-unknown risks a race between the default stream and the EPLB transfer stream: buffers/weights may be read or copied before prior GPU work completes, leading either to incorrect weights (silent) or, more relevant to this review, a deadlock/hang in the P2P communicator if event ordering assumptions are violated. Because the original author could not explain why it was required, removing it without replacing the ordering guarantee is a real regression risk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run tests/distributed/test_eplb_execute.py and test_eplb_fused_moe_layer.py repeatedly (many iterations, multi-GPU EP) to detect intermittent hangs or weight mismatches after rebalance; also stress rearrange_expert_weights_inplace under concurrent default-stream GPU load.

findings.claim: If the removed synchronize was in fact dead (no real dependency), deleting it is a legitimate latency improvement: it removes a full device synchronization on the critical rebalance path, which would otherwise stall the CPU until all queued GPU work drains on every EPLB rearrange. The performance direction of this change is positive; the only concern is correctness/hang, not slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end EPLB rearrange_expert_weights_inplace wall-clock time before/after, and check for any downstream synchronize that now becomes the effective barrier.

findings.claim: For the SM100 and SM120 default (large-M) configs, the TileScheduler is changed from `void` (CUTLASS auto/default scheduler) to an explicit `cutlass::gemm::PersistentScheduler`. `Fp4GemmSm100`/`Fp4GemmSm120` now instantiate `GemmKernel` with this scheduler unconditionally, so this change is NOT limited to the batch-invariant path: the ordinary (non-batch-invariant) large-M dispatch (`mp2 > 256`) now runs the persistent scheduler instead of whatever the builder previously selected. Forcing a persistent scheduler can change occupancy/tile-launch behavior for large-M GEMMs and may reduce throughput relative to the auto-selected scheduler (e.g. for very large problems where stream-K/tile-quantization heuristics previously kicked in). This is a silent performance change to a hot production path, not just the deterministic-mode path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark cutlass_scaled_fp4_mm on SM100 for large M (e.g. M=1024..8192, N/K typical of MLP) with VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT unset, comparing this commit vs parent, to check whether forcing PersistentScheduler on the default config regresses large-M throughput.

findings.claim: In batch-invariant mode all M values are routed to `sm100_fp4_config_default` / `sm120_fp4_config_default`, whose tiles are sized for large M (SM100 TileShape 256x256x256, PerSmTile 128x256x256; SM120 MmaTileShape 256x128x128). For small-M workloads (decode, M=1..16) this large tiling severely under-utilizes the GPU compared to the M16/M256 configs used in the normal path, because most of each output tile's M dimension is padding/wasted MMA work. This is inherent to the batch-invariance requirement (fixed tiling across batch sizes) rather than a regression versus the prior emulation fallback, but it means decode-phase latency under VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT will be dominated by an oversized GEMM tile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT=1, benchmark per-call latency of cutlass_scaled_fp4_mm at M=1 and M=16 vs the non-BI M16 config to quantify decode-phase overhead of the forced large-M tile.

findings.claim: `_build_moe_ep_load_spec` is called once during LoRAModelManager construction and does a linear scan over `self.modules.values()` (via `next(...)`) to find the first FusedMoEWithLoRA. This is a one-time O(#modules) cost at init and negligible relative to model construction; not a runtime hot-path regression.

In `from_local_checkpoint`, `_is_remote_expert_key` is now invoked once per checkpoint key on the safetensors path. Each call does string.find/isdigit/int parsing. For MoE adapters with many experts x projections x layers (e.g. 128 experts x 3 proj x 48 layers = ~18k keys) this adds tens of thousands of cheap string ops per adapter load. This runs at adapter load time (not per-forward) and is far cheaper than the safetensors deserialization it gates, so it's not a real regression; on the plus side it avoids deserializing non-local tensors entirely, which is a net memory/time win.

In `_create_merged_loras_inplace`, the change now pops ALL `packed_module_names` (the full pre-slice candidate list) from `lora_model.loras` rather than only the modules that were actually replaced (`replaced_module`). For the `.experts` EP path this is intentional (frees non-local expert tensors), but for non-MoE packed modules `packed_module_names == new_module_names` includes candidate sub-modules that had NO LoRA (lora is None) and previously were left in `lora_model.loras`. If any downstream code expected those unmerged entries to persist, they are now removed; if not, this is a memory improvement. Low confidence of perf impact.

The real op `mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang` gained new trailing parameters (`tile_n`, `norm_weight`, `norm_eps`) and the fake impl `_mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang_fake` was updated to add `tile_n`, `norm_weight`, `norm_eps`. However the sibling op `mhc_pre_tilelang`'s real signature gained `norm_weight`/`norm_eps` while `_mhc_pre_tilelang_fake` was updated too — but note the `mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang` REAL function signature (line ~186) still lacks the new `tile_n`/`norm_weight`/`norm_eps` params in the version registered via direct_register_custom_op unless the diff added them. If the fake and real signatures диverge, torch.compile will hit a schema mismatch and either graph-break or fall back to eager on the whole MoE/attention decoder block on every forward, silently killing CUDA-graph capture and throughput. Verify the registered op schema matches the new call sites passing keyword args n_splits/tile_n/norm_weight/norm_eps.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek-V4 decode with torch.compile enabled and count graph breaks / recompiles (TORCH_LOGS=recompiles); compare tokens/s before vs after.

findings.category: extra kernel launch / lost fusion on router path
findings.claim: Previously `NormGateLinear` on DSV4-Pro (M<=16) fused RMSNorm + router GEMM into a single custom op `dsv4_norm_router_gemm`, producing normed_x and router_logits in one kernel. The new code splits this into (a) RMSNorm fused into the mHC pre tilelang kernel and (b) a standalone `GateLinear.forward` running `dsv3_router_gemm`. For the non-mega-moe `_forward_fused_moe` non-internal-router path, `self.gate(hidden_states)` now runs an extra standalone router GEMM that used to be fused with the norm; and the norm that fed it is no longer applied to the gate input at all (gate now consumes raw hidden_states, not normed_x). This is a correctness+perf divergence: the router previously received RMSNormed input, now it does not, and an extra GEMM/kernel launch replaces the fused op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark router path: time norm+gate for M<=16 with old fused dsv4_norm_router_gemm vs new split RMSNorm(in mhc)+dsv3_router_gemm; measure per-layer latency.

findings.category: extra shared memory + two-pass over hidden → higher latency / lower occupancy
findings.claim: `mhc_pre_big_fuse_with_norm_tilelang` runs two passes over the hidden dimension (Pass 1 to accumulate sumsq and stash the full hidden vector into `output_shared` of size hidden_size bf16, Pass 2 to rescale and write to HBM). The original `mhc_pre_big_fuse_tilelang` performed a single pipelined pass writing directly to layer_input. The extra pass doubles the reads/computation of the weighted-sum output and adds a hidden_size-sized bf16 shared buffer (e.g. 7168*2=14KB) plus two pipelined loops (num_stages=3 and 2) with only 96 threads, which increases shared-memory pressure and can reduce occupancy relative to the fused-without-norm kernel. This adds latency per pre block, and this kernel now runs on every attn_norm and ffn_norm in every decoder layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Nsight-Compute the with_norm kernel vs the original big_fuse kernel at hidden_size=7168, hc_mult=4, tokens in {1,4,16}; compare duration and achieved occupancy.

findings.claim: `hidden_block = math.gcd(1024, hidden_size)` in the new with-norm kernel vs `math.gcd(512, hidden_size)` in the original. With hidden_size=7168 both give gcd (7168%1024!=0 → gcd(1024,7168)=1024 vs gcd(512,7168)=512), so the new kernel uses a 1024-wide block. Combined with the two-pass structure and fp32 fragments of size (hc_mult, hidden_block)=(4,1024), register/shared pressure roughly doubles versus the 512 block, which may spill registers under TL_PTXAS_REGISTER_USAGE_LEVEL=10 and hurt performance for small token counts where the kernel is latency-bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel duration and register usage (ptxas -v) with hidden_block 512 vs 1024 for hidden_size=7168.

findings.claim: In both `mhc_pre_tilelang` and `mhc_fused_post_pre_tilelang`, norm_weight is converted via `.to(torch.bfloat16)` and `.contiguous()` on every call if not already bf16/contiguous. Since norm_weight is passed as `self.attn_norm.weight.data` / `self.ffn_norm.weight.data` (RMSNorm weights are typically bf16 and contiguous), this is usually a no-op, but if the RMSNorm dtype is fp32 this triggers a device-to-device cast every forward for every layer. Cache the converted weight instead of converting per call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Assert norm_weight.dtype in deployment; if fp32, measure the per-layer cast overhead across all decoder layers.

findings.claim: The connector __init__ now unconditionally creates a ThreadPoolExecutor with `num_writer_threads` (default 8) worker threads. These threads are created once per connector instance and persist for the process lifetime. If the connector is instantiated per-worker in a multi-worker/TP deployment, this multiplies thread count. This is minor overhead and generally justified by the async-write optimization, but the pool is created even when no writes may occur (e.g. store-only paths that never fire).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure thread count and init latency of ExampleHiddenStatesConnector construction across TP sizes.

findings.claim: load_hidden_states() acquires a shared flock (LOCK_SH) that blocks the calling thread until the async writer releases its exclusive lock. In the benchmark this is dispatched to a ThreadPoolExecutor with max_workers=num_clients, so a slow disk write will pin a consumer thread for the full write duration. With more concurrent clients than writer/consumer threads, consumers can serialize behind writes, reducing the intended overlap of I/O with generation. Correctness is fine but the effective concurrency ceiling is bounded by pool sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Vary num-clients vs num_writer_threads and measure tokens/s to detect consumer-thread starvation.

findings.claim: load_hidden_states opens the companion `.lock` file read-only and assumes it already exists. If a reader calls before the writer has created the lock file, this raises FileNotFoundError rather than blocking, which in an online serving loop could cause retries/spins and wasted work. Not a direct throughput regression but a possible source of unnecessary work under contention.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Race a reader immediately after request_finished before the writer thread creates the lock file.

findings.claim: The new _encoder_chunk sizes the encoder chunk from *currently-free* GPU memory (min(free//2, total//10)) rather than a fixed budget. During normal serving, after the KV cache is allocated (typically ~90% of GPU memory), very little memory remains free. This drives the chunk size far smaller than the previous fixed 5%-of-total budget, so many more (tiny) encoder passes are launched to process the same number of image/video patches. That greatly increases per-batch Python/kernel-launch overhead and reduces GPU utilization on the vision tower relative to the prior implementation, hurting multimodal prefill throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure vision-encoder throughput (images/sec) for a batch of same-resolution images/video frames under realistic serving conditions where the KV cache is already allocated, comparing chunk sizes / total encoder passes before and after this commit.

findings.claim: current_platform.mem_get_info() is now called once per resolution bucket inside _process_image_input (and once per video call). mem_get_info triggers a CUDA driver query which synchronizes/costs a few microseconds-to-milliseconds. For inputs with many distinct image resolutions this adds a per-bucket driver call that the old fixed-budget path avoided after the first initialization. Minor but non-zero overhead on the hot multimodal path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark cost of current_platform.mem_get_info() and multiply by the number of resolution buckets in representative multi-image requests.

findings.claim: The chunk budget now depends on transient free memory read at call time. If a prior bucket's activations have not yet been freed when the next bucket's mem_get_info() is read, the reported free memory will be lower than it will actually be during that bucket's encode, causing overly-conservative (small) chunk sizes and extra encoder passes. Conversely inconsistent readings can cause non-deterministic chunking. This makes vision-encoder throughput input-order dependent and generally lower than a stable fixed budget.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Process multiple resolution buckets back-to-back and log the chunk size chosen per bucket to observe variance and count of encoder passes.

findings.claim: For synchronous (non-async, non-elastic) EPLB, the default communicator is changed from "torch_nccl" to "torch_gloo" whenever the nixl/rixl package is not installed. torch_gloo uses CPU staging: expert weights must be copied GPU->CPU, transferred over the gloo (CPU/TCP) backend, then copied CPU->GPU, whereas torch_nccl does direct GPU->GPU NCCL transfers. Since the vast majority of deployments do not have nixl/rixl installed, this makes the CPU-staged gloo path the effective default for sync EPLB, substantially increasing expert-rearrangement latency and adding host memory traffic and PCIe transfers on every EPLB rebalance step. The old branch only used torch_gloo for the async case (where it was needed to avoid NCCL multi-thread hangs); applying it to the sync case is a clear throughput/latency regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable EPLB (enable_eplb=True, use_async=False) on a multi-GPU MoE model without nixl installed, and measure per-rebalance expert-weight transfer latency and end-to-end step time before vs after this commit (torch_nccl vs torch_gloo).

findings.claim: When nixl/rixl IS available, sync EPLB now defaults to the "nixl" communicator instead of "torch_nccl". Depending on interconnect (e.g. intra-node NVLink), the direct NCCL GPU path may outperform NIXL's staged send/recv buffers for expert-weight movement, so this could regress rearrangement latency on NVLink-connected nodes even though it may help in RDMA/cross-node setups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an NVLink-connected multi-GPU node with nixl installed, compare EPLB rearrange latency using communicator=nixl vs communicator=torch_nccl for a sync EPLB workload.

findings.claim: is_nixl_available() uses importlib.util.find_spec, which triggers Python import-machinery filesystem scanning of sys.path to locate the package. This is invoked during config validation on startup; it is a one-time cost (not per-step), so the impact is negligible, but it does add a small filesystem-stat cost at initialization on every engine start that enables EPLB.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark importlib.util.find_spec('nixl') call latency during ParallelConfig validation on a cold and warm filesystem cache.

findings.claim: The added `g = g * RCP_LN2` after `chunk_local_cumsum` introduces a new full-tensor elementwise multiply that materializes a fresh tensor of shape (B, T, H, K) on every `chunk_kda_fwd` call. This is an extra CUDA kernel launch plus a read+write pass over the entire gate tensor per prefill step. The cost is small relative to the surrounding chunked matmuls, but it is strictly additional work that could have been folded into the preceding cumsum kernel (which already reads/writes g) or into the exp2 arguments inside the Triton kernels. On the prefill path this recurs for every layer and every forward, so it is measurable memory-bandwidth overhead that is avoidable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark chunk_kda_fwd prefill latency (e.g. B=1, T=4096, H, K matching a KDA model) before/after this commit, isolating the g = g * RCP_LN2 op with a CUDA profiler to measure the added kernel launch + bandwidth cost vs folding the scale into chunk_local_cumsum.

findings.claim: exp2 is assigned to tl.exp2 in both the FLA_USE_FAST_OPS and the default branch, so the fast-ops path does not use a fast/approximate exp2 (e.g. tldevice.fast_exp2f if available) even though the other ops (exp/log) do switch to fast variants. This means enabling FLA_USE_FAST_OPS no longer speeds up the exp evaluations that were converted to exp2 in the KDA kernels; the intended fast-math speedup is lost for the decay computations. Not a regression against the parent for default settings, but it removes a potential optimization for fast-ops users.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run KDA chunk prefill with FLA_USE_FAST_OPS=1 before and after; compare kernel runtime of chunk_kda_scaled_dot_kkt / recompute_w_u kernels to confirm whether exp2 lowers to a fast HW instruction vs the previous fast_expf path.

findings.claim: The XPU code path for the top-k step now calls `ops.top_k_per_row_prefill`/`ops.top_k_per_row_decode`, which dispatch to `torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_prefill`/`decode`. These `_C` kernels are registered only for the CUDA device (csrc/torch_bindings.cpp uses `ops.impl(..., torch::kCUDA, ...)`, implemented in csrc/sampler.cu). Prior to this change the XPU branch used the dedicated pure-PyTorch `xpu_ops.top_k_per_row_*` fallbacks (which the diff deletes). On an XPU build there is no `_C` XPU implementation, so this top-k call will either fail or, if a generic fallback kicks in, run on the wrong device / with an implicit host-device sync — degrading decode latency or hanging the indexer path. The new XPU `fp8_mqa_logits` kernels are wired up, but the follow-on top-k step is not given an XPU implementation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the DeepSeek sparse-attention indexer decode + prefill path on XPU and measure per-step latency / verify the top_k_per_row_* op actually executes on the XPU device rather than falling back to CPU.

findings.claim: In the XPU decode branch, `seq_lens_xpu = seq_lens[:, -1].contiguous()` introduces an extra tensor materialization/copy every decode step (when seq_lens is 2D). The prior code passed `seq_lens` through without a contiguous copy. This is a small per-step allocation on the decode critical path; magnitude is proportional to batch size only, so minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark decode-step overhead of the indexer on XPU with vs. without the extra .contiguous() slice copy across batch sizes.

findings.claim: In the refactored processDeepseekV4Slot helper, the cos_sin_cache RoPE loads changed from VLLM_LDG (which maps to __ldg / read-only data cache on CUDA per cuda_compat.h) to plain dereferences (`*reinterpret_cast<float4 const*>`). cos_sin_cache is a read-only, position-shared table; dropping the __ldg hint forgoes the read-only/texture cache path and can increase L1 pressure and effective latency for these loads, especially in the reduced-grid kernel where a warp iterates many slots per token and reissues these loads repeatedly. Restoring __ldg (or loading cos/sin once and reusing across slots of the same token) would be cheaper.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill microbench of the fused DSv4 Q-norm/RoPE/KV-insert kernel at num_tokens>=1024 with and without __ldg on the cos/sin float4 loads; compare kernel duration and L1 hit rate via ncu.

findings.claim: The reduced-grid kernel adds software double-buffering (v0_curr/v1_curr + v0_next/v1_next = four uint4 = 16 registers live across the slot loop) on top of the 16-float `elements` array inside the force-inlined helper. This raises per-thread register usage relative to the original one-slot-per-warp kernel, which can lower achievable occupancy (fewer resident blocks/warps per SM) and partially offset the intended gains of the grid refactor. Worth checking `-Xptxas -v` register counts and occupancy for the ReducedGrid variant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile with -Xptxas -v to compare register usage of the original vs ReducedGrid kernel, and run ncu occupancy analysis at num_tokens=2048.

findings.claim: The reduced-grid kernel launches exactly one 256-thread (8-warp) block per token and gates work with `if (warpId < slot_end)` where slot_end = num_heads_q(+1). When num_heads_q+1 < 8 (small tensor-parallel Q-head counts), some warps in every block are permanently idle, wasting SM warp slots — whereas the original dense-packing grid kept all warps busy across (token,head) pairs. Impact depends on num_heads_q; for large per-GPU head counts it is negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the kernel at num_tokens=2048 with small num_heads_q (e.g. 4) and compare achieved occupancy / duration of the ReducedGrid path vs the original packed-grid kernel.

findings.claim: The Mx8 GEMM microkernel is fixed to vl=8 FP32 elements per vector op (LMUL_256). At VLEN=128, LMUL_256=m2 = exactly 8 FP32 lanes, fully used. But at VLEN=256, LMUL_256=m1 = 16 FP32 lanes, of which only 8 are used every FMA/load/store. The kernel therefore leaves half the datapath idle on VLEN=256 hardware, so per-op vector throughput is ~50% of what the machine could deliver. This is not a regression versus the parent (VLEN=256 previously fell back to scalar VEC/VEC16), but the new VLEN=256 path is far from optimal — the tile width (8) was designed for the VLEN=128 register geometry and was not widened to 16 columns for VLEN=256. Consider a VLEN-parameterized tile width (e.g. Mx16 at VLEN=256) to use the full vector width.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark gemm_micro_rvv_fma_Mx8_Ku4 (QK and PV phases) on a VLEN=256 RVV CPU vs. an Mx16-tiled variant; measure attention throughput (tokens/s) for a decode workload with head_dim=128.

findings.claim: The register-budget comment now advertises that at VLEN=256 the M=8 microkernel uses only 9 of 32 vector registers (each m1 accumulator = 1 reg) versus 18/32 at VLEN=128. With so many registers free at VLEN=256, the current K-unroll-by-4 with a single reused B temp leaves ILP on the table (only one B load in flight at a time, no software pipelining of the B conversions). This limits how well the FMA pipeline is fed on wide-VLEN hardware. Not a regression, but the kernel is under-tuned for the VLEN=256 register file it now targets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile IPC / FMA-unit occupancy of the QK GEMM on a VLEN=256 core; compare K-unroll-by-4 vs. deeper unroll with multiple B temps in flight.

findings.claim: reshape_and_cache still uses hardcoded __riscv_vsetvl_e32m2 / __riscv_vle32_v_f32m2 / __riscv_vsse32_v_f32m2 (and the e16m1 16-bit path) rather than the RVVI(..., LMUL) macros the rest of the file was migrated to. This remains functionally correct at VLEN=256 (vsetvl adapts vl at runtime, so at VLEN=256 m2 processes up to 16 fp32 per iteration), so there is no regression; but the strided-store transpose (vsse with stride = block_size*sizeof) is a gather/scatter-style access whose cost scales with element count, and the mismatch with the macro-based convention is an inconsistency that could mask a future LMUL/VLEN bug. Worth confirming the strided store is not the bottleneck of the KV write path at VLEN=256.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark cpu_attn_reshape_and_cache throughput (GB/s of KV writes) for fp16/bf16 and fp32 on VLEN=256 vs VLEN=128, focusing on the strided vsse transpose store.

findings.claim: In FlashInferB12xExperts.apply, `topk_ids.to(torch.int32)` runs on every forward pass. If topk_ids is not already int32 this launches a device-to-device cast kernel each MoE call; for large expert counts / high token throughput this adds a small per-layer overhead. This mirrors the existing cutedsl path so it's not a regression, but the b12x kernel could accept int32 topk directly from the router to avoid the cast.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MoE forward at large E (256) / high M measuring the added cost of the topk_ids int32 cast per call.

findings.claim: process_weights_after_loading materializes float() copies of w13/w2 weight_scale and calls flashinfer_convert_sf_to_mma_layout on the full weight-scale tensors, creating additional cached tensors (w1_sf_mma / w2_sf_mma). This increases peak host/device memory during model loading and permanently retains the extra MMA-layout scale tensors alongside the originals. It's a one-time cost (not per-forward), so throughput is unaffected, but resident memory grows by the size of the converted scale factors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory footprint after loading an NVFP4 MoE model with backend=flashinfer_b12x vs cutedsl.

findings.claim: The stated goal of this commit is to eliminate the CPU-GPU synchronization on the align path. Previously, postprocess used `self.num_accepted_tokens.gpu[:num_reqs].cpu().numpy()` (a blocking sync) then wrote per-request values into `num_accepted_tokens_cpu`. The new code replaces that with a fused GPU kernel and only records `num_accepted_tokens_event`. However, the downstream consumer at gpu_model_runner.py ~line 2002 still calls `self.num_accepted_tokens_event.synchronize()`, which blocks the CPU on the GPU. If any downstream logic still reads `num_accepted_tokens_cpu` on the host after this sync (which the previous code fed via a copy), the sync point is retained rather than removed. Whether the fused kernel truly makes the path 'fully async' depends on whether that synchronize() is still on the critical path; the removed `.cpu()` sync was replaced by an event-based sync that still stalls the CPU when awaited.

In _prepare_inputs, stage_postprocess_inputs_to_gpu now iterates per-request over req_ids and reads from scheduler_output dicts / self.requests / mamba_state_idx to fill CPU staging buffers, then copies to GPU. This adds an extra per-request Python loop and an extra host->device copy every step on the align+spec-decode+hybrid path, in addition to the existing preprocess_mamba loop. If num_reqs is large this per-step Python overhead can offset the savings from removing the .cpu() sync, since Python-level per-request loops are a known bottleneck in the hot path.

postprocess_mamba_fused_kernel uses grid=(num_reqs, num_layers*num_state_types) and performs an element-wise byte copy loop with COPY_BLOCK_SIZE per (request,state) program. Each program copies at most a single mamba state block via a scalar-strided uint8 loop. For temporal states with large inner_size and many layers this launches num_reqs*num_states programs each doing a serial for-loop over copy_size in COPY_BLOCK_SIZE (e.g. likely 1024-byte) chunks. If COPY_BLOCK_SIZE is small relative to block byte size, memory bandwidth utilization can be poor compared to a batched contiguous copy; also many programs return early (needs_copy false) causing launch overhead with little useful work. Confirm COPY_BLOCK_SIZE is tuned; the truncated diff hides its value.

_get_mamba_bufs() is now called unconditionally inside the preprocess path (mamba_bufs = self._get_mamba_bufs()) with an assert that mamba_cache_mode == 'align'. This is lazily created once so amortized cost is low, but the added assert and attribute indirection run every step. Minor.

`_encoder_bytes_per_patch = hidden_size * 2 * num_hidden_layers` multiplies per-patch cost by the full layer count, modeling activation memory as if every layer's activations are simultaneously resident. For a deep vision tower this hugely overestimates the true per-call footprint of a non-checkpointed forward. Combined with the very conservative budget of only 5% of total device memory (`total_mem * 0.05`), `_encoder_max_batch` returns `budget // cost` which can easily collapse to 1 for medium/large images. That would silently reduce the batched encoder call back to per-item processing (the exact loop this commit set out to eliminate), leaving throughput no better than the parent while adding the extra bucketing/cat overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gemma4_mm image_input_features with a batch of same-resolution images (e.g. 8 images at 280 soft tokens) and log the computed max_batch_size / number of encoder chunks vs. batch size to confirm batching actually engages rather than degenerating to chunk size 1.

findings.claim: The pooling stage still runs a strictly per-image (and per-frame in the video path) Python loop that calls `vt.pooler(...)` on a single unsqueezed item at a time. Each iteration launches its own set of GPU kernels serially, so the pooler remains a sequential, dynamism-heavy hot path. Batching only the patch_embedder + encoder while leaving the pooler per-item leaves a substantial serial component; for many small images/frames the pooler launch overhead (and CPU-side loop) can dominate and limit the realized speedup of the change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile _process_image_input / _process_video_input with 32 video frames; compare wall-clock and CUDA kernel launch counts for the pooling loop vs. the batched encoder call.

findings.claim: The new image path builds per-resolution buckets by iterating all images in Python, then for each chunk does `torch.cat([item.unsqueeze(0) ...])` to reassemble a batch tensor. When inputs already arrive as a single stacked same-resolution tensor (the common batched case), this unstacks into a list and re-concatenates, adding an avoidable copy and Python-level per-item work versus indexing the existing contiguous tensor directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark _process_image_input when pixel_values arrives as a single stacked (N, max_patches, patch_pixels) tensor of uniform resolution; measure the added copy/allocation time from the split+cat round-trip.

findings.claim: `last_hidden_states_map` retains the encoder output for every image across all buckets simultaneously (indexed by original position), and the video path builds `last_hidden_states = torch.cat(last_hidden_states_list)` holding all frames' hidden states at once. This raises peak activation memory relative to the parent's per-image path that freed each image's hidden state before processing the next, partially offsetting the memory budget the encoder chunking is trying to respect.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) for a large multi-image / long-video request before and after the change.

findings.claim: This override makes XPU set `self.use_custom_op_call = True` in GroupCoordinator (parallel_state.py). As a result, all_reduce / all_gather / reduce_scatter now dispatch through the registered `torch.ops.vllm.*` custom ops (an extra torch dispatcher indirection and group-name lookup per collective) instead of calling `self._all_reduce_out_place` / device_communicator directly. When the model is NOT compiled (eager mode), this adds a small constant per-collective Python/dispatcher overhead on every collective call with no benefit, since the custom-op wrapper only pays off when captured by torch.compile/Dynamo. On XPU, cudagraph/torch.compile paths are frequently disabled (check_and_update_config sets cudagraph_mode=NONE when XPU graph unsupported/disabled or world_size_across_dp>1), so in those configs the collectives take the slower custom-op dispatch path unconditionally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark all_reduce/all_gather latency on XPU with TP>1 in eager (non-compiled) mode, comparing direct device_communicator dispatch vs torch.ops.vllm.* custom-op dispatch, measuring per-call CPU overhead at small tensor sizes.

findings.claim: In the FP8-Q fused path, the kernel computes `score_scale = scale * tl.load(q_scale) * tl.load(k_scale)` and `value_scale = tl.load(v_scale)`. These `tl.load` of scalar scale pointers happen once per program (before the tile loop), so cost is negligible vs. the attention flops. No measurable regression, but worth noting the loads exist in the new hot-path prologue.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench kernel_unified_attention with FP8 Q + FP8 per-tensor KV vs bf16 Q path at various seq lengths.

findings.claim: For the FP8-Q fused path the epilogue adds `acc *= value_scale` (both in the 3D branch and the 2D branch). This is one extra BLOCK_M x HEAD_SIZE_PADDED scalar multiply per program in the epilogue. It replaces per-tile V dequantization (which is now skipped since `_cast_kv_tile` returns a plain cast when Q is fp8), so this is a net reduction in work inside the loop and should be a performance improvement, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare wall-clock of unified_attention with fp8 Q/KV before and after this commit at decode and prefill shapes.

findings.claim: A new constexpr `Q_IS_FP8=(q.dtype == current_platform.fp8_dtype())` is added to the kernel launch. Since it is a new constexpr specialization key, the first invocation with an fp8 query will trigger an additional Triton autotune/JIT compilation (one-time cost). This only affects startup/warmup, not steady-state throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-call compile latency of unified_attention with fp8 query dtype.

findings.claim: In the new non-persistent-cache path, `fi_utils.autotune()` is invoked with NO arguments (previously `fi_utils.autotune(tune_mode=True, cache=str(cache_path))`). FlashInfer's `autotune()` defaults to `tune_mode=False`, which means the context manager does NOT actually run benchmarks to select the best per-op tactic — it merely enables the autotune context while dispatching default/heuristic tactics. As the function's own docstring warns, 'Without autotuning, FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower.' Thus this change effectively disables autotuning entirely (not just the persistent cache), so all subsequent FlashInfer GEMM/MoE/attention ops run with heuristic tactics for the whole serving lifetime, degrading steady-state throughput/latency. If the intent was only to skip the persistent file cache while still tuning, the call should be `fi_utils.autotune(tune_mode=True)`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On Hopper/Blackwell, run FlashInfer-backed decode+prefill throughput (e.g. an FP4/FP8 MoE model) comparing steady-state tokens/s with tune_mode=True autotune vs the new default-arg autotune() call, to quantify the heuristic-vs-tuned kernel gap.

findings.claim: The new early-return path still performs a full `_dummy_run` at max_num_batched_tokens under an autotune context and then a world barrier. If autotuning is effectively a no-op (tune_mode default False), this dummy run mainly serves as JIT warmup but no tuning benefit is retained, so the one-time warmup cost is incurred without the persistent-cache reuse across ranks/restarts, marginally increasing startup time on every launch and across all ranks (each rank now runs the full dummy tuning pass instead of only rank 0 tuning and broadcasting).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup/warmup wall-clock time on a multi-GPU (TP>1) deployment before and after this commit.

findings.claim: The removed branch previously disabled cudagraph_mode (set to NONE) when world_size_across_dp > 1, with the explicit rationale that 'XPU Graph doesn't support capture communication ops.' After this change, full cudagraph capture is enabled (AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS on XPU) even for multi-node/DP setups. If XPU graphs still cannot safely capture xccl collective communication ops, capturing/replaying graphs that contain all-reduce/all-gather could deadlock or produce incorrect/serialized execution, i.e. a hang or throughput regression at TP/DP > 1. This is speculative from the code alone since ca_comm is set to None (so custom all-reduce stays uncaptured), but the removed guard specifically targeted this case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run XPU with tensor_parallel_size>1 and/or data_parallel_size>1 with VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1 and confirm cudagraph capture completes and steady-state decode throughput matches or exceeds the previous PIECEWISE fallback without hangs.

findings.claim: Marking the FlashAttention builder as AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS on XPU (previously UNIFORM_BATCH, since FA on XPU is not v3) upgrades XPU flash-attn to full cudagraph support. Since aot_schedule remains False on XPU (get_flash_attn_version() != 3), the FA3-specific full-graph buffer preallocation path is skipped; if the underlying sycl-tla FMHA kernels cannot actually be replayed correctly under a full graph across varying batch shapes, decode could fall back to eager recompute or misbehave rather than benefiting from the graph. Net effect is expected to be a performance improvement, but the ALWAYS claim is stronger than the previous FA2-style UNIFORM_BATCH support and should be validated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On XPU with flash-attn backend and VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1, benchmark decode latency/throughput for mixed prefill-decode and uniform-decode batches, comparing against the prior PIECEWISE fallback to confirm full-graph capture actually improves and does not regress or hang.

findings.claim: cast_overflow_tensors was changed to unconditionally torch.clamp the tensor instead of first checking `tensors.isinf().any() or tensors.isnan().any()`. The old code returned the tensor unchanged (no allocation, no kernel) in the common case where there were no inf/nan values; the guarding `isinf/isnan().any()` did trigger a D2H sync, which is presumably what this PR removed. But now every call runs a full clamp kernel and allocates a new output tensor on every forward pass (called on hidden_states in qwen2_5_vl, whisper, minicpmo vision/audio encoders). This adds an extra elementwise GPU kernel + memory traffic on the hidden-state tensor for every invocation, whereas before it was usually a no-op. On large encoder hidden states this is a measurable per-layer/per-forward overhead and extra memory allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark qwen2_5_vl / whisper vision or audio encoder forward pass throughput comparing the always-clamp vs conditional-clamp implementations on large hidden-state tensors.

findings.claim: In _get_audio_features_mask, the input_features_mask is now built on CPU and copied to device via `input_features_mask.pin_memory().to(target_device, non_blocking=True)`. The pin_memory() call allocates a pinned host buffer and performs an extra host-side copy each call. For small masks this is fine, but pinning memory on the fly has non-trivial per-call cost (pinned allocation is expensive) and is done unconditionally on the non-cpu path. If this runs per-request it could add latency; a cached pinned buffer or async_tensor_h2d would be preferable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark repeated calls to _get_audio_features_mask on GPU measuring the cost of pin_memory().to(non_blocking=True) vs a direct .to().

findings.claim: Several changes add `.to(device, non_blocking=True)` on tensors that were created without pinned memory (e.g. pos_ids in the vision rotary path, window_index_thw, compute_mrope_for_media outputs). If the source CPU tensor is not in pinned memory, non_blocking=True has no effect and the copy is still synchronous; if it IS pinned, subsequent indexing (cos[pos_ids]) that reads the just-copied device tensor forces an implicit sync anyway. So the added non_blocking flags may not deliver the intended overlap and, in the worst case, indexing with a not-yet-materialized async-copied index tensor is a subtle hazard. Low severity because torch guards correctness, but the intended sync-elimination benefit may not materialize.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile qwen2_5_vl / qwen2_vl vision transformer forward measuring whether the pos_ids H2D copy overlaps with compute or remains a blocking sync.

findings.claim: enc_streaming_mask.to(device) was replaced with enc_streaming_mask.contiguous().to(device, non_blocking=True). The added .contiguous() forces a copy on the host side when the mask is non-contiguous. If the streaming mask is typically already contiguous this is a no-op, but the extra call and potential host copy is added per encoder invocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark phi4mm audio encoder forward comparing .to() vs .contiguous().to(non_blocking=True) for the streaming mask.

findings.claim: In flashinfer_autotune, the newly added `world.barrier()` is called only inside the `else` branch (when tune_results is not None). Every rank calls `world.broadcast_object(...)` first, but `tune_results` is derived per-rank from the broadcast, so all ranks agree on None vs not-None — so far consistent. However, the barrier is a collective on the world group; it is entered by all ranks only when tune_results is non-None. The write is gated on `world.local_rank == 0` while the barrier is unconditional inside the else, so all ranks reach it — this is fine for correctness but the barrier is a full world-group synchronization added on every startup. More importantly, the previous per-rank file write (`if not is_leader`) wrote the cache on every non-leader rank; now it writes only on `world.local_rank == 0`. On multi-node deployments, non-leader nodes' local_rank==0 rank writes the file to that node's filesystem, and the barrier ensures the file exists before other ranks on the same node call `load_configs`. But if the cache directory is on a shared network filesystem, multiple nodes writing the same path concurrently can race; and if it is node-local, ranks with local_rank!=0 on the leader node (rank_in_group 0's node) rely on rank 0 (which is the leader, is_leader=True, so it does NOT write in the else branch's write clause since that is gated to non-leader). The leader wrote the file via the autotune context manager, so it exists — acceptable. Net: the added barrier is a mandatory global sync at warmup; low runtime cost but a potential deadlock source if any rank skips the else branch inconsistently.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-node (2+ node) startup with FlashInfer autotune enabled; verify no hang and measure warmup wall-clock.

findings.claim: The cache path now depends on a sha256 over `aot_compile_hash_factors(runner.vllm_config)`. If those factors are not stable across runs (e.g., include ephemeral values), the persistent cache will miss every startup and re-run the full FlashInfer autotune (a `_dummy_run` at max_num_batched_tokens plus per-tactic benchmarking), negating the intended startup speedup. Correctness of the persistence hinges on hash stability.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run engine startup twice with identical config; confirm the second run loads the cache (log 'cache loaded') and skips autotuning, and measure startup time delta.

findings.claim: `_resolve_flashinfer_autotune_file` calls `output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` on every rank (it is invoked before the is_leader gating). This creates the directory tree from all ranks simultaneously; on a shared filesystem this is redundant metadata I/O and mild contention at startup, though generally negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure mkdir contention across N ranks on a shared FS at startup.

findings.claim: fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_fp8_cutedsl eagerly compiles BOTH coarsen variants (1 and 4) via IndexerQFp8Kernel.compile(...) in the `for coarsen in (1, 4)` loop, even though only one variant is used for a given num_tokens. Although compile() is @cache-decorated (so it is a one-time cost per process), the first FP8 indexer invocation now triggers two CuteDSL JIT compilations instead of one, doubling the first-call warmup/latency spike relative to the pre-change Triton path which compiled a single Triton kernel lazily. On the request that first hits this code, this adds a noticeable stall.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end latency of the very first fused_indexer_q_rope_quant(use_fp4=False) call with has_cutedsl() True vs False (cold process), and compare to compiling only the needed coarsen variant.

findings.claim: This commit changes the DEFAULT FP8 path (use_fp4=False) to route to the new CuteDSL kernel whenever has_cutedsl() is True, replacing the previously-used Triton _fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_kernel. Any environment with the cutlass package installed silently switches kernels for the hot indexer path. If the CuteDSL FP8 kernel is not at least as fast as the tuned Triton kernel for the common small-batch (num_tokens<512 -> coarsen=1) case, this is a per-invocation throughput/latency regression on a frequently-called op. No benchmark comparing the two implementations is included in the diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark fused_indexer_q_rope_quant(use_fp4=False) throughput/latency across num_tokens in {1,7,32,257,1023,2048} comparing CuteDSL vs Triton kernel on target GPU.

findings.claim: For the coarsen=1 case (num_tokens<512), threads_per_token = (num_heads/coarsen)*subwarp_size = 64*8 = 512 with tb_size=128, so must_in_bounds is False and per-thread bounds checks (in_bounds guards) are retained inside the kernel, unlike the coarsen=4 case where a token maps into fewer threads. This keeps predicated loads/stores and may reduce effective utilization for the small-batch path that is selected precisely when tokens are few.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel time for coarsen=1 vs coarsen=4 at num_tokens just below and above 512 to validate the heuristic threshold.

findings.claim: Raising KMP_BLOCKTIME from 1ms to 5ms makes idle IOMP worker threads spin-wait ~5x longer before sleeping between parallel regions. For workloads with bursty/sparse parallel regions this increases busy-spin CPU/power consumption and can contend with the reserved scheduler core (only 1 core reserved on x86). Intended as a throughput improvement (masks thread underutilization), but a possible latency/power downside under low occupancy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU decode/prefill throughput and per-core utilization under low request concurrency (batch=1) comparing KMP_BLOCKTIME=1 vs 5.

findings.claim: This commit reverts the `_patch_should_realize_on_reuse` monkeypatch that was applied only for torch 2.11.0. Per the reverted code's own docstring (and vLLM issue #27828), without this patch Inductor's materialization heuristic inlines a chain of computations when compiling the whole model graph at once, so the residual in `fused_add_rms_norm` gets recomputed on every use and this recomputation cascades through the entire model. Removing the patch therefore reintroduces redundant recompute in the compiled forward pass on torch 2.11.0, increasing GPU work and degrading throughput / raising per-step latency for compiled models on that torch version. Users on torch <2.12 (before the upstream fix pytorch#176994 lands in 2.12) lose the size-aware realize-on-reuse cost model.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a torch 2.11.0 build, run an end-to-end torch.compile (Inductor, fullgraph) forward-pass benchmark of a transformer model that uses fused_add_rms_norm (e.g. a Llama-family model) with and without this patch present, measuring steady-state decode/prefill throughput and per-step latency to quantify the recomputation cascade.

findings.claim: The reverted patch's cost model chose to materialize (write out) reused pointwise/reduction results when re-reading them would cost more total memory traffic than materializing. Reverting restores the default heuristic which favors inlining/re-reading; for large fp32 intermediates this could change the read-vs-write balance. Whether this is net-positive or net-negative depends on the graph, so the runtime effect on memory bandwidth is model-dependent and should be measured rather than assumed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare Inductor-generated kernel memory-traffic profiles (bytes read/written) for a compiled model on torch 2.11.0 with and without the patch.

findings.claim: The patched should_realize_on_reuse uses a size-aware cost model to decide realization. In edge cases it may over-materialize tensors (realizing when recompute would actually be cheaper), which increases peak activation memory and write traffic. This is heuristic and version-gated to torch 2.11.0 only; the overall intent (avoiding cascading recompute of e.g. fused_add_rms_norm residuals) is a runtime improvement, so any regression is speculative and confined to specific graph shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile a representative model (e.g. Llama) on torch 2.11.0 with and without this patch and compare decode throughput and peak GPU memory to confirm the cost model does not over-materialize.
