findings.claim: The patched heuristic computes get_dep_size_hint over all reads and an optimization_hint sizevar query per reused StorageBox, which is more work than the original num_reads()>threshold check. This adds one-time torch.compile compilation overhead but does not affect steady-state runtime. Gated to torch 2.11.0 only.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile wall-clock time for a large model graph on torch 2.11.0 with and without the patch.

findings.claim: Enabling FULL cudagraph capture (UNIFORM_BATCH) for TRITON_MLA freezes the dynamically-computed `num_kv_splits` at capture time. In `forward_mqa`, `num_kv_splits` is derived from `attn_metadata.max_seq_len` (line 155) which is a Python int read on the host and used to shape the `attn_logits` tensor and to launch the decode kernel. Under CUDA-graph replay, only device memory contents change; host-derived control values like `num_kv_splits` and the `attn_logits` shape are frozen to whatever `max_seq_len` was at capture time. If the captured `max_seq_len` differs from the real workload's `max_seq_len`, the kernel will either (a) use too few splits for long contexts -> serialized decode / poor GPU utilization, or (b) use too many splits (over-allocating `attn_logits` and launching redundant split-reduction work) for short contexts. Unlike FlashMLA, which added persistent `cg_buf_*` buffers precisely to make its split metadata cudagraph-safe, this change adds no such handling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture full cudagraphs for TRITON_MLA decode, then run decode with sequence lengths both shorter and much longer than the capture-time max_seq_len; measure decode latency/throughput vs. eager (non-cudagraph) mode across the seq-len range.

findings.claim: `forward_mqa` allocates `o` (torch.zeros), `lse` (torch.zeros), and `attn_logits` (torch.empty) on every call. During CUDA-graph capture these allocations are baked into the graph as fixed pointers/sizes; the code contains no persistent pre-allocated cudagraph buffers (contrast FlashMLA's `cg_buf_tile_scheduler_metadata` / `cg_buf_num_splits`). The existing in-code TODO 'Allocate ahead of time' flags this. When capturing across multiple batch-size buckets, each capture pins its own allocation, and the `torch.zeros` init work is captured into the replayed graph, adding avoidable memset overhead per decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step decode latency and peak memory for TRITON_MLA with full cudagraphs enabled vs disabled, comparing the fresh-allocation path against a preallocated-buffer variant.

findings.claim: The new autotune warmup calls world.broadcast_object(tune_results, src=0). On non-leader ranks, the leader runs the autotuning inside `with fi_utils.autotune(tune_mode=True, ...)` while non-leaders run a plain `_dummy_run`. The autotuning dummy run on rank 0 (which benchmarks many kernel tactics for all token counts up to max_num_batched_tokens) can take dramatically longer than the plain dummy run on other ranks. Because a subsequent `world.broadcast_object` is a collective that all ranks must reach, every non-leader rank must idle-wait until rank 0 finishes tuning. If _dummy_run itself performs any internal collectives (all-reduce/all-gather across TP/EP ranks), the divergent code paths (tune_mode context on leader only) risk mismatched collective counts and a distributed hang.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-GPU (TP>1 / EP) startup: measure warmup wall-time and confirm no NCCL hang when only rank 0 runs autotune(tune_mode=True) and other ranks run plain _dummy_run, then all hit broadcast_object.

findings.category: runtime kernel dispatch loses autotune context
findings.claim: In flashinfer_cutedsl_moe.py, trtllm_mxfp4_moe.py (both monolithic and modular apply paths), the per-inference call was previously wrapped in `with autotune(_is_fi_autotuning):` (i.e. autotune(False) at runtime). This change removes the autotune context manager entirely from the runtime dispatch path. FlashInfer's AutoTuner consults its active context to select the tuned/best tactic; without any autotune() context active during the forward pass, the kernels may fall back to default heuristics instead of the tactics chosen during warmup, negating the autotune speedup and making every MoE forward call slower.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Blackwell MXFP4/NVFP4 MoE model decode throughput before vs after removing the runtime `with autotune(...)` wrapper, with autotune warmup enabled, to confirm the tuned tactics are still selected.

findings.claim: enable_flashinfer_autotune is flipped from False to True at optimization levels O1 and O2. This adds an autotuning dummy run at every server startup that benchmarks kernel tactics for all token counts up to max_num_batched_tokens, materially increasing startup/warmup latency. It was previously disabled due to a flashinfer correctness issue (#3197); re-enabling by default trades startup time for (intended) steady-state speed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure server cold-start / warmup wall-clock with enable_flashinfer_autotune True vs False on a large-token-budget config.

findings.category: warmup broadcast of large object over CPU
findings.claim: The autotune cache JSON is read into a Python bytes object and broadcast via broadcast_object (pickle over the CPU/gloo path). For large tune caches this serialize+broadcast happens once at startup on every rank and could add noticeable startup latency, though it is one-time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time broadcast_object for a representative autotune_cache.json size across N ranks.

findings.claim: The new block_size_m tuning loop in batched_fused_marlin_moe compares against BATCH_TOKENS_MAX (the fixed per-batch token *capacity*), not the number of *valid* tokens per expert. Since BATCH_TOKENS_MAX is a constant that is typically >= the block sizes in the list, the condition `BATCH_TOKENS_MAX / b_m < 0.9` is almost never satisfied for realistic capacities, so block_size_m remains 64 (identical to the pre-change behavior). The stated intent ('reduce padding overhead') is therefore not achieved for the common decode case where few tokens per expert are valid but capacity is large -- padding still uses block_size 64. Conversely, when BATCH_TOKENS_MAX is small (< ~57) it will select an 8/16/32/48 block, giving smaller, less GPU-efficient GEMM tiles. Net: no throughput gain in the common case and possible small under-utilization for tiny-capacity configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Batched (EP/DP) AWQ-Marlin MoE decode with realistic expert capacity vs actual valid tokens; measure per-layer MoE kernel time across block_size_m=64 vs the new heuristic.

findings.claim: batched_fused_marlin_moe now threads input_dtype into _fused_marlin_moe, enabling per-invocation activation quantization (marlin_quant_input) and, for int8, an extra elementwise `a_scales * input_global_scale` multiply on each MoE call. For the default bf16 activation path (input_dtype=None) this is a no-op, but for int8/fp8 activation configs it adds per-token quantization work in the hot path that the batched path previously did not perform. Verify the extra scale-multiply is not redundant with kernel-side scaling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Batched AWQ-Marlin MoE with 8-bit activations enabled; compare end-to-end MoE latency with/without the a_scales*input_global_scale step.

findings.claim: `cpu_gdn_attention_core` calls `torch.cpu._is_amx_tile_supported()` at the top of the function on every invocation (once per GDN linear-attention layer, per forward step). This dispatches into the C++ extension to probe CPU capabilities on the hot path. The result is a static hardware property that never changes at runtime, so it should be cached once (e.g. module-level constant) rather than re-queried on every decode/prefill step. The per-call cost is small but it is paid repeatedly across all layers and all steps.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated calls of torch.cpu._is_amx_tile_supported() vs a cached boolean over N=1e6 iterations to quantify per-call overhead, then multiply by (num_gdn_layers * decode_steps) for a representative model.

findings.claim: In the AMX path, `mixed_qkv.contiguous()`, `a.contiguous()`, and `b.contiguous()` are called unconditionally on the full tensors before slicing into decode/prefill portions. If the incoming tensors are already contiguous these are cheap no-ops, but if they are non-contiguous views this forces a full materializing copy of the entire batch's qkv/a/b tensors even when only sub-slices are needed. The original non-AMX path only made contiguous copies of the specific gathered slices. Consider making these contiguous only where required or verifying inputs are already contiguous.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile cpu_gdn_attention_core_amx with non-contiguous mixed_qkv/a/b inputs vs contiguous inputs; measure time spent in .contiguous() relative to the conv1d/delta-rule kernels.

findings.claim: The prefill AMX branch does `initial_state = ssm_state[prefill_state_indices]` (advanced-index gather that copies the selected SSM states), then `initial_state[~prefill_has_initial_state, ...] = 0`, then later `ssm_state[prefill_state_indices] = last_recurrent_state`. This gather+in-place-zero+scatter pattern materializes and writes back the full SSM state for each prefill sequence. For large hidden state (num_v_heads * k_dim * v_dim) and many prefill sequences this adds measurable extra memory traffic each prefill step; the decode branch avoids the gather by passing state_indices into the kernel via state_slot_stride, but the prefill branch still round-trips through Python advanced indexing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark prefill-heavy GDN workload and measure time/bytes moved by the ssm_state gather/scatter vs. the chunk_gated_delta_rule kernel itself.

findings.claim: MHCPreOp.forward_hip now unconditionally calls the pure-PyTorch reference implementation mhc_kernels.mhc_pre_torch, disabling the fused aiter kernel path (torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter) that was used when hidden_size % 256 == 0. The torch reference is a composition of many elementwise/reduction ops (RMS norm, sinkhorn iterations, mixing) vs a single fused kernel, so per-layer MHC-pre cost on ROCm/DeepSeek-V4 increases substantially. This is a correctness-motivated regression (aiter accuracy bug at large token counts) but is a clear throughput/latency degradation on the ROCm path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V4 forward on ROCm (GFX942/950), measure per-layer MHC pre op latency and end-to-end decode/prefill throughput before vs after forcing mhc_pre_torch, at large token counts (e.g. 8k-32k tokens).

findings.claim: MHCPostOp.forward_hip now unconditionally calls mhc_kernels.mhc_post_torch, disabling the fused torch.ops.vllm.mhc_post_aiter kernel for hidden_size % 256 == 0. Same as MHC-pre: the fused single-kernel combine is replaced with an eager torch composition, increasing kernel launches and memory traffic per decoder layer on ROCm.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V4 ROCm forward, measure MHC post op latency (fused aiter vs torch) and full-model throughput.

findings.claim: The ROCm forward_hip removed the native fallback (rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer_native invoked directly when skip_k_cache_insert or aiter disabled). Now the non-aiter case raises RuntimeError. This is functionally a hard requirement for aiter rather than a perf regression per se; however, when aiter IS enabled the code now routes through torch.ops.vllm.rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer (a registered custom op) even for the skip_k_cache_insert case that previously went through a direct Python call. Any per-call custom-op dispatch overhead is negligible; main concern is only that a previously-supported native path is no longer available.
findings.suggested_benchmark: N/A (functionality change); verify aiter-enabled ROCm sparse indexer throughput is unchanged.

findings.claim: The removal of _topk_indices_prefill/_topk_indices_decode collapsed the dispatch to always call torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_prefill / top_k_per_row_decode directly. Previously topk_tokens not in the fused fast-path set {2048} fell back to _topk_indices_torch (a torch.topk-based implementation). Now the C++ fused kernel is always invoked regardless of topk_tokens value. If the C++ kernel is only optimized/valid for the fast-path values, non-2048 topk_tokens may hit a slow or unsupported branch; conversely if the C++ kernel handles all values well this is a performance improvement. Cannot fully substantiate direction without kernel source, but the behavioral change is real.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run ROCm sparse indexer with topk_tokens != 2048 (e.g. 512, 1024) and compare top_k_per_row kernel latency vs the removed torch.topk fallback.

findings.claim: In _build_fp8_prefill_ps_metadata (called from build() on every step that has prefill tokens), the code performs several .item() calls that force device->host synchronizations: `int(qo_indptr_cpu[-1].item())` and, more importantly, `num_partial_tiles = int(self.fp8_ps_reduce_indptr[-1].item())` reads from a *device* tensor (self.fp8_ps_reduce_indptr) which forces a full CUDA sync/stall on the hot metadata-build path. This adds a synchronization to every prefill/chunked-prefill batch build, reducing overlap between metadata prep and GPU execution and lowering throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill/chunked-prefill throughput (tokens/s) on gfx950 with FP8 MLA prefill enabled vs. the flash_attn_varlen_func fallback, measuring per-step build() latency and end-to-end tok/s.

findings.claim: Per-batch metadata build allocates/clones several CPU int32 tensors every step: qo_indptr_cpu (slice + subtract), kv_indptr_cpu = qo_indptr_cpu.clone(), and seq_lens_cpu computed via diff. These small-tensor CPU allocations and get_ps_metadata_v1 CPU processing run on the critical build() path for every prefill batch and add per-step overhead that scales with request count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark build() latency with many concurrent prefill requests, comparing with/without the FP8 PS metadata path.

findings.claim: _mla_fp8_prefill_attn casts q, k, v from bf16 to fp8 on every prefill forward call (q.to(fp8_dtype), etc.). These casts allocate new tensors and add memory-bandwidth-bound copies each layer/step. For large prefill batches (total_q * num_heads * head_dim) this is a non-trivial per-layer overhead compared to the flash_attn path which consumes bf16 directly. Whether the FP8 kernel speedup outweighs this depends on shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-layer forward_mha latency for a large prefill batch comparing FP8 ASM path (including casts) vs bf16 flash_attn_varlen_func.

findings.claim: _init_fp8_prefill_ps_buffers pre-allocates persistent PS metadata buffers sized for max_num_reqs x max_prefill_qlen (=min(max_model_len, max_num_batched_tokens)). With large models/long contexts these worst-case-sized buffers add persistent GPU memory that is held for the process lifetime even when actual prefill batches are much smaller, reducing memory available for KV cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident GPU memory of the builder buffers for representative max_model_len / max_num_batched_tokens / max_num_seqs on gfx950.

findings.claim: The new code writes the bmm result directly into `out.transpose(0, 1)`. `torch.bmm(x, self.W_UV, out=out.transpose(0,1))` produces a result in shape (N, B, V) written into a non-contiguous view of `out` (whose natural layout is (B, N*V) => (B, N, V)). While this eliminates the previous explicit `out.copy_(out_new)` extra copy and `resize_`, writing a bmm output into a transposed (non-contiguous, strided) tensor forces the bmm kernel to write with a non-contiguous output stride. On some CUDA/cuBLAS paths a strided output is materialized into a temporary contiguous buffer and then scatter-copied back, which can silently reintroduce the very copy the change intended to remove, or select a slower kernel. The net win depends on the downstream consumer expecting (B, N*V) contiguous layout; if a later op requires contiguity it will trigger an implicit `.contiguous()` copy that was previously handled explicitly. Verify that the caller (mqa_output_slice at ~line 793) can consume the transposed/strided buffer without an implicit copy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _v_up_proj for DeepSeek-V3 decode shapes (N=128 heads, B=batch of decode tokens, L=Lkv=512, V=128) comparing bmm-into-transposed-view vs the old transpose+bmm+copy path; measure kernel time and whether cuBLAS falls back to a strided-output copy (nsys/torch profiler).

findings.claim: The old code returned `out` as a contiguous (B, N*V) tensor after copying `out_new` into it. The new code leaves the result written into a transposed view of `out`; the underlying storage layout of `out` is now (N, B, V) ordering rather than the (B, N, V) that a contiguous (B, N*V) view implies. If the projection weight W_O consumer treats `out` as contiguous (B, N*V), the removal of the explicit reshape/copy could change the effective memory access pattern and force downstream matmuls to hit a non-contiguous input, degrading GEMM throughput. Confirm downstream `@ W_O` receives a contiguous input.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end DeepSeek-V3 decode throughput (tokens/s) before vs after, plus profiler check for any inserted .contiguous() / copy kernels around the out_proj GEMM.

findings.claim: The new XPU branch of per_token_group_quant_fp8 calls torch.ops._C.per_token_group_fp8_quant with only 9 positional args (x, x_q, x_s, group_size, eps, fp8_min, fp8_max, use_ue8m0), whereas the CUDA branch passes 11 args including column_major_scales and tma_aligned_scales. If the underlying XPU op does not honor column-major/TMA-aligned scale layout, downstream w8a8_triton_block_scaled_mm may receive scales in a non-optimal layout, forcing extra transpose/contiguous materialization at matmul time (the .contiguous() copies in the E8M0 branch). Confirm the XPU kernel produces the layout requested by callers to avoid a per-call scale re-layout copy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark per_token_group_quant_fp8 + w8a8_triton_block_scaled_mm on XPU with column_major_scales=True vs the Triton fallback; measure per-call latency and any extra tensor allocations/copies.

findings.claim: Adding is_xpu() to the E8M0 upcast branch in w8a8_triton_block_scaled_mm makes every XPU block-scaled matmul with float8_e8m0fnu scales run _upcast_e8m0_to_fp32(As).contiguous() / _upcast_e8m0_to_fp32(Bs).contiguous() before the kernel. This introduces an extra dtype-conversion plus contiguous copy of the scale tensors on each matmul invocation. This is likely unavoidable (Triton cannot bind E8M0), but it is a per-call overhead worth confirming does not run on the hot path for non-E8M0 checkpoints (the As.dtype guard should short-circuit it).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark w8a8_triton_block_scaled_mm on XPU for both E8M0 and fp32-scale checkpoints; verify no extra copy occurs when scales are already fp32.

findings.claim: This change is a performance restoration: for topk_tokens==2048 it now dispatches to fused C++ kernels (top_k_per_row_prefill / top_k_per_row_decode) instead of the generic torch.topk path (_topk_indices_torch). torch.topk over the full logits row followed by a torch.where and copy_ is significantly slower on ROCm than the dedicated fused kernel that the commit restores, so the change should IMPROVE performance for the common 2048-token case rather than degrade it. No performance regression is introduced by the diff itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer_native decode+prefill top-k with topk_tokens=2048 vs the prior _topk_indices_torch path, measuring per-call latency and GPU kernel time.

findings.claim: For the decode path, when topk_tokens is NOT in the fast-path set (i.e. the else branch), the code now sizes topk_out to num_padded_tokens and does topk_out.copy_(_topk_indices_torch(logits, topk_tokens)). Previously the torch fallback sliced [:num_decode_tokens] before copy. Since logits.shape[0] == num_padded_tokens, _topk_indices_torch now runs topk over the full padded batch including padding rows, doing slightly more topk work than the old code that effectively only needed the decode rows. This is a small, only-when-not-2048 overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode top-k with requires_padding=True and topk_tokens != 2048 (e.g. 1024) to compare padded vs unpadded topk cost.

findings.claim: On every forward, _replay -> BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.replay() iterates a Python list of segments and invokes each callable individually (one CUDAGraph.replay per graph segment plus one Python-lambda call per eager attention break). For a model with N attention layers this is ~2N+1 Python-level dispatches per forward, versus a single graph.replay() for a monolithic FULL cudagraph. In the decode regime (small batch, CPU-launch-bound), this per-layer Python loop adds measurable per-step CPU overhead and can lower GPU utilization compared to a full cudagraph. It is an intrinsic cost of the breakable design and is gated behind the opt-in VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH flag.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only latency (single-request, small batch) with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH=1 vs. full cudagraph baseline; measure per-step host launch time and tokens/s on a deep model (e.g. DeepSeek MLA).

findings.claim: _capture() calls gc.collect() and torch.accelerator.empty_cache() once per unique BatchDescriptor before capture. During warmup, cudagraphs are captured for many batch sizes/descriptors, so this full gc + cache-empty is repeated once per descriptor. This increases warmup/startup time (each gc.collect can take tens of ms on a large process). The code comment acknowledges the tradeoff (avoiding per-segment gc), but it is still repeated per descriptor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure warmup/graph-capture wall time with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH=1 across the full set of captured batch sizes vs. baseline cudagraph capture.

findings.claim: When VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH is enabled, eager_break_during_capture wraps hot-path custom ops (unified_mla_attention_with_output, deepseek_v4_attention, sparse_attn_indexer). On the replay/eager path (capture is None or not _capturing) the wrapper still runs several checks per attention-op call: thread-local current() lookup, is_forward_context_available(), and get_forward_context().cudagraph_runtime_mode. This is a small per-layer, per-forward Python overhead added to every attention op invocation when the feature is on (default off means fn is returned unwrapped, zero overhead).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark attention custom-op call overhead with the decorator active but not capturing (eager fallback path) vs. undecorated.

findings.claim: The `@torch.compile(backend=current_platform.simple_compile_backend)` decorator was removed from `HCHeadOp.forward_hip`, together with the code comment stating 'This @torch.compile is necessary for accuracy as well as performance.' Removing torch.compile means the HIP head-reduction path now runs the Python/eager sequence of ops (kernel launch for `torch.ops.vllm.hc_head_triton` plus the surrounding view/empty operations) without the inductor fusion/graph optimization that was previously applied. On ROCm this eager path incurs extra per-call kernel-launch overhead and loses fusion, degrading decode latency for every layer that invokes the HC head op. The removed comment explicitly flagged this as a performance (and accuracy) requirement.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeekV4 decode on ROCm/HIP: measure per-token latency and HCHeadOp kernel time with vs without @torch.compile on forward_hip.

findings.claim: Two new persistent CUDA-graph buffers are eagerly allocated per metadata builder sized to `max_num_batched_tokens * c128a_max_compressed` (and `max_num_batched_tokens * window_size` for SWA) int32 elements. These are worst-case-sized buffers allocated unconditionally at builder construction and held for the lifetime of the model. With large `max_num_batched_tokens` and large compressed/window sizes, this permanently reserves substantial GPU memory (e.g. tens to hundreds of MB) that reduces the KV-cache budget, even for batches that never reach the max size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak/reserved GPU memory of the DeepSeekV4 ROCm sparse builders with large max_num_batched_tokens; compare KV-cache capacity before/after.

findings.claim: Each `build()` now performs an additional device-to-device copy of the ragged indices/indptr into the persistent graph buffers (`_copy_ragged_to_graph_buffers` with two `copy_` calls). This adds a per-step overhead in metadata construction that did not exist before. The copy is needed for CUDA-graph address stability but adds bandwidth cost proportional to the number of ragged entries every decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile metadata build() time per decode step for DeepSeekV4 ROCm sparse path with and without the buffer copy.

findings.claim: `import ray` was moved from a lazy import (inside the `mode == "ray"` branch of `trainer_send_weights`) to a module-level top import. Importing `ray` is heavy and now happens unconditionally whenever `ipc_engine` is imported, even for HTTP or custom-callable send modes that never use ray. This adds import-time latency and forces a hard ray dependency on paths that previously did not need it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `python -c 'import vllm.distributed.weight_transfer.ipc_engine'` cold-import time with and without the top-level ray import.

findings.claim: Packed IPC transfer (packed=True) concatenates weights into fixed-size contiguous packed buffers via torch.cat before generating the IPC handle. Unlike the default IPC path, which shares tensors zero-copy, packing forces a full extra GPU-memory copy of each chunk (buffer allocation + torch.cat of the chunk's tensors). This trades throughput/extra memory-bandwidth for bounded peak memory; callers enabling packed=True on systems that are not memory-constrained will pay a measurable extra copy per chunk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare weight-sync wall time and peak GPU memory for a large model with packed=True vs packed=False over IPC.

findings.claim: In receive_weights, `torch.cuda.current_device_index()` and especially `torch.cuda.get_device_properties(device_index)` are called once per parameter inside the loop to recompute the (invariant) physical GPU UUID. For models with thousands of parameters this repeats an unnecessary device-property query every weight; hoisting it out of the loop avoids per-parameter overhead. (Present in parent; verify the rewritten method still queries per-iteration.)
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark receive_weights over a state dict with several thousand entries, comparing hoisted vs per-param get_device_properties.

findings.claim: The FSDP MoE example `_full_param_iter` un-fuses experts with per-expert `.contiguous()` slices (expert[:inter], expert[inter:], tensor[i]) inside Python loops over num_experts per layer, plus a full-tensor all-gather (`param.full_tensor()`) and `.contiguous()` per parameter. This produces many small GPU allocations/copies during each weight sync for large MoE models. As example code the impact is limited, but users copying this pattern into a training loop will see per-sync overhead scaling with expert count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time gather_and_broadcast_weights_ipc for Qwen3-30B-A3B and compare against un-fusing via a single reshape/view instead of per-expert contiguous copies.

findings.claim: In load_row8_B_as_f32<c10::Half>, the non-Zvfh fallback performs a scalar per-element conversion loop (8 iterations writing to a stack buffer then reloading) on every B-row load. This function is called once per K-iteration in the innermost GEMM loop (gemm_micro_rvv_fma_Mx8_Ku4), so on RVV hardware lacking the Zvfh extension every QK/PV multiply pays a scalar round-trip through memory for each K step, greatly reducing throughput of FP16 attention. Only affects Zvfh-less hardware (explicitly a correctness fallback), and does not affect the primary Zvfh path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/cpu/benchmark_cpu_attn.py with --isa rvv and float16 KV cache on an RVV VLEN=128 host without Zvfh

findings.claim: reshape_and_cache writes the Key cache with a strided vector store (__riscv_vsse32/vsse16, stride = block_size) to perform the transpose to column-major layout. Strided (scatter) stores are substantially slower than contiguous stores on most RVV implementations, so KV-cache writes for the RVV backend may be memory-bound. This is an inherent layout choice mirroring NEON rather than a regression, but worth benchmarking against a blocked/transposed contiguous approach.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cpu_attn_reshape_and_cache throughput on RVV VLEN=128 comparing strided store vs. small-tile contiguous transpose

findings.claim: In reshape_and_cache the loop is collapse(2) over token_idx x head_idx, but slot_mapping[token_idx] (and the block_idx/block_offset divisions) are recomputed for every head_idx even though they depend only on token_idx. Minor redundant load and integer divide per head; negligible relative to the memcpy/strided-store work but avoidable by hoisting per-token computation.

The new `use_v2_model_runner` property is not memoized and, when the env var is unset (the common default path), falls through to `_get_v2_model_runner_unsupported_features()`, which calls `importlib.metadata.entry_points(group="vllm.logits_processors")`. `entry_points()` scans all installed distribution metadata on disk and can cost tens of ms in large environments. The property is evaluated at several distinct initialization sites (VllmConfig verification, Scheduler.__init__, gpu_worker.__init__, xpu_worker, and FlashInferMetadataBuilder.__init__), so the same expensive metadata scan is repeated redundantly ~4-5 times per process at startup. Caching the property (e.g. functools.cached_property or storing the result) would avoid the repeated scans. Impact is limited to startup time, not steady-state throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time engine startup (config verification + scheduler/worker init) for a Qwen3 dense model with several dozen packages installed, comparing against parent commit; instrument the number of entry_points() calls.

findings.claim: `_get_v2_model_runner_unsupported_features()` unconditionally imports and calls `entry_points(group=...)` even when the v2 runner won't be used (e.g. non-Qwen3 models), because `use_v2_model_runner` now runs the full unsupported-feature scan on the default path. Previously the entry_points scan for logits-processor plugins was only reached inside `_validate_v2_model_runner`, which ran only when the env flag was explicitly set. This shifts a metadata-scan cost onto the default startup path for all generate models. Steady-state runtime is unaffected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare startup time for a non-Qwen3 generate model (e.g. opt-125m) before/after this commit to measure the added entry_points scan cost on the default path.

findings.claim: TOKENSPEED_MLA is inserted at priority 2 in the Blackwell (SM100) MLA decode backend priority list (vllm/platforms/cuda.py), directly behind FLASHINFER_MLA and ahead of CUTLASS_MLA/FLASH_ATTN_MLA/FLASHMLA. The comment in the diff itself states this backend 'wins past bs≈8, regresses at bs≤2'. Because backend selection is a one-time, config-level decision (not per-batch), on any DeepSeek-R1/Kimi FP8-KV workload dominated by small decode batches (bs<=2, e.g. low-concurrency / latency-sensitive serving), it will be auto-selected over the previously higher-priority backends and produce measurably slower decode latency. Prior backends were chosen by benchmark; giving this one priority 2 unconditionally changes default performance for a whole class of workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA decode latency on Blackwell with DeepSeek-R1 dims + fp8 KV cache at batch sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, 16; compare auto-selected backend (now TOKENSPEED_MLA) vs FLASHINFER_MLA/CUTLASS_MLA.

findings.claim: run_prefill_new_tokens and run_prefill_context_chunk unconditionally call v = v.contiguous() on every prefill invocation. Because v is a split-view of kv_nope along the last dim (non-contiguous), this forces a real device-to-device copy of the entire value tensor on every prefill call, adding per-call memory bandwidth and a transient allocation. trtllm_ragged (the analogous backend) does not do this. If the kernel could accept the strided view or the copy could be avoided, prefill throughput would improve.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA prefill throughput with TOKENSPEED_MLA at varying context/chunk sizes, measuring the extra v.contiguous() copy cost vs trtllm_ragged.

findings.claim: TokenspeedMLAPrefillBackend.__init__ eagerly calls warmup_compile_prefill for both bfloat16 and float8_e4m3fn dtypes (JIT-compiling CuTe DSL kernels) during backend construction. This adds JIT compilation cost at model init time even for the dtype that will never be used (e.g. compiling the bf16 kernel when only fp8 KV is used, or vice versa). This is startup latency rather than steady-state, but doubles the warmup compilation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model initialization/warmup time with TOKENSPEED_MLA prefill backend vs pre-compiling only the required dtype.

findings.claim: The global decode workspace (_g_workspace) is sized using _TOKENSPEED_MAX_Q_LEN=8 regardless of the actual maximum query length in use. For deployments without spec decoding (q_len=1), this over-allocates the workspace by up to 8x (num_sms * num_heads * 8 * (kv_lora_rank+1) * 4 bytes), holding extra persistent GPU memory that reduces KV-cache capacity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure persistent workspace memory footprint of TOKENSPEED_MLA decode with q_len=1 vs the fixed MAX_Q_LEN=8 sizing.

findings.claim: invokeNormRouterGemm hardcodes programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed = 1, unconditionally enabling Programmatic Dependent Launch (PDL). The equivalent DSV3 kernel (dsv3_router_gemm_float_out.cu) gates this on getEnvEnablePDL() which only returns true when TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL=1 and SM>=90. The kernel also always emits the griddepcontrol.wait / launch_dependents PTX. If PDL is unconditionally requested but the surrounding launch graph/stream is not set up to satisfy the dependency (as it is for DSV3 only under the env flag), the griddepcontrol.wait can stall the block waiting for a preceding kernel's launch_dependents signal that never comes, risking added latency or a hang, and at minimum diverges from the vetted DSV3 behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run dsv4_norm_router_gemm at M=1..16 with and without TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL set, measure kernel latency and check for stalls under the model's actual launch stream; compare against dsv3_router_gemm PDL gating.

findings.claim: Every one of the kNumExperts (384) blocks re-loads norm_weight (H=7168 bf16) and each token's x row in Phase 1 to accumulate ss[m] (the RMS sum-of-squares) independently. The sum-of-squares is identical across all 384 blocks, so the RMSNorm variance is redundantly recomputed 384x. This is inherent to fusing norm into a per-expert-column GEMV, but it multiplies the RMS reduction cost by num_experts vs the standalone rms_norm kernel. For the low-latency M<=16 regime the extra bf16->float conversions and FMAs are small, but it is measurably more compute than the unfused path's single-pass RMS.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare fused vs unfused (rms_norm + dsv3_router_gemm) kernel time at M=1..16, H=7168, E=384 as in benchmark_norm_router_gemm.py.

findings.claim: Kernel uses __launch_bounds__(128,1) forcing min 1 block/SM and per-thread register arrays partial[kNumTokens], ss[kNumTokens], plus k_bases[k_iterations] (k_iterations = 7168/(8*128)=7). At kNumTokens=16 the two fp32 accumulator arrays alone are 32 registers/thread plus temporaries; high register pressure at M=16 may cap occupancy to 1 block/SM, though the grid of 384 blocks over-subscribes SMs so latency impact for a memory-bound GEMV is likely minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile achieved occupancy and register count for norm_router_gemm_kernel at M=16 vs M=1.

findings.claim: In FlexAttentionMetadataBuilder.__init__, if a user sets flex_attn_kv_block_size to a value different from the cache block_size, `self.direct_build` is forced to False. `direct_build` selects the fast path `_build_block_mask_direct()` (which avoids the expensive generic `create_block_mask_compiled` recomputation and is the path optimized for CUDA-graph capture). Falling back to the generic `build_block_mask()` path is significantly more expensive per-step (extra compile/recompile and full block-mask construction) and disables the direct-build optimization intended for PyTorch >= 2.9. Exposing kv_block_size without recognizing that any override != block_size disables direct_build can silently regress throughput/latency for the whole attention path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step attention latency and decode throughput for flex attention with flex_attn_kv_block_size == block_size (direct_build) vs != block_size (direct_build disabled), on PyTorch >= 2.9.

findings.claim: The new code lets flex_attn_block_m/block_n (Triton kernel BLOCK_M/BLOCK_N tile sizes) be forced to small values (test uses (8,16) and (16,16)). Small tile sizes reduce arithmetic intensity and GPU occupancy of the flex attention Triton kernel, lowering throughput. Additionally, when VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is set, block_m/block_n default to 16 and IS_DIVISIBLE=False is forced, which the kernel must honor — these small, non-divisible-optimized tiles are slower than the auto-tuned defaults. This is intentional for determinism but is a real throughput cost whenever these overrides are used.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark flex attention decode/prefill throughput with default kernel_options vs forced BLOCK_M=BLOCK_N=16 and IS_DIVISIBLE=False.

findings.claim: The block_m/block_n and q/kv_block_size values feed into kernel_options (BLOCK_M/BLOCK_N) and into BLOCK_SIZE for block-mask construction. Different values than the previous fixed defaults change the shapes/constants passed to the torch.compile'd flex_attention and create_block_mask, potentially triggering additional Dynamo recompilations (recompile_limit is 16). If users vary these values across runs/instances, extra guard failures and recompiles add startup/warmup latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile recompilation count / warmup time when varying flex_attn_block_m/n vs a single fixed value.

findings.claim: The workspace view is now captured once in __init__ via get_simultaneous() and cached as self._workspace_buffer, instead of being re-fetched on each prepare_metadata/run call. The WorkspaceManager returns a *view* into a single shared, growable backing tensor and resolves the slot via dbo_current_ubatch_id(). If the shared workspace is grown by any other consumer after this backend is constructed (before lock_workspace()), or if the backend runs under a different ubatch slot than the one active at construction, the cached view points at a stale/orphaned buffer. While primarily a correctness concern, holding a view into an orphaned pre-resize workspace can also keep that dead segment alive and inflate reserved/peak GPU memory (the manager explicitly calls empty_cache() to avoid this, which is defeated if a live view is retained). Confirm the workspace is fully grown/locked before these backends are constructed, and that DBO ubatch slotting is not in play.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MLA prefill (FlashInfer/TRTLLM ragged) with DBO enabled and with a workspace that grows after backend init; compare peak reserved GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_reserved) vs. the pre-change per-call fetch.

findings.claim: Moving get_simultaneous() out of run_prefill_new_tokens / run_prefill_context_chunk into __init__ removes a per-forward-pass workspace lookup (byte-size computation, offset accumulation, ubatch lookup, and view/reshape) from the attention hot path. This is a small latency improvement per prefill step; no regression identified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark run_prefill_new_tokens call overhead before/after the change for many small prefill iterations.

findings.claim: The CPU fallback path `torch.equal(w.cpu(), target.weight.cpu())` copies BOTH full weight tensors (lm_head / embed_tokens, each of size vocab_size x hidden_size — often hundreds of MB to several GB for large-vocab LLMs) from GPU to host and runs a full elementwise CPU comparison. This is a large synchronous D2H transfer plus CPU work that did not exist before this commit. Worse, this branch is taken exactly when GPU memory is tight (low free memory), i.e. during a memory-pressured model load, adding a significant one-time stall (potentially seconds) and transient host-memory spike.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load an MTP/EAGLE draft model with a large-vocab lm_head under constrained GPU free memory and measure load_eagle_model wall-clock time and peak host RSS vs parent.

findings.claim: The GPU-path heuristic `torch.cuda.mem_get_info(w.device)[0] < w.numel() * 2` compares bytes (free memory) against element count times 2, a unit mismatch. The actual comparison temporary allocated by torch.equal is roughly `w.numel() * dtype_size` bytes (plus device sync), so the threshold does not correctly estimate headroom. It can select the GPU path when there is actually too little free memory (risking an OOM during model load) or, for large fp16/bf16 weights, misjudge in the other direction. torch.equal on GPU also allocates a bool mask the size of the input and forces a device synchronization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare estimated vs actual free-memory needs for the torch.equal temporary across fp16/bf16/fp32 lm_head sizes; verify no OOM at load under tight memory.

findings.claim: _should_share now performs a full-tensor weight comparison (torch.equal over the entire lm_head / embed_tokens / per-layer shared_head.head) on every eagle model load whenever the draft reports owning its own copy. The parent code simply used the has_own_* boolean flag with no tensor comparison. This adds a full GPU (or CPU) tensor scan and device sync per shared weight — including once per MTP layer for shared_head.head — to the load path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure load_eagle_model time with a multi-layer MTP draft that owns its own lm_head (triggering the per-layer comparison path) vs parent.

findings.claim: The new `_in_flight_store_gpu_blocks` set is only ever shrunk in `_process_store_event` (via `difference_update`). If a store event never fully completes across all workers (e.g. a request is preempted or finishes and its store event is never fully reported, or a completion path other than `_process_store_event` is taken), the corresponding GPU block IDs are never removed. Over a long-running server these stale IDs accumulate, growing the set unboundedly and permanently deduping (suppressing) legitimate future stores of those same GPU block IDs after the block is reused for a different request — degrading CPU offload hit rate and leaking memory in the set.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Long-running eager-mode CPU-offload workload with high request churn/preemption; monitor len(_in_flight_store_gpu_blocks) over time and CPU-offload cache hit rate.

findings.claim: Dedup is now keyed by raw GPU block ID persisting across steps. Because GPU block IDs are recycled by the allocator once a block is freed and reallocated to a different request/content, a still-in-flight entry for a recycled block ID can incorrectly cause a genuinely new block to be skipped from CPU offload (false-positive dedup), reducing effective CPU cache population and thus lowering future prefix-cache hit rates and increasing recompute cost. The prior per-step `gpu_blocks_this_step` set avoided this by being scoped to a single step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Eager CPU-offload run with tight GPU block reuse (small num_gpu_blocks, high churn); measure CPU cache hit rate vs. parent.

findings.claim: When use_td is enabled (auto-on for XPU), TILE_SIZE_PREFILL and TILE_SIZE_DECODE are clamped down via `min(TILE_SIZE_*, block_size)`. With a common block_size=16, the prefill tile size drops from 32 to 16 and, for fp8/int8 KV (element_size==1) where the decode tile must be >=32, this clamp forces it to 16 as well. Smaller tiles double the number of inner-loop iterations and the number of block-table loads and dot products in the attention loop, reducing arithmetic intensity and hurting throughput on the platform that enables TD. The comment claims 'zero overhead when disabled', which is true for non-TD, but on XPU (where TD auto-enables) this is a real slowdown for small block sizes and can also violate the fp8 'tile size >= 32' invariant.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run unified_attention decode/prefill on XPU (or with use_td=True) with block_size=16 and fp8 KV cache; compare tokens/s and per-tile iteration count vs use_td=False.

findings.claim: Moving segm_*_ptr, k/v_scale_cache_ptr and the six scale strides from positional to keyword defaults (=None) changes the kernel's compilation specialization signature. Triton specializes on argument types/values; passing Python None for the stride ints when a branch is dead avoids materializing those args, which is the intended micro-optimization, but it also forces a recompile of all existing cache entries (one-time JIT warm-up cost) and means the non-TD/non-quant path now passes None where int64 strides were passed before. If any downstream branch dereferences these under a constexpr that Triton cannot prove dead, it would fault; assuming the constexpr gating is correct this is only a one-time compile cost, not steady-state.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-call JIT compile latency before/after and confirm steady-state kernel timings are unchanged for the non-TD CUDA path.

findings.claim: The new host-side gating in unified_attention (power-of-2 checks, triton.next_power_of_2(head_size), two stride assertions) runs on every forward call. These are cheap Python-level operations but execute on the hot attention path for every layer/step; the two `assert q.stride(1)==head_size` checks only run when use_td_qo is True so they don't affect the default CUDA path. Impact is negligible but non-zero on the CPU launch path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CPU-side unified_attention launch overhead per call with and without the added gating code on a many-layer model.

findings.claim: destroy() now spawns a daemon thread to run ncclCommAbort and calls abort_thread.join(timeout=5.0). By the code's own comment, ncclCommAbort blocks until CUDA graphs that captured NCCL ops are released, and those graphs are dropped LATER in the same main-thread teardown (after destroy() returns). Therefore in the intended/common case the abort thread is still blocked when join() is reached, so join() waits the full 5 seconds every time. This adds up to a fixed ~5s wall-clock penalty to every communicator teardown, whereas the previous ncclCommDestroy call returned promptly. Slower shutdown directly increases process exit / restart / worker-respawn latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time full engine/worker shutdown (destroy of parallel groups) before vs after; measure wall-clock from teardown start to process exit.

findings.claim: The 5s join timeout is incurred per PyNcclCommunicator.destroy(). A process typically holds multiple pynccl communicators (TP, PP, DP, EP groups), and cuda_communicator.destroy() calls them serially. The 5-second stalls therefore accumulate: N communicators can add up to N*5 seconds to teardown, multiplying the shutdown/restart latency in multi-dimensional parallel deployments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure total teardown time with TP+PP+DP enabled (multiple communicators) vs single communicator.

findings.claim: If ncclCommAbort truly blocks past the 5s timeout, join() returns while the daemon thread is still running with the comm handle. self.available/self.disabled are then set and the object may be freed; the still-blocked abort thread continues to hold GPU/NCCL resources until it eventually unblocks, leaving resources tied up longer than the nominal shutdown and potentially retaining GPU memory during that window.

The DP all-reduce coordination tensor is now built on CPU (`torch.zeros(4, dp_size, dtype=torch.int32)` without pin_memory) and then uploaded with `.to(device, non_blocking=True)`. When `disable_nccl_for_dp_synchronization` is set, `device` is 'cpu', so this now does a redundant CPU->CPU `.to()` copy (harmless). But in the normal GPU path, the source CPU tensor is NOT pinned, so `non_blocking=True` silently degrades to a blocking (synchronous) H2D copy. This does not eliminate the intended sync and each DP coordination step (once per forward step) still blocks. The prior code wrote scalars directly on a small GPU tensor; the scalar assignments `tensor[i][dp_rank] = python_int` are host scalars, which do NOT incur a D2H sync anyway. So the change adds an H2D copy per step that was not present before while not removing a real sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP>1 decode loop: measure per-step latency of coordinate_batch_across_dp before/after; check whether the new .to(device) copy is truly non-blocking (pinned) or blocking.

findings.claim: `bincount` now calls `expanded_idx_mapping.long()` which allocates a new int64 GPU tensor every call if the input isn't already int64. index_fill_ avoids the advanced-indexing scatter sync, which is the intended win, but the added `.long()` cast introduces a small extra allocation/kernel per invocation. Minor relative to the sync removal.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench penalties bincount() with int32 idx mapping, compare kernel count and latency.

findings.claim: gather_specific_token_logprobs now builds token_ids/valid_mask on CPU then uploads non-blocking, followed by writing column 0 on-device from the GPU `sampled` tensor. This is correct and removes the D2H of sampled, but the two separate `.to(device, non_blocking=True)` H2D copies plus a subsequent in-place GPU write add a couple extra small kernels/copies per step compared to a single device allocation. This path is only used by generative_scoring so impact is limited.
findings.suggested_benchmark: generative_scoring API with logprob_token_ids: compare per-request latency before/after.

findings.claim: Every MambaMixer2 layer now runs `_warmup_ssd_kernels` during the V1 profile run, invoking `mamba_chunk_scan_combined_varlen` twice (HAS_INITSTATES False/True) with seqlen=chunk_size (default now 2048). This triggers full Triton autotuning across all mamba layers at startup. For a many-layer hybrid model this adds substantial one-time engine-init latency. It also allocates several fresh temp tensors (x, dt, B, C, out, initial_states) per layer and calls `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` per layer, adding avoidable allocator churn during warmup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup / profile-run wall time for a Mamba2 hybrid model before and after this commit (e.g. number of layers x warmup cost).

findings.claim: `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` is called once per layer inside the warmup. empty_cache forces the CUDA caching allocator to release cached blocks back to the driver; calling it once per layer during profile run causes repeated expensive cudaFree/cudaMalloc cycles and can fragment/slow subsequent allocations. A single empty_cache after all layers are warmed would suffice.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocator calls (cudaFree count) and warmup time with per-layer vs single empty_cache.

findings.claim: The comment updated to say the Mamba1 default chunk size is now 2048 (matching the code). Note the actual constant was already 2048 in the parent; only the comment changed. If the intent were to raise 1024->2048, a larger chunk size increases the per-chunk SSD kernel working set / peak memory during prefill though it can improve throughput. No runtime change here since the value is unchanged.

The warmup uses fixed seqlen=chunk_size and batch=1. Triton autotune keys generally depend on tensor dtype and pointer alignment but the chosen shape may not match runtime block/grid configs for other sequence lengths, so some autotune cache misses can still occur on first real inference, partially negating the warmup benefit (i.e. warmup cost paid without fully avoiding first-inference JIT). Low confidence without kernel autotune-key inspection.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare first-inference latency spike with/without warmup across varied prefill lengths.

findings.claim: This commit reverts the 'device cache + async D2H pipeline' routing-replay implementation back to the older SharedMemory + fcntl-based approach. The reverted-to file re-imports fcntl, os, tempfile, and multiprocessing.shared_memory, and drops the async pipeline (_copy_stream/_copy_event, _device_staging/_pinned_staging, sync_fwd_experts_buffer_DtoH/_scatter_to_host/finalize_pending_copy). The parent implementation issued a non-blocking D2H copy on a dedicated CUDA stream and deferred the host scatter to the next step so the transfer overlapped the next forward pass; the old restored path performs a synchronous .cpu()/.numpy() D2H transfer that drains the GPU command queue and stalls the compute stream on every step for which routing is captured. When enable_return_routed_experts is set, this measurably reduces MoE throughput / increases per-step latency versus the parent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/latency of a MoE model (e.g. Qwen3-MoE / DeepSeek) served with --enable-return-routed-experts, ISL=1024/OSL=1024, comparing parent (async device cache) vs this revert; measure tokens/s and per-step decode latency.

findings.claim: The revert restores fcntl file-locking plus multiprocessing.SharedMemory as the routing-data transport from GPU workers to the scheduler. File-based locking and SharedMemory copies add per-step synchronization and copy overhead on the critical path when routing capture is active, which the parent eliminated by routing data through the Ray DAG / ModelRunnerOutput. This raises latency and can serialize workers under contention when enable_return_routed_experts is on.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step latency and worker CPU time with --enable-return-routed-experts across TP>1, comparing lock/SharedMemory transport (this revert) vs Ray-DAG transport (parent).

findings.claim: The revert removes the traceable vllm::capture_routing custom op and the persistent per-layer _routing_replay_out buffer attribute, along with the FusedMoE auto-incrementing moe_layer_id used to bind slices of the (L,N,K) device buffer. The parent's design was specifically arranged so all TP ranks write to a live buffer for symmetric CUDA graph structure and graph replay always targets the live buffer. Reverting to the callback-based capture reintroduces the stale-tensor-on-replay behavior the parent fixed; besides correctness, the older callback path can force graph breaks / disable full CUDA-graph capture for MoE layers when routing capture is enabled, lowering GPU utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify full-CUDA-graph capture stays enabled for MoE layers with --enable-return-routed-experts under this revert vs parent; compare decode latency.

findings.claim: The parent explicitly used int16 device/host buffers for expert IDs (half the D2H bandwidth and GPU memory of int32). If the reverted-to older implementation transfers routing IDs as int32/int64 (as was common before the int16 buffer design), D2H bandwidth and host memory for routing capture roughly double when the feature is enabled. This is speculative pending the exact dtype used in the restored code.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare D2H bytes transferred per step and host memory footprint for routing capture under this revert vs parent.

findings.claim: basic_cache was rewritten from a single flat view + advanced-index write (`kv_cache.view(num_blocks*block_size,...)[slot_mapping] = to_cache`) to a 2D indexed write with on-the-fly integer arithmetic (`kv_cache[slot_mapping // block_size, slot_mapping % block_size] = to_cache`). This runs on every drafter forward pass and now launches two extra elementwise integer kernels (floor-div and mod) over the slot_mapping tensor each step, plus a 2D advanced-index scatter that is generally no faster than the flat 1D scatter. For the extract_hidden_states path this adds small per-step GPU launch overhead. Minor, but avoidable by precomputing indices once or keeping the flat view form.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark basic_cache with the old flat-view scatter vs the new div/mod 2D scatter for representative num_tokens/block_size on GPU, measuring per-call latency and kernel-launch count.

findings.claim: extract_from_kv_cache changed from `kv_cache.flatten(0,1)[slot_mapping]` (flatten is a zero-copy view for contiguous tensors, then a single gather) to `kv_cache[slot_mapping // block_size, slot_mapping % block_size]` which materializes two extra integer index tensors (div and mod) before the gather. This adds two small elementwise kernels per save. The save path runs once per request rather than per decode step, so impact is small.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark extract_from_kv_cache old vs new for varying num_tokens on GPU.

findings.claim: save_kv_layer now slices `attn_metadata.slot_mapping` per request in a Python loop (offset bookkeeping) instead of using a precomputed per-request slot_mapping tensor. The slot mapping is no longer precomputed on the scheduler side, so the GPU slot_mapping tensor is sliced and consumed on the worker during save. This is a minor CPU/Python overhead per request and is only on the save path, not the decode hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile save_kv_layer wall time per request with many concurrent requests.

findings.claim: _post_init_kv_transfer_config is now invoked earlier and the HMA gating block additionally instantiates the connector class via KVConnectorFactory.get_connector_class (and, for MultiConnector, recursively resolves every sub-connector class) during config post-init to test supports_hma. This adds one-time import/class-resolution work at startup only; no runtime hot-path impact. Included for completeness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup time with and without kv_transfer_config set.

findings.claim: The core intent of this change is a performance improvement: in build(), seq_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu() is now guarded so the blocking device→host copy only happens when num_prefills > 0 or num_extends > 0. In the pure-decode path (the common per-step case for LLM inference), this eliminates a synchronous .cpu() transfer that used to occur on every build() call, along with the removal of num_actual_kv_tokens = torch.sum(seq_lens).item() (another blocking device sync). This is a latency win in the decode hot path, not a regression. The decode metadata no longer references seq_lens (max_seq_len/query_start_loc removed from AiterFlashAttentionDecodeMetadata), so no correctness dependency on the removed CPU copy remains for decode-only batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only steady-state throughput/latency benchmark on ROCm with AITER FA backend; measure per-step CPU time and any host-device sync stalls (e.g. torch profiler) before vs after.

findings.claim: In batch-invariant mode the new Cutlass FP8 dispatch functions force a fixed, M-independent tile config (sm90: M64_N1280/M64_N8192 with swap_ab and small Shape<_64,_16/_64,_256> tiles; sm100: M64/M64_swap_ab; sm120: M64; sm89: sm89_fp8_config_M64) regardless of the actual M. These tiles are tuned for small M (in (16,64]); for large batches (M >> 64) they will under-utilize the GPU relative to the normal M-adaptive dispatch (cutlass_gemm_sm*_fp8_dispatch), yielding lower throughput on large prefill/batched decode. This is an intentional trade-off required for batch invariance and only takes effect when VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is enabled (an opt-in determinism mode), so it is not a regression to the default path — but users enabling batch invariance on large batches should expect reduced GEMM efficiency vs. the non-invariant kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark cutlass_scaled_mm FP8 on SM90/SM100 with VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT=1 vs =0 across M in {64, 256, 1024, 4096} at fixed N,K to quantify the throughput gap of the fixed M64 tile config at large M.

findings.claim: Unconditionally forcing the KV cache layout to "HND" whenever the OffloadingConnector is configured overrides the default "NHD" layout for the compute path. Layout is a global, per-process choice (via get_kv_cache_layout -> get_kv_connector_cache_layout), so the entire attention/reshape-and-cache pipeline is affected, not just the offload transfer. Several attention/cache kernels are known to be layout-sensitive: e.g. the Triton reshape_and_cache path does not support HND (benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_reshape_and_cache_flash.py returns NaN for triton+HND), and FlashAttention/FlashInfer default paths are tuned for NHD on many GPUs. If the deployed attention backend is faster with NHD (or unsupported in HND), this hard-coded requirement can force a slower kernel path or an extra layout permutation on the hot path, reducing decode/prefill throughput and GPU utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a GPU where the selected attention backend defaults to NHD (e.g. FlashAttention on non-Blackwell), benchmark decode throughput (tokens/s) and prefill latency with the offloading connector enabled before vs after this change (NHD vs forced HND), for a representative model such as Llama-3-8B.

findings.claim: Returning a fixed "HND" regardless of vllm_config means that on hardware/backends where HND is not the natural attention layout, the framework may insert layout-conversion permutes for compute or transfer (the utils module contains kv_postprocess_layout_on_receive/kv_postprocess_blksize_and_layout_on_receive permute helpers). If layout no longer matches the offload buffer expectation vs the compute path, per-block permute+index_copy operations could be triggered on each offload/reload, adding memory traffic. The value should ideally be gated on backend/platform support rather than hard-coded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request offload (d2h) and reload (h2d) latency and observe whether layout permute kernels appear in a profiler trace with the connector enabled after this change.

findings.claim: create_chat_completion / create_completion / create_responses are now unconditionally routed through the extra async wrapper `_with_kv_transfer_rejection_cleanup`. For the overwhelmingly common case where no KV connector is configured (`self.has_kv_connector` is False), this still adds one extra coroutine frame plus attribute lookups (`self.has_kv_connector`, `request.kv_transfer_params`) and an additional `await` per API request. The cost is small (an extra await/frame), but it is paid on every single request to these three endpoints, including all normal serving, not just disaggregated KV-transfer setups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark request dispatch latency (e.g. many small chat completions) with and without the wrapper to measure the added per-request async overhead on the non-KV path.

findings.claim: For streaming requests, the wrapped `_create_*` coroutine returns an AsyncGenerator object immediately (before the engine actually admits/generates), so the wrapper sees a non-ErrorResponse and sets notify=False. If a streaming remote-prefill request is later rejected while the generator runs, no rejection notification is issued, so P-side prefill blocks may remain pinned until other cleanup runs — a potential memory-hold on the prefill instance. This is a correctness gap that manifests as stranded KV memory rather than a clear CPU slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Disaggregated P/D setup: issue streaming remote-prefill requests that get rejected/errored mid-stream on the D node and monitor P-node KV block occupancy for leaks.

findings.claim: The abort_immediately path calls scheduler.add_request(request) and then abort_requests immediately, which allocates full Request state (block hasher, request tracking) only to tear it down. This is only exercised on the rejection path (not a hot path), so impact is limited, but each rejected KV-transfer request now performs a full add+abort cycle in the engine core loop rather than being handled purely at the connector layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress a D node with a high rate of pre-admission rejections of remote-prefill requests and measure engine-core step throughput impact from the add/abort churn.

findings.claim: get_mla_metadata_v1(...) is now called unconditionally on every build() (i.e. every decode/forward step). This launches an extra GPU metadata-computation kernel plus host-side Python/aiter dispatch on each step. In the analogous dense implementation (rocm_aiter_mla.py _build_decode) the identical call is guarded by `if max_qo_len == 1:` so it is skipped whenever the shape doesn't support the persistent path; the sparse version has no such guard, adding fixed per-step overhead in the attention metadata build hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode-step latency / tokens-per-second for sparse MLA (DeepSeek V3.2 sparse) on ROCm before and after this commit, isolating the ROCMAiterMLASparseMetadataBuilder.build() time with and without the get_mla_metadata_v1 call.

findings.claim: The persistent work-splitting buffers (_mla_work_meta_data, _mla_work_info_set, work_indptr, reduce_* maps) are sized via get_mla_metadata_info_v1(max_num_batched_tokens, 1, ...) because in the sparse case each query token is its own batch entry. This is much larger than the dense builder which sizes with max_num_reqs. These buffers are allocated persistently per builder and scale with max_num_batched_tokens (which can be thousands), increasing steady-state GPU memory usage; the per-step get_mla_metadata_v1 computation over qo_indptr of length num_tokens+1 also scales with token count rather than request count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare persistent GPU memory reserved by the sparse MLA builder for varying max_num_batched_tokens, and measure metadata build time scaling vs num_tokens.

findings.claim: The persistent metadata is always attached to the sparse metadata (work_meta_data is never None), so the aiter kernel always takes the work-stealing persistent path. If that path is not actually faster for small decode batches (the dense version only enables it selectively), forwarding it unconditionally could reduce GPU utilization for small batch shapes rather than improve it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark sparse MLA decode throughput at small batch sizes (1-4 reqs) comparing the persistent work-stealing kernel path vs the kernel computing metadata internally.

findings.claim: The fused RoPE+KVCache+q_concat kernel `concat_and_cache_mla_rope_fused_kernel` launches with `grid = dim3(num_tokens, 1, 1)` where `num_tokens = slot_mapping.size(0)` (the un-padded token count). This is a change from the parent, which used `num_tokens = q_pe.size(0)` (padded count) — however the *positions/q_pe/k_pe/kv_c* tensors are now indexed by `blockIdx.x` up to num_tokens only, while they are sized `num_padded_tokens`. So the fused path itself is fine, but note that the whole point of this commit is to replace two separate ops (RoPE + concat_and_cache_mla) with one kernel launch. If the fusion pass fails to match (e.g. because DeepseekScalingRotaryEmbedding.forward_static path or use_flashinfer variants don't match the traced graph exactly), the model silently falls back to the unfused two-kernel path with no perf regression, but also with no benefit — the gain is fully dependent on pattern-match success. Verify the pattern actually matches at runtime for the target model.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer MLA attention prep latency (RoPE + KV cache write) with and without fuse_rope_kvcache_cat_mla on DeepSeek-R1 decode; confirm the fused kernel is actually being invoked (not the fallback).

findings.claim: `MLARoPEKVCacheCatFusionPass.__init__` registers a pattern for the cross product of {is_neox: 2} x {use_deepseek_scaling: 2} x {use_flashinfer: 2 when RotaryEmbedding.enabled()} = up to 8 patterns per MLA layer. Although `if _USE_LAYERNAME: break` limits this to a single layer's worth of patterns, when layer-name encoding is NOT used every MLA layer registers its own 8 patterns, which for a 60+ layer DeepSeek model is ~480 pattern matchers. Each registered pattern increases torch.compile / inductor pattern-matching pass time at startup. Most of these variants (flashinfer vs native, neox vs gptj) are mutually exclusive for a given deployment, so most registered patterns can never match, wasting compile time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile / post-grad pass wall-clock time for a DeepSeek MLA model with fuse_rope_kvcache_cat_mla on vs off, in the _USE_LAYERNAME=False configuration, to quantify added startup latency from the ~8*num_layers registered patterns.

findings.claim: The kernel signature now templates an extra `cos_sin_t` type and nests a second `VLLM_DISPATCH_FLOATING_TYPES` over `rope_cos_sin_cache.scalar_type()` inside the existing `qk_t` dispatch. This roughly doubles the number of instantiated kernel specializations (qk_t x cos_sin_t combinations), increasing compiled binary size and CUDA JIT/module-load footprint. It also removes the previous constraint `rope_cos_sin_cache.scalar_type() == q_pe.scalar_type()`, enabling e.g. fp32 cos_sin_cache with bf16 q — correct, but each additional cast (`static_cast<qk_t>(...)`) is negligible at runtime. Main cost is code size / instantiation, not runtime throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare compiled csrc object size and kernel launch/module-load latency before vs after adding the cos_sin_t dispatch dimension.

findings.claim: The `compile` classmethod is decorated as `@cache` wrapping `@staticmethod` (in that order). `@staticmethod` is applied first, producing a `staticmethod` object which `@cache` then wraps. This ordering is inverted from the correct `@staticmethod` over `@cache`. On Python <3.10 a `staticmethod` object is not callable, so `functools.cache` cannot invoke/cache it and every call would raise or bypass caching; even on 3.10+ this relies on `staticmethod` being callable. If caching does not take effect, `cute.compile(...)` (an expensive JIT compile) is re-run on EVERY forward pass through `fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_mxfp4_cutedsl`, causing a massive per-step slowdown rather than a one-time compile. Because the fast path depends entirely on this cache working, the fragile decorator ordering is a real performance risk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call fused_indexer_q_rope_quant(use_fp4=True) repeatedly (e.g. 100 iterations) with cutedsl available and time each call; verify only the first call pays JIT compile cost and subsequent calls are ~constant-time launches.

findings.claim: On every invocation, the wrapper eagerly loops `for coarsen in (1, 4): IndexerQMxFp4Kernel.compile(...)` to compile both kernel variants before selecting one via the heuristic. This is intended to be amortized by the `@cache` on `compile`, but it still incurs two cache lookups plus dict/hash work of the full argument tuple on the hot path every forward pass, and always builds/compiles the unused variant at least once. Compiling both variants up front (rather than lazily compiling only the selected coarsen) doubles the one-time JIT cost and wastes GPU/CPU compile time for the coarsen value that a given deployment's typical batch size never selects.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-call latency and steady-state per-call overhead of fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_mxfp4_cutedsl with only-selected-variant compilation vs. compiling both variants.

findings.claim: The CuteDSL path performs synchronous JIT compilation (`cute.compile`) of two kernel variants on the first request that hits it. This introduces a one-time blocking latency spike (potentially seconds) on the first token/prefill after the indexer path is first exercised, which can manifest as a stall in serving unless warmed up ahead of time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the very first call to fused_indexer_q_rope_quant(use_fp4=True) on a cold process to quantify the compile-induced latency, and confirm whether a warmup pass hides it.

findings.claim: `has_cutedsl()` is now called on every fused_indexer_q_rope_quant invocation to branch between kernels. `_has_module` is @cache-decorated so the underlying find_spec is cheap, but the branch plus the Python-side wrapper (float() conversions, tuple building, cache lookups) adds constant per-call overhead on a hot inference path relative to the previous direct Triton launch. Minor, but non-zero on high-QPS decode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark per-call Python overhead of the dispatch wrapper (excluding kernel time) for the cutedsl path vs. the direct triton launch.

findings.claim: encode_pooling_output_float_or_ndarray relies on try/except around data.numpy() as its control flow. If the pooling output tensor is not on CPU (e.g. still a CUDA tensor), tensor.numpy() raises RuntimeError on EVERY item of EVERY request, and the code silently falls back to .tolist() (which itself copies GPU->CPU). Raising and catching an exception per embedding item in the response hot path adds per-request overhead and negates the intended zero-copy speedup in that configuration. Prefer an explicit device/dtype check (e.g. data.device.type == 'cpu' and dtype supported by orjson) instead of exception-driven fallback.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark /v1/embeddings float-encoding latency with pooler outputs left on GPU vs already on CPU; measure per-request time in _openai_json_response for batch sizes 1/32/256.

findings.claim: orjson only serializes numpy arrays of specific dtypes (float32/float64/contiguous int types). For half-precision embeddings (float16/bfloat16) data.numpy() may succeed (float16) or raise inside the returned-value serialization step rather than inside the try/except in encode_pooling_output_float_or_ndarray. When ORJSONResponse(content=ndarray_response) later fails to serialize the dtype, the fallback in encode_pooling_output_float_or_ndarray is never reached, so instead of degrading gracefully to .tolist() the whole request errors — turning a fast path into a failure/retry cost. The dtype should be validated (or cast to float32) before returning the ndarray.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Send float-encoding embedding requests for a model whose pooler emits float16/bfloat16 and measure success and latency vs float32.

findings.claim: In apply_scaled_mm, B is the static layer weight `w` (from _get_layer_params(layer)), not a per-call activation. When N or K is not 16-aligned, the padding path runs on EVERY forward pass: `B = torch.nn.functional.pad(B.t().contiguous(), (0, pad_k, 0, pad_n)).t()`. This does a full `.contiguous()` copy of the N*K FP8 weight matrix plus a second allocation for the pad, on the GEMM hot path, for every token batch. Because the weight never changes, this padding should be done once in `process_weights_after_loading` (as CutlassInt8ScaledMMLinearKernel does with weight transposition). As written it adds per-forward memory allocation and a large device-to-device copy proportional to weight size (e.g. Qwen2.5-VL vision MLP 3420x1280 etc.), which for these unaligned layers is pure overhead repeated on every step and can dominate the actual GEMM cost for small batch sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark apply_scaled_mm for an unaligned layer (e.g. N=3420,K=1280) at small M (M=1,32) comparing per-call latency of this padding path vs. pre-padding the weight once at load; measure allocator activity and copy time.

findings.claim: The per-channel weight scale Bs is also a static parameter but is re-padded via _pad_to_alignment on every forward when pad_n>0 (Bs.numel()>1). Like the weight, this allocation+copy of the N-length scale vector is redundant work per forward that could be precomputed once at load time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-call overhead of Bs padding for a per-channel unaligned layer vs precomputed padded scale.

findings.claim: When pad_n>0, `output = output[..., :N].contiguous()` allocates and copies the full M*N output every forward to strip padded columns. This is an added allocation+copy proportional to output size on the hot path. It is somewhat inherent to padding N, but note that padding only N (not K) still incurs this output copy; if K is the only unaligned dim this copy is avoided, so the cost is specific to N-unaligned layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end latency of an N-unaligned layer with vs without the output slice/contiguous, at various M.

findings.claim: This change forces unaligned shapes through padded CUTLASS instead of the prior Triton fallback. The padded GEMM computes on K_padded/N_padded (extra columns/contractions) plus incurs the padding and output-slice copies described above. Whether this is net-faster than Triton depends on shape; for very small padding-to-size ratios on small M it may be slower than the previous fallback due to the added per-call copies. Worth validating that padded CUTLASS actually beats the prior path for the targeted (small-M vision MLP) shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare padded CUTLASS path vs prior Triton fallback across UNALIGNED_MNK_FACTORS shapes for representative M.

findings.claim: _maybe_fuse_gate_weights() does torch.cat([self.gate.weight, self.shared_expert_gate.weight], dim=0) and caches the result in self._combined_gate_weight, but never frees the original gate.weight / shared_expert_gate.weight tensors. Since torch.cat allocates a fresh contiguous tensor, the router+shared gate weights are now stored twice for the lifetime of the model (original modules + combined copy). For every FSE MoE layer this permanently increases resident GPU memory by the size of the two gate weight matrices. Only a suggestion in magnitude (gate weights are small vs experts) but it is a real, unbounded-lifetime duplication.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident GPU memory of a Qwen3-Next FSE model before/after; compare sum of gate.weight sizes across MoE layers.

findings.claim: _maybe_fuse_gate_weights() is invoked lazily inside the hot _forward_impl gate path. The first forward that hits this path will run torch.cat (an allocation + copy) inline; if that first forward happens during CUDA graph capture / torch.compile tracing, the allocation is captured or graph-breaks. The guarded `if self._combined_gate_weight is None` check runs on every forward thereafter, which is negligible, but the first-call allocation timing is fragile for graph capture.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture a CUDA graph over a FSE MoE forward and confirm no allocation happens inside the captured region.

findings.claim: rocm_aiter_ops.fuse_sigmoid_in_kernel(aiter_topK_meta_data) is called on every routing forward.
