It is wrapped by @if_aiter_supported which calls is_aiter_found_and_supported() (invokes current_platform.is_rocm() and on_mi3xx()) and then is_fusion_moe_shared_experts_enabled() plus a cached topk_softmax_supports_fused_sigmoid(). These are cheap Python-level checks but run per-token-batch per-MoE-layer on the routing hot path; the capability could be resolved once at router construction and cached on the instance instead of re-dispatching every forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark routing latency per MoE layer with FSE enabled, comparing per-forward capability probe vs. a cached instance flag.

findings.claim: In the non-fused fallback path, torch.sigmoid(shared_logits) allocates a new tensor per forward and inject_shared_expert_weights performs slice copies into the pre-allocated AITER buffer (total_topk_weights[:, :topk] = topk_weights, etc.). Compared to a fully fused kernel this adds an extra elementwise sigmoid kernel launch plus two device-to-device copies per MoE forward when the aiter kernel does not support the 7-arg fused form. This is the intended fallback, but it is measurably slower than the fused path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer routing time on the fused (7-arg) vs. fallback path for a FSE Qwen3-Next model.

findings.claim: In build_connector_meta the persistent heartbeat table is attached by reference (meta.heartbeat_by_engine = self._heartbeat_by_engine) rather than being a delta. Each time the heartbeat interval elapses, the ENTIRE set of currently in-flight remote-prefill request IDs grouped by every remote engine is placed in the connector metadata, which is serialized/handed to the worker process. Under high concurrency (many decode requests waiting), this metadata payload grows with the number of outstanding requests and is re-sent every heartbeat_interval (= kv_lease_duration // 6, e.g. every 5s by default), adding recurring serialization + IPC cost on the scheduler->worker path that scales with in-flight request count rather than with new activity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Disaggregated P/D run with a large decode-side WAITING queue (e.g. thousands of queued remote-prefill requests); measure scheduler build_connector_meta latency and scheduler->worker metadata size with heartbeats enabled vs. disabled.

findings.claim: _send_heartbeats sends one send_notif per remote agent per remote engine every heartbeat interval, and the heartbeat message packs ALL tracked req_ids for that engine into a single 'HB:req1,req2,...' string. For heterogeneous-TP / multi-consumer deployments the per-engine agent fan-out multiplies the number of notif sends, and the message size grows linearly with the number of outstanding requests to that engine. This is a recurring background cost proportional to (num_remote_engines * agents_per_engine * outstanding_requests) that did not exist before, competing for the NIXL notification channel with actual KV read completions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Heterogeneous TP disaggregated setup with many queued decode requests; measure NIXL notification channel throughput / heartbeat send time and its effect on KV read-completion latency.

findings.claim: _send_heartbeats calls _ensure_handshake for every remote engine each heartbeat cycle, which acquires self._handshake_lock. This lock is also held on the request-ready path and in the handshake done_callback. Adding a periodic per-engine lock acquisition inside the worker step loop introduces a small amount of extra contention with in-progress transfer handshakes; effect is minor because it is throttled by the heartbeat interval.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile _handshake_lock hold/wait time during concurrent handshakes while heartbeats are active.

findings.claim: A new connector.on_new_request(request) hook is invoked for every request added to the waiting queue on the hot scheduling path. For the Nixl connector it does a handful of dict lookups/inserts (O(1)); for MultiConnector it fans out to every sub-connector. Overhead is negligible for single connectors but adds a fixed per-request cost that scales with the number of sub-connectors in MultiConnector setups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark request-enqueue rate (add_request throughput) with a MultiConnector containing several sub-connectors, before vs. after the on_new_request hook.

findings.claim: GDN_AITER_TRITON_AVAILABLE is evaluated at module import time by calling rocm_aiter_ops.are_gdn_triton_kernels_available(), which performs `import aiter.ops.triton.causal_conv1d_update_single_token` and `import aiter.ops.triton.gated_delta_net`. These aiter Triton modules commonly trigger JIT/Triton kernel registration at import, adding startup latency on every import of gdn_linear_attn even on non-ROCm platforms (though `if_aiter_supported`/`_AITER_ENABLED` gate this) and, on ROCm, unconditionally regardless of whether a GDN model is actually loaded. This is a one-time import-cost regression rather than a per-step slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure vllm import/startup time on ROCm with aiter installed vs. parent commit.

findings.claim: In the new _forward_core_rocm non-decode-fast branch, `core_attn_out.zero_()` and `z_out.zero_()` are executed on every forward before the kernels that fully overwrite these buffers (prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs writes z into z_out via `z_out[:] = z`, and _forward_core writes core_attn_out). These two full-buffer memsets are added work not present in the prior CUDA path and cost extra GPU memory bandwidth per step for the prefill/mixed path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step GPU time for GDN prefill on ROCm; compare with the zero_() calls removed.

findings.claim: ChunkGatedDeltaRule.forward_cuda now, when core_attn_out is provided, computes o via FlashInfer and then does an extra host-visible flatten+copy `co_flat[:o_flat.numel()].copy_(o_flat)` to fill the caller's buffer, rather than computing directly into it (as forward_native/chunk_o now supports via core_attn_out). This adds a full extra device-to-device copy of the attention output on the FlashInfer path each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark GDN prefill with FlashInfer backend on SM90 comparing with/without the extra copy.

findings.claim: prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs and rearrange_mixed_qkv are newly decorated with @torch.compile(fullgraph=True). Each new dynamic shape (varying num_tokens per batch) can trigger recompilation and guard evaluation overhead; if token counts vary widely across steps this causes repeated Triton recompiles and increased CPU dispatch overhead. The methods also build one big torch.cat buffer then slice, which materializes the full qkv/z/b/a into one contiguous allocation each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run GDN model with varying batch/token counts and count torch.compile recompilations / measure step latency variance.

findings.claim: In forward_hip, `z` buffer is allocated with dtype=projected_states_qkvz.dtype while core_attn_out uses hidden_states.dtype; if these differ, the subsequent norm/copy paths may incur implicit dtype conversions. Speculative — depends on projection quantization producing a different dtype.

In make_local_attention_virtual_batches, batch_indices/block_indices are created via torch.from_numpy(...).to(device, non_blocking=True). torch.from_numpy produces pageable (non-pinned) CPU memory, so non_blocking=True is silently ignored and the H2D copy is synchronous. Additionally, the change now performs two extra H2D uploads per forward (the index tensors) that previously did not exist: the old code indexed the (GPU) block_table with CPU tensors implicitly. If block_table is on GPU, moving these small index arrays each step adds transfers; if it were on CPU the indexing stayed on CPU. The claimed sync elimination is not achieved because the source is not pinned, so this adds copies without removing the sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark make_local_attention_virtual_batches per-step latency with local/chunked attention (e.g. Llama4) at moderate batch sizes; compare CUDA sync count and wall time before/after.

findings.claim: unique_static_unsorted now allocates a full (B, N+1) padded tensor and does a scatter_ over all elements plus a slice copy, replacing the torch.nonzero + advanced-index path. While it removes the D2H sync, it converts the work from O(number of kept elements) to O(B*N) scatter over the entire tensor and allocates B*(N+1) extra memory each call. For large B*N this is more GPU work/memory per call, though it removes a serializing sync so is likely net positive.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark unique_static_unsorted on representative [B,N] shapes measuring kernel time and peak memory before/after.

findings.claim: TreeAttentionMetadataBuilder now runs several torch.diff / .max() on CPU tensors and int() conversions on every build. These are on CPU so cheap, but they add unconditional CPU-side work in the builder even when prefill_metadata/decode_metadata properties are never accessed (previously computation was lazy inside the cached property). Minor CPU overhead per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure TreeAttentionMetadataBuilder.build CPU time per step vs previous lazy computation.

findings.claim: final_mask_mod changed from torch.where(is_valid, mask_mod(...), False) to `is_valid & mask_mod(...)`. This unconditionally evaluates logical_mask_mod even for invalid indices (torch.where also evaluates both branches under vmap/flex tracing, so this is equivalent), but it removes a select op. Likely neutral-to-positive; flagged only as a behavior change to verify the compiled block mask still prunes identically.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FlexAttention end-to-end and compare block-mask sparsity / kernel time before and after the mask_mod change.

findings.claim: config_exists() changed from an O(1) dict membership test (config_key in platform_data) to an O(n) linear scan over a list of entries (any(entry["key"] == target ...)), and each comparison builds/compares a dict. This is a regression in lookup complexity, though config_exists is only invoked during autotuning/saving workflows rather than the runtime kernel hot path, so impact is limited.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Repeatedly call ConfigManager.config_exists() against a platform file containing many entries and compare wall-clock vs. the prior dict-keyed format.

findings.claim: get_config_keys() now sorts CaseKey objects with key=str, which triggers JSON serialization of every key on each call; and get_platform_configs() re-parses the platform file into a fresh ConfigSet and iterates sorted keys. These run at kernel/config load time (typically once per process) so the added serialization/sort cost is bounded, but it is strictly more work than the prior string-keyed path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time ConfigManager.load_config_set()/get_platform_configs() for the silu_mul_fp8 configs (hundreds of entries) before vs. after the change.

findings.claim: DecodeStream is now referenced via the module attribute `tokenizers.decoders.DecodeStream` instead of a pre-bound local name imported at module load. This adds two extra dynamic attribute lookups per reference. However, both changed sites are outside the per-token hot path: one runs once per request in FastIncrementalDetokenizer.__init__, and the other runs only in the rare invalid-prefix error-recovery branch. The per-token detokenization hot loop (self.stream.step) is unchanged. The overhead is therefore negligible and not a measurable regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark constructing FastIncrementalDetokenizer for many short requests to confirm the module-attribute lookup vs. bound-name import has no measurable per-request overhead.

findings.claim: On ROCm with AITER MoE + correction bias, the gate is now forced to emit fp32 router logits (self.gate.set_out_dtype(torch.float32)). Since the DSV3/cuBLAS specialized router GEMM tiers are CUDA-only, the ROCm path falls to Tier 3 (F.linear) which computes in weight dtype and then does an explicit output.to(torch.float32) cast on the router logits every forward pass. This adds a small per-forward-per-layer cast and produces fp32 (vs. previously bf16) router logits. The router-logits tensor is tiny ([num_tokens, n_routed_experts]), so the throughput/memory impact is negligible, but it is a strictly-added op relative to the pre-change behavior where out_dtype was None. Note this is a required correctness change for the AITER biased_grouped_topk kernel (bias dtype must match logits dtype), so it is essentially unavoidable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure DeepSeek-V3.2 TP4 decode throughput on ROCm with AITER MoE enabled before/after; router GEMM output dtype fp32 vs bf16 should show no measurable difference given tensor size.

findings.claim: forward_xpu does not short-circuit the common case where both k and p are None. The analogous forward_cuda and forward_hip paths explicitly fall back to the cheap forward_native path when `(k is None and p is None)`, because there is no top-k/top-p filtering to do and the specialized rejection-sampling kernel adds overhead. forward_xpu always dispatches into the torch.ops.vllm.xpu_topk_topp_sampler kernel regardless, doing the full top-k/top-p sampling machinery even when neither filter is active. This wastes GPU work on every decode step for requests using pure random (greedy-off, no top-k/top-p) sampling.

On every sampling step forward_xpu calls generator.get_state() and then materializes a fresh CPU tensor `seeds = torch.tensor([seed, offset], ...)`. get_state() forces reading the RNG state (a host-side/blocking operation) each iteration, and building a new small CPU tensor per step adds Python + allocation overhead in the hot decode loop. Because seeds is on CPU while logits are on XPU, the kernel must consume host-side data each call, adding per-token latency that scales with decode length.

forward_xpu allocates a new `random_sampled` output tensor (and `logits_to_return` in processed_logits/logprobs modes) via torch.empty / torch.empty_like on every call. In the decode hot path this is a fresh device allocation per step; reusing a preallocated buffer would reduce allocator pressure.

`assert len(generators) != logits.shape[0]` is effectively dead because `generators` was already confirmed empty (falsy) at the top of the function, so len is 0. It is harmless for perf but the condition looks inverted/incorrect and could mask an intended fallback, causing the kernel to run on unsupported batch-wise-generator inputs.

CPUOffloadingManager.lookup() now returns None (instead of False) for blocks whose store is still in-flight (not ready). In the offloading scheduler, a None from lookup() propagates through _maximal_prefix_lookup/_sliding_window_lookup to _lookup(), which sets defer_lookup=True and causes get_num_new_matched_tokens() to return None. Returning None tells the core scheduler the request cannot be admitted this step and must be re-queried on a later step. Previously the not-ready block was treated as a cache miss (False), so the request proceeded immediately by recomputing those tokens on the GPU. As a result, this change can add end-to-end scheduling latency for a request whenever any of its prefix/suffix blocks is being written to the CPU offload cache: the request is stalled and re-polled across multiple scheduler steps until the store completes, rather than making forward progress. It also incurs repeated lookup() scans of the same keys on every retry step. This is likely a net win (avoids redundant recompute and yields a later cache hit), but it trades immediate progress for a wait on the in-flight store, which is a latency regression in the worst case (e.g. slow/large stores).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an offloading workload where requests share prefixes with recently-stored (still in-flight) blocks; measure per-request time-to-first-token and scheduler step count for admission, comparing the False-as-miss vs None-as-defer behavior. Also measure count of repeated lookup() calls per request during store-in-flight windows.

findings.claim: The `.tolist()` conversion of `routed_experts` (shape [gen_len, num_layers, top_k]) per completion choice, and `prompt_routed_experts` at the response level, materializes a nested Python list of size gen_len*num_layers*top_k (and prompt_len*num_layers*top_k). For a 40-layer top-22 model with 1024 generated tokens this is ~900K Python ints per choice built on the request-serving (async) event loop. This adds significant CPU/GC cost and latency to response building whenever routing replay is enabled and multiplies by `n` for multi-completion requests (prompt routing is shared, but gen routing is per-choice). The same pattern is duplicated in completion/serving.py.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end request latency and server CPU time serving /v1/completions with --enable-return-routed-experts on a 40-layer MoE, ISL=1024/OSL=1024, comparing .tolist() serialization cost vs. a compact encoding, at n=1 and n=4.

findings.claim: The new design performs a per-layer `.copy_()` of topk_ids into the device buffer inside every forward pass (documented as the dominant ~2% throughput overhead). Because the copy is issued per MoE layer on the main compute stream, it serializes L small device-to-device copies (plus a `.to(torch.int16)` cast) into the critical path each step. For deep MoE models (40+ layers) this many tiny kernels can be a measurable launch-overhead tax, especially in decode where the batch is tiny and kernel launch latency dominates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-phase throughput (small batch) with and without routing replay on a deep MoE model; profile per-step kernel launch count and the aggregate time of the per-layer copy_ kernels.

findings.claim: All TP ranks allocate a full (L, N, K) int16 device buffer plus a pinned staging buffer of the same size, even though only rank 0 uses the host cache (the docs justify this for CUDA-graph symmetry). This is a fixed per-GPU memory cost (~14MB per the docs for 40 layers/8192 tokens/top-22) that reduces KV-cache headroom slightly on every rank. Minor, but it is a real memory reduction that scales with max_num_batched_tokens and layer count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare available KV cache blocks / max concurrent sequences with and without --enable-return-routed-experts across N and L configurations.

findings.claim: Enabling --enable-return-routed-experts now rejects async scheduling at config time (and docs state sync scheduling becomes the default). Async scheduling overlaps the next-step CPU prep with GPU execution; forcing sync scheduling can reduce throughput/GPU utilization for users who otherwise run async. This is an intentional constraint but a real performance regression for that configuration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput with async scheduling vs sync scheduling on a MoE model at high concurrency, to quantify the cost of forcing sync when routing replay is on.

findings.claim: Disabling `enable_flashinfer_autotune` for optimization levels O1, O2, and O3 (changing True->False) causes FlashInfer to fall back to heuristic-based kernel selection at runtime instead of benchmark-selected best implementations. Per the `flashinfer_autotune` docstring in vllm/model_executor/warmup/kernel_warmup.py, 'Without autotuning, FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower.' On Hopper (SM 9.0) and Blackwell (SM 10.0) GPUs this can degrade steady-state throughput and per-token latency for FlashInfer attention/GEMM/MoE kernels, since these are the default optimization levels (O2 is default). This is an intentional correctness workaround (flashinfer issue #3197), but the runtime performance cost is real and applies to all users on the default settings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an H100/H200 or B200, benchmark end-to-end decode/prefill throughput and TTFT/ITL at O2 with a FlashInfer-backed model, comparing enable_flashinfer_autotune=True vs False, at max_num_batched_tokens.

findings.claim: As a counterbalancing effect, disabling autotune skips the `flashinfer_autotune` warmup path in kernel_warmup.py, which previously executed a `_dummy_run` at max_num_batched_tokens during startup. This reduces model warmup/startup time. Noting so the net tradeoff (faster startup, potentially slower steady-state kernels) is captured.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine initialization/warmup wall-clock time with autotune True vs False on a FlashInfer model.

findings.claim: Changing `max_seq_len = int(seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound.max())` to `max(int(seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound.max()), attn_metadata.max_seq_len)` means the per-ubatch max_seq_len is now floored at the full-batch max_seq_len rather than reflecting only the requests in this slice. Outside of CUDA-graph capture (the case this bugfix targets), a micro-batch that contains only short sequences will now report an inflated max_seq_len equal to the largest sequence across the whole batch. Because several attention backends size kernel parameters / workspaces / split-K heuristics off max_seq_len (e.g. mla/triton_mla.py `ideal_splits = max_seq_len // min_work_per_split`, flex_attention block-table width `cdiv(max_seq_len, block_size)`, flashinfer/rocm max_seqlen_k), the sliced ubatch may select a larger/less-optimal kernel configuration or allocate more workspace than the slice actually requires, slightly reducing DBO micro-batch efficiency. This is a correctness-motivated change and the effect is bounded, hence low severity/confidence.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DBO (dual-batch overlap / ubatching) with a mixed batch where one micro-batch holds only short-context requests and another holds a long-context request; compare per-ubatch attention kernel time and workspace allocation before/after the change to confirm no regression in the short-context micro-batch.

findings.claim: The default for VLLM_MULTI_STREAM_GEMM_TOKEN_THRESHOLD is lowered from 4096 to 1024. This threshold gates the multi-stream pre-attention GEMM overlap in deepseek_v4_attention.py (attn_gemm_parallel_execute passes enable=hidden_states.shape[0] <= VLLM_MULTI_STREAM_GEMM_TOKEN_THRESHOLD). Batches whose token count falls in (1024, 4096] will now take the sequential (non-overlapped) path instead of running the fused_wqa_wkv GEMM concurrently with the lighter compressor/indexer input GEMMs on aux streams. The original in-code comment claimed the empirical overlap-beneficial crossover was ~4096 on B300 (148 SMs) and ~3072 on B200 (132 SMs), where overlap was documented as a 5-45% win. If those prior estimates were correct for that hardware, this change disables a documented throughput win for medium-sized batches in that token range. The new comment asserts a corrected empirical crossover of 1024 (per PR #41526), which would instead mean the old 4096 default was incurring pure cross-stream sync overhead on (1024,4096] batches and this change removes it. The direction of the net effect thus depends entirely on which empirical crossover is accurate; the change is a pure constant tune with no code-path change, and carries regression risk on any hardware/workload where the true crossover sits above 1024.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On B200/B300, sweep prefill batch token counts across {512, 1024, 2048, 3072, 4096, 8192} and measure DeepSeek-V4 MLA pre-attention GEMM stage latency (and end-to-end prefill throughput) with the threshold at 1024 vs 4096, to confirm the (1024,4096] range is not regressed by disabling multi-stream overlap.

findings.claim: `envs.VLLM_MULTI_STREAM_GEMM_TOKEN_THRESHOLD` is read on every forward() call via the module-level `__getattr__` in envs.py, which re-invokes the `os.getenv(...) + int(...)` lambda each time (envs.py's __getattr__ does not cache results). This is a per-layer, per-token-step Python/os call on the attention hot path. The cost is small relative to the GEMMs, but caching the threshold once at __init__ (e.g. self._multi_stream_gemm_threshold) would remove a repeated env lookup from the per-forward critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated envs.__getattr__ access vs a cached int attribute over 100k iterations; also profile deepseek_v4 attention forward to confirm env-read overhead is negligible vs GEMM time.

findings.claim: get_humming_moe_gemm_type() no longer defaults to the grouped GEMM on TMA-capable devices. Previously, when VLLM_HUMMING_MOE_GEMM_TYPE was unset, the function checked current_platform.has_device_capability(90) and returned 'grouped' (grouped-contiguous GEMM, the preferred path on Hopper/Blackwell that leverages TMA, per the removed comment 'for device that supports TMA, use grouped gemm'). The rewritten function drops that branch entirely: with an unset env var it always falls through to the else branch and returns 'indexed'. As a result, on capability>=90 GPUs the Humming MoE will silently run the indexed GEMM instead of the TMA-optimized grouped-contiguous GEMM, degrading MoE throughput/GPU utilization for any deployment that does not explicitly set the env var. Because is_supported_config() in the new mxfp4 oracle path also uses get_humming_moe_gemm_type() to pick the experts class, the same default now selects HummingIndexedExperts over HummingGroupedExperts for the new mxfp4 'humming' backend on Hopper by default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Hopper (SM90) GPU, benchmark an MoE model with the humming backend (VLLM_HUMMING_MOE_GEMM_TYPE unset) before vs after this commit; compare MoE-layer latency/throughput between the indexed GEMM (new default) and grouped-contiguous GEMM (old default).

findings.claim: VLLM_HUMMING_MOE_GEMM_TYPE parsing was changed from maybe_convert_bool(...) to raw os.environ.get(...), and the consumer no longer maps a bare 'grouped' value to grouped GEMM through the old default branch; it now only recognizes 'indexed', 'grouped', and 'grouped_contiguous'. Deployments relying on prior accepted values/spellings could unintentionally fall through to the 'indexed' default and lose the grouped GEMM, causing a performance regression. Worth confirming the documented/accepted values match operator expectations.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify that setting VLLM_HUMMING_MOE_GEMM_TYPE=grouped still selects the grouped-contiguous GEMM path post-change and measure MoE throughput vs indexed.

findings.claim: In dynamic_quant_epilogue the parent code used `for (; j < hidden_size - vec_elem_num; ++j)` while each iteration loads/multiplies/stores a full vec_elem_num-wide (16-element) vector at offset j. This diff changes the stride to `j += vec_elem_num`. The parent's `++j` was a live performance bug: it ran ~hidden_size iterations instead of hidden_size/vec_elem_num (~16x more work) with heavily overlapping unaligned vector loads and stores. This change is therefore a large speedup for the per-token dynamic-quant epilogue path (used by onednn_scaled_mm PER_TOKEN). Flagging so reviewers confirm the corrected stride is intended and that output correctness/overlap is preserved by the following tail block.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark onednn_scaled_mm with dynamic per-token activation quant (bias and/or azp) on CPU across hidden sizes (e.g. 4096, 11008) and token counts; compare epilogue throughput before/after.

findings.claim: The new AVX2 FP32Vec16::min(b, elem_num)/max(b, elem_num) tail helpers spill both operands to memory (AliasReg union) and loop scalar because AVX2 lacks masked vminps/vmaxps. This is materially slower per call than the AVX512 masked variant, but is only invoked once per token in dynamic_scaled_int8_quant_impl to handle the ragged last vector, so the aggregate cost is small. Worth confirming it is not accidentally invoked in the main body loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark dynamic_scaled_int8_quant on AVX2-only CPU for hidden sizes not divisible by 16 vs divisible by 16 to isolate tail-path overhead.

findings.claim: FP16Vec16/BF16Vec16 save(ptr, elem_num) now fall back (when __AVX512BW__ is undefined) to a 16-element stack spill (_mm256_storeu_si256) followed by a scalar copy loop instead of a single masked store. This adds a full-vector store + scalar loop for every partial (tail) store on AVX2/AVX512F-without-BW builds. It only affects tail elements so per-call impact is bounded, but on kernels invoking partial stores frequently it is slower than the masked-store path. AVX512BW builds are unaffected (guarded by #ifdef).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare CPU quant/dequant kernels that use BF16Vec16/FP16Vec16 partial stores on an AVX512F-only (no BW) or AVX2 machine vs an AVX512BW machine.

findings.claim: In `maybe_make_thread_pool._borrow_from_pool`, when the pool queue is empty (`queue.Empty`), it synchronously performs `copy.deepcopy(og_tokenizer)` — a full deep copy of the HF fast tokenizer (including cloning the Rust backend) — inline on the request-handling tokenization path, and then `put`s the new copy back into the queue in the `finally` block. This means whenever concurrent tokenizer usage exceeds the pool size (`renderer_num_workers + 1`, which defaults to 2), each excess concurrent call pays an expensive full-tokenizer deepcopy, and the pool permanently grows to peak concurrency (steady-state memory grows with the highest observed concurrency and is never released). Under bursty load with the default small pool this can cause repeated heavy deepcopies and unbounded (up to peak concurrency) tokenizer memory growth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Drive the HfRenderer tokenizer with N concurrent async encode/decode calls where N >> renderer_num_workers+1; measure p99 latency and RSS growth versus the pre-change deep-copy approach.

findings.claim: Every public tokenizer method call (`__call__`, `encode`, `decode`, `batch_decode`, `apply_chat_template`, `convert_tokens_to_ids`, etc.) on the renderer tokenizer now routes through the `_borrow_from_pool` context manager, adding a `queue.get_nowait()` + `queue.put()` (plus contextmanager/try-finally) on every call. Tokenization is on the per-request hot path, so this adds a small constant overhead to every encode/decode that did not exist before (previously the tokenizer methods were called directly).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark encode()/decode() throughput on a fast tokenizer wrapped by maybe_make_thread_pool vs the raw tokenizer.

findings.claim: `maybe_make_thread_pool` eagerly creates `renderer_num_workers + 1` deep copies of the fast tokenizer at HfRenderer construction. Previously at most two deep copies were created (for mm_processor and the optional readonly mm_processor). For larger `renderer_num_workers` (e.g. 4 → 5 copies) this increases startup time and steady-state resident memory proportionally to the worker count, even for text-only models where the extra copies may rarely all be in use.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure renderer init time and RSS for renderer_num_workers in {1,4,8} before/after the change.

findings.claim: In the new prepare_encoder_metadata, when max_seqlen_override is None (the eager, non-CUDA-graph path), max_seqlen_full/max_seqlen_window are still computed with self.compute_attn_mask_seqlen(cu_seqlens) exactly as before, so the eager path is not obviously slower. However the refactor moves all the cu_seqlens/rotary/window_index host tensor construction into a separate function that is now always called from forward() when encoder_metadata is None. The reshape/patch-embed happen first, but the metadata building (Python loops over grid_thw, cumsum, F.pad, several .to(device, non_blocking=True) transfers) is unchanged in cost. No net regression on the eager path is evident, but the added dict allocation and function-call indirection per forward is a minor overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare Qwen2.5-VL image encoder eager forward latency before/after this commit with cudagraph_mm_encoder disabled, single image, to confirm no per-call regression from the refactor.

findings.claim: When *_override is set (CUDA graph capture path), max_seqlen_full/max_seqlen_window are created as torch.tensor(..., dtype=int32) on CPU (no device= argument), whereas the non-override path produces a value from compute_attn_mask_seqlen. If downstream attention calls .item() on max_seqlen during capture this is fine (baked in), but a CPU int32 tensor passed where a CUDA tensor is expected could force an implicit host copy per layer. This is capture-time only (not replay), so runtime impact is limited to graph capture, not steady-state throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument capture path to check device of max_seqlen_full/window tensors and whether any per-block host-device transfer occurs during ViT capture.

findings.claim: select_encoder_cudagraph_items and prepare_encoder_cudagraph_replay_buffers rebuild grid_thw via .tolist() and Python list comprehensions plus a torch.cat over per-item slices of pixel_values on every replay. For batches with many items this per-replay CPU concatenation and metadata rebuild (prepare_encoder_metadata runs its full Python loop + cumsum + F.pad + multiple .to(non_blocking) transfers) executes on every multimodal batch, adding host-side overhead on the critical path even when the ViT compute itself is graph-captured. This can erode the latency benefit of CUDA graphs for encoder inputs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark end-to-end multimodal prefill throughput with cudagraph_mm_encoder enabled vs disabled on Qwen2.5-VL for a batch of several images/videos, measuring CPU time spent in prepare_encoder_cudagraph_replay_buffers per step.

findings.claim: prepare_encoder_cudagraph_capture_inputs sizes cu_window_seqlens padding to max_window_seqs_per_batch = min(max_num_batched_tokens, max_model_len). For large max_model_len (e.g. 128k) this allocates a cu_window_seqlens buffer of that many int32 entries per captured graph, and the buffer is retained for the lifetime of each captured graph size. With multiple captured batch sizes this multiplies static memory reserved for encoder CUDA graphs, potentially reducing KV-cache headroom.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory reserved by encoder CUDA graph buffers across captured sizes for Qwen2.5-VL at large max_model_len, and confirm cu_window_seqlens buffer size scales with max_model_len.

findings.claim: MeanPool now builds `segment_ids` via `torch.repeat_interleave` on the CPU (using `prompt_lens_cpu`) and then uploads the result to the device non-blocking, whereas before the repeat_interleave ran as a GPU kernel. This correctly removes the implicit GPU->CPU sync that GPU-side repeat_interleave triggers to learn its data-dependent output length. The tradeoff is that for large batches the segment-id construction is now single-threaded host work and the H2D copy of an int64 tensor of length == total scheduled tokens (8 bytes/token) is added. For pooling workloads this is normally a net win (one sync removed dominates), but on very large token counts the added host compute + transfer could partially offset the gain. Worth benchmarking on large-batch mean pooling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MeanPool.forward on a large batch (e.g. 512 sequences, avg 1024 tokens) measuring end-to-end pooling latency and host CPU time vs. parent commit.

findings.claim: For groups after the first (token_offset != 0), DispatchPooler now issues element-wise GPU subtractions on `first_token_indices_gpu`/`last_token_indices_gpu` via dataclasses.replace to rebase indices into the sliced hidden_states. These are tiny (num_seqs-length) kernels, so the cost is negligible, but it does add a couple of extra kernel launches per non-first task group. This is dwarfed by the GPU->CPU syncs (.item() calls) it eliminates in AllPool, so net positive; noting only for completeness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-task DispatchPooler batch (e.g. mixed token_classify + classify) measuring kernel-launch count and latency vs. parent.

findings.claim: The new hc_head_fuse_tilelang kernel launches a grid of exactly num_tokens blocks (T.Kernel(num_tokens, threads=n_thr)), i.e. one CTA per token. In the decode phase of LLM inference the number of tokens is very small (often 1 per request, tens in a batch), so the kernel occupies only a handful of SMs and leaves the GPU almost entirely idle. hc_head is applied in compute_logits, which runs every decode step. The prior PyTorch implementation used F.linear (a batched GEMM) which, while launching more kernels, kept the tensor cores busy and had well-tuned occupancy. For small token counts the tile kernel will not necessarily be faster and can under-utilize the GPU.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark hc_head latency at num_tokens=1,8,32,128 comparing the new fused tile kernel vs the previous F.linear-based implementation, on the target SM100 GPU.

findings.claim: Pass 1 of the kernel recomputes the fn projections with a per-thread scalar accumulation loop over hc_mult*n_h*hc_mult inner blocks using T.alloc_reducer cross-thread reducers, replacing the original F.linear GEMM. The dot-products (mixes) are hc_mult x hc_dim = hc_mult x (hc_mult*hidden_size) reductions per token computed with a reducer instead of tensor-core MMA. For larger batch/token counts this scalar reduction path can be substantially slower than the GEMM it replaces, since it does not use the tensor cores and re-reads fn from global memory for every token.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare throughput of hc_head at moderate-to-large num_tokens (e.g. prefill / chunked-prefill sizes 512-4096) between the fused reducer kernel and the F.linear baseline.

findings.claim: residual[i] (shape hc_mult x hidden_size) is read twice from global memory: once in Pass 1 to compute sqrsum/mixes and again in Pass 2 (apply_mix) to form the weighted sum. This doubles global-memory traffic for the residual tensor compared with a single-pass formulation. Whether this matters depends on cache residency for the per-token slice.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile global memory bytes read for hc_head kernel vs a single-pass variant at prefill token counts.

findings.claim: hc_head_fuse_tilelang is decorated with @tilelang.jit and specializes on dynamic num_tokens plus the several static shape args (hidden_size, hc_mult, etc.). If the JIT recompiles for each distinct shape combination seen at runtime, the first invocation for each new shape incurs a compilation stall that adds latency; ensure the kernel is warmed up / compiled during capture so it does not compile on a live decode step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-call vs steady-state latency of hc_head across the range of num_tokens/hidden_size combinations encountered during a serving run.

findings.claim: The custom op `rocm_aiter_fused_allreduce_rmsnorm` is registered via `direct_register_custom_op` without a `mutates_args` list (defaults to empty). But the underlying aiter `fused_ar_rms` performs the RMSNorm+residual computation in-place on the `residual`/`input_` buffers (the corresponding flashinfer op declares `mutates_args=['allreduce_in','residual',...]`). Declaring no mutated args means Inductor/AOT-autograd treats the op as pure/functional, so it may reorder, CSE, or drop the residual write, or fail to insert the correct copies during functionalization. When `fix_functionalization` runs it will not fuse the op efficiently and may keep a redundant defensive clone of the input/residual, adding an extra full-tensor copy per fused allreduce+RMSNorm site (one per transformer layer per forward). This adds measurable memory-bandwidth overhead vs. the intended in-place path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP=2 ROCm decode throughput with fuse_allreduce_rms enabled vs disabled; compare per-layer copy count in the compiled graph.

findings.claim: In `AiterAllreduceFusedRMSNormPattern._replacement` a fresh `residual = torch.empty_like(input)` is allocated on every invocation of the fused op (the non-residual RMSNorm case). Since this replacement fires per matched allreduce+rmsnorm site and per forward step, it adds a per-call full-hidden-state device allocation. The residual is only used as scratch, so a reused/preallocated buffer would avoid repeated allocator churn on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP=2 ROCm decode latency with allreduce-rms fusion; profile allocator activity for empty_like calls.

findings.claim: `_rocm_aiter_fused_allreduce_rmsnorm_impl` recomputes `use_1stage` eligibility (numel*element_size, hidden/token/size checks) and calls `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` on every op invocation. These are host-side Python computations executed once per fused allreduce+RMSNorm per layer per forward, adding CPU dispatch overhead that can become the bottleneck for small decode batches where kernels are tiny. Precomputing the size gating once (shapes are static within a compile range) would remove this per-call Python work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP=2 ROCm decode with small batch (1-8 tokens), measure CPU-side per-step overhead / kernel launch gaps.

findings.claim: `test_tp2_ar_rms_fusions` was expanded from a single attention backend (TRITON_ATTN) to four backends (TRITON_ATTN, FLASHINFER_ATTN, ROCM_ATTN, ROCM_AITER_UNIFIED_ATTN) crossed with the rms_norm custom_ops combos and n_layers, roughly quadrupling the number of multi-GPU e2e test instantiations. This is CI-time (not runtime) cost but each case spins up a 2-GPU vLLM instance, materially increasing test-suite wall-clock.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure wall-clock of test_tp2_ar_rms.py before/after on a 2-GPU runner.

findings.claim: This commit's primary intent is a performance improvement: it removes a redundant per-forward dtype conversion. Previously DeepseekV4MoE.forward() ran `input_ids = input_ids.to(dtype=self.hash_indices_dtype)` on every layer's MoE forward (once per decoder layer, per step). Since input_ids is identical across all layers, converting it inside the top-level Model.forward once (`input_ids.to(torch.int64)` when use_mega_moe) and passing the already-converted tensor down eliminates N-1 redundant device casts per forward pass (N = number of decoder layers). This is a genuine reduction of small GPU kernels / host-side overhead. No performance regression is introduced by the diff itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step decode latency for DeepSeek-V4 with mega_moe/hash routing before vs after; count launched cast kernels via a profiler across the layer loop.

findings.claim: The new conversion `input_ids = input_ids.to(torch.int64)` in Model.forward is gated only on `self.use_mega_moe`, whereas the old code converted to `self.hash_indices_dtype` (int64 only when use_mega_moe, else int32) and only when `self.gate.tid2eid is not None` (hash routing active). If hash routing is NOT active but use_mega_moe is True, the new code performs an int64 cast that the old per-layer code would still have performed (the old cast was unconditional on tid2eid presence... actually gated on tid2eid). Net: when tid2eid is None but use_mega_moe True, the new code now does one cast that previously was skipped entirely. This is a negligible single cast, not a regression of concern, but worth noting the gating differs from the removed code.

AsyncTPPass now registers two additional pattern/replacement pairs (FlashInferAllGatherBMMFP8Pattern and FlashInferBMMFP8ReduceScatterPattern) on Blackwell/bf16 models whenever torch.ops.vllm.bmm_fp8 exists. Each registered pattern is traced and matched against the whole FX graph on every compiled range, so this adds pattern-matching work at compile time. This is one-time compilation cost (not per-forward runtime), so impact on steady-state throughput is negligible, but it does lengthen graph-compilation time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile pass time for AsyncTPPass on an FP8 bf16 model with FlashInfer available (compile-time only).

findings.claim: On Blackwell (sm100 family) SP_MIN_PER_GPU_SIZE_MB is set to 32 (vs 8 on H100), which raises the min_token_num threshold 4x, so sequence parallelism (and the downstream GEMM+reduce-scatter / all-gather+GEMM fusions) will only activate at larger batch sizes on Blackwell. If the 32MB value is too conservative, medium batch sizes on Blackwell will miss the communication-overlap fusions that they would have gotten under the 8MB threshold, leaving communication un-overlapped. This is a deliberate tuning choice; correctness of the value is unverified here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Blackwell TP8 config, sweep batch sizes and compare per-step latency with SP_MIN_PER_GPU_SIZE_MB=8 vs 32 to confirm the higher threshold does not leave beneficial batch sizes without SP fusion.

findings.category: potential extra memory copy in fused FP8 matmul path
findings.claim: flashinfer_scaled_fp8_mm_out and the reduce-scatter adapter assert A.is_contiguous(). The symm-mem fused all-gather/reduce-scatter machinery slices/gathers A into shards; if the sliced A view passed to _flashinfer_scaled_mm_out is not contiguous, the assertion would fail at runtime rather than being handled by a copy, so no silent perf cost — but if upstream must insert a .contiguous() to satisfy this, that adds an FP8 copy of the activation per call. Unverified whether the symm-mem impl guarantees contiguity of A.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trace whether A passed into _flashinfer_scaled_mm_out from _fused_all_gather_matmul_impl / _fused_scaled_matmul_reduce_scatter_impl is already contiguous or requires a copy.

findings.claim: In _dcp_a2a_unpack_combine_kernel the packed fp32 LSE is re-loaded and re-unpacked (2x uint16 loads + shift/or/bitcast for the bf16/fp16 path) three separate times per rank (max pass, sum-exp pass, weighted-combine pass). The previous kernel also read LSE three times, but as a single fp32 scalar load; the new packed layout adds extra ALU (bit unpacking) each of the three times. On a memory-bound combine kernel this is negligible, but the unpacked lse_val could be computed once per rank and reused across passes to avoid the repeated bit manipulation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _dcp_a2a_unpack_combine over varying (B, H_per_rank, N, D) in bf16 vs fp32, comparing kernel time against the pre-change dcp_lse_combine_triton.

findings.claim: _dcp_a2a_send_recv_buffers now sources send/recv buffers from the shared WorkspaceManager via get_simultaneous when initialized. This avoids two torch.empty allocations per call (good), but ties the DCP A2A payload into the global workspace sizing; if this path requests a larger (world_size, B, H_per_rank, D+lse_pack_dim) buffer than previously reserved and the workspace is locked, it will raise instead of allocating. Not a slowdown per se, but worth confirming the reserved workspace covers the largest B (num_tokens) seen at runtime to avoid a lock-time assertion or, if unlocked, a resize (which calls torch.accelerator.empty_cache()) on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run decode with --dcp-comm-backend a2a and a growing batch after lock_workspace(); verify no per-step empty_cache()/resize occurs in the A2A path.

findings.claim: Setting the PyTorch caching allocator's max_split_size_mb to the minimum allowed value (20 MiB) for the entire model-loading window forces the allocator to avoid splitting any cached block larger than 20 MiB. During weight loading, most weight tensors are far larger than 20 MiB and many transient buffers get allocated/freed; with this cap the allocator can no longer satisfy large requests by splitting/reusing existing cached blocks, so it must issue many additional synchronous cudaMalloc (and later cudaFree) calls. Each cudaMalloc is a device-synchronizing, relatively expensive operation. For large models with many weight shards / conversion buffers this increases model-load latency measurably. The docstring asserts the overhead is 'negligible in practice', which is not substantiated and is optimistic for large models where load is dominated by many allocations.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end load_model() wall-clock time (and count of cudaMalloc via CUDA_LAUNCH tracing / torch.cuda.memory allocator stats 'num_alloc_retries' and segment counts) for a large model (e.g. 70B sharded) with and without the max_split_size_mb:20 scope.

findings.claim: torch._C._accelerator_setAllocatorSettings mutates a process-global allocator configuration, not a thread- or stream-local one. Any other GPU allocation happening in the same process during the load window (e.g. concurrent warmup, other worker threads, background CUDA work) will also be subject to the 20 MiB split cap and incur the same extra-cudaMalloc penalty. The scope is broader than the intended 'model loading' region.
findings.suggested_benchmark: In a multi-threaded/DP setup, time allocations performed by unrelated threads while one worker is inside _scoped_allocator_max_split.

findings.claim: On exit the code restores only max_split_size_mb, parsed via regex from PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF, defaulting to SIZE_MAX when unset. This ignores that the user may have configured max_split_size_mb via the newer PYTORCH_ALLOC_CONF variable or programmatically; in those cases the original limit is not detected and gets reset to SIZE_MAX (no limit), silently discarding a user's intended fragmentation cap for the remainder of the process and potentially changing later steady-state allocator behavior/memory footprint.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Set PYTORCH_ALLOC_CONF=max_split_size_mb:512, run load_model, then inspect the effective allocator setting afterwards and compare fragmentation/peak memory.

findings.claim: The previous NIXL implementation cached prepared descriptor lists and xfer handles in `self._xfer_cache`, reusing them across repeated `execute()` calls. This commit removes the cache entirely: `_create_peer_xfer` now calls `get_xfer_descs`, `prep_xfer_dlist` (twice, for local and remote) and `make_prepped_xfer` on every `execute()`, and releases all handles in the `finally` block. EPLB rebalancing runs periodically and per-MoE-layer, so for stable/repeating rebalance patterns this reintroduces per-round descriptor-list and xfer-handle preparation/teardown overhead that the cache used to amortize. This is a latency cost paid on every rebalance step. Note the memory footprint is otherwise reduced by this commit (send buffer shrinks from world_size*partition to a single local-expert-sized buffer), so this is a targeted tradeoff, not a broad regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Repeatedly invoke NixlEplbCommunicator.execute() with a fixed old_indices/recv pattern (e.g. 100 iterations) and measure wall-clock time per call vs the parent commit, isolating time spent in get_xfer_descs/prep_xfer_dlist/make_prepped_xfer vs the actual READ transfer.

findings.claim: execute() now recomputes `expert_to_send_slot` (a list of dicts built by iterating `rank_experts` for all world_size*num_local_experts entries) on every call, and builds per-peer local_descs/remote_descs lists with a Python loop over expert_ids. This per-call CPU-side setup scales with world_size*num_local_experts and runs each rebalance step. For large expert counts this adds Python-loop overhead on the critical path before the collective barrier, though it is small relative to the actual transfer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile execute() CPU time (excluding the NIXL transfer/barrier) as a function of world_size and num_local_experts to confirm the per-call reshape/dict-comprehension cost is negligible relative to transfer time.

findings.claim: The new ROCm `triton_convert_req_index_to_global_index` defaults `BLOCK_N=128` while the kernel's per-token guard `if tile_id * BLOCK_N + seq_start >= seq_end: return` only skips whole 128-column tiles. With ragged sparse seq-lens (the common decode case where most tokens attend to far fewer than topk_tokens=2048 KVs), the kernel still launches num_tokens x (2048/128)=16 program instances per token and processes and masks columns instead of early-exiting per-element. This is a fixed O(num_tokens * topk_tokens) memory-traffic cost per forward on the critical attention path, regardless of actual valid indices, whereas the old bf16 path derived paged_kv_indptr from the actual (topk_indices != -1) count and produced a ragged output via fetch_id_to_ragged. If the ragged sparse seqlens are small, this over-processes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark triton_convert_req_index_to_global_index at various actual-sparse-seqlen/topk ratios (e.g. seqlen 128 vs topk 2048) measuring kernel time and DRAM traffic.

findings.claim: In build(), an extra Triton kernel `generate_sparse_seqlen_triton` plus a `torch.cumsum` and a scalar `.fill_(self.paged_kv_indptr[num_tokens])` (which reads a GPU scalar into the fill value, forcing a device->host sync) are now executed on every metadata build. The `self.paged_kv_indptr[num_tokens+1:].fill_(self.paged_kv_indptr[num_tokens])` indexes a 0-d GPU tensor as the fill value; depending on torch semantics this can trigger a synchronization/D2H copy per build, stalling the pipeline. Previously the cumsum/indptr work happened once in _forward_bf16_kv without a scalar host read.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile build() latency and check for cudaStreamSynchronize caused by paged_kv_indptr[num_tokens+1:].fill_(paged_kv_indptr[num_tokens]) using torch profiler / nsys.

findings.claim: build() now unconditionally does paged_kv_indices.fill_(0) and paged_kv_indptr.fill_(0) over full max_num_batched_tokens*topk_tokens buffers, then re-computes indptr via cumsum. paged_kv_indices is fully overwritten by triton_convert per token region but the zero-fill of the whole [max_num_batched_tokens * topk_tokens] indices buffer every step is a large memset (e.g. tens of millions of int32) on the critical path. Consider only clearing the padded tail.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure time of paged_kv_indices.fill_(0) for max_num_batched_tokens*2048 int32 elements vs restricting the memset to the padding region.

findings.claim: The new ROCm kernel writes results into paged_kv_indices via out_ptr + seq_start + indice_id with masking (ragged layout), replacing the old dense per-token output. Because it uses `tl.where(is_invalid_tok | ~valid_block, 0, ...)` (writing 0, not -1) and relies on the indptr from generate_sparse_seqlen to bound valid entries, any mismatch between generate_sparse_seqlen's computed sparse_seqlen and the actual number of valid topk indices could cause the mla_decode kernel to read stale/garbage indices, potentially doing extra KV gathers. Not a guaranteed slowdown but worth validating.

deepseek_v2.py Indexer.forward now calls current_platform.is_rocm() on every forward invocation to select the RoPE path. is_rocm() is typically cached/cheap, but placing a platform branch inside the per-token indexer forward adds a small constant overhead and prevents torch.compile from specializing cleanly; hoisting the branch to init time would avoid it.

On the cooperative radix path (max_seq_len > RADIX_THRESHOLD=32768 with occupancy>1) the change reserves a full 'headroom' of one CTA per SM (headroom = num_sms) and subtracts it from max_resident_ctas before computing num_groups. This deliberately throws away up to a full wave of resident CTAs, so num_groups = (num_sms*occupancy - num_sms)/ctas_per_group can drop by roughly num_sms/ctas_per_group groups compared to the old code. Fewer concurrent groups means fewer rows processed in parallel on the exact large-sequence path where the persistent kernel is used, reducing GPU occupancy and throughput. This is a correctness (anti-deadlock) tradeoff, but it measurably lowers achieved parallelism whenever ctas_per_group is small relative to num_sms.
findings.suggested_benchmark: persistent_topk with TopK=1024/2048, num_rows in (8..32], max_seq_len>32768, measuring kernel wall time before vs after the headroom subtraction (compare num_groups and total_ctas launched).

findings.claim: When needs_cooperative && total_ctas > hw_resident_cap the code now falls back to FilteredTopKRaggedTransform instead of launching the persistent radix kernel. FilteredTopK is a different algorithm chosen only for large num_rows earlier in the function; invoking it here for the small-num_rows / large-max_seq_len oversubscription case may be substantially slower per row than the persistent path it replaces. It avoids a hang, but in the corner case it triggers it can be a latency regression relative to a persistent launch that just fit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Construct inputs that trigger total_ctas > num_sms*occupancy (large stride forcing high ctas_per_group with occupancy==1) and >=128KB smem, compare FilteredTopK fallback latency vs a persistent launch.

findings.claim: The occupancy query is now specialized per vec_size (1/2/4). For vec_size==1 or 2 the corresponding kernel instantiation may report a different (often higher) occupancy than the previously-hardcoded vec=4 variant. If it reports higher occupancy, the combination of the corrected estimate plus the headroom subtraction still shifts num_groups relative to before; downstream this can either raise or lower launched CTA count, so throughput behavior for the vec=1/2 paths changes and should be re-validated.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare num_groups/total_ctas and kernel time for strides not divisible by 4 (vec_size==1/2) before vs after the change.

findings.claim: In `_triton_dequantize_nvfp4`, the tile-sizing heuristic for the dominant weight-dequant case (`total_rows_flat >= 4096`) sets `tile_blocks = next_power_of_2(num_blocks)` with `num_warps=1`. For typical LLM weights (e.g. hidden/intermediate of 4096-8192, block_size=16 → num_blocks=256-512), this makes each CTA process a `[num_blocks, BLOCK_SIZE]` tile (up to ~8K elements) with only 32 threads, forcing ~128+ serial elements per thread and low occupancy/ILP. A single-warp CTA that loads/decodes an entire row is likely to under-utilize the SM/CU and can spill registers on large num_blocks, throttling the on-the-fly weight dequant that runs every forward in the emulation MoE path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark _triton_dequantize_nvfp4 for weight shapes like [num_experts*2*intermediate, hidden//2] with k in {4096,8192} sweeping num_warps in {1,2,4,8} and tile_blocks, comparing against the reference PyTorch path.

findings.claim: Both new Triton kernels take `BLOCK_SIZE`, `TILE_BLOCKS` (and `has_batch_global_scale`) as `tl.constexpr`, and `tile_blocks` is derived from `next_power_of_2(num_blocks)` which varies per weight/activation shape. Each distinct combination triggers a separate Triton JIT compilation. On the activation quant-dequant path (`ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant`) input row/K sizes vary at runtime (prefill vs decode, different sequence lengths), so first-touch of each new shape incurs compilation latency spikes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end latency of first vs subsequent forwards across varying token counts (1, 8, 64, 512) to quantify JIT compile spikes on the activation QDQ path.

findings.claim: `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant` now dispatches to the Triton kernel unconditionally on CUDA-alike platforms and this runs on the per-forward activation path (via moe_kernel_quantize_input). The Triton launch allocates a fresh output tensor and derives grid dims from tensor shapes each call; if this path is captured into a CUDA graph, the launch config computation (next_power_of_2, python-side grid math) executes on capture only, but the added device-side E2M1 handle `.to(device)` was explicitly moved out to load time — confirming the maintainers were aware of graph-capture constraints. No hang identified, but the extra torch.empty per forward adds allocator pressure relative to the previous reference implementation which also allocated intermediates.

In request_finished, the completion-status gate was broadened from `request.status != FINISHED_LENGTH_CAPPED` to also accept FINISHED_STOPPED, and (with bidirectional enabled) D-node requests (is_d_node) now flow into the delay_free_blocks path. This means that on a NORMAL decode completion (FINISHED_STOPPED is the common terminal state for decode requests), the D node will now register the request in _reqs_need_send and hold its KV blocks alive for up to VLLM_NIXL_ABORT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT (default 480s) waiting for a future P node to pull them. If the next conversation turn never arrives (client abandons the chat, single-turn requests, or no matching pull), those blocks are pinned for the full timeout rather than being freed immediately. Under high request churn this can substantially inflate KV cache occupancy on the D node, reducing the number of concurrently schedulable sequences and lowering throughput. Previously only P-side max_tokens=1 prefill requests (FINISHED_LENGTH_CAPPED) incurred this hold.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run disaggregated P/D with bidirectional_kv_xfer enabled and a mix of single-turn + multi-turn requests; measure D-node free KV block count over time and max concurrent sequences vs. baseline (bidirectional disabled).

findings.claim: ConversationKVCache never proactively evicts stale entries except on a GET /health call. evict_stale() is only invoked from the health endpoint, and get() only removes the single conversation being consumed. For conversations that receive a first turn but never a follow-up turn, the CachedKVEntry stays in the in-memory dict until a health probe happens to run. In deployments without regular health polling, the proxy's _store grows unbounded (one dict entry per abandoned conversation), causing steady memory growth in the proxy process. A periodic background eviction task would bound this.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Drive the proxy with many single-turn conversation_ids and no /health calls; observe RSS growth of the proxy process over time.

findings.claim: The proxy serializes the prefill and decode phases per request: it awaits the full non-streaming prefill response (_send_to_prefill) before beginning the decode stream (_stream_from_decode / _stream_from_decode_sse). This is inherent to the P/D handoff, but the prefill request is issued with httpx client timeout=None, so a slow/stalled prefill node has no upper bound and will block the request (and hold the client connection) indefinitely rather than failing fast. This can manifest as effectively hung requests under prefill-node degradation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Inject a stalled prefiller and measure client-observed request latency / hang duration with timeout=None vs a bounded timeout.

findings.claim: sync_dp_state builds a fresh CPU int32 tensor and issues a Gloo/CPU all-reduce on every invocation (called from _has_global_unfinished_reqs every 32 steps). This is essentially the same cost as the previous has_unfinished_dp path (one all_reduce over a small tensor), so it is not a regression, but tensor[0].item() and tensor[1].item() force two host-device/sync reads. On CPU tensors .item() is cheap; the extra element does not materially change collective cost. Low performance impact overall.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark ParallelConfig.sync_dp_state vs has_unfinished_dp latency on a 2-8 rank Gloo group; confirm all-reduce latency is dominated by network round-trip, not tensor construction.

findings.claim: The new DPEngineCoreProc._pause_complete() always sets self.engines_running = True and pending_pause = True, forcing every DP rank to keep entering the stepping loop and executing dummy batches until all ranks reach pause consensus. In two-phase pause, if one rank is slow to receive the pause request, the other ranks burn GPU cycles running dummy batches (execute_dummy_batch / EP all-to-all) for potentially many iterations rather than idling. This trades correctness (deadlock avoidance) for extra GPU work during the pause window.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU utilization and number of dummy-batch executions during a staggered pause(keep) across DP ranks with varying inter-rank pause-request skew; compare against the single-phase pause baseline.

findings.claim: Pause consensus is only re-checked in _has_global_unfinished_reqs, which early-returns True when self.step_counter % 32 != 0. This means the all-reduce that detects pause consensus runs at most once per 32 steps, so after all ranks set pending_pause, the engines may continue running dummy batches for up to ~31 extra steps before consensus is observed and ignore_start_dp_wave is set. This adds latency and wasted GPU work to pause completion proportional to the 32-step cadence.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument the number of steps between the last rank setting pending_pause and pause_consensus being reached; confirm it can be up to the 32-step check interval.

findings.claim: resume_scheduler now performs an additional ParallelConfig.has_unfinished_dp all-reduce (a full DP-group collective barrier) on every resume to synchronize ranks before stepping. This is a new blocking collective on the resume path that did not exist before; resume latency now includes a DP-group round-trip. Impact is small (once per resume) but it is a new synchronization point.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resume_generation() latency before vs after with DP_SIZE=2..8; attribute the added time to the new has_unfinished_dp all-reduce.

findings.claim: The diff inserts a bare markdown-style backtick line (`max_num_batched_tokens` is also appended...) into what is a triple-quoted docstring in the source but the surrounding docstring context is not shown as reopened; if this text lands outside the docstring it is invalid Python and the module fails to import, which would prevent the engine from starting at all. Assuming it is inside the docstring, there is no runtime impact from this line, but it must be verified to be inside the docstring.

Appending max_num_tokens as an extra cudagraph capture size (when it falls off the 8/16 stride) increases the number of CUDA graphs captured by one. Each additional captured graph increases startup time (graph capture is expensive) and consumes additional GPU memory for the captured graph's workspace/buffers. Impact is small (one extra size), but it is a real one-time startup-latency and steady-state memory cost per engine, incurred whenever max_num_batched_tokens is off-stride and <= max_cudagraph_capture_size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine init/warmup time and captured-graph GPU memory with max_num_batched_tokens set to an off-stride value (e.g. 257) vs an on-stride value, comparing before/after this commit.

findings.claim: Pre-fix, the MLA broadcast notification to non-read prefill (P) ranks was keyed by the local decode request id (`req_id`) instead of the prefill-side request id (`meta.remote.request_id`). Prefill ranks track `_reqs_to_send` by their own request id, so a notification keyed by the decode id is unrecognized in `_get_new_notifs` ('Potentially invalid KV blocks for unrecognized request'), meaning the skipped P-ranks never release their KV blocks promptly. The blocks only free via the abort/expiry timeout, which wastes KV cache memory (blocks held far longer than necessary) and can lead to timeouts/stalls under P_tp > D_tp + MLA. This fix (using `meta.remote.request_id`) is a performance/correctness fix, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Disaggregated P/D setup with MLA model, P_tp=4, D_tp=1: measure KV block hold time and free-block availability on prefill ranks under sustained request load, comparing before/after the fix.

findings.claim: This commit flips VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_SAMPLER from default-off (None -> PyTorch-native path) to default-on (True -> forward_cuda / flashinfer_sample) for every supported CUDA GPU. flashinfer_sample introduces a per-step CPU-GPU synchronization. The code's own removed docstring explicitly said so: 'This function includes CPU-GPU synchronization, while random_sample does not. Call this function at the end of the forward pass to minimize the synchronization overhead.' The sampler runs on every decode step, and v1 relies on the sampling path being sync-free so that async scheduling / async output copy (async_output_copy_stream, prev_sampled_token_ids) can overlap the next forward with the previous step's device->host copy. Making the default path synchronize each step serializes the CPU on the GPU every decode iteration, reducing GPU utilization and decode throughput for all whole-vocab sampling workloads that were previously on the sync-free native path. Whether FlashInfer's rejection sampling is a net win depends heavily on batch size and vocab; forcing it on by default regresses latency for small-batch / latency-sensitive deployments where the sync stall dominates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput and inter-token latency (async scheduling enabled) with temperature>0 + top_p/top_k, comparing VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_SAMPLER=0 (native) vs =1 (flashinfer) across batch sizes 1, 8, 64, 256 on a supported GPU.

findings.claim: The forward_cuda comment explaining the deliberate preference of random_sample over flashinfer_sample 'because random_sample does not require CPU-GPU synchronization while flashinfer_sample does' was replaced, and the corresponding warning docstring on flashinfer_sample was deleted. The synchronization behavior itself is unchanged, but making the sync path the default removes the previous guard against the sync cost. Callers unaware of the sync may now place it in a position that stalls the pipeline. The perf-critical property (avoid per-step sync) that motivated the original design is silently dropped from the code's documentation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Nsight/torch profiler trace of a single decode step showing device sync/stall duration in the sampler with flashinfer default-on vs native.

findings.claim: With the sampler now default-on, TopKTopPSampler.__init__ unconditionally imports vllm.v1.attention.backends.flashinfer and probes device capability on any supported CUDA GPU (previously gated behind explicit opt-in). This adds a FlashInfer import at sampler construction for all default CUDA runs; minor one-time startup cost but broadens the set of runs that pay it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-start engine init time on CUDA with FlashInfer installed, default env vs VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_SAMPLER=0.

findings.claim: The forward path (lines 850-920) builds IntermediateTensors carrying the full-length _ds_buffers and, before this fix, injected the entire buffer (self._ds_buffers[lvl]) whose leading dim is the max CUDA-graph batch size rather than the actual token count n. Slicing to [:n] (the fix) is what restores correct behavior; without the slice the deepstack add in Granite4VisionLLMModel.forward triggers the buf_len != num_tokens branch every decode step, invoking torch.nn.functional.pad — an extra allocation + copy kernel per deepstack layer per decode step. In compiled/CUDA-graph decode mode this pad/copy on the padded max batch size is exactly the reported decode slowdown; the [:n] slice avoids it. This diff is the fix, so it improves performance rather than degrading it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput/latency for Granite4Vision in compiled mode with deepstack enabled, comparing full-buffer vs [:n]-sliced ds tensors.

findings.claim: The new is_reasoning_end scans the entire input_ids sequence backward on every call. Since the base is_reasoning_end_streaming forwards the full input_ids, and this method is invoked once per decode step during structured-output constrained generation (see StructuredOutputManager.should_advance / should_fill_bitmask), the scan is O(N) per step and O(N^2) cumulative over the reasoning phase (no match is found until the end marker appears, so the full sequence is walked each step). This is NOT a regression — the previous code called self.model_tokenizer.decode(input_ids) which was also O(N) per step plus a far larger constant (Python-level detokenization + substring search), so this change is a net performance improvement. The remaining scan could be bounded by only inspecting the recent tail of the sequence (e.g. the last rest_len+1 tokens, or delta_ids) as gptoss/step3p5 parsers do, to make it O(1) per step. Also, `list(input_ids[i+1:i+1+rest_len])` allocates a small list per iteration; comparing without materializing (or precomputing a tuple) would trim minor overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark is_reasoning_end on token sequences of length 1k/8k/32k that are still in reasoning (no </think>), comparing the old decode-based implementation vs. the new integer scan, and also vs. a tail-only variant, to confirm the improvement magnitude and the residual O(N)-per-step cost.

findings.claim: Before this change, each DeepSeekV4MultiTokenPredictorLayer created its own single aux CUDA stream (one Stream per MTP layer, one AuxStreamType.Attention). After the change, DeepSeekV4MultiTokenPredictor allocates a shared list of 3 CUDA streams once and passes it to all MTP layers, mirroring DeepseekV4Model. Net effect on resource usage is a small increase in the number of allocated CUDA streams (3 shared vs 1 per layer) unless num_mtp_layers>3. CUDA streams are cheap handles, so runtime throughput/latency impact is negligible; the change is primarily an API alignment. No measurable degradation is expected. Sharing the streams across all MTP layers is actually beneficial (fewer distinct streams than per-layer allocation when there are >3 layers, and consistent overlap behavior with the main model).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek-V4 speculative decoding (MTP draft) end-to-end and compare draft-model per-step latency and GPU stream count/utilization before and after this commit; verify overlap of attention aux-stream work is unchanged.

findings.claim: The MTP layer now forwards aux_stream_list to DeepseekV4DecoderLayer, which asserts len(self.aux_stream_list) >= 3 (deepseek_v4_attention.py:340). The predictor supplies exactly 3 streams, satisfying the assertion. If aux_stream_list were ever None (default), the decoder layer's assert would fire and abort the run rather than degrade performance; but the predictor always passes 3 streams, so this path is safe. No performance regression identified, only a hard dependency on the >=3 stream contract.

In execute_in_parallel, a single shared start_event is recorded once on the current stream and then wait()-ed on inside each aux stream's context. Because start_event.record() is issued before default_fn() launches, the aux streams correctly only wait for work enqueued before the fan-out. However, all launched aux streams share the SAME start_event object, and CUDA events are single-slot: repeated wait() on the same event from multiple streams is fine, but re-recording the same event object each layer (start_event == self.ln_events[0]) while ln_events[1] is also reused as both a GEMM-done event and later as the post-GEMM overlap event (per the code comment '[1] doubles as post-GEMM event1') creates event aliasing across the two overlap phases in the same forward. If the second maybe_execute_in_parallel phase records ln_events[0]/ln_events[1] before the first phase's recorded values have been waited on, the earlier synchronization guarantee is overwritten, risking a lost dependency (read-after-write on kv_score/qr_kv) rather than a hang, but more commonly forcing extra serialization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 decode throughput (tokens/s) with indexer layers, compare against parent; also correctness check on logits determinism across many steps

findings.claim: The refactor hoists three previously-fused input GEMMs (compressor kv_score, indexer.weights_proj, indexer.compressor kv_score) out of the compressor/indexer forwards and runs them in a separate execute_in_parallel phase BEFORE fused_q_kv_rmsnorm and the wq_b/kv_insert overlap phase. This introduces an additional global join point (the 'for ev in pending: ev.wait()' loop) between the pre-attn GEMM phase and the RMSNorm phase. Previously these GEMMs overlapped with kv_insert on the aux stream in a single fused overlap region; now there are two sequential overlap regions with a synchronization barrier between them. If the aux-stream GEMMs finish after fused_wqa_wkv on default, the default stream stalls at the join, potentially reducing the overlap benefit for small-batch decode where GEMMs are launch-bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 decode latency at batch size 1-8 (launch-bound regime), compare per-layer attention time vs parent

findings.claim: aux_stream count increased from 1 to 3 per DeepseekV4Model (torch.cuda.Stream() for _ in range(3)). Additional CUDA streams have negligible memory but each concurrent aux stream can hold its own memory-pool allocations for the GEMM outputs (kv_score, indexer_weights, indexer_kv_score) live simultaneously across the join, increasing transient activation memory footprint versus the previous single-stream sequential path where these intermediates were shorter-lived inside the fused forwards.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak GPU memory during DSV4 prefill at max_num_batched_tokens, compare vs parent

findings.claim: execute_in_parallel enters a torch.cuda.stream(aux_streams[i]) context and issues start_event.wait() + done_events[i].record() per non-None aux fn, for up to 3 aux fns per attention layer per forward step. In decode (many layers, tiny per-token work) this per-slot Python-level stream switching and event bookkeeping adds CPU launch overhead on the hot path that scales with number of aux slots (was effectively 1 in the parent). For launch-bound decode this CPU overhead can offset the intended overlap gains.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU-side per-step launch time (nsys/py-spy) for DSV4 decode, batch 1, compare vs parent

findings.claim: CudaGraphManager.capture now builds and returns a dict[BatchExecutionDescriptor, CapturedAttentionState] holding references to every captured descriptor's attn_metadata and per-layer slot-mapping tensors. This dict (captured_attn_states) is passed to and retained by the Eagle speculator's prefill capture path, keeping those attention-metadata/slot-mapping tensors alive for the process lifetime. This is a small, bounded, one-time memory retention (proportional to the number of capture sizes), not a hot-path cost, but it is new persistent state that did not exist before. Verify these tensors are the same buffers already retained by the model manager (aliases) rather than extra copies.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident GPU/CPU memory after cudagraph capture with Eagle spec decode enabled, comparing parent vs this commit across a range of cudagraph_capture_sizes.

findings.claim: The default rejection sampling method changes from 'strict' to 'standard' (probabilistic). Previously the default speculative-decoding verification path used strict_rejection_sample, a single lightweight Triton kernel that only compared draft tokens against the target argmax. The new default routes every request through probabilistic_rejection_sample, which launches four Triton kernels per step (_compute_block_stats_kernel over the full vocab computing per-block max/sumexp, _probabilistic_rejection_kernel, _resample_kernel, _insert_resampled_kernel) plus an apply_sampling_params pass. Even in the new 'greedy' draft_sample_method (HAS_DRAFT_LOGITS=False) these kernels still run and compute full-vocab block statistics.
