This measurably increases per-verification-step GPU work for the default config compared to the prior strict default, trading throughput for a higher acceptance rate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Speculative decoding throughput (tokens/s) and per-step verify latency comparing rejection_sample_method='strict' (old default) vs 'standard'+draft_sample_method='greedy' (new default) at temperature=0, on a model with large vocab (e.g. 128k+).

findings.claim: When draft_logits is None (the new greedy default), probabilistic_rejection_sample allocates a fresh dummy tensor via target_logits.new_empty(1, 1, 1) on every call. This is a small GPU allocation on the per-step hot path that could instead be a preallocated shared buffer, adding minor allocator overhead each verification step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocator/CUDA-call overhead per verify step in the greedy standard path; compare with a preallocated dummy buffer.

findings.claim: The new FP8 ViT attention path adds 3 extra Triton quantization kernel launches (Q/K/V) plus head_dim un-padding on every ViT attention forward. For small/medium images this overhead outweighs the FP8 tensor-core speedup: the commit's own docs report HD (720x1280) end-to-end at 0.87x (i.e. ~15% SLOWER than BF16) and FullHD roughly at parity. Users enabling --mm-encoder-attn-dtype fp8 on non-QHD/4K workloads will see degraded ViT encoder throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_vit_fp8_attn.py at --seq-lens 2304 4096 (small images) comparing BF16 vs FP8 end-to-end ViT forward

findings.claim: Default FP8 mode (no scale file) uses dynamic scaling driven by a 16-entry amax history buffer, which per the docs 'adds a small per-forward overhead' and, more importantly, the docs state 'Dynamic scaling is not compatible with ViT full CUDA graphs.' Disabling ViT CUDA graph capture removes the graph-replay launch-overhead reduction, increasing CPU launch overhead and latency for the entire ViT encoder, not just the attention op. This is the default behavior when a user simply passes --mm-encoder-attn-dtype fp8.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end ViT encoder latency with dynamic FP8 (CUDA graphs disabled) vs static FP8 (CUDA graphs enabled) on Qwen3-VL

findings.claim: The FP8 quant kernel pads head_dim up to a multiple of 16 (e.g. 72 -> 80) and always writes a fresh contiguous (S,H,padded_D) output tensor for Q, K, and V. This increases both memory traffic and peak memory allocation relative to the BF16 path which consumes the QKV views directly, and adds an un-padding step on the output. For head_dim not near a multiple of 16 the padding waste (e.g. 80/72 ≈ 11%) is pure overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure allocated memory and kernel time of quantize_fp8_pad_head_dim_triton vs BF16 no-op at head_dim=72, various seq_lens

findings.claim: MultiModalConfig validation now performs synchronous filesystem stat calls (Path.is_file / Path.parent.is_dir) during config construction for the FP8 scale paths. This is negligible per engine but runs on every config init; only relevant if config objects are re-created frequently. Minor.

The continuation-prefill dequant buffers (k_buf, v_buf) are now taken from the shared WorkspaceManager via get_simultaneous(). Every layer's forward call re-fetches the same underlying workspace segment, so the buffers are only valid until the next get_simultaneous() call. In the continuation path k_cached/v_cached are read into k_full/v_full via matmul (k_flat @ Pi_half) and slice copies. Because the shared workspace is reused across layers and even within the same forward (the decode path also calls get_simultaneous), any interleaving that issues another get_simultaneous before the dequant results are consumed will silently overwrite k_buf/v_buf. This turns the previously per-layer persistent buffer into a hazard that, to be safe, forces serialization and additional copies (k_full/v_full pre-allocated and copied every call). Compared to the old cached per-layer buffer this adds two full seq_len×Hk×D allocations + copies every continuation prefill, offsetting the memory savings with extra bandwidth in the hot prefill path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Long-context chunked-prefill throughput with turboquant KV cache: measure prefill tokens/s and peak memory at 8K-32K context vs parent.

findings.claim: The continuation-prefill path now always allocates two fresh k_full/v_full tensors of shape (seq_len, Hk, D) every call (torch.empty + 4 slice-copies), whereas the shared workspace only backs k_buf/v_buf (the dequant scratch). At long context seq_len is large, so this replaces the previous torch.cat (2 allocations) with torch.empty (2 allocations) + 4 elementwise copies — the .to(qdtype) copy of cached K/V plus the current-chunk copy. The k_full[:cached_len] = k_cached_trim.to(qdtype) still materializes a dtype-converted copy, so the claimed 'no cat alloc' saving is marginal and net extra copy traffic is introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Continuation-prefill microbenchmark: q_len>128, cached_len 4K/16K/32K; compare kernel+copy wall time vs parent.

findings.claim: Dropping .zero_() on the dequant buffers relies on the kernel writing every position in [0, alloc_len). alloc_len is rounded up to a block multiple (math.ceil(cached_len/block_size)*block_size) so positions in [cached_len, alloc_len) are never written but also never read ([:cached_len] only). If a future/edge case reads the padded tail, uninitialized workspace memory (reused across layers) will be consumed. Not a direct slowdown, but removes a safety guarantee that made the buffer reuse correct; if it forces a fallback path it could regress.

The _arange_cache is keyed only on shape > max_seq: `if _ac is None or _ac.shape[0] <= _max_seq`. When max_seq_len grows across steps the arange is rebuilt (one kernel launch), which is fine, but it never shrinks and is allocated per-impl-instance (per layer). With many layers this holds N_layers arange tensors of size max_seq+1 int, minor memory. Also the slice synth_seq_lens = _arange_cache[cached_len+1:seq_len+1] returns a view whose values equal position+1; correctness depends on dtype matching seq_lens.dtype, which it does. Minor.

Moving centroid init out of Attention.__init__ (removed _init_turboquant_buffers) into TurboQuantAttentionImpl._ensure_on_device means get_centroids() + sort() now runs lazily on the first forward for each layer rather than at model build time. This adds a small one-time host/device cost (and a get_centroids call plus .to(device)) to the first inference step per layer; it does not repeat, so only first-request latency is affected. The removed comment warned this pre-allocation prevented the memory profiler from over-allocating KV blocks and OOMing on first decode — relying on the workspace manager instead is the intended fix, but if the workspace is not sized during profiling the first decode could still hit a large lazy allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-token latency (TTFT) and peak memory during profiling run with turboquant cache vs parent.

findings.claim: OUTPUT_FP16 constexpr in _fwd_kernel_stage2 and output_buf in query dtype eliminate the trailing output.to(query.dtype) copy after stage2. This is a genuine improvement (removes one full B*Hq*D copy kernel per decode) but adds a branch/cast inside the reduction kernel. Verify that stage2 is recompiled for OUTPUT_FP16=0 vs 1 (two triton specializations) — the extra constexpr doubles the number of cached compiled variants of this shared kernel, adding a one-time compile the first time each variant is hit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode step latency turboquant fp16 vs bf16; confirm removal of float16_copy in profiler trace.

findings.claim: The block that called `self.gate.set_out_dtype(torch.float32 if ... DeepSeekV3 else torch.bfloat16)` was removed, so `GateLinear.out_dtype` now stays `None` and `set_out_dtype()` is never invoked. In `GateLinear`, the cuBLAS bf16xbf16->fp32 router-GEMM fast path (Tier 2, `allow_cublas_router_gemm`) is only enabled when `out_dtype == torch.float32` (either at init or via `set_out_dtype`). With `out_dtype=None`, `allow_cublas_router_gemm` remains False for the DeepSeekV3 monolithic path, so router GEMMs for batches > 16 tokens (prefill / large decode) fall back from the specialized cuBLAS kernel to the generic Tier-3 `F.linear` path, degrading router-GEMM throughput on Hopper/Blackwell. The DSV3 specialized kernel (Tier 1) is also now invoked with `output_dtype=None` instead of an explicit fp32 dtype, changing/degrading its behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepSeekV3 (fp8 monolithic TRTLLM MoE) router gate GEMM latency on Hopper/Blackwell across batch sizes (e.g. 1, 16, 64, 512, 2048) comparing out_dtype=fp32 (old) vs out_dtype=None (new); measure MoE-layer forward time end-to-end.

findings.claim: The early-return guard `if fi_utils._is_fi_autotuning: return` was removed from the modular fp8 (and nvfp4) apply paths. The comment stated `trtllm_fp8_block_scale_routed_moe does not support autotuning`, so the kernel is now actually invoked during the FlashInfer autotuning warmup pass. If this kernel does not participate meaningfully in autotuning, this adds extra kernel launches during warmup; if it errors or hangs under autotuning, warmup could break. This mainly affects startup/warmup time, not steady-state.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure kernel_warmup / FlashInfer autotuning phase duration for a fp8 block-scale TRTLLM MoE model before vs after removing the _is_fi_autotuning skip.

findings.claim: This is a positive perf change (noted for completeness): the modular fp8 path previously called the kernel returning `result` and then did `output.copy_(result)` (an extra full-size device-to-device copy per MoE call). It now passes `output=output` so the kernel writes in place, eliminating one output-sized copy per forward. No regression here.

The change disables PyTorch's expandable_segments feature for the entire duration of the memory pool context. In gpu_worker.py the memory pool context (use_memory_pool) wraps `load_model` (tag='weights') and KV cache allocation (tag='kv_cache'). Allocations made while expandable_segments is off use the classic non-expandable segment allocator, which is more prone to fragmentation and can waste GPU memory / cause more cudaMalloc/cudaFree churn. Since the whole model weight loading happens inside this context with expandable_segments forced off, users who intentionally enabled expandable_segments (typically to reduce fragmentation and OOMs) lose that benefit precisely during the largest allocations of the run. This can measurably increase peak GPU memory usage and fragmentation for sleep-mode users.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable sleep mode with PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True, load a large model, and compare peak GPU memory / fragmentation (torch.cuda.memory_stats reserved vs allocated) against a baseline.

findings.claim: torch.cuda.memory._set_allocator_settings changes a *process-global* allocator setting rather than a scoped one. Toggling expandable_segments off then back on around every use_memory_pool invocation mutates global allocator state that also governs all other (non-pool) allocations on the device. Changing allocator settings mid-run can force the allocator to reconfigure segment behavior and may interact poorly with already-cached blocks, potentially triggering extra segment splitting/merging work. It relies on a private (underscore-prefixed) API whose behavior/cost is not guaranteed to be cheap or side-effect-free.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure time and allocator state (memory_stats) before/after repeated use_memory_pool enter/exit cycles with expandable_segments enabled to detect reserved-memory growth or reconfiguration overhead.

findings.claim: expandable_segments is only re-enabled if expandable_was_enabled was captured as True at context entry. If the setting were changed concurrently, or if _set_allocator_settings('expandable_segments:False') silently no-ops on a given PyTorch/ROCm build, the code could leave expandable_segments disabled after exit, degrading fragmentation behavior for the remainder of the run. Low probability but worth noting.

In `render_chat`/`render_completion` (vllm/entrypoints/serve/render/serving.py), `skip_mm_cache` now defaults to `False` and is only forced `True` for the standalone renderer-only paths (render_chat_request / render_completion_request). Previously these core methods always passed `skip_mm_cache=True`. For the OpenAI chat/completion serving paths that delegate to `render_chat`/`render_completion` (via OpenAIServingChat/OpenAIServingCompletion), the call now uses `skip_mm_cache=False`, which routes multimodal processing through the sender `mm_processor` cache (per BaseRenderer._process_singleton at line 659 selecting `_readonly_mm_processor` only when skip_mm_cache is True). If this was intentional (keep-cache for engine execution) it is correct; but if any delegating caller relied on the previous unconditional skip, multimodal items that should have been processed with the read-only processor will instead populate/evict the shared sender cache, increasing cache pressure and potentially causing redundant reprocessing (cache thrash) under mixed tokenize/generate workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a mixed workload of tokenize-only and generate requests with multimodal inputs and measure mm processor cache hit rate and per-request preprocessing latency before/after the change.

findings.claim: The renderer warmup now runs an *additional* full multimodal processor `apply()` for the readonly (`_readonly_mm_processor`) processor when it exists. `processor.apply()` on dummy inputs sized to `max_model_len` is the expensive HF preprocessing path (image/video/audio processing). This roughly doubles multimodal warmup wall-clock time at renderer startup for multimodal models that have a processor-only cache configured. This is a one-time startup cost, not per-request, but it does increase time-to-ready.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure renderer startup / time-to-first-ready for a multimodal model with processor_only_cache enabled, comparing warmup duration before and after this commit.

findings.claim: The readonly warmup populates the readonly processor's own cache during `apply()`, then clears it via `_clear_processor_cache`. This mirrors the existing sender-processor behavior (which clears via clear_mm_cache), so peak memory during warmup transiently holds dummy processed multimodal features for the readonly processor in addition to the sender processor. Transient extra memory at startup only; cleared afterwards.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track peak RSS during renderer warmup for a large-image multimodal model with a processor-only cache enabled.

findings.claim: The replica-selection logic in the per-element EPLB mapping Triton kernel changed from a single 32-bit modulo (`offs % replica_count`) to a heavier sequence: a runtime integer division `offs // num_active_experts` (num_active_experts is a runtime scalar, not a compile-time constexpr, so Triton cannot strength-reduce it to shifts/multiplies), a cast to int64, a 64-bit multiply by KNUTH_MULTIPLIER (2654435769 > INT32_MAX forces 64-bit emulated arithmetic, several 32-bit ops on GPU), an AND, and finally a modulo. This adds one true integer division plus 64-bit multiply per top-k element on every EPLB-enabled MoE forward. However this kernel is strongly memory-bound (3-4 global loads + store + atomic_add per element), so the extra register arithmetic will almost entirely overlap with memory latency; a measurable slowdown is unlikely. Note the change's net effect on distributed throughput is positive: it fixes replica collapse onto a single physical expert when top_k is a multiple of R, improving GPU load balance across replicas.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _eplb_map_and_record_i32_kernel on realistic shapes (num_tokens=8192, top_k in {2,4,8}) comparing pre/post-change kernel wall time; separately measure end-to-end distributed MoE throughput/GPU-utilization balance with a hot expert to confirm the intended load-balance improvement.

findings.claim: EngineCore.shutdown() now calls gc.unfreeze(), which globally reverses the gc.freeze() performed at engine startup. If the engine's process is not immediately torn down after shutdown (e.g. in-process reuse, or a host process that runs other work / other engines after one engine shuts down), the previously-frozen static startup heap becomes tracked by GC again, and subsequent oldest-generation collections will scan it, increasing GC pause times. In the normal deployment case (shutdown == process exit) this is harmless, so the impact is limited to in-process/multi-engine scenarios.
findings.suggested_benchmark: In a single process, start EngineCore, call shutdown(), then measure gc.collect() pause time vs. a run without the unfreeze while a second engine or workload is active.

findings.claim: supports_kw now wraps the lru_cached _supports_kw with an extra Python function call plus a hasattr(callable, '__func__') check on every call and unwraps bound methods to their underlying function. This is strictly more work per call than before, but supports_kw is only invoked during model/connector registration and setup (interfaces_base, interfaces, kv_connector factory, tokenizer template rendering), not on any per-token or per-request hot path, so the added overhead is negligible at runtime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark supports_kw() call latency before/after; confirm it is only reached at engine init, not per step.

findings.claim: This change is a performance IMPROVEMENT for MiMoV2 attention when V has a smaller head dim than K/Q. Previously (mimo_v2_flash.py forward) V was materialized and padded from v_head_dim up to head_dim before attention (torch.nn.functional.pad + two .view() calls per forward), and the attention output was sliced/reshaped back to v_head_dim afterward. That padding meant the KV cache stored head_size + head_size (padded V) per token, wasting KV-cache memory proportional to (head_dim - v_head_dim). The new DiffKV backend stores head_size + head_size_v (SlidingWindowSpec.real_page_size_bytes now uses head_size + head_size_v instead of 2*head_size), reducing per-token KV cache footprint and removing the per-forward pad/view/slice ops on the value tensor and output. Net effect: lower memory usage (more KV blocks / longer context) and fewer eager-mode PyTorch ops in the decode hot path. No performance regression identified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure KV cache blocks allocated and decode throughput for MiMoV2 with v_head_dim != head_dim before/after this commit; also compare attention forward CPU overhead (the removed pad/view/slice).

findings.claim: FlashAttentionDiffKVImpl.__init__ now re-derives vllm_flash_attn_version via get_flash_attn_version(...) at layer construction, reading FlashAttentionDiffKVBackend.head_size_v (a class-level mutable set via set_head_size_v). Since head_size_v is a class attribute, all layers share the last-set value; if a model instantiated layers with differing v_head_dim, the FA3->FA4 upgrade decision could be based on the wrong head_size_v. This is a correctness/backend-selection risk that could pick a slower/faster kernel than intended, but for MiMoV2 all diffkv layers appear to use the same v_head_dim so impact is likely nil.

The prefill path now routes every flash_attn_varlen_func call through a new Python wrapper `_flash_attn_varlen`, adding one extra Python-level function call plus a `self.fa_version is None` branch per invocation. In the per-request continuation-prefill loop this executes once per request per layer. The overhead is tiny relative to the attention kernel itself, so real-world impact is negligible; noted only for completeness. `fa_version` itself is correctly computed once in __init__ and cached, so there is no repeated version-detection cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark prefill throughput for a TurboQuant model with many short requests (per-request loop path) before/after this commit; expect no measurable difference.

findings.claim: The new `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` inside `_ensure_workspace_size` releases ALL cached (reserved-but-unused) segments from PyTorch's CUDA caching allocator back to the driver, not just the freed workspace segment. This forces subsequent allocations (including the very `torch.empty` allocation two lines below, plus all activation/KV tensors after it) to go back to the driver via cudaMalloc, which is a device-synchronizing, high-latency operation (often milliseconds). This runs every time a workspace needs to grow. If workspace growth happens outside a one-time warmup (e.g. during elastic-EP unlock/resize, DBO ubatch resizes, or any first-touch of a larger shape during serving), it will stall the GPU, tank throughput, and defeat the caching allocator's purpose by fragmenting/re-requesting the whole pool.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trigger repeated workspace growth (e.g. sequential get_simultaneous calls with increasing required_bytes, or elastic-EP scale up/down) and measure wall-clock per resize plus end-to-end decode throughput with/without the empty_cache() call. Also measure cudaMalloc count via CUDA profiler.

findings.claim: empty_cache() returns all reserved memory to the driver, but this can actually INCREASE peak fragmentation/allocation cost for co-existing pools (e.g. the CuMemAllocator sleep/wake pool used by cudagraphs and the KV cache pool). Repeatedly emptying and re-growing the cache causes the allocator to re-map segments, and with expandable_segments or a custom mempool active it may error or cause extra reserved memory rather than reduce it (see cumem.py comment: 'calling torch.cuda.empty_cache() will error'). The claimed benefit (reclaiming a single dead segment) does not require flushing the entire cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare torch.cuda.memory_reserved()/max_memory_reserved() across a resize sequence with and without empty_cache(); test interaction with cudagraph capture and cumem sleep/wake.

findings.claim: empty_cache() implicitly synchronizes the device (it must wait for outstanding work referencing freed blocks). If any workspace resize can occur while the workspace is unlocked during normal execution, this introduces a host/device sync into what is otherwise an async execution path, hurting GPU utilization and pipeline overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile with nsys to check for an inserted device synchronize at the empty_cache call during a resize under load.

findings.claim: This commit is a performance improvement, not a regression. Previously Qwen3MoeSparseMoeBlock.forward unconditionally computed `router_logits, _ = self.gate(hidden_states)` and passed the result into `self.experts(...)`. But because `self.gate` is also passed into the FusedMoE constructor (`gate=self.gate`), FusedMoE.is_internal_router is True and the FusedMoE runs the gate/router internally as well. That meant the gate matmul (hidden_size x num_experts GEMM) was executed twice per MoE layer per forward pass on every token batch. The fix routes to the internal-router branch (passing router_logits=hidden_states so the gate runs once inside FusedMoE), matching the existing deepseek_v2/qwen3_next pattern, and eliminates the duplicate gate GEMM. No new slowdown or hang is introduced; the change removes redundant work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/latency of Qwen3-MoE decode+prefill (tokens/s) before vs after; per-layer profiling of the gate ReplicatedLinear GEMM count should drop from 2 to 1 per MoE layer.

findings.claim: get_encoder_cudagraph_budget_range now caps max_budget at min(max_num_batched_tokens, max_model_len). When max_model_len < max_num_batched_tokens (a common config, e.g. a model with a short context served on a scheduler configured for large batched-token budgets), the largest auto-generated encoder CUDA-graph token budget shrinks. In EncoderCudaGraphManager._execute_local the greedy packer uses max_budget = self.token_budgets[-1]; any single vision item whose output-token count exceeds this smaller max_budget now yields token_budget=None and falls back to the slow eager path (encoder_eager_forward) instead of a captured graph. Large images/videos that were previously graph-eligible will therefore run in eager mode, degrading encoder throughput and GPU utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run qwen3_vl with --enable-vit-cuda-graph on a config where max_model_len < max_num_batched_tokens, feed high-resolution images/long videos, and compare vision-encoder latency / graph_hits vs graph_misses against the parent commit.

findings.claim: max_batch_size is auto-derived as max_budget // min_budget in EncoderCudaGraphManager. Reducing max_budget (via the new min() cap) proportionally reduces max_batch_size, so fewer vision items are packed per graph replay. This produces more, smaller encoder batches and more graph replays for the same workload, reducing GPU utilization and increasing per-request encoder overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare number of encoder batches/replays and end-to-end vision-encoder time with many small images per request, before vs after this commit, when max_model_len < max_num_batched_tokens.

findings.claim: maybe_add_vit_cuda_graph_compilation_config sets encoder_cudagraph_max_vision_items_per_batch to 1 (image/video) or 2 (image+video). Combined with cudagraph_mm_encoder=True, this caps the encoder graph batch size to 1-2 items, which for multi-image requests forces one graph replay per image instead of batching, reducing encoder throughput compared to the auto-inferred (larger) batch size. This is example code so real impact depends on user copying the pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark the example with several images per prompt using vision_items_per_batch=1 vs the auto-inferred default and compare encoder throughput.

findings.claim: Flipping VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS to True by default causes cudagraph_memory_estimate_applied to be subtracted from available_kv_cache_memory_bytes in determine_available_memory() (vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py). This reduces the KV cache size for a given gpu_memory_utilization. The gpu_memory_utilization default bump (0.9->0.92, a 2% offset) only partially compensates: the CUDA graph memory estimate for many models exceeds 2% of total GPU memory, so the net effective KV cache shrinks. A smaller KV cache means fewer allocatable blocks -> lower max concurrent sequences / smaller effective batch -> reduced steady-state throughput under load and more preemption/recomputation. This is a deliberate OOM-safety tradeoff but does degrade throughput for workloads that previously ran comfortably at 0.9 without cudagraph accounting.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure max concurrent sequences / KV cache blocks and sustained throughput (tokens/s) at high request concurrency before vs after this commit for a mid-size model (e.g. Llama-3-8B) with default settings, confirming the reduction in available KV cache memory and its effect on batch size.

findings.claim: Raising the default gpu_memory_utilization from 0.9 to 0.92 increases requested_memory (request_memory() = total_memory * util). On GPUs shared with other processes or with tight headroom, this raises the chance of hitting the ValueError in request_memory() or of allocation failure / driver-level OOM at startup, which is a hard failure rather than a slowdown. The higher target also leaves less fragmentation headroom which can occasionally trigger reallocation stalls.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a GPU with modest free-memory headroom, verify startup success and peak allocated memory at util=0.92 vs 0.9 to quantify OOM margin.

findings.claim: The liveness `del` emission now has an added guard `i < len(nodes) - 2`, so `del` statements for tensors whose last consumer is the penultimate node in the stitching graph are suppressed. Those intermediate tensors stay alive until the execution function returns instead of being freed early. In practice this holds at most a few tensors for one extra step right before the return, so the memory impact is negligible, but it slightly widens the peak-live window at the tail of the generated function versus the prior behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak activation memory of the piecewise stitching execution_fn for a large graph before/after the `del` guard change; expect no measurable difference, but confirm no tensor that should be freed mid-graph is now retained.

findings.claim: The new group-FP8 registration loop iterates over quant_key(2) x col_major(2) x is_e8m0(2) x tma_aligned(2) = 16 flag combinations, and for each it registers MLAAttnFp8GroupQuantPattern for every MLA layer (when _USE_LAYERNAME is False). For a model like DeepSeek-V3 (~61 MLA layers), this registers up to 16*61 ~= 976 additional pattern/replacement pairs. Each self.register() call goes through pm.register_replacement -> fwd_only tracing (plus _fx_view_to_reshape / _remove_noop_permutes) under enable_fake_mode, which is expensive, and each registered pattern must then be tested against the graph during every fusion pass run. This substantially increases torch.compile / warmup time and pattern-matching cost. Existing FP8-static and NVFP4 MLA registrations used a single unconditional pattern per layer, so this is a large multiplicative increase.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile warmup / graph-compilation time for a block-FP8 MLA model (e.g. DeepSeek-V3) before and after this commit, counting number of registered MLA fusion patterns and total mla_attn_quant_fusion pass registration time.

findings.claim: The four boolean flags (col_major, is_e8m0, tma_aligned) plus group_size are runtime characteristics of the model's quantization config; a given deployment only uses one combination. Registering all 16 combinations means 15/16 of the registered group-FP8 patterns can never match at runtime yet are still traced at registration time and still checked against the graph on every pass invocation, wasting compile time and pattern-match work with no throughput benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count how many of the 16 group-FP8 pattern combinations actually match during a real DeepSeek-V3 block-FP8 compile; compare pass registration time when registering only the config-relevant combination vs. all 16.

findings.category: per-call overhead in hot forward path
findings.claim: forward_impl now calls _detect_output_quant_key() on every attention forward, which does dtype checks and integer division to re-derive the quant key that the fusion pass already knew statically. Previously the check was a single boolean (output_scale is not None or output_block_scale is not None). This adds minor per-invocation Python overhead in the attention forward hot path; likely negligible relative to the attention kernel but repeated for every layer and every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark forward_impl dispatch overhead per attention layer per decode step with and without _detect_output_quant_key.

findings.claim: This commit reverts PR #38730 ('[Bugfix] Restrict TRTLLM attention to SM100, fixing GB300 (SM103) hang'). supports_trtllm_attention() is changed back from current_platform.is_device_capability(100) (exact SM100 / 10.0 match) to current_platform.is_device_capability_family(100) (any 10.x device). Because is_device_capability_family(100) evaluates (capability.to_int() // 10) == (100 // 10) == 10, it returns True for SM103 (GB300): 103 // 10 == 10. This re-enables TRTLLM attention on GB300/SM103 devices, which the reverted bugfix disabled precisely because it caused a hang there. Reintroducing this path can hang the engine on SM103 hardware (a critical, availability-level runtime regression) and otherwise dispatch attention through an unsupported/broken backend.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run attention (prefill+decode) on a GB300 / SM103 device with FlashInfer available and artifactory reachable; verify use_trtllm_attention() no longer selects TRTLLM and that the model does not hang. Compare against SM100 where TRTLLM should still be used.

findings.claim: The reverted tests re-patch current_platform.is_device_capability_family, but two tests (test_supports_non_sm100_platform and test_supports_sm100_without_artifactory) still patch current_platform.is_device_capability rather than ...is_device_capability_family. Since supports_trtllm_attention() now calls is_device_capability_family, these mocks no longer intercept the real capability check; the tests may pass/fail based on the actual device capability rather than the intended mock, weakening the guard against the SM103 gating regression.

PyAVVideoBackendMixin.decode_frames performs one container.seek() per sampled frame followed by next(container.decode(video=0)). av's container.seek(pts, stream) seeks to the nearest KEYFRAME at or before the requested pts and flushes/reinitializes the decoder; the subsequent decode then re-decodes from that keyframe. For dense sampling (e.g. num_frames in the tens/hundreds, or fps-based sampling of a normal video), consecutive target frames typically live in the same GOP, so each per-frame seek discards decoder state and repeatedly re-decodes the same GOP prefix. This is substantially more expensive than a single sequential decode pass (which the OpenCV grab() path effectively does), and directly contradicts the docstring claim that cost 'scales with the number of sampled frames rather than the video length' — for many-frame sampling it can be O(num_frames * GOP_length) rather than O(total_frames). This regresses per-request video preprocessing latency/CPU under serving load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode N=8,32,120 frames from a 30fps H.264 video (with typical GOP ~250) using backend='pyav' vs backend='opencv'; measure wall time and CPU. Increase num_frames to observe superlinear cost of the seek-per-frame path.

findings.claim: After container.seek(pts, stream=stream), the code takes next(container.decode(video=0), None), which returns the first decoded frame at/after the keyframe rather than the frame whose pts matches the requested target. Because seek lands on a keyframe (often well before the target pts), the returned frame's index will frequently not equal idx. The metadata records idx as a 'valid' index regardless, but the decoded content is the keyframe. If downstream code detects the mismatch (or if models are re-run because sampled frames don't match expectations), this can cause redundant reprocessing; more directly, if the intent were to reach the exact frame, the decoder would need to keep decoding forward until pts>=target, which would add even more per-frame work than currently coded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare pyav vs opencv sampled frame pixel content for a fixed set of indices to quantify divergence; measure any downstream reprocessing triggered by frame-index/content mismatch.

findings.claim: stream.thread_type is set to 'SLICE' but thread_count is never configured, so PyAV/FFmpeg uses its default (often 1). The docstring asserts the seek 'releases the GIL between frames' and enables 'concurrent decoding under serving load', but nothing in this code sets up multi-threaded decode or verifies GIL release; the concurrency benefit is speculative. Under the default single-thread decode, the per-frame-seek path (see other finding) has no threading to offset its overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run K concurrent pyav decode_frames calls in a thread pool and measure aggregate throughput vs sequential; verify whether SLICE without thread_count actually parallelizes decode.

findings.claim: This revert removes three concurrent startup optimizations: (1) background preloading of torch+transformers .so/modules on the main thread (~2-4 s of dlopen/import overlapped with other CPU work), (2) early forkserver prewarm that pre-forks and preloads vllm.v1.engine.async_llm so a later Process.start() forks from a warm sibling instead of paying full spawn() cost (~5 s in child), and (3) parent-side weight prefetch that reads model shards into the OS page cache while the child is still doing fork/CUDA/dist init. Removing all three serializes torch import, EngineCore spawn, and weight loading on the critical startup path, measurably increasing `vllm serve` cold-start / time-to-first-ready, especially for large (tens of GB) models on cold disks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `vllm serve <large-model>` wall-clock from process launch to server-ready on a cold page cache, comparing parent vs child commit; also time-to-EngineCore-import.

findings.claim: Removal of _startup_prefetch_weights means weight shards are no longer warmed into the OS page cache from the parent process during config resolution/fork. The in-child prefetch (weight_utils._prefetch_all_checkpoints) still runs, but it can no longer overlap the ~10-15 s of fork + CUDA context + distributed init that precedes it in the child, so the prefetch+load phase is less well hidden for large models on cold storage.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time model weight load phase for a multi-shard model with dropped page cache (echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches) with and without the parent prefetch.

findings.claim: VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD no longer accepts 'forkserver'. forkserver startup (fork from a preloaded warm server) can be faster than 'spawn' for repeated worker creation and safer than 'fork' with CUDA; removing it as an opt-in eliminates a faster multiprocessing path for setups that had tuned for it.

This commit renames all preprocessor uses from __HIP__GFX11__/__HIP__GFX12__ to __GFX11__/__GFX12__, but nowhere in the diff (nor in the parent tree) are __GFX11__ or __GFX12__ ever #defined. The parent only defines __HIP__GFX11__/__HIP__GFX12__ from the compiler-provided __gfx11xx__/__gfx12xx__ predefined macros, and those definition blocks are deleted here. Unless a header defining __GFX11__/__GFX12__ was added (none is among the 3 changed files), the new combined macro `#if defined(__GFX11__) || defined(__GFX12__)  #define __HIP__GFX1X__` will never be true, so __HIP__GFX1X__ is undefined on ALL gfx11/gfx12 builds. That collapses every `#if defined(__HIP__GFX9__) || defined(__HIP__GFX1X__)` skinny-GEMM kernel (wvSplitK_hf_sml_, wvSplitK_hf_, wvSplitK_hf_big_, etc.) to the UNREACHABLE_CODE stub on RDNA. The optimized v_dot2_f32_f16 DOT2C path is also disabled. Result: skinny GEMMs either fall back to a much slower generic path or abort at runtime — a severe throughput regression / functional break on all RDNA GPUs, exactly the arches this PR claims to enable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Build for gfx1100/gfx1200 and micro-benchmark wvSplitKQ / wvSplitK skinny-GEMM throughput vs parent; verify kernels are not compiled to UNREACHABLE_CODE.

findings.claim: The FP8 skinny-GEMM kernels wvSplitKQ_hf_sml_/wvSplitKQ_hf_ are guarded by `#if defined(__HIP__MI3XX__) || defined(__GFX12__)`. With __GFX12__ never defined (see the deleted definition blocks), the gfx12 code path is compiled to UNREACHABLE_CODE, and the per-lane v_dot4_f32_fp8_fp8 accumulation path (`#ifdef __GFX12__`) inside the surviving kernels is dead. FP8 GEMM on gfx1200/gfx1201 will hit the UNREACHABLE_CODE assert / generic fallback instead of the intended optimized dot4 path — severe FP8 inference slowdown or crash on RDNA4.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FP8 wvSplitKQ throughput on gfx1200; confirm dot4 path active.

findings.claim: attention.cu removes the `#define __HIP__GFX11__` and `#define __HIP__GFX12__` blocks and switches the paged_attention kernel selection to `#elif defined(__GFX11__)` / `#elif defined(__GFX12__)`. Since __GFX11__/__GFX12__ are never defined, the RDNA-specific paged_attention_ll4mi_QKV / reduce kernels are never compiled for gfx11/gfx12; the build falls through to the non-RDNA branch (or leaves the kernel unimplemented). This silently disables the optimized wave32 attention kernels on all RDNA GPUs, causing a major decode-attention latency/throughput regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: paged-attention decode latency on gfx1100/gfx1200 vs parent.

findings.claim: The parent-side weight prefetch (`_startup_prefetch_weights`) reads ALL weight shards (full safetensors + bin files) with 8 parallel threads at 16MB blocks, but the existing in-child prefetch (`_prefetch_all_checkpoints`) shards work per-rank (`sorted_files[rank::world_size]`). On multi-GPU (TP>1) setups the parent reads the entire model into page cache while N child ranks each begin reading their 1/N slice a few seconds later. If the model is larger than available page-cache RAM, the parent's reads of shards that a given rank does NOT need will evict/compete with the shards the ranks DO need, causing extra disk I/O rather than saving it. On systems where the model does not fit in RAM (the very case where prefetch matters), this can make loading slower, not faster.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache `vllm serve` startup time for a model larger than free RAM with TP=4, comparing weight-load phase duration with vs. without parent-side prefetch.

findings.claim: The parent-side prefetch may overlap in time with the child's own in-child prefetch (both use 8 threads). If both run concurrently against the same disk, there are up to 16 concurrent readers per shard, which can reduce sequential-read throughput on spinning disks / single-queue storage and yield little net speedup versus the sequential head start the comment assumes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure disk read throughput and total load time when parent and child prefetch overlap on HDD vs NVMe.

findings.claim: The background torch/transformers preload thread in cli/main.py and the forkserver prewarm thread compete for the GIL and CPU with the main thread's argparse/config work. `import torch`/`import transformers` are largely GIL-bound Python work (only .so dlopen releases the GIL), so the claimed overlap with main-thread imports is partial; on a single-core/constrained container this could slightly slow the main-thread path rather than help.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time `vllm serve --help` and full startup on a 1-2 vCPU container with and without the background preload thread.

findings.claim: Forcing forkserver + preloading vllm.v1.engine.async_llm keeps a long-lived forkserver process holding a warm copy of the imported modules (torch etc.) resident for the server's lifetime. This is an intentional startup tradeoff but increases baseline RSS versus fork/spawn, which may matter on memory-constrained hosts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare steady-state RSS of the forkserver helper process under forkserver vs fork start method.

findings.claim: In the TritonExperts.apply path, the change replaced an unconditional moe_align_block_size() call with _prepare_expert_assignment(). However, _prepare_expert_assignment() is called with ignore_invalid_experts defaulting to False here (unlike fused_experts_impl which passes ignore_invalid_experts=True). If the naive_block_assignment low-latency branch is taken, sorted_token_ids becomes None and invoke_fused_moe_triton_kernel must correctly handle the naive path; if invoke_fused_moe_triton_kernel does NOT support the sorted_token_ids-is-None (naive_block_assignment) path the way dispatch_fused_moe_kernel does, this path can misbehave. This should be verified: dispatch_fused_moe_kernel sets naive_block_assignment=(sorted_token_ids is None), but invoke_fused_moe_triton_kernel must expose the same handling for the quantized TritonExperts path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark TritonExperts.apply with small num_tokens (decode, few tokens) vs large num_tokens against the pre-change moe_align_block_size path; measure per-layer latency.

findings.claim: The refactor into _prepare_expert_assignment() adds an extra Python function call per MoE invocation plus keyword-argument packing. In decode-time low-latency scenarios (very small token counts, many MoE layers per step) this per-layer Python overhead is on the critical path and slightly increases host-side latency versus the previous inlined code. Minor, but the whole point of this change is low-latency decode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile host-side CPU time of fused_experts_impl for single-token decode across many layers before/after.

findings.claim: In the naive_block_assignment branch, num_tokens_post_padded is set to topk_ids.numel() * BLOCK_SIZE_M, which makes num_pid_m = cdiv(EM, BLOCK_SIZE_M) launch one M-block per (token,topk) pair even though each block processes only a single token (naive path uses one row per block). This wastes the remaining BLOCK_SIZE_M-1 rows per block and inflates grid size; it is only worthwhile when num_tokens*top_k*4 <= global_num_experts. If the heuristic threshold is mis-tuned relative to BLOCK_SIZE_M, the low-latency path can be slower than aligned path due to under-utilized M blocks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep num_tokens near the num_tokens*top_k*4==global_num_experts boundary and compare kernel time of naive vs aligned assignment.

findings.claim: The CPU path is switched from NaiveAll2AllManager (which used broadcast/all_reduce, both implemented in CpuCommunicator) to AgRsAll2AllManager, whose dispatch()/dispatch_router_logits()/combine() call dist_group.all_gatherv(...) and dist_group.reduce_scatterv(...). CpuCommunicator does NOT override all_gatherv or reduce_scatterv, so it inherits DeviceCommunicatorBase.all_gatherv/reduce_scatterv, which both `raise NotImplementedError`. Any CPU EP/DP run that reaches MoE dispatch/combine will therefore throw instead of running — effectively an infinite performance regression (the workload cannot execute at all) rather than a slowdown. Even the warning branch now silently sets backend to allgather_reducescatter for any non-naive/non-agrs backend, so all CPU all2all now hits this broken path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a small MoE model with data_parallel_size>1 on the CPU backend (VLLM_CPU) and exercise a forward pass through the FusedMoE dispatch/combine; verify it does not raise NotImplementedError from all_gatherv/reduce_scatterv.

findings.claim: The 'naive' backend name is now aliased to AgRsAll2AllManager on all backends (cuda/xpu/cpu). AgRsAll2AllManager.dispatch asserts sizes[dist_group.rank_in_group] == hidden_states.shape[0] and relies on dp_metadata.get_chunk_sizes_across_dp_rank() being populated, whereas the old naive path used cu_tokens_across_sp and broadcast. If any caller previously selected 'naive' for a configuration where chunk sizes are not available/aligned, the new path will assert-fail or behave differently at the shape level rather than degrade gracefully. This is a functional risk more than a pure perf regression, but a failed assert stalls the run.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Exercise all2all_backend='naive' with a DP MoE model on GPU and confirm dispatch()'s size assertion holds for the configured chunk sizes.

findings.claim: supports_trtllm_attention() is broadened from is_device_capability(100) (exactly SM100 / CC 10.0) to is_device_capability_family(100) (any CC 10.x). This re-enables TRTLLM attention on SM103 (GB300), which the removed inline comment and the prior commit 6183cae1b ('[Bugfix] Restrict TRTLLM attention to SM100, fixing GB300 (SM103) hang', flashinfer issue #2939) documented as *hanging* with FlashInfer >= 0.6.7. The commit only bumps FlashInfer to 0.6.8.post1 to fix MoE GEMM activation templates (flashinfer #2926/#2738) and gives no evidence that the GB300 attention hang (#2939) was fixed. If the hang persists in 0.6.8.post1, GB300 users will experience a full inference hang once auto-detection selects TRTLLM decode/prefill attention.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an SM103 (GB300) device with FlashInfer 0.6.8.post1, run a decode workload with FLASHINFER attention and TRTLLM auto-detection enabled (num_tokens<=256, kv_cache_dtype=auto) and confirm forward passes complete without hanging.

findings.claim: FlashInferExperts._supports_current_device() changes is_device_capability(120) to is_device_capability_family(120), enabling SM121 (DGX Spark) for the CUTLASS fused MoE path. The removed comment noted the bf16 unquantized CUTLASS MoE GEMM previously threw 'Invalid activation type' (missing Relu2 template) on SM121, fixed by flashinfer #2926. If that fix is genuinely in 0.6.8.post1 this is correct; otherwise it would raise at MoE dispatch rather than degrade performance. Gated on the flashinfer version bump being complete, so low performance risk, but worth verifying the Relu2 path actually runs on SM121.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM121 (DGX Spark) with FlashInfer 0.6.8.post1, run Nemotron-H (RELU2_NO_MUL) MoE forward through FlashInferExperts and confirm the CUTLASS GEMM does not error and matches reference throughput.

findings.claim: `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` is decorated with `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` and is called once per image tile / thumbnail / video frame-batch in the multimodal preprocessing hot path. The `size` argument is passed as a Python `tuple[int,int]` and varies per image (dynamic-resolution tiling produces many distinct target sizes), and the norm_mean/norm_std/dtype arguments toggle between None and tensors. torch.compile treats the Python tuple `size` and the None-vs-tensor branches as guards, so each new (size, arg-shape, dtype) combination triggers a fresh graph compile. Preprocessing that formerly ran as an eager `F.interpolate` will now repeatedly pay Dynamo tracing + Inductor compilation overhead (hundreds of ms to seconds per new shape), which for varied-resolution image/video inputs can dominate and severely slow down request preprocessing latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Preprocess a stream of images with many distinct resolutions (e.g. 50 images of different sizes) through the Nano Nemotron VL processor and measure end-to-end preprocessing wall time vs the eager _bicubic_from_ndarray baseline; count torch.compile recompilations via TORCH_LOGS=recompiles.

findings.claim: The tensors fed to `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` originate from `torch.from_numpy(...)` (CPU) with no device transfer anywhere in the file, so the compiled kernel is a CPU interpolate+normalize. torch.compile provides little to no speedup for a single CPU bicubic interpolation (antialias=True bicubic is not fusible into a fast Inductor kernel) while adding significant per-shape compilation cost and warmup. This likely regresses preprocessing throughput on CPU rather than improving it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark a single CPU F.interpolate(mode='bicubic', antialias=True) + normalize with and without torch.compile, including compile warmup, across a range of image sizes.

findings.claim: For images that need a thumbnail, `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` is now invoked twice per image (once for the full resized image, once for the thumbnail), and each call independently repeats the permute NHWC->NCHW and float32 cast on the same source `tensor`. Combined with the torch.compile guard on differing `size`, the thumbnail path guarantees at least one extra compilation per distinct thumbnail size. Minor compared to the compile overhead itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare preprocessing time for images with use_thumbnail=True vs False under the new code path.

findings.claim: In transfer_run_periodically, the wait on the rearrange trigger was changed from `await asyncio.to_thread(state.rearrange_event.wait)` to a synchronous `state.rearrange_event.wait(stream=cuda_stream)`. CpuGpuEvent.wait() calls `self._recorded.wait()` (a blocking threading.Event wait with no timeout) directly on the asyncio event-loop thread. This blocks the entire event loop rather than cooperatively yielding, so any other coroutines scheduled on that loop cannot run while the worker is idle-waiting. In the previous design the wait was offloaded to a thread-pool so the loop stayed responsive.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure async EPLB worker responsiveness / event-loop stall while idle-waiting for a rearrange trigger.

findings.claim: After each layer transfer the async worker calls `consumed_event.wait(stream=cuda_stream)`, whose first step is `self._recorded.wait()` — a blocking CPU wait on threading.Event until the MAIN thread records the event in _move_to_workspace. The main thread only calls _move_to_workspace once per forward pass (from step()), so the async worker thread is blocked for a full forward-pass interval per layer. Combined with the earlier `cuda_stream.synchronize()` (which blocks the thread until GPU copy completes), the worker thread spends most of its time hard-blocked. Any coroutine cancellation/shutdown that relies on the event loop being able to progress will not fire until the main thread records, which can delay shutdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time per-layer async transfer throughput and worker-thread idle time under a running forward loop.

findings.claim: `cuda_stream.synchronize()` is retained inside the per-layer loop and now runs unconditionally for every one of num_moe_layers layers before the result is published. This is a full host-side stall on the async worker's stream per layer; if the transfer for a layer is trivial/unchanged, the synchronize still forces a host<->device round trip each iteration, serializing layer transfers with no overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare EPLB rebalance wall-clock with vs. without per-layer stream.synchronize() for a model with many MoE layers.

findings.claim: CpuGpuEvent.record() allocates a brand-new torch.cuda.Event on every call (`self._event = torch.cuda.Event()`), and one CpuGpuEvent (with its own CUDA event) is constructed per layer transfer in the async worker loop. For models with many MoE layers this creates and discards one CUDA event per layer per rebalance cycle. CUDA event creation/destruction is cheap but non-zero; reusing a single event object would avoid repeated allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark CUDA event allocation churn during a full multi-layer rebalance cycle.

findings.claim: NixlEplbCommunicator._init_registered_buffers allocates a send buffer of size world_size * peer_partition_bytes on GPU (self._send_buffer = torch.empty(peer_partition_bytes * world_size, ...)) plus a recv buffer of peer_partition_bytes, and registers them with NIXL. peer_partition_bytes is sized to the total bytes of one MoE layer's expert weights across all dtypes. On large EP deployments (world_size in the tens/hundreds), this is a persistent world_size-scaled VRAM reservation held for the lifetime of the communicator, which the code's own comment acknowledges 'can cause OOM on large models'. This is a substantial, permanent GPU memory footprint that reduces available KV-cache/activation memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory reserved by NixlEplbCommunicator init vs torch_nccl/pynccl backends across EP world sizes (8, 32, 64) with a DeepSeek-class MoE layer.

findings.claim: execute() calls torch.distributed.monitored_barrier(group=cpu_group, timeout=5min) on every rearrangement transfer. monitored_barrier is a gloo/CPU barrier that is significantly more expensive than a plain barrier (it does rank-by-rank round-trips for diagnostics). Since execute() is invoked once per MoE layer during rearrangement (rearrange_expert_weights_inplace iterates layers), this adds a full CPU-side monitored barrier per layer, serializing all ranks and adding measurable latency proportional to num_moe_layers * world_size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time execute() with monitored_barrier vs plain barrier for a 60-layer MoE across 32 ranks; compare total rearrangement wall time.

findings.claim: _wait_for_all_transfers busy-polls check_xfer_state in a Python loop with time.sleep(0.0005) (500us) between full rescans of all pending handles. This fixed 500us sleep granularity adds up to ~500us latency floor per polling iteration and burns a CPU thread spinning; for many small transfers or fast completions it introduces avoidable latency, and for long transfers it repeatedly re-checks all handles. Combined with being called per-layer, this polling overhead accumulates over the rearrangement.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare rearrangement completion latency of NIXL polling loop vs a NIXL notification/event-based wait for varying transfer counts.

findings.claim: execute() performs a full CUDA stream synchronize (self._cuda_stream.synchronize() or torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize()) after packing, before the barrier. A device-wide stream sync stalls the GPU pipeline and blocks the calling thread; done per-layer this serializes packing against transfer and prevents overlap between layers' pack/transfer phases.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU idle time during rearrangement with per-layer stream.synchronize() vs a pipelined approach.

findings.claim: NixlEplbCommunicator.__init__ performs two all_gather_object calls (agent metadata and send-buffer meta) plus per-peer add_remote_agent, and _make_agent_name uses uuid. These are init-time costs only (not per-step), so impact is limited to communicator construction, but on large world sizes all_gather_object of Python objects (pickled bytes) across all ranks adds startup latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time NixlEplbCommunicator construction across world sizes 8/32/64.

findings.claim: In train_step, the distillation tensor-shape adjustment is now gated on `has_nvidia_modelopt and getattr(args, 'modelopt_enabled', False)`. This is a positive change: previously, whenever the modelopt package was merely importable (`has_nvidia_modelopt` True), `get_tensor_shapes_adjust_fn_for_distillation(...)` was invoked every iteration even for non-distillation runs, which reads parallel_state and builds an adjust-shapes closure per iteration. Adding the `modelopt_enabled` guard avoids this per-iteration overhead for runs that merely have modelopt installed but are not doing distillation. This is not a regression, but note the equivalent call at line ~3956 (validation path) was NOT similarly guarded in this diff, so any per-iteration cost there remains.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time train_step over N iterations with modelopt installed but modelopt_enabled=False, before vs after the guard; confirm per-iteration reduction and check validation path (~line 3956) is not left ungated.

findings.claim: gemma4_fused_routing_kernel_triton launches the grid as (T,) with a hardcoded num_warps=1, i.e. exactly one 32-thread warp per token and one token per program. During decode the number of routed tokens T equals the running batch (often 1-few-hundred), so the whole routing kernel occupies only T blocks × 1 warp. On a GPU with dozens of SMs this leaves the device almost entirely idle and makes the launch latency-bound, adding a serialized per-step kernel to the decode critical path. Increasing num_warps or processing multiple tokens per block would greatly improve small-batch latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time the routing function for T in {1,4,16,64,256} with E=128,K=8 comparing num_warps=1 vs 4/8, and end-to-end decode step latency for Gemma4 MoE.

findings.claim: The kernel performs a full bitonic tl.sort over all BLOCK_E (=128 for Gemma4) elements per token just to extract the top-K=8. A full sort is O(N log^2 N) (~6k comparisons for N=128) whereas a top-8 selection needs far fewer; on a single warp this dominates the per-token cost. A partial top-K selection would reduce compute substantially, especially since only the first K sorted entries are ever stored.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench the kernel with E=128,K=8 comparing full tl.sort against a top-K selection variant across T.

findings.claim: gemma4_fused_routing_kernel_triton calls .contiguous() on both gating_output and per_expert_scale unconditionally on every invocation. If the router logits tensor is ever non-contiguous this triggers a full [T,E] copy each forward pass; even when already contiguous the call is a cheap no-op, but the gating_output copy for large T (up to 250k) could be a measurable allocation/copy on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel wrapper time with and without .contiguous() for large T where gating_output is already contiguous.

findings.claim: During RL inference mode, the code previously forced `model[0].config.cuda_graph_scope = []`, which (per the surrounding comment and arguments.py deprecation note) means 'full-layer capture' — the entire transformer layer is captured into a CUDA graph for inference decode. The change replaces this with `= args.cuda_graph_scope`, i.e. whatever the user configured for training. If the user's `cuda_graph_scope` does not include `full_iteration_inference` (and in RL training it typically holds only training-oriented scopes such as attn/moe_router, or is left at its training default), inference will no longer capture full-layer graphs. Per dynamic_engine.py, when `full_iteration_inference` is absent from the scope, inference CUDA graphs are NOT created at all (it logs a warning telling users to pass `--cuda-graph-scope=full_iteration_inference`). The consequence is that rollout generation (decode) falls back to eager per-step execution instead of graph replay — a substantial per-token latency and throughput regression for the generation phase, which usually dominates RL wall-clock time. This is a behavioral change that only produces the old fast path when the user happens to have set full_iteration_inference in args.cuda_graph_scope.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run an RL rollout generation loop with a typical training-side cuda_graph_scope (e.g. empty or [attn, moe_router]) and measure decode tokens/sec and per-step latency before vs after this commit; verify whether inference CUDA graphs are actually captured (check for the dynamic_engine warning about full_iteration_inference).

findings.claim: The new `else` branch sets the training-restore cuda_graph_scope to `args.cuda_graph_scope` minus `full_iteration_inference`, whereas previously (implicitly) the scope was left as the value chosen at inference-entry. If the inference-entry scope now differs from the training-restore scope on each pass through this context manager (because entry uses `args.cuda_graph_scope` including full_iteration_inference and restore strips it), the config toggles between two distinct scope lists on every rollout/train transition. Changing cuda_graph_scope typically invalidates and forces re-capture of CUDA graphs; repeatedly flipping the scope across the inference/training boundary can trigger repeated graph re-capture, adding capture overhead each RL step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument CUDA graph capture count (or capture time) across repeated megatron_rl_inference_mode enter/exit cycles and confirm whether graphs are being re-captured each cycle due to the scope differing between entry and restore.

findings.claim: BufferKey now includes a fourth dimension `is_managed_by_layer_wise_optimizer`, so what was previously a single bf16 buffer is split into two separate buffers (LayerWise-managed matrix weights vs DistOpt-managed biases/LayerNorm/embeddings). In partition_buckets Case 2 (no fp8 buffer) each buffer produces its own set of single-bucket bucket groups, so the number of param all-gather and grad reduce-scatter collective launches roughly doubles for the affected dtype. With CUDA_DEVICE_MAX_CONNECTIONS=1, more back-to-back small collectives reduces overlap with compute and increases per-kernel launch/latency overhead, especially harmful for the many small bias/LN parameters that now live in their own buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Layer-wise (Muon) + DistributedOptimizer run with use_layer_wise_param_layout enabled; measure number of NCCL all-gather/reduce-scatter kernels per step and end-to-end step time vs the pre-commit single-optimizer path.

findings.claim: After this change two chained optimizers (LayerWiseDistributedOptimizer and the sibling DistributedOptimizer) each issue their own start_param_sync over their subset of bucket groups in the non-overlap path. The subsets are dispatched from separate call sites (LayerWise's own sync + DistOpt's start_param_sync_for_bucket_group_subset), so the two all-gather phases are no longer coalesced into a single _coalescing_manager region. This serializes what could be one fused all-gather into two, adding a second synchronization point per step and reducing NCCL bandwidth efficiency for the smaller (DistOpt) bucket group.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare params-all-gather timer and step latency for layer-wise + DistOpt path against a run where all params share one optimizer's coalesced sync.

findings.claim: start_param_sync_for_bucket_group_subset iterates over every model chunk's bucket_groups + expert_parallel_bucket_groups every optimizer step and, for each, calls _bucket_is_managed_by_layer_wise_optimizer (which does a getattr/hasattr on the first param) plus a deferred module import (`from .layer_wise_optimizer import ...`). The import is cached after first call but the per-bucket-group Python filtering runs every step; for models with many bucket groups this adds CPU overhead on the critical optimizer path relative to the previous single `model_chunk.start_param_sync()` call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CPU time in step_with_ready_grads for a model with many bucket groups (large VPP/many chunks) before vs after the change.

findings.claim: The refactor into _start_bucket_group_param_sync preserves the FP8 param scan, but because bucket groups are now iterated one-by-one from two call sites (DDP.start_param_sync and DistOpt.start_param_sync_for_bucket_group_subset) rather than once, the fp8/mxfp8 param scanning loop over bucket.params runs per bucket group per caller. In the mxfp8 reuse-grad-buf path this is unchanged, but any duplicate dispatch across the two chained optimizers would repeat the O(num_params) scan; verify buckets are not visited by both paths.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count invocations of post_all_gather_processing / mxfp8 copy loop per step in the chained LayerWise+DistOpt configuration.

findings.claim: This change is a net performance improvement, but note one behavioral change worth verifying: prepare_encoder_metadata now builds cu_seqlens/max_seqlen/sequence_lengths once per vision_tower_forward call using numpy on CPU, instead of the previous per-forward torch ops on GPU. Previously max_seqlen was recomputed inside every encoder block via `(cu_seqlens[1:] - cu_seqlens[:-1]).max()`, which produced a GPU->host sync (the .max() result feeds FlashAttn/FlashInfer as a python/tensor arg) on every one of the num_hidden_layers blocks. Hoisting it to once-per-model and computing on CPU eliminates per-block GPU syncs and the per-block reduction kernel — a real latency win for the ViT. No regression identified here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark MoonViT3dEncoder.forward on a multi-image batch, counting cudaStreamSynchronize calls per layer before/after.

findings.claim: Setting keep_on_cpu=True for grid_thws avoids an unnecessary H2D copy of the tiny grid_thws tensor into GPU memory during input assembly; the tensor is only consumed by .tolist()/numpy conversions on the host in prepare_encoder_metadata. This is a small but valid savings (avoids a per-request device transfer and pinned-memory allocation) and matches the pattern used by qwen2_vl/gemma3. No regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-request multimodal input assembly time and H2D copies with/without keep_on_cpu.

findings.claim: In the non-data-parallel branch of vision_tower_forward, prepare_encoder_metadata is called explicitly and passed into vision_tower(...). MoonViT3dPretrainedModel.forward also computes grid_thw_list = grid_thws.tolist() again (grid_thws is already a list here, so tolist() is skipped) and, if encoder_metadata is None, would recompute it. Since it is passed, no double-compute occurs. The only minor redundancy: grid_thw.tolist() is done once in vision_tower_forward and grid_thw_list re-derived cheaply downstream. Negligible.

In QuarkOCP_MX_MoEMethod.apply the refactored call to self.moe_kernel.apply no longer passes the shared_experts argument (it only passes shared_experts_input). The base FusedW8A8Fp8/other apply signatures accept shared_experts, and the previous oracle path passed shared_experts=shared_experts. If the modular kernel supports fusing shared-expert computation into the MoE kernel, dropping shared_experts forces shared experts to be computed on a separate (unfused) code path, losing the AITER shared-expert fusion optimization and adding an extra GEMM / kernel launch per layer. This is a measurable throughput regression for models with shared experts (e.g. Qwen3.5-A3B).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-MXFP4 on GFX950 with AITER MoE fusion enabled; compare decode throughput before/after this commit to detect loss of shared-expert fusion.

findings.claim: The new AITER_MXFP4_MXFP4 weight-conversion path in convert_gpt_oss_weight_to_mxfp4_moe_kernel_format performs e8m0_shuffle on w13/w2 weight scales and rocm_aiter_ops.shuffle_weights on the fp4 weights. This is one-time (load-time) work, not per-forward, so it does not affect steady-state throughput; but note it is duplicated relative to _setup_kernel which also sets is_shuffled=True, so double-shuffle would corrupt/duplicate work if both paths run. Verify only one runs to avoid redundant load-time cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load time and validate weights are shuffled exactly once for the W4A4 path.

findings.claim: _filter_by_activation now retains EMULATION in the candidate list whenever an activation key is requested (`or b == Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION`). Emulation performs dequant + high-precision compute and is far slower than native kernels. If EMULATION is ever ordered ahead of, or selected instead of, a viable native backend due to this inclusion (e.g. when the native backend's is_supported_config returns False for a shape-specific reason), the layer will silently fall back to the emulation path with a large runtime slowdown. EMULATION was also removed from the gpt_oss priority list, changing fallback behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a config where the native W4A4 backend is unavailable, confirm whether the model silently selects EMULATION and measure resulting per-token latency vs native.

findings.claim: `supports_per_group_quant` is a property that calls `build_supports_per_group_quant()` every time it is accessed, and that function does a fresh `from aiter.dist.device_communicators.custom_all_reduce import CustomAllreduce` import plus a `hasattr` check on each read. The previous implementation (`has_fused_allreduce_rmsnorm_quant_per_group`) was also computed on demand, but here the extra module import on each property access adds overhead if this property is read frequently. It is only read once during pass registration in the current code, so impact is low, but caching would be cleaner.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated access to AiterCustomAllreduce.supports_per_group_quant vs a cached value.

findings.claim: Adding a top-level `from vllm._aiter_ops import rocm_aiter_ops` import to cuda_communicator.py pulls in the heavy _aiter_ops module (which imports pandas, torch ops registration, sparse-attn ops, etc.) at module load time on all CUDA platforms, not just ROCm. This can slightly increase import/startup latency on non-ROCm builds. Previously _aiter_ops was not imported by cuda_communicator.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `python -c 'import vllm.distributed.device_communicators.cuda_communicator'` import time before/after.

findings.claim: When VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_CUSTOM_AR is enabled (default True on ROCm), the CudaCommunicator now allocates an AiterCustomAllreduce whose IPC buffer is MAX_SIZE = 8192*1024*8*2 = 128 MiB per TP rank, allocated eagerly in __init__ for every TP group with world_size>1. Previously the AITER allreduce was only created lazily inside the fusion pass (initialize_aiter_allreduce) and only when the RocmAiterAllReduceFusionPass ran. Now it is always allocated whenever custom AR is enabled, regardless of whether fusion is used, increasing GPU memory footprint by ~128MiB per rank even in configs that never exercise the fused path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory of a ROCm TP=2/4 run with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_CUSTOM_AR=1 vs =0.

findings.claim: On ROCm, QuickAllReduce (qr_comm) is now unconditionally initialized whenever use_custom_allreduce and world_size>1, even when the AITER custom allreduce path is selected (aiter_ar_comm is not None). Previously QuickAllReduce was only created in the branch that also built vLLM's CustomAllreduce. With the AITER backend, both AITER's IPC buffers AND QuickAllReduce's buffers are now allocated together, increasing GPU memory and startup work versus a mutually-exclusive-backend expectation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GPU memory and init time on ROCm MI300 TP run with AITER custom AR enabled before/after this commit.

findings.claim: The all_reduce dispatch chain adds a new AITER branch after flashinfer/quick-reduce and before vLLM custom AR. Each all_reduce call now performs an additional `aiter_ar_comm.should_custom_ar(input_)` check (a Python-level gate) on every collective. This is a small per-call overhead but occurs on the hot allreduce path; when AITER is disabled (aiter_ar_comm is None) the extra check short-circuits cheaply.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark all_reduce dispatch overhead per call with AITER enabled vs disabled.

findings.claim: The old fusion path explicitly tore down aiter's custom-allreduce (destroy_aiter_allreduce) when falling back for unsupported hidden dims, to avoid its IPC handles racing with vllm's ca_comm. The new code removes this teardown and the __del__ cleanup; since the AITER instance is now owned by CudaCommunicator and shared, the fallback no longer frees it. This is likely intentional (shared ownership) but means the IPC buffers remain allocated for the process lifetime even when fusion is disabled, which combined with QuickAllReduce keeps more device memory resident.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify device memory residency on a ROCm run where fusion falls back for unsupported hidden_dim.

findings.claim: In FlashAttentionImpl.forward, when mm_prefix ranges are present (Gemma4 multimodal PrefixLM), the code calls _make_mm_prefix_mask_mod(max_ranges) on every forward invocation. That helper performs `import cutlass`/`import cutlass.cute` and constructs a fresh `@cute.jit` mask_mod closure each call. This runs once per attention layer per model step (dozens of layers x every decode step). Re-importing cutlass and re-creating a cute.jit callable in the per-step hot path adds large per-call Python/JIT overhead and can trigger repeated kernel (re)compilation, severely degrading decode latency and GPU utilization. The mask_mod should be built once (e.g. cached by max_ranges on the impl/builder) rather than per forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Gemma4 multimodal (image) decode with FA4 enabled and measure per-step latency / tokens-per-second with vs without a cached mask_mod; profile time spent in _make_mm_prefix_mask_mod and cutlass import.

findings.claim: Previously _set_mm_prefix_range_for_metadata computed compute_mm_prefix_range_tensor exactly ONCE and shared the resulting device tensor across all attention-group metadata objects (explicitly 'to avoid redundant host-to-device transfers'). The refactor moves the computation into each backend builder's build() (FlashAttentionMetadataBuilder.build and TritonAttentionMetadataBuilder.build). Gemma4 has heterogeneous layers (sliding-window + full attention), so there are multiple attention groups/builders, each of which now independently rebuilds the padded ranges list on CPU and issues its own async_tensor_h2d transfer for the same data every step. This multiplies the CPU list-building and H2D transfers by the number of attention groups on each forward, adding per-step CPU overhead and PCIe traffic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 multimodal batch with both sliding-window and full-attention layers; count compute_mm_prefix_range_tensor calls and H2D transfers per step and compare against the pre-change single-computation path.

findings.claim: The mm_prefix range computation was moved earlier so it now runs in the common metadata preparation path (building req_doc_ranges by iterating over every request's mm_features and calling extract_embeds_range) even in cases where the ranges are ultimately unused. This Python-level per-request iteration executes each step for is_mm_prefix_lm models; it is generally small but is now on the critical prepare path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure prepare_inputs CPU time per step for Gemma4 multimodal batches with many concurrent requests each having several image features.

findings.claim: MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel is inserted into _POSSIBLE_FP8_BLOCK_KERNELS[CUDA] ahead of TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel (but after Cutlass). The Marlin kernel's can_implement() unconditionally returns True and its is_supported() returns True for compute capability 7.5 <= cc < 89 (and for cc >= 89 only when VLLM_TEST_FORCE_FP8_MARLIN is set). This means that for the block-scaled FP8 path on GPUs where the DeepGEMM/FlashInfer/Cutlass kernels are unavailable but Marlin is (e.g. cc 7.5-8.6 lacking native FP8 hardware, or when CUTLASS_BLOCK_FP8_SUPPORTED is False), the selection loop will now pick the Marlin weight-only FP8 kernel instead of TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel. FP8 Marlin is a weight-only emulation path that dequantizes on-the-fly and is generally slower than a dedicated block-scaled Triton GEMM at larger batch sizes, potentially degrading throughput for block-scaled FP8 layers on these platforms. Because Marlin now precedes Triton and always claims it can_implement, it may shadow the Triton block kernel that was previously chosen.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Block-scaled FP8 (group_shape (1,128)) linear-layer GEMM microbenchmark on a GPU where Cutlass/DeepGEMM block kernels are unavailable (e.g. cc 8.0/8.6), comparing throughput/latency of MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel vs TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel across batch sizes 1..4096.

findings.claim: MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.can_implement() always returns True regardless of the block/group activation quant config, so when selected for a block-scaled (per-group) config it is instantiated via the new FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel branch (with the weight/weight_scale/input_scale/input_scale_ub param names). Its block_quant path keys only on kFp8Static128BlockSym; other block group shapes fall into the non-block branch and may perform an extra weight transpose/repacking at load time and use a suboptimal apply path. This is primarily a correctness/robustness concern but can incur unnecessary weight-processing work and a slower runtime path if it is chosen over a purpose-built block kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instantiate the block-scaled FP8 selection path with various group shapes and verify which kernel is chosen and measure process_weights_after_loading time and per-call latency.
