findings.claim: The fused torchembed path adds two full-tensor layout transposes with explicit materialization per call: `t_kernel = t.movedim(0, -2).contiguous()` and `t = t_out.movedim(-2, 0).contiguous()`. For the common `[seq_length, ..., dim]` layout, moving dim 0 to second-to-last produces a non-contiguous view whose `.contiguous()` forces a full device-memory copy; the same happens on the way back. The original reference path (`t*freqs_cos + _rotate_half(t)*freqs_sin`) needs no transpose and no extra buffer. These two extra copies (each ~O(seq_len*heads*rot_dim) elements) run on every q and every k for every layer, and the transpose/contiguous ops are also recorded in the autograd graph so they are re-executed (again with copies) in the backward pass. For short/medium sequence lengths the copy + kernel-launch overhead can equal or exceed any savings from the triton kernel, making the 'fused' path a net slowdown and increasing peak memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark apply_rotary_pos_emb on CUDA with torchembed installed vs the reference path across seq_len in {128,512,2048,8192}, dim=128, heads=32, in fp16, timing both forward and forward+backward and measuring peak memory; compare against the pure-PyTorch broadcast path.

findings.claim: `freqs_cos.reshape(freqs_cos.shape[0], rot_dim)` / `freqs_sin.reshape(...)` may trigger an extra copy when the incoming freqs tensor is not contiguous over the collapsed dims (e.g. a `[seq_len, 1, rot_dim]` broadcast tensor sliced from a larger cache). This is a small per-call overhead added only to the fused path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark reshape cost on typical non-contiguous freqs shapes vs contiguous ones.

findings.claim: The added guard evaluates `get_accelerator().on_accelerator(t)`, `freqs_cos.numel()`, and several `.shape` accesses on every call to apply_rotary_pos_emb, including in the FPDT chunked path which calls this function many times per layer per chunk. Each is cheap individually but adds Python-level overhead on the hot path before falling back to the reference implementation in the common (non-fused-eligible) case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Python overhead of the guard evaluation across many small apply_rotary_pos_emb calls when torchembed is not installed / not eligible.

findings.claim: With fp32_optimizer_states=False the CPU Adam moments (exp_avg, exp_avg_sq) are stored in bf16 while the kernel computes in fp32 (Step_1 casts `(float)grads[k]` and reads `_exp_avg[k]` into float locals, then writes back rounded to bf16). The scalar/AVX loop must therefore perform bf16->fp32 upconversion on load and fp32->bf16 downconversion on store for the two moment buffers on every element, every step. This is extra per-element conversion work versus the fp32-states path where moments are read/written natively. For CPU-offloaded optimizer the step is already on the CPU critical path, so this can measurably slow the optimizer step even though it halves the moment memory footprint. This is a precision/memory-vs-CPU-throughput tradeoff that the commit does not benchmark.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepSpeedCPUAdam.step() wall time for a large param set (e.g. 100M-1B params) with fp32_optimizer_states=True vs False, on AVX512 and scalar builds, to quantify any per-step slowdown from bf16 moment conversion.

findings.claim: `_configure_basic_optimizer` now does `dict(self.optimizer_params() or {})` to shallow-copy the optimizer config before popping keys. This is a one-time O(number-of-config-keys) copy at engine init, negligible; noted only for completeness. No runtime hot-path impact.

The selective-gather persistence budget now computes available_mem as usable_mem - transient_peak instead of usable_mem - peak_resident_alloc (and the code now uses budget['available_mem'] instead of the runtime current_available_mem). Because transient_peak (record[3], the max_mem peak) is always >= peak_resident_alloc (record[1], the resident allocation), the computed headroom is strictly smaller. This will cause fewer ZeRO-3 parameters to be marked persistent (set_persistent). Every parameter that is NOT kept persistent must be re-all-gathered on each forward/backward step at runtime, adding collective-communication latency and reducing throughput/GPU utilization. This is an intentional OOM-avoidance fix, but it trades runtime throughput for lower peak memory; on workloads that previously fit, fewer persistent params means more per-step all-gather traffic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ZeRO-3 DeepCompile training step throughput (tokens/s) and count of persistent params before/after, on a model where profiled transient_peak significantly exceeds peak_resident_alloc.

findings.claim: Removing the `node.meta.setdefault('recompute', CheckpointPolicy.MUST_SAVE)` branch means non-parameter activations are no longer forced to be SAVED; they now fall to the min-cut partitioner's default rematerialization policy. min-cut may choose to RECOMPUTE many activations in the backward pass to save memory. This reduces peak memory (the stated goal, avoiding long-sequence OOM) but adds extra recompute FLOPs in the compiled backward pass, increasing backward-pass compute time and lowering GPU throughput for graphs that previously fit in memory without recomputation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward-pass wall time and recomputed-node count for DeepCompile ZeRO-3 on a moderate-sequence model that did not previously OOM.

findings.claim: schedule_prefetch and selective_gather now additionally skip their optimizations when mem_complete/fwd_mem_complete/bwd_mem_complete is False (incomplete memory profile), and completion is synchronized to the MIN across ranks. If profiling fails or is incomplete on even a single rank, all ranks disable prefetch and selective-gather for that graph. This can silently fall back to the unoptimized (non-prefetched, non-persistent) execution path, degrading runtime throughput cluster-wide whenever profiling is flaky. This is safer for correctness but can cost throughput when profiling incompleteness is transient.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare step throughput with a forced incomplete memory profile on one rank vs. complete profiles on all ranks.

findings.claim: _sync_memory_profile_complete performs an additional all_reduce (ReduceOp.MIN) per profiled graph during profiling. This is compile-time only overhead (not per training step), so the runtime impact is negligible, but it adds a collective synchronization point per graph profile that could add latency to the profiling/warmup phase on large clusters.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepCompile compile/profiling phase duration with and without the added all_reduce on a large rank count.

findings.claim: This change enables a fused NVFP4 RHT + amax hadamard grouped-GEMM kernel path for the SReLU (unary) grouped MLP, previously only enabled for SwiGLU. When the environment supports it (cuDNN FE >= 1.26.0, NVFP4 RHT with post-RHT amax), the SReLU forward will now dispatch to the fused hadamard kernel instead of the separate activation + quantize-with-amax path. This is intended as a performance improvement (fusing activation, RHT, and amax computation), not a regression. No performance degradation is introduced: when the kernel is unavailable the code falls back to the previous non-hadamard path, and the new version guard (grouped_gemm_act_hadamard_kernel) is lru_cached so repeated dispatch decisions are cheap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark grouped SReLU MLP forward with NVFP4 RHT recipe on SM100 with cuDNN FE >= 1.26 vs < 1.26, comparing FC1 activation+quantize latency for the fused hadamard kernel vs the fallback activation-kernel + group_quantize_with_amax path.

findings.claim: Both zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 and zeropower_via_gram_newtonschulz are decorated with @compiler.compile() (torch.compile) and now call get_accelerator().is_bf16_supported()/is_fp16_supported() inside the compiled region. On CUDA, is_fp16_supported() invokes torch.cuda.get_device_capability() and reads os.environ, and is_bf16_supported() calls torch.cuda.is_bf16_supported(). These are Python/host-side device queries that torch.compile cannot trace as tensor ops; they will force a graph break (or be captured as a constant guard requiring a recompile if the value changes). The device-capability query result is invariant per device, so it is wasteful to recompute it inside the hot per-parameter iteration; it should be hoisted to module import time / a cached constant. Impact is small (one host query per call) but avoidable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time muon_update over many small parameters with torch.compile enabled, comparing hoisted vs in-loop dtype selection; also check for TORCH_LOGS=graph_breaks.

findings.claim: muon_update now records orig_dtype and unconditionally does `if update.dtype != orig_dtype: update = update.to(orig_dtype)` before returning. Previously the update was returned in the compute dtype (bf16) and the dtype conversion happened implicitly inside p.add_/g.data.copy_. The explicit .to(orig_dtype) allocates a new full-size tensor and performs an extra element-wise cast for every Muon parameter every step when the parameter dtype differs from the NS compute dtype (which is the normal fp32/bf16 master-weight case). This is a per-step, per-parameter extra allocation+copy proportional to weight size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark optimizer.step() wall time and peak allocator memory for a model with many large 2D Muon weights, before vs after the added .to(orig_dtype) cast.

findings.claim: The standard NS path changed compute dtype from unconditional G.bfloat16() to bf16-if-supported-else-fp32. On accelerators where is_bf16_supported() returns False, the Newton-Schulz iteration now runs the matmul chain (X@X.mT, A@A, B@X) in fp32 instead of bf16, roughly doubling memory traffic and, on hardware with fast bf16 tensor cores, reducing matmul throughput for the orthogonalization step. This is intended behavior (correctness on non-bf16 HW) but is a throughput/memory regression on those devices.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare NS iteration runtime and memory on a device that reports is_bf16_supported()==False (fp32 path) vs the old bf16 path.

findings.claim: get_graph_order() now runs an O(N) assertion `all(isinstance(needs_backward, bool) ...)` over every frame on each call, and the function is invoked multiple times per graph compilation (in make_fw_graph via set/len, and freshly in make_bw_graph). This adds compile-time work proportional to the number of frames. It only runs during graph compilation (not per training step), so runtime throughput is unaffected; the overhead is small and bounded by the number of compiled graphs. Consider removing the assertion or downgrading it to a debug-only check.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure total DeepCompile warmup/compilation time on a model with many distinct dynamo frames, comparing with and without the get_graph_order() assertion.

findings.claim: wrap_partition_fn is now applied unconditionally (previously only when z3_partition was True). For the non-z3 case the wrapper still constructs get_wrapped_partitioner and adds frame_id to frames_partitioned around every partition call, but the expensive DSGraphParamManager construction and fix_placeholder_meta traversal are now correctly guarded behind `if z3_partition`. Net effect is a very small added indirection layer at partition time only; no runtime-step cost. Note: this is part of a change whose overall intent is to REMOVE per-iteration backward recompilation, which is a large performance win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-iteration step time before and after the patch for a training run that previously exhibited backward graph recompilation each step (the repro in the commit), to confirm the recompilation is eliminated.

findings.claim: The per-call SDMA path is gated on `group is torch.distributed.group.WORLD`, but ZeRO-3 frequently issues `all_gather_into_tensor` on a non-WORLD data-parallel/zero-param subgroup while some ranks may pass WORLD (or vice versa). Because the SDMA `_handle` collective is a synchronizing intra-node op bound to the 'default'/WORLD group, if the routing decision (SDMA vs RCCL fallback) is not identical on all ranks for the same logical collective, ranks diverge: some enter `mori_cpp.AllGatherIntoTensor` (a collective over all WORLD PEs) while others take the RCCL path on a subgroup. That mismatch will deadlock. The `supports()` check depends on per-rank properties (numel <= _max_numel, dtype) which are normally symmetric, but the group check plus a possibly-different `_handle is None` state (init succeeds on some ranks, fails on others) can produce divergent routing and a hang.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run ZeRO-3 with a non-WORLD DP group and with mori init succeeding on a subset of ranks; verify no deadlock.

findings.claim: init() unconditionally allocates transit buffers sized for DS_SDMA_ALLGATHER_MAX_NUMEL (default 64M elements). input_buffer_size = 64M*4 = 256 MiB per rank, and output_buffer_size = input_bytes * npes = 256 MiB * 8 = 2 GiB on 8 ranks. These uncached SDMA transit buffers are allocated once at init and held for the entire run regardless of the actual bucket sizes used (stage3_prefetch_bucket_size default 5e7 = 50M elements). This is a fixed ~2.25 GiB of extra device memory per rank whenever DS_SDMA_ALLGATHER=1, reducing memory available for activations/params. The README claims 'peak memory unchanged' but that may only hold because the measured workloads had headroom.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak device memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) SDMA off vs on for a model sized near HBM limit.

findings.claim: _SdmaWork.wait() only issues current_stream().wait_event(event); the SDMA copy is enqueued on the *current* (compute) stream via stream.cuda_stream = get_accelerator().current_stream(). In ZeRO-3, allgather prefetch is meant to run on a separate comm stream so it overlaps with compute. By enqueuing the mori SDMA op on the current/compute stream, the collective is serialized against compute on that stream, defeating the prefetch overlap the coordinator relies on. If the caller's expectation is that the op runs on a dedicated stream, this can *reduce* effective overlap and slow the step. (This is consistent with the README caveat that gains 'invert when allgather can't be overlapped'.)
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile stream timeline (rocprof) to confirm SDMA op lands on comm stream vs compute stream during ZeRO-3 prefetch.

findings.claim: all_gather_into_tensor now performs an extra Python-level module import (`from . import mori as _mori`) and a `_mori.allgather_into_tensor` call with several attribute lookups and env-independent branch checks on every single allgather, even when SDMA is disabled/unavailable. For workloads with many small allgathers per step this adds measurable Python overhead on the hot collective path. The import is cached by Python so cheap, but the `supports()` checks (numel comparison, dtype dict lookup) run per call unconditionally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark all_gather_into_tensor call rate with SDMA disabled, before vs after this change.

findings.claim: allgather_into_tensor uses input_tensor.data_ptr()/output_tensor.data_ptr() and records an event on the current stream, but does not appear to synchronize against or record_stream the transit buffers or user tensors across streams. If the mori handle copies into an internal transit buffer that is reused by the next call before the previous SDMA copy completes (only a stream event is recorded, wait() is non-blocking), overlapping back-to-back allgathers into the same shared transit buffer could corrupt data or force implicit serialization. Substantiation is limited without mori internals.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stress test many concurrent/back-to-back WORLD allgathers with SDMA on and validate output correctness against RCCL.

findings.claim: In `_build_flat_partition_grad_views`, the flat buffer is created with `torch.zeros(partition_size)` (a fresh zeroed device allocation) on every gradient accumulation boundary, and then `updateGradBuffer` in C++ (`csrc/includes/deepcompile.h`) zeros it *again* via `grad_buffer.zero_()` for each per-parameter view. The full flat partition is therefore zeroed twice per step (once by torch.zeros, and once more piecewise via each view's zero_()), which is redundant work proportional to the total gradient partition size on every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step optimizer/backward-prologue time for ZeRO-1 DeepCompile with a large model (e.g. Qwen3-8B) comparing double-zeroing vs single-zeroing of the flat grad buffer.

findings.claim: The previous ZeRO-1 path allocated the flat gradient buffers once at compile init and reused them across steps. The new `set_z1_grad_buffer` path allocates a brand-new `torch.zeros(partition_size)` flat buffer per group on every gradient-accumulation boundary and releases it after the step. This trades reduced peak memory (the stated goal) for repeated per-step device allocation/free of buffers the size of the gradient partition, which can add allocator/CUDA-caching pressure and per-step latency, especially with many param groups.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare step 10-30 average time and allocator fragmentation between reused-buffer and per-step-allocated buffer for ZeRO-1 DeepCompile.

findings.claim: `set_z1_grad_buffer` now iterates over all param groups and all params in each group every gradient-accumulation boundary (building views and calling `dc.update_param_grad_buffer` per registered param, plus `release_grad_buffer` iterating again). Previously this was a single `copy.copy(grad_buffer)`. For models with many parameters this adds O(num_params) Python-level and pybind11 crossing overhead per step in the backward prologue.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile deepcompile_backward_prologue wall time as a function of parameter count for ZeRO-1.

findings.claim: In the WITH_GEMM_SWIZZLED_SCALES columnwise-scale store path, the previously vectorized store (a single `ScalesVec::store_to` of SCALES_PER_CHUNK_Y=8 contiguous bytes on the fast path) is replaced with a scalar loop that, for each of up to 8 scales, calls `swizzle::gemm_swizzled_scale_idx` (which performs multiple integer divisions/modulos) and issues a 1-byte scattered global store to a non-contiguous swizzled offset. This turns one coalesced vector store into 8 uncoalesced single-byte stores plus per-element index arithmetic, increasing store transactions and instruction count for the columnwise scale write. Because this is the default cached-weight quantize path (the whole point of the change), the extra store traffic runs on every quantize invocation for eligible shapes. Note this trades against removing a separate swizzle kernel launch, so net effect on the fused path is likely positive; the concern is purely the in-kernel store efficiency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark quantize_transpose_nvfp4_2D_kernel with WITH_GEMM_SWIZZLED_SCALES true vs false (columnwise + rowwise) on 128-aligned shapes (e.g. 8192x5120, 16384x8192), measuring kernel time and global store transactions via ncu (l1tex__t_sectors on stores).

findings.claim: gemm_swizzled_scale_idx performs several 64-bit integer divisions and modulos (row_idx/128, row_idx%128, rem/32, rem%32, col_idx/4, col_idx%4, and a DIVUP for cbg_cnt). Integer division is expensive on GPUs (multi-instruction). In the columnwise store this helper is invoked once per emitted scale inside a loop (up to 8x per thread), and once per rowwise scale write, adding non-trivial ALU cost to the scale-store phase. Since the divisors are compile-time constants (128, 32, 4), the compiler will likely lower them to multiply-shift, mitigating cost, but the per-scale recomputation of cbg_cnt and the full index each iteration is redundant work compared to computing a base offset once.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ncu on the 2D swizzled kernel measuring integer-op throughput / instruction count in the scale-store region vs the non-swizzled path.

findings.claim: This commit swaps the avg_m/avg_n/avg_k derivation for cuBLASLt grouped-GEMM algorithm-selection heuristics (avg_m now = compute_avg_last_dim(outputD) instead of first_dim, avg_n = first_dim, and avg_k uses transa ? last : first). These averaged dimensions are passed to cuBLASLt's CUBLASLT_MATMUL_PREF_GROUPED_DESC_D_AVERAGE_ROWS/COLS/K preference so cuBLAS can pick an appropriate kernel/algorithm. If the previous mapping was in fact the correct one (m<->rows, n<->cols in the same convention as the rest of the setup code), then this change would feed cuBLAS transposed m/n hints for non-square problems (M != N), causing it to select a suboptimal grouped-GEMM kernel and reducing GEMM throughput. Conversely, the commit message claims this is a heuristics *fix*; if so, the prior code was the regression. Because the correctness of the column-major convention cannot be fully verified from the diff alone (need to confirm cuBLAS treats D as column-major so rows=last-dim), this is flagged as a lower-confidence performance concern to validate with a grouped-GEMM throughput benchmark across rectangular shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped GEMM (nvte_grouped_gemm and _with_discrete_out/inputA) throughput on rectangular shapes where M != N (e.g. MoE expert dims), measuring cuBLASLt algorithm selection / TFLOPs before vs after.

findings.claim: When backward_override == 'dequantized', the change now forces save_original_input = False. In the forward path (the non-all-gather branch, ~line 388), columnwise quantized input caching is already suppressed whenever backward_override is not None ('... and backward_override is None'), so this override does not add a columnwise-quantize kernel. The main effect is that the (possibly bf16) original input is no longer saved; instead a rowwise-only quantized input is saved for backward. This is intended to reduce the saved-activation memory footprint relative to save_original_input=True (which caches the full-precision input). Net effect should be neutral-to-positive on memory; no throughput regression is evident. Flagging only because forcing save_original_input=False silently overrides an explicit user setting, which could surprise callers relying on high-precision saved input for backward accuracy, but that is a correctness/behavior concern rather than performance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak activation memory and forward+backward latency for te.Linear / te.GroupedLinear with an FP8/MXFP8 recipe using backward_override='dequantized', comparing save_original_input=True vs the new forced-False path.

findings.claim: destroy_ub() now unconditionally calls torch.compiler.reset(), which flushes ALL compiled graphs/caches in the process (not just those depending on get_ub_is_fp8). If an application destroys and re-initializes UB communicators between phases (e.g. switching TP configs, or shutting down comm+GEMM overlap while keeping other torch.compiled modules alive), every compiled function must be re-traced/re-compiled from scratch on the next call. torch.compile recompilation can cost seconds to minutes, causing a large one-time latency spike each time destroy_ub is invoked. Since destroy_ub is not on a hot path this is a suggestion, but the reset is broader than necessary (a targeted invalidation would suffice).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile a model with torch.compile, run a step, call destroy_ub(), then run another step; measure the recompilation stall on the second step vs. no destroy_ub call.

findings.claim: The new clear_tensor_data(grad_output) for column-parallel SP FP8 backward frees the quantized grad_output earlier, which reduces peak memory in column-parallel SP FP8 backward. This is a memory improvement, not a regression, provided grad_output is not read after this point. Verified from the diff that clear_tensor_data(grad_output) sits at the end of the wgrad block (after the wgrad GEMM has consumed grad_output), so there is no correctness/perf hazard from premature clearing. No action needed; flagged only to note the change is net-positive for memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory during backward for a column-parallel sequence-parallel FP8 Linear before/after the change.

findings.claim: The new Quantizer.__eq__/__hash__ compute _value_key() for value quantizers by iterating _value_field_names and doing a getattr per field (plus _check_value_has_no_process_group and building a tuple of (name, value) items) on every call. As the commit message notes, these run per compiled-function invocation via torch.compile's EQUALS_MATCH guard, whereas previously quantizers used the trivial object.__hash__/identity comparison. This adds a small Python-level per-guard-eval cost for every value quantizer (MXFP8/blockwise/current-scaling/NVFP4) used with torch.compile. It is inherent to enabling value semantics (which in return avoids recompilation), so it is a minor tradeoff, but the per-call tuple construction could be cached/precomputed since the value fields are static.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark hash()/__eq__ on a value quantizer in a tight loop, and measure torch.compile steady-state guard-evaluation time for a Linear layer using an MXFP8/NVFP4 quantizer versus the parent commit.

findings.claim: NVFP4Quantizer.copy() previously copied the rht_matrix tensor by reference (quantizer.rht_matrix = self.rht_matrix), which was essentially free. It now passes with_random_sign_mask into the constructor, which calls _rebuild_derived_state() -> get_rht_matrix(bool, device). get_rht_matrix is lru_cached so this is a cache hit (hashing (bool,int) args), but it is strictly more work than the prior reference copy. copy() is invoked in update_quantized whenever a dst carries an amax_reduction_group (e.g. FSDP2 weight all-gather), so this adds marginal per-copy overhead on that path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark NVFP4Quantizer.copy() in a loop before/after the change; and measure the FSDP2 weight-gather path (update_quantized with a non-None amax_reduction_group) end-to-end.

findings.claim: The 1D and 2D columnwise dequantize kernels (group_dequantize_blockwise_1d_cw_kernel and _2d_cw_kernel) read the transposed (K, M_t) input with a fully scalarized loop: `in_vec.data.elt[e] = input_base[expert_data_off + (c + e) * info.tensor_M + r_local]` for each of kVec=16 elements. Each element is a separate global load with stride tensor_M (columns are the fast-varying quantize layout but here they are strided), so a warp's 16 loads are uncoalesced and cannot vectorize, in contrast with the rowwise kernels which use a single Vec<16> load_from(). This is inherent to reading a column-major buffer along rows, but it does mean the columnwise dequant path is materially more memory-bandwidth-bound than the rowwise path. If a rowwise variant of the data is also available, dequantizing from it would be faster.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare grouped FP8 1D/2D block-scaling dequantize throughput for rowwise vs columnwise data on Hopper across K in {128,256,512} and varying M.

findings.claim: All four dequantize kernels launch kThreadsPerBlock threads but use only kThreadsPerRow=8 threads per row with kVec=16, so a 128-column tile is covered by 8 threads * 16 = 128 columns. With kThreadsPerBlock (typically 256) threads, 32 rows are processed per iteration over kIters=4 iterations. When K==128 (blocks_X==1) this is fine, but the fixed 8-threads/row/16-vec scheme is not tuned to K; for the columnwise scalarized path in particular the 16 strided scalar loads per thread serialize memory traffic that a wider thread mapping (fewer elements per thread) could parallelize.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep thread-per-row / vec-width mapping for the columnwise dequant kernel and measure achieved DRAM bandwidth.

findings.claim: group_dequantize_blockwise_2d_cw_kernel invokes compute_2d_cw_expert_offset (a CTA-cooperative warp-shuffle prefix sum with a __shared__ warp_offset_partials[kNumWarps] and an implied __syncthreads()) on every tile for the VARYING_FIRST_DIM case. Because the grid dimension is (blocks_X, total_row_blocks), this cooperative prefix-sum over all preceding experts is recomputed by every CTA on the y-axis rather than once per expert, adding a per-tile synchronization/reduction cost that scales with num_tensors. For large expert counts this is repeated redundant work across the many CTAs mapping to the same expert.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark 2D columnwise grouped dequantize with VARYING_FIRST_DIM and large num_tensors (e.g. 64-256 experts) vs SAME_BOTH_DIMS closed-form path.

findings.claim: The bwd dispatcher routes NVTE_BLOCK_SCALING_1D/2D dbias through group_quantize_blockwise_{1d,2d} with a two-call workspace-sizing protocol (first call returns workspace size, second does the work) mirroring MXFP8. If the caller does not cache/reuse the workspace between the sizing call and the compute call, the extra allocation and the in-kernel per-tile column-partial dbias plus grouped_reduce_dbias reduction add launch overhead on the backward path; ensure the workspace is allocated once and reused rather than reallocated each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile grouped-linear backward with FP8 block scaling + bias to confirm workspace is reused and the dbias reduction launch overhead is amortized.

findings.claim: The new THD zero-fill in FusedAttnFunc.backward uses `torch.arange(...) >= cu_seqlens_*_padded[-1]` followed by boolean-mask assignment `dq[q_pad_mask] = 0`. This runs unconditionally on every backward pass (both eager and capture), and boolean-mask scatter is substantially more expensive than the previous contiguous slice-fill (`dq[boundary:].fill_(0)`): it allocates a full-length int64 arange plus a full-length bool mask for each of dQ/dK/dV, and performs a masked scatter over the entire tensor length rather than zeroing a contiguous tail. For long THD sequences this adds extra device allocations and a full-tensor pass three times per backward step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FusedAttnFunc backward with qkv_format='thd', large total token count, compare wall-time/allocations of arange+mask assignment vs slice fill_(0).

findings.claim: The pad_between_seqs auto-detect semantics changed. Previously it compared padded vs unpadded cu_seqlens content with torch.equal and returned False when they were numerically equal (no inter-sequence padding). Now, unless cu_seqlens_q_padded IS the same object as cu_seqlens_q, it sets pad_between_seqs=True whenever any padded cu_seqlens tensor is supplied. Many callers pass a distinct-but-numerically-equal padded tensor; those cases now flip to pad_between_seqs=True, which enables the slower padding-aware attention path and can disable/deprioritize FlashAttention v2/v4 and the unfused backend (see utils.py disabling logic for pad_between_seqs), degrading throughput for a common THD case that previously used the fast path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: THD forward with cu_seqlens_*_padded provided as separate tensors equal in content to cu_seqlens_*; compare selected backend and step time before/after.

findings.claim: The added THD zero-fill in the CP backward (AttnFuncWithCPAndKVP2P) uses arange+boolean-mask assignment for dQ/dK/dV even in the non-capture (eager) path (the second block at ~2923 runs unconditionally for qkv_format=='thd', with no is_graph_capturing guard, unlike the earlier block). This replaces the cheap slice `x[boundary:].fill_(0)` with a full-length arange + bool mask + masked scatter for each tensor on every CP backward step, adding device allocations and a full-tensor pass instead of zeroing only the padded tail.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CP (KV P2P) backward with qkv_format='thd' in eager mode; compare arange+mask fill vs slice fill_(0) timing and memory.

findings.claim: The graph-safe aux-loss forward kernel recomputes C_coeff (with two float divisions) in every reduction-lane of every CTA (grid_size = 2*SM count) rather than folding it once on the host as the original path does. This is negligible relative to the reduction but is redundant per-CTA arithmetic; only needed once per block. Minor.

The new 'Step 2b' amax-only pass for columnwise-only 2D mode re-runs the full load-from-shared, local-amax, and 2D warp/smem reduction (num_iterations=4 iterations with two __syncthreads() per iteration) purely to repopulate amax_smem. Because it deliberately mirrors Step 2's amax reduction rather than merging it, the columnwise-only path does the same amount of amax-reduction work that Step 2 already does in the both-directions path, plus extra __syncthreads() barriers. This makes the columnwise-only kernel nearly as expensive as the both-directions kernel while producing only half the outputs — i.e. the '(rowwise=False, columnwise=True, 2D)' case is not meaningfully cheaper than '(rowwise=True, columnwise=True)'. This is a new path (previously it errored out), so it is not a regression, but the added redundant reduction/sync work is a missed optimization. Confirm with a benchmark comparing columnwise-only vs both-directions 2D throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark quantize_transpose_vector_blockwise_fp4 with 2D scaling for (rowwise=False, columnwise=True) vs (rowwise=True, columnwise=True) across large M,N (e.g. 4096x4096, non-bf16 or non-32-aligned to hit this fallback), measuring kernel time; the columnwise-only case ideally should be faster but the amax-only pass largely negates that.

findings.claim: Adding the RETURN_ROWWISE template parameter to the 2D kernel dispatch doubles the number of instantiations of quantize_transpose_nvfp4_2D_kernel (across the RETURN_ROWWISE x RETURN_TRANSPOSE x other switch dimensions). This does not affect runtime per-kernel performance but increases compiled code size / register pressure risk and compile time; no measurable runtime degradation expected on the existing both-directions/rowwise paths, which keep RETURN_ROWWISE=true.

For MLA (head_dim_qk != head_dim_v) with FlashAttention 2, _pad_qkv_head_dim now uses F.pad on query/key (when qk<v) or value (when v<qk) on every forward call. F.pad materializes new, larger contiguous tensors for Q, K, and V (or V alone) each step, adding both extra device memory allocation and a memory-bandwidth-bound copy proportional to the full QKV tensor size (batch*seqlen*heads*head_dim). This is a per-iteration overhead that previously did not exist (MLA fell back to fused attention without this padding). For large sequence/THD workloads the copies of Q/K/V can be significant added HBM traffic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FA2 MLA THD attention forward+backward throughput with head_dim_qk=192, head_dim_v=128 vs the previous fused-attention path, measuring per-iter latency and peak memory.

findings.claim: Padding Q/K up to max(head_dim_qk, head_dim_v) (or V up) increases the effective head dimension processed by the FlashAttention kernel with zeros. The attention matmuls (QK^T and PV) now run at the padded head_dim rather than the true head_dim, so the kernel does extra FLOPs on zero-padded lanes. When the head-dim gap is large (e.g. 128 vs 192) this is ~50% more compute in the affected dimension for no useful result, and may also cross the >192 head-dim threshold that restricts FA2 to specific SM archs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare FA2 kernel time for head_dim 128 vs padded-to-192 on the target GPU.

findings.claim: _trim_output reshapes and slices the FA2 output (attn_out[..., :orig_head_dim_v]) when qk>v. The slice is non-contiguous, and the trailing .reshape(*out_shape, -1) forces a contiguous copy of the whole output tensor on every forward, adding an extra output-sized memory copy each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure attention forward latency with qk>v (e.g. qk=192,v=128) to isolate the reshape/slice copy cost.

findings.claim: The new NVFP4 wgrad path adds per-backward-call host-side tensor ops: two _nvfp4_amax(...).to(torch.float32) / global_scale_denom computations (one for grouped_dy, one for grouped_x) per FC (FC1 and FC2), i.e. 4 small GPU ops plus dtype casts and a scalar division each grouped-MLP backward. These operate on tiny per-group amax tensors so overhead is small, but they are launched every step and were not present before (the prior code skipped the cuDNN wgrad kernel entirely for NVFP4). Net effect is still expected to be a win since it replaces a slower fallback GEMM.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark grouped-MLP backward wall time with NVFP4 recipe (num_groups experts) comparing the cuDNN fused wgrad path vs. the previous _nvfp4_single_group_wgrad_gemm / general_grouped_gemm_for_grouped_tensor fallback.

findings.claim: For the zero-token NVFP4 case, two torch.zeros((num_groups,), float32) tensors are freshly allocated per call for global_scale_a/global_scale_b instead of reusing a cached buffer. This is a small, rare-path allocation but occurs each time a wgrad runs with total_tokens == 0.

The new forward launchers now call CUDA runtime query APIs on every kernel launch to size the persistent grid and pick buffer count. compute_persistent_grid() calls cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor + cudaGetDevice + cudaDeviceGetAttribute, and choose_num_buffers() calls cudaGetDevice + cudaDeviceGetAttribute. cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor in particular is not free and these queries are re-executed for every router forward call (per training step). The parent code computed grid_size with a pure arithmetic expression and had zero host-side device queries. For small problem sizes the router kernel itself is very short, so this added per-launch host latency (multiple driver round-trips) can become a measurable fraction of the total call cost and is not amortized/cached across launches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated fused_score_for_moe_aux_loss_forward / fused_topk forward launches on small num_tokens/num_experts, measuring end-to-end wall time (host+device) vs the parent commit; isolate host overhead with an empty-kernel launch loop that reuses the same occupancy/attribute results.

findings.claim: cp_async_wait_all() is implemented as __pipeline_wait_prior(0), which drains ALL outstanding cp.async groups. In the double-buffered forward loop the prefetch for round+gridDim.x is committed as its own group right after wait(); on the next iteration the wait(0) blocks on every committed group, not just the one needed for the current buffer. This limits the compute/copy overlap the double-buffering scheme is intended to provide, so the achieved latency hiding may be smaller than expected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the optimized forward kernel with Nsight Compute to compare stall-on-memory-barrier cycles vs a variant using __pipeline_wait_prior(1); measure kernel duration at large num_experts.

findings.claim: In the simple aux_loss forward kernel the intermediate_output '-inf' initialization was refactored into an unconditional loop over num_experts with an 'if (score_function == 1)' test inside the loop body. For sigmoid/sqrtsoftplus (score_function 0/2) the warp now iterates the full num_experts stride loop only to evaluate a false predicate every element, whereas the parent guarded the entire loop with the branch and skipped it entirely. No extra global writes occur, but it adds a wasted strided loop over num_experts per token for the non-softmax score functions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark simple aux_loss forward path with score_function=sigmoid at large num_experts and compare against hoisting the score_function==1 test outside the loop.

findings.claim: The persistent grid caps grid size at num_sms * blocks_per_sm. If total_blocks (ceil(num_tokens / tokens_per_block)) slightly exceeds max_resident, the persistent loop serializes extra rounds on already-resident blocks. When num_tokens is only modestly larger than the resident-block capacity this creates a tail/wave-quantization effect where the last partial wave of work runs on a subset of blocks while others idle, which can be slower than the parent's 1-shot grid that launched exactly total_blocks and let the scheduler balance waves.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep num_tokens around the max_resident boundary and compare kernel duration of persistent-grid vs one-shot-grid launches.

findings.claim: In the new nvfp4_group_quantize_with_amax path, the amax all-reduce (allreduce_nvfp4_amax_tensors) is issued *before* group_quantize_nvfp4_impl runs the RHT cast fusion. In the pre-existing quantize_impl path the reduction happens after the quantize kernels are enqueued. Issuing the collective earlier is fine only if the amax buffers already hold the final per-group values (they are the precomputed amaxes). This is likely correct, but note that allreduce_coalesced(...)->wait() blocks on the collective's completion inside NVTE_SCOPED_GIL_RELEASE; if this path is hit on the hot per-layer forward path, each grouped quantize now incurs a synchronous coalesced all-reduce whose latency is exposed on the critical path rather than being overlapped with subsequent GEMM work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark nvfp4_group_quantize_with_amax with with_amax_reduction=True across N ranks; measure end-to-end forward latency vs. the previous fused-quantize+reduce ordering to confirm the earlier collective does not stall the pipeline.

findings.claim: group_quantize_nvfp4_impl allocates a fresh 1-element int32 tile-scheduler workspace tensor (at::empty({1}, kInt32)) on every call. With the new compute_amax=false path, nvfp4_group_quantize_with_amax invokes group_quantize_nvfp4_impl per grouped-quantize call, so this per-call device allocation persists on the hot path. Minor, but a cached/reused workspace would avoid repeated allocator calls per forward step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocator activity (torch.cuda memory stats / nvtx) across many forward iterations of the grouped MLP to quantify overhead of the per-call int32 workspace allocation.

findings.claim: In _group_quantize_with_amax_for_grouped_mlp the single-group NVFP4 path calls rowwise_amax.view(-1)[:1] and columnwise_amax.view(-1)[:1], each creating small tensor views/slices per forward call. This is negligible but adds a few Python/ATen ops per invocation on the hot path compared to passing the tensors directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure Python-side overhead of _group_quantize_with_amax_for_grouped_mlp for num_groups==1 vs baseline over many iterations.

findings.claim: In the NVFP4 SReLU backward path, the FC2 input for wgrad is now recomputed by re-quantizing a BF16 tensor at runtime. Previously (MXFP8 path) the DSReLU kernel returned the columnwise FP4 data and its column scale directly (sfd_col_d_srelu_tensor), and grouped_fc2_x was built by cheap views/permutes over that kernel output. The new NVFP4 branch instead materializes fc2_x_bf16 via .view(...).contiguous() (a full BF16 copy of the recomputed FC2 input) and then invokes _group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp, which runs a full grouped quantization kernel (rowwise=False, columnwise=True) over all tokens x fc2_in_features each backward step. This adds an extra device-side quantization pass plus a contiguous copy per backward call that the MXFP8 path avoids.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward pass of a grouped MLP with ScaledSReLU under nvfp4 recipe (recompute_input_from_dsrelu=True), measuring per-step backward latency and peak memory vs. the mxfp8 path; profile the added group_quantize kernel and the .contiguous() copy.

findings.claim: The .contiguous() on fc2_x_bf16 = d_srelu_tensor.view(...).contiguous() allocates a fresh BF16 buffer of shape (total_tokens, fc2_in_features) each backward pass in the NVFP4 SReLU path. If d_srelu_tensor is already contiguous this is a no-op, but when it is not, this is an additional transient allocation proportional to the activation size that can raise peak memory during backward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory in grouped-MLP SReLU nvfp4 backward with large token counts, comparing with and without the contiguous copy.

findings.claim: The commit message states the PyTorch splits_to_offsets_multi wrapper 'drops non_blocking=true on the host->device migration' when coercing split_sizes to int64/CUDA. If split_sizes originate on CPU (or need dtype coercion), the resulting synchronous cudaMemcpy blocks the host thread on the copy each forward call, inserting a CPU/GPU sync point in the grouped-MLP hot path and serializing CPU work with the GPU. In the benchmark path split_sizes is already CUDA int64 so this is a noop, but general/CPU callers pay a per-step blocking copy. This is a deliberate correctness-vs-throughput tradeoff worth flagging.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark forward_grouped_mlp with CPU-resident m_splits and measure per-step host stall vs. the non_blocking=true variant.

findings.claim: The shared prefix-scan kernel is launched with a single block (<<<1, kThreadsPerBlock>>>) and 256 threads. For large num_splits (num experts) the scan is serialized over ceil(num_splits/256) chunks on one SM, leaving the rest of the GPU idle. This matches the pre-existing behavior (the old splits_to_offsets_kernel was also single-block), so it is not a regression, but the metadata prep remains single-block. For typical num_groups (<=256 experts) this is a single chunk and negligible, so impact is low.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Vary num_splits (e.g. up to a few thousand) and measure splits_to_offsets kernel time vs a multi-block scan.

findings.claim: nvte_splits_to_offsets_multi caps outputs per launch at KernelArgs::kMaxNumOutputs=8 and loops launching a fresh kernel per chunk of 8 outputs, each re-reading all split_sizes and re-computing the full prefix scan. When num_outputs>8, split_sizes are scanned redundantly once per chunk (extra global loads + redundant scan work + extra launch). Typical grouped-MLP uses only 2 outputs so this is not hit in the hot path, but heavy general callers with many outputs incur redundant scans.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call splits_to_offsets_multi with num_outputs > 8 and compare against a single-launch design that reuses the scan.

findings.claim: load_split_size/store_output now perform a per-element switch on DType inside the inner loop of the scan kernel. For int32/int64 the branch is predictable, but the added default-case NVTE_DEVICE_ERROR path and per-element dispatch are marginally more work than the old hardcoded int64 path. Negligible for small metadata arrays.

The new code computes FA4 head-dim alignment via `_fa4_alignment = 16 // torch.empty(0, dtype=qkv_dtype).element_size()`, which allocates a (zero-element) torch tensor purely to read the dtype's element size. This could be done without allocation, e.g. `torch.tensor([], dtype=qkv_dtype).element_size()` is equally wasteful — a plain dtype-to-itemsize lookup (e.g. via `torch.finfo`/a static dict) would avoid the allocation. However, this runs inside get_attention_backend, which is cached and only re-executed when attention_params change (shape/dtype changes), not per forward step, so the impact is negligible in steady-state training/inference.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark get_attention_backend() invocation cost before/after with varying head dims; verify it is not called per-step in a steady training loop.

findings.claim: For SM100 with head_dim_qk == head_dim_v == 256 and cross-attention (max_seqlen_q != max_seqlen_kv), FA4 is now explicitly disabled and the workload falls back to another backend (e.g. cuDNN fused or unfused attention). If the fallback backend is materially slower than FA4's dedicated hd256 kernel for such shapes, cross-attention with head_dim=256 on SM100 will run slower than it would with FA4. This is an intentional correctness workaround (dedicated hd256 kernel diverges from reference for sq=1/decode-like shapes), so the slowdown is a deliberate trade-off rather than a bug, but users of hd256 cross-attention on SM100 may see reduced throughput compared to the self-attention path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare hd256 cross-attention (sq != skv) throughput on SM100 between FA4 (if forced) and the actual fallback backend selected after this change.

findings.claim: In the NVFP4 backward path, the FC2 dgrad kernel emits a bf16 `d_row_tensor` which is then materialized with `.contiguous()` (fc1_dy_bf16 = ...view(...).contiguous()) and subsequently re-quantized to NVFP4 via `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp`. The MXFP8 path avoids this by directly reusing the already-quantized `d_row/d_col` tensors from the kernel output. This introduces (a) a full bf16 allocation + copy of the FC1 grad-output of shape (total_tokens, fc1_out_features) and (b) an additional quantize pass over that tensor per backward step, increasing peak memory and HBM bandwidth relative to the MXFP8 path. It appears required by the NVFP4 grouped-quantize design, but is a measurable per-step overhead for large token counts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fused grouped MLP backward with NVFP4 recipe vs MXFP8 recipe at large total_tokens (e.g. 16384) x fc1_out_features; measure backward wall-time and peak memory.

findings.claim: `get_quantizer_roles` builds Python lists proportional to `num_groups` (forward appends 2 QuantizerRole objects per group; backward one per group) on every invocation. For MoE configs with many experts this is a per-call O(num_groups) Python allocation. Minor, but if invoked per step it adds host-side overhead; consider caching since the roles are static for a given op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark get_quantizer_roles for num_groups in {8,64,256} and measure per-call latency.

findings.claim: `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp` special-cases single-tensor NVFP4 by calling `tex.quantize` then, if `optimize_for_gemm`, unconditionally calling `tex.swizzle_scales_for_gemm_` even when the tensor may already carry swizzled scales; combined with the several `.reshape(-1)` calls on rowwise/columnwise/scale data these produce extra kernels/views per call. For num_groups==1 NVFP4 this is invoked in both the forward re-quant and the backward fc1_dy re-quant, so the swizzle cost recurs each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile _group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp for a single NVFP4 group and count swizzle/quantize kernel launches per call.

findings.claim: In the BYTEMAP (default) path of both fused_topk_with_score_function_forward_kernel and fused_score_for_moe_aux_loss_forward_kernel, the per-token buffer-clear loop that previously cleared probs/routing_map/intermediate_output in a single strided loop over num_experts has been split into two separate strided loops over num_experts (one for probs/intermediate_output, a second `if constexpr (!kIsBitmap)` loop just for routing_map). This adds an extra loop setup/iteration pass over num_experts per token per round for every existing caller (all callers route through the deprecated V1 entry point -> BYTEMAP). The total global-store bytes are unchanged, but the extra loop adds instruction/loop overhead in a hot per-token kernel; effect is small but nonzero.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fused_topk_with_score_function_fwd (BYTEMAP) over a range of num_tokens x num_experts (e.g. 8192x128, 8192x256) before/after this commit, measuring kernel time via nsys/ncu.

findings.claim: On the new BITMAP_U8 path, the final row write-out stores the packed bitmap as individual 1-byte global stores strided across lanes (bitmap_row[j] = local_bitmap_bytes[j] for j = lane_id; j < bitmap_row_bytes). Byte-granularity, lane-strided stores are uncoalesced/narrow relative to the previous 32-bit false/true writes; for large num_experts (many bytes per row) this write pattern is less memory-efficient than a wider store. This affects only the new BITMAP path, not existing callers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare BITMAP_U8 vs BYTEMAP fused router forward kernel time at large num_experts (e.g. 256/512) to quantify the write-out cost.

findings.claim: The BITMAP_U8 path adds a per-warp bitmap accumulator to dynamic shared memory (bitmap_words_per_warp * num_token_per_block * sizeof(uint32_t)). Additional dynamic shared memory raises the per-block smem requirement and can reduce achievable occupancy on the SM for the BITMAP path. Only affects the new format, and the added amount (ceil(E/32) words per warp) is modest.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Use ncu to compare achieved occupancy of the forward kernel between BYTEMAP and BITMAP_U8 formats at high num_experts.

findings.claim: The legacy (non-fused) forward path now does `m_splits = m_splits.tolist()` unconditionally after the new grouped-tensor guard. When m_splits is passed as a CUDA int64 tensor (the new benchmark/graph-safe API materializes it as a device tensor), `.tolist()` forces a device-to-host copy and a full CUDA stream synchronization on every forward. This blocks kernel launch pipelining, hurts CPU-launch overlap, and is incompatible with CUDA Graph capture in the legacy path. Previously m_splits was already a Python list of ints, so no sync occurred.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time GroupedLinear forward with m_splits provided as a CUDA tensor vs Python list, measuring per-iter CPU launch time and any implicit cudaStreamSynchronize (e.g. with NVTX / nsys) with the fused path disabled.

findings.claim: In the grouped-tensor backward, `grad_biases = [dbias_packed[i].to(dtype=ctx.activation_dtype) for i in range(N)]` and the fused-path `grad_biases[i]` / origin-weight loops iterate per-GEMM in Python. For large num_gemms (MoE with many experts) this reintroduces per-expert Python-level tensor ops and small kernel launches, defeating the purpose of the fused grouped GEMM and increasing CPU launch overhead. The non-fused dbias also calls compute_grouped_dbias then splits it per-expert.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark backward of GroupedLinear with fused grouped-GEMM path enabled and use_bias=True, sweeping num_gemms (e.g. 8, 64, 256), measuring CPU launch time for the dbias construction loop.

findings.claim: `_is_grouped_tensor_path_supported` reads `os.getenv("NVTE_GROUPED_LINEAR_USE_FUSED_GROUPED_GEMM", "0")` on every forward call. os.getenv + int + bool conversion on the hot path adds small but nonzero per-iteration Python overhead; it should be cached at import/module init time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark forward CPU overhead with and without caching the env var, high num_gemms and small tensors to isolate Python overhead.

findings.claim: `_make_grouped_bias` does `torch.stack([...]).contiguous()` to pack biases into a new buffer every forward. This allocates and copies bias data each iteration even though biases are typically static parameters; the packed bias buffer could be cached/reused across microbatches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark forward with use_bias=True across many microbatches, measuring allocation and copy overhead from repeated torch.stack of biases.

findings.claim: ScaledSReLU.fuser_forward calls input_.contiguous() unconditionally before maybe_dequantize (and fuser_backward calls x.contiguous() and grad_output.contiguous()), whereas the analogous _ScaledGLU op does NOT force contiguity in its fused forward/backward. If the incoming tensors are already contiguous these calls are cheap, but if they are non-contiguous views this forces an extra full-tensor copy (activation-sized) on every forward and backward invocation of the non-fused fallback path, increasing memory traffic and transient allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark ScaledSReLU eager forward+backward on a large activation tensor passed as a non-contiguous view, comparing against _ScaledGLU which omits the .contiguous() calls.

findings.claim: ScaledSReLU.fuser_backward recomputes tex.srelu(x, None) a second time (the forward already computed it) whenever extra_input_requires_grad is True, in order to form the vecdot for grad_extra_input. This mirrors the _ScaledGLU pattern (trades compute for memory), so it is intentional, but it is an extra activation-sized elementwise kernel launch each backward when the scale input needs a gradient.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare ScaledSReLU backward wall-time with extra_input_requires_grad=True vs. False to quantify the recompute cost.

findings.claim: ScaledSReLU.fuser_forward always saves the full-precision scales tensor via save_for_backward regardless of whether the gradient will be needed, and fuser_backward only clears the input tensor (ctx.saved_tensors[0]) but never clears the saved scales. _ScaledGLU by contrast conditionally saves scales (None when not needed). For ScaledSReLU the saved scales are relatively small (one element per row), so the retained memory is modest, but it is a small unnecessary retention/allocation compared to the sibling op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track peak activation memory across a grouped-MLP training step using ScaledSReLU vs ScaledSwiGLU with matched shapes.

findings.claim: The autotuned-grid fix (grid as callable meta->tuple) was applied to _permute_kernel (permutation.py:589), _unpermute_kernel (:1000), and _sort_chunks_by_map_kernel (:1727), but the analogous lowering for _unpermute_bwd_with_merging_probs_kernel (around permutation.py:1289-1296) still uses a fixed grid = (num_tokens,) and computes block_size = _get_min_block_size(...). If that kernel is autotuned with a BLOCK_SIZE constexpr and its grid should depend on cdiv(hidden_size, BLOCK_SIZE), it retains the exact over-launch bug this PR fixes: every config is dispatched with a grid sized for the smallest BLOCK_SIZE, so larger BLOCK_SIZE configs over-launch by the BLOCK_SIZE ratio (the same ~3x waste described in the commit message for sort_chunks). Verify whether this kernel's grid should be 2D (num_tokens, cdiv(hidden_size, BLOCK_SIZE)); if so it was missed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark unpermute_bwd_with_merging_probs at 524k tokens, hidden=4096, fp32 on GB200; compare wall time before/after converting its grid to a callable(meta)->(num_tokens, cdiv(hidden_size, meta['BLOCK_SIZE'])).

findings.claim: The change lowers the non-autotuned default num_warps from 32 to 4 (utils.py). This is the intended fix (32 warps = 1024 threads/block over-provisions and hurts occupancy, ~8x slowdown per the commit message). However, num_warps=4 is now applied uniformly as the fallback for ANY non-autotuned kernel that previously relied on 32 warps. For memory-bandwidth-bound kernels over wide hidden dims (e.g. large per-block work), 4 warps/block could under-utilize memory-level parallelism relative to a tuned value. This is a global default change affecting all non-autotuned Triton lowerings, not just _make_chunk_sort_map_kernel; worth confirming no other non-autotuned kernel regressed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep num_warps in {4,8,16} for each non-autotuned kernel (_make_chunk_sort_map_kernel, row_id_map pass 1/2/3) across representative token/hidden/expert sizes and confirm 4 is not a regression vs the old 32 for any of them.

findings.claim: grid is now a Python callable evaluated per autotune config inside the lowering loop (config_grid = _normalize_grid(grid_callable(config_constexprs))). This adds per-config Python evaluation at lowering (trace/compile) time, not per-launch, so runtime impact is negligible; noting only for completeness. num_stages 1->3 also increases pipeline depth which raises shared-memory/register pressure per compiled config and could marginally reduce occupancy for kernels whose loops don't benefit from 3-stage pipelining, though it matches Triton defaults.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-kernel occupancy and shared-mem usage with num_stages=1 vs 3 for the autotuned kernels to confirm no occupancy regression from deeper pipelining.

findings.claim: The refactor replaces a positional non_tensor_args tuple and inline ctx.<attr> assignments with two dataclasses (LinearFwdArgs / LinearBwdArgs) that each carry ~40 fields. Constructing and populating these dataclasses on every forward/backward call adds per-call Python object-allocation and attribute-assignment overhead versus the prior tuple/ctx plumbing. This is purely CPU-side host overhead per layer invocation (no GPU work added), so it is only measurable at very small per-layer sizes / high layer counts where launch overhead dominates. Note slots=True mitigates memory but not the per-field assignment cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark a stack of many small Linear layers (e.g. hidden=256, seq=128) measuring forward+backward wall-clock CPU time before vs after the refactor to detect per-call Python overhead.

findings.claim: Positive change: nulling ctx.backward_objects immediately after _linear_backward releases saved tensors, quantizers, weakrefs and main_grad closures earlier under retain_graph=True, reducing peak GPU memory vs relying on ctx GC. No performance regression here; this is an improvement worth confirming.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a retain_graph=True backward loop over a Linear layer and compare torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() before vs after the change.

findings.claim: The old `tex.bulk_allocate(..., [256]*num_gemms)` guaranteed each per-group wgrad output tensor started on a 256-byte boundary. The replacement `torch.empty(num_gemms, *weight_shape)` packs the per-group wgrad matrices contiguously, so tensor `i` starts at byte offset `i * prod(weight_shape) * elementsize`. This is only 256-byte aligned when `prod(weight_shape)*elementsize` is a multiple of 256. For weight shapes whose (out_features*in_features*dtype_bytes) is not a multiple of 256, the wgrad output base pointers for groups i>0 become misaligned, which can force the grouped GEMM (cuBLAS/CUTLASS) onto slower unvectorized code paths (reduced memory throughput / GPU utilization for the wgrad GEMM). The 256-byte alignment was passed explicitly and was presumably chosen to hit the fast kernel path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward-pass wgrad grouped GEMM throughput for GroupedLinear with weight dims where out_features*in_features*dtype_size is not a multiple of 256 (e.g. odd out_features), comparing 256-byte-aligned per-expert buffers vs. contiguous packed buffer.

findings.claim: The bulk_allocate path recorded/handled stream usage via the shared-buffer deleter machinery, whereas the packed `torch.empty` slices are plain views over one allocation. The added comment in allocate.cpp notes stream usage is not recorded and there may be race conditions if compute is performed on multiple streams. If the wgrad GEMMs for these buffers run on a stream other than the allocating stream (e.g. delayed wgrad compute), the caching allocator may reuse/free the buffer prematurely, risking a stall or synchronization overhead if guarded. This is speculative and depends on the streaming model of delayed wgrad.

naive_topk_and_mask now dispatches via a runtime switch into 8 distinct __device__ template instantiations naive_topk_and_mask_smallk<1..8>, all of which are force-inlined into the calling kernels (topk_and_mask is __forceinline__). Because topk is a runtime value, the compiler must emit code for all 8 branches inline in each router kernel. The K=8 variant holds a `selected[K]` register array plus per-element unrolled masking loops, so kernel register usage is set by the worst-case branch. Increased register footprint and code size can lower SM occupancy and add I-cache pressure for router kernels even when the small-k fast path is not taken. Worth checking `-Xptxas -v` register counts before/after.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile the fused_router kernels with ptxas -v before and after; compare registers-per-thread and achieved occupancy for fused_topk_with_score_function_forward_kernel at num_experts=192, topk=8.

findings.claim: warp_reduce_sum_float replaces the previous butterfly all-reduce (5 __shfl_xor_sync ops that leave the full sum in every lane) with a __shfl_down reduction (5 shuffles, sum only in lane 0) followed by an extra __shfl_sync broadcast. That is one additional shuffle instruction per reduction versus the original xor-based code it replaces in the two backward kernels. The cost is tiny (one shuffle per token per backward call) but it is a small net increase, not a speedup, on those paths.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fused_score_for_moe_aux_loss_backward and fused_topk backward for sigmoid score_function with num_experts=128 to compare per-iteration warp-reduce cost of xor all-reduce vs down+broadcast.

findings.claim: The fast-path routing condition was tightened: previously `attn_mask_type.is_causal() and window_size is None` matched all causal masks (including bottom-right causal / PADDING_CAUSAL_BOTTOM_RIGHT). Now `not attn_mask_type.is_bottom_right()` is added, so bottom-right causal masks that previously took the O(T) fast causal path are now routed to the new O(T*max_segments_per_seq) slow path. For bottom-right causal self-attention (s_q==s_kv), this is a regression in per-step metadata compute cost even though the change is motivated by correctness for cross-attention. In practice for THD with CP ring / large max_segments_per_seq this adds one-hot scatter + reductions of shape [B, T, max_segments_per_seq+1] per attention call for a case that used to be O(T).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark seqlen/offset computation for PADDING_CAUSAL_BOTTOM_RIGHT THD self-attention (s_q==s_kv) comparing parent (fast path) vs this commit (slow path) across max_segments_per_seq in {1,4,16,64} and T in {2k,8k}.

findings.claim: The new slow path allocates one-hot tensors of shape [B, T_kv, max_segments_per_seq+1] and [B, T_q, max_segments_per_seq+1] (kv_oh/q_oh) plus masked aggregate copies (kv_agg/q_agg) of the same shape, giving O(T*max_segments_per_seq) intermediate memory and compute. This is the intended improvement over O(T^2), but for large max_segments_per_seq (e.g. thousands of tiny segments) the constant factor of allocating and reducing several such tensors (one-hot bool, where, take_along_axis) may approach or exceed the old mask cost for moderate T. The reduction is done via jnp.max/jnp.min over the T axis, materializing the full [B, T, S+1] tensor before reduction, rather than a fused segment-reduction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak device memory and wall time of _get_seqlens_offsets_thd vs the old mask-based path for T in {1k..16k} and max_segments_per_seq in {8,64,512}, identifying the crossover point where O(T*S) exceeds O(T^2).

findings.claim: In the bottom-right and causal branches, run_length_fill and jax.nn.one_hot are computed for both Q and KV independently even when the slow path is invoked repeatedly per ring-attention step; there is no caching of segment-derived one-hot encodings across the multiple invocations that CP p2p ring performs, so the O(T*max_segments_per_seq) one-hot construction is repeated for each rotated KV step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile a CP p2p ring THD run and count how many times _get_seqlens_offsets_thd (and its one_hot builds) is invoked per attention layer per step; measure fraction of layer time spent in metadata computation.

findings.claim: MMParams.__post_init__ and QParams.__post_init__ now eagerly build and store a `_cached_repr` string on every instance construction, even if the repr is never requested. Recipes such as Float8CurrentScaling and Float8BlockScaling instantiate several QParams/MMParams objects (fp8_quant_fwd_inp/weight/bwd_grad, fp8_gemm_fprop/dgrad/wgrad) at construction time, so each recipe construction now performs several f-string formattings that would otherwise be lazy. Since the pre-change repr was computed lazily (only in __repr__), this shifts formatting cost to construction. Impact is small because recipe/param construction is infrequent, but it is a strict regression versus the lazy pattern used for the Recipe base class in the same commit (which caches lazily via __repr__).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark: time constructing Float8CurrentScaling()/Float8BlockScaling() in a tight loop before vs after; compare per-construction latency.

findings.claim: The new Recipe.__setattr__ intercepts every attribute assignment and performs an extra object.__setattr__(self, '_cached_repr', None) for each non-_cached_repr write. Because the recipe subclasses are (non-frozen) dataclasses, every field assignment during __init__ now incurs two object.__setattr__ calls instead of a normal store, and any later mutation of a recipe attribute pays the same overhead. This is minor (recipes are constructed rarely) but adds constant per-write CPU cost that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark construction of DelayedScaling() in a loop before/after to measure __init__ overhead from the custom __setattr__.

findings.claim: In bulk_allocate_nvfp4_tensors the criterion for setting contiguous_data_and_scale=false was rewritten. The old code set it false whenever alignment rounding actually inserted padding between adjacent tensors in the packed buffer (offset != buffer_size). The new code sets it false whenever product(data_shape)/2 % 256 != 0 or product(scale_shape) % 16 != 0. These are not equivalent: the old test looked at whether the *running offset* was already aligned (which depends on the accumulated sizes of all prior tensors), whereas the new test only inspects each tensor's own size divisibility. For the very common case where per-tensor FP4 data byte size is not a multiple of 256 (which happens whenever a data tensor's byte size isn't 256-aligned even though the first tensor starts at offset 0 and is trivially 'contiguous'), the new code will report contiguous_data_and_scale=false even when the old code would have reported true (e.g. a single tensor, or the first tensor). When contiguous_data_and_scale is false, the downstream caller (cast.cpp ~line 1535) takes a fallback path that re-does work / re-allocates, so this misclassification causes extra allocations/copies per quantization call in the NVFP4 grouped path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark tex.split_quantize / grouped NVFP4 quantize over a range of per-expert token counts, comparing wall time and the fraction of calls hitting the !contiguous_data_and_scale fallback before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: The new generic bulk_allocate always pads the base buffer by an extra base_alignment bytes (up to 256B for FP4/FP8 data) to defensively handle the case where the underlying CUDA allocation is not aligned. The previous per-call implementations did not add this trailing pad. Since the base buffer comes from at::empty (caching allocator), which returns 256B-aligned pointers in practice, this pad is essentially always wasted. For workloads that allocate many small packed buffers (grouped MoE with many experts, called every microbatch), this is a small but systematic per-buffer memory overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak allocator memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) for a grouped-linear forward/backward with many experts, before vs after, to quantify the extra base_alignment padding per buffer.

findings.claim: The refactor to bulk_allocate builds intermediate std::vector<std::vector<size_t>> shapes / dtypes / alignments by copying and inserting the row/columnwise/scale/amax shape lists (shapes = data_shapes; shapes.insert(..scale_shapes..)), then returns a std::vector<at::Tensor> that is split back out. Several split loops use push_back(tensors[i]) (copy of at::Tensor, incrementing refcount) rather than std::move. Compared to the previous code which constructed views directly in place, this adds per-call heap allocations and Tensor refcount churn. This is pure host-side overhead in the quantization hot path (called per grouped GEMM), not a GPU regression, but for large num_tensors (many experts) it adds measurable CPU work per iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU-time profile (e.g. py-spy / nsys nvtx) of tex.split_quantize with large num_tensors comparing host-side time in bulk_allocate_*_tensors before vs after.

findings.claim: The commit message states grouped-linear wgrad buffers are now bulk-allocated and aligned to 256B. Bulk-allocating wgrads into a single buffer is generally a win (one allocation instead of N), but the 256B per-tensor alignment plus the trailing base_alignment pad increases total bytes allocated for the wgrad buffer relative to N tightly-packed weight tensors. For small per-expert weights this padding overhead can be non-trivial as a fraction of the buffer. Confirm the wgrad buffer size increase is acceptable for memory-constrained MoE training.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated during GroupedLinear backward for a range of expert weight sizes before vs after.

findings.claim: The forward launcher now issues three device operations per call instead of one: a cudaMemsetAsync to zero Coeff_buf[1], the main reduction kernel, and a separate convert_accum_to_output<<<1,1>>> kernel. Each launch/memset adds fixed CPU-side launch latency and a stream dependency. For a tiny op like the MoE aux-loss (which runs once per layer per step), the extra ~2 launch latencies can dominate wall-time when the GPU is otherwise idle waiting on the router. The single-thread convert kernel in particular is pure launch overhead to move one float.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fused_moe_aux_loss_forward end-to-end latency (CPU launch + GPU) before/after this commit for a few (num_tokens, num_experts) sizes, e.g. (4096,128), measuring per-call latency with CUDA events and comparing kernel-launch count.

findings.claim: The reduction over rows (tokens) is parallelized only across gridDim.x=sm_count*2 blocks, with a single thread per (block,column) accumulating num_rows/gridDim.x rows serially in the inner loop. When num_experts (num_cols) is small but num_tokens is large, block_size=min(1024,num_cols) leaves most of each block's warps idle and the per-column serial row loop is long, so GPU occupancy/throughput can be worse than the old cluster-based reduction that distributed rows across many warps. The commit targets large-expert counts, so this is mainly a concern for small/medium expert counts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark the forward kernel across a grid of (num_tokens, num_experts): small experts (8,16,64) x large tokens (16k,64k) and compare kernel time vs the pre-change implementation.

findings.claim: probs is read from global memory num_rows*num_cols times in the reduction loop with no shared-memory staging of tokens_per_expert; tokens_per_expert[col] is re-read once per thread which is fine, but the whole probs matrix (num_tokens x num_experts) is streamed once through the reduction. This is inherent to the computation and coalesced (consecutive threads read consecutive col), so no regression versus before, but the kernel is fully memory-bound and its performance is gated by achieving full coalescing/occupancy which depends on num_cols being a multiple of the warp size (already handled by block_size rounding).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile achieved DRAM bandwidth of the forward kernel with Nsight Compute for large (num_tokens, num_experts) to confirm it is memory-bound and coalesced.

findings.claim: In the forward the split_sizes tensor is normalized with `split_sizes.to(dtype=torch.int64)` and `split_sizes.to(device=device)` before dispatch. On the legacy `_fuser_forward_split_quantize` path this tensor is subsequently converted via `[int(s) for s in split_sizes.tolist()]`, which forces a device->host synchronization (`.tolist()`) on every forward. This existed before too, but the added unconditional `split_sizes.to(dtype=torch.int64)` / `.to(device=device)` can introduce an extra H2D/dtype-conversion kernel + copy each iteration when the caller already passes an int tensor on-device (a common case), adding a small per-step overhead. Guarding these conversions with dtype/device equality checks (as the code does for device) is fine, but the dtype cast is unconditional whenever dtype != int64.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark GroupedLinear.fuser_forward on the legacy split-quantize path with an int32 vs int64 on-device split_sizes tensor and measure per-iteration CPU overhead / extra kernel launches.

findings.claim: `fuser_forward` now computes the quantizers/with_quantized_compute state inline, and then `fuser_forward_save_ctx` recomputes `FP8GlobalStateManager.is_fp8_enabled()` and rebuilds all three quantizer lists (input/weight/grad_output) by calling `self.get_quantizer(...)` 3*num_groups times again. This duplicates work already done at the top of `fuser_forward` (which also builds input/weight quantizers). For large num_groups (MoE with many experts) this doubles the per-forward quantizer-lookup overhead on the host critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fuser_forward for GroupedLinear with num_groups=64/128 under FP8 and count get_quantizer calls / measure host-side forward time before vs after.

findings.claim: In `_get_grouped_weight_for_gemm`, the quantized-weight-but-non-quantized-compute branch does a per-group dequantize loop followed by `torch.stack(dequantized, dim=0).contiguous()`, allocating a fresh full-size weight buffer and copying every group each forward. The code itself flags this as 'isnt optimized yet'. When this path is hit (e.g. FP8 params but BF16 compute) it materializes the entire grouped weight every iteration, increasing memory traffic and peak memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark the graph-safe grouped-tensor forward with quantized weights and unquantized (BF16) compute, measuring allocations and time from the dequantize+stack path.

findings.claim: The single_grouped_weight path calls `grouped_fc1_weight.copy()` and `grouped_fc2_weight.copy()` to clone weights before swizzling for the GEMM (to keep the original for save_for_backward). This clones the full FP8 weight buffers of both FC1 and FC2 on every forward pass rather than swizzling in place / caching, adding weight-sized memory traffic and allocations per step. (Pre-existing in this file but on the hot path being extended.)
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the fused grouped MLP forward for allocation volume attributable to grouped_fc*_weight.copy().

findings.claim: backward_dw's fused-MLP GroupedTensor cleanup was changed from `clear_tensor_data(activations.data, ...)` to `clear_tensor_data(activations.rowwise_data, ...)`. If `rowwise_data` can be None while `data` was the freed buffer in some configurations, activation memory may no longer be released promptly, causing memory growth across steps when delaying wgrad compute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run delayed-wgrad grouped MLP for many steps and track peak/steady GPU memory to confirm activations are still freed.
