findings.claim: The new `is_fsdp2` property caches `_is_fsdp2` via `hasattr(self, '_is_fsdp2')`, but the cache attribute is never set on the module until the first access, and — more importantly — for the non-FSDP2 case the first access goes through a try/except that imports `_get_module_fsdp_state` and calls it, catching RuntimeError/ImportError. `_get_module_fsdp_state` (torch FSDP internal) raising a RuntimeError for every non-FSDP2 module means the first forward of every TE module pays an exception-raising cost, and the import (`from ..distributed import _get_module_fsdp_state`) is executed on first access. This is amortized (cached thereafter), so the steady-state cost is small, but if any code path resets/deletes `_is_fsdp2` or creates fresh module instances per step it would re-trigger the import+exception. Confidence moderate because it is cached after the first call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark first-forward latency of a non-FSDP2 te.Linear vs. parent, and steady-state per-step CPU time to confirm the property is only evaluated once.

findings.claim: In FSDP2 mode the forward no longer produces columnwise/transpose data for non-quantized weights (columnwise_usage forced False) and does not save the FP8 workspace (wt_save=None). Backward must therefore re-quantize the weight from the all-gathered high-precision weight (`ctx.weight_quantizer(saved_weight)`), which reintroduces a quantize + transpose kernel per layer per backward that previously could be reused from the saved workspace. This trades memory for extra backward compute; on FSDP2 fp8 paths this is a deliberate memory/throughput tradeoff, but it is a measurable added backward cost vs the parent behavior of reusing the saved workspace.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare backward-pass wall time and quantize-kernel count per layer for an FSDP2 fp8 Linear before/after this change.

findings.claim: For FSDP2 modules, cache_name_fc1/fc2 (and Linear/LayerNormLinear cache_name) are forced to None even when is_first_microbatch is set, disabling the persistent `_fp8_workspaces` weight cache. This means the weight is re-quantized every microbatch instead of being cached across microbatches within an accumulation window, adding one weight-quantization (and its kernels) per microbatch per layer. This is intentional to fix the leak, but it removes an optimization and will increase forward compute for gradient-accumulation / multi-microbatch FSDP2 runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure forward time with is_first_microbatch=True over N microbatches for an FSDP2 LayerNormMLP, comparing quantization kernel counts vs parent.

findings.claim: In the new grouped_swizzle_scaling_variable_shape_kernel, the persistent-grid loop performs an O(num_tensors) linear scan for every linear_block_id it processes to map a block index to (tensor_id, local_block_id). Each block iterates over roughly total_blocks/gridDim work items, and each work item redoes the scan (reading m_array[i]/k_array[i] from device memory and recomputing round_up_to_multiple / DIVUP / vec_load_size). Total scheduling cost across all blocks is ~O(total_blocks * num_tensors), which for large num_tensors (e.g. MoE with many experts) can dominate the actual swizzle work. A prefix-sum / cumulative-block-offset array (computed once, e.g. on host or in shared memory) plus a binary search would reduce this to O(log num_tensors) per work item.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark swizzle_grouped_scaling_factors on variable-shape input with large num_tensors (e.g. 64-256 experts) vs. small tensor sizes, measuring kernel time; compare against uniform-shape path with equivalent total elements.

findings.claim: m_array and k_array are device pointers (input->first_dims.dptr / last_dims.dptr). The kernel repeatedly dereferences these arrays from global memory both in the initial total_blocks computation and in the per-iteration tensor-discovery scan, with no caching in shared memory. For a persistent grid of num_SMs*max_active_blocks blocks each rescanning the arrays, the same shape metadata is re-read from global memory a very large number of times. Loading the shape arrays once into shared memory (or a precomputed cumulative-block table) would avoid this repeated global traffic.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the variable-shape kernel with nsight-compute measuring L2/global load transactions vs. useful swizzle bytes for large num_tensors.

findings.claim: The initial total_blocks reduction runs on only 32 threads (threadIdx.x<32 && threadIdx.y==0) of a TB_DIM*TB_DIM=1024-thread block, while all other threads spin at the following __syncthreads(). For blocks whose useful work is small (few tensors / few tiles), this serial single-warp O(num_tensors) preamble is pure overhead paid by every one of the persistent_blocks blocks, since total_blocks is recomputed independently in each block rather than passed in from the host.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel launch-to-first-useful-work latency for small variable groups; measure impact of precomputing total_blocks on host and passing as a kernel argument.

findings.claim: cudaFuncSetAttribute(...MaxDynamicSharedMemorySize...) for the variable-shape kernel is invoked on every call to swizzle_grouped_scaling_factors (per invocation, not cached like max_active_blocks_per_sm). This adds a synchronous host-side CUDA API call per swizzle launch; for workloads that swizzle grouped scales frequently (every GEMM), this per-call driver overhead is avoidable by caching whether the attribute was already set for the device/kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPU-side launch overhead (e.g. with a tight loop of swizzle_grouped_scaling_factors calls) with and without caching the cudaFuncSetAttribute call.

findings.claim: fsdp_post_all_gather derives columnwise data locally via out._create_columnwise() on every all-gather. _create_columnwise() launches an nvfp4_data_transpose kernel plus an nvfp4_2d_scale_transpose kernel and, when out already has no columnwise buffer allocated, also allocates a fresh torch.empty columnwise_scale_inv. For FSDP2 this executes on every forward unshard (and again on backward unshard for reshard_after_forward=True). The tradeoff of halving all-gather comm volume is reasonable, but the added per-unshard GPU work (two transpose kernels over the full unsharded weight) is on the critical path of the forward/backward and is a measurable added cost vs. the alternative of all-gathering columnwise data directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FSDP2 NVFP4 training step time: measure forward+backward unshard latency with local columnwise derivation (this PR) vs all-gathering columnwise data, across representative weight shapes.

findings.claim: In the out= (in-place update) path of fsdp_post_all_gather, out._rowwise_data and out._rowwise_scale_inv are reassigned to the newly all-gathered buffers rather than copied into the existing pre-allocated buffers. This defeats the purpose of the out= fast-path buffer reuse: the old buffers are dropped and the columnwise buffers may be reallocated by _create_columnwise when shapes are re-derived, causing extra allocation/free churn each iteration instead of reusing FSDP2's persistent unsharded buffers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocator activity (torch.cuda.memory_stats num_alloc_retries / reserved bytes) across many FSDP2 iterations to confirm whether columnwise buffers are reused or reallocated.

findings.claim: fsdp_pre_all_gather slices rowwise_scale_inv[:shard_M, :] to unpad the scale on every pre-all-gather, and fsdp_post_all_gather re-pads via F.pad allocating a new tensor when current_m < target_m. This unpad/repad round trip adds a per-unshard host dispatch plus a padding kernel + allocation, and (per the test comments) introduces small numerical error. Minor relative to the transpose cost but still per-iteration overhead not present before this change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-unshard CPU dispatch time and any extra kernel launches from the scale unpad/pad path.

findings.claim: The newly added per-iteration event record/wait logic serializes the split GEMM chunks against each other. For i>=1 the code does cudaEventRecord(_start_compute, _stream_compute[(i-2)%N]) followed by cudaStreamWaitEvent(_stream_compute[stream_id], _start_compute). This forces the GEMM on chunk i to wait for the completion of the GEMM launched on chunk (i-2) before starting, imposing an explicit ordering/dependency chain between compute streams. Previously the per-chunk GEMMs on the round-robin _stream_compute streams could run concurrently (limited only by SM availability); now they are constrained into a stricter staggered pipeline. On configs where multiple compute streams could overlap GEMM chunks, this reduces achievable GEMM/comm overlap and GPU utilization, increasing end-to-end latency of split_overlap_rs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark CommOverlapP2PBase::split_overlap_rs (reduce-scatter overlap) throughput/latency with num_splits==tp_size on Hopper, comparing before/after this commit, both with and without CUDA graphs, across several tp_size and hidden sizes. Measure per-iteration GEMM overlap via Nsight Systems.

findings.claim: A tiny empty delay kernel (tiny_delay_kern) plus an extra cudaEventRecord is launched once per split_overlap_rs call on _stream_send[0]. Each split_overlap_rs invocation now pays an additional kernel launch + event record. In a non-CUDA-graph path this is a small but nonzero per-call host launch overhead added to every reduce-scatter overlap; it also occupies a stream/scheduler slot. Impact is minor for large GEMMs but measurable for many small RS calls.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark repeated small split_overlap_rs calls (small m/n) outside CUDA graphs and measure host-side launch overhead delta from the extra tiny_delay_kern + cudaEventRecord.

findings.claim: The single _start_compute event is now re-recorded inside the loop (cudaEventRecord(_start_compute, _stream_compute[(i-2)%N])) each iteration and also recorded on _stream_send[0] before the loop. Reusing one event as a rolling dependency marker across iterations means later cudaEventRecord calls can overwrite the event's captured state before earlier waits have executed on the host-launch order, potentially reducing the intended ordering benefit or, in some launch orderings, adding an unnecessary extra sync point. This can create either weaker-than-intended overlap constraints or extra serialization depending on scheduling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile split_overlap_rs with Nsight Systems to verify per-chunk GEMM start/stop dependencies match the intended staggered ordering and that no unintended extra waits are introduced.

findings.claim: Downgrading TritonMLAMetadataBuilder._cudagraph_support from AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH (2) to UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE (1) narrows the set of batches that can be replayed via full CUDA graphs. UNIFORM_BATCH allows full-graph capture/replay for any uniform-query-length batch, including speculative decode where the 'decode' query length is 1+num_speculative_tokens; UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE restricts full CUDA graphs to query_len==1 decodes only. As a result, spec-decode (and any uniform batch with query_len>1) using the TRITON_MLA backend will no longer be dispatched to the full CUDA graph and will instead fall back to eager/piecewise execution, incurring per-step kernel-launch overhead and losing the latency reduction from graph replay. The framework will also downgrade FULL cudagraph_mode to FULL_AND_PIECEWISE/FULL_DECODE_ONLY when this backend is present, meaning uniform spec-decode batches no longer benefit from a captured full graph.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek/MLA model with speculative decoding enabled (num_speculative_tokens>0) on the TRITON_MLA backend and compare decode latency/throughput before vs after this change; also compare with cudagraph_mode=FULL to observe the mode downgrade and per-step launch overhead for uniform (query_len>1) batches.

findings.claim: In FlashAttention.forward, the FA4 dispatch uses `flash_attention_backend > PkgVersion("4.0.0b")` while FA3 was changed to `PkgVersion("3.0.0b") < flash_attention_backend < PkgVersion("4.0.0")`. A version like `4.0.0b8` compares `> PkgVersion("4.0.0b")` as True (since `4.0.0b8 > 4.0.0b0`), so the tested beta correctly hits FA4. But note that `4.0.0b8 < 4.0.0` is also True, so if the FA3 branch were reached first for a 4.x version, mis-dispatch could occur. As written FA4 is checked first, but the boundary logic is fragile; a 4.0.0 stable release would fall through neither `> 4.0.0b` incorrectly nor the FA3 `< 4.0.0` window, correctly hitting FA4. Low direct perf risk but a mis-dispatch would fall back to a slower backend or crash.

On SM90 the code explicitly disables FA4 to prefer FA3. If FA4 is faster than FA3 for some Hopper shapes (forward-only / inference), this hard preference could leave performance on the table. The choice is defensible (FA3 more mature, FA4 SM90 backward has limitations) but it is a blanket disable rather than shape/training-aware, so forward-only inference workloads on SM90 never get FA4 even if it would be faster.

The FA4 forward path wraps the call result with `if isinstance(output, (List, Tuple)): output = output[0]`. If FA4's Python interface returns extra tensors (e.g. softmax_lse) that are needed for a custom backward, discarding them here forces the framework to rely on FA4's own autograd. This is functional but could mean an extra recompute or a suboptimal backward path versus reusing forward artifacts; verify FA4 backward reuses forward LSE internally.

Module import now attempts three separate try/except import blocks for FA2, FA3, and FA4 at import time. Each `get_pkg_version` and `importlib` import adds a small one-time cost. Negligible at runtime but the FA4 import path (`flash_attn.cute.interface`) plus cutlass-dsl can be heavy to import; if FA4 is installed this increases process startup latency. One-time only, not per-iteration.

The new grouped_bias_add kernel sizes its row-block grid as row_blocks = min(max_row_chunks, num_sms*kBlocksPerSM/col_blocks). When n is large enough that col_blocks > num_sms*kBlocksPerSM, integer division yields 0 and the launch collapses to a single row-block per column (std::max(1,...)). Each of those blocks then serially grid-strides over all total_rows/16 row chunks, dramatically reducing occupancy/parallelism relative to the old scheme that scaled blocks with total work. For wide bias-add (large hidden dim) this can substantially under-utilize the GPU.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark grouped_bias_add for a grouped output with large hidden dim (e.g. n>=32768) and many rows, comparing achieved memory bandwidth against a simple element-wise add; watch SM occupancy.

findings.claim: Every block/CTA re-computes valid_rows = sum(first_dims) via a warp reduction over num_tensors at kernel entry, and each block also performs a linear find_tensor_for_row scan (O(num_tensors)) plus per-chunk boundary walking. With up to kMaxGroups=64 groups and a large 2D grid, this per-block redundant scan work is repeated across all blocks. It is small relative to the memory traffic but represents added overhead compared with the old one-block-per-tensor kernel that computed offsets once.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile grouped_bias_add with 64 groups and small per-group row counts to measure the fixed per-block scan overhead vs. total kernel time.

findings.claim: kVec was raised to 8 for <=2-byte dtypes (bf16/fp16). This requires n divisible by 8 (checked) but with kThreads=128 the per-block column coverage is 1024 elements; if n is not a multiple of block_cols=1024 the last column block still launches kThreads threads of which only some pass the col<n guard, wasting a warp fraction. Minor, dtype/shape dependent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare effective bandwidth for n values that are and are not multiples of kThreads*kVec (1024 for bf16).

findings.claim: The commit removes `@torch.compile(fullgraph=True)` from `rearrange_mixed_qkv`. The commit message states this nested torch.compile was causing CUDA graph capture failure. That is a correctness/hang fix (nested compile inside an already-compiled/cudagraph region breaks capture). The tradeoff is a mild potential performance regression: the rearrange split/reshape ops (torch.split + reshape into contiguous q/k/v tensors) will now run as eager PyTorch ops rather than a fused compiled kernel, so per-call CPU dispatch overhead and possible extra intermediate allocations increase slightly on this hot path (called per decode/prefill step, twice per forward for spec + non-spec). This is expected to be small because the parent op `prepare_gdn_attention_core_inputs` remains torch.compiled and the wrapping forward can now be captured into a CUDA graph, which typically nets a win. Net effect is likely positive; the isolated slowdown from de-compiling this tiny function is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3-Next / GDN decode throughput and per-step latency with CUDA graphs enabled, comparing before/after; verify graph capture succeeds and steady-state tokens/s does not regress.

findings.claim: check_shared_memory_capacity_num_experts() calls cudaGetDevice() + cudaDeviceGetAttribute(cudaDevAttrMaxSharedMemoryPerBlockOptin) on every kernel launch. It is now invoked in all four router launchers (topk fwd/bwd and aux-loss fwd/bwd), which run per-router-layer and per-training-iteration. cudaDeviceGetAttribute is a CUDA driver query whose result never changes for a given device; querying it on the launch hot path adds host-side latency to every router forward/backward. The device max shared-memory attribute should be cached (e.g. static/thread_local computed once per device) instead of being re-queried each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark launch latency of fused_topk_with_score_function_forward for small num_tokens (e.g. 2048) over many iterations, comparing parent vs this commit; measure CPU time per launch to isolate the added cudaGetDevice/cudaDeviceGetAttribute cost.

findings.claim: cudaFuncSetAttribute(..., cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize, ...) is now issued on every launch of all four router kernels, whereas the parent code issued none. This is a per-call host-side driver call, and it now runs unconditionally even when the requested dynamic shared memory is below the default 48KB limit (where it is unnecessary). It should be set once per (kernel, size) rather than on every invocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Same launch-latency micro-benchmark as above; measure the incremental CPU cost of the added cudaFuncSetAttribute call per launch when dynamic smem < 48KB.

findings.claim: radix_topk_and_mask performs NUM_PASSES=8 radix passes, and each pass warp-reduces a 16-element histogram via warp_allreduce_sum (16 * 5 = 80 __shfl_xor per pass, ~640 shuffles total), plus two gather passes over E. For moderate expert counts and topk in the 16-32 range this fixed 8-pass overhead can be substantial; if the naive/radix crossover (topk<16) is mistuned for a given num_experts, launches with topk>=16 may run slower than a tuned naive path. Worth validating the crossover empirically across (num_experts, topk).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark radix vs naive topk_and_mask across num_experts in {128,256,1024} and topk in {8,16,24,32} to confirm the topk<16 crossover choice minimizes kernel time in the fused router forward.

findings.claim: This commit removes the fused RMSNorm+router GEMV kernel (dsv4_norm_router_gemm) and the NormGateLinear layer that dispatched to it. In the pre-change hot path, for M<=16 tokens (decode / small-batch regime, the common latency-sensitive case for DSV4-Pro routing), a single fused kernel launch computed both normed_x and router logits in one pass over x. Removing it forces the model onto the unfused path: a separate torch.ops._C.rms_norm launch plus a separate GEMM (dsv3_router_gemm or torch.mm). That is two kernel launches instead of one, plus an extra read of x from global memory (the fused kernel read x exactly once). For a memory-bound GEMV at small M this roughly doubles the launch overhead and DRAM traffic for the routing step at every decode step, degrading decode latency for DSV4-Pro.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark routing step (rms_norm + router_gemm) vs the removed fused dsv4_norm_router_gemm for M=1..16, H=7168, E=384 on SM90+; measure per-step latency and kernel-launch count.

findings.claim: programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed is now hard-coded to 1 instead of gated behind getEnvEnablePDL() (which returned false unless SM>=90 AND TRTLLM_ENABLE_PDL=1). This unconditionally enables Programmatic Dependent Launch for the dsv3 router GEMM regardless of arch/env. If the kernel body does not emit griddepcontrol.wait/launch_dependents on the target arch, or on archs <SM90, enabling PDL serialization semantics could add launch-attribute overhead or alter overlap behavior versus the previous default-off state. Likely a net positive on SM90+ but changes behavior for all users who did not opt in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare dsv3_router_gemm launch latency/throughput with PDL forced on vs the previous env-gated default across SM80/SM90 archs.

findings.claim: The per-iteration KV offset computation was changed from a single fused `kv_loc = kv_page_number * PAGE_SIZE + offs_n % PAGE_SIZE` (one value reused as `kv_loc * stride_buf_kbs`) to a two-term product `kv_page_number * stride_buf_kpbs + kv_in_page * stride_buf_kbs`. This adds an extra integer multiply per BLOCK_N vector in the inner loop of both `_fwd_kernel_stage1` and `_fwd_grouped_kernel_stage1`. The impact is tiny (integer ALU work fully hidden behind the KV-cache global loads that dominate the decode kernel), but it is a strictly larger address computation than before on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark test_decode_attention / triton MLA decode latency on a contiguous (packed-page) KV cache before vs after, e.g. B=32, seq_len=8192, comparing stage1 kernel time.

findings.claim: By making MLA backends opt into a cross-layer (block-major) KV cache stride order (1,0,2,3), a per-layer view now has stride(0) inflated by num_layers and a non-zero storage offset. When this layout is actually used, page-to-page global loads for a single layer are spaced num_layers apart in memory instead of packed, reducing L2/TLB locality across pages relative to the previous contiguous-per-layer layout. This does not affect the default packed layout (which still gives the equivalent packed stride), so it is only a concern once cross-layer allocation is enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end MLA decode throughput (e.g. DeepSeek-style model) with cross-layer KV layout enabled vs the prior contiguous per-layer layout, at long context to stress L2/TLB reuse.

findings.claim: The guard was tightened from `is_only_trtllm_decode` (prefills==0 && decodes use trtllm) to `all_uses_trtllm` (num_prefills==0 OR prefill_use_trtllm) AND (num_decodes==0 OR decode_use_trtllm). This BROADENS the set of cases where seq_lens_cpu and paged_kv_indices are skipped to include mixed prefill+decode batches that all use TRTLLM. The change trades a CPU sync-avoidance for computing cum_seq_lens_kv on the GPU via a cumsum kernel launch. This is net beneficial for the mixed-TRTLLM case (avoids the expensive CPU seq_lens_cpu retrieval in async mode) so is likely intended, but the previous pure-decode-only optimization is preserved. No clear regression here — just noting the broadened scope is intentional.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FlashInfer TRTLLM path with mixed prefill+decode batch on B200: measure per-step metadata build latency before/after (should improve due to avoided seq_lens_cpu sync).

findings.claim: The new code adds a per-step GPU cumsum kernel launch (torch.cumsum into paged_kv_indptr_prefill_gpu[1:]) plus an elementwise ceil-div (`(prefill_seq_lens + page_size - 1) // page_size`) on every metadata build when the TRTLLM prefill path is taken. Previously this cum_seq_lens_kv was derived from already-computed CPU arrays without extra device kernels. These are small kernels but they run on the critical per-forward-step metadata build path; a scalar write `paged_kv_indptr_prefill_gpu[0] = 0` also issues an individual device write. For very small batches the launch overhead of these extra kernels could partly offset the CPU-sync savings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark metadata build for small (batch=1-4) TRTLLM prefill batches, counting CUDA kernel launches and wall-clock, before vs after.

findings.claim: `paged_kv_indptr_prefill_gpu[0] = 0` performs a single-element host-to-device scalar assignment. In async/graph-capture contexts a single-element indexed GPU write can trigger an implicit synchronization or a tiny dedicated memcpy/kernel each step. Consider fusing this into the cumsum output (e.g. writing a leading zero via a padded cumsum) to avoid the extra device operation on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step device operations on the TRTLLM prefill path; confirm whether the `[0]=0` write introduces a sync point.

findings.claim: The removed code freed the overlap param-gather buffers (model_chunk.free_overlap_buffers()) and released cached GPU memory (torch.cuda.empty_cache()) right before checkpointing. Its documented purpose was to give the async checkpoint worker process enough GPU headroom for D2H tensor transfers. Removing it means at checkpoint time the process retains all overlap param-gather buffers and the full CUDA caching-allocator reservation. If the async checkpoint worker (a separate process) needs GPU memory for D2H staging, it can now hit OOM, or D2H transfers may spill / slow down, degrading checkpoint throughput and possibly the concurrent training step that shares the device. This is a plausible regression for configs with overlap_param_gather + async checkpointing under tight GPU memory budgets.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run async distributed checkpoint save with overlap_param_gather enabled on a memory-tight config; measure peak GPU memory of the training process and the async checkpoint worker, checkpoint save latency, and check for OOM before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: Removing torch.cuda.empty_cache() at checkpoint boundaries eliminates a periodic defragmentation point. While empty_cache() itself is costly (device sync + reclaim) and its removal can actually speed up the checkpoint path, in workloads where the memory footprint shifts around checkpoints the caching allocator may hold onto fragmented reserved memory, increasing peak reserved memory over time. This is speculative and dependent on the allocator behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track torch.cuda.memory_reserved() and max_memory_reserved() across many checkpoint cycles before/after this commit to detect fragmentation-driven reserved-memory growth.

findings.claim: Routed-experts routing data is now returned inside ModelRunnerOutput, which is msgpack-serialized and sent from every worker step to the scheduler process over ZMQ. The comment in outputs.py explicitly warns 'ModelRunnerOutput is serialized and sent to the scheduler process. This is expensive for torch.Tensor so prefer to use list instead.' Adding a per-step routing ndarray/list of shape ~(num_tokens, num_layers, top_k) to this per-step message adds serialization + IPC cost on the hot path for every step when enable_return_routed_experts is on, whereas the previous shared-memory design kept the bulk data out of the per-step IPC entirely (scheduler read it directly from shared memory only on request completion). This grows the per-step output message by several MB and is paid every step rather than only at request completion.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable enable_return_routed_experts on an MoE model (e.g. Qwen3-MoE) and measure decode throughput / inter-token latency and scheduler update_from_output time vs. baseline, with large max_num_batched_tokens to maximize per-step payload.

findings.claim: The new design issues a per-step D2H copy of the full device_buffer (sized max_num_batched_tokens * num_layers * top_k * int32) from the worker on every step to feed RoutedExpertsLists in ModelRunnerOutput. The previous code only copied the [:num_tokens] slice into shared memory. If the copy is blocking or not overlapped with compute via the async copy stream, this synchronizes the GPU each step and reduces GPU utilization / throughput for MoE models with routed-experts capture enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step GPU idle/sync time with routed-experts capture on vs off; check whether the D2H copy is issued on the async output copy stream and overlapped.

findings.claim: The SP + modular-kernel path in RoutedExpertsCapturer.capture now performs get_tp_group().all_gather(topk_ids, dim=0) inside the forward pass for every MoE layer when SP is active. This adds a per-layer TP all-gather collective on the critical decode path that did not exist before (previously only TP rank 0 wrote to shared memory with no extra collective). For deep MoE models this is num_layers extra all-gathers per step, which can measurably increase step latency and stall the pipeline.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With SP enabled on an MoE model, measure per-step latency with routed-experts capture on vs off, isolating the all_gather overhead per layer.

findings.claim: RoutedExpertsManager.routed_experts_by_slot is sized for the entire block pool (num_blocks * block_size * num_layers * top_k * (1 or 2) bytes) and allocated on CPU in the scheduler process. The code itself warns this 'can reach multiple GB'. Previously the shared-memory buffer was sized to max_num_kv_tokens; the new full-pool sizing can substantially increase resident scheduler-process memory for large KV caches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure scheduler-process RSS with a large KV cache (many blocks) and a deep MoE model, capture enabled, comparing new full-pool sizing vs. old max_num_kv_tokens sizing.

findings.claim: store_batch performs a numpy fancy-index assignment routed_experts_by_slot[slot_mapping] = data once per scheduler step. Fancy indexing over a multi-GB buffer with narrowing dtype conversion (int32->uint8/uint16) is CPU work on the scheduler hot path proportional to num_tokens*num_layers*top_k every step. Combined with the D2H and IPC costs this adds measurable scheduler-thread overhead per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time update_from_output / store_batch per step with capture enabled at high batch sizes and deep MoE models.

findings.claim: In serving.py the routed_experts array is np.save'd into a BytesIO then base64-encoded per response. For large routing arrays (num_tokens*num_layers*top_k) this is O(payload) CPU work plus ~33% size inflation from base64 on the API-server request path. While pybase64 mitigates constant factors, the encode still scales with sequence length and layer count and runs synchronously in the response path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure response-building latency for long-sequence MoE generations with routed_experts enabled, varying num_layers and output length.

findings.claim: clip_qk() issues a separate torch.distributed.all_reduce over the DP+CP group for each attention layer inside the per-layer loop, every optimizer step. These are many small, serialized collectives (one per decoder layer) rather than a single batched reduction. In deep models (dozens to hundreds of layers) this adds a non-trivial synchronization/latency overhead to every training step, and the collectives cannot overlap. Coalescing all layers' max-logit tensors into one all_reduce would be far cheaper.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable --qk-clip on a model with e.g. 60+ decoder layers across multiple DP ranks and measure per-iteration optimizer time vs. baseline; compare against a batched (single) all_reduce implementation.

findings.claim: clip_qk() calls torch.max(...).item() once per attention layer every optimizer step. Each .item() forces a GPU->CPU synchronization, stalling the CUDA stream. With one sync per layer per step (plus the per-layer all_reduce it follows), this serializes device work and blocks the training loop, degrading throughput even when weights are never actually clipped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile train_step with --qk-clip enabled on a many-layer model and count cudaStreamSynchronize calls; compare wall-clock optimizer time with a version that aggregates all layer maxima on device and does a single .item().

findings.claim: When qk_clip or log_max_attention_logit is enabled, return_max_logit=True is passed to TE and every microbatch forward now computes and returns per-head max attention logits plus an extra torch.max() elementwise reduction against the running max (self.current_max_attn_logits). This is added work on the attention hot path executed on every forward of every microbatch, and keeps an extra tensor resident on the GPU. For log-only use it is pure overhead with no training-behavior benefit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure attention-layer forward time and step throughput with log_max_attention_logit enabled vs disabled, holding batch/seq constant.

findings.claim: The `use_triton_conv1d` config option and the auto-enable of the Triton varlen conv1d kernel during CUDA-graph steps were removed. Previously, when running under CUDA graphs the code forced `_use_triton_conv1d_this_step = True` to avoid the per-request causal_conv1d_fn loop that launches a variable number of kernels with .item() calls (incompatible with graph capture). If the new intermediate-extraction path does not preserve an equivalent single-kernel conv1d path, hybrid-model prefill under CUDA graphs may fall back to the per-request causal_conv1d loop, increasing kernel-launch overhead and reducing throughput. This is speculative because the mamba_mixer conv1d dispatch changes are not fully visible in the diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Hybrid (Mamba+attention) model prefill throughput with CUDA graphs enabled and prefix caching on, measuring tokens/sec and per-step kernel launch count vs. parent.

findings.claim: In `_update_intermediate_metadata`, `intermediate_counts_gpu.tolist()` forces a GPU->CPU synchronization (blocking .item()-equivalent) on every prefill step that has intermediate offsets. This runs during the mamba metadata update on the critical path of each decode/prefill step for hybrid models. While the surrounding vectorization removed Python loops, this device-to-host sync introduces a serialization point that can stall the pipeline and reduce GPU utilization, especially at high request concurrency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step latency of mamba_metadata.update on a hybrid model with prefix caching enabled and many concurrent prefill requests; profile for the cudaStreamSynchronize caused by tolist().

findings.claim: `is_chunked_prefill_enabled()` for hybrid models previously returned False during CUDA graph creation (`not self.is_creating_cuda_graphs`), and the EP-matching path forced eager mode when explicit chunked prefill or mamba-state-extraction was active. Those guards were removed. If the new path now allows chunked-prefill batches or mamba-extraction batches to match a captured CUDA graph across EP ranks where some ranks are eager, the expert-routing all-to-all could pad inconsistently, risking either a hang or forcing more eager fallbacks. This is a correctness/perf risk that depends on the new extraction path being fully CUDA-graph-safe.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run hybrid MoE model with expert parallelism, chunked prefill, and prefix caching under CUDA graphs; verify no hang and compare throughput/eager-fallback frequency vs. parent.

findings.claim: New persistent GPU buffers `_intermediate_chunk_indices_buffer` and `_intermediate_abs_positions_buffer` are allocated with size MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST * max_requests and are re-filled (`.fill_`) on every step even when no extraction is needed (the else branch fills the full buffer each update). These per-step full-buffer fills add small kernel launches on every hybrid decode step regardless of whether extraction occurs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step kernel launches on hybrid decode-only steps with prefix caching enabled but no intermediate extraction active; compare vs. parent.

findings.claim: The `_A_neg_exp_cache` is only refilled when `_A_neg_exp_cache_stale` is True or when the stream is currently capturing a CUDA graph. Under CUDA-graph replay (the common decode fast path), `torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` is False and the stale flag is False, so the cache is correctly not recomputed -- good. However, because the recompute is conditioned on `is_current_stream_capturing()`, every capture will always execute the `copy_(-torch.exp(...))` even if the value is already fresh, baking a redundant cast+exp+neg+copy kernel sequence into each captured graph. This adds a few small elementwise kernels per Mamba layer to every graph replay, partially undermining the stated goal of eliminating those kernels from the decode profile. The net win over the old code (which recomputed unconditionally) is still positive, but the capture-time recompute is unnecessary when the cache is fresh.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile decode step latency with CUDA graphs enabled for a Mamba model; compare kernel count/time in captured graph with and without the `is_current_stream_capturing()` condition.

findings.claim: `_batch_indices_decode_buffer` was widened from int32 to int64, doubling this buffer's memory (max_requests * 4 bytes extra). This is negligible in absolute terms and is offset by removing a per-layer `.to(torch.int64)` upcast kernel in the decode path (previously executed once per Mamba layer per step), so the change is a net latency improvement. Reported only for completeness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-token decode latency for a many-layer Mamba model, counting the eliminated int64 upcast kernels per layer.

findings.claim: `torch.topk(..., sorted=torch.is_grad_enabled())` disables the sort of top-k results only when grad is disabled (inference). This can slightly speed up MoE routing during inference by avoiding the sort. Correctness depends on downstream code not relying on top-k output being sorted by score during inference; if any consumer assumes sorted order (e.g., tie-breaking or first-expert selection), this could change results, but it is not a performance regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark torch.topk with sorted=True vs sorted=False on the router scores tensor shape used in inference.

findings.claim: Each of TELinear, TELayerNormColumnParallelLinear, and TEGroupedLinear now unconditionally calls `_get_fp8_autocast_for_quant_params(self.te_quant_params, self.training)` and wraps `super().forward()` in a `with quant_context:` block on every forward. When no per-module quant config is set (the default, `te_quant_params is None`), this still allocates a `nullcontext()` object and enters/exits a context manager on every linear forward call. This is a small, constant CPU-side Python overhead per linear per microbatch — negligible per call but multiplied across every linear layer, every microbatch, every step. It adds Python interpreter work in the training hot path that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark forward throughput of a TELinear-heavy GPT model with quant_recipe=None, comparing steps/sec before and after this commit to detect CPU-bound Python overhead regressions.

findings.claim: When a per-module quant override IS active, `_get_fp8_autocast_for_quant_recipe` is invoked on every forward call and reconstructs a fresh TE recipe object (e.g. `te.common.recipe.Float8CurrentScaling(...)`, `MXFP8BlockScaling(...)`, `NVFP4BlockScaling()`) plus calls `FP8GlobalStateManager.is_fp8_enabled()`, `model_parallel_is_initialized()`, and `get_amax_reduction_group(...)` per forward. Building a recipe object and resolving the amax reduction group on every forward of every targeted layer/microbatch is redundant work — these are effectively static per-module and could be built once at `finish_init`. This adds measurable per-forward Python/CPU overhead scaled by number of quantized modules × microbatches × steps, potentially becoming CPU-bound and reducing GPU utilization for small batch/short-seq configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: With a per-module quant recipe enabled on all linears (e.g. mxfp8), profile forward-pass CPU time and steps/sec vs. a version that caches the constructed recipe and amax group once, to quantify per-forward recipe-construction overhead.

findings.claim: For custom recipes, `_get_fp8_autocast_for_quant_recipe` does `from megatron.core.fp8_utils import _get_custom_recipe` and calls `_get_custom_recipe(qrecipe.custom_recipe_factory)` on every forward. `_get_custom_recipe` performs `importlib.import_module(...)` / attribute resolution via `_resolve_callable_from_python_import_path` and constructs a new CustomRecipe each call. Doing an import-path resolution and recipe construction per forward (rather than once at init) is unnecessary repeated work in the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable a custom_recipe_factory override and measure forward-pass CPU overhead per step compared to caching the resolved recipe once.

findings.claim: The non-drop-and-pad permute path was rewritten from masked_select over an [num_experts, num_tokens] boolean map to a full argsort over the flattened routing map: `routing_map.reshape(-1).argsort(descending=True, stable=True)`. A stable argsort over num_experts*num_tokens elements is O(N log N) (and stable sort typically allocates additional temporaries), whereas the previous masked_select was O(N) with a boolean gather. For large token counts and expert counts this makes token permutation materially more expensive on the critical MoE dispatch path. Additionally `probs.T.contiguous().reshape(-1)[flat_sorted]` gathers over the whole flattened prob tensor rather than the previous masked_select, adding cost. This is done to make output shape fixed for CUDA graph capture, but it is now unconditionally applied to the eager training path as well, not just graph capture.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark permute() eager path with num_tokens in {4k,16k,64k} and num_experts in {8,64,128}, comparing argsort-based vs masked_select-based implementations for latency and peak memory.

findings.claim: The argsort path allocates a full int64 permutation index of size num_experts*num_tokens (`flat_sorted`) before slicing to num_out_tokens, plus a full-size flattened contiguous copy of probs. Previously masked_select produced only num_out_tokens-sized results. Peak activation/temporary memory during dispatch grows by roughly num_experts*num_tokens int64 elements, which can be significant for large expert counts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory of dispatch_postprocess for large num_experts and num_tokens before/after this change.

findings.claim: In _forward_mlp for MoE, after the router graph replay a new per-step CUDA event is recorded and synchronized on the host: `self._router_dtoh_event.record(); self._router_dtoh_event.synchronize()`. `Event.synchronize()` is a host-side blocking wait, executed on every MoE layer on every training step. This forces a CPU/GPU sync per MoE layer per microbatch, serializing the router's D2H copies with the host and preventing overlap of the router graph with subsequent work. Across many layers/microbatches this can materially reduce throughput and GPU utilization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile MoE training step with partial cudagraphs; measure GPU idle bubbles introduced by per-layer event.synchronize() vs a stream-only wait, across model depth.

findings.claim: _forward_mlp_postprocess now unconditionally re-assigns all token_dispatcher_attrs via a Python loop (setattr per attribute) on every invocation, and _forward_mlp does the same restore again after the event sync. This duplicated attribute restoration runs per MoE layer per step; while individually cheap, doubling the restore adds Python-level overhead on the hot path for deep MoE models.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step CPU overhead of token_dispatcher_attrs restoration loops for models with many MoE layers.

findings.claim: PackedSeqParams.__post_init__ now computes seq_idx (cat + diff + repeat_interleave + arange + to(int32) + unsqueeze) every time a PackedSeqParams is constructed with total_tokens set, launching several small GPU kernels. Previously _create_packed_seq_idx was only called inside the mamba mixer forward when sequence packing was active. Now this runs on every PackedSeqParams construction, including non-Mamba (attention/MoE) paths in rl_utils.get_logprobs and pretrain_mamba, adding small per-construction kernel-launch overhead even when seq_idx is never consumed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count kernel launches / measure host overhead of PackedSeqParams construction with total_tokens set across many microbatches in a non-Mamba model.

findings.claim: On ROCm, profile_cudagraph_memory() is now always skipped, so cudagraph_memory_estimate is forced to 0. When the opt-in flag VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS=1 is enabled on a ROCm host, the KV-cache size computation (available_kv_cache_memory_bytes) no longer subtracts any reserved memory for CUDA graph capture. This deliberately allocates more memory to the KV cache; the intent of the fix is to avoid startup OOM from negative/incorrect estimates. However, it removes the safety margin the estimate provided, so subsequent CUDA graph capture during warmup could exceed the profiled budget and trigger an OOM/allocator fragmentation on ROCm if graphs are captured (enforce_eager=False). This is a startup-time / one-time-allocation concern, not a steady-state runtime regression. Confidence is low because the estimate on ROCm was described as unreliable/negative to begin with, so removing it is plausibly net-positive for OOM avoidance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a ROCm GPU with enforce_eager=False and VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS=1, measure peak GPU memory and startup OOM rate at high --gpu-memory-utilization (e.g. 0.95) before vs after this change to confirm no new OOM during cudagraph capture.

findings.claim: The DCP context attention workspace is now sized to `max(n, self._dcp_max_num_tokens)` where `_dcp_max_num_tokens = scheduler_config.max_num_batched_tokens`. Previously the workspace was sized to `n` (the actual gathered token count for the current step). Since the workspace manager grows to the largest requested spec and keeps it resident, this change forces the DCP context-out buffer to be permanently sized for the full `max_num_batched_tokens * num_heads * dcp_world_size * head_size`, increasing steady-state GPU memory usage even for small batches. The over-allocation is intentional (to keep the buffer CUDA-graph/shape stable) but represents a real memory-footprint increase versus the parent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable DCP (dcp_world_size>1) with a hybrid model and measure peak GPU memory for a small batch vs. parent; observe the DCP context workspace buffer size.

findings.claim: all_gather now always routes the non-symm-mem path through `pynccl_comm.all_gather(output, input_.contiguous())` instead of the base class's `dist.all_gather_into_tensor`. The added `input_.contiguous()` call can trigger an extra device-to-device copy when the input tensor is non-contiguous (previously `all_gather_into_tensor` was fed `input_` directly). For the common dim=0 uniform sequence-parallel case the input is usually already contiguous, so impact is small, but for arbitrary `dim` all-gathers a non-contiguous input now pays for an extra copy each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark all_gather on non-contiguous inputs at various dims before/after the change and compare latency.

findings.claim: Prefix caching is now reported as supported for hybrid models (ModelConfig change returns True), enabling the prefix-cache lookup machinery for hybrid KV cache coordinators. Although the arg default keeps it opt-in via `not model_config.is_hybrid`, when users enable prefix caching on hybrid models the HybridKVCacheCoordinator.find_longest_cache_hit path now runs per-request block-hash lookups across multiple groups (with per-group block-size scaling). This adds per-step scheduling overhead for hybrid models that previously skipped it entirely; ensure the cache-hit lookup cost is acceptable for large multi-group configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable prefix caching on a hybrid (Mamba+attention) model and measure scheduler step latency vs. prefix caching disabled.

findings.claim: FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the FRONT of _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA], ahead of the previously top-priority FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel. Because kernel selection iterates in priority order and returns the first kernel whose is_supported()/can_implement() pass, on any SM10x (Blackwell) device this new cutedsl kernel now becomes the DEFAULT for every NVFP4 linear layer under --linear-backend auto. Its is_supported() only checks device-capability-family 100 and has_flashinfer() and can_implement() unconditionally returns True, so it will win for all shapes/token counts. Unless the cutedsl backend is genuinely faster than the mature cutlass path across the full range of GEMM shapes and batch sizes vLLM sees, this reorders auto-selection to a newly-added, less-battle-tested kernel and can measurably regress prefill/decode throughput on SM100. Auto-priority ordering is a performance-critical decision and placing a brand-new backend first is a likely regression risk.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an SM100 (B200/GB200) device, run NVFP4 model GEMM latency/throughput (e.g. tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py-style end-to-end plus a per-shape GEMM microbench) comparing --linear-backend flashinfer_cutedsl vs flashinfer_cutlass across representative M (decode batch, prefill seq) and N/K sizes, and confirm cutedsl >= cutlass before making it the auto default.

findings.claim: Unlike FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel, the new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel does not override input_quant_key() (it inherits the base default of None) and does not use as_quantized_activation(). As a result the fusion machinery (expose_input_quant_key / QuantizedActivation) will never hand this kernel a pre-quantized activation, and apply_weights() unconditionally calls scaled_fp4_quant(x, ...) on every forward. In configurations where an upstream fused op could produce the quantized activation directly (as cutlass does), the cutedsl path incurs a redundant per-token FP4 quantization + swizzle each call, adding launch overhead and memory traffic in the hot inference path. Since this kernel is now also the auto default on SM100, that redundant quantization applies broadly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer forward latency and kernel launch count (nsys/torch profiler) for an NVFP4 layer using flashinfer_cutedsl vs flashinfer_cutlass with activation-quant fusion enabled, measuring the extra scaled_fp4_quant launches per forward.

findings.claim: AsyncTPPass now unconditionally registers an additional FlashInferAllGatherFP4Pattern for the cute-dsl backend alongside the existing cutlass/other FP4 all-gather patterns. Each registered pattern is matched during inductor graph passes at compile time; adding a pattern that is only usable on SM10x increases pattern-matching work during torch.compile on every startup (including on hardware where cute-dsl is never selected), slightly increasing compilation/warmup time. This is a startup-cost regression rather than a steady-state one, hence low severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure AsyncTPPass / inductor pattern-matching time during torch.compile warmup before and after this commit on a non-SM100 GPU to quantify added compile-time overhead.

findings.claim: Enabling dynamic speculative decoding forcibly downgrades the cudagraph_mode from any full-cudagraph mode to PIECEWISE. Piecewise cudagraphs generally have higher per-step launch overhead and lower GPU utilization than full cudagraphs, so users who had full cudagraphs configured will see a measurable decode latency/throughput regression whenever DSD is turned on. This is by design (documented as a limitation), but it is a real runtime performance cost of the feature.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode TPOT/throughput with eagle spec decode under FULL cudagraph vs. the forced-PIECEWISE mode triggered by adding num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size.

findings.claim: AsyncScheduler._update_after_schedule now rebuilds self._spec_token_placeholders = [-1] * num_spec_tokens_to_schedule on every scheduling step. Previously this list was allocated once in __init__ and reused read-only. This adds a small per-step list allocation on the scheduler hot path. Impact is minor (list length is K, typically <=8) but it defeats the original 'reusable read-only placeholder' optimization noted in the code comment.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark scheduler step latency at high request concurrency with async scheduling enabled and DSD active.

findings.claim: When dynamic SD is enabled the scheduler indexes self.dynamic_sd_lookup[len(num_scheduled_tokens)] every step. len(num_scheduled_tokens) counts requests that have scheduled tokens (which can include prefills / chunked prefill), not the decode batch size, so K may be chosen based on an inflated 'batch size'. This can select a smaller-than-optimal K (fewer draft tokens) during mixed prefill+decode steps, reducing speculative acceptance and effective decode speedup. Not a crash, but a potential throughput regression relative to intended behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run eagle DSD with a workload mixing prefills and decodes and measure how often K is downgraded vs. a pure-decode batch of the same decode size.

findings.claim: Adding `self.weight.dtype == torch.bfloat16` to `allow_dsv3_router_gemm` disables the fast Tier-1 DSV3 specialized router GEMM whenever the gate weight is fp32. For GLM-5.2 (`glm_moe_dsa`) with shape (hidden_size=6144, n_routed_experts=256), the gate is now instantiated with params_dtype=fp32 (via `_get_moe_router_dtype`), so: Tier-1 DSV3 kernel is skipped (weight not bf16), Tier-2 fp32 specialized kernel does not support (6144,256) (only {(3072,256),(6144,128)} are instantiated), and Tier-3 cuBLAS requires a bf16 weight. The router therefore drops to the Tier-4 F.linear fallback running a full fp32 GEMM, which is slower than the specialized kernel that (6144,256) shapes previously used. This is intended for fp32 routing correctness, but it is a real per-layer slowdown of the gate for these models.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark GateLinear.forward for shape (hidden_size=6144, num_experts=256) at M in {1,8,16,32,64}, comparing bf16 weight (DSV3 kernel path) vs fp32 weight (Tier-4 F.linear fp32 path); measure per-call latency and its share of a full MoE-layer forward.

findings.claim: With the new fp32 gate weight, the Tier-4 fallback executes `x = x.to(self.weight.dtype)` on every forward, upcasting the bf16 activations to fp32 before the GEMM (and router logits are then produced/kept in fp32). This adds a per-forward cast plus a wider (fp32) router GEMM and larger router-logits buffers versus the previous bf16 path. The gate matmul is small relative to expert compute, so the absolute cost is modest, but it recurs for every MoE layer at every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end decode throughput / step latency for a glm_moe_dsa-style config with fp32 gate vs the prior bf16 gate, isolating the router-logits dtype effect.

findings.claim: For chunked-prefill continuation batches (query_len>1 and seq_len>query_len), the persistent work-stealing sparse-MLA decode kernel is now disabled (work_meta_data=None). The persistent path exists specifically for better load balancing across CUs; disabling it forces the non-persistent kernel path for exactly the long-context / large-context batches where CU load imbalance matters most, reducing GPU utilization and increasing attention latency for those decode/prefill steps. This is an intentional correctness fix (numerical bug), but it is a real performance regression for chunked-prefill workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Long-context DeepSeek-style sparse-MLA decode with chunked prefill enabled on ROCm; measure per-step attention latency and end-to-end tokens/s for continuation steps vs. the parent commit.

findings.claim: use_persistent = not is_chunked_continuation.any() disables the persistent kernel for the ENTIRE batch as soon as a single request is a chunked-prefill continuation. In mixed batches (pure-decode requests batched together with one chunked continuation), all tokens — including the pure-decode ones that would benefit from the persistent path and are numerically correct — lose the persistent work-stealing kernel. This over-broadly penalizes mixed batches rather than gating per-request.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Continuous-batching mix of decode requests plus one chunked-prefill continuation on ROCm sparse MLA; compare attention step latency to a batch with no continuations.

findings.claim: total_seq_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy() performs a second host-side tensor->numpy conversion of the same slice already converted a few lines earlier for clamped_seq_lens. This adds a small redundant per-build CPU cost on the attention-metadata build hot path (executed every forward step). The conversion result could be reused.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark build() CPU time per step at high request counts (large num_reqs) to measure the added host overhead.

findings.claim: In handle_event for 'input_audio_buffer.append', base64 decoding and NumPy conversion (np.frombuffer(...).astype(np.float32)/32768.0) run synchronously on the asyncio event loop thread for every incoming audio chunk. With many concurrent realtime WebSocket connections or large chunks, this CPU work blocks the single event loop and can add latency to all connections (including HTTP request handling on the same server). Offloading to a thread executor or bounding chunk size would help.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Open N concurrent /v1/realtime WebSocket connections streaming audio and measure p99 latency of concurrent HTTP completion requests vs baseline.

findings.claim: serve_http now iterates app.routes twice (once for method routes, once for 'other' endpoints) to log routes at startup. This doubles the route-logging loop, but it runs only once at server startup and route counts are small, so the impact is negligible.

In `get_encode_kwargs`, the condition to disable tokenizer-level (HF) truncation was broadened from `self.truncation_side == "left"` to `self.truncation_side is not None and self.truncate_prompt_tokens is not None`. Previously, right-side truncation could rely on the tokenizer's `max_length` to stop encoding early. Now, any request that explicitly sets `truncation_side` (including `"right"`) will have `truncation=False` passed to the tokenizer, so the tokenizer encodes the *entire* prompt and the truncation happens afterward in Python via `_token_truncation` (`tokens[:N]`). For requests with a very long input prompt truncated to a small N, this performs tokenization work proportional to the full prompt length rather than N, incurring extra per-request CPU cost on the frontend. This only affects requests that explicitly set `truncation_side`, and is functionally required for correct right-side slicing on generation tokenizers that default to left truncation, so the impact is bounded but worth noting.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Send completion requests with a large prompt (e.g. 100k tokens) and `truncate_prompt_tokens=4`, comparing frontend tokenization latency with `truncation_side="right"` (new path, disables HF truncation, tokenizes full prompt) vs. `truncation_side=None` (HF max_length truncation caps tokenization).

findings.claim: In SingleTypeKVCacheManager.allocate_new_blocks and allocate_new_computed_blocks, block IDs are unconditionally appended to self.new_block_ids whenever the spec is FullAttentionSpec (which is the common case for standard non-Mamba models). However, take_new_block_ids() is only called from the scheduler when needs_kv_cache_zeroing is True (i.e., only for Mamba/Qwen3.5 models). For all non-Mamba models, needs_kv_cache_zeroing is False, so the list is never drained. Since allocate_new_blocks runs on essentially every scheduling step for running requests, self.new_block_ids grows without bound over the process lifetime, causing a monotonic memory leak proportional to the total number of blocks ever allocated. Over long-running serving, this list can grow to millions of ints.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a long non-Mamba serving workload (e.g. Llama) for many thousands of decode steps and monitor RSS / len(single_type_managers[0].new_block_ids) growth over time.

findings.claim: The `type(self.kv_cache_spec) is FullAttentionSpec` checks and list.extend of block IDs were added into the two hottest allocation paths (allocate_new_blocks, allocate_new_computed_blocks) that run for every request every step, regardless of whether the model needs KV zeroing. For the vast majority of deployments (non-Mamba), this bookkeeping (isinstance-style check plus generator-expression extend) is pure overhead that produces data that is never consumed. It should be gated on a per-manager flag set at construction time when zeroing is actually needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark allocate_new_blocks in a tight loop for a non-Mamba config and compare per-call latency before/after the added block-id tracking.

findings.claim: zero_block_ids uses `self._ids_pinned[:n_blocks].numpy()[:] = block_ids`, which converts a Python list into the pinned tensor element-by-element via numpy assignment each scheduling step. For steps that allocate many new blocks (large prefills), this per-element Python->numpy copy adds host-side latency on the critical scheduling/dispatch path. Only affects Mamba models but runs every step that allocates blocks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark zero_block_ids host-side time as a function of n_blocks (e.g. 1..8192) for a Mamba model prefill workload.

findings.claim: For Mamba/Qwen3.5 models, _zero_block_ids launches a Triton kernel every scheduling step that allocates new full-attention blocks, adding a synchronous host->device copy (non_blocking id copy plus kernel launch) into the per-step critical path. If block allocation happens most steps during decode, this is an extra kernel launch + small H2D copy per step contributing to per-step latency and reducing GPU utilization overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step decode latency for a Qwen3.5/Mamba model with and without the zeroing kernel enabled.

findings.claim: get_kv_cache_block_dim calls get_kv_cache_shape with a magic sentinel (1234567) and uses shape.index() to discover the block dimension. This is only invoked during init_meta (one-time setup), so runtime cost is negligible, but the sentinel approach is fragile if any other dimension happens to equal the sentinel value; not a runtime perf issue.

This commit flips the default FlashInfer autotune path from 'all ranks tune in lockstep' (the old _FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE=False branch) to 'only rank 0 tunes with tune_mode=True, then broadcast the cache'. In the persistent path only the leader runs the expensive multi-tactic benchmarking loop while every other rank does a single _dummy_run and then blocks at world.broadcast_object/barrier. This introduces a large startup-time rank skew: non-leader ranks sit idle waiting for the leader to finish tuning. The DeepEP a2a backends are explicitly excluded (their comment states dispatch/combine can time out under exactly this skew), but other all2all backends that also perform collective dispatch/combine at startup (e.g. pplx, flashinfer_all2allv, flashinfer_nvlink_*, nixl_ep) are NOT excluded and could experience similar skew-driven slowdowns or communicator timeouts during warmup. Consider whether the exclusion set should be broader.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup (warmup) wall-clock with EP enabled on >=2 ranks for each all2all backend (deepep excluded vs. pplx/flashinfer/nixl included), comparing leader-only autotune vs. all-rank autotune to quantify startup skew and confirm no collective timeouts.

findings.claim: MHCPreOp.forward_hip (the ROCm path) previously dispatched to the fused torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter kernel whenever hidden_size % 256 == 0. This commit comments out that branch and now unconditionally calls the pure-PyTorch reference implementation mhc_kernels.mhc_pre_torch for all inputs. The torch reference implements the RMS-norm / sinkhorn / mix reduction as a sequence of separate ATen ops rather than a single fused kernel, so on ROCm this replaces one fused kernel launch with many small unfused ops per layer per forward pass, increasing kernel-launch overhead and memory traffic and reducing throughput on DeepSeek V4. This is done deliberately for accuracy (aiter bug at large token counts) but is a real runtime slowdown until aiter is upgraded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DeepSeek V4 decode+prefill throughput on ROCm/MI300 with hidden_size%256==0 before vs after; compare per-layer time of mhc_pre/mhc_post (fused aiter vs mhc_*_torch).

findings.claim: MHCPostOp.forward_hip similarly no longer dispatches to torch.ops.vllm.mhc_post_aiter when hidden_size % 256 == 0; it now always runs the unfused mhc_kernels.mhc_post_torch reference. Same effect as mhc_pre: extra kernel launches and memory traffic per layer on ROCm, lowering throughput for the MHC post block. Executed once per decoder layer, so overhead scales with model depth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark mhc_post_aiter vs mhc_post_torch on ROCm at typical (num_tokens, hidden_size) shapes and measure end-to-end DeepSeek V4 throughput delta.

findings.claim: The _TOPK_FAST_PATH_VALUES / _topk_indices_prefill / _topk_indices_decode helpers were removed and top_k_per_row_prefill/top_k_per_row_decode are now called directly. Previously topk_tokens values not in {2048} fell back to a torch.topk implementation; now the fused C++ kernel is always invoked. If the fused kernel is faster this is a win, but if any deployment relied on the torch fallback for unsupported topk sizes, behavior/perf changes. Net perf impact is likely neutral-to-positive but the code path selection changed, so worth confirming the C++ kernel supports all topk_tokens used in production without a slow internal path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare top_k_per_row_prefill/decode C++ kernel latency vs prior torch.topk fallback across topk_tokens values actually used by DeepSeek V4 sparse attention.

findings.claim: The new TrtLlmBf16Experts.apply() calls flashinfer.fused_moe.trtllm_bf16_moe(...) directly instead of going through the previously registered custom op torch.ops.vllm.flashinfer_fused_moe_bf16. The deleted flashinfer_trtllm_moe.py registered this op via direct_register_custom_op with a fake_impl and tags=(torch.Tag.needs_fixed_stride_order,). Removing the custom-op wrapper means the FlashInfer MoE call is no longer a single opaque op understood by torch.compile / the Inductor graph: it can cause graph breaks (falling back to eager for the MoE region), loss of CUDA-graph capture for this path, and the loss of the needs_fixed_stride_order tag which previously guaranteed the kernel received a fixed-stride layout (a mismatch can force extra layout/contiguity copies at runtime).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Blackwell (SM100) BF16 MoE (e.g. Llama4 / DeepSeekV3) decode with VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP16=1 and torch.compile+cudagraphs enabled; compare tokens/s and count graph breaks vs parent commit.

findings.claim: select_unquantized_moe_backend now, in the auto path, instantiates candidate kernel classes and calls is_supported_config on each backend in priority order (FLASHINFER_TRTLLM, FLASHINFER_CUTLASS, TRITON, ...). Some of these checks (_supports_current_device) invoke has_flashinfer_trtllm_fused_moe()/has_flashinfer_cutlass_fused_moe() which import submodules and probe device capability. These are @functools.cache'd so the cost is one-time per process, but backend_to_kernel_cls now performs lazy imports of multiple experts modules during selection. This only affects model-load/warmup time, not steady-state throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load / first-forward warmup time for an unquantized MoE model on CUDA vs parent commit.

findings.claim: By adding 2/3/5/6/7-bit to WNA16_SUPPORTED_TYPES_MAP and routing all pack_quantized sub-byte weights through CompressedTensorsWNA16, layers that previously took the WNA8O8 path now must find a mixed-precision kernel in _POSSIBLE_KERNELS. Marlin (query_marlin_supported_quant_types) and Machete only support 4/8-bit (and Machete needs SM90). For 2/3/5/6/7-bit weights, the only kernel that can implement them is the new HummingLinearKernel, which requires the `humming` package to be installed. If humming is not installed, choose_mp_linear_kernel will fall through the entire priority list and raise ValueError at model load (a correctness/availability regression), rather than the previous WNA8O8 handling. Even when humming is present, these odd bit widths cannot use the high-throughput Marlin/Machete kernels, so inference for such layers runs on the (typically slower) Humming path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a compressed-tensors model with 4-bit and separately a 3-bit pack_quantized checkpoint; compare weight-only linear throughput of the selected kernel (Humming vs Marlin) and confirm no ValueError when humming is absent.

findings.claim: self.pack_factor is changed from an int (32 // num_bits) to fractions.Fraction(32, num_bits). This value is passed as packed_factor to PackedvLLMParameter / PackedColumnParameter and used in weight-loading/sharding arithmetic. Fraction arithmetic is materially slower than int arithmetic; if packed_factor is used on per-shard or per-weight-slice code paths during load, weight loading for these layers becomes slightly slower. This is one-time (load-time) cost, not steady-state inference.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model weight-loading time for a WNA16 checkpoint before/after switching pack_factor from int to Fraction.

findings.claim: launch_cooperative_cluster() calls cudaFuncSetAttribute (MaxDynamicSharedMemorySize, and NonPortableClusterSizeAllowed for CS=16) on every single launch of the op. These are host-side CUDA runtime API calls that are only needed to be set once per kernel function. Since this kernel is explicitly targeted at the low-latency decode path (num_rows<=32, single-token decode), the per-invocation host overhead of these attribute-setting calls is added to the critical latency path of every decode step. They should be cached behind a call_once / static-bool guard as done elsewhere. This also blocks CUDA-graph capture friendliness since cudaFuncSetAttribute is not a stream-ordered op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-call host launch latency of cooperative_topk (num_rows=1, k=2048) with and without hoisting the cudaFuncSetAttribute calls out of the per-launch path; compare CPU-side dispatch time in a tight decode loop.

findings.claim: During the tie-refinement phase only rank 0 of the cluster performs work (all other CS-1 blocks return early after `if (rank != 0) return;`). For CS=16 with num_rows<=4 this leaves 15/16 of the launched CTAs idle during refinement while occupying SM slots via the cluster. For inputs with many ties this serializes a non-trivial radix-sort refinement on a single block. This is a design tradeoff for the low-latency small-batch case, but is a potential GPU-utilization limiter for tie-heavy logits.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark cooperative_topk latency on logits with a large number of tied values at the threshold bin (e.g. many identical fp32 scores) vs. well-separated scores, for k=2048, CS=16.

findings.claim: mbarrier_wait busy-spins on mbarrier_try_wait_parity without any backoff. This is standard for TMA pipeline barriers, but if a TMA load stalls (e.g. cache/DRAM pressure at large seq_len) the waiting warp fully spins, consuming issue slots. Only lane 0 waits and then __syncwarp, so impact is bounded, but worth confirming the pipeline depth (kStages=2) is deep enough to hide TMA latency at max_model_len; a too-shallow double-buffer can leave the compute warps stalling on the barrier.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile cooperative_topk with Nsight Compute for memory-stall / barrier-wait cycles at the largest supported seq_len (k=2048, CS=4) and compare kStages=2 vs a deeper pipeline.

findings.claim: Pinning now runs in a background thread and the worker constructor returns immediately, so transfers (submit_store/submit_load -> transfer_async) can be issued before cudaHostRegister completes. Any offload/load transfer issued during the pinning window operates on pageable (unpinned) host memory, which uses substantially slower DMA (typically ~2-3x lower H2D/D2H bandwidth) and can force implicit stream synchronization. For large KV caches (tens of GB) the background registration can take seconds, so early transfers after startup are measurably slower until pinning finishes. Previously pinning was synchronous in __init__, guaranteeing all transfers ran on pinned memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure offload/load transfer bandwidth (GB/s) for transfers issued in the first few seconds after worker init vs. steady state, with a multi-GB CPU KV cache on CUDA.

findings.category: CUDA API lock / launch latency contention
findings.claim: cudaHostRegister is a heavyweight, synchronizing CUDA runtime call. Executing it in a background thread concurrently with transfer_async's CUDA operations (Stream creation, event.record, kernel launch) can contend for the CUDA driver API lock, causing latency spikes / stalls for transfers submitted while the pin thread is still running. The previous synchronous pin avoided any overlap with live transfers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-transfer submit latency (transfer_async wall time) during the pinning window vs. after pin_thread has joined.

findings.claim: When there is no mmap_region, pinning is now done per-cpu_tensor via cudaHostRegister in a loop (one registration call per KV cache tensor) rather than relying on PyTorch's allocation-time pin_memory. This changes the number of registration calls and their timing but does not itself add per-transfer cost; the main effect is that many separate registrations occur serially in the background thread, extending the window during which memory is unpinned.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare total pinning wall time and unpinned-window duration for many small cpu_tensors vs. a single mmap region.

findings.claim: With _FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE=False the new early-return path runs fi_utils.autotune() (tune mode) + a full _dummy_run(num_tokens=max_num_batched_tokens) on EVERY rank. The previous persistent-cache path only ran the expensive autotune benchmarking on the leader (rank 0) and broadcast the resulting cache to all other ranks, which just did a plain dummy run and loaded the leader's tactics. Now every rank in the world redundantly benchmarks all FlashInfer kernel variants, multiplying autotune wall-time by the number of ranks and increasing distributed startup/warmup latency. This is a one-time startup cost, not steady-state, but is a clear measurable slowdown in multi-GPU launches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure server startup/warmup time (time from launch to ready) on a TP>=4 configuration with FlashInfer autotune enabled, comparing this commit vs parent.

findings.claim: Disabling the persistent cache removes cross-restart reuse of autotune results, so the full FlashInfer autotune benchmarking is re-run from scratch on every process start instead of being loaded from disk. This re-introduces the per-startup autotune cost that the persistent cache (added in da03e549b) was meant to amortize. One-time per-launch cost only.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare cold vs warm restart warmup time with autotune enabled before and after this commit.

findings.claim: Per the commit message, the persistent cache could produce cache-key collisions (e.g. use_8x4_sf_layout True/False) causing invalid/suboptimal kernel tactics to be selected. If accurate, this change actually improves steady-state inference kernel performance by avoiding wrongly-cached tactics, at the expense of higher startup cost noted above.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode/prefill throughput of a FlashInfer-backed model on Blackwell/Hopper with fp4 layouts to confirm no steady-state regression and possible improvement in tactic selection.

findings.claim: The SILU entry of _CPU_MOE_ACT_FN was changed from `SiluAndMul.forward_native` (a plain static method, zero-cost to reference and call) to `lambda x: SiluAndMul(compile_native=False).forward_native(x)`. This dict entry is invoked from `cpu_fused_moe_torch` once per active expert inside the Python per-expert loop, i.e. many times per forward pass on the CPU MoE inference path. Each invocation now fully constructs a new SiluAndMul CustomOp: it runs nn.Module.__init__ (allocating the module's parameter/buffer/module OrderedDicts and hook dicts), and CustomOp.__init__ -> dispatch_forward, which calls get_cached_compilation_config(), self.enabled() (another config lookup), and mutates the shared compilation_config.enabled_custom_ops / disabled_custom_ops sets on every call. All of this per-expert, per-token-batch object construction and config bookkeeping is pure overhead that did not exist before and adds measurable per-forward latency on CPU inference (scaling with number of experts activated). The object is discarded immediately after computing silu.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cpu_fused_moe_torch (or CPUFusedMOE.forward_torch) on a DeepSeek-style MoE layer with many experts on CPU; compare per-forward latency before/after this change. Also directly time 1e5 calls to `SiluAndMul.forward_native(x)` vs `SiluAndMul(compile_native=False).forward_native(x)` on a small tensor to isolate construction overhead.

findings.claim: compute() and packed_compute() gained a `const float limit` parameter and a HAS_CLAMP template branch. For the non-clamp path (HAS_CLAMP=false, used by silu_and_mul, mul_and_silu, gelu_and_mul, gelu_tanh_and_mul) the clamp code is compile-time eliminated via `if constexpr`, so no arithmetic is added. However act_and_mul_kernel now always takes an extra `limit` kernel argument that is unused on the non-clamp path; this is negligible (one extra scalar in the kernel param bank) but adds a tiny amount of constant-bank usage across all existing activation kernels. No meaningful runtime regression expected for the existing (non-clamp) kernels.
