findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare silu_and_mul kernel throughput (existing non-clamp path) before/after via tests/kernels/core/test_activation benchmark to confirm no regression from the added kernel parameter.

findings.claim: The FlashAttention metadata builder now reports AttentionCGSupport.ALWAYS on XPU (get_flash_attn_version()==3 OR current_platform.is_xpu()). Combined with the removal in xpu.py of the fallback that forced cudagraph_mode to PIECEWISE for FLASH_ATTN, this enables full-graph capture of the FMHA sycl-tla attention kernels. The removed comment previously asserted 'FMHA sycl-tla kernels cannot be captured with XPU graphs'. If that limitation still holds for some kernel paths/shapes, enabling full cudagraph capture could produce incorrect capture, a launch failure, or a hang during graph replay rather than a graceful PIECEWISE fallback. This is a correctness/hang risk that depends on the XPU graph runtime actually supporting these kernels now; if it does, the change is a net performance win.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On an XPU device with VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1, run FLASH_ATTN decode with full cudagraph mode across mixed prefill-decode and uniform-decode batches; verify no capture/replay hang and compare throughput vs PIECEWISE mode.

findings.claim: The branch that set cudagraph_mode = NONE when world_size_across_dp > 1 was removed, so XPU graphs will now be captured even when data-parallel/communication ops are present. The removed warning stated 'XPU Graph doesn't support capture communication ops'. If communication collectives still cannot be captured in an XPU graph, multi-DP runs could hang or error during graph capture/replay instead of falling back to eager. Whether this is a regression depends on new XPU graph support for collectives.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run XPU with world_size_across_dp > 1 and VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1; confirm graph capture completes without hang and measure per-step latency vs cudagraph disabled.

findings.claim: The DP prefill-cadence throttle defers new prefills unless the step index is a multiple of prefill_schedule_interval (DPEngineCoreProc._should_throttle_prefills returns True when step_counter % prefill_schedule_interval != 0). With interval=N>1, a newly arriving prefill request that lands on a non-aligned step is forced to wait up to N-1 additional engine steps before any prefill compute is committed, directly increasing time-to-first-token by up to (N-1) forward-pass durations per admission. This is an intentional balancing tradeoff, but it is a real per-request latency regression for the tail of requests that arrive just after a cadence boundary.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP MoE deployment (data_parallel_size>1, enable_expert_parallel) with staggered request arrivals; measure P50/P99 TTFT with prefill_schedule_interval=1 vs 4 under light-to-moderate load.

findings.claim: On throttled steps, defer_prefills causes the scheduler to break out of the waiting-queue loop and skip in-flight prefill chunks (running loop `continue` on request.is_prefill_chunk), so a step that could have filled its token budget with prefill work instead runs decode-only (or fewer tokens). When there are no decodes to overlap with (e.g. a single DP rank has only waiting prefills but the guard `any(not r.is_prefill_chunk for r in self.running)` is False, or decode work is small), the GPU is under-utilized on those steps. Even with the capacity-bound guard, up to interval-1 steps per admission cycle process less prefill token throughput than they otherwise could, reducing aggregate prefill throughput on that rank in exchange for cross-rank alignment.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill-heavy workload on a DP deployment; measure prefill tokens/sec and GPU SM occupancy with prefill_schedule_interval=1 vs >1.

findings.claim: The added `defer_prefills` computation runs `any(not r.is_prefill_chunk for r in self.running)` on every schedule() call when throttle_prefills is True. This is an O(len(running)) scan added to the hot scheduling path. For large running batches this is a small but non-zero per-step cost; it only executes when throttling is active (DP path with interval>1), so impact is limited.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark schedule() wall time with a large running list (e.g. 512 requests) and throttle_prefills=True vs False.

findings.claim: The capacity-bound guard only backs off for one step (prefill_capacity_bound is recomputed each release step). If the waiting queue is persistently non-empty but the throttle keeps re-engaging on non-aligned steps, prefill admission is still gated to cadence-aligned steps most of the time, which can grow the waiting backlog and increase queueing latency under sustained high load with interval>1. The single-step back-off may be insufficient to prevent backlog growth in some arrival patterns.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sustained high-QPS prefill load on DP with interval>1; monitor waiting-queue length and queueing latency over time.

findings.claim: Adding `pipeline_parallel_size == 1` to enable_allreduce_rms_fusion disables the fused all-reduce + RMS-norm kernel for all TP>1 configurations that also use PP>1. In those setups the all-reduce and RMS normalization now run as separate, unfused ops, adding an extra kernel launch / extra memory traffic per layer versus the fused path. This is a real throughput/latency cost for PP>1+TP>1 deployments. However, per the commit message the fused op deadlocks (GPU-side peer-signal spin-wait) under PP>1, so this is a deliberate hang-avoidance tradeoff, not an unintended regression; the fusion could not be safely used there anyway.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-token latency/throughput of a TP=2,PP=2 model at optimization level O2/O3 before vs after this change (before will hang, so the practical comparison is fused-path-on-TP-only vs unfused PP path).

findings.claim: In UBatchWrapper.__call__, `num_tokens` is still computed as `(ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.stop - start) * 2`, hardcoding 2 ubatches. This commit generalizes ubatching to N ubatches (ubatch_size > 2), but this line was not updated. `num_tokens` is used as the CUDA graph cache key (`self.cudagraphs[num_tokens]`). For ubatch_size != 2 the key under/over-counts the true total tokens, so a captured graph is never found again on replay. This forces repeated graph recapture (expensive, and leaks graph memory into the pool) and/or replay of a mismatched shape, degrading throughput and inflating GPU memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run with --ubatch-size=4 and full cudagraphs; measure per-step latency and count of cudagraph captures vs replays across identical token shapes.

findings.claim: In _capture_ubatches, `num_tokens = ubatch_metadata[0].num_tokens + ubatch_metadata[1].num_tokens` sums only the first two ubatches. With ubatch_size > 2 (the new XBO path) the remaining ubatches' tokens are omitted, so the cudagraph is stored under a wrong key and later replay lookups in __call__ will mismatch, causing redundant recaptures / lookup misses and wasted GPU memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture cudagraphs with --ubatch-size=3/4 and verify the stored key equals the replay-time num_tokens; measure recapture frequency.

findings.claim: Two new `logger.debug("ubatch_slices: %s, ...", ubatch_slices, ubatch_slices_padded)` calls are added in the per-forward-step prepare paths. Although %s formatting is deferred, the calls sit in the hot per-step input-preparation path and pass live list objects; when debug logging is enabled they trigger repr() of UBatchSlice lists every step, adding measurable CPU overhead. Minor when debug is off.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable DEBUG logging and measure decode step CPU time vs INFO level with ubatching enabled.

findings.claim: The added check in build_connector_meta runs `all(rs.req.is_finished() for rs in self._req_status.values())` on every scheduler step. This is O(num_tracked_requests) per step and executes even in the common steady-state case where requests are still running (so the `all(...)` will short-circuit quickly on the first non-finished request, but the `if self._req_status` guard means it only runs when there is at least one tracked request). Impact is small because is_finished() is cheap and short-circuits, but it adds a per-step iteration to the scheduler hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark build_connector_meta with many concurrently tracked offloading requests to measure per-step overhead of the all(is_finished()) scan.

findings.claim: When the last tracked request finishes, all pending jobs (both stores and loads) are flushed via `self._current_batch_jobs_to_flush.update(self._jobs.keys())`. Forcing an eager flush of every in-flight job may cause a synchronous drain / early fence that batches transfers less efficiently than allowing them to complete on subsequent steps, potentially reducing effective offloading throughput at request-boundary transitions. This is a correctness-motivated change (avoids stuck jobs with no future step), so the trade-off is likely justified, but it can add a burst of flush work at each drain point.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare offloading transfer batching efficiency / throughput with bursty request completion patterns before and after this change.

findings.claim: For FULL/decode-mode cudagraph descriptors, create_forward_fn(desc) is now invoked twice per descriptor: once at the top of the loop (for warmup) and again immediately before the graph capture to produce a fresh attention state. create_forward_fn -> prepare_inputs_to_capture builds dummy InputBatch, dummy block tables, slot mappings, and calls model_state.prepare_attn(...), so this input-preparation work is duplicated for every FULL graph size. This increases CUDA-graph capture / warmup time (startup latency), scaling linearly with the number of FULL capture sizes. Note this is confined to the one-time capture phase and does NOT affect steady-state inference throughput, latency, GPU utilization, or runtime memory; the fix is required for correctness (backends like FlashMLA do lazy initialization that must be captured inside the graph).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end model warmup / cudagraph capture wall-clock time before vs after this commit for a model using FULL cudagraphs (e.g. DSV4/FlashMLA) across the full set of cudagraph_capture_sizes.

findings.claim: The new MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA.apply() routes every non-tp1 / non-unsharded case through _mcp_apply(), which performs a tensor_model_parallel_all_gather on the LoRA shrink buffers on every forward pass. In the parent code, the standard (non-fully-sharded) merged-column LoRA path went through BaseLinearLayerWithLoRA.apply -> _apply_sync, which calls punica_wrapper.add_lora_linear directly with NO collective communication. For TP>1 non-fully-sharded LoRA deployments this introduces a per-layer all-gather collective into the decode/prefill hot path, adding latency and a synchronization point that was not there before. This can materially reduce throughput and GPU utilization on multi-GPU merged-column layers (gate_up_proj, etc.).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run LoRA serving throughput/latency with TP=2 (or 4), fully_sharded_loras=False, on a model with MergedColumnParallelLinear layers (e.g. gate_up_proj) and compare tokens/s and per-step latency against the parent commit.

findings.claim: ReplicatedLinearWithLoRA.apply() now calls _apply_base_forward -> self.base_layer(x), invoking the full base-layer forward() (bias handling, return_bias tuple packing, potential dtype casts, and any subclass-custom kernel dispatch) instead of the previous direct self.base_layer.quant_method.apply(base_layer, x, bias). This adds Python-level dispatch and possibly extra ops per call on a layer that is invoked once per decode step per replicated linear. In addition, this override bypasses the VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM async overlap path in BaseLinearLayerWithLoRA.apply, so LoRA and base GEMM can no longer be overlapped on separate CUDA streams for these layers, reducing achievable overlap when dual-stream LoRA is enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode latency for a LoRA model with ReplicatedLinear targets both with and without VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM=1, comparing against the parent commit.

findings.claim: The new MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA.apply() and ReplicatedLinearWithLoRA.apply() overrides do not go through BaseLinearLayerWithLoRA.apply(), which is the only place that dispatches to the dual-stream async LoRA path (torch.ops.vllm.lora_linear_async) when VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM is set. As a result, for these wrapped layers the base-layer GEMM and the LoRA shrink/expand can no longer overlap on the auxiliary CUDA stream, losing the intended latency-hiding.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable VLLM_LORA_ENABLE_DUAL_STREAM=1 and measure per-step latency on a merged-column LoRA model vs parent; check for loss of stream overlap via profiler.

findings.claim: is_in_target_modules now allocates set(target_modules) and, in the packed_modules_mapping branch, iterates over the mapping with an any(...) comprehension for every module checked during LoRA target matching. This runs at adapter-load / model-build time (not per token), so runtime inference is unaffected, but adapter load can be marginally slower for very large module lists. Minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure LoRA adapter load time for a model with many target modules against parent.

findings.claim: ModelOptFp8MoEMethod.apply() always routes through the generic Triton fused_experts kernel with use_fp8_w8a8=True. It computes self.cutlass_fp8_supported in __init__ but never uses it in apply(), so even on hardware where CUTLASS FP8 grouped GEMM would be faster, the MoE forward always falls back to the Triton path. This can leave GPU throughput on the table for FP8 MoE inference on Hopper/Blackwell.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Llama4 FP8 MoE decode/prefill throughput on Hopper comparing the Triton fused_experts path vs a CUTLASS FP8 grouped-GEMM path at various batch sizes.

findings.claim: ModelOptFp8Config.from_config calls cls.get_from_keys(config, ["quantization"]) three separate times (for quant_algo, kv_cache_quant_algo, exclude_modules) instead of fetching the quantization dict once. This is a one-time config parse so negligible, but redundant dictionary traversal.

process_weights_after_loading performs a nested Python loop over every expert and each of its two shards, doing a dequant + scaled_fp8_quant per shard on the host-driven loop. For models with many experts this adds many small kernel launches at load time. This is one-time startup cost (not per-inference), so impact on runtime performance is minimal, but it lengthens model load/warmup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load time for a Llama4 ModelOpt FP8 checkpoint with a large number of experts.

findings.claim: When disk offload is enabled and a request's estimated staging bytes exceed the usable budget, the previously single bulk `store.batch_get_into_multi_buffers(...)` call is replaced by a loop of N sequential sub-batch GETs (`_split_disk_offload_load_batches`). Each sub-batch is an independent synchronous/blocking store round trip, so a large prefill that used to be fetched in one call now incurs multiple serialized network+disk round trips, increasing KV-load latency (and reducing GPU utilization since the recv thread blocks longer before marking the request finished). The number of round trips grows with prefill size divided by (budget * VLLM_MOONCAKE_DISK_STAGING_USABLE_RATIO), which the docs note lowering the ratio increases.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure KV-cache load latency (time in KVCacheStoreRecvingThread._handle_request) for a large prefill under standalone-store disk-offload mode, sweeping VLLM_MOONCAKE_DISK_STAGING_USABLE_RATIO (e.g. 0.9 vs 0.3) and prefill length, counting the number of batch_get calls.

findings.claim: When VLLM_MOONCAKE_STORE_TIER_LOG=1, each sub-batch GET is preceded by an extra synchronous `store.batch_get_replica_desc(batch_keys)` RPC plus per-key Python reflection (getattr + try/except predicate calls in _classify_replica_tier) and an unconditional logger.info per sub-batch. This roughly doubles the number of store round trips on the KV-load critical path and adds Python overhead proportional to the number of keys. It is disabled by default, so impact only occurs when the observability flag is set, but users may leave it on in production.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare load-path latency and store RPC count with VLLM_MOONCAKE_STORE_TIER_LOG=0 vs 1 for a batch of many keys.

findings.claim: For every load request (when disk offload budget is set), the code computes `total_staging_bytes = sum(_estimate_disk_offload_staging_bytes(size) for size in size_list_c)`, iterating all keys and their scatter-gather size lists and doing alignment math per key, even for small requests that never need splitting. This adds O(keys x segments) Python work to each load on the recv thread. Minor, but it runs on the hot path unconditionally whenever offload is active.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _handle_request overhead for many small requests with disk offload budget set vs unset.

findings.claim: The `naive_block_assignment` fast path is now gated behind `not fully_sharded`. When `fully_sharded_loras` is enabled, the cheap path (which merely reshapes `topk_ids`, avoiding allocation of `sorted_ids`, `expert_ids`, and `num_tokens_post_pad` and a CUDA `moe_lora_align_block_size` launch) can no longer be taken, forcing the more expensive block-alignment path (three device tensor allocations plus a kernel launch) on every fused-MoE LoRA forward under fully-sharded LoRA. This is likely a correctness fix (naive assignment is incompatible with fully-sharded layout), but it does introduce extra per-forward allocation/launch overhead in the fully-sharded LoRA case relative to the prior behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fused MoE LoRA decode/prefill throughput with fully_sharded_loras=True vs. False, measuring per-step latency and device allocation count in moe_lora_align_block_size.

findings.claim: FusedMoEConfig.__post_init__ now performs `from vllm._aiter_ops import rocm_aiter_ops` on every construction (to populate rocm_aiter_fmoe_enabled / aiter_fmoe_shared_expert_enabled). This adds an import-time lookup and platform capability probe to each config instantiation. FusedMoEConfig is typically built once per layer at load time (not per forward), so the impact is confined to model construction / EP reconfiguration rather than steady-state inference; only a concern if configs are (re)constructed frequently (e.g. elastic EP rescaling, which uses dataclasses.replace and re-runs __post_init__ per MoE module).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time model load and elastic EP reconfigure with and without the added rocm_aiter_ops probe in FusedMoEConfig.__post_init__.

findings.claim: The QK-norm fusion was moved from a compile-time PatternMatcher pass (MiniMaxQKNormPass, applied once during graph compilation and only for decode-size compile ranges) into a runtime `forward_qkv` on MiniMaxText01RMSNormTP that is now 'applied automatically at runtime'. Whatever kernel-selection / workspace-lookup logic the old pass baked into the graph at compile time must now execute on every attention forward call (per layer, per step). For the Lamport fused path this includes calling `get_allreduce_workspace(...)`, which takes a `threading.Lock` (`_cache_lock`) and performs a dict lookup on each invocation. In a decode loop with dozens of MiniMax attention layers per step this adds per-call Python + lock overhead that did not exist when the choice was resolved once at compile time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput (tokens/s) for MiniMax-M2 with tp_size>1, comparing this commit vs parent at small batch (1-8 seqs); also profile per-step CPU time in the attention forward path.

findings.claim: The old approach only ever activated the fused Lamport kernel for compiled decode-size ranges (compile_range.end <= max_token_num, capped at 2048) and let prefill fall through to the plain eager RMSNorm path, and it was off by default. Doing the fusion 'manually' inside forward_qkv means the token-count branch (fused kernel vs. plain allreduce+rms) is now a data-dependent Python branch executed inside a @support_torch_compile'd model. A Python-level branch on qkv.shape[0] inside the compiled region risks guard specialization / recompilation or a graph break, which can degrade throughput relative to the previous statically-specialized compiled graph.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure torch.compile recompilation count / guard failures and prefill+decode mixed-batch latency for MiniMax-M2 before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: get_allreduce_workspace computes comm_size for max_tokens (default path uses min(2048, max_num_batched_tokens)) and allocates a 3x triple-buffered IPC Lamport buffer plus does IPC handle exchange on first call. If the runtime forward_qkv can now be reached with token counts exceeding the workspace's max_tokens (previously guaranteed <= max_token_num by is_applicable_for_range), the fused kernel could either read/write out of the allocated slot or force a fresh (larger) workspace allocation + IPC exchange mid-run, which is expensive. Ensure the runtime path still guards token count against the workspace's max_tokens before selecting the fused kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MiniMax-M2 tp>1 with a large prefill batch (> workspace max_tokens) and verify no repeated workspace reallocation / IPC exchange occurs per step.

findings.claim: For async EPLB the communicator is now auto-selected to 'torch_gloo', which stages every expert-weight transfer through CPU (device->host copy on the sender, gloo CPU-to-CPU transfer, host->device copy on the receiver). The pre-change async path performed direct GPU-to-GPU transfers via torch.distributed batch_isend_irecv (NCCL) on the device group. Routing large expert weights over the host with gloo is dramatically slower (lower bandwidth, extra PCIe copies) than NCCL P2P, degrading EPLB rebalance latency/throughput. This is the new default for all async EPLB configs that don't explicitly override the communicator.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run async EPLB rebalance on a multi-GPU MoE model and compare per-layer expert-weight transfer wall-clock time with communicator='torch_gloo' (new default) vs communicator='torch_nccl', measuring rebalance completion latency and effective transfer bandwidth.

findings.claim: TorchDistGlooStagedEplbCommunicator.execute() calls self._cuda_stream.synchronize() (or current_stream().synchronize()) in the middle of every transfer to wait for D2H copies before issuing gloo ops. This is a hard host-side sync that serializes the async worker stream against all pending GPU work, killing overlap and adding latency each rebalance step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the async EPLB worker with the gloo backend and measure the time spent blocked in stream.synchronize() per transferred layer versus the nccl backend which never forces a full stream sync.

findings.claim: In the gloo staged path, each recv allocates a fresh non-pinned CPU tensor via torch.empty_like(tensor, device='cpu') and each send creates a CPU copy via tensor.to(device='cpu', non_blocking=True). Because the CPU staging tensors are not pinned, the non_blocking=True copies cannot actually run asynchronously and effectively behave as blocking copies; additionally repeated per-transfer CPU allocations add allocator churn. A reusable pinned staging buffer would be faster.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare D2H/H2D copy time for expert-weight-sized tensors using pageable torch.empty(device='cpu') vs pinned memory, and measure allocation overhead of repeatedly creating CPU tensors per rebalance.

findings.claim: EplbCommunicator.set_stream stores the stream on the instance, but for PyNcclEplbCommunicator the stream is captured lazily at send/recv time via self._cuda_stream; if set_stream is not called before the first add_send/add_recv, the pynccl call receives stream=None and falls back to current_stream(), which may not be the async worker's dedicated stream, reducing overlap. The async worker does call set_stream each iteration so this is low risk, but the coupling is fragile.

_resolve_gdn_prefill_backend now calls _is_libs_cu13_install_intact() for the Blackwell (SM10.x) path whenever backend resolution runs. Previously (parent) the SHA-256 rehash of the whole nvidia-cutlass-dsl-libs-cu13 wheel was only reached when backend was in [flashinfer, auto] AND the head_k_dim/runtime gates passed. Now supports_flashinfer = _is_libs_cu13_install_intact() is evaluated for any Blackwell config with head_k_dim==128 & cuda>=13, even when the user explicitly requested backend='cutedsl' or 'triton', doing needless blocking file-hashing I/O over the entire wheel at layer init. It is @functools.cache'd so once-per-process, but it runs an extra time in cases that previously short-circuited.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model-load/first-layer-init time on a Blackwell box with backend='cutedsl' and backend='triton' vs parent; assert the SHA-256 rehash is not performed when cutedsl/triton is explicitly requested.

findings.claim: The new forward_cutedsl path lazily imports chunk_gated_delta_rule_cutedsl inside the forward function, and CuteDSL kernels are JIT-compiled on first invocation. Unlike the FlashInfer SM90 path which emits a warning about first-run JIT cost, the cutedsl path adds a first-call compilation stall on the critical path with no warmup guard visible in the resolver. If not warmed up during _prefill_kernels_warmed_up, the first prefill request will incur kernel JIT latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-request prefill latency with --gdn-prefill-backend cutedsl on SM100 and compare to steady-state to quantify JIT stall; verify warmup runs the cutedsl kernel.

findings.claim: forward_cutedsl always applies l2norm_fwd to q and k when use_qk_l2norm_in_kernel is True (default), performing an extra normalization pass over q/k on the host-launched path. This mirrors the flashinfer path but if the cutedsl kernel could fuse the l2norm it would avoid two extra elementwise kernels per prefill; as written it is an unfused pre-pass.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile prefill with cutedsl backend and count l2norm_fwd kernel launches vs total kernel time.

findings.claim: Enabling AllReduce+RMSNorm fusion by default for -O2/-O3 (fuse_allreduce_rms now defaults to enable_allreduce_rms_fusion instead of False) means that on any TP>1, Hopper+, flashinfer-installed deployment the AllReduceFusionPass is added to the post-grad pass pipeline for every compiled/piecewise subgraph. This adds real per-graph pattern-matching/rewrite work during torch.compile, increasing compilation (startup/warmup) time. Because this is now the default rather than opt-in, all standard TP2/TP4/TP8 Hopper users pay this cost even if the fused flashinfer allreduce is not actually a net win for their tensor sizes (the fusion is only beneficial under the world-size/device-capability-dependent size thresholds in fi_allreduce_fusion_max_size_mb; for communicated tensors above those thresholds the pass either does nothing useful or forces a code path that may be slower than the plain NCCL allreduce). This is a runtime-relevant default flip that should be benchmarked against the prior default (no AR+RMS fusion) for representative TP configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode/prefill throughput and per-token latency on a TP2/TP4/TP8 Hopper (SM90) node with flashinfer installed, comparing fuse_allreduce_rms=False (old default) vs enable_allreduce_rms_fusion (new default), plus measured torch.compile warmup time.

findings.claim: enable_allreduce_rms_fusion() calls has_flashinfer() (and platform capability checks) during config initialization for the default O2/O3 path. has_flashinfer() performs a module-import/availability probe which can be non-trivial (import attempt) on the first call. Because this now runs unconditionally when computing default pass config for O2/O3, it adds a small one-time startup cost even on setups where TP==1 — although the TP>1 short-circuit is evaluated first, so this is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine init/config-resolution wall time with and without the new enable_allreduce_rms_fusion default callback for a TP1 and TP2 setup.

findings.claim: PassConfig.eliminate_noops default was flipped from None to True and the explicit 'eliminate_noops: False' entry was removed from OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_00 (the -O0 'no optimization' level). At -O0 cudagraph_mode is NONE and no torch.compile passes run, so the NoOpEliminationPass is not expected to execute; however, if any code path constructs a PassConfig without going through the O0 override (e.g. custom mode=NONE with an inductor backend, or direct PassConfig usage), the NoOpEliminationPass now runs by default where it previously did not, adding pattern-matching work to graph compilation. Low confidence because in the standard O0 path no passes are invoked.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare compile-time pass execution count and warmup latency for mode=NONE/O0 configurations before vs after removing the explicit eliminate_noops=False override.

findings.claim: This change flips `enable_flashinfer_autotune` from True to False for optimization levels O1, O2, and O3 (the default is O2). With autotune disabled, `kernel_warmup()` skips `flashinfer_autotune()` entirely (see vllm/model_executor/warmup/kernel_warmup.py: `if enable_flashinfer_autotune is False: ... Skipping`). As the docstring of `flashinfer_autotune` states, FlashInfer then falls back to heuristic kernel selection instead of benchmarked best implementations, which 'may be significantly slower'. Concretely this degrades steady-state FlashInfer attention/GEMM/MoE kernel throughput and latency on Hopper (SM 9.0) and Blackwell (SM 10.0) GPUs for all runs at the default optimization level. This is an intentional correctness-vs-performance trade-off (autotune has a correctness bug per the linked issue), but the runtime performance impact is real: lower GPU kernel efficiency for all affected deployments until re-enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Hopper/Blackwell GPU, benchmark end-to-end decode/prefill throughput (tokens/sec) and per-request latency of a FlashInfer-backed model at -O2 with enable_flashinfer_autotune=True vs False to quantify the kernel-selection regression.

findings.claim: The unconditional time.sleep(0.001) adds a fixed 1ms latency penalty to any step where the model did not execute but there are still unfinished requests. This is intended for the WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS / NIXL-handshake case, but the condition (not model_executed and scheduler.has_unfinished_requests()) is broader: it also fires whenever a step legitimately schedules nothing but requests are still in flight (e.g. all requests are blocked awaiting async results, transient scheduling stalls). In those cases the sleep injects a mandatory 1ms delay per iteration into the hot engine loop, which can raise TTFT/tail latency for requests that would otherwise resume immediately once the executor frees up. Since the busy loop is the critical path, gating the sleep only on the actual WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS condition would avoid the latency cost while still fixing the tight-loop GIL starvation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a disaggregated/KV-transfer scenario (WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS path) and a normal decode workload; measure TTFT and per-token latency with and without the sleep to confirm the sleep does not regress the non-remote-KV path where model_executed can be False but requests are pending.

findings.claim: has_unfinished_requests() is now called every step that returns model_executed=False, on the hot path. If that method iterates the waiting/running queues it adds a small per-step CPU cost. This is minor but is an added call in the inner loop compared to before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the engine step loop under high request concurrency to measure the added cost of has_unfinished_requests() per idle step.

findings.claim: SiluAndMulWithClamp now falls back to forward_native (pure PyTorch: two clamps + F.silu + elementwise mul, materializing several intermediate tensors) on ROCm instead of the fused torch.ops._C.silu_and_mul_with_clamp kernel. On CUDA-alike/XPU it uses a single fused kernel; on ROCm every call now launches multiple elementwise kernels and allocates temporaries per token, degrading throughput and increasing memory traffic for MoE shared-expert activations.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark SiluAndMulWithClamp.forward on ROCm vs the fused CUDA kernel for typical MoE shapes (num_tokens x 2d), and end-to-end DeepSeek-V4 decode throughput on ROCm.

findings.claim: AiterFp8BlockScaledMMKernel.apply_block_scaled_mm now unconditionally upcasts/converts As and Bs to fp32 (and .contiguous()) whenever As.dtype != Bs.dtype. This runs on every GEMM invocation (per layer, per forward) rather than once at weight-load time, adding an extra dtype conversion + contiguous copy of the scale tensors on the hot path. If dtypes commonly differ (e.g. e8m0 activation scale vs fp32 weight scale), this is a per-step allocation and conversion cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark AiterFp8BlockScaledMMKernel.apply_block_scaled_mm with mismatched As/Bs dtypes over many iterations to measure added conversion/copy overhead per GEMM.

findings.claim: On ROCm aux_stream_list is None, so attn_gemm_parallel_execute passes aux_streams=None to execute_in_parallel and the four input GEMMs (fused_wqa_wkv + three lighter GEMMs) run serially on the default stream instead of overlapping across aux streams. This removes the multi-stream GEMM overlap optimization on ROCm, reducing GPU utilization / increasing per-step latency versus the CUDA path (expected given no aux streams, but still a measurable serialization).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare DeepSeek-V4 attention step latency on ROCm with serial GEMM execution vs a hypothetical multi-stream path; profile GEMM overlap gaps.

findings.claim: For num_experts 192/320/448/576 on ROCm the LDG width is narrowed to ELTS_PER_LDG=1 (BYTES_PER_LDG_MULTIPLE_64_NARROW = 2 or 4 bytes) instead of the wider 8/4-byte load used for 384. This produces more, smaller global-memory transactions per warp for the gating softplus_sqrt kernel on those expert counts, reducing memory-load efficiency. It is a correctness-driven tradeoff (alignment on WARP_SIZE=64) but does cost bandwidth on ROCm.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark topkGatingSoftplusSqrt kernel on ROCm for num_experts=192/320/448/576 comparing narrow vs wide LDG (where alignment allows) to quantify memory throughput difference.

findings.claim: rocm_fp8_mqa_logits / rocm_fp8_paged_mqa_logits fall back to fp8_*_logits_torch when the aiter Triton module is unavailable. These torch reference paths use per-batch Python loops (fp8_paged_mqa_logits_torch loops over batch and blocks with .tolist() and per-iteration allocations) and full einsum materialization, which are dramatically slower than the fused kernel. If aiter is not installed/tuned on a given ROCm deployment, indexer logits computation becomes a major latency bottleneck.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure indexer logits latency on ROCm with the torch fallback vs the aiter fused kernel for realistic batch/context sizes.

findings.claim: When any `kv_transfer_config` (KV connector) is set, the hybrid KV cache manager is now unconditionally disabled. For models with sliding-window attention or Mamba/hybrid attention layers, this forces the scheduler to allocate a uniform full-size KV cache for every layer instead of the memory-efficient hybrid allocation. That reduces the number of available KV cache blocks, lowering achievable max concurrency and thus throughput, and increases GPU memory footprint. The commit's own warning message acknowledges this performance reduction. The impact applies to all users running a connector (e.g. disaggregated prefill/decode, offloading) regardless of whether their connector actually needs HMA disabled, since there is no per-connector capability check yet (the SupportsHMA gating is only a TODO).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a sliding-window model (e.g. Gemma-2/Mistral SWA) or a Mamba/hybrid model with a KV connector configured vs. without; measure available KV cache blocks / max_num_seqs and steady-state throughput (tokens/s) at high concurrency to quantify the regression from disabling HMA.

findings.claim: The default for VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE was changed asymmetrically from the other version guards in this commit. Every other guard was bumped from "2.10.0.dev" to "2.10.0" (i.e. remaining enabled on the 2.10 release), but use_aot_compile() was bumped to "2.11.0.dev". As a result, after upgrading to Torch 2.10.0, AOT compile is now DISABLED by default, whereas on the prior 2.10.0.dev nightly builds it was enabled by default. AOT-compiled artifacts avoid re-running TorchDynamo tracing/guard evaluation on each captured region, so disabling it by default reintroduces per-run compilation/dispatch overhead (slower startup warmup and, where guards are re-evaluated, extra per-step Python overhead) relative to the prior nightly-based configuration. If this asymmetry is unintentional it is a measurable regression; if intentional (2.10.0 release lacks a needed fix), it is still worth flagging that the default path is now the slower one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model compilation warmup time and steady-state per-step latency on a torch==2.10.0 build with VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE default vs. explicitly forced to 1, for a representative compiled model (e.g. gpt2/Llama), and compare against a 2.9/2.10.0.dev baseline.

findings.claim: The native fused triton_kernels.routing.routing / routing_from_bitmatrix functions were removed in triton_kernels 3.6.0 and replaced by pure-Python reimplementations (legacy_routing / legacy_routing_from_bitmatrix). These run on every MoE forward pass and now perform routing via a chain of host-side ops (optional torch.softmax, topk(), SparseMatrix construction, make_ragged_tensor_metadata, flatten()[combine_indx] gather, RoutingData assembly). If the removed native routing() was a more fused/optimized implementation, this reconstruction can add extra CUDA kernel launches and Python/host overhead per decode step for gpt-oss-style MoE models, which is latency-sensitive at low batch sizes. Impact depends on whether the new path launches more kernels than the removed one; cannot be confirmed from vLLM code alone since the removed implementation lives in triton_kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark legacy_routing()/legacy_routing_from_bitmatrix() latency and kernel-launch count vs. the previous triton_kernels routing() at small M (decode) and large M (prefill) on a gpt-oss MoE model, and compare end-to-end decode latency before/after the triton_kernels 3.5.0->3.6.0 bump.

findings.claim: When draft_vocab_size != target_vocab_size, a new nn.Parameter of shape [draft_vocab_size] (int64) is allocated on the draft model. This is a small one-time device memory cost (draft_vocab_size * 8 bytes, e.g. ~1 MB for a 128k vocab) and does not affect per-step throughput. Previously draft_id_to_target_id was always None (never allocated). This is a functional/correctness change (enabling vocab remapping) rather than a performance regression, and the memory cost is negligible.

The encoder batch-size budget is computed as 5% of TOTAL device memory (current_platform.get_device_total_memory() * 0.05), not free/available memory. At inference time model weights + KV cache already occupy the vast majority of device memory. Using total memory as the budget systematically over-estimates how much headroom exists for encoder activations. Combined with the per-patch cost model (see other finding) this can size encoder chunks large enough to trigger CUDA OOM, which is a hard failure rather than a gradual slowdown.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Gemma4 multimodal inference near KV-cache saturation with a batch of many/large images and confirm the encoder chunk does not push past free memory (compare to torch.cuda.mem_get_info free bytes).

findings.claim: _encoder_bytes_per_patch = hidden_size * 2 * num_hidden_layers models per-patch activation memory as LINEAR in the number of patches. But the encoder is a transformer whose self-attention scores/activations scale QUADRATICALLY with sequence length (patches per item). For high-resolution buckets (large patches_per_item) the true peak memory per encoder call grows much faster than this linear estimate, so _encoder_max_batch can return a chunk size that exceeds available memory and OOMs the encoder call. The estimate also ignores the patch_embedder/pooler intermediate tensors and dtype (assumes 2 bytes/element regardless of fp32 vision-tower outputs).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep image resolution buckets (max_soft_tokens 70..1120) with maximal batch and measure peak encoder activation memory vs the linear estimate to check for underestimation at high patch counts.

findings.claim: After batching the encoder, pooling is still performed in a Python for-loop calling vt.pooler once per image/frame (one unsqueeze(0) + pooler launch per item). For large image/frame counts this serializes many small GPU kernel launches on the host, adding per-item launch overhead that partially offsets the batching win. Pooling could be batched per resolution bucket like the encoder step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare end-to-end vision-encode latency for a batch of e.g. 64 same-resolution frames with per-item pooling vs a batched pooler call.

findings.claim: Building the batched encoder input via torch.cat([item.unsqueeze(0) for item ...]) materializes a fresh contiguous copy of all pixel_values/position_ids in a bucket. When same-resolution inputs already arrive as a stacked contiguous tensor, this is an unnecessary copy; torch.stack/indexing of the existing tensor could avoid the extra allocation and copy for the common same-resolution path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile memory allocation and copy time when a single stacked same-resolution image tensor is passed vs the new per-item cat path.

findings.claim: Every call to sample_tokens and pool is now wrapped with @step_eplb_after(), which unconditionally invokes self.eplb.step() on the hot per-forward-pass path. When EPLB is disabled (the common case), step() still runs a few attribute checks and returns early, but the decorator also adds a Python function-call layer (wraps + kwargs.get lookups) to every decode/prefill step. This is a small but non-zero per-iteration overhead added to the critical decode loop that previously had none. For very high request throughput this decorator indirection is measurable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark decode throughput (tokens/s) with EPLB disabled, before vs after, at high batch size to isolate per-step decorator overhead.

findings.claim: When EPLB is enabled, self.eplb.step() runs on every sample_tokens/pool call. Inside EplbState.step, when log_balancedness is on and the step interval hits, _sync_load_pass performs an all_reduce collective plus several reductions across ranks synchronously on the main forward path, which can stall the decode loop and reduce GPU utilization if not overlapped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark decode latency with EPLB enabled and log_balancedness=True at the step interval boundary vs disabled.

findings.claim: In pool(), postprocess_pool(input_batch) was moved from before AsyncPoolingOutput construction to after it. If postprocess_pool records state used to build the async output (e.g. request bookkeeping), the reordering could change when the CPU-side postprocessing overlaps with the async copy stream. This is likely intentional to overlap postprocess with the async copy, but if postprocess_pool must complete before async_output.get_output() is safe, it could serialize work; verify no added synchronization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare pooling model end-to-end latency (embedding requests/s) before vs after the postprocess_pool reorder.

findings.claim: The gumbel sampling kernel now casts the entire per-block logits vector (BLOCK_SIZE=1024) to tl.float64 and performs all downstream arithmetic in FP64: temperature division, gumbel-noise transform (-log(-log(u))), the tl.where masking, and the tl.max reduction over 1024 elements. This kernel runs on the full vocabulary (grid = num_tokens x cdiv(vocab_size,1024), i.e. 128-256 blocks per token for typical 128K-256K vocabs) and is executed every sampling step. FP64 arithmetic throughput on GPUs is far lower than FP32 (~1/2 on A100/H100 native DP units, but ~1/32-1/64 on inference/consumer GPUs such as L4/L40S/A10/RTX40xx). This significantly slows the elementwise transform and the reduction, and doubles register/shared-memory pressure for the tl.max reduction, reducing occupancy. Only the final uniform value u actually needs the extra precision; casting the whole logits block and reduction to FP64 is broader than necessary.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark gumbel_sample() latency for a single decode step with vocab_size in {128K, 256K} and batch sizes {1, 8, 64} on both a datacenter GPU (H100) and an inference GPU (L4/L40S), comparing this commit vs parent.

findings.claim: local_max is now allocated as torch.float64 instead of float32, doubling that intermediate buffer's size ([num_tokens, num_blocks]) and doubling the bytes moved by the subsequent local_max.argmax(dim=-1) call. Minor but directly attributable to the change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure allocation size and argmax time on local_max for large num_tokens x num_blocks.

findings.claim: tl_rand64 performs two 32-bit randint outputs (via randint4x), 64-bit bit ops (shift/or), and an FP64 multiply per element, versus the previous single tl.rand FP32 call. In the gumbel kernel this executes per vocab element across the whole block, adding extra ALU work on top of the FP64 cost. In the rejection kernel it is only a single scalar call in a num_warps=1 loop, so the added cost there is negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare tl_rand64 vs tl.rand throughput inside a full-vocab elementwise kernel.

findings.claim: `seq_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu` was replaced by `seq_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu()`. The old `seq_lens_cpu` property was memoized in `_seq_lens_cpu`, which the model runner already pre-populates (e.g. `_seq_lens_cpu=self.seq_lens.cpu[:num_reqs_padded]` in gpu_model_runner.py), so accessing it was a cache hit with no device->host copy. `seq_lens.cpu()` unconditionally issues a fresh D2H copy on every build() call. Because the result is immediately used in host-side ops (`.max().item()`, `torch.sum(seq_lens).item()`, slicing/subtraction into `computed_kv_lens`), this forces a CUDA stream synchronization each build, on the per-step attention-metadata critical path, adding latency and potentially defeating the async-scheduling overlap the cached CPU tensor was designed to preserve.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark AiterFlashAttentionMetadataBuilder.build() latency per step on ROCm with a decode-heavy batch, comparing the cached seq_lens_cpu path vs. seq_lens.cpu(); measure end-to-end decode throughput and check for added D2H sync stalls in a profiler trace.

findings.claim: `suffix_kv_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu - common_prefix_len` was replaced by `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu() - common_prefix_len`. Previously the cached `_seq_lens_cpu` tensor (pre-computed by the model runner) was reused with no copy; now every cascade-attention build forces a fresh device->host copy of seq_lens plus the implied CUDA synchronization before the subsequent `.to(self.device)`. This adds a per-build D2H sync on the ROCm attention metadata path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable cascade attention on ROCm and profile RocmAttentionMetadataBuilder.build(); compare per-step build latency and D2H sync count between cached seq_lens_cpu and seq_lens.cpu().

findings.claim: precompute_and_store_context_kv() contains several per-layer Python for-loops each iteration issuing separate CUDA kernel launches: (1) a per-layer ops.rms_norm loop over K (`for i in range(L): ops.rms_norm(all_k_normed[i], ...)`), and (2) a per-layer cache-insert loop calling `attn.impl.do_kv_cache_update(...)` for each of L layers. For a draft model with L attention layers, this is O(L) discrete kernel launches per forward instead of a single fused/batched kernel, adding host-side launch overhead on the critical spec-decode path. The K-norm loop is particularly wasteful because the fused RoPE already runs as one batched op over L*num_ctx, but the RMSNorm was not similarly batched.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark precompute_and_store_context_kv over increasing draft-model layer counts (L) and context lengths, measuring wall time vs a batched-RMSNorm / batched-cache-insert variant to quantify per-layer launch overhead.

findings.claim: Each call to precompute_and_store_context_kv allocates multiple large temporary tensors per step: normed_context_states (empty_like), all_kv_flat (fused GEMM output of size num_ctx*L*2*kv), an explicit .contiguous() copy producing all_kv of shape [2, L, num_ctx, nkv, hd], and all_k_normed (empty_like of the K half). The .contiguous() permute materializes a full extra copy of the entire K/V projection every step. For long contexts this is a per-step allocation + copy proportional to L*num_ctx*hidden that increases memory traffic and peak memory on the spec-decode hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile peak GPU memory and copy bandwidth of precompute_and_store_context_kv across context lengths; compare with an in-place / view-based layout that avoids the .contiguous() materialization.

findings.claim: Enabling DFlash forces parallel_drafting=True (speculative.py), which sets extra_slots_per_request = num_speculative_tokens and reduces scheduler_config.max_num_scheduled_tokens accordingly. The added guard warns when max_num_scheduled_tokens < 8192, indicating that DFlash meaningfully shrinks the effective per-step token budget (each request now reserves num_speculative_tokens extra draft slots). If max_num_batched_tokens is not increased to compensate, batch throughput drops because fewer real tokens can be scheduled per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end throughput test enabling dflash with default max_num_batched_tokens vs. a raised value, measuring tokens/s and effective batch size to quantify the scheduling-budget reduction from parallel-drafting extra slots.

findings.claim: precompute_and_store_context_kv lazily builds fused KV buffers via _build_fused_kv_buffers() guarded by `if not hasattr(self, '_num_attn_layers')`. If for any reason the buffers weren't pre-built at load time, the first hot-path call performs a full torch.cat over all layer KV weights (an O(L*kv*hidden) allocation) inline during inference, causing a one-time large stall on the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-step latency of DFlash proposal when buffers are built lazily vs. eagerly at load time.

findings.claim: In the new LoRA path of Qwen3_5GatedDeltaNet.forward, the fused single-GEMM input projection (in_proj_qkvz, computed inside the fused custom op torch.ops.vllm.gdn_in_proj which also fused in the ba projection) is replaced by three separate matmuls: self.in_proj_qkv(hidden_states), self.in_proj_ba(hidden_states), and self.in_proj_z(hidden_states). This splits what was previously one/two combined GEMMs (qkvz merged, and the custom op that co-schedules the ba projection) into three separate CUDA kernel launches per linear-attention layer per forward step, increasing kernel-launch overhead and losing the memory-bandwidth benefit of a single fused projection. Since this path is only taken when LoRA is enabled (a functional requirement), the degradation is scoped to LoRA runs, but it does add per-layer per-step overhead relative to the non-LoRA fused path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Qwen3.5 decode throughput (tokens/s) and per-layer forward latency with enable_lora=True vs the non-LoRA fused gdn_in_proj path, at small batch (decode) where kernel-launch overhead dominates.

findings.claim: The LoRA path bypasses the torch.ops.vllm.gdn_in_proj custom op entirely. That custom op previously encapsulated the input projection (and may have provided multi-stream/aux-stream scheduling or torch.compile-friendly boundaries). Calling the linear layers directly in eager Python inside the compiled region may prevent the projection from being co-scheduled/overlapped as in the fused op, and changes how torch.compile sees the graph, potentially reducing GPU overlap for the linear-attention layers under LoRA.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GPU utilization / stream overlap (nsys trace) for linear-attention layers in the LoRA path vs the fused gdn_in_proj path.

findings.claim: In the NVFP4 KV-cache path, every attention forward (both prefill and decode) writes the attention result into a temporary FP8 buffer (`_nvfp4_fp8_out`) and then executes `output[...].copy_(out.to(output.dtype))`. This adds an extra full-size dtype-conversion kernel plus a device-to-device copy of the whole attention output on the critical path, for every attention layer, on every step. Compared to the non-quantized/fp8 paths (which write directly into `output`), this is a measurable per-layer overhead in kernel launches and HBM bandwidth. It only triggers when the trtllm-gen NVFP4 kernel is used (which forces FP8 output), so it is inherent to the feature, but it should be avoided where possible (e.g. by having the model consume FP8 output or fusing the dequant).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode/prefill latency and attention-kernel time for a model with --kv-cache-dtype nvfp4 on SM100, comparing forward() time with and without the extra copy (e.g. vs an fp8 KV cache of the same model).

findings.claim: Each FlashInferImpl instance (one per attention layer) that uses NVFP4 KV cache pre-allocates `_nvfp4_fp8_out` of shape (max_num_batched_tokens, num_heads, head_size) in FP8. With many attention layers and a large max_num_batched_tokens this persistent scratch adds up (num_layers * max_num_batched_tokens * num_heads * head_size bytes), reducing memory available for the KV cache. Since each layer's forward is serialized, a single shared/builder-level buffer would suffice instead of one per layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure resident GPU memory / achievable KV-cache blocks for a many-layer model with --kv-cache-dtype nvfp4 and a large --max-num-batched-tokens, vs sharing one scratch buffer.

findings.claim: The new nvfp4 branch in base AttentionSpec.real_page_size_bytes multiplies the packed full_dim (already in bytes: head_size//2 + head_size//16) by get_dtype_size(self.dtype). For nvfp4 the spec dtype is uint8 (size 1) so this happens to be correct, but if self.dtype were ever a wider dtype this would over-count page size, inflating estimated KV-cache page bytes and reducing the number of allocatable blocks (lower effective KV cache capacity / throughput). The formula mixing packed byte counts with a dtype-size multiplier is fragile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Verify computed page_size_bytes for nvfp4 matches the actual allocated kv-cache shape bytes; check number of KV blocks allocated.

findings.claim: In the non-monolithic (Triton) MoE path, routing capture inserts a per-layer `routing_replay_out[:topk_ids.shape[0]].copy_(topk_ids.to(torch.int16))` after `select_experts()` in the MoE runner. This runs once per MoE layer per forward pass (e.g. 40 layers) on the critical compute stream and includes both a dtype cast (int32->int16, allocating a temporary tensor) and a device-to-device copy into the persistent buffer. The change's own design doc measures ~2% throughput degradation, dominated by these per-layer copies. Only incurred when --enable-return-routed-experts is set, but it is a real steady-state slowdown of the forward pass whenever enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput (tokens/s) with a MoE model (e.g. 40 layers, top-k routing) with vs. without --enable-return-routed-experts at ISL=1024/OSL=1024.

findings.claim: Routing data now flows from the worker through ModelRunnerOutput (Ray DAG) to the scheduler every step. ModelRunnerOutput is explicitly documented as expensive to serialize ('This is expensive for torch.Tensor so prefer to use list instead'). Carrying per-step routing arrays of shape [total_tokens, num_layers, top_k] int16 through the worker->scheduler serialization boundary each step adds per-step serialization/IPC cost proportional to tokens*L*K, growing the size of every ModelRunnerOutput while the feature is active. This is on the per-step critical path between worker and scheduler.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step scheduler round-trip latency / inter-token latency (ITL) with routing replay enabled vs disabled, especially multi-node Ray DAG.

findings.claim: In the OpenAI serving paths, `output.routed_experts.tolist()` and `final_res.prompt_routed_experts.tolist()` convert numpy int16 arrays of shape [gen_len/prompt_len, num_layers, top_k] into nested Python lists for the JSON response. For a 100-token prompt with 40 layers and top-22 routing this is ~88k Python ints (and the gen array grows with output length); .tolist() materializes millions of Python objects across a batch and inflates the JSON payload substantially, adding latency to response finalization. Feature-gated, but expensive per finished request when enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time to finalize a completion response (per request) with large prompt_len/gen_len and many MoE layers, measuring .tolist()+JSON encode cost.

findings.claim: A persistent GPU device buffer of shape (num_layers, max_num_batched_tokens, top_k) int16 is allocated on every TP rank (the doc notes ~14 MB per GPU for 40 layers / 8192 tokens / top-22), plus per-request lazily-grown host numpy caches and a pinned staging buffer. All TP ranks allocate the device buffer for CUDA-graph symmetry even though only rank 0 uses the host pipeline, so the GPU memory cost is paid on all ranks. Modest but non-zero and scales with max_num_batched_tokens*num_layers*top_k.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak GPU memory (per rank) with vs. without --enable-return-routed-experts at large max_num_batched_tokens.

findings.claim: `get_current_memory_usage` on ROCm now calls `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` on every invocation. This forces a device synchronization and releases cached allocator blocks back to the driver, which is a relatively expensive operation. This function is used by `DeviceMemoryProfiler.current_memory_usage()` (invoked around model weight loading) and by `MemUtils.get_current_memory_usage`. It runs only a handful of times during startup/profiling (not per inference step), so the impact is bounded to a small one-time startup cost and now matches the existing CUDA implementation. Note also the returned semantic changed from total-minus-free device memory to torch `max_memory_allocated`, which excludes non-torch allocations; this is a behavior change but aligns ROCm with CUDA.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model load / memory-profiling wall time on a ROCm device before and after this change to confirm the added empty_cache() sync does not materially extend startup.

findings.claim: The native OCP-MX GEMM path now adds an explicit `y = y + bias` tensor addition after `gemm_with_dynamic_quant` when bias is present. This is an extra elementwise kernel launch per forward call for biased projections (e.g. qkv_proj with qkv_bias). The cost is small relative to the GEMM and is required for correctness (bias was previously silently dropped), but it is a genuine additional per-forward op on the ROCm inference hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark forward latency of a quark OCP-MX linear layer with bias enabled to quantify the added elementwise add overhead.

findings.claim: enable_allreduce_rms_fusion() changed the GPU gate from has_device_capability(90) (True for ANY capability >= 90) to is_device_capability(100) or is_device_capability(90) (True ONLY for exactly sm_90 or exactly sm_100). is_device_capability uses `==` on the integer capability. As a result, GPUs whose compute capability is neither exactly 90 nor exactly 100 but was previously >= 90 (e.g. sm_103, sm_120 Blackwell workstation parts, and any future >= sm_90 arch) will no longer enable the all-reduce + RMS-norm fusion pass. On those platforms the fused collective+norm kernel is replaced by separate allreduce and rmsnorm ops, adding a kernel launch and an extra memory round-trip per norm on the TP critical path, reducing throughput and increasing latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a TP>1 sm_120 (or non-90/100) CUDA node with flashinfer, run a decode/prefill throughput benchmark at -O2/-O3 before vs after; compare tokens/s and per-step latency with fuse_allreduce_rms on vs off.

findings.claim: The added `and cfg.parallel_config.data_parallel_size == 1` condition unconditionally disables the all-reduce+RMS fusion for EVERY TP>1 configuration that also uses DP>1, regardless of model or quantization. The linked issue (34458) concerns a specific broken TP-DP combination, but this gate is applied globally, so previously-working TP>1+DP>1 deployments lose the fused collective+norm kernel and pay extra allreduce/rmsnorm kernel launches and memory traffic per layer, lowering throughput and raising latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP>1, DP>1 deployment on Hopper/Blackwell with flashinfer at -O2: measure tokens/s and step latency with the DP==1 gate removed vs present to quantify the fusion loss for DP>1.

findings.claim: DeepseekV3ForCausalLM.verify_and_update_config now disables fuse_allreduce_rms by default whenever the model is NVFP4-quantized (fuse_allreduce_rms is None -> set False). This is a correctness workaround (issue 34395) but it removes the AR-RMS fusion optimization for all DeepSeekV3/V3.2 NVFP4 runs, so those models pay separate allreduce + rmsnorm kernels on the TP path where they previously (attempted to) fuse. Also every DeepseekV32 config now runs the extra super().verify_and_update_config work plus is_nvfp4_quantized() at startup; this is one-time config cost, negligible at runtime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeekV3 NVFP4 checkpoint, TP>1 on Blackwell: compare tokens/s with fuse_allreduce_rms forced on vs the new default-off to quantify the optimization that is being intentionally given up.

findings.claim: The CUDA stream `priority` argument was removed from both offloading handlers. Previously CPU->GPU loads used priority=-1 (higher) and GPU->CPU offloads used priority=1 (lower), so that latency-critical prefetch/load transfers would be scheduled ahead of background offload work on GPUs supporting stream priorities. Now all transfer streams are created with default priority, so background KV offloads can compete on equal footing with loads (and with model compute), potentially delaying latency-sensitive load transfers. The new wait_stream + deferred-store logic mitigates but does not fully replace the prioritization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent load+offload workload measuring load-transfer completion latency with vs without stream priorities under heavy offload traffic.

findings.claim: For GPU->CPU offloads, the offload stream now issues stream.wait_stream(current_stream()) before the copy. This forces the offload to wait for ALL work currently enqueued on the default/compute stream to complete before it begins, reducing the overlap between offload D2H copies and subsequent model compute. This is the intended correctness fix (offload must not read KV before compute writes it), but it does reduce achievable copy/compute overlap versus the previous (incorrect) behavior, so offload completion latency will increase.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step offload completion time and end-to-end decode throughput with offloading enabled, comparing overlap before/after.

findings.claim: Store transfers are no longer submitted immediately in prepare_store_kv; they are appended to _unsubmitted_store_jobs and only submitted at the start of the NEXT engine step (start_kv_transfers). This delays offload start by one engine step, which in turn delays store completion and thus delays freeing/reuse of the source GPU KV blocks that must be retained until the store finishes. Under heavy offloading this can increase GPU KV-cache block residency and memory pressure by roughly one step's worth of pending stores.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track pending store count and GPU block-pool utilization per step with high offload rate, comparing immediate vs deferred submission.

findings.claim: Previously `max_seq_len` was the constant `self.max_model_len` (zero per-step cost). The change replaces it with `int(seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound[:num_reqs].max().item())`, adding a per-step CPU reduction plus a Python scalar extraction (`.item()`) on every non-capture build_metadata call (both DefaultModelState and WhisperModelState). This is host-only work (numpy-backed CPU tensor, no D2H GPU sync), so it is cheap and O(num_reqs), and a tighter max_seq_len can actually reduce attention-kernel work — but it is new per-iteration Python/host overhead that did not exist before and runs on the critical dispatch path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark build_metadata / prepare_attn latency (CPU wall time) at large batch sizes (e.g. 256 reqs) before vs after, focusing on the per-step .max().item() cost.

findings.claim: build_input_batch now allocates a fresh `np.zeros(num_reqs_padded)` and runs `np.add(..., out=...)` every step to materialize seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound_np, then wraps it in a torch tensor via torch.from_numpy. This is a new per-step host allocation + O(num_reqs) fill on the critical path. Reusing a preallocated persistent buffer (as done for optimistic_seq_lens_cpu in the other runner) would avoid the per-step allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step CPU allocation churn in build_input_batch at high request counts; compare against a preallocated-buffer variant.

findings.claim: Two hot paths (execute_model and sample) now each perform `self.req_states.num_computed_tokens_np[idx_mapping_np] += input_batch.num_scheduled_tokens`, a fancy-indexed numpy scatter-add executed every step. This is small O(num_reqs) host work but is duplicated maintenance of a CPU mirror of GPU state; the net effect is still a large win versus the removed D2H syncs, but it does add unconditional per-step host bookkeeping even for backends that never consume seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration host time of the num_computed_tokens_np scatter-add at max_num_seqs; verify it is negligible relative to removed sync savings.

findings.claim: The new admission gate calls kv_cache_manager.can_fit_full_sequence() for every waiting request being considered, and can_fit_full_sequence() internally invokes self.coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate() over all single-type KV cache managers. This is exactly the same block-requirement computation that allocate_slots() performs immediately afterward (allocate_slots also calls coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate). For every request that IS admitted, this block-accounting work is now paid TWICE per scheduling step. Since scheduling runs on the hot path once per engine step and iterates the waiting queue, this doubles that portion of scheduler CPU overhead for admitted prefill requests. With scheduler_reserve_full_isl defaulting to True, all users pay this cost by default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark scheduler.schedule() latency (or end-to-end throughput at high QPS with many waiting requests) with scheduler_reserve_full_isl=True vs False, using short prompts so scheduler overhead dominates over model compute.

findings.claim: scheduler_reserve_full_isl defaults to True, so the extra can_fit_full_sequence() admission check (an additional per-request get_num_blocks_to_allocate pass) is enabled for every deployment by default. Even setups that never over-admit (e.g. no chunked prefill, or KV cache far larger than max_model_len) unconditionally incur this extra per-request scheduling work each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure schedule() CPU time per step with the feature default (True) vs explicitly False under a workload with a large backlog of waiting requests.

findings.claim: When can_fit_full_sequence() returns False the loop executes `break`, terminating admission of the entire waiting queue for this step rather than skipping just this request. Because admission now requires enough free blocks for the FULL input sequence length (not just the first chunk), under memory pressure fewer requests are admitted per step and long-ISL requests can block admission of shorter ones behind them. This can lower batch occupancy / GPU utilization and throughput compared to chunked-prefill's previous first-chunk-only admission, especially with a mix of long and short prompts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare running-batch size, KV cache utilization, and throughput with mixed short/long prompts under memory pressure, scheduler_reserve_full_isl=True vs False.

findings.claim: `supports_kw` is now wrapped with a bare `@lru_cache` (default maxsize=128). Several callers pass bound methods rather than unbound functions/types, e.g. `_check_vllm_model_init` calls `supports_kw(model.__init__, ...)` and `_check_vllm_model_forward` calls `supports_kw(getattr(model, 'forward'), ...)` when `model` is an instance. Bound methods hash/compare on `(func, instance)`, so distinct model instances produce distinct cache keys, reducing hit rate and causing the 128-slot cache to retain strong references to model objects/instances (and their bound methods), keeping them alive until evicted. When `model` is a class/type the key is stable and caching works well, but for instance inputs the cache is largely ineffective while still holding references. Impact is bounded by maxsize=128 so it is minor; overall the change is a net win for the common (type-keyed) path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated calls to is_vllm_model / _check_vllm_model_forward on both class objects and many distinct model instances, measuring cache hit ratio (supports_kw.cache_info()) and retained-object count before vs after the change.

findings.claim: The new draft_logits buffer added to RequestState is sized [max_num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, vocab_size] in the model dtype and is allocated whenever cache_draft_logits is True (i.e. rejection_sample_method != 'strict'). For a typical config (e.g. max_num_reqs=256, num_speculative_steps=4, vocab_size=128k, fp16) this is 256*4*128000*2 bytes ≈ 256 MB of GPU memory reserved for the entire lifetime of the runner. Larger vocabs / more spec steps / larger batch push this into the multi-GB range, permanently reducing KV-cache space and thus max concurrency/throughput. The config docstring itself notes 'more memory to cache draft logits' but this is a large persistent allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory reserved (torch.cuda.memory_reserved) and resulting KV cache block count with cache_draft_logits=True vs strict, across vocab_size and num_speculative_steps.

findings.claim: The gumbel_sample kernel now always applies temperature division inside the main-path (moved out of the temp!=0 block) and, when processed_logits_out is provided, additionally does a full [num_reqs, vocab_size] store of the temperature-applied logits on every draft step during speculation. This extra global-memory write of the entire vocab per request per spec step (num_speculative_steps writes per request) adds significant HBM write bandwidth in the spec-decode hot path even before any rejection sampling occurs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the eagle draft step (propose) latency with cache_draft_logits enabled vs disabled at a fixed batch size and vocab_size, isolating the added processed_logits store.

findings.claim: probabilistic_rejection_sample computes torch.softmax over the full target_logits ([num_draft_tokens+num_reqs, V]) and draft_logits ([num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, V]) on every spec-decode step. These are dense softmaxes over the entire vocabulary for the whole batch's speculative logits, which is substantial extra compute/memory-bandwidth compared to the previous strict path that only compared already-sampled token IDs (a tiny int comparison kernel). This runs on the critical decode path whenever probabilistic sampling is enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark end-to-end decode throughput and per-step latency with rejection_sample_method='probabilistic' vs 'strict' at various vocab sizes and speculative token counts.

findings.claim: The probabilistic rejection kernel launches with num_warps=1 and one program per request, doing a serial python-style loop over num_tokens-1 speculative steps with scalar loads (target_prob, draft_prob) and a per-step tl.rand. With single-warp occupancy this is likely under-utilizing the GPU; combined with the residual-logits kernel launch, the added launch/sync overhead per decode step can be non-trivial for small batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare kernel launch count and small-batch decode latency between strict and probabilistic rejection sampling.

findings.claim: probabilistic_rejection_sample allocates several new tensors per call (target_probs, draft_probs softmax outputs, sampled, rejected_steps, residual_logits [num_reqs, vocab_size], residual_pos). These per-step allocations (especially the two full softmax tensors over vocab) add allocator pressure on the decode hot path compared to the previous strict kernel which allocated only two small tensors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Track CUDA caching allocator alloc/free counts and peak memory per decode step in probabilistic vs strict mode.

findings.claim: supports_trtllm_attention() now uses is_device_capability(100) (exact CC 10.0 / SM100) instead of is_device_capability_family(100) (any SM10x). This is a correctness fix for the SM103 (GB300) hang, but it also disables the TRTLLM attention fast path on GB300 and any other SM10x device that is not exactly SM100. On GB300 the change trades a hang for a working-but-slower fallback (FlashInfer native / other backend), so decode/prefill attention throughput on GB300 will be lower than TRTLLM would provide if it worked. This is the correct trade-off (a slow kernel beats a hang), but it is a measurable performance reduction relative to a device where TRTLLM works. Note that other TRTLLM/Blackwell paths (MoE, MLA, deep_gemm, etc.) still use is_device_capability_family(100), so only attention is narrowed here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Attention decode/prefill throughput on GB300 (SM103) before vs after: measure whether the fallback backend materially reduces tokens/s vs TRTLLM on SM100.

findings.claim: Because the check is now exact-match (== CC 10.0), any future SM10x SKU that would benefit from TRTLLM attention will silently fall back to a slower backend until it is explicitly added, potentially leaving performance on the table on newer 10.x parts. Consider an allowlist of validated capabilities rather than a single exact value.

The new parent-side `_startup_prefetch_weights` reads *all* weight shards (all safetensors/bin files) into the OS page cache, but the existing in-child prefetch (`_prefetch_all_checkpoints` in weight_utils.py) shards files by rank (`sorted_files[rank::world_size]`) and is often gated on filesystem type / available-RAM checks (auto-prefetch is skipped on NFS or when weights don't fit in RAM). The parent version has no RAM-fit or filesystem check and reads the entire model. On hosts where the model does NOT fit in page cache (large 70B+ / MoE checkpoints on machines with limited RAM), the parent prefetch will read tens of GB that get evicted before the child reads them, causing wasted disk bandwidth and, worse, evicting other useful pages — a net slowdown rather than a speedup. It also runs even when the in-child auto-prefetch would deliberately be disabled (e.g. NFS), defeating that safeguard.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache startup time for a model larger than host RAM on NFS vs local disk, measuring total time-to-first-token and total bytes read from disk (iostat) with and without the parent prefetch.

findings.claim: The parent-side prefetch uses a ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8) reading in 16MB blocks. The in-child prefetch (which starts ~10-15s later) also uses 8 threads. Because the parent prefetch thread is a daemon that is never joined and only bounded by finishing all reads, for large models it will still be actively reading when the child EngineCore begins its own 8-thread prefetch of the same files. This produces up to 16 concurrent readers on the same shards, doubling random-access pressure on the storage device and potentially slowing the child's reads (the ones on the critical path) rather than accelerating them, especially on spinning disk or throughput-capped network storage.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine model-load phase duration on a bandwidth-limited disk with the parent prefetch enabled vs disabled; check whether concurrent parent+child reads reduce effective per-reader throughput.

findings.claim: Switching the default worker multiproc method to `forkserver` with `vllm.v1.engine.async_llm` preloaded means the forkserver process imports torch/CUDA-adjacent modules once, then all EngineCore workers are forked from it. If the preloaded forkserver process initializes any CUDA context or module-level state before fork, forked children can inherit a broken/duplicated CUDA state (CUDA is not fork-safe), which can cause hangs or reinitialization overhead. Even if async_llm avoids CUDA at import, preloading a heavy module into the forkserver adds a fixed cost paid on the `serve` path regardless of whether the forkserver is ultimately used (e.g. if the user overrides the multiproc method).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare EngineCore worker spawn time and correctness (no CUDA re-init hang) under forkserver vs spawn for TP>1 across CUDA driver versions.

findings.claim: The `_bg_preload_torch` background thread imports torch/transformers concurrently with the main thread's imports. Python's per-module import lock serializes same-module imports across threads; if the main thread reaches `import torch` (via vllm.logger -> vllm/__init__ -> env_override) while the background thread holds torch's import lock but is executing Python-level init (not releasing the GIL in .so dlopen), the main thread blocks. The claimed overlap only materializes during .so file I/O; the remaining Python-level torch init still contends for the GIL, so the practical speedup may be much smaller than the ~2s implied, and in the worst case adds thread scheduling overhead with no benefit.
