findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `vllm serve --help` and full startup wall-clock with and without the background torch/transformers preload thread across warm and cold disk caches.

findings.claim: For local-path models that fully fit in page cache, the parent prefetch reads every shard end-to-end (read into page cache and discarded). This is pure extra CPU/IO work up front; it only pays off if the child reads the same files soon after and they stay cached. If the child uses mmap-based safetensors loading with lazy faulting or a different sharding, some prefetched pages may never be touched, wasting the prefetch read.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare total bytes read (parent+child) and startup time for a small model that comfortably fits in RAM, with and without parent prefetch.

findings.claim: The eager-execution hot path in HelionKernelWrapper.__call__ now performs an extra get_proxy_mode() lookup on every kernel invocation to decide between the HOP-tracing path and eager execution. get_proxy_mode() reads the current torch dispatch/proxy mode stack via thread-local state; while individually cheap, this is a Python-level check added to a path that may be called once per kernel invocation. For kernels called at high frequency this adds a small fixed per-call overhead. Note the change also removed the previous per-call hasattr/getattr on torch.ops.vllm_helion, so net eager overhead may be roughly neutral; benchmarking is warranted to confirm it is not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated eager invocation of a HelionKernelWrapper-wrapped kernel (e.g. 10^5 calls) comparing parent vs this commit to measure per-call Python overhead of get_proxy_mode() vs the removed hasattr/getattr.

findings.claim: get_configured_op() now memoizes the ConfiguredHelionKernel in self._configured_kernel, avoiding reconstruction of ConfiguredHelionKernel (which re-loads platform configs via ConfigManager and re-decorates the kernel) that could otherwise recur. This is a positive change for the HOP/eager path, but note that in the CustomOp fallback path (_get_or_register_custom_op) the previous fast-return-if-already-registered behavior is preserved separately; no regression, informational only.

In the naive_block_assignment path, each block (pid_m) is forced to correspond to a single token: `offs_token = tl.where(offs==0, pid_m, num_valid_tokens)`. This means only 1 of BLOCK_SIZE_M rows in each block is a valid token (token_mask true only for offs==0), so the fused_moe_kernel computes a full [BLOCK_SIZE_M, BLOCK_SIZE_N] tile per token but masks out (BLOCK_SIZE_M - 1) rows. GPU tensor-core throughput is effectively 1/BLOCK_SIZE_M (typically 1/16 or 1/32) of the tile it launches. This is only a win when the number of experts is huge relative to tokens*top_k (guarded by SPARSITY_FACTOR<=global_num_experts), but within that regime it wastes most of each block's compute vs. a packed layout.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark fused_experts for m in {1,2}, top_k in {1,2}, e in {128,256}, comparing naive path vs. moe_align path throughput/latency (test_naive_block_assignment_moe shapes).

findings.claim: For the naive path EM = num_tokens * config['BLOCK_SIZE_M'] and grid uses triton.cdiv(EM, BLOCK_SIZE_M) = num_tokens blocks in the M direction. But expert_ids = curr_topk_ids.view(-1) has length num_tokens (M*top_k) while max_num_tokens_padded/num_tokens_post_padded is set to topk_ids.numel()*BLOCK_SIZE_M. The early-exit `if pid_m*BLOCK_SIZE_M >= num_tokens_post_padded: return` uses num_tokens_post_padded = numel*BLOCK_SIZE_M which is much larger than EM, so it never prunes any blocks; the kernel launches one full N-tiled block per token regardless of expert collision, increasing launched program count relative to a compact assignment.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count launched Triton programs / measure kernel time for small-M naive path vs baseline moe_align path.

findings.claim: The naive path allocates a new num_tokens_post_padded tensor and issues a `.fill_()` on the GPU every chunk/every call (torch.empty + fill_). This adds a small host launch and a device kernel per invocation that could be avoided (e.g. reuse a cached constant tensor), a minor per-call overhead in the hot MoE path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-call CPU/launch overhead of fused_experts_impl in the naive path for decode-sized batches.

findings.claim: The eager backend now always partitions the joint graph with min_cut_rematerialization_partition (via get_wrapped_partitioner passing min_cut_rematerialization_partition), replacing the previous default_partition-style logic that only invoked min-cut when has_recomputable_ops(joint_module) was true. Min-cut aggressively selects nodes to recompute in the backward pass to save memory; recomputing forward ops (allgather-fused casts, aliasing/transpose ops, and — with autocast — parameter downcasts) adds extra GPU work in the backward pass. The commit's own benchmark table confirms measurable throughput regressions from this: eager+autocast 551.92ms -> 571.24ms (~+3.5%) and eager+bf16 419.87ms -> 445.76ms (~+6.2%). This trades peak memory for runtime, and the added recompute is unavoidable per-step for the life of the model.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration forward+backward wall time (and backward-only time) for a ZeRO3 model under DeepCompile eager backend with autocast/bf16 before vs after this commit, matching the gist configuration.

findings.claim: The author's own TODO in _recompute_param_aliases states 'min_cut_rematerialization_partition may recompute more nodes than required for ZeRO3. Need investigate its performance implications.' Because MUST_RECOMPUTE is set on all cast ops and no_copy_ops that transitively consume a gathered parameter (not just the minimal set needed to avoid saving the gathered param), the partitioner can recompute more forward work than the previous targeted approach, increasing backward-pass compute. The exact overhead is graph-dependent and unquantified.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare the number of recomputed nodes and backward FLOPs/time between the old choose_saved_values_set path and the new MUST_RECOMPUTE tagging on representative ZeRO3 graphs.

findings.claim: min_cut_rematerialization_partition builds a data-flow network and runs a min-cut/max-flow computation over the joint graph, which is more expensive than the previous linear default_partition scan used when no recomputable ops were present. This increases one-time compilation/partitioning time per graph; not a per-iteration runtime cost but affects warmup/compile latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure DeepCompile partition_fn wall time (opt_pass_times / partitioning phase) per graph before vs after the switch to min-cut for both eager and inductor backends.

findings.claim: For the inductor backend, wrap_partition_fn now wraps the incoming partition_fn with get_wrapped_partitioner(True, ...) which tags nodes with MUST_RECOMPUTE/MUST_SAVE before delegating, instead of only patching choose_saved_values_set. This changes which activations inductor recomputes; the commit's benchmark shows inductor+autocast regressing 546.97ms -> 570.09ms (~+4.2%) and slightly higher memory (12.84 -> 13.04 GB), indicating the new recompute selection is a net runtime/memory pessimization for the inductor+autocast case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark inductor backend with autocast: per-iteration time and peak memory before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: The non-drop-and-pad `permute` path was changed from a linear `masked_select` (with a `torch.arange` expand) to `routing_map.reshape(-1).argsort(descending=True, stable=True)`. The argsort operates on the full flattened routing map of size `num_experts * num_tokens`, giving O(E*N log(E*N)) work instead of the previous O(E*N) masked_select. For large token counts and expert counts this is a materially more expensive kernel on the critical MoE dispatch path (executed on every forward for every MoE layer). Additionally `probs.T.contiguous().reshape(-1)[flat_sorted]` gathers over the full E*N flattened prob tensor instead of a masked_select of only the selected entries, increasing memory traffic. This is done for CUDA-graph fixed-shape compatibility, but it degrades throughput of the eager/non-graph path that still calls this function.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark `permute()` for a MoE layer with realistic num_tokens (e.g. 8192) and num_experts (e.g. 64-128), comparing masked_select vs argsort-based implementation for forward latency and peak memory.

findings.claim: In `_forward_mlp` (partial cudagraph / MoE training path) a `self._router_dtoh_event.record()` followed by `self._router_dtoh_event.synchronize()` was added after the router graph replay. `event.synchronize()` blocks the host (CPU) until the router's D2H copies complete on every MoE layer forward. This introduces a mandatory CPU-GPU synchronization per MoE layer per microbatch, serializing the host against the device and removing the ability to overlap subsequent CPU-side launch work with GPU execution. On models with many MoE layers this can noticeably reduce GPU utilization / increase step time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile MoE training step (with rl_training_cuda_graphs, partial scope) with and without the event.synchronize() to measure per-step latency and GPU idle gaps via nsys.

findings.claim: `PackedSeqParams.__post_init__` now unconditionally builds `seq_idx` via `torch.cat`, subtraction, `torch.arange`, and `torch.repeat_interleave` whenever `total_tokens` is set and cu_seqlens is a Tensor. This runs on every PackedSeqParams construction (per forward step in packed/RL paths), launching several small CUDA kernels even for non-Mamba models that never consume `seq_idx`. Previously this work was done lazily inside the Mamba mixer only when sequence packing was active. For attention-only or dense transformer configs that set total_tokens (e.g. via get_logprobs default single-sequence path), this is wasted GPU work and extra kernel launches per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step overhead of PackedSeqParams construction (kernel launch count / CPU time) for a non-Mamba packed-sequence config where seq_idx is never used.

findings.claim: The new fallback in `make_weak_ref` catches RuntimeError and returns the original tensor (strong reference) instead of a weak ref. When triggered (e.g. float64 dtypes), captured graph inputs retain strong references and are not freed, increasing memory footprint of the cudagraph pool. This is a correctness/robustness fallback but has a stated memory overhead; if it fires broadly it could grow memory during long runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture graphs with a config that triggers the fallback dtype and compare peak GPU memory vs the weak-ref path.

findings.claim: The new `input_fits_in_drafter` computation calls `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.max()` on every step whenever speculative decoding is active. `seq_lens` here is the common attention metadata's sequence-length tensor, which normally lives on the GPU. Calling `.max()` and then comparing it against a Python int (`+ num_speculative_tokens <= effective_drafter_max_model_len`) forces a device-to-host synchronization / implicit `.item()` on the hot decode path. This adds a GPU->CPU sync every decode step for all Eagle spec-decode runs, which can stall the pipeline and reduce throughput, and it is especially harmful in the `use_padded_batch_for_eagle` branch that was specifically designed to avoid waiting for bookkeeping (the comment states it 'does not need to wait for bookkeeping to finish'). The sync partially defeats that optimization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run Eagle speculative decoding decode-throughput benchmark (e.g., serving eagle draft model with steady-state decode batch) before/after this commit and compare tokens/sec and per-step latency; add an nsys/torch profiler trace to confirm the added D2H sync from seq_lens.max().

findings.claim: `effective_drafter_max_model_len` is recomputed from config attributes on every model step even though all inputs (self.max_model_len, model_config.max_model_len, draft_model_config.max_model_len, num_speculative_tokens) are static for the lifetime of the runner. This is minor CPU overhead but could be hoisted to __init__ to avoid repeated attribute lookups/branching on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark of the propose-path branch overhead per step; negligible unless combined with the seq_lens.max() sync.

findings.claim: For DDP bf16 training (stage=0, model_dtype=bf16, grad_accum_dtype now defaults to bf16), the new DDP_BFLOAT16 path routes through FP16_Optimizer with low_precision_dtype=torch.bfloat16. FP16_Optimizer maintains an fp32 flattened master copy of every parameter (self.fp32_groups_flat = fp16_groups_flat.clone().float()) plus fp32 flattened gradient buffers built each step. Prior to this commit, bf16 non-ZeRO forced grad_accum_dtype=fp32 and raised NotImplementedError for the bf16-wrapper case, so this fp32-master DDP path did not run. The persistent fp32 master weights roughly double parameter memory vs. a pure bf16 DDP path, and the per-step fp32 grad flattening allocates a full fp32 copy of all gradients. This is a real memory increase for the newly-enabled DDP bf16 mode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory for stage=0 bf16 DDP training of a mid-size model vs. a naive bf16 DDP baseline, comparing parameter+grad buffer footprint.

findings.claim: In FP16_Optimizer.step(), for the new bf16 (use_grad_scaling=False) path the code still runs the full overflow check (self.overflow_checker.has_overflow(fp16_params)) every step even though the result is now ignored (the overflow-handling block is gated behind self.use_grad_scaling). has_overflow iterates over all params and performs device reductions, adding per-step overhead that serves no purpose when grad scaling is disabled for bf16.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile FP16_Optimizer.step() overhead with low_precision_dtype=bf16 measuring time spent in has_overflow relative to the rest of the step.

findings.claim: The grad_accum_dtype default changed so that for bf16 non-ZeRO training gradients are now accumulated in bf16 instead of the previous fp32. While this reduces grad buffer memory/bandwidth, bf16 gradient accumulation across many micro-steps is numerically lossy; if users compensate by adding steps or fall back to configs, effective throughput could be affected. This is a behavioral change flagged by the removed fp32-accumulation guard.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare convergence/step-count to target loss for bf16 grad accumulation vs fp32 grad accumulation on the same model.

findings.claim: register_z3_param now performs a blocking process_group->allgather(...)->wait() plus per-rank device-to-host copies (all_padded_counts[i].to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>()) for EVERY parameter registered. This function is called in a loop over all model parameters during init (init_z3.py registers each p). For a model with thousands of parameters this adds thousands of separate blocking NCCL allgather collectives, each followed by world_size synchronous D2H item() reads that force GPU-CPU synchronization. This serializes initialization and can add substantial (potentially minutes-scale on large models) startup latency. The check is also purely a redundant safety validation: padded_per_rank is computed identically from ds_shape on every rank, so the allgather can only ever confirm equality it already guarantees.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure init_z3 registration wall-time for a model with many parameters (e.g. a multi-billion-param transformer) at world_size>=2, comparing parent vs this commit; expect large increase proportional to parameter count.

findings.claim: In allgatherParam, the isValid (cache-hit) fast path previously returned param_registry_->getGatheredParam(ds_id) directly. It now constructs base.flatten().index({Slice(0,true_numel)}).view(shape) on every call. This is the hot path hit repeatedly during forward/backward for every gathered parameter. Constructing a flatten+slice+view builds new tensor metadata (and an index/slice op dispatch) each access instead of returning a cached tensor, adding per-access CPU dispatch overhead to a frequently executed path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated allgatherParam calls on a valid/cached param and compare to returning the cached tensor directly.

findings.claim: Output buffers are now allocated at padded_numel = world_size * ceil(true_numel/world_size) instead of true numel (param.getShape()). For parameters whose true size is not divisible by world_size this over-allocates up to (world_size-1) elements per parameter. More importantly the returned view retains a reference to the full padded buffer, so the padding cannot be freed while the view is alive, marginally increasing peak GPU memory versus the previous exact-shape allocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak GPU memory for a ZeRO-3 model with parameters not divisible by world_size, parent vs commit.

findings.claim: register_z3_param allocates world_size CUDA tensors via torch::empty_like in a loop plus a torch::tensor on device per parameter, for a validation that is otherwise deterministic. These small per-parameter GPU allocations add allocator pressure/fragmentation during init.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile CUDA allocator activity during init_z3 registration loop.

findings.claim: The per-request local count adjustment changed from incrementing a single count by 1 (`min_counts[0] += 1` or `min_counts[1] += 1`) to `current_counts[eng_index][0] += self.client_count`, always incrementing the *waiting* count by client_count even when the engine is idle. Combined with the new scoring `score = waiting*4 + running`, adding to waiting instead of running when running slots are free biases the estimate. If client_count is misconfigured too high, requests will be spread too thinly (each request makes an engine look client_count-heavier), causing suboptimal packing and under-utilization of individual engines between coordinator updates; if too low, requests pile on one engine. This directly affects DP load balance quality and hence aggregate throughput/latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-client DP=4+ throughput/latency test measuring per-engine queue imbalance under bursty concurrent request submission across multiple API server processes.

findings.claim: The coordinator poll loop introduces a `min_timeout` of 50ms when `last_step_counts is None`, meaning the coordinator will wait at least 50ms before publishing stats even when a stats update is pending. This adds up to 50ms of staleness to load-balancing information propagated to front-ends, which can slightly worsen balancing responsiveness under rapidly changing load. Also the default idle publish interval increased from 4000ms to 5000ms.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end latency of load-stat propagation from engine to front-end under load spikes for the internal DP LB path.

findings.claim: `_get_engine_counts(do_copy=True)` now performs `copy.copy` of each engine's request_counts list on every stats update where stats already changed, and DPLBAsyncMPClient's decode path now assigns sliced lists each stats update. These are small per-update allocations in the hot coordinator loop; negligible individually but occur frequently under high request rates.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile coordinator process CPU under high request throughput with DP internal LB.

findings.claim: A new `logger.debug("Received counts: ...")` is called in the stats update decode path on every coordinator broadcast received by each client. Even at debug level, string args are constructed only lazily, but the branch runs per broadcast; the added warning path for out-of-order steps could also fire frequently in edge cases and produce log spam that stalls the async loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run internal DP LB under sustained load and monitor coordinator/client log volume and event-loop latency.

findings.claim: On SM90 (Hopper), the new logic in get_attention_backend unconditionally disables FA4 whenever FA3 is available (`if use_flash_attention_4 and use_flash_attention_3 and device_compute_capability == (9,0): use_flash_attention_4 = False`). This is a deliberate preference, but it hard-codes FA3 over FA4 for all shapes on Hopper. For workloads where FA4 would actually be faster (e.g. certain head dims / bf16 forward), this prevents the framework from ever picking the faster kernel, capping achievable throughput on Hopper.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM90, benchmark forward+backward DPA throughput for FA3 vs FA4 across common head dims (64/128) and seqlens; compare tokens/s to verify FA3 is actually the faster choice being forced here.

findings.claim: In the FA4 forward path in backends.py, `num_splits` is passed unconditionally as a kwarg (`fa_4_optional_forward_kwargs['num_splits'] = num_splits`). The default num_splits=1 should be fine, but if a caller sets num_splits and the SM<100 branch in utils.py already disabled FA4 for num_splits, there is a coupling risk where a suboptimal split count could be forwarded. Worth confirming num_splits=1 does not force a split-KV path with extra reduction overhead when not needed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure FA4 forward latency with num_splits=1 vs an unset/None default to confirm no extra split-KV reduction kernel is launched.

findings.claim: The FA4 forward wraps the result with `if isinstance(output, (List, Tuple)): output = output[0]`. If FA4 returns a tuple including LSE/aux tensors that were computed but discarded here, no extra work is added per se, but discarding max_logit / LSE means downstream that requests return_max_logit cannot use FA4 and must fall back to a slower backend. This is a correctness/fallback interaction rather than direct slowdown.

has_mnnvl_fabric(comm->mydev) is now called inside register_user_buffer_collective(), which runs on every user-buffer registration. Each invocation performs nvmlInit_v2 + nvmlDeviceGetHandleByIndex_v2 + nvmlDeviceGetGpuFabricInfoV + nvmlShutdown, plus CUDA driver entry-point lookups for cuDeviceGet/cuDeviceGetAttribute. NVML init/shutdown is relatively heavy (driver handshake, per-device enumeration). Since multiple buffers are registered per communicator (comm_gemm_overlap.cpp calls register_user_buffer_collective multiple times), this repeats an expensive fabric-capability probe that could be computed once at communicator creation and cached in the communicator struct. This only affects setup/initialization time, not steady-state training, so the impact is one-time startup latency rather than per-step throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time create_communicator + repeated register_user_buffer_collective calls; measure wall-clock spent in has_mnnvl_fabric (nvmlInit/nvmlShutdown) across N buffer registrations vs. caching the result once.

findings.claim: has_mnnvl_fabric is also called once in create_communicator_grouped2. Combined with the per-registration calls, the total number of nvmlInit_v2/nvmlShutdown cycles during framework startup grows with the number of registered buffers. Repeated NVML init/shutdown cycles add to distributed-init latency; caching the fabric-support flag in the communicator would avoid redundant NVML handshakes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument nvmlInit_v2/nvmlShutdown call counts during a full comm-gemm-overlap init sequence.

findings.claim: MambaManager.reachable_block_mask now allocates a Python list of length (end_block - start_block) and runs a Python loop over the block range on every cache_blocks() call whenever VLLM_PREFIX_CACHE_RETENTION_INTERVAL is set. Previously the base-class implementation returned None immediately (constant time). This adds per-block Python-level work on the hot scheduler path (cache_blocks runs each scheduling step per request). For long sequences this is O(num_full_blocks) allocation + iteration per step. It only affects the retention-enabled path, so impact is limited, but for very long Mamba sequences with retention enabled it adds measurable CPU scheduling overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable VLLM_PREFIX_CACHE_RETENTION_INTERVAL on a Mamba/hybrid model and measure scheduler step latency vs. a dense (None) baseline with long prompts (e.g. 32k tokens).

findings.claim: In cache_blocks_this_step the assertion `assert block.block_hash is not None` was replaced with a `block.block_hash is None -> continue`. With sparse retention, intermediate (masked-out) state snapshots now carry no hash and are correctly skipped; but if this guard silently absorbs an unexpected None (e.g. a mask bug that drops a boundary block that should serve a hit), it degrades prefix-cache hit rate rather than crashing. A lower Mamba prefix-cache hit rate means more state recomputation and reduced throughput on repeated prompts. This is speculative and depends on mask correctness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure Mamba prefix-cache hit rate / TTFT on repeated-prefix workloads with retention enabled vs dense.

findings.claim: The refactored build() introduces several new tensor arithmetic operations in the prefill pathway that were previously computed differently. Specifically, `qo_indptr_prefill_gpu = qo_indptr[prefill_start:] - qo_indptr[prefill_start]` now performs a GPU tensor subtraction on every build for TRTLLM prefill, and `max_q_len_prefill = int(query_lens_prefill_cpu.max().item())` triggers a .item() sync. These existed before in similar form, so net impact is likely neutral, but the added GPU op on qo_indptr per-build is a small recurring cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark FlashInferMetadataBuilder.build() latency for a mixed prefill+decode batch on Blackwell (TRTLLM path) comparing parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: A new persistent host buffer `paged_kv_indptr_cpu_buffer` (torch.zeros_like of paged_kv_indptr.cpu, pin_memory) is retained in addition to the CpuGpuBuffer's own cpu tensor. This is functionally equivalent to the old `paged_kv_indptr_buffer` so it is not new memory growth, just a rename. No net regression.

The guard `needs_seq_lens_cpu`/`needs_paged_kv_indices` is intended as an optimization to skip seq_lens_cpu retrieval and paged_kv_indices computation for the pure-TRTLLM-decode case (is_only_trtllm_decode). This should reduce CPU work and a potential device->host sync in async mode for the common decode path — likely a performance improvement, not a regression. Worth verifying that the skipped `_compute_flashinfer_kv_metadata` does not leave the persistent paged_kv buffers stale for a subsequent cudagraph replay that still reads them.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only steady-state throughput on TRTLLM decode path; also validate correctness under CUDA graph replay to ensure skipping paged_kv metadata population does not read stale buffers.

findings.claim: set_skip_topk() iterates over all MTP layers and walks a chain of getattr/hasattr calls (mtp_block -> self_attn -> mla_attn) each time it is called. In propose() it is called twice per proposal (before step 0 with skip=False and after step 0 with skip=True). This is pure-Python overhead on the speculative-decoding hot path, adding a small fixed cost per draft proposal. For a small number of MTP layers this is minor, but it runs on every propose() invocation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-token draft latency with DSA MTP + index_share_for_mtp_iteration enabled vs disabled, isolating the set_skip_topk toggling cost.

findings.claim: The topk_indices_buffer sharing now iterates over every module in the draft model via named_modules() and sets an attribute on each module that has topk_indices_buffer, replacing what was previously a single top-level assignment. named_modules() traverses the full module tree. This runs once at model load, so it is not on the inference hot path; the concern is only startup cost, which is negligible for typical model sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure load_eagle_model wall time for a large DSA MTP draft model before/after the change.

findings.claim: _get_qk_rope_head_dim() may trigger get_hf_file_to_dict('config.json', model_path), which reads/parses config.json from disk and can fall back to a HuggingFace Hub download (network I/O) when the config's qk_rope_head_dim equals qk_nope_head_dim. get_head_size() is invoked during config conversion/model setup. This adds file/network I/O to startup, but only in the corruption-detection branch and only at configuration time, not on the inference path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model config conversion time for a DeepSeek MLA model whose config triggers the fix-up branch, with and without local config cache.

findings.claim: The fix-up in _get_qk_rope_head_dim patches cfg.qk_rope_head_dim so subsequent calls short-circuit (qk_rope_head_dim != qk_nope_head_dim after patch), so the disk/network read should only happen once. However, if the corrupted config leaves qk_rope_head_dim unchanged (raw value equals current), the guard does not memoize and every get_head_size() call would re-read config.json. If get_head_size() is called repeatedly, this is redundant I/O.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count get_hf_file_to_dict invocations during a full startup for a config where raw qk_rope_head_dim equals the corrupted value.

findings.claim: safe_apply_chat_template now runs `any(msg["role"] == "developer" for msg in conversation)` on every request, adding an O(n) scan over all conversation messages even when no developer role is present (the common case). This is cheap relative to the actual Jinja chat-template rendering that follows, so the impact is small, but it is unconditional added work on the hot request path. If it were combined with the developer-detection short-circuit differently it could be avoided; as written it evaluates the generator fully in the no-developer case only after scanning all messages.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark safe_apply_chat_template with a long conversation (e.g. 200 messages) containing no developer role, before vs after this commit, measuring per-call latency.

findings.claim: _detect_developer_role_support is @lru_cache(maxsize=32) keyed on the full chat_template string. Chat template strings can be large (many KB), so the cache holds up to 32 full template strings as keys, and each cache lookup hashes the entire template string. For a single served model this is fine (one template), but the string-hashing cost per lookup scales with template length; it is small and cached so impact is negligible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure hashing cost of _detect_developer_role_support lookup with a large (10KB+) chat template versus template rendering time.

findings.claim: For Gemma4 sliding-window layers, this change stops the model runner from dropping image bidirectional mm_prefix ranges that exceed the sliding window (~1100 tokens > 1024 window). Keeping those ranges means USE_MM_PREFIX stays enabled on those sliding layers. In compute_tile_loop_bounds (triton_attention_helpers.py / triton_unified_attention.py), sliding-window tile pruning is gated by `if SLIDING_WINDOW > 0 and not USE_MM_PREFIX`, so with mm_prefix active the kernel iterates over ALL tiles of the sequence rather than only the sliding-window tiles. On sliding layers (which are the majority of Gemma layers and are specifically the ones that get a small window for speed), the attention loop now scans the full KV range per query block instead of just ~window/TILE_SIZE tiles. For long image+text prefills this converts sliding-layer prefill attention from O(window) back to O(seq_len) per query block, materially increasing prefill latency and GPU work exactly where the sliding window was meant to save it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4-mm prefill latency / attention kernel time with a large image (>= max_soft_tokens exceeding the 1024 sliding window) on Triton and FA4 backends, comparing before vs after this commit; measure sliding-layer attention tile-loop iteration counts.

findings.claim: In compute_kv_seq_mask, when MM_PREFIX_CLAMP_SW is True the per-range mm mask now includes an extra elementwise comparison `(query_abs_pos - seq_offset) < SLIDING_WINDOW` inside the MAX_MM_RANGES loop, executed for every KV tile. This adds a small per-tile vector op per mm range. Impact is minor and only affects Gemma4 sliding layers, but combined with the disabled tile pruning it runs over more tiles than before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark the Triton unified attention kernel with MM_PREFIX_CLAMP_SW toggled, measuring per-tile mask compute cost.

findings.claim: In _make_mm_prefix_mask_mod (FA4 CuTE-DSL path), when sliding_window > 0 an extra `(q_idx - kv_idx) < sliding_window` term is added to the mm mask per range. This is a small added per-element op in the mask_mod evaluated for the bidirectional block; only affects Gemma4 sliding layers on FA4 (vllm_flash_attn_version == 4). Minor overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FA4 mm_prefix mask_mod microbenchmark comparing clamped vs unclamped sliding window on Gemma4 image prefill.

findings.claim: In DPSupervisor._probe_all_children, once the supervisor is marked ready, each probe uses conn_err_failure_threshold=dp_supervisor_probe_failure_threshold (default 3) with conn_err_retry_delay=dp_supervisor_probe_interval_s (default 5s). This means detecting an unhealthy child after a connection error can take up to (threshold-1)*interval = ~10s before shutdown is triggered, delaying failover. It also means a single probe cycle can block that long, extending the effective health-detection latency well beyond the nominal 5s interval. This is a control-plane concern (does not affect inference throughput) but degrades responsiveness of the readiness/health endpoint under child failure.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Simulate a child /health connection error and measure time from failure to supervisor reporting SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE vs. configured interval.

findings.claim: Both _monitor_children and _probe_all_children run independent wait loops that each wake every dp_supervisor_probe_interval_s and issue debug logs plus liveness checks. This is redundant duplicate polling of the same shutdown_event; the monitor loop and probe loop could be unified. The overhead is trivial (a few HTTP GETs and process.is_alive() calls every 5s) and only affects the supervisor process, not workers.

Child API servers are spawned with multiprocessing 'spawn' context and each re-imports vLLM and re-initializes; the supervisor readiness gating waits for all children via 5s-interval polling. During startup, readiness detection granularity is bounded by dp_supervisor_probe_interval_s, so aggregated readiness can lag actual child readiness by up to ~5s. Minor and only affects perceived startup time, not steady-state performance.

The large path uses an inter-CTA producer/consumer barrier implemented as a global-memory spin loop: wait_ge() does `while (ld_acquire(ptr) < target_val) {}` on thread 0 (with `#pragma unroll 1`). This is a device-wide busy-wait across a group of CTAs coordinating via RadixRowState.arrival_counter/output_counter. If the grid is launched with more resident blocks than the group's producers can be co-scheduled (grid oversubscription relative to SM occupancy, which is very likely given the ~36KB dynamic smem + 1024 threads/block limiting occupancy to ~1 block/SM), the waiting CTAs occupy SMs and can prevent the CTAs they are waiting on from ever being scheduled — a classic cooperative-kernel deadlock/hang. Correct behavior requires launching no more blocks than can be simultaneously resident (cooperative launch semantics); a plain <<<grid>>> launch does not guarantee this. Even when it does not hang, spinning CTAs waste SM cycles that could run other rows.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSA decode/large-context (seq_len>64K) with batch sizes that push grid size above the device's max resident blocks; measure for hangs and for throughput vs the old large_context_topk one-block-per-row kernel.

findings.claim: The medium path requests kSmemMedium = 35968 bytes (~35KB) of dynamic shared memory with kThreadsPerBlock=1024 threads, and the decode path uses the full extern smem region up to SBASE+8 = 8192 ints = 32KB. At ~32-36KB/block with 1024 threads, occupancy is capped near 1 block/SM on most NVIDIA GPUs (which have 48-100KB usable smem after carveout), leaving SMs underutilized. Since this is now the unified kernel that also handles the decode/short-seq path (previously served by the separate, lighter top_k_per_row_decode kernel), routing short-sequence decode rows through this heavy-smem kernel can reduce occupancy and hurt latency for the common decode case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode latency for seq_len<=8192 short rows between the previous top_k_per_row_decode path and the new unified persistent_topk decode path across batch sizes.

findings.claim: histogram_2048_topk builds a 2048-bin histogram via per-element shared-memory atomicAdd (4 atomicAdds per thread per float4 load). With 1024 threads hammering a 2048-entry histogram, bin collisions cause atomic serialization, particularly for the '10LSBits'-style distributions where values cluster into few bins. The decode path also re-reads logits from global memory in phase 2/3 (logits[bufs[0][i]]) rather than caching, adding extra global traffic on the refinement passes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark decode kernel on clustered-value logits (10LSBits generator) vs uniform random to measure histogram-atomic contention sensitivity.

findings.claim: The new kernel takes max_seq_len as a scalar launch argument used for 'early CTA exit'. If max_seq_len (and the medium/large-vs-decode dispatch) is computed host-side from metadata and baked into the launch, then under CUDA graph replay with a different actual per-row length distribution the wrong path/exit bound could be used, forcing a fallback that is slower than a length-specialized dispatch. The old code branched on max_seq_len>8192 host-side (use_large_context_topk); the new 'dynamic per-row dispatch' shifts this into the kernel, which is more robust but adds per-row branch/dispatch overhead inside every block.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture a CUDA graph over a mixed decode batch (some rows <8K, some >64K) and measure per-step latency vs the pre-change branch-on-host implementation.

findings.claim: The new gRPC health service invokes async_llm.check_health() on every probe (per the docs note: 'checks the engine status on every probe'). With Kubernetes liveness (periodSeconds=10) and readiness (periodSeconds=5) probes, this issues ~3 health RPCs every 10s, each of which round-trips to the engine-core process. check_health typically sends an IPC message to the background engine process; if that call contends with the engine's request-serving loop or blocks the event loop under heavy load, it could add minor latency jitter to inference requests. The overhead is small but not zero and scales with probe frequency. Consider caching/rate-limiting the engine health check so rapid probes don't each trigger a fresh engine round-trip.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Under sustained inference load, measure p99 token latency with and without gRPC health probes firing every 5s (readiness) + 10s (liveness), and measure the added event-loop time per Check() call.

findings.claim: `_is_supported_kv_cache_dtype` is invoked from an `assert` inside `triton_reshape_and_cache_flash`, which runs on every attention layer for every forward step. The change adds `current_platform.has_device_capability(89)`/`(80)` calls to this per-call function. `has_device_capability` calls `get_device_capability()`, which on the concrete CUDA platform ultimately calls `torch.cuda.get_device_capability(...)`; unless that path is cached, this introduces a small per-call Python/CUDA-query cost on the KV-cache-write hot path. The added string formatting (`cap.as_version_str()`, `get_device_name()`) in `triton_attn.py` is only in `__init__`, so that part is one-time. The reshape path cost is small but repeated. If `python -O` strips asserts, the check is skipped entirely in optimized runs (a correctness concern, not perf).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark `triton_reshape_and_cache_flash` call overhead (CPU-side launch) before/after, over many iterations with fp8 and non-fp8 kv_cache_dtype, to measure any per-call regression from the added capability queries.

findings.claim: In the new non-streaming path, `HarmonyContext.append_output` now calls `self.response_parser.process_chunk(output_token_ids)` on the *entire* set of output token IDs at once. `process_chunk` builds and appends one `Segment` NamedTuple per token (segments.append(Segment(...)) inside the per-token loop) and returns the full list in `ChunkResult.segments`. In the previous non-streaming implementation the parser processed tokens without materializing a per-token object list. For a long generation (thousands of output tokens), this allocates a Python list holding one Segment object per generated token in a single call, and that list is then stored in `self.last_append_segments`, retaining O(num_output_tokens) objects in memory until the context is discarded. This is a notable extra allocation/retention that scales with sequence length and is unnecessary for the non-streaming case, which only needs `completed_message` segments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Non-streaming Responses API request with GPT-OSS producing a long output (e.g. 4k-8k tokens); measure peak Python heap / RSS growth and append_output CPU time vs parent commit.

findings.claim: `self.last_append_segments` retains the full list of per-token Segment objects from the most recent `append_output` call for the lifetime of the HarmonyContext. In the streaming loop this list is consumed each turn, but in the non-streaming path (`_make_response_output_items_with_harmony` replacement in serving.py) `last_append_segments` is never read yet is still populated with one object per output token, holding memory proportional to the generation length until GC.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak memory of a non-streaming long-output response between parent and this commit.

findings.claim: The streaming loop now iterates `for segment in ctx.last_append_segments:` on every generated chunk. Because `process_chunk` emits one Segment per token, each streaming step iterates over all tokens in that chunk and calls `emit_content_delta_events` per segment. This is functionally similar to the previous per-token parser.process loop, so no clear regression, but the added per-segment NamedTuple construction and channel/recipient normalization (`_normalize_recipient` string.find on every token) is slightly more work per token than the prior direct parser access.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming Responses API decode-time-per-token microbenchmark comparing parent vs this commit under GPT-OSS.

findings.claim: The overridden `get_hf_processor` now constructs a brand-new `MiniCPMVProcessor` (and for MiniCPMO a new `MiniCPMOProcessor`) on every call, wrapping the already-cached HF processor from `ctx.get_hf_processor`. `ProcessorMixin.__init__` (invoked via `super().__init__`) performs non-trivial setup and attribute validation. Whereas the parent's `ctx.get_hf_processor` result is cached via `cached_processor_from_config`, this new wrapper layer is not memoized, so each call to `get_hf_processor` (called during profiling/dummy-input generation, prompt-update resolution, and every multimodal prompt processing) pays the reconstruction cost. If `get_hf_processor` is on a hot path per request, this adds per-request CPU overhead that did not exist before (previously the cached processor was returned directly).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure MiniCPMV/MiniCPMO multimodal request preprocessing latency (calls per request to get_hf_processor) before/after; e.g. process 1000 image+text prompts and compare CPU time in the processing pipeline.

findings.claim: For MiniCPMO, `get_hf_processor` converts `image_processor.mean`/`std` numpy arrays to Python lists via `.tolist()` on each call. If the image processor later normalizes using these attributes as Python lists rather than numpy arrays, per-image normalization could fall back to slower Python-level iteration instead of vectorized numpy ops. Combined with the un-cached reconstruction, this is minor but repeated overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare image normalization throughput with list vs ndarray mean/std attributes in the MiniCPMV image processor.

findings.claim: The vision tower is still invoked once per image inside the Python `for pv, pp in zip(...)` loop (each `pv.unsqueeze(0)` gives a batch of 1). The commit only changes iteration to accept an unstacked list of variable-resolution tensors; it does not batch same-resolution images together. When many same-resolution images arrive as a stacked tensor, they are now iterated one at a time, so N separate small vision-tower forward passes (each launching its own set of GPU kernels with per-call launch overhead and low GPU occupancy) are executed instead of one batched pass. This is a latency/GPU-utilization regression relative to a batched path, though it matches the pre-existing per-image behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure vision-tower encode latency and GPU utilization for a batch of e.g. 16 same-resolution images (stacked tensor) vs the per-image loop; compare against a single batched forward pass.

findings.claim: Accepting `list[torch.Tensor]` for pixel_values/pixel_position_ids (dynamic np dim) means concurrent mixed-resolution image batches are kept unstacked. This avoids padding all images to the max patch count, which is a memory win, but it also forgoes any contiguity/pinned-buffer benefits of a single stacked tensor for host-to-device transfer. The net effect is workload-dependent; for mixed resolutions the unstacked form is generally cheaper.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare H2D transfer + processing time for mixed-resolution image batches with stacked+padded tensors vs unstacked list of tensors.

findings.claim: Every streaming/non-streaming feed now routes through a new `_feed` wrapper that additionally calls `_preprocess_feed`. On the base ParserEngine both are trivial (identity return), so this adds one extra Python function-call layer per delta on the streaming hot path for all engine-based parsers (Qwen3, Gemma4, etc.). The cost is small but is incurred per output token during streaming; it is pure overhead for parsers that don't override `_preprocess_feed`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark streaming parse_delta throughput (deltas/sec) for Qwen3Parser before vs after the `_feed`/`_preprocess_feed` indirection over a long generation.

findings.claim: Gemma4Parser._preprocess_feed performs several substring membership checks (`CHANNEL_START in delta_text`, `CHANNEL_END in delta_text`, `startswith`) on the first feed. Guarded by `_is_first_feed` so it runs once per stream/reset, but each reset (`_reset`) re-arms `_is_first_feed=True`; for the single-pass non-streaming extract paths that reset+feed the entire model_output as one delta, these scans run over the full output string. This is O(len(output)) per call but only once, so impact is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark Gemma4Parser.extract_reasoning / extract_tool_calls on large model outputs to confirm the first-feed substring scans are negligible.

findings.category: recursive arg parsing on partial input
findings.claim: _parse_gemma4_args / _parse_gemma4_array are invoked by _gemma4_arg_converter to convert Gemma4's custom key:value tool-arg format to JSON. If the arg converter is called with the full accumulated argument string on each streaming delta (a common pattern for stream_arg_deltas-style parsers), re-parsing the entire (growing) args body plus json.dumps on every token yields O(n^2) work over the length of a tool call's arguments. Could not confirm the exact call cadence from the parent snapshot, so this is speculative but worth verifying, since tool-call argument bodies can be long.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stream a tool call with a large arguments body through Gemma4Parser.extract_tool_calls_streaming and measure total time vs. arguments length to detect quadratic scaling in _gemma4_arg_converter.

findings.claim: In GLM46VVideoBackend.compute_frames_index_to_sample, `timestamps = [i * duration_per_frame for i in range(total_frames_num)]` materializes a full Python list of one float per source frame every call. For long videos (the added tests exercise total_frames up to 150000) this is a large O(total_frames_num) allocation and Python-level loop that runs unconditionally. The list is only used inside the sampling loop as `timestamps[frame_index]`, which is exactly `frame_index * duration_per_frame` and can be computed inline, so the entire list is avoidable. This adds measurable CPU time and transient memory to the media-preprocessing path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call GLM46VVideoBackend.compute_frames_index_to_sample with total_frames_num=150000 (5000s @30fps) and time it; compare against an inline `frame_index * duration_per_frame` variant.

findings.claim: The frame-selection loop `for frame_index in range(total_frames_num): ...` is a pure-Python loop that walks essentially every source frame (it only breaks once current_second >= max_second, which is ~= duration, i.e. after iterating almost all frames). For high-frame-count videos this is a Python-level O(total_frames_num) loop on the preprocessing path, whereas the base VideoBackend uses vectorized np.linspace. This is slower than a vectorized formulation, though it mirrors the reference HF sampling behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark compute_frames_index_to_sample with total_frames_num in {18000, 72000, 150000} and measure wall-clock time of the sampling loop.

findings.claim: In `_adjust_params_for_parsing`, for Gemma4 models the code calls `tokenizer.get_vocab()` on every `chat()` invocation (whenever thinking is enabled or tools are provided). `get_vocab()` typically materializes the full tokenizer vocabulary dict (tens of thousands of entries) each time, which is a non-trivial CPU cost repeated per chat call. Since the tokenizer/vocab and the special-token membership check are static for a given model, this result should be computed once and cached (e.g., in __init__ or memoized) rather than recomputed per call. For high-frequency offline chat calls this adds measurable per-request CPU latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time repeated LLM.chat() calls on a Gemma4 model with enable_thinking=True and compare per-call latency vs. a cached special-token lookup; measure get_vocab() cost in isolation for the model's tokenizer.

findings.claim: The `needs_parsing` gate and `_adjust_params_for_parsing` are entered on every chat() call when tools are passed or thinking is enabled, but the expensive vocab/special-id work only matters for Gemma4. The Gemma4 architecture check is inexpensive and short-circuits, so overall impact is small; nonetheless the whole check (arch inspection + potential vocab build) runs per call rather than being precomputed once, adding a small constant overhead to the chat path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark overhead of _adjust_params_for_parsing on non-Gemma4 vs Gemma4 models across many chat() calls.

findings.claim: The prefix-based lookup step (step 3) now iterates over all `_quantized_layer_prefix_candidates(prefix)` (up to 2 candidates), and for each candidate performs a full O(N) scan over `self.quantized_layers` (`for key, info in self.quantized_layers.items(): if key.startswith(prefix_dot)`). This roughly doubles the worst-case work of what was already an O(N) scan per layer. Since `_get_quant_algo_for_prefix` is called once per layer during `get_quant_method`, the total cost becomes O(L * K * N) where L=layers, K=candidates(<=2), N=size of quantized_layers dict. This only affects one-time model-load / config resolution, not the runtime inference path, so impact is minor. Confidence is moderate that this is measurable only for very large MoE checkpoints with huge quantized_layers dicts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time model load / quantization config resolution for a large MoE ModelOpt-quantized checkpoint (many entries in quantized_layers) before vs. after this commit.

findings.claim: Step 2 (packed/fused lookup) now nests the shard loop inside a loop over base-prefix candidates, multiplying dict lookups by the number of candidates (<=2). Each `_quantized_layer_prefix_candidates` call also allocates a list, does several startswith/endswith string checks and builds a dict+tuple. All of this is on the one-time config-resolution / model-load path (called once per layer), not the inference hot path, so runtime performance is unaffected; the added per-layer string/allocation work is negligible relative to weight loading.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _get_quant_algo_for_prefix over a synthetic quantized_layers dict with thousands of entries, before vs. after.

findings.claim: Gemma4MTPMaskedEmbedder.forward allocates a full (num_tokens, vocab_size) tensor filled with -inf (torch.full) and then scatters the sparse logits into it. This materializes a dense full-vocabulary logits tensor on every compute_logits call, which defeats the entire purpose of the sparse centroid-based selection (the docstring states it computes logits only for ~top_k*(vocab/num_centroids) tokens). For a large Gemma vocab (~256k) this is a large allocation + a full-vocab memory write per draft step, adding GPU memory pressure and bandwidth cost proportional to vocab_size rather than num_selected.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-draft-step latency and peak GPU memory of compute_logits() for Gemma4 MTP with masked_embedding enabled vs. the sparse get_top_tokens() path, at batch sizes 1/8/64 with vocab~256k.

findings.claim: _get_full_lm_head_weight() calls tensor_model_parallel_all_gather on the entire lm_head weight matrix (vocab_size x hidden_size) every time compute_logits/get_top_tokens is invoked when TP>1. This all-gathers O(vocab_size*hidden_size) elements on each draft step. The standard LogitsProcessor.get_top_tokens optimization exists precisely to avoid this by gathering only O(batch*2*tp) (value,index) pairs. Re-gathering the full weight per step is far more communication-heavy and is not cached across steps.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare draft-step latency of Gemma4 MTP get_top_tokens (all-gather full lm_head weight) vs LogitsProcessor local-argmax reduction under TP=2/4/8 with a large vocab.

findings.claim: Gemma4MTPAttention.forward allocates a fresh kv_dummy tensor (torch.empty of size num_tokens*num_kv_heads*head_dim) on every forward call for every KV-shared layer, purely to satisfy the Attention API even though the K/V are never consumed. This is repeated allocation per-layer per-draft-step. It could be avoided/hoisted to a persistent buffer, reducing allocator churn on the hot draft path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step allocator activity / step latency for Gemma4 MTP with N decoder layers, comparing per-call torch.empty vs a reused zero-size/persistent dummy tensor.

findings.claim: _select_and_score performs lm_head_weight[selected.reshape(-1)] which materializes an (num_tokens * num_selected, hidden_size) gathered embedding tensor before the einsum. num_selected = top_k * (vocab/num_centroids); with default top_k=32 and vocab~256k/2048 centroids this is ~4096 embeddings per token, so the gathered tensor and einsum scale as num_tokens*4096*hidden. For larger batches this indexing-gather may become a memory-bandwidth bottleneck and defeats some of the intended sparsity savings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark _select_and_score latency and memory vs batch size (1..256), checking whether the gather+einsum outperforms a dense matmul at larger batch sizes.

findings.claim: In the new generic ND x ND path, both `a` and `b` are `.expand()`ed to `broadcast_shape` and then `.reshape()`ed to 3D. When the broadcast actually adds/enlarges batch dimensions (e.g. 2D x 4D, or a genuinely broadcast batch dim of size 1 -> N), `expand()` produces a non-contiguous view with stride-0 dims, and the subsequent `.reshape(batch_dim, m, k)` cannot be a view, so PyTorch materializes a full contiguous copy of the expanded tensor. This replicates the operand N times in memory and adds a device-to-device copy before the matmul. Previously the 2D x 3D case broadcast only `a` (the small operand), whereas the generic code may now broadcast/materialize the large operand as well, increasing memory traffic and peak memory for those shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark matmul_batch_invariant on broadcast shapes like (1,H,S,D)x(B,H,D,S) and 2Dx4D vs the old paths, measuring peak GPU memory and latency.

findings.claim: The generic path always calls `a.expand(...)` / `b.expand(...)` even for the common equal-batch ND x ND case (e.g. 3D x 3D or 4D x 4D where batch dims already match). Although expand on matching shapes is a no-op view, the extra `torch.broadcast_shapes`, `math.prod`, and two `expand` calls add small per-call Python/host overhead on a hot matmul dispatch path compared with the previous direct reshape for the equal-shape 4D x 4D case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark host-side dispatch overhead of matmul_batch_invariant for 3Dx3D and 4Dx4D equal-batch shapes before/after this change.

findings.claim: Changing `stride_x_token` and `stride_o_token` from `tl.constexpr` to `tl.int64` promotes all token-offset address computations in the causal_conv1d kernels to 64-bit arithmetic (this is the intended fix for the illegal-memory-access on long sequences, where `sequence_start_index * stride_x_token` overflowed int32). The cost is a modest increase in integer/address-computation work per pointer: 64-bit multiplies/adds occupy more registers and instructions than 32-bit ones, which can add a little register pressure and marginally slow the pointer math in the inner per-token loop. Since these kernels are memory-bandwidth bound, the runtime impact is expected to be very small, and correctness (avoiding the crash) clearly outweighs it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _causal_conv1d_fwd_kernel and _causal_conv1d_update_kernel throughput on both short sequences (where int32 previously worked) and long sequences, comparing this commit vs parent, to quantify any per-token overhead from 64-bit token-stride arithmetic.

findings.claim: Previously `stride_x_token`/`stride_o_token` were `tl.constexpr`, so the value (e.g. 1 for contiguous, or the exact channel count) was baked in at compile time, allowing the compiler to fold or eliminate the multiply for contiguous cases and specialize per stride value. As `tl.int64` runtime scalars, the multiply can no longer be constant-folded and the kernel is no longer specialized per stride, potentially producing slightly less optimal generated code. Impact is minor for a bandwidth-bound kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare generated PTX / kernel timing for the common contiguous-token layout (stride_x_token == 1) before and after the change to see whether the folded multiply mattered.

findings.claim: The added os.fsync(fd) in _fini() introduces a blocking, synchronous durability barrier on every checkpoint save/close. fsync forces all outstanding data and metadata for the file to stable storage before returning; on the checkpoint write path this is a serialized stall that scales with file size / device flush latency. The commit message itself quantifies this at ~5% wall-time overhead at ~10 GB/s peak. For a 60 GB/iter checkpoint workload this is tens of GB flushed synchronously per save, directly reducing save throughput and increasing per-iteration latency. The os.close() alone would already fix the fd leak; fsync is the added performance cost (the author notes it can be dropped and the leak is still fixed).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time a save()/close() cycle for a large (multi-GB) FastFileWriter checkpoint with and without the os.fsync() line, on NVMe/ext4, measuring end-to-end wall time and device flush stall.

findings.claim: _fini() is invoked from both close() and __del__(). After close() sets self._aio_fd = INVALID_FD, the guard `if fd != INVALID_FD` correctly prevents a double fsync/close in __del__, so no double-flush occurs. However, if a writer object is only garbage-collected (never explicitly closed), the expensive fsync now runs during GC/finalization, which can introduce unpredictable latency spikes at arbitrary points rather than at a controlled close() call site.

When check_ep_fault is enabled, AsyncGPUModelRunnerOutput.__init__ runs query_fault() on every model step. This issues a device kernel to populate the mask buffer (low_latency_query_mask_buffer/query_mask_buffer), a GPU (current != last).any() reduction, and an additional small D2H non_blocking copy on the async output copy stream. These extra ops add per-step GPU work and stream traffic on every decode/prefill step for the (currently NIXL-EP-only) path. The overhead is small but constant per step and scales with step count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput / per-step latency for a NIXL-EP MoE deployment (support_fault_tolerance=True) with and without check_ep_fault, measuring added ms/step from the mask query + reduction + extra D2H copy.

findings.claim: get_output() reads self._has_fault.item() every step when check_ep_fault is set. Although the value is copied non_blocking and the copy event is synchronized earlier, the extra tensor materialization and Python-side branch runs unconditionally each step; combined with the extra D2H copy started in __init__, this is per-step host/device work that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure get_output() wall time per step with check_ep_fault True vs False.

findings.claim: query_fault() never updates _last_mask to the newly observed current mask (only initializes it to zeros once). This means the fault comparison is always against an all-zero baseline rather than the previous step's mask. Besides correctness implications, if a benign non-zero mask is present it can trigger the RuntimeError in get_output() every step, which raises and converts to a FAILURE response — effectively aborting execution rather than just adding overhead.

The inline f-string `f"token_id:{token_id}"` was replaced by a call to `format_token_id_placeholder(token_id)` in the per-token logprob loop (also in completion/serving.py and engine/serving.py `_get_decoded_token`). This adds a Python function-call frame per generated token whenever `return_tokens_as_token_ids` is enabled. The overhead is a single trivial call per token and only on that non-default path, so the practical slowdown is negligible; flagged only for completeness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark logprob response construction with return_tokens_as_token_ids=True over a long generation (e.g. 2k tokens, top_logprobs=20), comparing inline f-string vs helper-function call.

findings.claim: In the new /derender path, `_resolve_logprobs` invokes `resolve_token_id_placeholder` once per logprob entry and once per top_logprob, and each call runs `tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens([token_id])` + `convert_tokens_to_string([token_repr])` on a single-element list. This performs O(N) separate (single-token) tokenizer round-trips instead of a single batched decode; for responses with many tokens and large top_logprobs the per-call Python/tokenizer overhead dominates. This is a new endpoint (no regression to existing paths), but the loop could batch the placeholder resolution to reduce per-token overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Call /v1/chat/completions/derender with a GenerateResponse containing a long output (e.g. 1-2k tokens) and top_logprobs populated with token_id:N placeholders; measure end-to-end latency of derender_chat_response vs a batched convert_ids_to_tokens implementation.

findings.claim: `_clear_mm_prefix_for_full_attn_layers()` is invoked on every forward pass (both prefill and decode) whenever `_full_attn_layer_idxs` is non-empty. On each call it fetches the forward context, then iterates over the per-layer attn_metadata dict (one entry per attention layer), and for each layer name does string splitting/int parsing (`layer_name.split('.layers.')[1].split('.')[0]`) plus attribute checks. For a model with N attention layers this is O(N) Python-level string parsing per forward step. During decode this executes every single token step, adding measurable CPU overhead that competes with GPU scheduling and can lower throughput / raise inter-token latency, especially for models with many layers. The precomputed frozenset only removes regex work, not the per-call per-layer iteration and string parsing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode-phase inter-token latency and tokens/sec for a Gemma4 bidi-vision config with a large layer count, comparing forward with and without the per-call _clear_mm_prefix loop (e.g. cache the layer_name->layer_idx mapping once instead of re-parsing strings each step).

findings.claim: The mm_prefix_range clearing sets `metadata.mm_prefix_range = None` on the full-attention layers' metadata objects every forward call, but these metadata objects are rebuilt each step and mm_prefix_range is only set on models that are mm_prefix_lm. The string parsing over all layer names is repeated even during pure text/decode steps where no image ranges exist. Consider gating the loop on whether any mm_prefix_range was actually set this step (e.g. early-out when attn_metadata has no mm_prefix_range) to avoid unconditional per-step iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare decode throughput on text-only continuation requests (no images) with and without an early-out check before the per-layer parsing loop.

findings.claim: In the input-prep path for mm_prefix_lm models, the per-range filtering now iterates each image doc range individually with a Python loop and a per-range size comparison against sliding_window, replacing a single `list.extend`. This adds a small per-request, per-mm-feature Python overhead in the hot input-preparation path. Impact is minor for typical small numbers of images but grows with many multimodal features per batch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark prepare_inputs CPU time for batches with many multimodal features per request, comparing extend() vs the per-range filter loop.

findings.claim: For Qwen3.5 (non-interleaved layout) under AWQMarlin/AutoGPTQ/INC quantization, ba_proj is now replicated across all TP ranks (disable_tp=True) instead of column-parallel sharded. Each rank now computes the full [num_v_heads]*2 projection GEMM (num_v_heads*2 output columns) rather than its 1/tp_size slice, and then in split_ba() discards (tp_size-1)/tp_size of the b/a output via slicing. This is a tp_size-fold increase in in_proj_ba FLOPs and weight-memory footprint per rank (e.g. at TP=4 each rank does 4x the ba_proj work and holds the full quantized weight). The Marlin GEMM output columns also grow by tp_size, increasing activation memory and kernel time. This is correctness-motivated (Marlin MIN_THREAD_N>=64 constraint) but is a real per-rank compute/memory regression for quantized Qwen3.5 at TP>=2.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3.5 GatedDeltaNet AWQ-Marlin/GPTQ at TP=2 and TP=4: measure per-layer in_proj_ba forward time and per-rank weight memory vs a TP-sharded baseline (or vs the non-quantized path).

findings.claim: split_ba() slices b and a per TP rank on every forward in the hot path. The b[:, start:start+chunk] slices are non-contiguous views; downstream code calls .contiguous() (line 812-813), materializing a copy. Because ba_proj now produces the full num_v_heads output only to be sliced down, the extra elementwise work plus contiguous copy runs every decode/prefill step for quantized Qwen3.5. Minor relative to the GEMM cost but adds a per-step slice+copy that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark split_ba() + subsequent .contiguous() vs plain ba.chunk() at typical decode batch sizes.

findings.claim: split_ba() replaces only one of the three 'b, a = ba.chunk(2, dim=-1)' sites (the one at the changed line). The parent file has additional chunk sites (around lines 884) in other forward paths; if any of those paths are reachable for the replicated-ba_proj quantized Qwen3.5 config, they would slice incorrectly and could produce wrong-shaped tensors leading to fallback/recompute or mismatched kernel dispatch. Worth confirming all consumers of the replicated ba output use split_ba().

When tracing is enabled, the forward hook on every TopKRouter performs a D2H copy of the top-k indices (top_indices.detach().to('cpu', torch.int32, non_blocking=True)) plus multiple int()/shape conversions on every router forward call, for every MoE layer, every step. Even though non_blocking=True is requested, the subsequent .numpy().tobytes() calls (hidden-state / logits sidecar writes) and the JSON serialization force host-device synchronization, serializing the GPU pipeline once per layer per step. This is significant per-step overhead whenever tracing is active and can materially reduce throughput/GPU utilization during traced runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run MoE inference/training with and without --moe-routing-trace-path and measure tokens/sec and per-step latency; compare hook path (capture-hidden-states/dump-weights) vs sink path.

findings.claim: In the capture_logits path, the hook re-invokes module.gating(hs_for_gating) to recompute the router gating linear (a matmul over [num_tokens, hidden] x [hidden, num_experts]) that the router already computed in its own forward. This duplicates router gating GPU compute on every router call for every layer when --moe-routing-trace-capture-logits is enabled, adding measurable GPU work per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark per-step latency with capture_logits on vs off on an MoE model with many experts (e.g. num_experts=512).

findings.claim: For hidden-state and logits capture, the hook converts tensors to CPU bfloat16, calls .view(torch.int16).numpy().tobytes() and writes to a file synchronously inside the forward hook. .numpy() forces a full CUDA sync and the synchronous file write (potentially many MB per layer for hidden states of shape [num_tokens, hidden_size]) blocks the forward stream. This can drastically slow traced runs and cause large memory/IO pressure.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable --moe-routing-trace-capture-hidden-states on a large MoE model and measure step latency and disk write volume per step.

findings.claim: When the tracer is active, each decode step builds a list comprehension over RouterReplay.global_router_replay_instances and does torch.from_numpy(routing_indices) + record_indices + advance_step. This is per-decode-step CPU overhead in the sampling loop; negligible when tracer is None (guarded by get_moe_router_tracer() is not None), so impact is limited to traced runs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode step latency with routing replay + trace sink enabled vs disabled.

findings.claim: top_indices.detach().to('cpu', torch.int32, non_blocking=True) copies to pageable (non-pinned) host memory with non_blocking=True. For D2H copies to non-pinned memory, non_blocking is effectively ignored/unsafe and provides no async benefit; the tensor is later read for JSON serialization which forces a sync anyway. No real speedup gained; risk of reading stale data if it were actually async.
findings.suggested_benchmark: N/A (correctness/perf inspection).

findings.claim: build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards() calls build_inference_pg_collection() once per shard on EVERY world rank. Each call issues ~13 collective process-group creations via HyperCommGrid.create_pg (each a world-collective new_subgroups_by_enumeration) plus, in the embedding loop, 2 * (number of PP groups) collective dist.new_group() calls. Total collective new_group operations therefore scale roughly as num_shards * (13 + 2*num_pp_groups). Since all of these are default-group-collective (every rank must participate even for shards it does not belong to), initialization latency grows linearly with the number of shards and PP groups. For many small shards this can add noticeable job-startup time (each new_group involves a global barrier / NCCL comm setup). This is a one-time cost, not steady-state, but it is a real and unbounded-by-shard-count overhead introduced by the multi-shard partitioning path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time build_inference_pg_collections_for_shards() end-to-end for spec strings that partition a fixed world into 1, 4, 16 shards (e.g. tp=1 dp=N) and plot init time vs. shard count to confirm linear growth in collective new_group calls.

findings.claim: In the embedding-group loop, dist.new_group(ranks=embd_ranks) and dist.new_group(ranks=pos_embd_ranks) are created for every PP group in the enumeration, and pos_embd_ranks is always [pp_ranks[0]] (a single-rank group) and embd_ranks for len==1 is also a single-rank group. Creating single-rank NCCL process groups is wasteful (a global collective to build a trivial group) and multiplies with shard count. This is pre-existing behavior carried over from the deleted parallel_utils.py, but the new multi-shard driver invokes it per shard, amplifying the cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count and time dist.new_group calls during collection building for a pp=1 layout with many dp shards; compare against a variant that skips single-rank group creation.

findings.claim: The fix changes the guard for the DP dummy-pass from `not self.is_sleeping()` to `not self.model_executor.is_sleeping`. Per the removed comment, `is_sleeping()` also returns True when the scheduler is paused (`is_scheduler_paused()`) and covers the KV-offload window before `model_executor.is_sleeping` flips. By narrowing the condition to only `model_executor.is_sleeping`, the engine may now execute a dummy batch during the KV-offload / scheduler-paused window when it previously would have skipped it. In DP, dummy batches trigger a synchronized forward pass across all ranks; running an unnecessary dummy pass (or diverging on whether to run one across ranks) wastes GPU cycles each idle iteration and, if ranks disagree, can stall the collective and cause a hang. This is a behavioral narrowing introduced alongside the syntax fix, not a pure no-op.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a DP=2 deployment, put one engine to sleep / trigger KV-offload sleep, and measure idle-loop GPU utilization and per-iteration latency; verify no extra dummy forward passes run and no cross-rank collective stalls occur during the offload window.

findings.claim: The torchcodec backend defaults to `num_ffmpeg_threads=0`, which per the docstring lets FFmpeg pick `min(cpu_count + 1, 16)` decoding threads. In a serving deployment, video decoding runs on the input-processing path, often across many concurrent requests and/or a multiprocess API server. Each concurrent torchcodec decode can spin up ~16 FFmpeg threads, causing CPU thread over-subscription and context-switch overhead that can degrade end-to-end preprocessing throughput. The opencv/pyav paths cap threads more conservatively (opencv min(cpu_count,16), pyav min(cpu_count,(h+15)/16) and SLICE threading). Consider a lower default or clamping based on concurrency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure preprocessing throughput/latency under N concurrent video requests with backend=torchcodec at num_ffmpeg_threads=0 vs a bounded value (e.g. 1-4), on a machine with high cpu_count.

findings.claim: The torchcodec backend defaults to seek_mode='exact', which per the code comments scans the entire file when the decoder is created to guarantee frame-accurate sampling. For long videos this full-file scan on each decoder construction adds per-request decode latency compared to 'approximate' mode (which skips the scan). Since vLLM only samples a small number of frames uniformly, the scan cost can dominate decoder setup time. Whether this is a regression depends on the required accuracy, but the default trades latency for accuracy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time make_torchcodec_decoder + decode_torchcodec_frames on a long (e.g. 10-60 min) video with seek_mode='exact' vs 'approximate' and compare decoder-creation latency.

findings.claim: decode_torchcodec_frames calls batch.data.numpy(), which for a torch tensor produced by torchcodec returns a view sharing memory with the tensor only when the tensor is CPU and contiguous; otherwise it forces a copy. Downstream the frames array is expected to be a standalone uint8 NHWC ndarray. If torchcodec keeps the full decoded batch tensor referenced via the numpy view, the underlying tensor buffer stays alive as long as the ndarray does. This is a minor concern versus opencv/pyav which build fresh numpy arrays.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile peak RSS when decoding a batch of frames via torchcodec vs opencv and check whether the returned ndarray retains the full decoder tensor buffer.

findings.claim: mm_serde.py constructs the MsgpackEncoder with size_threshold=2**62, forcing ALL multimodal tensors (including large pixel_values feature tensors) to be serialized inline into a single msgpack buffer, then base64-encoded (encode_mm_kwargs_item), then embedded into a JSON response. This causes each multimodal item's tensor payload to be materialized as a base64 string (~33% size inflation) and copied several times (msgpack buffer -> base64 string -> JSON). For image/video features that can be tens of MB, this dramatically increases the render endpoint's per-request memory footprint and CPU cost versus the normal out-of-band (zero-copy) transport that the encoder uses by default. It also inflates the HTTP payload transferred from render->generate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Render a request with a large image (e.g. 4K) via /v1/chat/completions/render and measure peak RSS and wall-clock time of _extract_mm_features / encode_mm_kwargs_item vs. the metadata-only path, and measure JSON payload size.

findings.claim: By adding skip_mm_cache=True to the render preprocessing paths (render/serving.py preprocess_chat and preprocess_completion) and to the generate fallback path (disagg/serving.py), multimodal feature tensors are no longer written to / read from the sender-side mm processor cache during render. Consequently repeated requests with identical images will re-run the (expensive) HF multimodal processor every time instead of hitting the cache, increasing CPU time and latency for the render step under workloads with repeated media.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Send the same multimodal request twice through /render and compare processing latency and mm cache hit stats with and without skip_mm_cache=True.

findings.claim: encode_mm_kwargs_item is invoked per multimodal item in a Python loop inside _extract_mm_features on every render request, each call doing a full msgpack encode + base64 encode of the tensor data. For requests with many multimodal items this serialization runs synchronously on the request path and scales linearly with item count and tensor size.
