findings.claim: `is_nvrx_min_version()` performs an `import nvidia_resiliency_ext` and packaging Version parsing on every call. This is cheap and only reached via `has_nvrx_async_support()` (called at import time, not in a hot path), so runtime impact is negligible; noted only for completeness.

For list/tuple KV caches (Mamba / Kimi Delta Attention), _fill_state_tensor fills the ENTIRE state tensor (kv_cache.normal_/fill_ over all rows) every time _fill_blocks runs. _fill_blocks is invoked once per (request, group) pair inside start_fill_kv's loop over metadata.reqs_to_fill. Unlike the block-indexed path — which only writes the specific block rows a request owns (work proportional to that request's block count) — the state-tensor path rewrites the whole buffer independently of how many blocks/tokens the request needs. When multiple requests are filled in the same scheduler step (or the same layer appears across several groups), the same full state buffer is re-filled N times, doing O(num_requests * state_size) redundant GPU writes instead of O(block_rows). This inflates GPU work and launch overhead during the fill phase.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DecodeBenchConnector against a Mamba/hybrid model with many concurrent requests (high --max-concurrency) and measure time spent in start_fill_kv vs. a single-fill baseline.

findings.claim: The dispatch uses `all(isinstance(t, torch.Tensor) for t in kv_cache)` on every _fill_blocks call for every layer. For layers with many state tensors this iterates the full container each call; it is invoked per (request, group, layer). Minor, but could be hoisted/cached since the KV cache structure is fixed after register_kv_caches.

load_tokenizer_json_with_extra_tokens introduces a full parse -> intermediate struct -> re-serialize-to-serde_json::Value round trip for the entire tokenizer.json. Previously HfTokenizer::from_file / FastokensTokenizer::from_file parsed the file directly (typically streaming/string-based). Now the whole document (including the potentially very large `vocab`/`merges`, captured verbatim via `#[serde(flatten)] extra: serde_json::Map`) is: (1) read to a String, (2) deserialized into TokenizerJson, (3) fully re-serialized into an in-memory serde_json::Value tree via serde_json::to_value, and (4) re-parsed via from_json / from_value. For large-vocabulary tokenizers (tens of MB) this materializes the entire JSON tree in memory and traverses it several extra times, increasing tokenizer load time and peak memory. This is one-time initialization cost (not per-request), so impact is bounded but measurable, especially when many tokenizers/workers load concurrently.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time HuggingFaceTokenizer::new()/new_hf()/new_fastokens() and measure peak RSS on a large real-world tokenizer.json (e.g. a 5-15MB Qwen/Llama tokenizer) before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: new_hf now uses serde_json::from_value::<HfTokenizer>(tokenizer_json) instead of HfTokenizer::from_file(path). from_value requires the complete serde_json::Value tree to be built first (done in load_tokenizer_json_with_extra_tokens), whereas from_file can parse directly from the file/string without constructing an intermediate Value. This adds an extra full allocation+traversal of the tokenizer document during the HF fallback load path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark new_hf() load time on a large tokenizer.json comparing from_file vs the from_value-based path.

findings.claim: _is_pre_v11_tokeniser(self.model_tokenizer) is now called twice during __init__ (once at the coroutine-guard branch on line ~145 and once to set self._is_pre_v11 on line ~148). For HF tokenizers this evaluates getattr(...)('get_vocab')() twice, and for uncached HF tokenizers get_vocab() can rebuild an O(vocab)-sized dict. This is only a one-time construction cost per parser instance (not per request/token), so impact is negligible; it could be de-duplicated by computing self._is_pre_v11 first and reusing it for the coroutine-guard branch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time MistralToolParser.__init__ with an uncached HF tokenizer (large vocab) before/after de-duplicating the _is_pre_v11 computation.

findings.claim: Positive change: the previous code invoked _is_pre_v11_tokeniser(self.model_tokenizer) inside extract_tool_calls_streaming, i.e. on every streaming delta/decoded token. For HF tokenizers the new implementation of that helper calls get_vocab() and does a dict membership test, which would have been paid per token. This commit caches the result into self._is_pre_v11 at __init__ and uses the cached boolean in the streaming path, removing per-token overhead. No regression; this is a latency improvement for streaming tool-call generation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming tool-call generation throughput/latency with an HF (non-Mistral) tokenizer, comparing per-token overhead of calling _is_pre_v11_tokeniser vs the cached self._is_pre_v11.

findings.claim: A new self.current_tool_name.replace('[ARGS]', '') runs on every streaming delta while in the name-parsing state. This is a small linear scan over the accumulated tool-name string and is negligible in practice, but it does add a per-delta allocation/scan that grows with the (short) tool name length.

The diff adds three new `grid.create_pg(...)` calls in `_build_grid`. Each `create_pg` calls `dist.new_subgroups_by_enumeration`, which creates one or more NCCL communicators via collective `new_group` participation across all grid ranks. The newly added `["tp", "cp", "dp", "pp"]` group spans every dimension of the grid, so its single subgroup equals the entire module rank span. Crucially, `pg_collection_from_grid` only retrieves `tp_dp` (`["tp","dp"]`) and `tp_dp_cp` (`["tp","dp","cp"]`); the full `tp-cp-dp-pp` group is created but never fetched anywhere via `get_pg`. This produces a redundant NCCL communicator that adds startup/init latency (extra collective communicator setup and NCCL handshake) and consumes GPU memory (each NCCL communicator reserves buffers, typically tens of MB) for no runtime benefit. It is also torn down only at grid.destroy(), so the memory is held for the whole run.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure topology init wall-clock time and per-GPU NCCL communicator memory (torch.cuda.memory_reserved / nvidia-smi) with vs. without the `["tp","cp","dp","pp"]` create_pg call across the 8-GPU hetero layout.

findings.claim: Adding `["tp","dp"]` and `["tp","dp","cp"]` process-group creation increases the number of collective communicator-creation operations at topology build time (each is a synchronization/handshake point across the grid ranks). These two are actually consumed (MoE router expert-bias all-reduce, cuda graphs), so the cost is justified, but it does add measurable one-time initialization latency, particularly at scale where NCCL communicator creation is serialized and non-trivial.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time create_topology() at increasing world sizes to quantify added init latency from the two extra communicators.

findings.claim: The refactor now routes *all* connections — including plaintext HTTP and plaintext gRPC — through the new `MaybeTlsStream` enum, whose `AsyncRead`/`AsyncWrite` impls (generated via `#[enum_derive(tokio1::AsyncRead, tokio1::AsyncWrite)]`) match on the `Plain`/`Tls` variant on every `poll_read`/`poll_write`. Previously the plaintext path served the bare `ListenerIo` directly, without this extra enum layer wrapping the stream. This adds one branch per poll on the byte-transfer hot path for non-TLS connections. The cost is tiny (a predictable match arm), so this is only a micro-overhead, but it is a new per-poll cost that did not exist for plaintext before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/latency benchmark of a plaintext HTTP request loop (many small requests) comparing bare ListenerIo vs MaybeTlsStream::Plain to measure any per-poll dispatch overhead.

findings.claim: The gRPC serve path previously boxed the two protocol arms' futures via `.boxed()` to unify their types; the new code uses a single `MaybeTlsListener` incoming type and drops `.boxed()`, removing a heap allocation and dynamic-dispatch of the server future. This is a (very minor) performance improvement, noted for completeness — no regression here.

The new list comprehension in _parse_image_data applies convert_image_mode(normalize_image(item), 'RGB') to every directly-supplied PIL image at parse time. normalize_image calls ImageOps.exif_transpose, which reads EXIF orientation and materializes a rotated copy of the full image when an orientation tag is present, and convert_image_mode may allocate a new RGB image (and an intermediate RGBA image for transparent inputs). For workloads that pass many/large PIL images directly, this adds a per-image full-image CPU pass and potential extra allocations on the request-processing critical path. When images have no EXIF tag and are already RGB, the cost is small (exif_transpose returns the image unchanged and convert_image_mode short-circuits), so the impact is bounded but non-zero. This overhead is inherent to the correctness fix (normalizing orientation/mode of direct PIL inputs to match the byte-loaded path in ImageMediaIO), so it is expected rather than a regression to be removed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Feed a batch of large (e.g. 4K) PIL images directly (some with EXIF orientation, some RGBA) into MultiModalDataParser._parse_image_data and measure added CPU latency/allocations per image vs. the parent commit.

findings.claim: decode_prompt_context returns (decoded, ids.to_vec()) on the success (common) path, cloning the id slice on every seed attempt. In seed_prefix's fallback branch this clones the entire prompt (potentially thousands of tokens) via ids.to_vec() and reassigns self.ids, whereas the previous code performed the fallback decode without any allocation or reassignment of self.ids. This adds one O(prompt_len) allocation+copy. It occurs only once per sequence (gated by prefix_seeded), so impact is small, but for long prompts on high-throughput serving it is unnecessary work; the Ok path could avoid the clone by returning a borrow / indicating 'unchanged'.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark seed_prefix / first push_token latency with a very long prompt (e.g. 8k tokens) comparing per-sequence detokenizer initialization cost before vs after.

findings.claim: On the (rare) decode error path, decode_prompt_context iterates all ids calling id_to_token per id, then performs a second full decode of the filtered set. For prompts containing out-of-vocab ids this roughly doubles the seeding decode cost plus adds a per-id vocab lookup. This is the intended tolerance behavior and only runs once per sequence when a decode error actually occurs, so throughput impact is negligible in normal operation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-token latency for a prompt containing out-of-vocab ids vs an in-vocab prompt of the same length.

findings.claim: The bugfix swaps which grid dimension maps to rows (mn) vs. K groups (sf_k): previously grid.x indexed sf_k (contiguous K groups) and grid.y indexed rows; now grid.x indexes rows and grid.y indexes sf_k. CUDA launches/co-schedules blocks roughly in blockIdx.x-fastest order, so the linearly-consecutive blocks that tend to co-reside on SMs now differ by row (mn) rather than by adjacent K group. In the row-major input (offset = mn_idx*groups_per_row*GROUP_SIZE + sf_k_idx*GROUP_SIZE), adjacent K groups of the same row are contiguous in HBM, whereas adjacent rows for a fixed K group are separated by groups_per_row*GROUP_SIZE elements (strided). For wide-K tensors (padded_groups_per_row large, so sf_k_blocks > 1), the fast-varying grid dimension now strides across rows instead of walking contiguous memory, which can reduce L2 reuse/locality among concurrently resident blocks and slightly lower achieved memory bandwidth. This is a necessary correctness fix (grid.y is capped at 65535, and mn can exceed that), so the swap is justified; the effect is a possible modest bandwidth regression on wide-K, moderate-mn shapes, not a hang or per-block access-pattern change (each block's internal footprint is unchanged).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark per_token_group_quant_8bit_packed throughput/bandwidth on wide-K shapes (e.g., k=4096-8192, groups_per_row 32-64 so sf_k_blocks>1) with moderate mn (a few thousand rows) before vs. after the grid-dim swap, measuring achieved HBM bandwidth / kernel time via nsight-compute L2 hit rate.

findings.claim: reshard_model_weights now unconditionally calls _harmonize_buffer_dtypes on every refit, which performs a torch.distributed.all_gather_object over the (potentially cross-cluster) group. Unlike the plan itself, which is cached in _plan_cache and only built once, this dtype-harmonization all_gather_object is NOT cached and runs on every swap_model_weights invocation. In RL training these refits happen every optimizer step / rollout, so a Python-object all_gather (pickle-based, serializing a dict of {buffer_name -> dtype} for every rank) is added to the critical path of each refit. all_gather_object is significantly slower than tensor collectives (CPU pickling + separate size-exchange + gather), and it scales with world size and buffer count. For large worlds this becomes measurable per-refit overhead that did not exist before.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure swap_model_weights latency (repeated calls) before/after this commit for a large-world MoE model; isolate the added all_gather_object cost per refit, especially at high world sizes.

findings.claim: _harmonize_buffer_dtypes rebuilds the source-side persistent-buffer dtype map and repeats the all_gather_object every call, even though buffer dtypes are effectively static after the trainer's first-forward upcast. Because the collective and dict-building are not cached (in contrast to the plan cache), the same information is recomputed and re-communicated on each refit. Caching the harmonization result (or performing the dtype fix once during prepare_swap_model_weights) would remove this repeated per-refit collective.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-refit wall time across N successive swap_model_weights calls to show the harmonization cost is paid every time rather than amortized.

findings.claim: named_refit_tensors (used in execution.py and planner.py) now iterates module.named_modules() to enumerate persistent buffers in addition to named_parameters(recurse=True). This doubles the module tree traversal during metadata extraction / param dict construction on every reshard. The extra traversal is O(num_modules) Python work; for very large models with many layers this adds modest per-refit CPU overhead on top of the existing named_parameters walk. Metadata extraction feeds the cached plan, so this is only paid on plan build, but the execution.py src_params/dst_params construction runs each refit.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile execute_reshard_plan's src_params/dst_params dict construction (named_refit_tensors vs named_parameters) for a large model to quantify the extra named_modules traversal cost per refit.

findings.claim: Switching the LoRA registry from BTreeMap to IndexMap changes unload_lora's removal from BTreeMap::remove (O(log n)) to IndexMap::shift_remove (O(n), since shift_remove shifts all subsequent entries to preserve insertion order). This is only a concern with a large number of loaded adapters and unload is a rare admin-path operation, so the impact is negligible in practice. It cannot be replaced with the O(1) swap_remove because that would break the load-order guarantee this change is introducing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark unload_lora with N=1000 loaded adapters, BTreeMap vs IndexMap shift_remove.

findings.claim: On every /v1/completions and /v1/chat/completions request, chat_completions/completions now call state.tokenizer_vocab_size(), which resolves through TextLlm -> backend default trait impl -> HuggingFaceTokenizer::vocab_size() -> tokenizers::Tokenizer::get_vocab_size(true) for the HF backend. In the HuggingFace `tokenizers` crate, get_vocab_size(true) constructs the full merged vocabulary (base model vocab HashMap clone + added-tokens map) just to return its length. For real LLM tokenizers this is an O(vocab_size) allocation of 100k-250k+ entries executed synchronously per request, before request dispatch. There is no caching of the vocab size; the value is recomputed on each request. This adds a per-request CPU/allocation cost (and churn on the allocator) that scales with vocabulary size and request rate, degrading request-admission latency and throughput. The fastokens backend path (t.vocab_size()) is cheap, but any model that falls back to the HF `tokenizers` backend pays this cost every request.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load an HF-backend model (one that falls back from fastokens to tokenizers), then hammer /v1/completions with a trivial prompt at high QPS and measure p50/p99 request-admission latency and per-request allocations with vs. without the validate_token_id_ranges call; compare against caching tokenizer_vocab_size once at startup.

findings.claim: tokenizer_vocab_size() and model_vocab_size() are immutable for the lifetime of the loaded model but are recomputed on every request via a trait-object dispatch chain (AppState -> ChatLlm -> TextLlm -> Box<dyn TextBackend>). Even for the cheap fastokens/tekken/tiktoken backends this is unnecessary indirection per request; caching these two values once (e.g. in AppState at construction) would remove the recomputation entirely and eliminate the risk of the expensive HF get_vocab_size path being hit repeatedly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark repeated AppState::tokenizer_vocab_size()/model_vocab_size() calls for each backend type to quantify per-call cost, then compare against a cached-at-startup variant.

findings.claim: In read_tb_logs_as_list, the non-broadcast (index != -1, default index=0) path previously loaded and Reload()-ed only a single event file (files[index]). After the change, _load_event_accumulators_with_scalars unconditionally iterates over ALL matching event files, constructing an EventAccumulator and calling ea.Reload() (which parses the entire event file into memory, with SIZE_GUIDANCE=0 meaning no truncation) for every file, even when only one accumulator is ultimately kept via accumulators[index]. This turns O(1) file parsing into O(N) parsing plus O(N) peak memory for the common single-index case. Because these are functional test utilities (not runtime training/inference code) and event-file counts per run are typically small, the impact is on test wall-clock and test-runner memory rather than production performance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Point read_tb_logs_as_list at a directory containing multiple large tfevents files and time the default index=0 call before vs. after the change; measure peak RSS during the call.

findings.claim: The oversize branch now emits `logger.warning_once` on a per-request multimodal hot path (called from get_and_update_item for every uncacheable item), whereas previously it was `logger.debug` which is suppressed at the default INFO log level. The intent of `warning_once` is to log only once, but it is defeated here: `warning_once` is backed by `_print_warning_once`, which is `@lru_cache`-decorated with the cache key being `(logger, msg, *args)`. The args passed are `mm_hash` (a unique hash per multimodal item) and `str(e)` (the exception message, which typically embeds the object size and is therefore also unique per item). Because these arguments differ on essentially every call, the dedup never kicks in and a full WARNING log line is written for every oversized item. String formatting + WARNING-level I/O per request on a large-media workload is meaningfully more expensive than the prior silenced debug call, adding latency to multimodal request processing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Repeatedly submit distinct multimodal items that exceed --mm-shm-cache-max-object-size-mb and measure per-request processing latency / log-write volume vs. the parent commit (debug branch).

findings.claim: `_print_warning_once` uses the default `@lru_cache` (maxsize=128). Passing per-item-unique args (`mm_hash`, `str(e)`) means each oversized item inserts a fresh entry, cycling out prior entries. This both prevents the intended one-time suppression and causes continual eviction/insertion churn in the lru_cache under sustained oversized-item traffic. Using `scope`/a static message without the varying args (or a dedicated set of already-warned states) would keep the 'once' semantics and avoid the per-call cache mutation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare number of emitted warnings and lru_cache size over N distinct oversized items; expect ~N warnings instead of 1.

findings.claim: The added torchrun flags --redirects "3" and --tee "0:3" cause each worker's stdout+stderr to be written to per-rank files under --log-dir (and rank 0 to be additionally teed to the parent's captured stream). This adds file-I/O for worker output on top of the pre-existing '> $SERVER_LOG 2>&1' redirect. If the inference server emits any log output on the request/token hot path, this extra logging I/O runs during the timed benchmark sweep and could perturb the measured latency/throughput numbers, making the perf results noisier or slightly worse. This is a CI-harness-only concern; it does not affect the framework's production runtime performance since no runtime code is changed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the perf harness with and without the --tee/--redirects flags on the GPT 583M case and compare reported latency/throughput to confirm logging I/O does not shift measured numbers.

findings.claim: The commit documents (in its own comment) that PR #3509 changed the default inference-dynamic-batching CUDA-graph sizing distribution from 'linear' to 'exponential' plus a mixed-prefill grid, which raised peak allocated memory by ~13.6% (~69GB vs ~61GB golden). Rather than addressing that regression in the default code path, this change pins only the CI test back to '--inference-dynamic-batching-cuda-graph-sizing-distribution: linear'. The default (exponential) path shipped to users still incurs the +13.6% peak memory. The test no longer guards against this regression because it opts out of the new default; any further memory growth on the default sizing distribution will go undetected here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run GRPO 8B TP4/PP1/DP2 dynamic-batching inference with the DEFAULT (exponential) sizing distribution and measure peak allocated bytes vs the pinned 'linear' config to quantify the standing regression on the default path.

findings.claim: Setting --num-workers: 0 forces synchronous, in-process data loading, removing the async prefetch/overlap and pin_memory H2D staging provided by the default 2 persistent workers. For these CI tests the data is tiny so impact is negligible (as the comment states), but if this pattern is copied to non-trivial datasets it will serialize batch preparation with the training step and lose input-pipeline/compute overlap, reducing throughput and leaving the GPU idle during data fetch. Note this trades a real (but rare) hang for guaranteed serialization.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare steps/sec with --num-workers 0 vs 2 on a realistic-sized T5 dataset to measure the input-pipeline stall introduced by synchronous loading.

findings.claim: When a tokenizer mismatch is detected, `_align_prompts_to_server_tokenizer` issues up to 2 HTTP round-trips per request (`_tokenize` then `_detokenize`) across the entire dataset before the benchmark starts. With the default of 1000 prompts (and potentially much larger `--num-prompts`) throttled to a semaphore of 64, this adds a large, fixed pre-benchmark latency and generates a burst of tokenize/detokenize load on the very server about to be measured. If alignment is triggered on a large dataset, benchmark wall-clock startup time grows substantially.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run serve.py with --dataset-name random --num-prompts 10000 against a server whose tokenizer differs from the client, and measure the time spent in _align_prompts_to_server_tokenizer vs. the actual benchmark.

findings.claim: Prompts were already produced (and length-controlled) locally by get_samples using the local tokenizer, yet the alignment path re-tokenizes every prompt on the server side (one /tokenize call per request) even for prompts whose length already matches. `_fix_one` re-tokenizes input_requests[0] a second time (it was already tokenized to detect the mismatch), wasting one round-trip. Filtering out requests already at target length client-side, or reusing the first tokenization result, would avoid this redundant network work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure number of /tokenize + /detokenize HTTP calls emitted during alignment vs. number of requests actually needing correction.

findings.claim: The aiohttp ClientSession/requests in _align_prompts_to_server_tokenizer set no total or per-request timeout. If the server stalls on a /tokenize or /detokenize request, the awaited call (and thus the whole gather) can block indefinitely, hanging the benchmark before it starts. A timeout on the ClientSession would bound this.

The TransformerEngine dependency bump (2.15.0 -> 2.16.0) coincides with a ~1.1% increase in mem-allocated-bytes for gpt3_7b_tp1_pp4_memory_speed (24540168192 -> 24808996864, ~+269MB) as reflected in the updated golden values. This memory growth is an effect of the upstream TE library, not of any inspectable code change in this repo, so it cannot be further substantiated from the diff. Other test cases (e.g. bert_mcore_tp2_pp2) show slightly reduced memory and iteration time, so the net effect is model/config dependent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak/allocated GPU memory and iteration time for gpt3_7b_tp1_pp4 training under TE 2.15.0 vs 2.16.0 to confirm whether the ~269MB allocation increase is a real regression or a one-time buffer layout change.

findings.claim: The NVTX range_push/range_pop calls are unconditionally invoked on every forward and backward of every FSDP unit, with no guard flag. Elsewhere in this codebase NVTX ranges are gated behind a profiling switch (configure_nvtx_profiling / nvtx_range_push helper in megatron/core/utils.py, which checks whether NVTX profiling is enabled). Here the raw torch.cuda.nvtx.range_push is always called plus an f-string label is always built via _nvtx_label. Each call is cheap individually, but across many FSDP units and every microbatch this adds a small, always-on Python + CUDA driver overhead even when no profiler is attached. Consider gating behind the same profiling flag used elsewhere.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark a training step with many nested FSDP units (e.g. deep transformer) with and without the NVTX push/pop + f-string label construction to measure per-step CPU overhead when no profiler is attached.

findings.claim: _nvtx_label builds a formatted string ('MFSDP {name} {phase}') and accesses the self.name property twice on every pre_forward and pre_backward for every FSDP unit. String formatting on the CPU-bound hook path runs once per unit per microbatch. Trivial per call, but purely for profiling metadata; when profiling is disabled this string work is wasted.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare CPU time of pre_forward/pre_backward hooks with and without the label-construction to quantify overhead at scale.

findings.claim: The default for --send-conversation-id is now False, so by default the benchmark no longer injects conversation_id into request payloads (and, per send_request, also no longer sends the X-Session-ID header, which is gated on conversation_id is not None). When run against the disaggregated multi-turn proxy without explicitly passing --send-conversation-id, every turn becomes a cache MISS: cross-turn KV cache reuse and the bidirectional KV transfer path are never exercised. Compared to the previous behavior (conversation_id always sent), the out-of-the-box benchmark now measures a strictly slower, no-cache path and will report worse TTFT/latency and 0% effective prefix reuse for proxy runs unless the operator remembers the new opt-in flag. This is a benchmark/tooling behavior change, not a change to the runtime engine itself, and is intentional for OpenAI-schema compatibility, so it does not degrade production runtime performance.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run benchmark_serving_multi_turn.py against disagg_proxy_multiturn.py with and without --send-conversation-id and compare TTFT, latency, and proxy cache HIT rate to confirm the default now yields all-MISS behavior.

findings.claim: The change replaces `yield from tensor_iter` with `yield name, tensor.clone()` in `runai_safetensors_weights_iterator`. This forces a full host-side deep copy of every weight tensor as it is streamed. For a large LLM (tens to hundreds of GB of weights) this adds a full extra allocation + memcpy of each tensor on the CPU during weight loading. The clone allocates fresh pinned/unpinned host memory for the whole tensor before it is (typically) copied to the GPU, transiently increasing peak host memory usage by up to one tensor's size and adding CPU memcpy bandwidth cost, slowing overall model load time. This is a functional-correctness fix (the RunAI streamer reuses/overwrites its internal buffer between yielded tensors, so consumers that hold references would see corrupted data), so the copy is necessary — but it does have a real, measurable weight-loading throughput/memory cost compared to the zero-copy path it replaces.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time `runai_safetensors_weights_iterator` (or full model load via RunAI streamer loader) on a multi-GB checkpoint before/after the change; measure wall-clock load time and peak host RSS.

findings.claim: The change exports NCCL_ALGO=Ring and NCCL_PROTO=simple in _run_training.sh, which now applies to ALL functional tests driven by this shell script (torchrun training path and jet workloads), not just the nemo_run docker path where these vars previously lived. Forcing the Ring algorithm and Simple protocol disables NCCL auto-tuning (LL/LL128 protocols, Tree/CollNet/NVLS algorithms). On H100/NVSwitch nodes this is measurably slower for all-reduce/all-gather/reduce-scatter than NCCL's default selection, so collective-bound training/inference tests will lose throughput. The updated golden values corroborate this: the MoE inference tpot rises (~0.0168 -> ~0.0185 s/token in the cuda-graphs case, ~10%).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-step iteration time and inference tpot for a TP>1 test case (e.g. gpt3_7b_tp4_pp1) with vs without NCCL_ALGO=Ring / NCCL_PROTO=simple on an H100 NVSwitch node.

findings.claim: NCCL_NVLS_ENABLE=0 disables NVLink SHARP (NVLS) in-network reduction, and NCCL_COLLNET_ENABLE=0 disables CollNet. On H100 systems with NVSwitch, NVLS gives large speedups for big all-reduce/all-gather operations (the dominant collective in TP/DP). Applying this to every functional test via the shared shell script degrades collective bandwidth relative to NCCL defaults, slowing tensor-parallel and data-parallel communication.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure all-reduce bandwidth (nccl-tests) and end-to-end step time with NCCL_NVLS_ENABLE=1 vs 0 on the H100 test node.

findings.claim: These NCCL tunables were previously scoped only to the nemo_run docker launcher's env_vars. Moving them into _run_training.sh broadens their scope to every test using the script. Test cases that were previously running with NCCL defaults (auto-tuned, faster) will now run under the pinned Ring/Simple/NVLS-off configuration, potentially increasing CI wall-clock time and requiring the golden-value regeneration seen in this diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare total CI functional-test suite wall-clock before and after this commit.

findings.claim: The removed block auto-enabled the cuDNN FusedAttention workspace-optimization path (via NVTE_FUSED_ATTN_FORCE_WORKSPACE_OPT=1 -> CUDNN_FRONTEND_ATTN_DP_WORKSPACE_LIMIT=-1) for cuDNN in [8.9.5, 9.0.0) when deterministic algorithms are enabled. Per the docs deleted in this same commit, on pre-9.0 cuDNN the workspace-opt path is 20-30% faster (and is the deterministic path). After this change, users on that old cuDNN range running deterministic attention no longer get the faster path auto-selected and fall back to cuDNN's default heuristic (workspace-opt only when <=256MB), which can be slower. Impact is limited to that narrow, legacy cuDNN window; on cuDNN >= 9.0 there is no runtime effect.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FusedAttention forward+backward with torch deterministic algorithms enabled on cuDNN 8.9.x (>=8.9.5), comparing throughput before vs after this commit.

findings.claim: In `online_process_loader` (invoked once per weight tensor during reload), when `len(LOADING_LAYERS) >= 2` the code eagerly computes `mem_used = sum(get_info_size(...))` and `sorted([...class names...])` on EVERY load call, then passes them to `logger.warning_once`. Because the expensive work is done before the call, the `warning_once`/`lru_cache` dedup only suppresses the actual log emission, not the computation. `get_info_size` itself iterates over `info.loaded_weights` and every bound argument tensor for each loading layer, so the cost per call is O(#loading_layers x #buffered_weights). During out-of-order reloads (precisely the scenario the warning targets) many layers stay buffered simultaneously, making this an O(N^2) scan repeated for every weight loaded. The message string / mem total is identical each time, so this recomputation is wasted work on the weight-reload hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Reload a model's weights out-of-order (interleave layers so many stay in LOADING_LAYERS) and measure reload_weights wall time with and without the mem_used/sorted computation guarded behind a one-shot flag.

findings.claim: `has_device_tensors(bound_args)` and the WeakSet add are now executed on every non-attention weight load during reload. This adds a per-tensor scan over all bound arguments plus a WeakSet insertion. Individually cheap, but it runs for every parameter of every layer on each reload; combined with the always-computed warning payload above it increases fixed overhead of the layerwise reload path relative to the parent implementation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark reload_weights on a large model (in-order loading) comparing parent vs this commit to measure added per-tensor overhead.

findings.claim: The @triton.autotune decorator over _AUTOTUNE_CONFIGS (keyed on N,K) is removed and replaced with a fixed hand-coded heuristic (_get_default_config) that selects BLOCK_SIZE_*, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages purely from M=num_tokens_hint, E, top_k. Autotuning previously searched ~25 configs and picked the empirically fastest launch parameters for the actual weight shape (N,K). A single fixed heuristic that ignores N and K (only branches on M) will almost certainly be slower than the autotuned optimum on many expert weight shapes, degrading grouped-GEMM throughput for both FC1 and FC2. The tradeoff (avoiding autotune warmup + tuning per-BLOCK_SIZE_M) is real but the steady-state kernel is likely to regress unless the fixed config happens to match the tuned one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark vllm_fused_moe FC1/FC2 grouped GEMM latency across representative (N,K,num_local_experts) shapes at decode (M small) and prefill (M large), comparing the removed autotuned kernel vs the new fixed-config kernel.

findings.claim: The GEMM grid is now sized host-side from num_tokens_hint (em_hint) rather than num_SMs or the worst-case padded length. On prefill spikes where actual num_tokens_post_padded exceeds the hinted grid, each CTA must serialize additional tiles via the outer tl.range loop, reducing parallelism and increasing latency for those steps. If num_tokens_hint is chronically underestimated relative to actual token counts, the GEMM will run under-parallelized on every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run inference with num_tokens_hint set below the actual per-step token count and measure grouped-GEMM latency vs a correctly-sized grid.

findings.claim: _moe_sum grid changed from (max_tokens,) to (BLOCK_M,) where BLOCK_M=_get_num_sms (~persistent grid). This is generally a launch-overhead win at decode, but for large valid_tokens (prefill) with few SMs relative to tokens*NUM_K_BLOCKS work, each CTA now serially strides over many tokens, and the inner topk*NUM_K_BLOCKS loop per token means the persistent grid can under-utilize when NUM_K_BLOCKS>1 (K reload per token). Likely net-positive but worth validating that the persistent form does not regress prefill moe_sum throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark _moe_sum for small (decode) and large (prefill) valid_tokens across K sizes, comparing (max_tokens,) grid vs (num_sms,) persistent grid.

findings.claim: ep_consensus_interval defaults to 20 (unchanged from the previous hardcoded %20), so no immediate regression. However making it configurable and larger would increase the worst-case delay before a pause is honored (~10-20ms per skipped step), trading per-step all-reduce cost for tail latency. Not a regression at the default but a latency knob to watch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode step time and pause-response latency at varying ep_consensus_interval values.
